Can Justin Trudeau's Liberals seize on the U.S. Democrats' success? Here's where they should start

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/can-justin-trudeaus-liberals-seize-on-u-s/docview/3097838861/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals+seize+on+the+U.S.+Democrats%27+success%3F+Here%27s+where+they+should+start&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a bit of bad luck for Justin Trudeau's Liberals, having to hold their annual cabinet retreat on the heels of the wildly successful Democratic convention in the U.S.
The comparisons and the questions were going to be inevitable - why can't the Trudeau Liberals do that kind of progressive political revival here in Canada?
As minister after minister explained, no one is talking about showing Trudeau the exit door, as the Democrats did with Joe Biden.
But leaving that nuclear option aside, one presumes that ministers would be drawing some lessons from how the Democrats rebranded themselves and their new leader, Kamala Harris, at last week's convention.
Some of the strategies on display in Chicago last week could be transferable techniques - at least to some measure.
The first one, the "joy" factor, is the most obvious but also the toughest. Government House Leader Karina Gould alluded to this when she was talking to reporters about possible parallels between Liberals here and the rebranded Democrats.
"Just because the Democrats do something in the United States does not then mean that we should be doing that here in Canada as Liberals," Gould said. "What I do think is that progressive voters are looking for continued hope."
Hope, positivity and joy can be hard to pull off, though, if you're a nine-year-old government, languishing as much as 20 points behind the Conservatives in the opinion polls.
The cabinet retreat featured a small flurry of announcements, but it would be a reach to call them sunny measures - tariffs on Chinese vehicles, limits to temporary foreign workers, a task force on trade barriers. They were meant to show a government hard at work, which is serious, as opposed to joyous business.
There have been signs the Liberals would dearly love to reframe their battle against Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives as the Democrats have shifted their framing of Donald Trump - from scary to "weird," from object of fear to object of ridicule.
We can probably expect to see Liberals making more forays in this direction, similar to Immigration Minister Marc Miller, talking in Halifax about Poilievre's "crap slogans." But can they diminish a 20-point-lead with punchlines or mockery? That won't be easy.
There was another new Democratic strategy on display in Chicago - or maybe it's more properly noted at what was not on display, conspicuous by its absence.
Harris was being very careful how she came at the idea of being the first female president or the first Black and South Asian female president. Instead, she spoke more of herself as someone from humble beginnings, evidence that ambitious dreams are available to all in America.
This was no doubt a legacy of what happened to Hillary Clinton and her bid for the presidency, which constantly reminded voters of the identity stakes in the 2016 election.
But it's also a product of many voters' fatigue with identity politics - even voters on the progressive left. The debates, often pitting one demographic against another, have been exhausting.
One hears this in Canada, too. Trudeau and his Liberals are often criticized for spending too much time on these issues - the accusation that they're too "woke." If Liberals are taking some cues from Harris and the Democrats in the wake of Chicago, they might want to take a closer look at the caution with which their American progressives are handling identity politics in 2024.
Thanks to their change in leadership, Democrats do have a luxury that is not available to the Trudeau Liberals - an opportunity to rebrand the party on a more fundamental level. CNN commentator Van Jones, a veteran Democrat, spoke often of this last week while watching the convention.
Whether it was in reclaiming the word "freedom" from Republicans or recasting what it means to be a patriot, Jones said, Harris is staking out a new definition of progressive politics.
"They are asserting that the value of freedom is not just the freedom to not pay your taxes, or the freedom to, say, get off my lawn. It's the freedom to live a full life," Jones said.
Though Liberals can hardly set about redefining progressive politics on their own right now - especially in a party that Trudeau has already rebranded in his own image a decade ago - they should be paying close attention to where the Democrats are moving the markers on the left-right political spectrum. If nothing else, political trends that start in the U.S. often end up here.
In front of the cameras, Trudeau and his team took every opportunity to point out that it would be a mistake to do too much comparing between Liberals in Canada and Democrats in the U.S. Behind the scenes though, it wouldn't be a surprise if Trudeau's ministers were seeing if their playbook could take a few pages from Chicago.
CAPTION: Vice President Kamala Harris briefly takes to the stage at the Democratic National Convention on Aug. 19, 2024, in Chicago.

Subject: Political conventions; Progressivism; Identity politics; Voters; Political leadership; Political parties

Political leaders who mock nurses don't have my vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/political-leaders-who-mock-nurses-dont-have-my/docview/3097838858/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+leaders+who+mock+nurses+don%27t+have+my+vote&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Our political leaders aren't doctors or nurses
Pierre Poilievre's overheated insults. Canadians of all stripes being nasty to each other online. It's past time for all of us to heed Jack Layton, Aug. 26
I have just finished reading the insults hurled towards Doris Grinspun, the head of the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario (RNAO), after she dared to stand up and say the idea of eliminating safe injection sites is the wrong approach. Sadly, her words won't have impact on our premier, the premiers of other provinces or federal leaders who made this call or support it, without an education in medicine. Do I think that safe injection sites are a great idea? Not really. However, at this moment in time I do not see much in the way of viable solutions to replace them. If the people standing in the way of solutions are so naïve and blind to the present situation then we are doomed to continue to see no end in sight. To hurl insults and negative comments at people who are trying to find a solution is counterproductive. I shall remember such political leaders when election time comes around. They will not be getting my vote.
Catherine Hetu, Merlin, Ont.
Much like the Greenbelt flip-flop, perhaps Premier Doug Ford would be wise to listen to the "folks" and logically and humanely pivot to maintain harm reduction resources. This should not be a case of "If I want your opinion, I'll give it to you." We must prioritize saving lives.
James Gervais, Toronto
Tariffs never work
Trudeau government matches U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs and Beijing warns of 'retaliation,' Aug. 26
The Trudeau government's decision to impose a 100 per cent duty on Chinese-made electric vehicles (EVs) raises significant concerns, particularly for its impact on lower-income Canadians. Because this will significantly increase the cost of these vehicles, the policy disproportionately affects those who already struggle with the cost of living. Access to affordable EVs would alleviate some financial burdens by offering a low-cost transportation option with reduced fuel expenses. The policy also appears inconsistent and potentially discriminatory, given that similar duties are not being applied to EVs from other countries. This discrepancy undermines the principle of fair trade. The decision limits the availability of cost-effective and environmentally friendly transportation options for Canadian families who need them most.
Ronald Strauss, Waterloo
Ready to move on
After the Republicans leaned way into weird, the Democrats made a big bet on normal, Aug. 24
The Democratic Party planned, organized and fine-tuned their mammoth convention in record time, weeks after Kamala Harris and Tim Walz took over the reins of the Democrat ticket. Remarkably, most everything went off as planned. Presenting Harris and Walz as "candidates of difference and joy" was highly successful and they were acclaimed by the overjoyed audience as leaders who will deliver on promises made. Remarkably, there was full agreement/cohesion among the different factions of the party as never before! When Harris thundered "we're not going back," it was clear Americans are ready to move on. The entire world is longing for a new era of calm, amid success, in the U.S.
Maureen Fernandes, Mississauga
Reader right on the Blue Jays
I've been Blue Jays fan for decades. Now I need to take a break, Aug. 24
This letter to the editor clearly indicated, in a very pointed manner, why so many long time fans are distancing themselves from the Blue Jays. It's time for some major changes. They should start at the top with general manager Ross Atkins and president Mark Shapiro, who have long overstayed their welcome and have to move on. But sadly, chairman Ted Rogers is no George Steinbrenner, the former owner of the New York Yankees. These are bottom-line people and, just like they run their other businesses, they are prioritizing turning a profit, not maintaining the ballpark experience we are used to.
Marty Fruchtman, Toronto
Who are they playing for?
Danny Jansen makes MLB history, playing for the Blue Jays and Red Sox in the same game, Aug. 26
While catcher Danny Jansen may be the only Blue Jay to also play for the other team during the same game, it seems like many of his former teammates in Toronto have been doing that on a regular basis.
Gregory Hoggarth, Stratford, Ont.
More equal than others
Italian prosecutors open shipwreck and manslaughter investigation into superyacht sinking, Aug. 24
Once again, the media proves there is a hierarchy of human beings. A luxury yacht sank off the coast of Sicily in which seven drowned, including a British billionaire, and media across the world covered it extensively. Over the past many years, thousands of migrants in boats have drowned in the Mediterranean Sea and Atlantic Ocean trying to reach Europe, and little is reported. Why the difference? The former tragedy concerned white Europeans who were also prominent and wealthy; the latter, poor migrants, who were often non-white Africans. Some people are more equal than others.
Jacob Mendlovic, Toronto
Lack of support
Two years ago, we moved to a new condominium, a first for us. It quickly became apparent that, along with our neighbours, there were concerns regarding the board of directors. The concerns range from a 33 per cent increase in maintenance fees in less than two years, a lack of transparency, overspending their own budget by hundreds of thousands of dollars, to not holding annual general meetings on time. Only one person, who was properly elected, lives in the building and sees the daily issues. The Condominium Authority of Ontario sets the rules that govern boards, but they have not been responsive and tend to point people to legislation and will not meet even for an hour to help guide us. We pay for their service but their lack of support is abysmal.
David Marskell, Kitchener
CAPTION: Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario (RNAO), calls safe injection sites "essential" to patient care.

Ford government plans to boost electricity production to keep up with demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-plans-boost-electricity/docview/3097838705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+plans+to+boost+electricity+production+to+keep+up+with+demand&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is touting protections for farmland and the possibility of more carbon-free power as it seeks proposals for 5,000 megawatts of electricity generation - enough to power five million homes.
"The message we're sending today is renewables are very much part of this," Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said Wednesday in a switch from Premier Doug Ford's decision to cancel 758 green energy projects after taking office in 2018.
Lecce added the approach to the electricity procurement is "technology-agnostic" with officials ready to weigh proposals using "affordability" as the main determinant to avoid shocks on consumers' hydro bills.
"Ontario must produce more affordable energy to keep up" with a fast-growing population and electrification needs fuelled by electric vehicles and artificial intelligence and data services, he said at a farm in King City, north of Toronto.
Keith Brooks of Environmental Defence cautioned against over-optimism about more green energy from the Conservatives.
He noted Lecce's "agnostic" reference makes the procurement "open to natural gas and nukes" and other forms of electricity generation in addition to renewables like solar and wind power.
"We look forward to the opportunity to compete," Leonard Kula of the Canadian Renewable Energy Association told reporters at the announcement with Lecce.
While applauding the government's "U-turn" on renewables, Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the province has been increasingly relying on natural gas, which produces carbon emissions that contribute to climate change.
"Renewables are cheaper, cleaner and make far more economic sense than fossil gas," he added in a statement.
As recently as 2017, Ontario's grid was 96 per cent free of carbon emissions but that has fallen to 87 per cent with nuclear reactors offline for refurbishments. It remains one of the cleanest systems in the world and the emission-free level will rise once nuclear plants return to service.
Promised protections for agricultural lands come in the wake of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, now under investigation by the RCMP as Ford considers an early election next year.
The protections include a prohibition on energy projects in "specialty crop areas" such as the Holland Marsh, ensure no ground-mounted solar installations in prime agricultural areas, and require "agricultural impact assessments" for projects proposed for prime farmland.
This approach "strikes the right balance between protecting how we grow our food and how we generate our energy," said Agriculture, Food and Agribusiness Minister Rob Flack.
The procurement will be handled by Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator. Energy developers proposing projects will be required to get resolutions of support from local municipal councils to ensure new facilities are welcome.
"We are ready to move quickly to secure supply from all available technologies," Lesley Gallinger, chief executive of the IESO, said in a statement.
Shortly after taking over the energy portfolio in June, Lecce said the province can't afford an "ideological" push to wean its electricity system off fossil fuels despite criticism the province has been increasing its reliance on natural gas-fuelled plants to meet peak demand.
The procurement plan also offers incentives for projects in northern Ontario, with a plan to make Crown lands available for renewable energy. Incentives will be provided for projects by Indigenous communities on their traditional territories.
CAPTION: Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said there will be protections for prime agricultural lands while local councils must approve of proposed projects within their boundaries.

Subject: Energy; Electricity; Scandals

Record low for new build home sales + calls for an inquest into Neveah's death

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/record-low-new-build-home-sales-calls-inquest/docview/3097838514/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Record+low+for+new+build+home+sales+%2B+calls+for+an+inquest+into+Neveah%27s+death&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, August 28 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a record low for home sales, calls for an inquest into the death of a young girl and Justin Trudeau's political future.
DON'T MISSSales of new build homes are way down
Sales of new builds hit an all-time low record for July, as buyers remain unwilling to buy and move into new homes. The total inventory of homes has crept up in the meantime, with experts projecting more demand as interest rates go down. New construction projects, an industry expert said, likely won't pick up until inventory in the resale market drops. Clarrie Feinstein breaks down what it all means for your house hunt.
Driving it home: The 654 new home sales in July were a 48 per cent decrease from the same time last year and 70 per cent below the 10-year average.Through the roof: "The cost to build, driven by excessive government fees and taxes, is simply too high," one building industry expert said, warning that the housing shortage would reach "unprecedented levels over the next few years" without government action.Calls are increasing for an inquest into Neveah's death
The NDP critic for child welfare wants an inquest into the death of Neveah - a 4-year-old whose remains were found in a Rosedale dumpster two years ago. The Star's Jennifer Pagliaro and Wendy Gillis revealed over the weekend that Neveah spent the majority of her life in Children's Aid Society's care. She was an example of a child who "completely fell through the cracks," NDP MPP Monique Taylor said. Here's what an inquest would mean.
To recap: Investigators spent 14 months trying to identify Neveah, who was never reported missing, despite her ties to CAS. She was placed back with her birth mother in 2020 at the request of child services.Inquest request: Doug Ford's office did not respond to the Star's questions. His minister of social services said the decision for an inquest lies with the chief coroner.Behind closed doors, Justin Trudeau ponders the road ahead
Does Justin Trudeau have what it takes to stick around? Those conversations are happening but Trudeau is "going to have to do stuff that is really tough," former ambassador to the U.S. David MacNaughton told the Star. That means bold policy swings on big items like health care and getting proactive on economic growth. Trudeau's future - and that of his party - hangs in the balance, Tonda MacCharles writes.
State of play: Trudeau isn't being elbowed out by any of his rank and file, but some are lacking enthusiasm for the coming year.Whack-a-mole: There have been a whirlwind of announcements from Trudeau's government amidst his cabinet's retreat, including new EV tariffs, changes to the TFW program and new housing developments.WHAT ELSE
Canada's ambassador to the U.S. is preparing for a Trump or Harris presidency. Here's what she has found.U.S. authorities filed a new indictment in the Trump Jan. 6 case, keeping the charges intact but narrowing its allegations.More than 200 former Republican presidential staffers signed an open letter endorsing Kamala Harris.Supt. Stacy Clarke is expected to be sentenced today in the Toronto police exam-cheating scandal.York Region Police are reporting a 92 per cent increase in shootings and a 106 per cent jump in carjackings.Rick Leary discusses his time as TTC CEO, allegations of bullying, the SRT derailment and that huge funding hole.An Israeli hostage was rescued from a tunnel in Gaza after 326 days of captivity.The TTC is increasing its service next month. Here's what you need to know.A Canadian airline has introduced a new $1 base fare. Here's why and what travellers should know.The Maple Leafs have changed. Now Nick Robertson's best move might be the one he didn't expect.The aughts were the Golden Age of television, but the '90s laid the groundwork. Here are 10 shows that defined the decade.From "Gremlins 2" to "Blair Witch" - these 10 movies shaped the '90s.POV
Can Justin Trudeau's Liberals seize on the U.S. Democrats' success? Here's where they should start.
CLOSE-UP
RAMSDEN PARK: Who let the dogs out? Not the dog walkers, at least not the ones at Ramsden Park, located in the affluent neighbourhood of Rosedale. They have been caught off guard with a ban on commercial dog walkers after complaints of an "acoustical anomaly" in the area. Here's how the dog-walking war starts.

Document 5 of 500

Down, but not out?: With PM staying on, Liberals plan for a tough challenge ahead with voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/down-not-out/docview/3097746889/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Down%2C+but+not+out%3F%3A+With+PM+staying+on%2C+Liberals+plan+for+a+tough+challenge+ahead+with+voters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was one unusually candid moment as a parade of Liberal ministers went before cameras at a cabinet retreat in a soulless downtown conference centre.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller, a friend of the prime minister's, knew he'd be asked about the party's prospects under Justin Trudeau and whether the Liberals had learned any lessons from a summer of talking to cranky voters or from the fluid presidential race south of the border.
Miller's blunt answer: Sure. But it's not for public discussion.
"The reality is, we have to have these conversations behind closed doors. It's important for the health of the party" and cabinet "cohesion," he said.
But one of the prime minister's key former advisers, David MacNaughton, Trudeau's former ambassador to Washington who briefed cabinet Tuesday, says Trudeau faces a massive challenge to persuade a weary electorate.
MacNaughton told the Star it's up to the prime minister if he wants to stay or go, but if he stays "you're going to have to do stuff that is really tough" because all the polls show voters have an intense desire for change.
Facing a "change election" means at the least the Liberals have to make bold policy decisions, said MacNaughton, a former Ontario campaign co-chair for Trudeau.
MacNaughton means big things: like taking on supply management in the agriculture sector, or jumping on AI to push Canadian companies big and small to embrace it and become a global leader.
MacNaughton sees AI as an opportunity to address health-care issues, and productivity challenges. The Trudeau government, he said, is "reactive," addressing problems as they arise, but it is not proactive enough, looking ahead to determine what needs to be done to goose economic growth.
MacNaughton is not one of those Liberals who thinks there is a magic solution - like replacing Trudeau with former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney - that can fix what ails them.
If there is reflection still going on within Liberal cabinet ranks about Trudeau's leadership, it is rarely voiced aloud.
Several ministers who spoke to the Star said nobody at the cabinet table is actively elbowing him out. Many seem resigned to a year of hard work, if not all that enthusiastic.
Trudeau himself publicly dodged the question of his own takeaways from the momentum the Democrats south of the border are suddenly seeing.
Asked whether the Democrats' change of party leader made him think twice about his own decision to stay on, Trudeau - and some of his ministers - insisted the U.S. is a "very different country than Canada," has a "very different" political dynamic (without elaborating) and said Canadians don't worry about such questions, but about whether there are economic opportunities for them and their children.
Trudeau repeated what he's said on the rare occasion he's answered media questions since losing a key Toronto seat in a byelection in late June: he intends to lead the Liberal party into the next campaign.
And that's coming no time soon.
"We're still a year-plus away from the election," said Trudeau, and his focus is "on making sure we're delivering through that election the kinds of things that Canadians need."
Trudeau and his cabinet tout their economic record, saying over and over again they will "continue" to deliver things like dental care, child benefits and affordable daycare, and will put a "very clear choice" before Canadians, as they sharpened attack lines aimed at the Conservatives under leader Pierre Poilievre.
They're shifting direction on immigration, cutting temporary foreign workers and flagging there could be robust cuts to permanent resident numbers unveiled this fall. They are prioritizing housing shortages and affordability, but even economist Mike Moffatt, who's briefed them before, says they haven't been bold enough.
"They need to go much further, much faster," as Canada's housing problem "continues to get worse," in his view. In an interview, Moffatt said the country's housing issue has "seen some tinkering around the edges," but that his message to the Liberals is "we need some particularly bold action." He urged them to use the infrastructure program to force provinces to freeze and drastically cut development charges.
Publicly, minister after minister pledged confidence in Trudeau, albeit in remarks that felt scripted, if not defensive.
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said cabinet was busy "focusing on what Canadians need, and for the rest, the prime minister has my full support."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Canadians want a government that "takes action," offers "a clear vision" and an "optimistic message."
"Optimism is contagious," he said, adding although the economic picture is brightening, and inflation is coming down, there's more work to do.
Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland was astonishingly brief when asked if a leadership swap would revive Liberal party fortunes.
"Absolutely not," she said, stepping back. Pressed to explain why not, she stepped theatrically forward again. "I, all of us here, have tremendous confidence in the prime minister."
As if to underline how Trudeau might have (but didn't) similarly voice confidence in her performance when news of his wooing of Carney came to light in July, Freeland listed the ways she backs Trudeau: "We have confidence in him as leader of our government, as the prime minister of Canada, and we have confidence in him as our party's leader, as the guy who's going to lead us into the next election."
Calls for Trudeau to step down may emerge at a bigger caucus retreat in September. One minister said MPs who haven't made it into cabinet after nine years are expected to air grievances there.
Still, behind the scenes, several acknowledged Trudeau may be a good campaigner - as evidenced by weeks of gripping-and-grinning with Canadians at summer festivals and barbecues - and may want to stay on, but the Liberals have a deep hole to climb out of.
Public opinion polls give a massive advantage to the Conservatives.
Several ministers and senior staffers who spoke to the Star on a background-only basis said they have worked hard during the summer to push initiatives out the door to deliver those "results" Trudeau wants to see - an effort one minister said was easier in the pandemic when everyone was working "in the same direction."
And there are questions about just how committed other ministers are to that job.
Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez tried to punt speculation he was leaving the federal ranks to run for the Quebec Liberal Party leadership, saying he had nothing to announce immediately. Still, several of his colleagues privately wondered whether he's with them for the long haul.
Trudeau's team made a stream of announcements: tariffs on Chinese-made EVs, steel and aluminum imports; the release of federal properties for housing development; and cuts to the temporary foreign workers program. Then there are minor process-oriented steps that never seem to change anything: a working group on productivity, plans by two ministers to meet premiers to reduce interprovincial trade barriers, and efforts to reduce regulatory red tape to expedite Canada-U.S. trade flow.
On the last day, unlike previous retreats, Trudeau took no media questions.
It was left to House leader Karina Gould, fresh off a seven-month maternity leave, to sum up the Liberals' thought process heading into a key caucus retreat in September, and a pre-election House of Commons.
Liberals are grappling with that desire for change seen in the polls, and asking "is it change for change's sake, or is it because we need to be doing something differently?" she said. "What is the change that Canadians are looking for?"
She insisted it's not a change in progressive policies, but admitted, "there are things that need to be different."
"We've been in government for nine years. That's a long time, and we've done a lot in that period of time. But now we need to show Canadians what's next."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 6 of 500

Her body was found in a Rosedale dumpster. NDP critic calls for inquest into Neveah's death

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/her-body-was-found-rosedale-dumpster-ndp-critic/docview/3097922214/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Her+body+was+found+in+a+Rosedale+dumpster.+NDP+critic+calls+for+inquest+into+Neveah%27s+death&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The government critic for child welfare says an inquest must be called into the death of Neveah, whose remains were found in a Toronto dumpster, and that the death of every child who has been involved in care should automatically trigger that public scrutiny.
"It's unacceptable that nobody knew this child was missing," said Hamilton Mountain NDP MPP Monique Taylor, who has been the critic for children, community and social services since 2011.
"There's no 'I'm sorry's. There's no 'We made a mistake.' It's completely unacceptable and can never happen again."
As reported by the Star this weekend, 4-year-old Neveah spent the majority of her life in Children's Aid Society care, first in York Region, then in Toronto. She was taken into protective custody at birth after toxicology reports found marijuana in her system. In March 2021, a CAS request and subsequent court order placed her back with her birth mother, who had a lengthy history with the child protection system.
By the end of that year, police said Neveah was already dead, her tiny, decomposing body left the following spring wrapped in blankets inside a construction dumpster outside a home in Rosedale.
Investigators, who spent 14 months trying to identify the girl who was never reported missing, resorted to a resource-intensive technique involving genetic genealogy that combines advanced DNA technology with ancestry research on public genetic genealogy sites, similar to Ancestry.com. By June 2023, they had determined Neveah's name (The Star is not using Neveah's full name because a court publication ban prevents identifying members of her family).
"This is a prime example of how a child completely fell through the cracks and nobody could put together the pieces of who that child was because systems weren't talking to each other," Taylor told the Star in an interview Tuesday.
Premier Doug Ford's office did not respond to the Star's questions and instead forwarded the paper's requests to the Ministry for Children, Community and Social Services.
In a statement, Minister Michael Parsa's press secretary, Sean Forsyth, called Neveah's death "an absolute tragedy to family, friends, and everyone in our communities."
"While calling an inquest is an independent decision made by the Chief Coroner, we will fully support and co-operate with his office to help prevent future tragedies like this."
Dr. Dirk Huyer, Ontario's Chief Coroner, recently told the Star it was too early to call an inquest into Neveah's death due to ongoing police involvement.
"My understanding is the investigation is ongoing, and so it would be premature to make a decision about that," he said in an interview.
A coroner's inquest is a quasi-judicial hearing that doesn't assign blame but probes the circumstances of a death, determining how a death happened and producing recommendations aimed at preventing future fatalities.
The Toronto police investigation into Neveah's suspicious death remains open but no charges have been laid to date.
Taylor likened Neveah's death to that of 7-year-old Katelynn Sampson, who died in 2008 and whose legal guardians were convicted of second degree murder after various agencies lost track of her.
An inquest led to 173 recommendations, which advocates hoped would lead to an overhaul of the system that better protects the province's most vulnerable children.
"I really hoped that after that, that from her inquest recommendations, that we would have been able to make strides to make a better system," Taylor said. "And now that we see what happened with Neveah it's very clear that ... whatever they've done has not been enough to ensure childrens' safety."
The MPP also called into question the provincial government's review of the child welfare system that the ministry says has been ongoing since 2020, saying the only apparent changes are a lack of funding which would also make implementing inquest recommendations more difficult.
"The government needs to come clean about what their review actually looks like because nobody knows," Taylor said. She also criticized the institutional secrecy when a child dies.
"How would we have known about Neveah if it wasn't for these stories coming out? I had no idea. I've been the critic for all this time, I had no idea. That's not OK."
After she was born, Neveah was placed in a foster home for nearly three years. She was eventually joined by a younger brother. She was non-verbal and was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder, but her complex needs were being met by her foster parents, who took her to specialist appointments and speech therapy and provided educational toys for children with autism.
In 2020, when Neveah was nearly three, she and her brother - who was also diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder - were removed from the foster family and placed under a supervision order that would return her to the custody of her mother while being monitored by York Region CAS.
All five of Neveah's siblings were also involved with the child protection system at various points in their lives due to ongoing concerns about neglect, unsanitary conditions, and the mother's substance use.
In March 2021, at the request of CAS Toronto, a judge terminated the supervision order altogether - meaning Neveah and her brother would be returned to the sole custody of their mother, with no supervision by any children's aid society.
Neveah's remains were found in a dumpster on Dale Avenue on May 2, 2022. Though it's not known how or exactly when she died, forensic testing on her remains determined she died that same year, after she was returned to her mother's care.
Critics say the decision to end the supervision order and close the CAS Toronto file must be reviewed now, knowing the tragic outcome.
"I can't fathom how a judge would release a child like Neveah, with the complex special needs that she had, back to a single mom who has her own battles around addictions...let alone poverty and other children," Irwin Elman, who served as the province's Child Advocate from 2008 to 2019, recently told the Star.
CAPTION: In November 2023,Neveah was given a municipal burial at Thornton Cemetery in Oshawa in a quiet plot courtesy of the cemetery.

Document 7 of 500

A menacing stranger harassed an MP at a lake. Onlookers stood up for democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/menacing-stranger-harassed-mp-at-lake-onlookers/docview/3097482867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+menacing+stranger+harassed+an+MP+at+a+lake.+Onlookers+stood+up+for+democracy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be a peaceful summer evening on Bass Lake in Northern Ontario.
Charlie Angus, NDP MP for Timmins-James Bay, was on its shores and about to go swimming at his "sacred space" when he was confronted by an ill-boding figure.
In a Facebook post written later, Angus wrote that, in this setting, there appeared to be no witnesses, no cameras, making him a clear and vulnerable target. Hurling insults and radiating hate, the angry figure sat on a picnic bench as Angus proceeded with his evening swim. When he finished his dip and retrieved his towel, the stranger continued his taunting invective as Angus headed toward his car.
The verbal assailant, however, may not have realized there were witnesses to the verbal assault and they came forward afterward. It turned out the menacing man was from Alberta.
In a recent phone call, I spoke with Angus about the incident and the alarming crescendo of threatening, bile-filled encounters he and other Canadian politicians have experienced in recent years.
Angus noted that since the so-called "Freedom Convoy" that besieged Ottawa in 2022, email and phone-message death threats against him have skyrocketed.
He recounted that when he publicly asked Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to distance himself from the endorsement of infamous, far-right conspiracy broadcaster Alex Jones, hours after photos of Angus's daughter appeared online detailing where she worked. (According to Angus, Poilievre has yet to disavow Jones's endorsement.)
And Angus is not alone in experiencing a deluge of right-wing execration. Such "politics of intimidation," as he calls it, has befallen many Canadian politicians, from city councillors in Pickering, Ont., as reported in the Star, to a plethora of parliamentarians, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
This heightened harassment is a strategy from the U.S. right-wing playbook, Angus claims, now imported and employed by Canadian "conservatives." It's "toxic entitlement," Angus continues, rooted in rage, which he likens to a fire that needs constant fuelling.
It is MAGA-creep north and its flames have seared the U.S. landscape.
As Time magazine recently reported, there were over 9,600 recorded threats against members of Congress last year, a nearly tenfold increase from 2016.
And the flames of enmity are spreading far beyond the homes and offices of politicians.
Threats against U.S. federal judges have jumped 400 per cent since 2018. And for those in the medical profession, the statistics are staggering. In a Johns Hopkins University survey of 583 local health departments, 57 per cent reported staff had been targeted with "personal threats, doxing, vandalism, and other forms of harassment" during the pandemic.
Such mass harassment has become so widespread that the U.S. Justice Department has initiated task forces to combat the intimidation of education workers and election administrators.
To date, Time reports, over 100 of the latter have "met the threshold for a federal criminal investigation."
The mainstreaming of rage, from broadcast booths to political party platforms, is a full-blown assault on democracy, fuelled by hate.
It is no surprise that the Nazi movement instigated public book burnings in 1933 in a nationwide "cleansing" billed as an "action against the un-German spirit," not to mention lighting the mega-bonfire of the Reichstag the same year, leaving the remaining supports of German democracy in a smouldering pile.
Clearly, these alt-right bonfires are a form of political arson, designed to extinguish democracy.
How do we build firewalls against these odious flames?
Tellingly, Angus' story may point to an answer. Due to the remote location, perhaps the man who hurled abuse toward Angus thought they were alone, and that he could make Angus feel isolated and afraid. This is perhaps true of all the merchants of hate - they want us to feel that we are alone in a world of selfishness and fear, and that the democratic values of compassion and respect are scorched beyond recovery.
But in showing up for democratic values, like the witnesses for Angus coming forward to call out hatred and intimidation, in standing up to political bullying, we can indeed create a firewall against the flames of hatred - against the "bonfires of the tyrannies."
CAPTION: NDP MP Charlie Angus attends a news conference in Ottawa on Oct. 17, 2023.
CREDIT: Stephen Bede Scharper, Contributor

Subject: Democracy; Politics; Politicians

Location: United States--US

People: Angus, Charlie

Document 8 of 500

Behind closed doors, Justin Trudeau ponders the road ahead - as one of his former advisers says the PM faces a massive challenge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/behind-closed-doors-justin-trudeau-ponders-road/docview/3097482766/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+closed+doors%2C+Justin+Trudeau+ponders+the+road+ahead+-+as+one+of+his+former+advisers+says+the+PM+faces+a+massive+challenge&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-There was one unusually candid moment as a parade of Liberal ministers went before cameras at a cabinet retreat in a soulless downtown conference centre.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller, a friend of the prime minister's, knew he'd be asked about the party's prospects under Justin Trudeau and whether the Liberals had learned any lessons from a summer of talking to cranky voters or from the fluid presidential race south of the border.
Miller's blunt answer: Sure. But it's not for public discussion.
"The reality is, we have to have these conversations behind closed doors. It's important for the health of the party" and cabinet "cohesion," he said.
But one of the prime minister's key former advisers, David MacNaughton, Trudeau's former ambassador to Washington who briefed cabinet Tuesday, says Trudeau faces a massive challenge to persuade a weary electorate.
MacNaughton told the Star it's up to the prime minister if he wants to stay or go, but if he stays "you're going to have to do stuff that is really tough" because all the polls show voters have an intense desire for change.
Facing a "change election" means at the least the Liberals have to make bold policy decisions, said MacNaughton, a former Ontario campaign co-chair for Trudeau. He means big things: like taking on supply management in the agriculture sector, or jumping on AI to push Canadian companies big and small to embrace it and become a global leader. MacNaughton sees AI as an opportunity to address health-care issues, and productivity challenges. The Trudeau government, he said, is "reactive," addressing problems as they arise, but it is not proactive enough, looking ahead to determine what needs to be done to goose economic growth.
MacNaughton is not one of those Liberals who thinks there is a magic solution - like replacing Trudeau with former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney - that can fix what ails them.
If there is reflection still going on within Liberal cabinet ranks about Trudeau's leadership, it is rarely voiced aloud.
Several ministers who spoke to the Star said nobody at the cabinet table is actively elbowing him out. Many seem resigned to a year of hard work, if not all that enthusiastic.
Trudeau himself publicly dodged the question of his own takeaways from the momentum the Democrats south of the border are suddenly seeing.
Asked whether the Democrats' change of party leader made him think twice about his own decision to stay on, Trudeau - and some of his ministers - insisted the U.S. is a "very different country than Canada," has a "very different" political dynamic (without elaborating) and said Canadians don't worry about such questions, but about whether there are economic opportunities for them and their children.
Trudeau repeated what he's said on the rare occasion he's answered media questions since losing a key Toronto seat in a byelection in late June: he intends to lead the Liberal party into the next campaign.
And that's coming no time soon.
"We're still a year-plus away from the election," said Trudeau, and his focus is "on making sure we're delivering through that election the kinds of things that Canadians need."
Trudeau and his cabinet tout their economic record, saying over and over again they will "continue" to deliver things like dental care, child benefits and affordable daycare, and will put a "very clear choice" before Canadians, as they sharpened attack lines aimed at the Conservatives under leader Pierre Poilievre.
They're shifting direction on immigration, cutting temporary foreign workers and flagging there could be robust cuts to permanent resident numbers unveiled this fall. They are prioritizing housing shortages and affordability, but even economist Mike Moffatt, who's briefed them before, says they haven't been bold enough.
"They need to go much further, much faster," as Canada's housing problem "continues to get worse," in his view. In an interview, Moffatt said the country's housing issue has "seen some tinkering around the edges," but that his message to the Liberals is "we need some particularly bold action." He urged them to use the infrastructure program to force provinces to freeze and drastically cut development charges.
Publicly, minister after minister pledged confidence in Trudeau, albeit in remarks that felt scripted, if not defensive.
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said cabinet was busy "focusing on what Canadians need, and for the rest, the prime minister has my full support."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Canadians want a government that "takes action," offers "a clear vision" and an "optimistic message."
"Optimism is contagious," he said, adding although the economic picture is brightening, and inflation is coming down, there's more work to do.
Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland was astonishingly brief when asked if a leadership swap would revive Liberal party fortunes.
"Absolutely not," she said, stepping back. Pressed to explain why not, she stepped theatrically forward again. "I, all of us here, have tremendous confidence in the prime minister."
As if to underline how Trudeau might have (but didn't) similarly voice confidence in her performance when news of his wooing of Carney came to light in July, Freeland listed the ways she backs Trudeau: "We have confidence in him as leader of our government, as the prime minister of Canada, and we have confidence in him as our party's leader, as the guy who's going to lead us into the next election."
Calls for Trudeau to step down may emerge at a bigger caucus retreat in September. One minister said MPs who haven't made it into cabinet after nine years are expected to air grievances there.
Still, behind the scenes, several acknowledged Trudeau may be a good campaigner - as evidenced by weeks of gripping-and-grinning with Canadians at summer festivals and barbecues - and may want to stay on, but the Liberals have a deep hole to climb out of.
Public opinion polls give a massive advantage to the Conservatives.
Several ministers and senior staffers who spoke to the Star on a background-only basis said they have worked hard during the summer to push initiatives out the door to deliver those "results" Trudeau wants to see - an effort one minister said was easier in the pandemic when everyone was working "in the same direction."
And there are questions about just how committed other ministers are to that job.
Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez tried to punt speculation he was leaving the federal ranks to run for the Quebec Liberal Party leadership, saying he had nothing to announce immediately. Still, several of his colleagues privately wondered whether he's with them for the long haul.
Trudeau's team made a stream of announcements: tariffs on Chinese-made EVs, steel and aluminum imports; the release of federal properties for housing development; and cuts to the temporary foreign workers program. Then there are minor process-oriented steps that never seem to change anything: a working group on productivity, plans by two ministers to meet premiers to reduce interprovincial trade barriers, and efforts to reduce regulatory red tape to expedite Canada-U.S. trade flow.
On the last day, unlike previous retreats, Trudeau took no media questions.
It was left to House leader Karina Gould, fresh off a seven-month mat leave, to sum up the Liberals' thought process heading into a key caucus retreat in September, and a pre-election House of Commons.
Liberals are grappling with that desire for change seen in the polls, and asking "is it change for change's sake, or is it because we need to be doing something differently?" she said. "What is the change that Canadians are looking for?"
She insisted it's not a change in progressive policies, but admitted, "there are things that need to be different."
"We've been in government for nine years. That's a long time, and we've done a lot in that period of time. But now we need to show Canadians what's next."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and members of his cabinet arrive at the retreat in Halifax on Aug. 26, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Election results; State elections; Voters; Prime ministers; Immigration

Document 9 of 500

I've changed my mind on supervised consumption sites. The Ford government should too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ive-changed-my-mind-on-supervised-consumption/docview/3097482682/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I%27ve+changed+my+mind+on+supervised+consumption+sites.+The+Ford+government+should+too&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rethinking safe injection sites
Here's what the Ford government doesn't get about addiction, Aug. 26
Last week, I wrote a letter to the editor suggesting that the personal concerns of the neighbours of safe injection sites needed to be recognized and taken into account (if not prioritized) in the debate over the location of their operation. My letter, thankfully, was not published, as I've since had a change of heart. I've read and listened to the opinions of the experts and the heart-wrenching stories of people whose very lives depend on the services that those sites provide, and I've had my eyes opened. I strongly suggest that Premier Doug Ford do the same and find the courage to admit that he's wrong.
Michael Bines, Toronto
A statement from the minister of health's office said "open, taxpayer-funded drug consumption should not be happening on the same block as schools and daycares. [This] action will help protect the public, especially for our most vulnerable: Ontario's children." This is the same government that is spending at least $225 million of our tax dollars to enable access to alcohol in convenience stores and gas stations, expanding availability by nearly 300 per cent. These stores can be as close to schools and daycares as they like. Perhaps the Ford government is unaware of the fact that over 6,000 people die each year in Ontario as a result of substance use issues related to alcohol, far exceeding the number of people who died from opioids and dwarfed only by Ontario's leading deadly drug, tobacco. According to the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, alcohol is by far the most costly drug in terms of health care, lost productivity, criminal justice and other direct costs, costing Ontario over $16 billion in 2020 alone. Expanding the availability of alcohol and the closing of supervised injection sites will cost lives. This is the legacy of a Ford government, and for me will be the ballot box issue in 2025.
Isabella Tatar, Toronto
Will Metrolinx do the honourable thing?
Her husband's dying wish was that she stay forever in the house they built. Now she's being pushed out by the Ontario Line, Aug. 22
After first assuring the Pape Avenue property owners that their properties were not up for expropriation and then telling them that they have to vacate by November 2025, I hope Metrolinx will do the honourable thing and ensure that these owners will get the best possible price for their properties and assist however needed as they move. Metrolinx must not wait until well into 2025 to close the deals and leave owners scrambling.
Jerry Michaels, Toronto
This situation underscores one of the fundamental problems at Metrolinx. It appears that not enough pre-engineering investigative work is being placed into these multimillion-dollar transit projects before preferred routes are selected. The inappropriate soil conditions for tunnelling should have been identified long before now. In my experienced observation as a civil engineer, it should have been done at the environmental assessment and pre-engineering stages. Is someone asleep at the switch at Metrolinx?
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville, Ont.
Highway 407 suggestions are pointless
For months now, there have been endless opinions as to how to better utilize Highway 407, with suggestions ranging from reducing tolls to allowing trucks to use it for free. People seem to forget, this highway is not owned by the province. It was leased to a private enterprise to operate. Unless the 99 year lease (only 74 years left) is somehow voided, we can't do a thing. Though many suggestions may have merit, it's a complete waste of time. If anyone has ideas on how we can get this highway back into public hands, by all means step forward.
Brad Ing, Markham
Get mitigation moving
Toronto's had a massive stormwater management project underway for years. Here's what these new tunnels will do during downpours, Aug. 11
I have been trying for over six years to get the city to get serious about sewer infrastructure. I live near Trinity-Bellwoods, where all the water that gets flushed and swept into the combined sewers from north of us goes past the basements of our homes. Greater density is a good thing, but expecting century-old sewage systems to handle ever increasing volumes of storm and wastewater is doomed to failure. At some point insurers will no longer provide flood protection or, if they do, it will have a prohibitive cost. Will the city help us then? Come on Toronto, you know the worst is coming. Let's get mitigation moving.
Bruce Van Dieten, Toronto
The Star reported about Toronto's massive, $3.7-billion stormwater management project, with a target storage capacity of 650,000 cubic metres when completed in about 15 years. For some perspective, the Pearson airport weather station reported 128.3 mm of rain over 12 hours on Aug. 17. That much rainfall across the 47 square kilometres of Ward 1 (Etobicoke North, close to the airport) would total 6,016,000 cubic metres, over nine times Toronto's planned stormwater management capacity. Similar rainfall across all of Toronto would be 124 times the planned capacity. Clearly, the present approach to stormwater management has little chance of preventing major flooding and avoiding the discharge of most stormwater directly into creeks, rivers and Lake Ontario. Toronto needs a serious rethink, perhaps following the model of Copenhagen (and over 60 other cities), which is transforming itself into a sponge city.
Martin Green, Toronto
Stop eating meat
Anyone interested in treating animals more humanely need only do one simple thing: stop eating meat. I did it 48 years ago, and haven't felt deprived for a second. With all of the healthy, delicious food options that are now available in our wonderfully multicultural city, it will be even easier for you than it was for me.
Scott Kennedy, Toronto
CAPTION: Staff and volunteers at the Moss Park safe consumption site assist hundred of drug users a month with a safe place to hang out, find resources, get a snack, have their drugs analyzed and have supervision and clean apparatus.

Here's the latest on lax vetting in the temporary foreign worker program, an ongoing manhunt in Etobicoke and a refugee claimant's blocked deportation.
DON'T MISSOfficers were told to skip steps when vetting temporary foreign worker applications
The federal government has been fast-tracking temporary foreign worker applications, directing processing officers to skip crucial steps meant to prevent fraud, the Star has learned. That included a pause on routine checks meant to ensure the system - which has been heavily criticized in recent months - is not abused by unscrupulous employers. Ghada Alsharif and Kenyon Wallace have the full story on "streamlining" in the TFW program.
ICYMI: The government announced a reduction to the number of low-wage temporary foreign workers yesterday.Wait, what? Canada's TFW program has faced scrutiny for exploiting migrant workers. A UN official called it "a breeding ground for contemporary forms of slavery."The manhunt continues for an Etobicoke double murder suspect
Police have expanded their search for Joseph Ayala, 33, who is wanted for second-degree murder in the deaths of two family members found in their Etobicoke home on Friday evening. A source close to the family said Ayala had been involved with the police in the past when he had refused to take medication for his schizophrenia. Here's an update as the manhunt enters its fifth day.
The victims: Sources said the two victims were Ayala's grandmother, 82-year-old Colleen Fraser, and his 60-year-old mother Sherry Fraser.Look out for: A five-foot-eleven man with a shaved head, known to wear a cowboy hat, black cowboy boots and a jacket with tassels on the sleeves. Ayala is considered dangerous and shouldn't be approached, police said.A judge blocked a refugee claimant's deportation over 'misleading' evidence
The government submitted "misleading" evidence in trying to deport a Nigerian refugee claimant, a judge said in a recent decision to block their deportation. Abideen Olalekan Oladipupo has struggled with depression and PTSD, and attempted suicide twice while in immigration detention - but border services still deemed him fit to fly. In a strongly-worded decision, a judge determined otherwise, Nicholas Keung reports.
From the judge: "This conduct borders on - and very closely borders on - misleading the Court," Justice Shirzad S. Ahmed said of the Canada Border Services Agency.Go deeper: Oladipupo sought asylum in Canada in 2016, claiming he faced persecution in Nigeria due to his political beliefs. His claim was denied in 2018 and subsequent appeals failed.WHAT ELSE

Full text: Reconsidering opinion on safe injection sites
Here's what the Ford government doesn't get about addiction, Aug. 26
Last week, I wrote a letter to the editor suggesting that the personal concerns of the neighbours of safe injection sites needed to be recognized and taken into account (if not prioritized) in the debate over the location of their operation. My letter, thankfully, was not published, as I've since had a change of heart. I've read and listened to the opinions of the experts and the heart-wrenching stories of people whose very lives depend on the services that those sites provide, and I've had my eyes opened. I strongly suggest that Premier Doug Ford do the same and find the courage to admit that he's wrong.
Michael Bines, Toronto
A statement from the minister of health's office said "open, taxpayer-funded drug consumption should not be happening on the same block as schools and daycares. [This] action will help protect the public, especially for our most vulnerable: Ontario's children." This is the same government that is spending at least $225 million of our tax dollars to enable access to alcohol in convenience stores and gas stations, expanding availability by nearly 300 per cent. These stores can be as close to schools and daycares as they like. Perhaps the Ford government is unaware of the fact that more than 6,000 people die each year in Ontario as a result of substance use issues related to alcohol, far exceeding the number of people who died from opioids and dwarfed only by Ontario's leading deadly drug, tobacco. According to the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, alcohol is by far the most costly drug in terms of health care, lost productivity, criminal justice and other direct costs, costing Ontario more than $16 billion in 2020 alone. Expanding the availability of alcohol and the closing of supervised injection sites will cost lives. This is the legacy of a Ford government, and for me will be the ballot box issue in 2025.
Isabella Tatar, Toronto
Will Metrolinx do honourable thing for owners?
Her husband's dying wish was that she stay forever in the house they built. Now she's being pushed out by the Ontario Line, Aug. 22
After first assuring the Pape Avenue property owners that their properties were not up for expropriation and then telling them that they have to vacate by November 2025, I hope Metrolinx will do the honourable thing and ensure that these owners will get the best possible price for their properties and assist however needed as they move.
Metrolinx must not wait until well into 2025 to close the deals and leave owners scrambling.
Jerry Michaels, Toronto
This situation underscores one of the fundamental problems at Metrolinx. It appears that not enough pre-engineering investigative work is being placed into these multimillion-dollar transit projects before preferred routes are selected. The inappropriate soil conditions for tunnelling should have been identified long before now.
In my experienced observation as a civil engineer, it should have been done at the environmental assessment and pre-engineering stages.
Is someone asleep at the switch at Metrolinx?
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville, Ont.
Highway 407 suggestions
are pointless
For months now, there have been endless opinions as to how to better utilize Highway 407, with suggestions ranging from reducing tolls to allowing trucks to use it for free.
People seem to forget, this highway is not owned by the province. It was leased to a private enterprise to operate. Unless the 99-year lease (only 74 years left) is somehow voided, we can't do a thing. Though many suggestions may have merit, it's a complete waste of time.
If anyone has ideas on how we can get this highway back into public hands, by all means step forward.
Brad Ing, Markham
Get mitigation moving already
Toronto's had a massive stormwater management project underway for years. Here's what these new tunnels will do during downpours, Aug. 11
I have been trying for more than six years to get the city to get serious about sewer infrastructure. I live near Trinity-Bellwoods, where all the water that gets flushed and swept into the combined sewers from north of us goes past the basements of our homes. Greater density is a good thing, but expecting century-old sewage systems to handle ever increasing volumes of storm and wastewater is doomed to failure. At some point insurers will no longer provide flood protection or, if they do, it will have a prohibitive cost.
Will the city help us then? Come on Toronto, you know the worst is coming. Let's get mitigation moving.
Bruce Van Dieten, Toronto
The Star reported about Toronto's massive, $3.7-billion stormwater management project, with a target storage capacity of 650,000 cubic metres when completed in about 15 years. For some perspective, the Pearson airport weather station reported 128.3 mm of rain over 12 hours on Aug. 17. That much rainfall across the 47 square kilometres of Ward 1 (Etobicoke North, close to the airport) would total 6,016,000 cubic metres, more than nine times Toronto's planned stormwater management capacity. Similar rainfall across all of Toronto would be 124 times the planned capacity. Clearly, the present approach to stormwater management has little chance of preventing major flooding and avoiding the discharge of most stormwater directly into creeks, rivers and Lake Ontario.
Toronto needs a serious rethink, perhaps following the model of Copenhagen (and more than 60 other cities), which is transforming itself into a sponge city.
Martin Green, Toronto
Stop eating meat
Anyone interested in treating animals more humanely need only do one simple thing: stop eating meat. I did it 48 years ago, and haven't felt deprived for a second.
With all of the healthy, delicious food options that are now available in our wonderfully multicultural city, it will be even easier for you than it was for me.
Scott Kennedy, Toronto

Document 12 of 500

Liberals and NDP eye PC stronghold

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-ndp-eye-pc-stronghold/docview/3097380757/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+and+NDP+eye+PC+stronghold&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's been a Progressive Conservative stronghold since 2011 but rival parties are hoping to end that in the Sept. 19 Bay of Quinte byelection prompted by the surprise resignation of PC cabinet minister Todd Smith.
In a race of local Belleville-area politicians hoping to make the leap to Queen's Park, the NDP is running school board trustee Amanda Robertson and the Liberals have tapped city councillor Sean Kelly - a radio morning show host.
They are looking to wrest votes from the Conservatives and their candidate Tyler Allsopp, also a Belleville city councillor, in the riding where Smith took 49 per cent of the votes for Premier Doug Ford's PCs in the 2022 election.
That was more than double the second-place New Democrat challenger at 20.9 per cent, 18.5 per cent for the Liberals and 6.3 per cent for the Greens, who do not yet have a candidate.
Despite Ford's commanding lead in provincewide public opinion polls, the opposition parties say the lack of progress improving access to doctors, long hospital emergency room waits and affordable housing will hurt the Tories as the RCMP investigates the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"People in Bay of Quinte deserve health care they can rely on, homes they can afford and schools that set kids up for success," said Robertson, who is also vice-chair of the Hastings and Prince Edward and District School Board.
In London on Monday, Ford said he is confident there will be an "explosion" in housing starts as interest rates fall.
"I've witnessed firsthand how Doug Ford's cuts are having a devastating impact on our community," said Kelly, in his second term on council in a city where officials declared a state of emergency last February after 13 drug overdose calls to 911 during a one-hour period.
Allsopp, a small businessman, said he is campaigning on keeping costs down for families and businesses as the PC government works to widen Highway 401 and build a new Prince Edward County Memorial Hospital.
"I understand the struggles many families like mine face," he said.
Smith had been education minister for 10 weeks, following a three-year stint as minister of energy and time in other cabinet portfolios, before he unexpectedly quit as MPP 10 days ago to take an unspecified job in the private sector.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Emergency medical care; Drug overdose; Housing starts

Document 13 of 500

Our democracy plagued by rage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/our-democracy-plagued-rage/docview/3097380542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+democracy+plagued+by+rage&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be a peaceful summer evening on Bass Lake in northern Ontario.
Charlie Angus, NDP MP for Timmins-James Bay, was on its shores and about to go swimming at his "sacred space" when he was confronted by an ill-boding figure.
In a Facebook post written later, Angus wrote that, in this setting, there appeared to be no witnesses, no cameras, making him a clear and vulnerable target. Hurling insults and radiating hate, the angry figure sat on a picnic bench as Angus proceeded with his evening swim. When he finished his dip and retrieved his towel, the stranger continued his taunting invective as Angus headed toward his car.
The verbal assailant, however, may not have realized there were witnesses to the verbal assault and they came forward afterward. It turned out the menacing man was from Alberta.
In a recent phone call, I spoke with Angus about the incident and the alarming crescendo of threatening, bile-filled encounters he and other Canadian politicians have experienced in recent years.
Angus noted that since the so-called "Freedom Convoy" that besieged Ottawa in 2022, email and phone-message death threats against him have skyrocketed.
He recounted that when he publicly asked Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to distance himself from the endorsement of infamous, far-right conspiracy broadcaster Alex Jones, hours after photos of Angus's daughter appeared online detailing where she worked. (According to Angus, Poilievre has yet to disavow Jones's endorsement.)
And Angus is not alone in experiencing a deluge of right-wing execration. Such "politics of intimidation," as he calls it, has befallen many Canadian politicians, from city councillors in Pickering, as reported in the Star, to a plethora of parliamentarians, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
This heightened harassment is a strategy from the U.S. right-wing playbook, Angus claims, now imported and employed by Canadian "conservatives." It's "toxic entitlement," Angus continues, rooted in rage, which he likens to a fire that needs constant fuelling.
It is MAGA-creep north and its flames have seared the U.S. landscape.
As Time magazine recently reported, there were over 9,600 recorded threats against members of Congress last year, a nearly tenfold increase from 2016.
And the flames of enmity are spreading far beyond the homes and offices of politicians.
Threats against U.S. federal judges have jumped 400 per cent since 2018. And for those in the medical profession, the statistics are staggering. In a Johns Hopkins University survey of 583 local health departments, 57 per cent reported staff had been targeted with "personal threats, doxing, vandalism, and other forms of harassment" during the pandemic.
Such mass harassment has become so widespread that the U.S. Justice Department has initiated task forces to combat the intimidation of education workers and election administrators.
To date, Time reports, more than 100 of the latter have "met the threshold for a federal criminal investigation."
The mainstreaming of rage, from broadcast booths to political party platforms, is a full-blown assault on democracy, fuelled by hate.
It is no surprise that the Nazi movement instigated public book burnings in 1933 in a nationwide "cleansing" billed as an "action against the un-German spirit," not to mention lighting the mega-bonfire of the Reichstag the same year, leaving the remaining supports of German democracy in a smouldering pile.
Clearly, these alt-right bonfires are a form of political arson, designed to extinguish democracy.
How do we build firewalls against these odious flames?
Tellingly, Angus's story may point to an answer. Due to the remote location, perhaps the man who hurled abuse toward Angus thought they were alone, and that he could make Angus feel isolated and afraid. This is perhaps true of all the merchants of hate - they want us to feel that we are alone in a world of selfishness and fear, and that the democratic values of compassion and respect are scorched beyond recovery.
But in showing up for democratic values, like the witnesses for Angus coming forward to call out hatred and intimidation, in standing up to political bullying, we can indeed create a firewall against the flames of hatred - against the "bonfires of the tyrannies."
Stephen Bede Scharper is associate professor of anthropology at the University of Toronto Mississauga.
CAPTION: NDP MP Charlie Angus noted that since the so-called "Freedom Convoy" in 2022, death threats against him have skyrocketed.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press File Photo
CREDIT: Stephen Bede Scharper Contributor Stephen Bede Scharper is associate professor of anthropology at the University of Toronto Mississauga.;

Subject: Democracy; Politics; Politicians; Canadians; Hostility; Political polarization; Harassment

Location: United States--US

People: Angus, Charlie

Company / organization: Name: New Democratic Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940
