Document 34 of 500

Why on earth is Doug Ford fighting against undercover videos in a troubled industry?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-on-earth-is-doug-ford-fighting-against/docview/3095626002/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+on+earth+is+Doug+Ford+fighting+against+undercover+videos+in+a+troubled+industry%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, images of the horrific suffering of animals trapped in our factory farming system were making their way onto our TV screens.The secretly-filmed videos were having a powerful and disturbing effect on a public that had simply assumed governments were regulating and monitoring agribusiness to assure basic animal welfare.One such video, shown on CTV's W5 in November 2020, captured footage of a very ill pregnant pig being roughly treated as she's sliced open with a knife and her unborn piglets are ripped out and thrown onto a filthy concrete floor. The viewers then see piglets squealing in pain as, wide awake, they're castrated and have their tails cut off.Inside the executive offices of agribusiness and the Doug Ford government, there was only one way to deal with the growing public anger: these videos must be stopped!So, the Ford government brought in a law, known as an agriculture gag law (or ag-gag) making it illegal, punishable with fines up to $25,000, to get hired at a factory farm or slaughterhouses while concealing an intention to make an undercover video documenting animal abuse.Interestingly, the farm featured in the pig video shown on W5 pleaded guilty to charges of animal cruelty and was fined $20,000 - a prosecution that would never have happened without the video evidence, which led the farm to plead guilty.Despite this conviction, demonstrating the value of undercover videos, the government has persisted with its ag-gag law, which has accomplished exactly what the industry wanted: an end to exposés of the miserable lives of animals behind farm factory walls.Not one such video has been made since the law came into effect almost four years ago, says Camille Labchuk, of the group Animal Justice, which shot the pig video.However, last April, Animal Justice won an important victory when the Ontario Superior Court struck down key sections of the ag-gag law. Justice Markus Koehnen ruled that parts of the law violated the Charter-protected right to free expression of activists, journalists and whistleblowers.But the Ford government is appealing the ruling, so the battle continues.The determination of government and industry to keep the curtain drawn on corporate farming practices suggests that there's a lot they want to remain hidden.For instance, they don't want us to know that baby calves, snatched from their mothers at birth, spend the entire four or five months of their lives confined to narrow crates that prevent them from even turning around. The crates are designed to stunt their muscle growth so that their flesh is tender, when it's served as veal.Such heartless practices are not surprising, considering that the powerful, multibillion-dollar corporate farming industry has been allowed to draw up its own codes of conduct and been mostly left to regulate itself behind factory walls.The replacement of the family farm with industrial farming in recent decades has meant a dramatic worsening of living conditions for millions of animals - who, while different from humans, suffer as we do.As renowned anthropologist Jane Goodall put it: "Farm animals feel pleasure and sadness, excitement and resentment, depression, fear and pain. They are far more aware and intelligent than we ever imagined."What range of emotions must they feel spending their lives as captives inside our wretched factory farming system?We can get a hint of how much they suffer by watching videos of the lucky few who end up in animal rescue sanctuaries. The joy of these suddenly-released animals as they frolic outdoors for the first time is not something easily forgotten.Certainly, we live today with a strange disconnect between the world of dogs and cats, who are increasingly loved, spoiled, indulged and treated as part of the family, and the hidden world of suffering farm animals, who typically experience the outdoors for the first time as they're loaded onto trucks bound for the slaughterhouse.
CAPTION: Animal rights activists protest Bill 156 outside of Queens Park on Sept. 14, 2020.

Subject: Activism; Farming; Animals; Video recordings; Criminal pleas; Factory farming; Agribusiness

Business indexing term: Subject: Agribusiness

Title: Why on earth is Doug Ford fighting against undercover videos in a troubled industry?

Doug Ford should know more about supporting those with addiction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-should-know-more-about-supporting-those/docview/3095625670/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+should+know+more+about+supporting+those+with+addiction&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing sites is a death sentence
Doug Ford to ban safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres, Aug. 20
I'm sure many people will be thinking what a great plan this is to help drug addicted people into a treatment centre, except we have not been told how that would work. Where are these proposed centres located and how would you staff them? Our hospitals and other medical centres are already stretched to the limit. And what about the enforcement part of getting someone into rehab if they refused?
Dorothy Low, Richmond Hill, Ont.
Given that addiction has been in his family, you would think Premier Doug Ford might understand addiction a little better. By shuttering safe consumption sites, the province will be in part responsible for any deaths that could have been prevented. The research is clear that the road to recovery starts with safe consumption. With such sites, syringes will be discarded everywhere, bodies will be found and overdoses will be instantly noticeable.
Russ Young, Fort Erie, Ont.
When I listened to Health Minister Sylvia Jones talk about shutting down safe injection sites, it was painfully obvious that she knows little about addiction. This move makes addicts a pawn in Premier Doug Ford's bid for re-election. The government will be complicit in the number of deaths this will cause.
Mel Tonken, Toronto
Address issues from the top down
Toronto ferry crash blamed on speed, safety failures: report, Aug. 19
After two full years of investigation, it has now been determined by the Canada Transportation Safety Board that speed in part caused the crash of the Sam McBride ferry in 2020. There were also "problems with passenger safety management and emergency preparedness." These are human errors. Would it not be prudent to address the top-to-bottom management issues before rushing out to spend $92 million for the new ferries as planned?
Jack Drury, Toronto
New model for 407 tolls
The biggest barrier to getting better use out of Highway 407 is the high rate of tolls. One solution that needs serious consideration would be to base tolls relative to the volume of traffic at the time of the trip. The more vehicles travelling between two points, the lower the toll rate. The volume of cars is already calculated. It would be easy to compute a lower toll for each vehicle. This would encourage use of all of our paid toll highways and eliminate the need for Highway 413 and reduce congestion on Highway 401. Volume pricing models are currently used in the airline industry. All drivers in the GTA would benefit in multiple ways, especially those who need Highway 407 to get to work but can't justify the current high tolls.
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
What did Pinball Clemons know?
Argos put wins above everything else as Chad Kelly returns from nine-game suspension: Cox, Aug. 18
Columnist Damien Cox got this sordid harassment affair, as well as Chad Kelly's past misbehaviours, correct when he said, "this isn't about Kelly getting a second chance. He's already had multiple second chances." Most perplexing to me is the response of General Manager Michael "Pinball" Clemons. Clemons has been associated with the Argos for 35 years in many different roles, from outstanding Hall of Fame player to general manager. To most Torontonians he is the face, image and branding of the Toronto Argonauts and he is beloved. He is regarded in high esteem for his character, integrity, values and support of social causes and events. When the Kelly allegations became public this year, Clemons told the media this was the very first time he became aware of them. How could that possibly be? The person in charge of all football operations could not possibly have been so ignorant of events and serious allegations involving two of his own employees.
The victim, a female assistant strength and conditioning coach, said she told assistant general manager John Murphy about Kelly's persistent harassment and escalating threatening behaviour towards her. She said Murphy told her that she "opened a can of worms that didn't need to be opened." Murphy didn't act on her complaint and for that he should be fired. If Murphy didn't tell Clemons, he should be fired for that as well. If Murphy told Clemons and Clemons did nothing, Clemons should also be fired. He would be so disgraced. It all boils down to what Clemons knew and when did he know it. Clemons has three daughters. How does he go home and explain to them that the winning star quarterback is more important and deserving of support than one of his other employees who has been harassed and let go?
Jamie Wynne, Richmond Hill, Ont.
I agree with Damien Cox. Allowing Chad Kelly to play again in the CFL is a very bad decision. His behaviour in the past is an excellent indicator of what his future behaviour may be. His escalating behaviour does not merit another chance. It merits extradition back to the United States. While you are at it, reinstate the woman who was his victim. This will earn your organization more respect than winning a few more football games.
Peter Johnson, Oxford Mills, Ont.
Subway station accessibility needs improvement
I had a baby this year, and I'm ashamed to admit I never noticed how horribly inaccessible subway stations are before I used a stroller. My closest station is Islington, which is without an elevator as the station undergoes upgrades. The closest elevators are either at Kipling (the opposite direction from where I want to go) or Royal York (elevators out of service until Sept. 30). On top of this, the TTC website often doesn't have accurate information about the status of elevators or it is hard to find. I witnessed a woman drop her small baby on the stairs at Sherbourne Station when trying to manoeuvre her stroller to the platform because the elevator was out of service with no warning on the TTC website or signage before the ticket gates. Some elevators have alternate routes posted, but they're sometimes comical in complexity. If it's this hard to navigate the TTC with a stroller, I shudder to imagine how difficult it is with a mobility device.
Emily Bull, Toronto
CAPTION: Ontario is set to ban consumption and treatment services sites within 200 metres of schools and child care centres.

Subject: Tolls; Addictions; Elevators & escalators; Human error; Roads & highways

Document 36 of 500

If Justin Trudeau really wants to be re-elected, there's one question he needs to stop dodging

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-justin-trudeau-really-wants-be-re-elected/docview/3095625668/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+Justin+Trudeau+really+wants+to+be+re-elected%2C+there%27s+one+question+he+needs+to+stop+dodging&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau got a bit of a reprieve this summer from the questions about whether he was staying on to fight the next election.
Two months since those questions rose to a fever pitch after the Liberals' loss of their St. Paul's seat in Toronto, Trudeau and his team are probably thinking they rode out that storm - as they have ridden out so many storms over the past decade.
"This too shall pass" is an integral part of the Trudeau crisis-management method.
It's been proven true in many cases. Donald Trump, a trigger of existential drama for Canada-U.S. relations, eventually did go away. And while the prospect of another Trump presidency is not totally gone, Trudeau's government appears braced to handle it, as it did before.
Doug Ford, similarly, has ceased to be the thorn in the side of Trudeau as he promised to be, long before COVID made him best pals with Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and the Ontario premier got into the habit of doing friendly, smiling joint announcements with the prime minister.
A pandemic, a convoy protest, foreign-interference blow-ups with China and India - all eventually receded from the headlines, although of course they took their toll on the government.
And while it would be a mistake to say Trudeau and his Liberals simply hunkered down and waited for all these crises to go away - they were highly active on all the files (too activist, some might argue) - they have learned that today's catastrophe can become tomorrow's distant memory.
This is what nine years in government can teach you: the long game. But there's a risk associated with that big-picture view: complacency.
Trudeau definitely has managed to escape the whirlwind of questions over his future that dominated the news in late June, some by his own design, others by bigger events.
U.S. politics, for instance, is an excellent distraction for political junkies. Joe Biden stepping down, the assassination attempt on Trump, and the rise of Kamala Harris are at least as interesting to Canadians, I'll bet, than another round of speculation over whether Trudeau is sticking around.
Then there's this whole issue of Trudeau travelling incognito. Journalist Paul Wells sent up the biggest flare about this earlier in August, accusing the prime minister and his office of blatantly lying about the itinerary posted daily.
"This has become standard operating procedure for Justin Trudeau and his staff during the difficult summer of 2024: they claim in public every day that the PM has 'no public events scheduled.' Even though he is in a different city every day. And he has public events scheduled. In fact, he is in the city in question so he can attend the public events he claims aren't on his schedule," Wells wrote on Substack.
It's worth remembering that prime ministers before Trudeau didn't issue daily itineraries, which are standard for leaders in other countries. I'm betting that Trudeau faced some opposition from the RCMP and his security detail when he vowed to be transparent about where he could be found, especially when opposition to him turned into violence and threats in recent years.
I've been watching these itineraries grow more cryptic and vague since the convoy of 2022 and the incident in which the gates of Rideau Hall were crashed by an armed man looking to "talk" to the prime minister.
But it's also true that Trudeau has, for the most part, avoided putting himself into a position to face more questions about his future, whether from the media, or from his caucus members, who were demanding a meeting so urgently in late June. These demands too, for now, have passed.
That doesn't mean they've gone away. Trudeau is meeting with his cabinet this weekend in Halifax, and with the full caucus in Nanaimo, B.C. in early September.
But there's a sharper question to be answered at these gatherings - and it's not just about whether Trudeau is staying on.
David Cochrane, host of CBC's "Power and Politics," put his finger on that question when he interviewed Trudeau in June. Rather than asking the prime minister (again) whether he was staying to fight the next election, Cochrane asked why he wanted another term in office.
Trudeau didn't have a quick answer, or at least a sufficient one. He said he wanted to continue to "meet the moment."
The number-one job for the cabinet and caucus meetings in the next two weeks should be to come up with a better answer. Asking for a fourth term is a major demand and Liberals should be clear with voters about what they want to do, rather than simply reacting to events and meeting moments.
Trudeau may well get through another few weeks without being asked whether he is staying on. But a harder question to dodge is: why?
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives to deliver a statement on the potential rail strike following an event in Gatineau, Que., on Aug. 21, 2024.

Subject: Politics; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: If Justin Trudeau really wants to be re-elected, there's one question he needs to stop dodging

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 37 of 500

CUPE demands resignation of vice-president for posting 'antisemitic' video

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cupe-demands-resignation-vice-president-posting/docview/3095396299/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CUPE+demands+resignation+of+vice-president+for+posting+%27antisemitic%27+video&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Canadian Union of Public Employees has "lost confidence" in national vice-president Fred Hahn and is demanding his resignation after he posted a video denounced as antisemitic.
"We've given him pretty strong direction," CUPE national president Mark Hancock told the Star in an interview Wednesday, a day after the union's executive board met over the growing controversy surrounding Hahn.
Hahn was re-elected earlier this year in a separate but related role as president of the union's Ontario wing, which also faces a human rights complaint filed last November in the wake of the Oct. 7 Hamas attack on Israel and the subsequent attack by Israel in Gaza that has killed more than 40,000 Palestinians. The complaint alleges systemic discrimination and promotion of antisemitism that isolates Jewish members of the massive public sector union.
Saying "the timing was wrong," Hahn apologized in late October for an Oct. 8 statement celebrating Palestinian resistance in the wake of the attack on Israel that killed some 1,200 people and saw more than 200 hostages taken.
"We've asked him to reply by noon Thursday," Hancock said of the resignation request. "I'm very optimistic and hopeful that he will do the right thing."
Hahn has not replied to requests for comment made through the union.
A union source, speaking on background, said the resignation demand does not directly apply to his role as Ontario president, but "it is going to trigger a chain reaction. It's hard to see how he would have the legitimacy to stay on."
A digitally manipulated video posted on Hahn's Facebook page on Aug. 11 pictured a Jewish athlete with a Star of David arm tattoo leaping off a diving board at the Paris Olympics and turning into a bomb that explodes.
Hahn removed the video Sunday and posted an explanation, but by then it was too late and the damage was done, said Hancock, who noted he considered the video "antisemitic. I felt it was inappropriate."
Hahn was informed of the executive board's decision Tuesday night and chose not to participate in the meeting, added Hancock, who spoke personally with Hahn in a call Wednesday morning.
"The board stated we have lost confidence in Fred to carry on as a national vice-president," said Hancock, referring to Hahn's other role in the union.
"This is a really sad day. Fred is a colleague and a friend."
In addition to concerns from the national executive about Hahn's activities, two CUPE locals in Ontario posted their thoughts on it, with developmental support workers in Vineland calling the diver video "harmful, hurtful and divisive."
In his Facebook explanation, Hahn expressed "deep regret" at causing pain "for some who viewed it."
"It is a terrible mistake, and antisemitic, to conflate abhorrent actions by the state of Israel with Jewish humanity or identity," he wrote.
The Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs said on X that Hahn has "an anti-Israel obsession."
The Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center said CUPE's decision to secure Hahn's resignation "sends a clear message that antisemitism and the toxic environment he created for Jewish CUPE members are no longer acceptable."
While Hahn got supportive messages from many Facebook friends, others called him to task on the post and the explanation.
"Fred, aren't you a union leader? Keep your lousy opinions on Mideast politics to yourself," wrote Sam Jonas.
"You left that post up for days - your apology rings totally hollow," said Naomi Levinson.
Hahn also faced a broadside Wednesday from Premier Doug Ford, who called him "a disgusting human being" and urged CUPE to discipline him.
"Here in Ontario, we have 110 nationalities speaking 200 languages, and we all need to get together," Ford said in St. Catharines.
The premier applauded David Piccini, his labour minister, for accusing Hahn of being an "antisemite."
The confrontation between Piccini and Hahn is shown in a video that Piccini posted on X Tuesday from this week's meeting of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa. Hancock said it came out as the CUPE executive board was meeting.
"Your members deserve better, Fred," Piccini told Hahn in a 44-second exchange that one CUPE official described privately as an "ambush."
"As a labour leader you've got to represent your members and what you have said about Jews ... is antisemitic and your members reach out to me," Piccini said.
"They just re-elected me, man," Hahn retorted at one point in the conversation.
"I am representing my members and I'm not talking about Jews, I'm talking about the state of Israel and I represent 290,000 workers," Hahn added.
"I get that you're filming this and I get that you're trying to do it for something. But you are the minister of labour," Hahn continued, chiding Piccini.
"This is about how you behave," Piccini shot back before walking away.
The controversial diving video has resulted in more CUPE members seeking to be part of the human rights complaint, said lawyer Kathryn Marshall, who is handling the case.
"My inbox has been flooded with people who want to join the action and it's not just Jewish members," she added.
"Hahn must resign but CUPE is ultimately responsible for the environment they have created where someone with his horrific views could thrive and hold leadership positions."
CAPTION: Fred Hahn, the Canadian Union of Public Employees' Ontario president, speaks at a news conference on June 6, 2024.

Subject: Antisemitism; Resignations; Jewish people; Leadership; Vice Presidents; Human rights

Business indexing term: Subject: Resignations Leadership

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Union of Public Employees; NAICS: 813930

Title: CUPE demands resignation of vice-president for posting 'antisemitic' video

Document 38 of 500

Quinte byelection set for Sept. 19

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/quinte-byelection-set-sept-19/docview/3095395693/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Quinte+byelection+set+for+Sept.+19&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford is wasting no time in calling a byelection to fill a seat vacated by a big resignation last week.
Education Minister Todd Smith resigned his cabinet post and his Bay of Quinte seat in eastern Ontario on Friday.
Now, Ford has called the Bay of Quinte byelection for Sept. 19.
The Progressive Conservatives announced Tyler Allsopp as their candidate for the riding on Wednesday.
Allsopp is a councillor in Belleville, a small-business owner, and has founded various community initiatives such as Community Cares Holiday Meal and served as the treasurer of the Enrichment Centre for Mental Health.
Smith has said he resigned to take a job in the private sector, but has not yet announced what it is.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 40 of 500

Ford helps factory farmers keep secrets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-helps-factory-farmers-keep-secrets/docview/3095395540/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+helps+factory+farmers+keep+secrets&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, images of the horrific suffering of animals trapped in our factory farming system were making their way onto our TV screens.
The secretly filmed videos were having a powerful and disturbing effect on a public that had simply assumed governments were regulating and monitoring agribusiness to assure basic animal welfare.
One such video, shown on CTV's "W5" in November 2020, captured footage of a very ill pregnant pig being roughly treated as she's sliced open with a knife and her unborn piglets are ripped out and thrown onto a filthy concrete floor. The viewers then see piglets squealing in pain as, wide awake, they're castrated and have their tails cut off.
Inside the executive offices of agribusiness and the Doug Ford government, there was only one way to deal with the growing public anger: these videos must be stopped!
So, the Ford government brought in a law, known as an agriculture gag law (or ag-gag) making it illegal, punishable with fines up to $25,000, to get hired at a factory farm or slaughterhouses while concealing an intention to make an undercover video documenting animal abuse.
Interestingly, the farm featured in the pig video shown on "W5" pleaded guilty to charges of animal cruelty and was fined $20,000 - a prosecution that would never have happened without the video evidence, which led the farm to plead guilty.
Despite this conviction, demonstrating the value of undercover videos, the government has persisted with its ag-gag law, which has accomplished exactly what the industry wanted: an end to exposés of the miserable lives of animals behind farm factory walls.
Not one such video has been made since the law came into effect almost four years ago, says Camille Labchuk, of the group Animal Justice, which shot the pig video.
However, last April, Animal Justice won an important victory when the Ontario Superior Court struck down key sections of the ag-gag law. Justice Markus Koehnen ruled that parts of the law violated the charter-protected right to free expression of activists, journalists and whistleblowers.
But the Ford government is appealing the ruling, so the battle continues.
The determination of government and industry to keep the curtain drawn on corporate farming practices suggests that there's a lot they want to remain hidden.
For instance, they don't want us to know that baby calves, snatched from their mothers at birth, spend the entire four or five months of their lives confined to narrow crates that prevent them from even turning around. The crates are designed to stunt their muscle growth so that their flesh is tender, when it's served as veal.
Such heartless practices are not surprising, considering that the powerful, multibillion-dollar corporate farming industry has been allowed to draw up its own codes of conduct and been mostly left to regulate itself behind factory walls.
The replacement of the family farm with industrial farming in recent decades has meant a dramatic worsening of living conditions for millions of animals - who, while different from humans, suffer as we do.
As renowned anthropologist Jane Goodall put it: "Farm animals feel pleasure and sadness, excitement and resentment, depression, fear and pain. They are far more aware and intelligent than we ever imagined."
What range of emotions must they feel spending their lives as captives inside our wretched factory farming system?
We can get a hint of how much they suffer by watching videos of the lucky few who end up in animal rescue sanctuaries. The joy of these suddenly-released animals as they frolic outdoors for the first time is not something easily forgotten.
Certainly, we live today with a strange disconnect between the world of dogs and cats, who are increasingly loved, spoiled, indulged and treated as part of the family, and the hidden world of suffering farm animals, who typically experience the outdoors for the first time as they're loaded onto trucks bound for the slaughterhouse.
LINDA MCQUAIG IS AN AUTHOR, JOURNALIST AND A FREELANCE
CONTRIBUTING COLUMNIST FOR
THE STAR.
CREDIT: Linda McQuaig OPINION LINDA MCQUAIG IS AN AUTHOR, JOURNALIST AND A FREELANCE CONTRIBUTING COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR.;

Bargaining goes down to the wire: Groups urge PM to step in ahead of possible walkout by thousands of CN and CPKC workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bargaining-goes-down-wire/docview/3095395506/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bargaining+goes+down+to+the+wire%3A+Groups+urge+PM+to+step+in+ahead+of+possible+walkout+by+thousands+of+CN+and+CPKC+workers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Talks to avert a railway work stoppage appeared to be going down to the wire.
Union and management negotiators remained at the bargaining table just hours ahead of a 12:01 a.m. Thursday deadline to avert a strike or lockout that could hammer the Canadian economy and scramble commutes for thousands of GTA residents.
"All parties remain at the bargaining table. Our union is making a good-faith effort to reach agreements before midnight," said Christopher Monette, spokesperson for Teamsters Canada, which represents 9,300 workers at CN Rail and Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CPKC).
Conductors, engineers and yard workers at both railways are in negotiations, as are rail traffic controllers at CN.
The companies have said they'll lock workers out if there's no deal before the deadline. The union can also strike against CPKC.
A work stoppage would mean GO Transit's Milton line wouldn't be operating, nor would trains operating out of its Hamilton station.
A spokesperson for Metrolinx suggested commuters who usually use the Hamilton GO station could instead use the West Harbour or Aldershot train stations, or take the Route 16 bus.
Commuters who usually take the Milton line are encouraged to take local transit, or GO's Lakeshore West or Kitchener train lines as an alternative.
At a press conference in Alberta, federal Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland urged both sides to reach a deal, saying a work stoppage would be an act of "self harm" to the national economy.
"Let's as a country, be united in sending a very clear message to the companies and to the union, it is not acceptable for us to do this act of self harm to the Canadian economy," Freeland said. "You have the responsibility to get a deal done. We are expecting you to rise to that responsibility and get a deal done."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford also urged both sides to keep talking, saying it would hurt businesses, and also exacerbate an affordability crisis.
Ford also said the companies shouldn't lock their workers out.
"Threatening to lock out workers is not the answer," said Ford. "We need everyone to treat each other with respect and be laser focused on getting the deal done."
Wednesday, a coalition of Canadian business associations called on federal Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon to refer the dispute to the Canada Industrial Relations Board for binding arbitration.
"The Government of Canada has a responsibility to protect the Canadian public and maintain national security, and it is time to act decisively to fulfil that obligation," said the statement, from the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, Canadian Manufacturers and Exporters, the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, and the Business Council of Canada.
In an interview, the head of the association representing Canada's manufacturers said a work stoppage would be catastrophic for the sector, and for the country's broader economy.
"This will be a catastrophe. It's going to be messy," said Dennis Darby, CEO of Canadian Manufacturers & Exporters. "So many companies have just recovered from COVID, and this is the last thing they need."
Already, some companies were starting to announce temporary shutdowns in anticipation of a stoppage. B.C.-based lumber processor Conifex said Wednesday it would cut back to one shift a day starting Monday.
Shipping giant Maersk this week stopped accepting Canada-bound goods that need to be transferred by rail.
A report released Wednesday by Moody's Analytics estimated that a stoppage would cost the Canadian economy $341 million per day, and could push inflation back up.
While there would be some short-term economic damage from a stoppage, there's also a longer-term reputational risk, said Pedro Antunes, chief economist at the Conference Board of Canada.
Hurting supply chains in Canada and the U.S. could put this country's reputation as a reliable trade partner in jeopardy, Antunes said. "This could put some of those trade relationships at risk."
With files from
Stephanie Levitz
and Robert Benzie
CAPTION: More than 9,000 workers at CN Rail and CPKC were poised to walk off the job at midnight if a new contract wasn't hammered out.Darryl DyckThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star With files from Stephanie Levitz and Robert Benzie

Document 42 of 500

Ford calls facilities 'failed policy': Premier dismisses concerns closures will increase overdose deaths, calling them a 'scare tactic'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-calls-facilities-failed-policy/docview/3095395315/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+calls+facilities+%27failed+policy%27%3A+Premier+dismisses+concerns+closures+will+increase+overdose+deaths%2C+calling+them+a+%27scare+tactic%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has denounced safe injection sites as a "failed policy" experiment that harms communities as well as people battling drug addiction.
In his first public comments since his government announced 10 of Ontario's safe injection sites will close by March 31 due to zoning restrictions forcing them to be 200 metres away from schools and daycares, Ford did not mince words.
"This was supposed to be the greatest thing since sliced bread - it's the worst thing that could ever happen to a community to have one of these safe injection sites in their neighbourhood," he said Wednesday in St. Catharines, the day after his changes were announced.
"How you would feel if I stuck one of these beside your house? You wouldn't like it ... neither would your neighbours and neither would anyone else."
Touting the new $378 million in funding for treatment hubs that will replace many of the sites, Ford said "giving someone, an addict, a place to do their injections, we haven't seen it get better."
Asked about health advocates warning there could be more overdose deaths by closing five of Toronto's 10 safe injection sites, Ford dismissed such concerns as "typical scare tactics that these folks have been saying for years."
Angela Robertson, executive director at Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre, which will be forced to close one of its two safe injection sites, said Ford's decision could mean more deaths among drug users who might otherwise survive an overdose.
Robertson said her two sites reversed 766 overdoses last year.
The premier - who has spoken candidly in the past about his late brother Rob Ford, the former Toronto mayor who was captured on video using crack cocaine - said treatment and rehabilitation services are more effective.
"You don't give them more drugs," he said.
"We're trying to help these people, we aren't going to give them a safe place to shoot up heroin and crack and every other thing they can get their hands on."
The premier noted he has been inundated with calls from residents, who live near the sites, praising the move due to concerns about drug dealing and other criminal activity.
At the same time, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs with 375 supportive housing units, as well as addiction recovery and treatment beds.
The new HART facilities, which will not offer supervised drug consumption or safe supply of narcotics or needle exchange programs, aim to help people transition to long-term housing.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted Ford was closing consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites against the advice of experts who have reported back to his government that they can be effective.
"People deserve a government that uses every tool they have to take on the addictions crisis, and that bases that support on evidence, not ideology," Stiles said Wednesday.
Five of the shuttered CTS sites are in Toronto, including Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet last summer.
CTS facilities in Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay will also close by next spring.
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "of course these sites should not be next to schools," she expressed concern that the Progressive Conservatives aren't doing enough to address addiction and mental health issues.
"It is so important we find smart, compassionate solutions to helping our loved ones and neighbours fight these challenges," said Crombie.
CAPTION: Toronto's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner- Makurat was killed by a stray bullet last summer, will close after Premier Doug Ford announced that safe injection sites must be 200 metres away from schools and child-care centres.Nick LachanceToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 43 of 500

Reports urged province to keep safe drug site open: Queen St. E. facility offers needed service, two reviews found in wake of shooting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reports-urged-province-keep-safe-drug-site-open/docview/3095393843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reports+urged+province+to+keep+safe+drug+site+open%3A+Queen+St.+E.+facility+offers+needed+service%2C+two+reviews+found+in+wake+of+shooting&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government had two provincially commissioned reports strongly urging continued safe drug consumption services at a Queen Street East health centre when it announced rules that will halt the service and close nine other such sites across Ontario.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones referred to the July 2023 gunfire slaying of a Leslieville mother outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre while announcing the changes Tuesday, citing a need especially to protect children. She did not mention the studies she has received in recent months, quietly posted online Monday, that affirm the community value of safe consumption and harm reduction.
Premier Doug Ford had voiced strong opposition to allowing supervised drug use, with medical supports to reverse possible overdoses and expose users to treatment options and other health services, before Jones revealed that any such sites within 200 metres of a school or child-care centre must close by March 31.
Four more of Toronto's 10 sites are set to shut, along with others in Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay.
Last year, after the stray-bullet slaying of Karolina (Caroline) Huebner-Makurat on Queen Street, Jones's ministry ordered a review of the South Riverdale site and its safe consumption services by Jill Campbell, the centre's provincially appointed supervisor, plus an external review by Unity Health Toronto, "as a means of identifying opportunities for improvement."
Both reviews concluded that safe consumption should continue but with improved community safety supports to address concerns of neighbouring residents, businesses and child-care providers about violence, discarded needles, visible drug deals and more.
After five months of research, a review team from Unity Health, a hospital network affiliated with the University of Toronto, "found a clear need for the (safe consumption) service at 955 Queen Street East, based on the number of clients being serviced and the broad range of health services clients are accessing via referrals and integrated services in the community health centre ... It is suitable to maintain funding" for the supervised consumption service, the review said.
The centre had 11,858 total visits for supervised drug use between September 2022 and September 2023. In 2022, centre staff reversed more than 100 potentially fatal overdoses. In the first seven months of 2023, they referred at least 165 clients to other services in the building, such as a dietitian and mental health supports.
However, Unity Health also concluded that health centre managers had been only "somewhat responsive to local community concerns with room for improvement" when it came to security around the site, and communications with community members who had raised concerns before Huebner-Makurat's slaying triggered community outrage and public calls for closure.
The report notes that the health centre did take steps to address concerns following the passerby's death, including expanded sweeps to pick up discarded needles, increasing outreach to Toronto police and improving exterior lighting, but said it should do more.
Recommendations include hiring a licensed security firm to provide better protection around the site, improved communication with local community members including regular open houses, improved use of security cameras, and establishing an "ongoing, formal avenue of communication" with Toronto police that "could help build relationships and address ongoing community and staff safety concerns."
Unity Health noted that neighbours, which include a public school and several child-care facilities, reported a long list of concerns such as intoxicated people outside the centre acting belligerent and making threats, people defecating and urinating in public nearby, and dropped needles. Some centre visitors in turn complained they had been needlessly harassed and photographed by residents.
Hayley Mick, a Unity Health spokesperson, said Wednesday that the network is pleased with $378 million announced by Jones for "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" hubs that include addiction and treatment beds - but no safe consumption facilities or harm reduction materials such as needle exchange, safe injection kits or safe inhalation kits.
However, "we are concerned about the reduction in access to supervised consumption, which evidence shows saves lives, reduces pressure on emergency services and helps connect people to treatment," she added. "We will continue to work closely with our community and government partners, as well as people use drugs, to address this health crisis."
Toronto paramedics respond to an average of six suspected overdose deaths per week and 87 non-fatal overdose calls, according to city data.
The April 2024 report by Campbell, a former Centre for Addiction and Mental Health executive, concluded: "Evidence shows that consumption treatment services are a necessary public health service, implemented to save lives and prevent accidental overdose death related to substance abuse."
Rather than shutter such sites, she urged the government to expand harm reduction services including safe consumption to "prevent further accidental substance-use death and provide additional safer treatment options for substance users across the spectrum of substance illness."
Campbell's other recommendations include the Health Ministry providing extra funding to sites close to schools and child-care centres for extra security officers, and to look at expanding the 15-metre zone around the centre where staff regularly search for needles.
She noted the valid community concerns, but said some residents assumed that anyone doing anything objectionable near the centre was associated with it. A Leslieville social media account wrongly reported needles being found in a daycare playground when police reported they were in a private driveway next door.
"Although facts don't always change feelings, the increase in visibility of poverty, drug usage, homelessness and mental illness since the pandemic has played a major factor in the experiences of the South Riverdale community, and likely other neighbourhoods in Toronto," Campbell concluded, adding the centre was established to serve the "weak and underserved" and should do so in collaboration with the surrounding community.
Campbell, who is no longer supervising the centre, could not be reached for comment Wednesday.
Alexandra Adamo, a spokesperson for the health minister, said in an email that "communities, parents and families across Ontario have made it clear that the presence of drug consumption sites near schools is leading to serious safety problems. We agree."
She added that "yesterday's actions will help protect the public, especially for our most vulnerable: Ontario's children," and said police statistics show elevated crime around the sites.
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 44 of 500

One question Trudeau needs to stop dodging

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/one-question-trudeau-needs-stop-dodging/docview/3095393765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+question+Trudeau+needs+to+stop+dodging&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau got a bit of a reprieve this summer from the questions about whether he was staying on to fight the next election.
Two months since those questions rose to a fever pitch after the Liberals' loss of their St. Paul's seat in Toronto, Trudeau and his team are probably thinking they rode out that storm - as they have ridden out so many storms over the past decade.
"This too shall pass" is an integral part of the Trudeau crisis-management method.
It's been proven true in many cases. Donald Trump, a trigger of existential drama for Canada-U.S. relations, eventually did go away. And while the prospect of another Trump presidency is not totally gone, Trudeau's government appears braced to handle it, as it did before.
Doug Ford, similarly, has ceased to be the thorn in the side of Trudeau as he promised to be, long before COVID made him best pals with Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and the Ontario premier got into the habit of doing friendly, smiling joint announcements with the prime minister.
A pandemic, a convoy protest, foreign-interference blow-ups with China and India - all eventually receded from the headlines, although of course they took their toll on the government.
And while it would be a mistake to say Trudeau and his Liberals simply hunkered down and waited for all these crises to go away - they were highly active on all the files (too activist, some might argue) - they have learned that today's catastrophe can become tomorrow's distant memory.
This is what nine years in government can teach you: the long game. But there's a risk associated with that big-picture view: complacency.
Trudeau definitely has managed to escape the whirlwind of questions over his future that dominated the news in late June, some by his own design, others by bigger events.
U.S. politics, for instance, is an excellent distraction for political junkies. Joe Biden stepping down, the assassination attempt on Trump, and the rise of Kamala Harris are at least as interesting to Canadians, I'll bet, than another round of speculation over whether Trudeau is sticking around.
Then there's this whole issue of Trudeau travelling incognito. Journalist Paul Wells sent up the biggest flare about this earlier in August, accusing the prime minister and his office of blatantly lying about the itinerary posted daily.
"This has become standard operating procedure for Justin Trudeau and his staff during the difficult summer of 2024: they claim in public every day that the PM has 'no public events scheduled.' Even though he is in a different city every day. And he has public events scheduled. In fact, he is in the city in question so he can attend the public events he claims aren't on his schedule," Wells wrote on Substack.
It's worth remembering that prime ministers before Trudeau didn't issue daily itineraries, which are standard for leaders in other countries. I'm betting that Trudeau faced some opposition from the RCMP and his security detail when he vowed to be transparent about where he could be found, especially when opposition to him turned into violence and threats in recent years.
I've been watching these itineraries grow more cryptic and vague since the convoy of 2022 and the incident in which the gates of Rideau Hall were crashed by an armed man looking to "talk" to the prime minister.
But it's also true that Trudeau has, for the most part, avoided putting himself into a position to face more questions about his future, whether from the media, or from his caucus members, who were demanding a meeting so urgently in late June. These demands too, for now, have passed.
That doesn't mean they've gone away. Trudeau is meeting with his cabinet this weekend in Halifax, and with the full caucus in Nanaimo, B.C. in early September.
But there's a sharper question to be answered at these gatherings - and it's not just about whether Trudeau is staying on.
David Cochrane, host of CBC's "Power and Politics," put his finger on that question when he interviewed Trudeau in June. Rather than asking the prime minister (again) whether he was staying to fight the next election, Cochrane asked why he wanted another term in office. Trudeau didn't have a quick answer, or at least a sufficient one. He said he wanted to continue to "meet the moment."
The number-one job for the cabinet and caucus meetings in the next two weeks should be to come up with a better answer. Asking for a fourth term is a major demand and Liberals should be clear with voters about what they want to do, rather than simply reacting to events and meeting moments.
Trudeau may well get through another few weeks without being asked whether he is staying on. But a harder question to dodge is: why?
CAPTION: If Justin Trudeau really wants to be re-elected as prime minister, Susan Delacourt writes, he needs to come up with a compelling reason why Canadians should give his government another term in office.Sean KilpatrickThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Letters: Doug Ford to ban safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres, Aug. 20 Toronto ferry crash blamed on speed, safety failures: report, Aug. 19 Argos put wins above everything else as Chad Kelly returns from nine-game suspension: Cox, Aug. 18

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3095393764/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+to+ban+safe+injection+sites+within+200+metres+of+schools+and+child-care+centres%2C+Aug.+20+Toronto+ferry+crash+blamed+on+speed%2C+safety+failures%3A+report%2C+Aug.+19+Argos+put+wins+above+everything+else+as+Chad+Kelly+returns+from+nine-game+suspension%3A+Cox%2C+Aug.+18&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shutting down injection sites is
a death sentence
Doug Ford to ban safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres, Aug. 20
Given that addiction has been in his family, you would think Premier Doug Ford might understand addiction a little better. By shuttering safe-consumption sites, the province will be in part responsible for any deaths that could have been prevented. The research is clear that the road to recovery starts with safe consumption. Without such sites, syringes will be discarded everywhere, bodies will be found and overdoses will be instantly noticeable.
Russ Young, Fort Erie, Ont.
I'm sure many people will be thinking what a great plan this is to help get drug-addicted people into a treatment centre. But we have not been told how that would work. Where are these proposed centres located and how would you staff them? Our hospitals and other medical centres are already stretched to the limit. And what about the enforcement part of getting someone into rehab if they refused?
Dorothy Low, Richmond Hill

When I listened to Health Minister Sylvia Jones talk about shutting down safe-injection sites, it was painfully obvious that she knows little about addiction. This move makes addicts a pawn in Doug Ford's bid for re-election. The government will be complicit in the number of deaths this will cause.
Mel Tonken, Toronto
Address island ferry issues from the top down
Toronto ferry crash blamed on speed, safety failures: report, Aug. 19
After two full years of investigation, it has now been determined by the Canada Transportation Safety Board that speed in part caused the crash of the Sam McBride ferry in 2020. There were also "problems with passenger safety management and emergency preparedness." These are human errors. Would it not be prudent to address the top-to-bottom management issues before rushing out to spend $92 million for the new ferries as planned?
Jack Drury, Toronto
Tolls should fall as traffic volume rises on the 407
The biggest barrier to getting better use out of Highway 407 is the high rate of tolls.
One solution that needs serious consideration would be to base tolls relative to the volume of traffic at the time of the trip. The more vehicles travelling between two points, the lower the toll rate. The volume of cars is already calculated. It would be easy to compute a lower toll for each vehicle.
This would encourage use of all of our paid toll highways and eliminate the need for Highway 413 and reduce congestion on Highway 401.
Volume pricing models are currently used in the airline industry. All drivers in the GTA would benefit in multiple ways, especially those who need Highway 407 to get to work but can't justify the current high tolls.
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
When did Pinball know about Kelly allegations?
Argos put wins above everything else as Chad Kelly returns from nine-game suspension: Cox, Aug. 18
Columnist Damien Cox got this sordid harassment affair, as well as Chad Kelly's past misbehaviours, correct when he said, "this isn't about Kelly getting a second chance. He's already had multiple second chances."
Most perplexing to me is the response of general manager Michael "Pinball" Clemons, who has been associated with the Argos for 35 years in many roles, from outstanding Hall of Fame player to general manager. To most Torontonians he is the face, image and branding of the Argonauts and he is beloved. He is regarded in high esteem for his character, integrity, values and support of social causes and events.
When the Kelly allegations became public this year, Clemons told the media this was the first time he became aware of them. How could that possibly be? How could the person in charge of all football operations possibly have been so ignorant of events and serious allegations involving two of his own employees?
The victim, a female assistant strength and conditioning coach, said she told assistant general manager John Murphy about Kelly's persistent harassment and escalating threatening behaviour towards her. She said Murphy told her that she "opened a can of worms that didn't need to be opened."
Murphy didn't act on her complaint and for that he should be fired. If Murphy didn't tell Clemons, he should be fired for that as well. If Murphy told Clemons and Clemons did nothing, Clemons should also be fired. It all boils down to what Clemons knew and when did he know it.
Clemons has three daughters. How does he explain to them that the star quarterback is more important and deserving of support than one of his other employees who has been harassed and let go?
Jamie Wynne, Richmond Hill

I agree with Damien Cox. Allowing Chad Kelly to play again in the CFL is a very bad decision. His behaviour in the past is an excellent indicator of what his future behaviour may be. His escalating behaviour does not merit another chance. It merits extradition back to the United States. While you are at it, reinstate the woman who was his victim. This will earn your organization more respect than winning a few more football games.
Peter Johnson, Oxford Mills, Ont.
Subway station accessibility needs work
I had a baby this year, and I'm ashamed to admit I never noticed how horribly inaccessible subway stations are before I used a stroller. My closest station is Islington, which is without an elevator as the station undergoes upgrades. The closest elevators are either at Kipling (the opposite direction from where I want to go) or Royal York (elevators out of service until Sept. 30).
On top of this, the TTC website often doesn't have accurate information about the status of elevators or it is hard to find. I witnessed a woman drop her small baby on the stairs at Sherbourne Station when trying to manoeuvre her stroller to the platform because the elevator was out of service with no warning on the TTC website or signage before the ticket gates.
Some elevators have alternate routes posted, but they're sometimes comical in complexity. If it's this hard to navigate the TTC with a stroller, I shudder to imagine how difficult it is with a mobility device.
Emily Bull, Toronto

Subject: Tolls; Addictions; Elevators & escalators; Human error; Roads & highways

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Document 46 of 500

Premier's decision doesn't solve problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-decision-doesnt-solve-problem/docview/3095393717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier%27s+decision+doesn%27t+solve+problem&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The war on drugs is now part of the culture wars in Doug Ford's Ontario.
The premier is shutting down many of the province's safe injection sites by declaring their locations unsafe - too close to schools and child-care centres.
He has finally found a way, bureaucratically, to have his way, politically. In practice, that means government-funded safe sites must be a safe distance of 200 metres or more from schools and child-care centres starting next March.
Ford points out that his government requires all cannabis storefronts to be at least 150 metres from any school. So why should needle exchange sites be any different?
Fair point, but he added an extra 50 metres of distancing for good measure - conveniently ensuring that most existing sites will henceforth be deemed too close for comfort. And destined to be closed down.
It's a deft approach by the usually blundering and blustering politician. Ford has bristled about supervised sites for years, biding his time until times changed.
Now, times are indeed different, compared to when governments began experimenting with safe injection sites - Toronto's first supervised consumption service opened in 2017. All these years later, an addiction crisis is decimating a generation of young people, while the fallout from fentanyl is disrupting neighbouring communities.
Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two, was killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre consumption and treatment site (CTS) last year. The government cites increased crime near safe sites, where assault and robbery incidents are roughly double those of other neighbourhoods in Toronto (and still higher in other cities); it also points to police reports of free hydromorphone from those sites being "diverted and trafficked" outside.
Now, Ford is using proximity as a pretext for putting these sites out of business. It's one way to thread the needle.
Ford claims he's not moralizing about drug users, merely protecting children from the harmful effects of harm reduction sites too close to home. But for all the focus on proximity as a measure of safety, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government is not allowing disqualified sites to relocate and reopen further from schools.
Which means it's not really about relocating. It's about reallocating resources from harm reduction to recovery.
Toronto now has 10 such sites. Six are provincially funded CTS centres covered by this week's announcement, of which five will be required to close due to their locations.
This week, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced an extra $378 million for 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs offering a more holistic approach. But that won't satisfy critics who insist an overdose emergency requires urgent measures like needle exchanges.
Safe drug sites are like a Rorschach blotter test. Proponents view them as harm reduction, while opponents see them as harm attraction.
Where you stand on these sites might depend on where you live, what drugs you've done, what harm(s) you've seen. If your child's school is near a so-called "safe injection site," it's unsafe; if your child is using dangerous drugs and dirty needles, maybe it's a safer site.
Ford styles himself an authority on how drug dealers operate: "I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around - it's a haven for drug dealers," he said last week.
Defenders of safe drug sites say they save the lives of hundreds of people with addictions who would otherwise die without help. What the statistics don't make clear is how many lives go unsaved, because safe sites don't change behaviours, they perhaps only perpetuate them (523 people died from opioid toxicity last year, according to Toronto Public Health figures).
"This government is cynically attacking health infrastructure and putting ideology over evidence," Ontario's NDP said in response.
Left unsaid was that B.C.'s NDP government faced an intense public backlash about drug use, forcing it to do a U-turn this year on a pilot project decriminalizing possession of small amounts of hard drugs. No politician can ignore public sentiment on public drug use near so-called safe spaces.
Perhaps that's why Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie seemed more measured in her reaction, acknowledging that "these sites should not be next to schools." While criticizing Ford's funding shortfalls, she promised only to ensure "treatment to those who need it" - which is different from defending existing safe sites.
The realities in Ontario and B.C., and the responses of politicians in both provinces, are a reminder that safe sites were always a quick fix that bought us more time, not more success. The premier is unquestionably determined to rip off the Band-Aid, but the bigger question is whether he can show as much passion for finding - and funding - a comprehensive approach to a problem that is only getting worse over time, safe sites or not.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has bristled about supervised sites for years, biding his time until times changed, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Christopher KatsarovTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Drug addiction; Schools; Drug trafficking; Politics; Harm reduction; Drug use

Document 47 of 500

Crombie embraces Trudeau critics: Ontario Liberal leader distances herself from PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-embraces-trudeau-critics/docview/3095393676/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+embraces+Trudeau+critics%3A+Ontario+Liberal+leader+distances+herself+from+PM&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin who?
Bonnie Crombie is distancing her provincial party from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's struggling federal Liberals.
As the Ontario Liberals gather in London, Ont., for a Sept. 20-22 policy conference, Crombie has invited some prominent Trudeau critics to speak to delegates.
"This is meant to send exactly the signal it looks like it's sending, that Bonnie Crombie is a different kind of Liberal," said an adviser, speaking confidentially in order to discuss strategy.
With Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives gearing up for a possible early election next spring, the provincial Liberal leader is also getting ready.
Mindful that Ford has spent millions of Tory campaign cash on attack ads trying to link her to Trudeau, Crombie has asked former British Columbia premier Christy Clark and former federal health minister Jane Philpott to address the party faithful.
Clark made headlines after the Liberals' shocking loss to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives in the June 24 Toronto-St. Paul's byelection by saying Trudeau "doesn't have the pull with voters that he once had."
"But more importantly, he isn't even able to motivate some of the people in the country who are most likely to vote for his party. That's a tough message for any leader," she said this summer.
Philpott resigned from Trudeau's cabinet with a splash in 2019, saying she had "lost confidence" in the prime minister's handling of the SNC-Lavalin scandal.
Ontario Liberals privately believe the national party's current unpopularity is contributing to their own challenges to break through against Ford's Tories.
Crombie has also invited former Toronto chief planner Jennifer Keesmaat and Dr. Samir Sinha, director of geriatrics at Mount Sinai and the University Health Network Hospitals.
"Ontario's Liberals are coming together to chart a path forward for our party and present an ambitious vision to deliver more for you and your family from a government that works for you," said the rookie leader, who took her party's helm last Dec. 2.
"After over six years of Doug Ford's failed leadership and giveaways to his rich friends and insiders, Ontario families are struggling," she said.
"We're going to get Ontario back on track with a plan to fix and protect our health-care system, cut class sizes, combat climate change, create jobs and make housing and your life more affordable."
Philpott, dean of health sciences at Queen's University, and Sinha, a leading expert on seniors' care, will discuss how Ontario's publicly funded health-care system can be improved.
Clark, who governed B.C. from 2011 until 2017, will share her insights on building an election platform that can help boost economic growth while improving health and education and protecting the environment.
Keesmaat, runner-up to John Tory in the 2018 Toronto mayoral race, will speak to how Ontario can tackle its housing crisis.
Although the next Ontario election is scheduled for June 2026, Ford, who leads in every public-opinion poll, has been considering a vote for next year.
"Stay tuned," the premier said coyly in May.
In June, NDP Leader Marit Stiles rallied her party, noting the last time a sitting premier with a majority in the legislature called a snap election, the New Democrats won.
Recalling the 1990 vote triggered by then Liberal premier David Peterson, who was well ahead in the polls until the campaign, Stiles reminded Ford that Bob Rae's NDP came from behind to win.
"If you're going to call an election ... early, I'm ready to fight that election on the things that matter to the people of Ontario," she said June 15.
"So bring it."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 48 of 500

Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-safe-injection-sites-are-failed/docview/3095751561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+safe+injection+sites+are+a+%27failed+policy%27+that+harms+communities+and+people+with+addictions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has denounced safe injection sites as a "failed policy" experiment that harms communities as well as people battling drug addiction.
In his first public comments since his government announced 10 of Ontario's safe injection sites will close by March 31 due to zoning restrictions forcing them to be 200 metres away from schools and daycares, Ford did not mince words.
"This was supposed to be the greatest thing since sliced bread - it's the worst thing that could ever happen to a community to have one of these safe injection sites in their neighbourhood," he said Wednesday in St. Catharines, the day after his changes were announced.
"How you would feel if I stuck one of these beside your house? You wouldn't like it ... neither would your neighbours and neither would anyone else."
Touting the new $378 million in funding for treatment hubs that will replace many of the sites, Ford said "giving someone, an addict, a place to do their injections, we haven't seen it get better."
Asked about health advocates warning there could be more overdose deaths by closing five of Toronto's 10 safe injection sites, Ford dismissed such concerns as "typical scare tactics that these folks have been saying for years."
Angela Robertson, executive director at Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre, which will be forced to close one of its two safe injection sites, said Ford's decision could mean more deaths among drug users who might otherwise survive an overdose.
Robertson said her two sites reversed 766 overdoses last year.
The premier - who has spoken candidly in the past about his late brother Rob Ford, the former Toronto mayor who was captured on video using crack cocaine - said treatment and rehabilitation services are more effective.
"You don't give them more drugs," he said. "We're trying to help these people, we aren't going to give them a safe place to shoot up heroin and crack and every other thing they can get their hands on."
The premier noted he has been inundated with calls from residents, who live near the sites, praising the move due to concerns about drug dealing and other criminal activity.
At the same time, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs with 375 supportive housing units as well as addiction recovery and treatment beds.
The new HART facilities, which will not offer supervised drug consumption or safe supply of narcotics or needle exchange programs, aim to help people transition to long-term housing.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted Ford was closing CTS sites against the advice of experts who have reported back to his government that they can be effective.
"People deserve a government that uses every tool they have to take on the addictions crisis, and that bases that support on evidence, not ideology," Stiles said Wednesday.
Five of the shuttered consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites are in Toronto, including Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by stray bullet last summer.
Outcry over that shocking crime put a new focus on the community concerns over safe injection sites.
CTS facilities in Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay will also close by next spring.
"The status quo of drug consumption sites is not working," Jones said Tuesday at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa.
"Parents are worried about discarded needles that their children could pick up. Some parents no longer feel comfortable sending their children to the local elementary school or have pulled them out of their local daycare," she said.
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "of course these sites should not be next to schools," she expressed concern that the Progressive Conservatives aren't doing enough to address addiction and mental health issues.
"It is so important we find smart, compassionate solutions to helping our loved ones and neighbours fight these challenges," said Crombie, who also has spoken about the toll of addiction in her family.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner predicted "Ontario will lose more lives because of this government's attack on harm reduction services."
While AMO president Colin Best said municipalities welcomed Tuesday's decision as "an important step towards the comprehensive, long-term strategy that is needed to address root causes of homelessness," there are concerns.
"Supervised safe consumption sites are a critical part of tackling the opioid crisis. The evidence shows that harm reduction saves lives, saves money, and reduces crime. It takes the pressure off our police, paramedics and hospitals," said Best.
Mayor Olivia Chow's office said she was "concerned that the closure of existing harm reduction services in Toronto will have significant consequences, such as increased overdose deaths, greater strain on first responders and emergency rooms, and more public drug consumption."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford doubles down following ban of safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres. Ten of 17 sites across Ontario will be shuttered after March 31, five of which are in Toronto.Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Title: Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions
