Document 61 of 500

Province releases streamlined plan: New rules for municipalities aim to boost construction, move focus from single-family homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-releases-streamlined-plan/docview/3095020780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+releases+streamlined+plan%3A+New+rules+for+municipalities+aim+to+boost+construction%2C+move+focus+from+single-family+homes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Reeling from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP, Premier Doug Ford's government is taking another stab at helping municipalities build homes faster to ease a housing shortage.
New rules taking effect Oct. 20 will encourage building near major transit stations and on underused and low-density properties such as strip plazas and shopping malls, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said Tuesday.
More housing options, such as for seniors and students, will also be encouraged as the population swells. Criticisms of previous plans included concerns that a focus on single-family homes encouraged sprawl and did not meet all needs in the real estate market.
"Even though housing starts have been impacted by global uncertainty and high interest rates, we are laying the groundwork for a long-term building boom as economic circumstances improve," Calandra told mayors, councillors and officials from across the province at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa.
The new rules are in a revised and streamlined provincial planning statement - the guidebook for municipalities as they plan future development - that, at 20,517 words, is 60 per cent shorter than the previous 51,177-word version.
"It will ensure one consistent set of planning direction across the province," Calandra added in a speech prepared for the conference.
"It recognizes that municipalities understand local challenges and priorities when it comes to building homes ... it is you who know best what you should be building."
More housing options, such as for seniors and students, will also be encouraged as the population swells. Criticisms of previous plans included concerns that a focus on single-family homes encouraged sprawl and did not meet all needs in the real estate market.
The opposition New Democrats said the province "continues to ignore" key recommendations from its own housing accountability task force, such as gently increasing density in all neighbourhoods.
"Instead, the Ford government has chosen to keep enriching land speculators who profit from unsustainable sprawl and farmland loss," NDP Leader Marit Stiles added in a statement. "It means people looking for more affordable options like semi-detached, townhome or fourplex apartments will continue to be unfairly shut out of the neighbourhoods where they want to live."
Liberal MPP and housing critic Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said Calandra's change does nothing but "tinker with policies ... while young people and seniors struggle to afford homes."
Housing construction delays caused by the Greenbelt scandal, pandemic-related shortages of construction materials and high interest rates have put Ford's Progressive Conservatives at risk of missing their target of building 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
The changes come just four months after Calandra brought in sweeping legislation called the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act.
Calandra's new rules follow three rounds of consultations in the last two years that included Indigenous communities and input from 21 Ontario government ministries with an eye to protecting agricultural areas and planning for infrastructure.
He said housing approvals are already on the upswing in several cities, such as London, which has green-lighted 10,000 so far this year, compared with 5,300 in all of 2023.
CAPTION: Criticism of Housing Minister Paul Calandra's previous plans included concerns that a focus on single-family homes encouraged sprawl.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Interest rates; Single family; Planning; Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Interest rates Housing starts

Title: Province releases streamlined plan:   New rules for municipalities aim to boost construction, move focus from single-family homes

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 21, 2024

Document 62 of 500

Canadian ambassador touts value of U.S. ties: Federal officials seek to connect with party as it develops foreign policy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-ambassador-touts-value-u-s-ties/docview/3095020582/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+ambassador+touts+value+of+U.S.+ties%3A+Federal+officials+seek+to+connect+with+party+as+it+develops+foreign+policy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's ambassador to the United States says she's telling Americans about the important relationship between the neighbours as Democrats develop their foreign policy and build unity behind U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris at the party's national convention.
Kirsten Hillman says she is talking with Democrats about how Canada makes the U.S. more resilient, secure and prosperous.
"The relationship with Canada makes the United States strong," Hillman said at the convention Monday.
Thousands of party faithful and politicians have gathered for the four-day event to build on the excitement over Harris since she quickly ascended to the top of this fall's election ticket.
Harris made a surprise appearance Monday night to thank Joe Biden before the president made a speech where he reflected on his administration's legacy and passed the leadership baton to his vice-president. Former president Barack Obama and wife Michelle are scheduled to address supporters Tuesday evening.
Democrats say speeches will compare a hopeful future under Harris with what the party calls Republican nominee Donald Trump's plan to take America backward.
Meanwhile, Harris and her running mate, Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, were scheduled to hold a rally in Milwaukee on Tuesday, hoping to shore up supporters in the important battleground state as the race to the November election heats up. She is expected to return to Chicago late in the evening.
Hillman is among a handful of Canadians looking to connect with as many lawmakers as possible during the convergence of Democrats this week. Liberal MP John McKay, co-chair of the Canada-U.S. interparliamentary group, was also attending the convention.
Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet also planned to make new contacts and attend meetings during the gathering.
Speeches at the event have not offered insight into whether Harris would take a different approach to trade with Canada, but experts have said she's likely to follow the path forged by Biden.
"The challenge for Canada is, as it always has been, to make sure that Americans know that the policy of job creation in the United States is not enhanced by cutting off effective supply chains with Canada," Hillman said Monday.
On the contrary, Hillman added, deepening supply chains and bilateral relationships makes both economies more resilient, self-sufficient and effective.
Hillman also attended the Republican National Convention in Milwaukee last month where she met with senators, representatives in Congress and members of Trump's previous administration.
The looming review of the Canada-United States-Mexico trade agreement in 2026 hangs heavy over Canadian observers of the presidential campaign.
During his presidency, Trump forced a renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement. Harris was one of 10 U.S. senators to vote against the revamped trade agreement, saying it didn't do enough to protect American workers or the environment.
Trump has threatened more tariffs and less aid to Ukraine if he wins a second term in office.
While Biden's tenure brought some stability to the relationship with Canada, there was also tension over his administration's Buy American procurement rules.
Softwood lumber and Canada's digital services tax are key areas of contention for both Republicans and Democrats.
Hillman said Harris, and especially Walz, have a specific understanding of the U.S.-Canada relationship.
Walz's state shares an 885-kilometre border with Canada along Ontario and Manitoba. Hillman said he goes to Canada Day celebrations in his state. "He is a great enthusiast when it comes to Canada."
Harris spent part of her youth living in Montreal and has a significant relationship with Canada.
"She has a high level of understanding of our country and that's important," Hillman said. "It doesn't solve every problem, it never will, but it certainly helps."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: It's not yet clear that Democratic presidential candidate U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris would take a different approach to trade with Canada, but experts have said she's likely to follow the path forged by President Joe Biden.E.JasonWambsganschicagotribune/ tribune newsservice
CREDIT: Kelly Geraldine Malone The Canadian Press

Document 63 of 500

Behind on its housing target, Ford government takes more steps to boost construction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/behind-on-housing-target-ford-government-takes/docview/3095248017/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+on+its+housing+target%2C+Ford+government+takes+more+steps+to+boost+construction&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Reeling from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP, Premier Doug Ford's government is taking another stab at helping municipalities build homes faster to ease a housing shortage.
New rules taking effect Oct. 20 will encourage building near major transit stations and on underused and low-density properties such as strip plazas and shopping malls, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said Tuesday.
More housing options, such as for seniors and students, will also be encouraged as the population swells. Criticisms of previous plans included concerns that a focus on single-family homes encouraged sprawl and did not meet all needs in the real estate market.
"Even though housing starts have been impacted by global uncertainty and high interest rates, we are laying the groundwork for a long-term building boom as economic circumstances improve," Calandra told mayors, councillors and officials from across the province at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa.
The new rules are in a revised and streamlined provincial planning statement - the guidebook for municipalities as they plan future development - that at 20,517 words is 60 per cent shorter than the previous 51,177-word version.
"It will ensure one consistent set of planning direction across the province," Calandra added.
"It recognizes that municipalities understand local challenges and priorities when it comes to building homes...it is you who know best what you should be building."
The opposition New Democrats said the province "continues to ignore" key recommendations from its own housing accountability task force, such as gently increasing density in all neighbourhoods.
"Instead, the Ford government has chosen to keep enriching land speculators who profit from unsustainable sprawl and farmland loss," NDP Leader Marit Stiles added in a statement.
"It means people looking for more affordable options like semi-detached, townhome or fourplex apartments will continue to be unfairly shut out of the neighbourhoods where they want to live."
Liberal MPP and housing critic Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said Calandra's change does nothing but "tinker with policies ... while young people and seniors struggle to afford homes."
Housing construction delays caused by the Greenbelt scandal, pandemic-related shortages of construction materials and high interest rates have put Ford's Progressive Conservatives at risk of missing their target of building 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
The changes come just four months after Calandra brought in sweeping legislation called The Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act.
Among other things, it eliminated parking requirements for high-density housing projects near transit stations, streamlined approvals for garden suites, laneway housing and basement apartments, and prioritized roads and waterworks for ready-to-go projects.
Calandra's new rules follow three rounds of consultations in the last two years that included Indigenous communities, and input from 21 Ontario government ministries with an eye to protecting agricultural areas and planning for infrastructure such as roads, hydro and gas lines.
He said housing approvals are already on the upswing in several cities, such as London, which has green-lighted 10,000 so far this year, compared with 5,300 in all of 2023.
Calandra credited Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath, a former leader of Ontario's NDP, for using strong mayor powers to transform parking lots into affordable housing.
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks to reporters at the Ontario Legislature in Toronto on March 7, 2024.

Document 64 of 500

Here's the most galling part of Doug Ford's safe injection site ban

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-most-galling-part-doug-fords-safe-injection/docview/3095247983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+the+most+galling+part+of+Doug+Ford%27s+safe+injection+site+ban&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After the provincial government's announcement that it will force the closure of more than half the supervised consumption drug sites in the province, including five in Toronto, can we start by acknowledging that we'd pretty much all prefer such places didn't need to exist?
They're not easy to have in your neighbourhood. They can wind up surrounded by desperate and dangerous characters, their very existence is a sad acknowledgment of our failings, as human individuals and as a society. The approach they are part of is called "harm reduction," a name that acknowledges the real harm still caused by what takes place in and around them.
Pretty much all of us would prefer that no one be so addicted to harmful substances as to require supervision to prevent a fatal overdose. Pretty much all of us would prefer that those who wind up addicted would seek successful treatment rather than continue using. Pretty much all of us would prefer there be no demand at all for these sites.
But we don't live in the world we prefer. And in this one, the choice is not between consumption sites and an addiction-free environment.
These places exist because our choice is between desperation under supervision or death on our neighbourhood streets. They exist because they are proven to save lives.
A study published in The Lancet covered in the Star earlier this year found that, in the year after Toronto safe consumption sites opened, overdose deaths in the immediate vicinity of them (within 500 metres) dropped 67 per cent.
The administrator of two Toronto sites told the Star they have reversed 776 overdoses within the past year. Overdoses that likely would have happened anyway.
As Gillian Kolla, a Memorial University substance abuse expert told the Star, the sites in Toronto were chosen in locations where data showed drug use was already happening. "Before the South Riverdale site was opened, people were dying in the alleys behind the centre because there wasn't a safe place to go," Kolla said.
When the centre closes, we can guess people will go back to dying in alleys. We don't want them to, but that's what seems likely to happen.
So Premier Doug Ford can spare us his stated preference for treatment over safe-injection and supervised consumption sites, because it is a false dichotomy. And because we all share that preference. But some addicts are not at a point where they are seeking treatment, and they don't magically go away if you force them outdoors into back alleys.
Moreover, many who seek treatment cannot find it.
This is the most galling part of the provincial government's rhetoric here. Here's Ford in 2018: "I don't believe in safe-injection areas, as I call them. I believe in supporting people, getting them help." Great. Six years of his alleged leadership on the issue later, we learned earlier this year that wait times for addiction treatment stretch as long as one year.
Here's the thing: if these sites were closing because the provincial government's addiction treatment supports were so successful that there was no more demand for supervised consumption sites and the risk of overdoses had disappeared, I think virtually everyone would be cheering. But that isn't what's happening.
Certainly, the province is announcing $378 million in funding for treatment and recovery centres, including some supportive housing units, alongside its new restrictions on safe consumption sites. That new funding is welcome. But no one can expect it is likely to immediately solve the problem the consumption sites are meant to address.
By all means, please spend more on treatment, and on counselling and outreach services that help people find their way into treatment. I think there are few among us who are not in favour of that aspect of the government's approach.
Like with emergency homeless shelters and food banks, we'd all prefer to see the underlying problem resolved so that safe consumption sites aren't needed anymore. But like in those other cases, the underlying problems only seem to have recently gotten worse.
And taking away such life-saving approaches to the worst symptoms of societal problems while demand for them is still high is worse than ineffective. It's cruel.
It is like taking a life preserver away from an actively drowning person because you strongly prefer they learn how to swim. Even if you're investing in some long-term learn-to-swim programs at the same time, taking the flotation devices away from those currently thrashing away in the water is only going to kill people.
With files from David Rider.
CAPTION: A sharps box outside of South Riverdale Community Health Centre, one of the safe injection sites in Toronto. The Ford government announced Tuesday that it is closing most safe injection sites in the province by forcing them 200 metres away from schools and daycares.

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Homeless people; Substance abuse treatment

Title: Here's the most galling part of Doug Ford's safe injection site ban

Document 65 of 500

Province's decision to close several Toronto supervised drug sites divides opinions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/provinces-decision-close-several-toronto/docview/3095020864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province%27s+decision+to+close+several+Toronto+supervised+drug+sites+divides+opinions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province's decision to shutter half of Toronto's supervised drug consumption sites is being met with mixed reaction from residents and city officials, with some predicting the move will provide relief to beleaguered communities near the facilities, and others warning it will only lead to more deaths amid an already devastating opioid crisis.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced Tuesday the province will ban supervised injection sites within 200 metres of schools or childcare centres. As a result, five of the ten facilities operating in Toronto must cease operation by March 31, 2025. Five more outside the city will also close.
At a city hall news conference, Toronto Board of Health Chair Chris Moise welcomed the province's commitment as part of the announcement of $378 million for new treatment facilities. But he declared that "closing supervised consumption services will not stop people from using drugs."
Coun. Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre) said that "if sites are forced to close, we will see more public drug use in our parks and in our laneways.
"We will see more discarded needles on our streets. We will see increased risk of transmission of infectious diseases due to shared needles such as HIV and Hep C, and we will see more of overdoses and deaths."
In a statement, a spokesperson for Mayor Olivia Chow also described the new treatment funding as "badly needed," but said the mayor was "concerned" closing the sites would have "significant consequences," including more overdose deaths and greater strain on emergency rooms.
Jones rejected the idea the closures will cause a spike in fatalities, telling reporters after her speech to the Association of Municipalities of Ontario in Ottawa: "People are not going to die, they're going to get access to service."
Premier Doug Ford's government says it's imposing the distance restrictions to address safety concerns around supervised consumption sites, which it says have fuelled an increase in nearby crime.
One of the facilities being shut down is at the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, where Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed last July. Police say a dispute erupted outside the building and Huebner-Makurat, 44, was hit by a stray bullet.
Jeri Brown, one of the residents who has been organizing about the site, said Tuesday she was pleased it will close. She argued it never should have been allowed to open close to a school, but she hoped the decision "will come with a full plan in place to actually provide real support" to people with addictions.
"This was never about trying to NIMBY away people who need help. This was about let's be really smart about it so that we keep everyone safe," she said.
East-end resident Joni Morgen also welcomed the news, telling the Star: "I'm glad I won't have to pick up more needles from the schoolyard," while displaying a photo of needles strewn outside a local school.
However, other local residents predicted needles will still be left on the ground, and there might even be more because provincially funded treatment sites poised to replace safe injection services can't offer needle exchanges.
Jamie Graham, an east-end resident and client support worker at a safe consumption site, got emotional talking about the impacts of withdrawing vital services to help keep people healthy amid a deadly overdose epidemic.
"People who use these sites are human beings with community, people who love them - closing sites is going to cause a massive amount of preventable death and tragedy," said Graham.
"Their safety, their lives are not being considered when we talk about community safety. They are not being thought of as the valuable people they are."
Outside the Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre at Queen Street West and Bathurst Street, which hosts another supervised site set to close, Mark Hawkins said he and many others rely on the service. Locations in Regent Park and Kensington Market, as well as the Works on Victoria Street, are also on the chopping block.
"They can't close these places because there's so many people that actually need these facilities. And there's got to be more information and more understanding as to the fact of how to help them and how we get people away from their addictions," Hawkins said.
The new provincial treatment funding will go to 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs across Ontario. Four of the five Toronto sites slated for closure are provincially funded and will be encouraged to transition to one of the hubs.
The province said the sites could be eligible for up to four times more funding under the new model, which would offer primary, mental and addiction health care, as well as shelter and transition beds - but no "harm reduction" services such as needle exchanges or supervised injection.
Angela Robertson, who oversees the site at Bathurst and Queen Street West, and another near Queen Street West and Dufferin Avenue that will stay open, said more funding for treatment won't replace supervised consumption service. She worried the province is reviving policies that were deemed ineffective decades ago, and that will put drug users at risk by making it more likely they will share needles and other supplies.
In a statement, Toronto Medical Officer of Health Dr. Eileen De Villa said Toronto Public Health was still reviewing the new provincial policy, but that it has advocated for a range of responses, including harm reduction, prevention, and treatment.
Earlier this year, the agency released preliminary figures that showed 523 people died from opioid toxicity in 2023, a 74 per cent increase from 2019.
With files from Mark Ramzy.
CAPTION: Jeri Brown, one of the residents who has been organizing against asupervised consumption site in Leslieville, said Tuesday she was pleased it will close. She argued it never should have been allowed to open close to a school, but she hoped the decision "will come with a full plan in place to actually provide real support" to people with addictions.South Riverdale Community Health Centre, one of the safe injection sites the Ford government is closing due to its proximity to schools.

Document 66 of 500

Doug Ford's new zoning restrictions could shut down most safe injection sites in Ontario, including 5 in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-new-zoning-restrictions-could-shut/docview/3094811033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+new+zoning+restrictions+could+shut+down+most+safe+injection+sites+in+Ontario%2C+including+5+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford will introduce new zoning restrictions to supervised drug consumption facilities that could close most of Ontario's safe injection sites.
After March 31, 2025, the sites will have to be at least 200 metres from a school or child-care centre, which would appear to affect 10 of the 17 provincially regulated consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites across Ontario, including five in Toronto.
The changes, which were first reported by the Toronto Sun, will be announced by Health Minister Sylvia Jones on Tuesday afternoon at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa.
The Progressive Conservative government confirmed to the Star there would be a 200-metre limit and that the province will earmark $378 million in new funding for recovery centres.
As well, new "treatment hubs" will be announced by Jones.
The Star reported Monday that the Tories wanted to move away from supervised injection sites to provide more of an emphasis on treatment and rehabilitation for people with addictions.
"I'll be very frank, I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around - it's a haven for drug dealers," Ford, who has spoken candidly in the past about the ravages of addiction in his own family, said in Thunder Bay earlier this month.
"Let's get these people the support they need and build more detox beds and I know our ministers will be making announcements shortly regarding that," the premier said Aug. 9.
Angela Robertson, executive director at Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre, said Monday that she expected the new 200-metre rule would force the closure of the safe consumption site at her Parkdale facility.
Robertson said the Ford government decision could result in the deaths of drug users who would otherwise survive.
Two sites she oversees reversed 766 overdoses last year.
"If any of those people had overdosed in a public washroom or another place without immediate response, they likely would have died ... If this goes through we'll see a further increase of overdose deaths and they'll be preventable deaths," she said.
The zoning changes are not a surprise - Ford has privately wondered to aides why CTS locations shouldn't face at least the same restrictions as provincially regulated cannabis stores, which under Ontario law must be at least 150 metres from schools.
Toronto's first supervised consumption service opened in 2017 and there are now 10 here, mostly downtown.
Six are provincially designated CTS centres that receive funding from the Ontario government. Five of those are expected to be affected by Ford's move.
All the facilities require an exemption to the federal Controlled Drugs and Substances Act granted by Health Canada.
The city of Toronto has been supportive of safe injection sites, pointing to studies showing the facilities save lives because they prevent overdoses while reducing the spread of disease and helping drug users access social services and treatment.
While Mayor Olivia Chow backs them, she welcomed the provincial review of the facilities Queen's Park announced last August weeks after the shooting death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat.
Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two, was killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre CTS in July 2023.
Two men were arrested and charged and a third suspect is believed to have fled Canada after the shooting. The men were in a dispute at Queen Street East and Carlaw Avenue when she was struck.
"We need a place where it's safe inside, but it's also safe outside. The residents deserve no less," Chow said at the time.
According to preliminary figures released by Toronto Public Health earlier this year, 523 people died from opioid toxicity in the city in 2023.
That's a 74 per cent increase from the 301 deaths in 2019.
At the six CTS provincially funded sites, there have been 7,444 non-fatal overdoses since 2017 with trained staff supervising drug consumption.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: The changes proposed by the government will be announced by Health Minister Sylvia Jones on Tuesday afternoon.Premier Doug Ford's government is expected to announce restrictions to supervised drug consumption sites.

Subject: Drug overdose; Shootings; Zoning ordinances

Language of publication: English

Document 67 of 500

Doug Ford is having a funny summer, but his Greenbelt woes are no laughing matter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-having-funny-summer-his-greenbelt/docview/3094811031/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+having+a+funny+summer%2C+but+his+Greenbelt+woes+are+no+laughing+matter&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is having one funny summer.
Funny as in politically peculiar - without having much fun or hearing many laughs from the home crowd.
First, he appealed to people not to "go pooping" on Wasaga Beach. It made late-night TV in the U.S. as a laugh line, but locals squirmed.
Then Ford joked that a new animal hospital in King City could reduce lineups for humans needing health care. His ribbon-cutting audience was polite, but the line fell flat in Ontario's overcrowded hospitals.
After the negative headlines, the premier was poised to party last Friday night, giving out free food and drinks to a "Ford Fest" crowd in Milton. But just before party time, it was quitting time for popular education minister Todd Smith, who left a big hole in cabinet by bailing out on short notice.
If the summer of 2024 feels unfunny, the summer of 2025 might be that much more miserable. For all the fuss about Ford's latest wisecracks in the news media, there is bigger (and badder) news behind the scenes.
The premier must be - should be - paying attention.
As the Star's Robert Benzie has revealed, a quiet RCMP investigation is fast gaining momentum, gathering critical statements about alleged wrongdoing in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt imbroglio of two summers ago. By next summer, rather than parsing the premier's laugh lines and press lines, the media may be writing more about the police line of inquiry at Queen's Park.
Interviews with members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that probes political crimes and corruption, are taking place in the Greater Toronto Area. While the force remains tight-lipped, sources say that current and former staff in Ford's Progressive Conservative government are systematically being questioned about the controversial land swap.
The probe comes in the wake of a devastating report by the provincial auditor general last summer, which placed an $8.28-billion value on the windfall from Ford's Tories opening up previously protected lands to development. Ford has gone back and forth on the Greenbelt - first declaring open season on its protected lands in early 2018, then reversing himself pre-election, then doing a U-turn upon re-election in 2022, and finally backing off again last year after the auditor's bombshell report landed.
For all his zigzags, Ford's apologia eased the public pressure - his polling numbers bounced back to record highs. But the police pressure isn't going away.
Indeed, the RCMP probe appears to be a major consideration behind Ford's remarkable game plan to call an election as early as next spring. That would be more than a year ahead of the fixed election date mandated by law for June 2026.
What political pretext could Ford's Tories conjure up to seek a fresh mandate? How could a government that won re-election with a massive majority a mere two years ago justify going back into pre-election mode so early in the legislative cycle?
Ford's priority, bizarrely, is the boutique issue of beer in corner stores, given his determination to "keep the promise" he made in 2018 after allowing it to languish for so many years. That decision came at considerable cost - hundreds of millions of dollars to pacify The Beer Store (and the big brewers that own it) in order to move up the 2026 end date for a previous contract governing beer sales.
There are other distortions and contortions tied to Ford's fear of an RCMP probe. Remember that the premier came to power by promising to clean up what he claimed was corruption by the previous Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne giving preferential treatment to insiders and political friends, though he never produced any evidence; now the tables may be turned on the Tories.
The allegations of racketeering and distractions of electioneering are stressing out the Tories, and the loss of influential cabinet ministers doesn't help: Todd Smith's departure will weaken the government at the very time it is trying to put its best face(s) forward.
As Ontario's long-serving energy minister, he kept the Tories out of trouble by disarming the opposition and stakeholders alike. But Smith was reluctant to soldier on when shuffled to the education portfolio this summer.
The ministerial heavyweights who left before him - Monte McNaughton, Rod Phillips and Christine Elliott - have also left big gaps at the cabinet table and on the front benches. In their places, Ford has opted for quantity over quality, appointing the most bloated cabinet in the province's history with a record 37 members.
Ford may yet prove the doubters wrong, romping to yet another election victory next spring. Perhaps he'll be sitting pretty by next summer, should the RCMP come calling for top Tories in and out of government.
But it doesn't sound like much fun.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a news conference in Oshawa on Aug. 1, 2024.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Education portfolios; Crowds; Summer; Politics

Document 68 of 500

What Doug Ford's government gets wrong about Ontario's homelessness crisis - and why it's a problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-doug-fords-government-gets-wrong-about/docview/3094811016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+Doug+Ford%27s+government+gets+wrong+about+Ontario%27s+homelessness+crisis+-+and+why+it%27s+a+problem&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Ontario's new deputy housing minister assumed the role this summer, he was given an internal document briefing him on the housing crisis. That document included a staggering figure the government now concedes is false - an "unofficial" estimate that nearly a quarter of a million Ontarians are homeless.
The 234,000-person "unofficial" estimate is repeated twice in the footnotes of the transition documents prepared for MPP Vijay Thanigasalam in June. First reported by the Trillium, it's a figure that bewildered experts - if true, it would suggest more than 95 per cent of Ontario's homeless population is either living outside Toronto or off the radar, as the city's own count in June sat at 10,627 people.
Now, as the province walks back from its estimate, experts are stressing that elected officials need to know how bad the housing crisis truly is in order to solve it.
"If you have a major problem and you want to address it, then you need to be able to track how bad it is - to see if it's getting better or worse, and if what you're doing is working or not," said Stephen Hwang, director of Unity Health's MAP Centre for Urban Health Solutions.
The suggestion that 234,000 Ontarians could be homeless ignited outrage last week, with opposing politicians accusing the government of allowing the problem to explode from an estimated 21,000 people in 2018, according to a provincial Auditor General's report.
"It is quite simply terrible that 234,000 Ontarians are homeless. That number is NINE times higher than the Auditor General's earlier estimate," MPP Jessica Bell, the NDP housing critic, wrote on X last week.
Pressed for detail on how the province reached that figure, the housing ministry has confirmed to the Star the count had combined homelessness tallies with other data, such as 150,000-plus households who reported to Statistics Canada spending more than half their income on housing.
"The 234,000 number does not represent he number of people experiencing homelessness in Ontario, which is significantly lower," spokesperson Justine Teplycky confirmed, pointing to a much smaller, more official count of those receiving social assistance payments while facing homelessness - 31,816 people as of June. Teplycky's statement did not account for the number of people who are homeless but not receiving social assistance payments, which a Toronto-based street survey from 2021 indicates is around 42 per cent.
Hwang was among those mystified by the province's 234,000-person estimate - describing it as "extraordinarily high," with all data he'd seen suggesting the population was in the tens, not hundreds, of thousands.
That wasn't to say the problem was less urgent or hadn't been expanding, he cautioned. While some homelessness is highly visible, official counts also are complicated by "hidden homelessness" - those relying on stopgap options like couch-surfing as well as those who don't interact with sites like shelters and respites.
The City of Toronto details the size of its known homeless population by counting how many people have used its services within three months and haven't moved into permanent homes. From June 2021 to June 2024, that population increased from 8,037 to 10,627 people.
The Star has meanwhile identified further data in the document that raises questions. In Toronto, the document cites 15,000 people on the wait-list for supportive housing, a form of affordable housing with embedded social services. But the true wait-list is nearly twice that size, at 28,116 people, according to The Access Point, the organization that oversees it, and previous data shared with the Star suggests it has been over 20,000 people since at least the spring of 2021.
Asked about the discrepancy, the housing ministry referred the Star to its associate minister of mental health and addictions, Michael Tibollo. Emails to a spokesperson for Tibollo and the health ministry went unanswered.
Andrew Boozary, executive director of social medicine at Toronto's University Health Network, shares Hwang's belief that accurate data is essential to tackle the issue. He also fears an oversized estimate of Ontario's homeless population risks the problem being seen as simply too large to fix.
"It's really important we get the magnitude of this issue right, instead of chalking it up to an intractable problem," he said.
CAPTION: An internal document meant to brief Ontario's new deputy housing minister contained inaccurate data on the homelessness crisis, Doug Ford's government says.

Subject: Homeless people

Title: What Doug Ford's government gets wrong about Ontario's homelessness crisis - and why it's a problem

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 20, 2024

Section: news/gta

A change to safe consumption sites + what the Ford government gets wrong about the homelessness crisis

Document 70 of 500

Ford expected to announce changes: Advocates fear province may be planning to pull funding and shutter facilities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-expected-announce-changes/docview/3094684025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+expected+to+announce+changes%3A+Advocates+fear+province+may+be+planning+to+pull+funding+and+shutter+facilities&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is expected to announce restrictions to supervised drug consumption sites and more of an emphasis on treatment and rehabilitation for people with addictions.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones is to speak to the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) on Tuesday afternoon in Ottawa.
A city hall source said the municipality is expecting an announcement that could affect the future of supervised consumption services in Toronto.
Ford, who has spoken candidly about how addiction has ravaged his own family in the past, is signalling there need to be changes around Ontario's 17 provincially regulated consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites.
"I'll be very frank, I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around," the premier said in Thunder Bay this month.
"Let's get these people the support they need and build more detox beds," Ford said Aug. 9.
Along with a renewed focus on weaning users off hard drugs, the premier has privately wondered to aides why CTS locations shouldn't face the same zoning restrictions as provincially regulated cannabis stores, which under Ontario law must be at least 150 metres from schools.
Toronto's first supervised consumption service opened in 2017. There are now 10 in the city.
Six are provincially designated CTS centres that receive funding from the Ontario government. In order to operate, all the facilities need an exemption to the federal Controlled Drugs and Substances Act granted by Health Canada.
The provincially funded locations also have to meet standards set out by the Ontario government and are subject to inspections from Toronto Public Health, which directly operates one supervised consumption service, at the Works at 277 Victoria St.
The city's position is that evidence from across Canada and around the world shows the facilities save lives because they prevent overdoses, reduce the spread of infections diseases, and connect drug users to social services and treatment.
Mayor Olivia Chow has been supportive, and while campaigning in the 2023 byelection vowed to dedicate funding to the sites.
However, Chow welcomed the provincial review of the facilities Queen's Park announced in August 2023 after the death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre CTS in July 2023.
Advocates for harm reduction have been waiting anxiously for the province's announcement.
Angela Robertson, executive director of the Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre, which operates two locations in the city's downtown west side, said if the decision is to see funding revoked and sites shuttered, the immediate impact will be preventable deaths.
Gillian Kolla, assistant professor in the faculty of medicine at Memorial University and an expert in the overdose crisis, said the concern has been that the province has been "looking for an excuse" to pull funding in the midst of an ongoing overdose crisis.
The opioid overdose crisis, fuelled by the ready availability of the highly potent drug fentanyl in recent years, has continued to wreak havoc across the province since the first supervised consumption site opened in Toronto in 2017.
At the six CTS provincially funded sites, there have been 7,444 non-fatal overdoses since 2017 with trained staff supervising drug consumption and intervening as necessary. There has never been a death at a supervised consumption site in Canada.
As Ford strongly hints that reforms are coming, municipal leaders have been urging Queen's Park for more help on mental health, addiction and homelessness.
Cambridge Mayor Jan Liggett commissioned a provincewide poll, which she shared with the Star, that was distributed Sunday at AMO.
It found 78 per cent of respondents want the province to set aside specific funding for mental-health and addiction services.
Liggett, whose city does not have a CTS site, said she hoped the survey by Campaign Research, the same polling firm used by Ford's Tories, would give "guidance" to elected officials as they wrestle with what to do about addiction in their communities.
Queried about what "should be prioritized with new funding," Campaign Research found 34 per cent felt youth and adult mental-health services, 26 per cent for mental-health crisis intervention services, 15 per cent for rehabilitation programs and treatment facilities, nine per cent for community supports such as Alcoholics Anonymous and Narcotics Anonymous and eight per cent for in-patient detox centres.
Fully 45 per cent said "governments must spend much more on mental health and addiction services to make significant progress on getting ahead of this ever-growing problem" while 33 per cent felt "the overall problem isn't a lack of funding" and 22 per cent were unsure.
Campaign Research surveyed 2,027 Ontarians July 30-31 using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
With files from
Jennifer Pagliaro
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Toronto Star Queen's Park Bureau Chief Ben Spurr Toronto Star

Document 71 of 500

Premier's Greenbelt woes no laughing matter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-greenbelt-woes-no-laughing-matter/docview/3094683681/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier%27s+Greenbelt+woes+no+laughing+matter&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is having one funny summer.
Funny as in politically peculiar - without having much fun or hearing many laughs from the home crowd.
First, he appealed to people not to "go pooping" on Wasaga Beach. It made late-night TV in the U.S. as a laugh line, but locals squirmed.
Then Ford joked that a new animal hospital in King City could reduce lineups for humans needing health care. His ribbon-cutting audience was polite, but the line fell flat in Ontario's overcrowded hospitals.
After the negative headlines, the premier was poised to party last Friday night, giving out free food and drinks to a "Ford Fest" crowd in Milton. But just before party time, it was quitting time for popular education minister Todd Smith, who left a big hole in cabinet by bailing out on short notice.
If the summer of 2024 feels unfunny, the summer of 2025 might be that much more miserable. For all the fuss about Ford's latest wisecracks in the news media, there is bigger (and badder) news behind the scenes.
The premier must be - should be - paying attention.
As the Star's Robert Benzie has revealed, a quiet RCMP investigation is fast gaining momentum, gathering critical statements about alleged wrongdoing in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt imbroglio of two summers ago. By next summer, rather than parsing the premier's laugh lines and press lines, the media may be writing more about the police line of inquiry at Queen's Park.
Interviews with members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that probes political crimes and corruption, are taking place in the Greater Toronto Area. While the force remains tight-lipped, sources say that current and former staff in Ford's Progressive Conservative government are systematically being questioned about the controversial land swap.
The probe comes in the wake of a devastating report by the provincial auditor general last summer, which placed an $8.28-billion value on the windfall from Ford's Tories opening up previously protected lands to development. Ford has gone back and forth on the Greenbelt - first declaring open season on its protected lands in early 2018, then reversing himself pre-election, then doing a U-turn upon re-election in 2022, and finally backing off again last year after the auditor's bombshell report landed.
For all his zigzags, Ford's apologia eased the public pressure - his polling numbers bounced back to record highs. But the police pressure isn't going away.
Indeed, the RCMP probe appears to be a major consideration behind Ford's remarkable game plan to call an election as early as next spring. That would be more than a year ahead of the fixed election date mandated by law for June 2026.
What political pretext could Ford's Tories conjure up to seek a fresh mandate? How could a government that won re-election with a massive majority a mere two years ago justify going back into pre-election mode so early in the legislative cycle?
Ford's priority, bizarrely, is the boutique issue of beer in corner stores, given his determination to "keep the promise" he made in 2018 after allowing it to languish for so many years. That decision came at considerable cost - hundreds of millions of dollars to pacify the Beer Store (and the big brewers that own it) in order to move up the 2026 end date for a previous contract governing beer sales.
There are other distortions and contortions tied to Ford's fear of an RCMP probe. Remember that the premier came to power by promising to clean up what he claimed was corruption by the previous Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne giving preferential treatment to insiders and political friends, though he never produced any evidence; now the tables may be turned on the Tories.
The allegations of racketeering and distractions of electioneering are stressing out the Tories, and the loss of influential cabinet ministers doesn't help: Smith's departure will weaken the government at the very time it is trying to put its best face(s) forward.
As Ontario's long-serving energy minister, he kept the Tories out of trouble by disarming the opposition and stakeholders alike. But Smith was reluctant to soldier on when shuffled to the education portfolio this summer.
The ministerial heavyweights who left before him - Monte McNaughton, Rod Phillips and Christine Elliott - have also left big gaps at the cabinet table and on the front benches. In their places, Ford has opted for quantity over quality, appointing the most bloated cabinet in the province's history with a record 37 members.
Ford may yet prove the doubters wrong, romping to yet another election victory next spring. Perhaps he'll be sitting pretty by next summer, should the RCMP come calling for top Tories in and out of government.
But it doesn't sound like much fun.
CAPTION: A quiet RCMP investigation into alleged wrongdoing in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal of two summers ago is fast gaining momentum, Martin Regg Cohn writes.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 72 of 500

When Ontario s new deputy housing minister assumed the role ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/when-ontario-s-new-deputy-housing-minister/docview/3094683641/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Ontario+s+new+deputy+housing+minister+assumed+the+role+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Ontario's new deputy housing minister assumed the role this summer, he was given an internal document briefing him on the housing crisis. That document included a staggering figure the government now concedes is false - an "unofficial" estimate that nearly a quarter of a million Ontarians are homeless.
The 234,000-person "unofficial" estimate is repeated twice in the footnotes of the transition documents prepared for MPP Vijay Thanigasalam in June. First reported by the Trillium, it's a figure that bewildered experts - if true, it would suggest more than 95 per cent of Ontario's homeless population is either living outside Toronto or off the radar, as the city's own count in June sat at 10,627 people.
Now, as the province walks back from its estimate, experts are stressing that elected officials need to know how bad the housing crisis truly is in order to solve it.
"If you have a major problem and you want to address it, then you need to be able to track how bad it is - to see if it's getting better or worse, and if what you're doing is working or not," said Stephen Hwang, director of Unity Health's MAP Centre for Urban Health Solutions.
The suggestion that 234,000 Ontarians could be homeless ignited outrage last week, with opposing politicians accusing the government of allowing the problem to explode from an estimated 21,000 people in 2018, according to a provincial auditor general's report.
"It is quite simply terrible that 234,000 Ontarians are homeless. That number is NINE times higher than the auditor general's earlier estimate," MPP Jessica Bell, the NDP housing critic, wrote on X last week.
Pressed for detail on how the province reached that figure, the Housing Ministry has confirmed to the Star the count had combined homelessness tallies with other data, such as 150,000-plus households who reported to Statistics Canada spending more than half their income on housing.
"The 234,000 number does not represent the number of people experiencing homelessness in Ontario, which is significantly lower," spokesperson Justine Teplycky confirmed, pointing to a much smaller, more official count of those receiving social assistance payments while facing homelessness - 31,816 people as of June. Teplycky's statement did not account for the number of people who are homeless but not receiving social assistance payments, which a Toronto-based street survey from 2021 indicates is around 42 per cent.
Hwang was among those mystified by the province's 234,000-person estimate - describing it as "extraordinarily high," with all data he'd seen suggesting the population was in the tens, not hundreds, of thousands.
That wasn't to say the problem was less urgent or hadn't been expanding, he cautioned. While some homelessness is highly visible, official counts also are complicated by "hidden homelessness" - those relying on stopgap options like couch-surfing as well as those who don't interact with sites like shelters and respites.
The City of Toronto details the size of its known homeless population by counting how many people have used its services within three months and haven't moved into permanent homes. From June 2021 to June 2024, that population increased from 8,037 to 10,627 people.
The Star has identified further data in the provincial document that raises questions. In Toronto, the document cites 15,000 people on the wait-list for subsidized housing, a form of affordable housing with embedded social services. But the true wait-list is nearly twice that size, at 28,116 people, according to the Access Point, the organization that oversees it, and previous data shared with the Star suggests it has been over 20,000 people since at least the spring of 2021.
Asked about the discrepancy, the housing ministry referred the Star to its associate minister of mental health and addictions, Michael Tibollo. Emails to a spokesperson for Tibollo and the Health Ministry went unanswered.
Andrew Boozary, executive director of social medicine at Toronto's University Health Network, shares Hwang's belief accurate data is essential to tackle the issue. He also fears an oversized estimate of Ontario's homeless population risks the problem being seen as simply too large to fix. "It's really important we get the magnitude of this issue right, instead of chalking it up to an intractable problem," he said.
CREDIT: Victoria Gibson Toronto Star

Subject: Homeless people; Housing conditions; False information; Statistical data; Provincial government

Document 73 of 500

Ford touts successes at Ottawa summit: Critics say cities need more assistance on mental health, addiction, homelessness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-touts-successes-at-ottawa-summit/docview/3094678790/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+touts+successes+at+Ottawa+summit%3A+Critics+say+cities+need+more+assistance+on+mental+health%2C+addiction%2C+homelessness&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In possibly his final speech to civic leaders before a potential early election, Premier Doug Ford boasted that his Progressive Conservative government is delivering for municipalities.
Against the backdrop of a looming 2025 vote, his New Democratic, Liberal and Green rivals beg to differ.
"We can't make the same mistakes that previous governments made," Ford told delegates to the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference (AMO) in a campaign-style address Monday in Ottawa.
"We have to plan for the future and build the roads, highways, public transit, hospitals, homes, schools and energy infrastructure we need," said the premier, who is considering a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"That's exactly what we're doing with the most ambitious plan to build in Ontario history - we're investing nearly $100 billion to expand and improve transit, roads and highways," he said, touting the fact the province has "added more than 800,000 new jobs since we came into office in 2018."
Ford unveiled an Invest Ontario website "that will help local and international investors better connect with municipalities through our new partner portal."
"Municipalities will be able to upload information about commercial and industrial properties in their communities and provide companies with information about the property including demographic trends, market analysis and First Nations treaty areas," he said.
The Tories hope the online tool will enable businesses to navigate government red tape so they can open new operations in Ontario sooner.
"Let's build a better Ontario together. Let's get it done," said Ford, reprising his successful 2022 re-election slogan, "Get It Done."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the website was the PC "government in a nutshell."
"Municipalities are literally begging the province to deal with the housing and homelessness crisis gripping our province, and instead they're getting an unrelated online portal," said Schreiner.
Indeed, AMO president Colin Best, a Halton Region councillor, reminded the premier more help is needed from Queen's Park.
"At last year's conference, the loudest applause was for a question asking the government when it would deliver on its commitment to a new municipal code of conduct. We are still waiting for an answer," said Best.
"Municipalities are the most accountable, transparent and trusted order of government. New provincial legislation supporting municipal codes of conduct can help us keep it that way," he said.
"AMO and the province did the work on this file back in 2021, so let's 'get it done' without further delay."
Best, noting there are now 1,400 homeless encampments across Ontario, said cities and towns also need more assistance on mental health and addiction - especially related to the opioid crisis that is ravaging communities large and small.
"More than 2,500 Ontarians died from an opioid-related overdose in 2023. That is about the same number of people who are at this conference right now," he said.
"Most were employed people. They worked regular day jobs in industries like construction. They should have enjoyed normal, productive lives. The rate of opioid deaths for Indigenous people in Ontario is also four times higher than it is for the rest of the population. It is a problem that cannot be ignored."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles told AMO delegates that municipalities "deserve better" than the Tories are delivering for them.
"Under my government, the province will pay its fair share, reversing downloads and cuts and making sure municipalities have permanent, sustainable funding you can count on," said Stiles.
"I will bring the province back to the business of building housing ... an NDP government will re-upload the financial responsibility for housing, shelters and homelessness prevention," she said.
"We will at least double the supply of community housing, non-profits and co-ops."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor, credited AMO with helping to force Ford to cancel the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap - which is now under investigation by the RCMP - 11 months ago.
"You said that we can build affordable housing in this province without selling off our Greenbelt to reward rich developers so Ford backed down," said Crombie.
"He said he was 'sorry,' but he's not sorry he did it. He's sorry he got caught."
CAPTION: In a campaign-style address Monday at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa, Premier Doug Ford unveileda website intended to improve investment in municipalities.Sean KilpatrickTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Advocates call out province's homeless estimate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/advocates-call-out-provinces-homeless-estimate/docview/3094678737/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Advocates+call+out+province%27s+homeless+estimate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 75 of 500

Canada's assisted-dying law faces constitutional fight: Advocates argue people with mental disorders should have access

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-assisted-dying-law-faces-constitutional/docview/3094678598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+assisted-dying+law+faces+constitutional+fight%3A+Advocates+argue+people+with+mental+disorders+should+have+access&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A man who says he suffers from chronic and worsening mental health issues is among those launching a court challenge of the federal government's assisted dying law, which excludes people suffering solely from a mental disorder.
An application filed by Dying with Dignity in Ontario Superior Court on Monday argues that it is discriminatory to bar people with mental disorders from being eligible for an assisted death when it is available to people who suffer physically.
The organization is asking the court to immediately quash the mental-health exclusion.
Plaintiff John Scully said going to court is his last hope.
No medication, treatment or therapy has eased the post-traumatic stress disorder the former war correspondent suffers from, along with depression and anxiety. All are made worse by sleep deprivation.
"In the last 36 hours, I've had four hours (of) sleep," he said in a recent interview. "And the sleep is polluted with nasty, vicious nightmares."
At 83, Scully said his condition is worsening by the day, not only mentally but physically.
"I feel it's incumbent on me to rattle whatever cage I can, to say, not 'Look at me,' but 'Look at us.' For God's sake. Do something about it."
The Liberal government announced in February it would delay a planned expansion to the assisted-dying regime that would have allowed people with mental disorders to be considered starting in March. The expansion has been delayed until 2027.
The government cited readiness concerns from provinces and outstanding questions from psychiatrists about how clinicians could determine whether someone's mental illness could be cured.
Dying with Dignity, Scully and a third plaintiff, Claire Elyse Brosseau, argue the mental-health exclusion violates Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which says people have the right to life, liberty and security.
"There is no constitutional justification for the prolongation of the enduring and intolerable suffering of those Canadians who are eligible for MAID but for the mental-illness exclusion," the documents state.
The filing argues people with severe and incurable mental disorders have been deprived of their security, forced instead to endure prolonged suffering, and the liberty to make the deeply personal decision to end their life.
The right to equality under the law is also violated, Dying with Dignity argues.
The Liberal government passed its assisted-dying law in 2016 and updated it after a 2019 Quebec lower court ruling found it was unconstitutional to require that a person's death be reasonably foreseeable to make them eligible.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has pledged to scrap the expansion altogether if his party forms government in the next election.
Helen Long, the CEO of Dying with Dignity Canada, said it's disappointing to have to turn to the courts to force the government to enact its own legislation. She believes the delays are rooted in a lack of "political will."
Long said her organization has heard from people and families about the hardship caused by the repeated delays.
"The fact that they have been forced to wait and then given a promise and given a promise and given a promise - that's what makes it so difficult for them to continue to soldier on and wait for an assessment," she said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Toronto resident John Scully, 83, a former war correspondent, says no treatment has eased his post-traumatic stress disorder. He says his condition is worsening by the day and going to court is his last hope.Cole Burston The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Stephanie Taylor The Canadian Press

The rate of opioid deaths for Indigenous people in Ontario is also four times higher than it is for the rest of the population. It is a problem that cannot be ignored."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner will speak to AMO attendees later Monday.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives for a meeting at the Council of the Federation gathering in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Drug overdose; Homeless people; Roads & highways; Narcotics

Title: Doug Ford touts Tory achievements to municipal leaders in Ottawa

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024
