Spending $150 million for a bike path is ridiculous. City hall needs to differentiate between need-to-have and nice-to-have

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/spending-150-million-bike-path-is-ridiculous-city/docview/3093582567/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Spending+%24150+million+for+a+bike+path+is+ridiculous.+City+hall+needs+to+differentiate+between+need-to-have+and+nice-to-have&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Metrolinx wants $150 million to build 2 kilometres of bike path. Who's taking who for a ride? Aug. 14
Toronto has no money to repair public housing, Bloor-Danforth trains are creaking towards the subway graveyard, essential service response times are dismal, and on and on we go. You live here, you know what I'm talking about.
But in city hall's alternate fluffy pink universe, there's an extra $150 million to build two kilometres of super-duper bike lanes. However they spin it, that's essentially what it is.
But that's chicken feed compared to city council approving a whopping $400 million to rebuild the St. Lawrence Centre for the Arts - $200 million over original estimate, and $350 million more than the cost to repair it.
I'm not against bikes or the arts, but this is ridiculous. Between these two questionable projects alone, that's half a billion-with-a-B of our tax dollars that aren't fixing the basics.
Every household knows there are need-to-haves and nice-to-haves. Get with the program, city hall!
Lisa Murray, Toronto
Fix the present and the future won't be so worrisomeA former Progressive Conservative who calls Pierre Poilievre 'terrifying' is launching a new political party, Aug. 13
The new Canadian Future Party has no future with that name and purpose. Voters may be interested in what's to come, but their main concern is what's going on nowadays. Politicians: First, fix the present. Then, worry about the future.
Jaime Oksemberg, Toronto
Public participation is a core principle in land-use planningEnvironmental and residents groups call new provincial rules barring OLT third-party appeals an attack on 'citizen rights,' Aug. 6
Prior to Bill 185, few residents could afford to appeal council decisions to the Ontario Land Tribunal. Accordingly, the majority of appeals came from developers, often arguing for more density or reduced setbacks, etc.
Provincial Bill 185 shuts the door on citizens looking to appeal rezoning decisions to the Ontario Land Tribunal but leaves the door wide open for appeals from developers and industry, like gravel-mine owners.
The public right to appeal council decisions whether it is used or not is a matter of fairness.
Like it or not, public participation is a core principle in land-use planning. As a former municipal councillor and land-use planner I am appalled that the public right of appeal - the duty to act fairly to all parties - has been trampled on under these new rules.
Susan Lloyd-Swail, Kitchener
Where's the risk-benefit analysis in the Ford government's decision-making process?
The WHO has just declared mpox a global public health emergency. Why some are calling on Canada to act, Aug. 14
A headline such as this calls into question Premier Doug Ford's budgeting priorities. Public health issues such as pandemics cause enormous concern, harm, expense, and mortality. The Star estimated that the pandemic cost Canada $1.5 billion per day, yet Ford cancelled the COVID wastewater surveillance program (despite its international reputation) to save a grand total of $11 million per annum. We live in an information economy . This is reminiscent of Ford's decision in 2020 to virtually cancel inspections of long-term-care homes in Ontario. How much suffering and death could have been avoided had the inspection program not been cancelled?
I worked in the financial services industry. Every major expense was subject to a risk-benefit analysis. I suggest the Ontario government implement this in their decision-making process.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
I put myself through law school behind the Wheat Sheaf Tavern's barInside one of Toronto's oldest bars - a grimy tavern with cockfights turned King West mainstay, Aug. 10
My family and I quite enjoyed David Silverberg's article last weekend about The Wheat Sheaf Tavern which included a great picture of my father Jerry Borins who owned the Wheat Sheaf from 1969 to 1994. Dad would have loved the article. My father bought the Wheat Sheaf with my two grandfathers, Louis Borins and Ben Freeman. I bartended my way through law school there becoming the 3rd generation in the family to serve at this historic landmark. My son Jack and his friends now frequent the bar. As for the tunnel to Fort York, you would need to buy me a pint to get the full story.
Richie Borins, North York
Rain gardens can help city residence deal with excess water
Toronto's had a massive stormwater management project underway for years. Here's what these new tunnels will do during downpours, Aug. 11
With thanks to the city for taking on this huge infrastructure program to store excess rainwater during extreme weather to prevent flooding, it's important to also recognize the role that nature can play in absorbing water. Many cities have made plans to become "sponge cities" with wetlands, permeable paved surfaces, trees and ravine restoration. In addition, homeowners can create beautiful rain gardens in their front or back yards, or in community spaces. My sister identified a location outside a church at the bottom of a sloped road and worked with the city to create a raingarden there. My sister also worked with RAINScapeTo to create her own front yard rain garden, consisting of a shallow depression to collect water from the downspouts, which is covered with flowering native plants. It is created by digging a rock filled area under the lawn and completed with native species on the soil above. Beautiful and functional!
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
Having the right stuff!
SpaceX to NASA's rescue? These stranded astronauts need a ride home, eventually, from somebody, Aug. 14
The plight of the two astronauts stuck on the International Space Station is a stark reminder of how unpredictable and dangerous space travel can be. It was originally intended to be only a nine-day mission, but NASA astronauts Barry Wilmore and Sunita Williams have now been stuck for seven weeks in space due to thruster problems and helium leaks with their Boeing Starliner capsule. Now, with no clear solution to their propulsion issues in sight, they may have to wait until February 2025 to finally come home! Such patience and stamina of these two astronauts gives credence to the movie "The Right Stuff." Clearly, Wilmore and Williams have the right stuff in ample supply to boldly go where few have travelled. God speed to both in finally getting home!
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek, Ont.
Prioritizing bonuses over jobs
CBC paid more than $18 million in bonuses in 2024 after eliminating hundreds of jobs, Aug. 12
After CBC downsized employees in order to reduce its budget shortfall to $20 million, and then paid $18.4 million in bonuses, are there any Canadian taxpayers who think CBC managers have their priorities straight and deserved a bonus for making their layoff decisions?
Marcia Zalev, Toronto

Doug Ford's 'Don't be pooping on the beach' catches Late Show host Stephen Colbert's attention

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-dont-be-pooping-on-beach-catches-late/docview/3093582250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%27Don%27t+be+pooping+on+the+beach%27+catches+Late+Show+host+Stephen+Colbert%27s+attention&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's pooping plea has made it to late-night TV.
During a segment on Thursday night's "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert," the American comedian brought up comments made by Ford earlier this month during a press conference in Mississauga in which he said, "Don't be pooping on the beach," while addressing rumours of people defecating in the sand at Wasaga Beach, on Lake Huron outside Collingwood.
Almost two weeks later, the comment has made it south of the border on late night TV.
"I love. There's a lot to love there," Colbert said as audience members laughed after seeing a clip of Ford's press conference. "I particularly love how defeated he seems at having to say that out loud."
Colbert ended the bit by doing an impression of Ford saying, "Don't be showing your boobs to a moose."
Ford and others have stressed that the rumours of people pooping at Wasaga Beach, which is the longest freshwater beach in the world, are not confirmed. At the press conference earlier this month, Ford said the province has given the beach $1 million to build more bathrooms and cover the costs of repairs and upgrades to beach grooming equipment.
With files from Andy Takagi

Toronto's youngest-ever murder + visitors with valid visas asked to file refugee claims or leave

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-youngest-ever-murder-visitors-with-valid/docview/3093582114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+youngest-ever+murder+%2B+visitors+with+valid+visas+asked+to+file+refugee+claims+or+leave&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, August 16 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a child pleading guilty to murder, why people with visitor visas were made to seek asylum and a reminder to leave the city's wildlife alone.
DON'T MISSA boy pleaded guilty to Toronto's youngest-ever murder
A young boy stood in the prisoner's box and pleaded guilty to second-degree murder for fatally stabbing his cousin last year, when both were just 12 years old, Jacques Gallant reports. The plea makes the now-13-year-old the youngest ever person found guilty of murder in Toronto's history. "It's a tragedy for everyone involved," his lawyer told the Star. Here's why we may never know why he did it.
How it happened: The boy had arrived from overseas and was living with his aunt and uncle and their children in their Scarborough apartment. On the day of the murder, he and his cousin were home alone. His aunt returned home to find her daughter in the bathtub with multiple stab wounds.Then what? The boy was found outside his aunt's balcony railing. First responders had to restrain the child after helping him off the railing, as he repeatedly screamed, "What did she say?"What now? He will be sentenced in fall. The maximum youth sentence for second degree murder is seven years, of which no more than four years can be served in custody.They had valid visas - but were asked to seek asylum or leave
Pranjal Singh was looking forward to visiting his sister in Canada. But when the 18-year-old from Delhi landed in Montreal, he was forced to file a refugee claim - despite having a valid visa - or leave. Singh is one of several recent visitors who've experienced such treatment, immigration lawyers and consultants tell Nicholas Keung. Take a look at why this might be happening.
Word from officials: "We are aware of these allegations and want to make it clear that the Canada Border Services Agency does not direct or counsel travellers to make refugee claims," a spokesperson said. "Having obtained a temporary resident visa (visitor visa) ... does not guarantee the right to enter Canada."Context: Refugee claims have skyrocketed since the border reopened after the pandemic, from around 24,000 in 2021 to 60,000 in 2022 and 138,000 in 2023.Meanwhile: Canada's Immigration and Refugee Board has been overwhelmed by a growing backlog of refugee claims - an inventory that could take almost four years to clear.People fed these swans bread. Now they'll never return to the wild
Two baby swans at the Scarborough Bluffs were left unable to fly - and can never return to the wild. Experts suspect they were fed bread and other highly processed food by humans, leading to nutritional deficiencies that caused their wings to deform. "Basically, they're filling up on McDonald's and not eating their salad," a volunteer from the Toronto Wildlife Centre told Mark Colley. What comes next for these swans?
Silver lining: The deformation, known as angel wing, can be treated when caught early. The two Scarborough swans will recover after at least a month of treatment - but by then, they'd have been abandoned by their parents.Wait, what? With no one to lead them south in migration or teach them the ropes, the babies will never return to the wild - or their parents.Family history: Their parents, Mango and Charlotte, went viral in a video last spring. But partly due to all that human adoration, they've now lost all five of their babies hatched in June.WHAT ELSE

Ford 'not sold' on safe injection sites: Premier hints at changes as municipalities urge help for mental health and addiction services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-not-sold-on-safe-injection-sites/docview/3093427174/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+%27not+sold%27+on+safe+injection+sites%3A+Premier+hints+at+changes+as+municipalities+urge+help+for+mental+health+and+addiction+services&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford hinting provincial moves are afoot to restrict supervised drug consumption sites, municipalities say more help is needed on mental health, addiction and homelessness.
"I'll be very frank, I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around - it's a haven for drug dealers," Ford said last week in Thunder Bay.
"Let's get these people the support they need and build more detox beds and I know our ministers will be making announcements shortly regarding that," the premier said.
That suggests Ford's cabinet members will be unveiling changes around consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa next week.
On the eve of that confab, Cambridge Mayor Jan Liggett commissioned a provincewide poll, which she shared with the Star and will be distributing to other civic leaders at the convention, that found 78 per cent of respondents want Queen's Park to earmark special funding for mental health and addiction services.
"It's a catastrophe that we are in right now," said Liggett, stressing "there's not a single mayor or councillor in this province" who isn't wrestling with mental health and addiction crises in their communities.
"We've been spinning in circles for years. We're all struggling with what needs to be done," she said in an interview Thursday.
Liggett, whose city does not have a CTS site, said she hoped the poll by Campaign Research, the same research firm used by Ford's Progressive Conservatives, would give mayors "some guidance" on addressing mental health and addiction across Ontario.
Asked what "should be prioritized with new funding," the poll found 34 per cent of respondents felt youth and adult mental health services, followed by 26 per cent for mental health crisis intervention services, 15 per cent for rehabilitation programs and facilities, nine per cent for community supports such as Alcoholics Anonymous and eight per cent for in-patient detox centres.
Some 45 per cent said "governments must spend much more on mental health and addiction services to make significant progress on getting ahead of this ever-growing problem" while 33 per cent said "the overall problem isn't a lack of funding" and 22 per cent were unsure.
Campaign Research surveyed 2,027 Ontarians July 30-31 using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm found 67 per cent believe "that Ontario's laws needed to change to allow families and physicians to provide mental health and addiction treatment to involuntary patients" with 14 per cent opposed to such mandatory intervention and 19 per cent unsure.
But Health Minister Sylvia Jones said two weeks ago she has "concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes that we want."
"Having said that, when we see the opportunity and the need for intervention, and people are willing to take on those treatments to make a difference, that's when we can show them our government is committed," Jones said on July 31.
Campaign Research also found 68 per cent of respondents would like to see more "drug-treatment courts" that provide judicially supervised rehab instead of incarcerating people with addiction issues related to their criminal activities.
Only 16 per cent opposed the expansion of such courts and 16 per cent weren't sure.
Liggett said drug-treatment courts are "a tool that is underused" because they can help people tackle their addiction while avoiding jail.
"We have to reach those that have that capacity (for rehabilitation)," she said.
Mental health issues and addiction are also big factors in the rise in homelessness - AMO estimates there were 1,400 homeless encampments across Ontario last year. The largest such tent encampment in Toronto is in Dufferin Grove Park, where local residents have repeatedly complained to the city and local councillor about drug use, fires, garbage, broken glass, public urination and other unsanitary conditions.
Encampment dwellers insist they have nowhere else to go because city shelters are full or unsafe and Toronto housing is expensive.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said he expects to hear about such concerns in some of his 35 scheduled meetings with municipal leaders over two days at the AMO conference.
"We're going to continue to have robust conversations and talk about the shared priorities that we have - and of course, homelessness, mental health and addiction, building more homes, building infrastructure," Bethlenfalvy said Tuesday.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked with homeless people, said the province needs to improve the overall delivery of mental health and addiction services.
"I have seen how red tape, bureaucracy, and, frankly, a lack of clarity about who has the power to fund certain programs and services does create a quagmire that slows down getting help to the people that need it most," Shamji said Wednesday.
"That process needs to be clarified."
While Ford has long been wary of supervised consumption sites - especially after mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre CTS in July 2023 - advocates argue they are safer for addicts than injecting in alleyways where fatal overdoses are common. Some research has shown the sites can save lives.
The city of Toronto has 10 safe injection sites, six of which are provincially designated as a CTS site and are subject to Ontario public health standards.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford signalled last week that cabinet members will be unveiling changes around consumption and treatment service sites at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa next week.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press file photo

Full text: Extending bike path is a desire, not a necessity
Metrolinx wants $150 million to build 2 kilometres of bike path. Who's taking who for a ride? Keenan, Aug. 14
Toronto has no money to repair public housing, Bloor-Danforth trains are creaking towards the subway graveyard, essential service response times are dismal, and on and on we go. You live here, you know what I'm talking about.
But in city hall's alternate fluffy pink universe, there's an extra $150 million to build two kilometres of super-duper bike lanes. However they spin it, that's essentially what it is.
But that's chicken feed compared to city council approving a whopping $400 million to rebuild the St. Lawrence Centre for the Arts - $200 million over original estimate, and $350 million more than the cost to repair it.
I'm not against bikes or the arts, but this is ridiculous. Between these two questionable projects alone, that's half a billion-with-a-B of our tax dollars that aren't fixing the basics.
Every household knows there are need-to-haves and nice-to-haves. Get with the program, city hall!
Lisa Murray, Toronto
Fix the present and future won't be so worrisome
A former Progressive Conservative who calls Pierre Poilievre 'terrifying' is launching a new political party, Aug. 13
The new Canadian Future Party has no future with that name and purpose. Voters may be interested in what's to come, but their main concern is what's going on nowadays. Politicians: First, fix the present. Then worry about the future.
Jaime Oksemberg, Toronto
Public access
to land planning is a crucial right
Environmental and residents groups call new provincial rules barring OLT third-party appeals an attack on 'citizen rights,' Aug. 6
Prior to Bill 185, few residents could afford to appeal council decisions to the Ontario Land Tribunal. Accordingly, the majority of appeals came from developers, often arguing for more density or reduced setbacks, etc.
Provincial Bill 185 shuts the door on citizens looking to appeal rezoning decisions, but leaves the door wide open for appeals from developers and industry, like gravel-mine owners.
The public right to appeal council decisions, whether it is used or not, is a matter of fairness.
Like it or not, public participation is a core principle in land-use planning. As a former municipal councillor and land-use planner I am appalled that the public right of appeal - the duty to act fairly to all parties - has been trampled on under these new rules.
Susan Lloyd-Swail,
Kitchener, Ont.
Ford should use more risk-benefit analysis
The WHO has just declared mpox a global public health emergency. Why some are calling on Canada to act,
Aug. 14
A headline such as this calls into question Premier Doug Ford's budgeting priorities. Public health issues such as pandemics cause enormous concern, harm, expense and mortality.
The Star estimated that the pandemic cost Canada $1.5 billion per day, yet Ford cancelled the COVID wastewater surveillance program (despite its international reputation) to save $11 million per annum.
This is reminiscent of Ford's decision in 2020 to virtually cancel inspections of long-term-care homes in Ontario. How much suffering and death could have been avoided had the inspection program not been cancelled?
I worked in the financial services industry. Every major expense was subject to a risk-benefit analysis. I suggest the Ontario government implement this in their decision-making process.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
Dad would have loved Star's story on Wheat Sheaf
Inside one of Toronto's oldest bars - a grimy tavern with cockfights turned King West mainstay, Aug. 10
My family and I quite enjoyed David Silverberg's article about The Wheat Sheaf Tavern, which included a great picture of my father Jerry Borins, who owned the Wheat Sheaf from 1969 to 1994. Dad would have loved the article.
My father bought the Wheat Sheaf with my two grandfathers, Louis Borins and Ben Freeman. I bartended my way through law school there becoming the third generation in the family to serve at this historic landmark. My son Jack and his friends now frequent the bar.
As for the tunnel to Fort York, you would need to buy me a pint to get the full story.
Richie Borins, North York
Rain gardens
can help store excess rainfall
Toronto's had a stormwater management project
underway for years, Aug. 11
With thanks to the city for taking on this huge infrastructure program to store excess rainwater during extreme weather to prevent flooding, it's important to also recognize the role that nature can play in absorbing water.
Many cities have made plans to become "sponge cities," with wetlands, permeable paved surfaces, trees and ravine restoration. In addition, homeowners can create beautiful rain gardens in their front or back yards, or in community spaces.
My sister worked with RAINScapeTo to create her own front yard rain garden, consisting of a shallow depression to collect water from the downspouts, which is covered with flowering native plants.
It is created by digging a rock-filled area under the lawn and completed with native species on the soil above. Beautiful and functional!
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
CBC executives put their bonuses before jobs
CBC paid $18 million in bonuses in 2024 after eliminating hundreds of jobs, Aug. 12
After CBC downsized employees in order to reduce its budget shortfall to $20 million, and then paid $18.4 million in bonuses, are there any Canadian taxpayers who think CBC managers have their priorities straight and deserved a bonus for making their layoff decisions?
Marcia Zalev, Toronto

Document 94 of 500

Doug Ford hints at changes around safe injection sites as municipalities urge help for mental health and addiction services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hints-at-changes-around-safe-injection/docview/3093630974/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hints+at+changes+around+safe+injection+sites+as+municipalities+urge+help+for+mental+health+and+addiction+services&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford hinting provincial moves are afoot to restrict supervised drug consumption sites, municipalities say more help is needed on mental health, addiction and homelessness.
"I'll be very frank, I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around - it's a haven for drug dealers," Ford said last week in Thunder Bay.
"Let's get these people the support they need and build more detox beds and I know our ministers will be making announcements shortly regarding that," the premier said.
That suggests Ford's cabinet members will be unveiling changes around consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa next week.
On the eve of that confab, Cambridge Mayor Jan Liggett commissioned a provincewide poll, which she shared with the Star and will be distributing to other civic leaders at the convention, that found 78 per cent of respondents want Queen's Park to earmark special funding for mental health and addiction services.
"It's a catastrophe that we are in right now," said Liggett, stressing "there's not a single mayor or councillor in this province" who isn't wrestling with mental health and addiction crises in their communities.
"We've been spinning in circles for years. We're all struggling with what needs to be done," she said in an interview Thursday.
Liggett, whose city does not have a CTS site, said she hoped the poll by Campaign Research, the same research firm used by Ford's Progressive Conservatives, would give mayors "some guidance" on addressing mental health and addiction across Ontario.
Asked what "should be prioritized with new funding," the poll found 34 per cent of respondents felt youth and adult mental health services, followed by 26 per cent for mental health crisis intervention services, 15 per cent for rehabilitation programs and facilities, nine per cent for community supports such as Alcoholics Anonymous and eight per cent for in-patient detox centres.
Some 45 per cent said "governments must spend much more on mental health and addiction services to make significant progress on getting ahead of this ever-growing problem" while 33 per cent said "the overall problem isn't a lack of funding" and 22 per cent were unsure.
Campaign Research surveyed 2,027 Ontarians July 30-31 using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm found 67 per cent believe "that Ontario's laws needed to change to allow families and physicians to provide mental health and addiction treatment to involuntary patients" with 14 per cent opposed to such mandatory intervention and 19 per cent unsure.
But Health Minister Sylvia Jones said two weeks ago she has "concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes that we want."
"Having said that, when we see the opportunity and the need for intervention, and people are willing to take on those treatments to make a difference, that's when we can show them our government is committed," Jones said on July 31.
Campaign Research also found 68 per cent of respondents would like to see more "drug-treatment courts" that provide judicially supervised rehab instead of incarcerating people with addiction issues related to their criminal activities.
Only 16 per cent opposed the expansion of such courts and 16 per cent weren't sure.
Liggett said drug-treatment courts are "a tool that is underused" because they can help people tackle their addiction while avoiding jail.
"We have to reach those that have that capacity (for rehabilitation)," she said.
Mental health issues and addiction are also big factors in the rise in homelessness - AMO estimates there were 1,400 homeless encampments across Ontario last year.
The largest such tent encampment in Toronto is in Dufferin Grove Park, where local residents have repeatedly complained to the city and local councillor about drug use, fires, garbage, broken glass, public urination and other unsanitary conditions.
Encampment dwellers insist they have nowhere else to go because city shelters are full or unsafe and Toronto housing is expensive.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said he expects to hear about such concerns in some of his 35 scheduled meetings with municipal leaders over two days at the AMO conference.
"We're going to continue to have robust conversations and talk about the shared priorities that we have - and of course, homelessness, mental health and addiction, building more homes, building infrastructure," Bethlenfalvy said Tuesday.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked with homeless people, said the province needs to improve the overall delivery of mental health and addiction services.
"I have seen how red tape, bureaucracy, and, frankly, a lack of clarity about who has the power to fund certain programs and services does create a quagmire that slows down getting help to the people that need it most," Shamji said Wednesday.
"That process needs to be clarified."
While Ford has long been wary of supervised consumption sites - especially after mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre CTS in July 2023 - advocates argue they are safer for addicts than injecting in alleyways where fatal overdoses are common. Some research has shown the sites can save lives.
The city of Toronto has 10 safe injection sites, six of which are provincially designated as a CTS site and are subject to Ontario public health standards.

Transparency, unity and a desire to restore what was

Full text: Open book? Yeah, right
As RCMP investigates Greenbelt scandal, finance minister says Ford government is 'an open book,' Aug. 13
Voters can and should acknowledge this Ford government for many things but being an open book is not one of them. I can't think of any government in recent years that has been more secretive than the Ford government. Such a remark shows the disdain Progressive Conservatives hold for voters.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is correct. Ford Conservatives are an open book because you can't hide bad decisions.
Beverly Northeast, Goodwood, Ont.
With the RCMP interviewing witnesses linked to Premier Doug Ford's government regarding their dealings involving Greenbelt lands, I can only hope that subjects will be asked if they are willing to take a polygraph test if police suspect any deception. I would suspect most of them would decline, including Ford if he is interviewed.
Russell Newman, Oak Ridges, Ont.
A united world in the city of love
The Paris Olympics were magical, beautiful, a reflection of the world. We needed this, Aug. 11
Columnist Bruce Arthur was able to put into words the feelings I had about the athletes who competed at the Olympics in Paris. Thank you! I felt very positive about the athletes who were competing at the Olympic Games, and appreciated their values when I saw them congratulating each other at the end of a race or the conclusion of a game. That's what we need more of in this world, I thought. It was affirming to me when Arthur wrote that "in a world full of politics built on anger and hate, these Games were an inoculation, even as they reflected and reinforced the problems that underpin the whole human experiment. It's complex. It's humans as they are." Exactly! Arthur's column also contained a fundamental truth, as stated by Cuban wrestler Miriam Lopez: "I think it all boils down to love. In everything you want to do with your life, you always have to have love." Yes, the games had flaws, just as humans do. However, the participants showed us a more positive way of looking at this world, and I want to thank Arthur for articulating that vision for us. Let's keep hope alive.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Please restore what once was
I am an old guy who grew up in the 1960s and 1970s, and I remember when the Canadian National Exhibition and Ontario Place were the centres of action in late summer. There were many exhibits, shows, and buildings to explore.I even was one of the first to park my sailboat at Ontario Place in summer, where there was constant activity, attraction and excitement. But later, Ontario Place started closing down and the fun disappeared. I hope Premier Doug Ford can appreciate what Ontario Place once was and somehow that part of harbourfront will be restored to its previous glory, rather than just made available to billionaires. Premier Ford, please think of the people you are supposed to represent.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington, Ont.
'Not sure who they really are'
A former Progressive Conservative who calls Pierre Poilievre 'terrifying' is launching a new political party, Aug. 14
The Conservatives are notorious for getting ticked off, then running off to form a new party under the Conservative or pseudo-Conservative party umbrella. Think of the Reform Party of Canada (turned Canadian Alliance). Isn't that part of their ongoing problem? Who wants to vote for a party that keeps splintering and is not sure who they really are. Hanging one's political laundry out to dry is not always a good idea.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
Concern for twentysomethings
Twentysomethings are more depressed and financially troubled than older generations, a new study has found, Aug. 14
As someone who counselled young people for most of my career, I find these survey results to be very concerning. The pandemic clobbered many young people, especially those in the creative arts sector. Venues closed, gigs dried up and agents all but locked their doors. For all youth, whether or not they live with their parents, unemployment is a major stress, giving the young person far too much time to brood on personal deficiencies real or imagined, and on the coming climate and political catastrophes. They often turn to social media that have become dystopian echo chambers. Widespread use of soft drugs on a daily basis further saps many young people of initiative that is needed at a time when one must send out hundreds of resumes to even get an interview. It's particularly painful to meet young people who are really bright and creative, but who cannot afford the high tuition at colleges and universities. Denmark actually pays youth to enhance their education. Instead, our provincial government would rather shutter inspiring venues like the Science Centre and Ontario Place and build highways to nowhere rather than support our grossly underfunded public education.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Flooding is not exclusive
Quebec recovering from historic rainfall, power outages as storm remnants move east, Aug. 10
Flooded basements, power outages, roads washed out ... sound familiar? What Quebec experienced is just like what happened to Toronto on July 16 as torrential downpours hit Montreal and its surrounding areas. Whether remnants of more and more extreme hurricanes or not, these are unnatural disasters brought about by global warming. We need to stop burning fossil fuels like coal, oil and gas, and switch to clean and cheap renewable energy. Our roads, bridges, and stormwater infrastructure were not built for once-in-a-hundred-year rainfall events that are happening more frequently. We need a city stormwater charge that will help pay for upgrading our stormwater infrastructure. The sooner, the better before the next torrential downpour.
Ray Nakano, Toronto

Document 96 of 500

Justin Trudeau's government radically transformed Canada's temporary foreign worker program. Young people and low wage workers are paying the price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-government-radically-transformed/docview/3093160957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+radically+transformed+Canada%27s+temporary+foreign+worker+program.+Young+people+and+low+wage+workers+are+paying+the+price&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you know a young person who struggled to find a summer job they are not alone. This has been the worst summer on record for youth employment outside of the pandemic. Many factors - from a weak economy to a population boom of young people - are at play with one of the largest being the federal government's 2022 decision to deregulate the low-wage stream of the temporary foreign worker program.
On April 4, 2022, a mere 13 days after the Liberals and NDP signed their Supply and Confidence Agreement, the federal government announced arguably the largest deregulation of the Temporary Foreign Worker program in Canadian history. The program's low-wage stream, which allows employers not in the agricultural industry (they have a separate stream) to bring in workers and pay them wages under the provincial median (currently $28.39 in Ontario), was radically transformed. The government removed the rule that employers could only bring in workers in some low-wage occupations if the local unemployment rate was less than six per cent allowing firms in areas of high unemployment to access the program. Companies had been limited to having only 10 per cent of their workforce be low-wage temporary foreign workers; this was raised to 20 per cent. In seven sectors, including accommodation and food services, this was raised to 30 per cent.
The government argued that the plan was necessary to reduce labour shortages, noting that "much of the unmet demand is in low-wage occupations." Accommodation and food services were cited as having the largest shortages. Business groups, such as the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, applauded the move, particularly the removal of the six per cent rule and raising the cap for low-wage employers. Companies responded by rapidly expanding the use of the program. The number of approvals for the low-wage program more than doubled in 2022 alone rising from an estimated 30,000 to more than 80,000. The total number of temporary foreign worker positions approved in all streams rose by 52 per cent in 2022 alone to 227,000. A Bloomberg report found that Tim Hortons' franchisees in Ontario hired 714 temporary foreign workers in 2023, up from just 59 in 2019.
While this deregulation worked as intended, it came at the expense of low-wage workers in Canada, as it reduced employment opportunities and suppressed wages. To bring in a temporary foreign worker, employers must show that there are no qualified domestic applicants at the "prevailing wage." A coffee shop owners' inability to find workers at the current wage is plausible in places with low unemployment. However, the government's reforms allowed employers in high-unemployment areas to access the program as well. But the term "prevailing wage" is important here. In a capitalist society, if a company cannot find someone to work for $17 an hour, they either need to raise their wage to attract more workers or do without. But the federal government has given them a third option, temporary foreign workers, which prevents employers from having to offer raises to attract new workers.
Liberals and New Democrats once understood that the low-wage temporary foreign worker program works by suppressing wage increases. When the Conservative Harper government deregulated the program, Liberal MP Scott Brison asked in the House, "Does the minister recognize that some economists are linking the growth in the number of low-skilled temporary foreign workers under the Conservatives with higher youth unemployment in Canada and wage suppression for Canadian youth?" And Liberal leader Justin Trudeau called on the Harper government to "reverse its wage-suppressing policies and fix its broken program."
Between the increase in the number of temporary foreign workers and international students, young workers are having to compete harder for jobs, and many are coming up empty-handed. The employment rate for 15- to 19-year-olds dropped from 55.3 per cent in July 2022 to 46.4 per cent this July, the lowest July on record outside of the lockdown year of 2020. The trend for 20- to 24-year-olds is similar, with this summer being the worst non-pandemic year for employment since 1997. Had the employment rates for these groups stayed at 2022 levels, an additional 200,000 15 - to 19-year-olds and 80,000 20-to 24-year-olds would have jobs this summer. The economists that the Liberals cited in 2014 were right then, and they are right today - deregulation of the low-wage temporary foreign worker stream suppresses wages and increases youth unemployment.
The federal government must phase out the non-agricultural low-wage stream of the temporary foreign worker program. The harms it causes to workers far outweigh the benefits to corporations.
CAPTION: Deregulation of theTemporary Foreign Workerprogram allowed many employers to increase temporary foreign worker hires. It's been so successful that many young Canadians can't find summer jobs.
CREDIT: Mike Moffatt, Contributor

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, August 15 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the drama of working for the Green Party, mpox being declared a global health emergency and a top graduate from a high school you may have forgotten about.
DON'T MISSLife in the Green Party: "Backstabbing, secret alliances and plenty of drama"
Before she resigned, a member of the board overseeing the Green Party's finances left a final message for its top brass: Get your house in order before it's too late. Miranda Woodland likened working for the Greens to navigating a soap opera, complete with a culture of bullying, gossiping and scapegoating, Mark Ramzy reports. Here's what it's like working for the Green Party.
Wait, what? In her resignation letter, Woodland said she faced "constant hostility," including public and private insults, and "rampant misogyny" within the party.Word from the Green Party: "We acknowledge the existence of conflicts and issues between certain members of our governance," a party official said. "We are actively addressing these matters to ensure that the party remains a professional organization and a safe working environment."Background: Woodland is the latest senior party member to resign in recent weeks, following deputy leader Jonathan Pedneault and executive director Kevin Dunbar. The Star learned Leader Elizabeth May even considered stepping down and handing the reins to Pedneault before he abruptly left.Here's how worried you should be about the mpox global health emergency
The World Health Organization declared mpox a global public health emergency for the second time in three years, amid soaring cases in Africa. This time, the wave's driven by a deadlier and more contagious variant of the virus. But while experts acknowledged the pandemic potential of the disease, they say it's "very unlikely" for it to be imported into Canada anytime soon. Should Canadians be concerned?
By the numbers: More than 14,000 mpox cases and 524 deaths have been recorded across Africa this year, mainly among children under 15. More than 96 per cent of the cases were in the Democratic Republic of Congo.On the homefront: There have been no confirmed cases of the new mpox variant in Canada yet. The milder strain behind the 2022 global mpox outbreak is still circulating, though, recently spiking in Toronto in the wake of summer festivities.Now what? The Africa CDC is calling for aid to fill its need for 10 million mpox vaccines. Canadian officials said there are no current plans to share our stockpile, believed to contain millions of shots. Some experts are urging the government to reconsider.Meet the top graduate of a school many don't realize still exists
One of Toronto's top students this year graduated from a place few realize still exists: the fully online Virtual Secondary School. Emma Brijlall Nakahara graduated with a 99.7 per cent average from the school born out of the pandemic, which she enrolled in to accommodate her medical issues, Janet Hurley reports. "It makes learning a lot more accessible to different kind of people," she said. This is her story.
Life in virtual school: Every morning, Nakahara would prepare her backpack for the commute to her desk in the family den, steps from her bedroom. She also partakes in online extracurriculars - "I was amazed by the number of clubs," she said.Context: The virtual school was launched in 2020 to accommodate students during the COVID-19 pandemic lockdowns. Today, upwards of 1,300 high school students are still enrolled, for reasons ranging from health issues to an interest in developing digital skills.WHAT ELSE

Document 98 of 500

Tiny houses and towering towers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tiny-houses-towering-towers/docview/3093087052/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tiny+houses+and+towering+towers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Feast your eyes upon this stunning nine-question quiz and enjoy a blend of modern luxury and classic charm in the following multiple-choice challenge, with plenty of space to store all your knowledge of this city's bonkers real estate market. Is there on-street parking? Do you take countertops for granite? If it's on Lake Ontario now, how long till it's in Lake Ontario? The answers to none of the above - and more! - can be found in our quiz below.
1.Even Toronto's most glamorous homes are still located in, well, Toronto. When Drake's Bridle Path mansion flooded last month, what did the rap star hope the liquid was?
a) Tahiti Treat
b) Espresso martini
c) Champagne, papi
d) Don River Perignon
2.Some of the city's most sought-after condos are in converted industrial buildings. What's the term for a newer unit with high ceilings and large windows that's designed to look like an older retrofits?
a) Mock box
b) Soft loft
c) Tahiti Treat
d) Commando condo
3.Which of the following is not an acronym describing how local residents might feel about increased development?
a) NIMBY
b) BANANA
c) YIMBY
d) GUMBY
4.The Little House, a 300-square-foot Toronto landmark designed by architect Arthur Weeden in 1912, sits on a seven-foot lot near St. Clair and Dufferin. When it last sold in 2010, how much did the city's smallest fully-divided home sell for?
a) $179,900
b) $379,900
c) $579,900
d) $879,900
5.Oh look! This condo listing boasts an inboard bedroom! What does that mean?
a) Floor-to-ceiling wood panelling
b) No windows
c) Enough room for a queen-size bed
d) A four-stroke automotive engine adapted for marine use
6.In a competitive market, realtor Larry Medina offers his clients a unique advantage. What is it?
a) If he can't find you a condo, you can crash on his couch.
b) Two-litre bottle of Tahiti Treat with every purchase
c) Psychic energy readings of listed properties
d) PC Optimum points with every purchase
7.Until this month, the Ontario Real Estate Association was headed by the former leader of which provincial political party?
a) Liberals
b) New Democrats
c) Progressive Conservatives
d) Greens
8.The pluralization of which Toronto neighbourhood name has historically been a sore spot with locals?
a) Baby Points
b) The Beaches
c) Leasides
d) The Annexes
9.According to a June report in the Star, the unfinished megacondo project at Yonge and Bloor known as The One has a market value of around $600 million. The building is currently in receivership. What's the minimum bid that receiver Alvarez & Marsal will accept for the unfinished luxury tower?
a) $500 million
b) $800 million
c) $1 billion
d) $1.2 billion
Answers
1. b)"This better be Espresso Martini," Aubrey "Drake" Graham wrote on his Instagram story. Fortunately Kendrick Lamar only owns Drake in the metaphorical sense and none of his physical properties were affected by July's flooding.
2. b) If you can't score a hard loft in the Candy Factory, Toy Factory, or Printing Factory, you might consider a soft loft. And as the real estate market proves, rhyme pays.
3. d) NIMBY stands for Not In My Backyard, while YIMBY means Yes In My Backyard. BANANA, in this context, means Build Absolutely Nothing Anywhere Near Anyone. GUMBY is just a delightful green cartoon and a welcome addition to any backyard, if you're lucky enough to have one.
4. a) The property at 128 Day Ave. is not currently for sale, but House Sigma estimates a current market value of between $521,144 and $799,029.
5. b) An inboard bedroom doesn't have windows to the outside, though it may offer "borrowed light" via windows to rooms with actual windows. City of Toronto guidelines state that bedrooms should have exterior light, but those are only guidelines.
6. c) As reported in the Star last year, Medina is "Toronto's only self-proclaimed psychic licensed realtor." He purports to "help people navigate the real estate market through a psychic lens."
7. c) Tim Hudak led Canada's largest provincial real estate association for the last seven years before he stepped down on Aug. 2.
8. b) In a non-binding 2006 plebiscite, 58 per cent of residents in the east-end waterfront voted for The Beach, singular. Kew, Woodbine, Scarboro and Balmy are all separate beaches in the area.
9. d) Given that the average one-bedroom apartment rents for $2,300 a month, that's enough to cover a 43,478-year lease, plus utilities.
You've been approved ...
0-3 Handyman's special!
Tough markets don't last, tough people do. Toronto's real estate market has been tough for 25 years, though, so maybe consult your actuary.
4-6 Tons of potential!
Getting a toe into the market is the main thing - and you've just wiggled a whole foot through the door! Now, to find a roof for the rest of your limbs.
7-9 Move in today!
Congrats! You did it! And showing this result to the bank will cover exactly zero per cent of your next bi-monthly payment.
Benjamin Errett publishes a charming weekly newsletter into some of the city's most desirable inboxes via GetWitQuick.com.

Document 99 of 500

Why your kid couldn't find a summer job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-your-kid-couldnt-find-summer-job/docview/3093086834/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+your+kid+couldn%27t+find+a+summer+job&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you know a young person who struggled to find a summer job they are not alone. This has been the worst summer on record for youth employment outside of the pandemic. Many factors - from a weak economy to a population boom of young people - are at play, with one of the largest being the federal government's 2022 decision to deregulate the low-wage stream of the Temporary Foreign Worker Program.
On April 4, 2022, a mere 13 days after the Liberals and NDP signed their Supply and Confidence Agreement, the federal government announced arguably the largest deregulation of the Temporary Foreign Worker Program in Canadian history.
The program's low-wage stream, which allows employers not in the agricultural industry (they have a separate stream) to bring in workers and pay them wages under the provincial median (currently $28.39 in Ontario), was radically transformed.
The government removed the rule that employers could only bring in workers in some low-wage occupations if the local unemployment rate was less than six per cent, allowing firms in areas of high unemployment to access the program.
Companies had been limited to having only 10 per cent of their workforce be low-wage temporary foreign workers; this was raised to 20 per cent. In seven sectors, including accommodation and food services, this was raised to 30 per cent.
The government argued that the plan was necessary to reduce labour shortages, noting that "much of the unmet demand is in low-wage occupations." Accommodation and food services were cited as having the largest shortages.
Business groups, such as the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, applauded the move, particularly the removal of the six per cent rule and raising the cap for low-wage employers.
Companies responded by rapidly expanding the use of the program. The number of approvals for the low-wage program more than doubled in 2022 alone, rising from an estimated 30,000 to more than 80,000. The total number of temporary foreign worker positions approved in all streams rose by 52 per cent in 2022 alone, to 227,000. A Bloomberg report found that Tim Hortons' franchisees in Ontario hired 714 temporary foreign workers in 2023, up from just 59 in 2019.
While this deregulation worked as intended, it came at the expense of low-wage workers in Canada, as it reduced employment opportunities and suppressed wages. To bring in a temporary foreign worker, employers must show that there are no qualified domestic applicants at the "prevailing wage."
A coffee shop owners' inability to find workers at the current wage is plausible in places with low unemployment. However, the government's reforms allowed employers in high-unemployment areas to access the program as well.
But the term "prevailing wage" is important here. In a capitalist society, if a company cannot find someone to work for $17 an hour, they either need to raise their wage to attract more workers or do without. But the federal government has given them a third option, temporary foreign workers, which prevents employers from having to offer raises to attract new workers.
Liberals and New Democrats once understood that the low-wage temporary foreign worker program works by suppressing wage increases.
When the Conservative Harper government deregulated the program, Liberal MP Scott Brison asked in the House, "Does the minister recognize that some economists are linking the growth in the number of low-skilled temporary foreign workers under the Conservatives with higher youth unemployment in Canada and wage suppression for Canadian youth?" And Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau called on the Harper government to "reverse its wage-suppressing policies and fix its broken program."
Between the increase in the number of temporary foreign workers and international students, young workers are having to compete harder for jobs, and many are coming up empty-handed. The employment rate for 15- to 19-year-olds dropped from 55.3 per cent in July 2022 to 46.4 per cent this July, the lowest July on record outside of the lockdown year of 2020.
The trend for 20- to 24-year-olds is similar, with this summer being the worst non-pandemic year for employment since 1997.
Had the employment rates for these groups stayed at 2022 levels, an additional 200,000 15- to 19-year-olds and 80,000 20- to 24-year-olds would have jobs this summer. The economists that the Liberals cited in 2014 were right then, and they are right today - deregulation of the low-wage temporary foreign worker stream suppresses wages and increases youth unemployment.
The federal government must phase out the non-agricultural low-wage stream of the Temporary Foreign Worker Program. The harms it causes to workers far outweigh the benefits to corporations.
Mike Moffatt is the Senior Director of the Smart Prosperity Institute and co-host of the podcast The Missing Middle.

Full text: Ford government is nowhere near
an 'open book'
As RCMP investigates Greenbelt scandal, finance minister says Ford government is 'an open book,' Aug. 13
Voters can and should acknowledge this Ford government for many things, but being an open book is not one of them. I can't think of any government in recent years that has been more secretive than the Ford government. Such a remark shows the disdain Progressive Conservatives hold for voters.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga

Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is correct. Ford Conservatives are an open book because you can't hide bad decisions.
Beverly Northeast, Goodwood

With the RCMP interviewing witnesses linked to Premier Doug Ford's government regarding their dealings involving Greenbelt lands, I can only hope that subjects will be asked if they are willing to take a polygraph test if police suspect any deception. I would suspect most of them would decline, including Ford if he is interviewed.
Russell Newman, Oak Ridges
A united world
in the city of love renewed my hope
The Paris Olympics were magical, beautiful, a reflection of the world. We needed this, Arthur, Aug. 11
Columnist Bruce Arthur was able to put into words the feelings I had about the athletes who competed at the Olympics in Paris. Thank you!
I felt very positive about the athletes who were competing at the Olympic Games, and appreciated their values when I saw them congratulating each other at the end of a race or the conclusion of a game. That's what we need more of in this world, I thought.
It was affirming to me when Arthur wrote that "in a world full of politics built on anger and hate, these Games were an inoculation, even as they reflected and reinforced the problems that underpin the whole human experiment. It's complex. It's humans as they are." Exactly!
Arthur's column also contained a fundamental truth, as stated by Cuban wrestler Miriam Lopez: "I think it all boils down to love. In everything you want to do with your life, you always have to have love."
Yes, the games had flaws, just as humans do. However, the participants showed us a more positive way of looking at this world, and I want to thank Arthur for articulating that vision for us. Let's keep hope alive.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Concern for
our young people
in troubled times
Twentysomethings are more depressed and financially troubled than older generations, a new study has found, Aug. 14
As someone who counselled young people for most of my career, I find these survey results to be very concerning.
The pandemic clobbered many young people, especially those in the creative arts sector. Venues closed, gigs dried up and agents all but locked their doors.
For all youth, whether or not they live with their parents, unemployment is a major stress, giving the young person far too much time to brood on personal deficiencies real or imagined, and on the coming climate and political catastrophes. They often turn to social media that have become dystopian echo chambers.
Widespread use of soft drugs on a daily basis further saps many young people of initiative that is needed at a time when one must send out hundreds of resumes to even get an interview. It's particularly painful to meet young people who are really bright and creative, but who cannot afford the high tuition at colleges and universities.
Denmark actually pays youth to enhance their education. Instead, our provincial government would rather shutter inspiring venues like the Science Centre and Ontario Place and build highways to nowhere than support our grossly underfunded public education.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Time to restore waterfront to its previous glory
I am an old guy who grew up in the 1960s and 1970s, and I remember when the Canadian National Exhibition and Ontario Place were the centres of action in late summer.
There were many exhibits, shows and buildings to explore. I even was one of the first to park my sailboat at Ontario Place each summer, where there was constant activity, attraction and excitement.
But later, Ontario Place started closing down and the fun disappeared. I hope Premier Doug Ford can appreciate what Ontario Place once was and somehow that part of the harbourfront will be restored to its previous glory, rather than just made available to billionaires.
Premier Ford, please think of the people you are supposed to represent.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
Conservatives never seem sure of who they are
A former Progressive Conservative who calls Pierre Poilievre 'terrifying' is launching a new political party, Aug. 14
The Conservatives are notorious for getting ticked off, then running off to form a new party under the Conservative or pseudo-Conservative party umbrella. Think of the Reform Party of Canada (turned Canadian Alliance).
Isn't that part of their ongoing problem? Who wants to vote for a party that keeps splintering and is not sure who they really are. Hanging one's political laundry out to dry is not always a good idea.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
Rise of flooding
is not exclusive
to Toronto
Quebec recovering from historic rainfall, power outages as storm remnants move east, Aug. 10
Flooded basements, power outages, roads washed out ... sound familiar? What Quebec experienced is just like what happened to Toronto on July 16, as torrential downpours hit Montreal and its surrounding areas.
Whether remnants of more and more extreme hurricanes or not, these are unnatural disasters brought about by global warming. We need to stop burning fossil fuels like coal, oil and gas, and switch to clean and cheap renewable energy.
Our roads, bridges and stormwater infrastructure were not built for once-in-a-hundred-year rainfall events that are happening more frequently. We need a city stormwater charge that will help pay for upgrading our stormwater infrastructure. The sooner, the better before the next torrential downpour.
Ray Nakano, Toronto
CREDIT: ;

Document 101 of 500

Opposition slams Ford for animal hospital joke: Premier commented that Ontarians waiting for MRIs, CT scans could find access at new facility

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opposition-slams-ford-animal-hospital-joke/docview/3093086808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+slams+Ford+for+animal+hospital+joke%3A+Premier+commented+that+Ontarians+waiting+for+MRIs%2C+CT+scans+could+find+access+at+new+facility&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's offhanded joke that a new King City animal hospital could ease Ontario's health crunch has left his political rivals galled and appalled.
"By the looks of it, we know where we can send the overflow patients now for MRIs and CAT scans and everything else," Ford quipped Tuesday at the grand open of the massive King Animal Hospital, a private veterinary facility 45 kilometres north of Toronto.
The premier's office emphasized he was making a lighthearted remark about the impressive four-storey, 60,000-square-foot clinic that boasts MRI, CT scan, X-ray, ultrasound and endoscopy services for horses, dogs, cats and other animals.
"Our government made record investments in our publicly funded health-care system, increasing the health-care budget by over 31 per cent since 2018, and investing over $85 billion this year alone," Grace Lee, the premier's director of media relations, said Wednesday.
"We are adding over 49 new MRI machines and 50 new CT scanners in hospitals across the province, increasing capacity to perform nearly 500,000 hours of MRI and CT scans. We recently announced an investment for 100,000 new publicly funded scans, which will continue to help Ontario achieve some of the shortest wait times for surgeries in Canada," she said.
But opposition leaders pounced on a quip they argued was no laughing matter.
"Remember, this is part of the plan," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Wednesday on social media.
"Doug Ford (and) his politicians are dismantling our health-care system (and) giving it over to folks like Galen Weston to make more billions off," said Stiles, referring to the wealthy owner of Shoppers Drug Mart, where more publicly funded health services are being made available by pharmacists.
"It's all a giant inside deal. Corrupt," the NDP chief said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie blasted the premier for his insensitivity. "After six years of deliberately underfunding our health-care system in order to justify his agenda to sell it off and privatize, Ford is now joking that if you're waiting for an MRI, you should just visit a veterinary clinic," Crombie said in a statement.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room doctor, said "jokes can be funny, but they can also be revealing," and called Ford's "cruel, callous and out of touch."
"Nearly 10,000 people died while waiting for MRIs and CT scans alone through the watch of Doug Ford," Shamji told reporters at Queen's Park.
"Another 2,000 ... Ontarians died while waiting for surgery. So against that backdrop, you don't joke that the hundreds of thousands of people who are still on waiting lists line up like animals at a veterinary hospital."
With 2.5-million Ontarians lacking access to a doctor, Ford's Progressive Conservatives are poised to give pharmacists more authority to prescribe medications for 14 common ailments such as insomnia, shingles, hepatitis A and B, rabies and human papillomavirus, as well as lab tests for strep throat.
While those additional powers for pharmacists would likely alleviate the strain on emergency rooms and doctors' offices, they would generate more revenue for pharmacy giants.
"For me, this is about access," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said three weeks ago.
The Ontario Medical Association - which represents the province's 43,000 physicians, medical students and retired doctors - has argued that's a "Band-Aid solution for a health-care system in crisis."
It maintains that the doctor shortage will worsen in the years ahead due to demographic changes and other factors.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Drug stores; Medical imaging; Pharmacists; Diagnostics; Medical equipment; Jokes; Health care

Document 102 of 500

No surprise tourism is down. Traffic congestion aside, we don't have anything fun for tourists

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-surprise-tourism-is-down-traffic-congestion/docview/3093240091/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+surprise+tourism+is+down.+Traffic+congestion+aside%2C+we+don%27t+have+anything+fun+for+tourists&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Take traffic out of congestion
Bumper-to-bumper traffic makes cottage commuting a nightmare on long weekends. It doesn't have to be that way, Aug. 5
Those who are not willing to help with the problem of congestion by even occasionally getting out of their cars and taking the bus or train, but would rather complain about it, are not welcome. It's not the poor on the bus, but the smart and the thoughtful. Those that don't want to help with the solution, please pay the tolls (if they come) and parking fees and fines without complaint. Remember two things: congestion is caused by too many cars on the road and most of the drivers of the cars are guests in the city.
Peter Rapsey, Toronto
Using road tolls as a solution to decrease congestion is definitely not an egalitarian method. Some can afford it and it won't bother them, while others can't afford it. Many cities with congestion problems have a different method. For instance, Saõ Paolo, Brazil, has instituted a method by which license plates that end in an even number are able to drive on even numbered days and license plates that end in odd numbers are able to use the roads on odd numbered days. It seems like a fair and easy way to deal with congestion. Of course the best way would be in increase rapid public transit at a cheap rate, but that's not about to happen any time soon.
Stephen Bloom, Toronto
No surprise tourism is down
Ontario's struggling tourism operators say even a visit by the world's biggest pop star won't solve their predicament, Aug. 11
The article on the decline in tourism was interesting but fails to answer the root question why. The answer is fairly obvious. The attractions that Toronto and the province offer are stale, closed or reruns. Niagara Falls, the CN Tower and our theatres just aren't that exciting any more. I recently walked north from Yonge and Dundas Streets after a show and couldn't find one decent place to go for a cocktail or even coffee. Live entertainment is so bad the bands have to pay many clubs to play and sell their own tickets. The Garth Drabinsky-produced theatre days are decades over yet there has been nothing really new and exhilarating to bring busloads of tourists here. The Science Centre is of course closed and Ontario Place boarded up. Restaurants have been closing everywhere as well as exciting clothing stores with anything unique.
I recently did a day trip to Harbourfront where there was absolutely nothing going on. It was basically dead. Parades and charity runs for causes bring certain audiences partial to those, but do they really spend any money? There is the Honda Indy race but many don't go near the Lake Shore. Some freebee events like Beaches International Jazz Festival (which is mostly not jazz music) bring massive crowds and traffic/parking congestion for amateur entertainment and sub par food from food trucks that is eaten while standing. We all know the traffic issues. Some theatre places like the Shaw are in need of a major facelift, and even Niagara on the Lake looks like people wandering around but not buying anything. I've lived in the GTA my whole life but what's happened in Toronto and Ontario? Where'd the buzz go?
Madis Kreem, Toronto
In reporter Kristin Rushowy's excellent article on the economic challenges facing the Ontario tourism industry, little attention is paid to what exactly attracts visitors to our province in the first place. For example, does the provincial government think that the lost revenue suffered by the closing of the highly popular Ontario Science Centre will be offset by tourists flooding the city in the hopes of buying beer at a corner store?
Michael Albano, Toronto
DiManno right about the Olympics, but not cricket
Stumbling, soggy and stymied: Covering the Paris Olympics was an endurance sport. And yet...: DiManno, Aug. 11
So, columnist Rosie DiManno doesn't think cricket should be an Olympic sport? Cricket is the second most popular sport (only behind soccer) on the planet. But she's OK with breakdancing and skateboarding? How is that?
Gregory Hoggarth, Stratford, Ont.
After reading about columnist Rosie DiManno's challenges, efforts, successes and failures in Paris where she was reporting on the Olympics, I think she deserves a gold medal!
Leslie Chiswell, Toronto
It sounds like Paris was an absolute nightmare according to Rosie DiManno. I can only imagine the chaos of trying to navigate through the city during this extreme event. Ironically, horrid experiences like this create never ending memories and very entertaining stories. Something you may not get from a perfect holiday. It's these negative experiences that will always be remembered with a great deal of enthusiasm.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby, Ont.
Conservatives set up TFW misuse
UN envoy doubles down on criticism that Canada's foreign worker program is a 'breeding ground' for slavery, Aug. 13
Thanks for your thoughtful article on the United Nations report. I was a NDP MP (York-South Weston) from 2011-2015. Former Prime Minister Stephen Harper recently said some righteous things about the report, but he was part of the problem. The Conservatives opened the door to use of temporary foreign workers in service industries, mostly responding to Alberta and Saskatchewan fast food giants complaining that they couldn't find workers. The issue was not that there were no workers, but that the industry was unwilling to raise wages to attract them. I'd rather spend a little more on my coffee and doughnut than watch slaves preparing them.
Mike Sullivan, Stratford, Ont.
Answers are obvious
Trudeau government's controversial choice to head human rights commission steps down, Aug. 12
Following this lengthy and thorough investigation into Birju Dattani's fitness to serve as the chief commissioner of the Canadian Human Rights Commission, it is clear that the correct decision was made. However, one important note in this disappointing affair is sadly missing; a simple "I'm sorry" to the Canadian people from all involved.
Bernie M. Farber, Toronto
CAPTION: Crystal Sheriff, owner of Gray Line Toronto and an executive member of the Tourist Guide Association of Toronto, says wildfires in Western Canada are impacting tourism in Ontario.

Opposition slams Doug Ford for joke about animal hospital

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opposition-slams-doug-ford-joke-about-animal/docview/3093240051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+slams+Doug+Ford+for+joke+about+animal+hospital&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

As Ford ponders an early election next year - ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - the political parties are gearing up for a campaign in which health care is expected to be a major issue.
