Document 121 of 500

A brief history of flipping the bird: From ancient Greece, to Pierre Trudeau, to one Toronto police officer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brief-history-flipping-bird-ancient-greece-pierre/docview/3091274924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+brief+history+of+flipping+the+bird%3A+From+ancient+Greece%2C+to+Pierre+Trudeau%2C+to+one+Toronto+police+officer&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In ancient Greece, while Athens was being ravaged by the plague and under siege by Sparta, comedic playwright Aristophanes put an insult into writing.
It appeared in "Peace," a 421 B.C. comedy, telling the story of a farmer searching for a lost goddess.
"Don't you see how the sickle-maker is happy," Aristophanes wrote, according to one translation, "and how he gave the spear maker the finger?"
That is, the middle finger.
The bird.
The one-finger salute.
That prodigious protrusion of pent-up frustration.
It's the same finger raised by a Toronto police officer, and made viral this past week in an online video, sparking a Toronto Police Service investigation for inappropriate behaviour.
The through-line from ancient times to today is, of course, not that straight.
The middle finger has a complicated history, one that has taken it from the Greeks to baseball players in the late 1800s to the hand of a prime minister, with stops in between.
Through time, it has been employed in different ways and attributed different meanings. It has been debated legally. And it has become perhaps the most recognizable gesture in the world, landing prisoners, politicians and police officers alike in hot water.
A questionable parking job; a finger of blame
An undated video posted online this week showed two police officers being confronted on a Toronto street. The man taking the video claimed the officers had parked illegally in a loading zone while getting coffee. One of the officers said she needed caffeine while working an 11-hour shift. Later, as the cruiser waited at an intersection, she said, "We're doing our job, bro."
"I'm not your bro," the man taking the video responded. The officer flipped him off and the video ended.
It generated a ton of criticism online.
"We acknowledge the officer's response was not appropriate," Const. Shannon Eames said in a statement. The force's Professional Standard Unit is now investigating.
Premier Doug Ford took a different view of the encounter.
"Let's thank them, rather than getting Google glasses and trying to harass our police. That's what I call those. Harassment," Ford said Wednesday. "As far as I'm concerned, they did nothing wrong."
So just where does this particular gesture sit in the spectrum of the rude and obscene? History has its suggestions, but no easy answers.
Poking a hole in some theories
Evidence of the middle finger being used in various insulting gestures dates back to Aristophanes' time, according to Max Nelson, a professor of Greek and Roman studies at the University of Windsor. Aristophanes referenced the finger in multiple works in the 420s B.C.
Later, in the second and third centuries A.D., Greek philosopher Epictetus and biographer Diogenes Laertius referenced it in their own writings.
The meaning of the finger likely differed from today, however. The way they gave it probably did, too. It sometimes involved snapping the middle finger and thumb, which was considered insulting. Other times, the middle finger was used to point horizontally at another person as a sexual insult.
There is, in fact, no evidence linking those middle finger insults of the ancient Greeks and Romans to modern times, according to Nelson.
"People like to say things go back to the Greeks and Romans, but there are very few things that have this kind of longevity," Nelson said. "Especially hand gestures. They're very culturally specific and change quite a bit. So it's kind of ridiculous to say something has survived all the way since ancient times."
Which doesn't mean there isn't more recent history.
From Beaneaters to the Trudeaus
In more modern times, the middle finger likely began to emerge as an insult in the U.S. in the late 19th century, according to American University Prof. Ira Robbins. It was documented on American soil for the first time in an 1886 team photo of baseball's Boston Beaneaters and New York Giants.
Its use has grown exponentially since - even playing a role in international relations.
In 1968, American soldiers imprisoned in North Korea flipped the finger in propaganda photos, explaining to their captors it was a Hawaiian good luck sign. It took U.S. media reports for the North Koreans to catch on.
The finger is now known the world over, in part thanks to the influence of American media, according to a 2019 analysis by University of California, San Diego cognitive science professor Benjamin Bergen.
It also has one enduring and uniquely Canadian nickname: the Trudeau Salute.
That finds its origins in August 1982, when then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau was vacationing in the British Columbia Rockies. Protesters upset with the economic hardship in Canada at the time - unemployment shot up to 11 per cent that year, while GDP dropped nearly three per cent - tossed tomatoes at Trudeau's train, UPI reported at the time.
Trudeau came to the window. He "smiled, then scowled," one protester said, "then gave us the old Trudeau one-finger salute and pulled down the shade."
Trudeau would not be the last politician to do so. In May 2003, the front page of the Star showed Ontario MPP John O'Toole flipping off the opposition. In April 2004, Calgary East MP Deepak Obhrai was accused of giving a Scarborough MP the finger in the House of Commons. Last year, a Thunder Bay councillor was accused of flipping off residents.
And, of course, the middle finger has been weaponized as part of a countrywide political rally cry: "f--- Trudeau." In recent years, it has found its way onto bumper stickers, flags and hats.
A 'God-given' Charter right
While rude - and some would say unbecoming of elected officials - there appears little doubt that it is legal.
A 45-year-old Quebec teacher tested that out in 2021. Returning home from a walk in Beaconsfield, just outside Montreal, Neall Epstein gave double-fisted middle fingers to a neighbour with whom he had previously clashed over children playing in front of Epstein's home.
Epstein was arrested for criminal harassment and prosecuted. But in 2023, a judge acquitted him. He ruled that giving someone the middle finger is protected by the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
"It is not a crime to give someone the finger," the judge wrote in his decision. "Flipping the proverbial bird is a God-given, Charter enshrined right that belongs to every red-blooded Canadian. It may not be civil, it may not be polite, it may not be gentlemanly. Nevertheless, it does not trigger criminal liability."
The same was found by a U.S. appeals court in 2019. It ruled that flipping someone off is a form of free speech, matching at least two earlier cases that reached similar conclusions.
In 2018, a Vermont man was arrested for giving the finger to a state police trooper - only to have the charges of disorderly conduct dismissed. He then launched a lawsuit against the state and the trooper and received $175,000 US this June to settle it.
But how about a police officer flipping off a citizen, as that Toronto cop did?
That may run up against Ontario's Community Safety and Policing Act, which stipulates that officers "shall not conduct themselves in a manner that undermines, or is likely to undermine, public trust in policing."
It could also be at odds with the Toronto Police Service's values, which include acting professionally and "treating all people with empathy, respect, equity and dignity."
In 420 B.C. or 2024 A.D., the middle finger has never been that.
CAPTION: flippingbird_print.jpg

Public gets credit for Whitby hospital plan: Ford announces funding after being pressured by creative lobbying from town's mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/public-gets-credit-whitby-hospital-plan/docview/3091260644/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+gets+credit+for+Whitby+hospital+plan%3A+Ford+announces+funding+after+being+pressured+by+creative+lobbying+from+town%27s+mayor&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a strange end to Whitby Mayor Elizabeth Roy's public campaign to pressure Premier Doug Ford to finally commit funding to build a new hospital in her rapidly growing town.
Earlier this month, after almost 5,000 constituents had emailed pleas to Ford and his four Durham Region MPPs, and a combative Ford earlier said he would not be "bullied" by Roy, the premier casually confirmed, at a health-related news conference in Oshawa, "in addition, we're going to build a new Whitby hospital."
Surprised local reporters pressed him for details. Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones stepped up to confirm that, yes, the region's Lakeridge Health will get a capital planning grant to get the ball rolling on a major new hospital to be built in Whitby within the next decade. "Congratulations!" Jones added.
The abrupt confirmation that Whitby will host Durham's much-needed new hospital was the culmination of a long public dispute between a local mayor and the provincial premier, suggesting a way other municipalities might successfully fight Queen's Park.
Roy was not at the public announcement to celebrate, or to ask questions, because the Ford government had not invited or notified her. Somebody who was present relayed the big news to the mayor, who then tuned in to an online video feed of the event.
"I was ecstatic and surprised," Roy said later in an interview with the Star, adding she isn't invited to every provincial and federal announcement.
A spokesperson for the health minister, asked about the exclusion of Roy and the timing of the announcement years after an independent panel selected Whitby as the best site for a new Durham hospital, praised the region's Progressive Conservative MPPs.
"Our government is proud to have four strong advocates for the Durham Region at Queen's Park and we look forward to continuing to get it done across Durham," Hannah Jensen wrote in an email.
But Roy, asked how she got what she wanted from a premier who has flexed his power over local governments, and seems to relish fights in the courts and public square to confirm his authority, said she believes the reason is people power.
"It came about because the community spoke up," the mayor said, referring to a campaign she launched early this year after more traditional lobbying efforts failed to get the planning grant included in successive provincial budgets.
In February, Roy, a retired health-care worker who was elected mayor in 2022, stood at the planned hospital site near Hwy. 7 and Halls Road North, and told reporters: "We have seen overcrowded emergencies, long wait times, patients waiting up to three days to get into a hospital. That's unacceptable."
At successive events, she urged residents to visit a Whitby webpage to fill out a form urging the Ford government to act. She highlighted personal stories, including a man who said a family member waited in an emergency room for five days with a broken neck and ribs before being admitted to a hospital room.
"Thousands of emails were sent to Premier Ford and the four (PC) Durham MPPs - they were hearing loud and clear from the community the need for this hospital," she said. "You have to continue to advocate. You need to have the ability to have an uncomfortable conversation and do what we were elected to do."
Some feared her tactics had backfired. There was no planning grant in the March 26 provincial budget. Then, in April, Ford publicly rebuked Roy for incentivizing participation in the pressure campaign by giving people a chance to win raffle-type prizes.
"Folks, that is wrong," the premier told reporters. "You can't be doing that. Elected officials cannot be pulling these games," he said, adding that he wasn't going "to be bullied by that mayor out (there)," and the province would decide the Durham hospital site.
Roy rejected the criticism. She now says she believes the campaign worked, although the fact there is talk of Ford calling an early provincial election likely didn't hurt.
"There is speculation (that) it could be tied into a future election, but I think they knew that we were not going to go away quietly," said Roy, who in the past ran unsuccessfully for the provincial Liberals, but now says she has no party affiliation.
The pressure campaign and public sparring with Ford is similar to the 2019 fight by municipal officials in Toronto and other municipalities against deep cuts planned for public health funding. That included John Tory and Joe Cressy, then mayor and health committee chair, knocking on doors in PC-held Toronto ridings before Ford relented one year prior to the start of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, who is using a similar email-triggering webpage to lobby Ford to "take immediate action on homelessness and mental health issues in cities across Ontario," said voters' voices often have the greatest impact.
"Whenever you can bring the public to a conversation so that the elected officials aren't just hearing from the politicians, they are hearing from the people, that's always the most effective way," said Meed Ward, fronting the campaign as chair of the Big City Mayors caucus of the Association of Ontario Municipalities.
"Our campaign wasn't specifically inspired by Whitby's example - there's a long history of getting people speaking directly to their elected representatives," she added.
CAPTION: Whitby Mayor Elizabeth Roy said the province finally announced the new hospital because her community spoke up in an online campaign she launched to apply pressure.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 123 of 500

Doug Ford said he wouldn't be 'bullied' by this Ontario mayor. So how did she get her way?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-said-he-wouldnt-be-bullied-this-ontario/docview/3091144315/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+said+he+wouldn%27t+be+%27bullied%27+by+this+Ontario+mayor.+So+how+did+she+get+her+way%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a strange end to Whitby Mayor Elizabeth Roy's public campaign to pressure Premier Doug Ford to finally commit funding to build a new hospital in her rapidly growing town.
Last week, after almost 5,000 constituents had emailed pleas to Ford and his four Durham Region MPPs, and a combative Ford earlier said he would not be "bullied" by Roy, the premier casually confirmed, at a health-related news conference in Oshawa, "in addition, we're going to build a new Whitby hospital."
Surprised local reporters pressed him for details. Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones stepped up to confirm that, yes, the region's Lakeridge Health will get a capital planning grant to get the ball rolling on a major new hospital to be built in Whitby with the next decade. "Congratulations!" Jones added.
The abrupt confirmation that Whitby will host Durham's much-needed new hospital was the culmination of a long public dispute between a local mayor and the provincial premier, suggesting a way other municipalities might successfully fight Queen's Park.
Roy was not at the public announcement to celebrate, or to ask questions, because the Ford government had not invited or notified her. Somebody who was present relayed the big news to the mayor, who then tuned in to an online video feed of the event.
"I was ecstatic and surprised," Roy said later in an interview with the Star, adding she isn't invited to every provincial and federal announcement.
A spokesperson for the health minister, asked about the exclusion of Roy and the timing of the announcement years after an independent panel selected Whitby as the best site for a new Durham hospital, praised the region's Progressive Conservative MPPs.
"Our government is proud to have four strong advocates for the Durham Region at Queen's Park and we look forward to continuing to get it done across Durham," Hannah Jensen wrote in an email.
But Roy, asked how she got what she wanted from a premier who has flexed his power over local governments, and seems to relish fights in the courts and public square to confirm his authority, said she believes the reason is people power.
"It came about because the community spoke up," the mayor said, referring to a campaign she launched early this year after more traditional lobbying efforts failed to get the planning grant included in successive provincial budgets.
In February, Roy, a retired health-care worker who was elected mayor in 2022, stood at the planned hospital site near Hwy. 7 and Halls Road North, and told reporters: "We have seen overcrowded emergencies, long wait times, patients waiting up to three days to get into a hospital. That's unacceptable."
At successive events, she urged residents to visit a Whitby webpage to fill out a form urging the Ford government to act. She highlighted personal stories, including a man who said a family member waited in an emergency room for five days with a broken neck and ribs before being admitted to a hospital room.
"Thousands of emails were sent to Premier Ford and the four (PC) Durham MPPs - they were hearing loud and clear from the community the need for this hospital," she said. "You have to continue to advocate. You need to have the ability to have an uncomfortable conversation and do what we were elected to do."
Some feared her tactics had backfired. There was no planning grant in the March 26 provincial budget. Then, in April, Ford publicly rebuked Roy for incentivizing participation in the pressure campaign by giving people a chance to win raffle-type prizes.
"Folks, that is wrong," the premier told reporters. "You can't be doing that. Elected officials cannot be pulling these games," he said, adding that he wasn't going "to be bullied by that mayor out (there)," and the province would decide the Durham hospital site.
Roy rejected the criticism. She now says she believes the campaign worked, though the fact that there is talk of Ford calling an early provincial election likely didn't hurt.
"There is speculation (that) it could be tied into a future election, but I think they knew that we were not going to go away quietly," said Roy, who in the past ran unsuccessfully for the provincial Liberals, but now says she has no party affiliation.
The pressure campaign and public sparring with Ford is similar to the 2019 fight by municipal officials in Toronto and other municipalities against deep cuts planned for public health funding. That included John Tory and Joe Cressy, then mayor and health committee chair, knocking on doors in PC-held Toronto ridings before Ford relented one year prior to the start of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, who is using a similar email-triggering webpage to lobby Ford to "take immediate action on homelessness and mental health issues in cities across Ontario," said voters' voices often have the greatest impact.
"Whenever you can bring the public to a conversation so that the elected officials aren't just hearing from the politicians, they are hearing from the people, that's always the most effective way," said Meed Ward, fronting the campaign as chair of the Big City Mayors caucus of the Association of Ontario Municipalities.
"Our campaign wasn't specifically inspired by Whitby's example - there's a long history of getting people speaking directly to their elected representatives," she added.
Meed Ward said the rumoured early election call might help focus the premier's attention on "the single most important issue for our caucus, and something we've been asking the government for action on for a number of years."
Though Roy said it was voters' voices and not hers that finally got the hospital pledge, she's the one who will now work with Ford to help ensure Whitby and its neighbours get the health-care resources they have long sought.
"It's about moving forward in the moment," Roy said, when asked about past tensions with Ford. The mayor said she texted Ford her thanks after the announcement.
"He responded with a positive response and we have sent him a followup email asking to have that conversation."
CAPTION: Elizabeth Roy, mayor of Whitby, said the province finally picked her town as the site of a new hospital because her community spoke up in campaign she launched to pressure the government.

Subject: Provincial elections; Mayors; Voters; Public officials; Anniversaries

Document 124 of 500

Democratic rights are being squandered and lost along with precious land

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/democratic-rights-are-being-squandered-lost-along/docview/3091144309/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Democratic+rights+are+being+squandered+and+lost+along+with+precious+land&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Environmental and residents groups call new provincial rules barring OLT third-party appeals an attack on 'citizen rights,' Aug. 6
With my community, I was part of an Ontario Joint Board hearing (now known as the Ontario Land Tribunal) that successfully protected source water and endangered species from a gravel mining proposal on Mount Nemo in Burlington, Ont. We hired independent experts to make our case, and the evidence we presented was instrumental in the board's decision to deny the quarry. As citizens, we had real stake in the outcome. Our well water, air quality and surrounding environment were at risk. Removing a community's right to participate threatens to remove decision maker's access to the on-the-ground knowledge and concerns of locals. Developers have the right to appeal local government decisions to the OLT. Taking away citizen's equal rights encourages poorer planning and riskier development. The Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act (Bill 185) bolsters corporate dominance, and renders the democratic voice of the people mute.
Sarah Harmer, Burlington
Reporter Noor Javed's excellent article about new rules barring OLT third party appeals illustrates how our democratic rights are being trampled. The anger is growing daily in this region about Premier Doug Ford's hypocritical statements about "governing for the people" as precious farmland and natural spaces are being squandered and lost. Our community of Ball's Bridge and Little Lakes Road in southwestern Ontario has invested years of time and thousands of dollars to try to protect a natural area well loved by the public. We have been abandoned by our local council and have now been abandoned by the provincial government.
Rebecca Garrett, Goderich, Ont.
Bill 185 denies citizens groups from speaking up to protect our precious places. Is this this fair? Is this democratic? Absolutely not. We must fight for our right to be heard!
Wendy Hoernig, Goderich, Ont.
Since the enactment of Bill 185, the right of citizen groups to influence, challenge or contest planning decisions pushed by developers has been stripped away. This egregious action continues to erode our civil society and our democratic process. This seems to be the unspoken Tory agenda. We are "Open for Business" but only for those who are developers. This is hardly "a government for the people." The worst kind of hypocrisy is to claim citizens are holding up development through appeals when the data shows that many of the appeals recorded over the past decade are by the developers themselves. Shame on the Ontario Conservatives for the their attack on democracy. Shame on MPP Jill Dunlop for supporting this. Bill 185 must be repealed.
David Jeffery, Tiny, Ont.
CAPTION: Ontario residents can no longer appeal development decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal after the Ford government introduced legislation that removes the ability of third-parties to do so.

Subject: Equal rights

Document 126 of 500

Poilievre vows tariffs on Chinese EVs: Ottawa working on policy response aimed at safeguarding Canada's industry from competition

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-vows-tariffs-on-chinese-evs/docview/3091094090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+vows+tariffs+on+Chinese+EVs%3A+Ottawa+working+on+policy+response+aimed+at+safeguarding+Canada%27s+industry+from+competition&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is adding his voice to the chorus of calls for stiff tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, pledging he'd enact a punitive tax regime should his party win the next election.
That pledge could end up a moot point, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government currently working on a policy response aimed at safeguarding Canada's burgeoning EV industry from Chinese competition, a move that would also represent efforts to align Canadian policy with the U.S approach.
Still, Poilievre's decision to weigh in - a moment that marks a rare show of unity with Premier Doug Ford - is a new foray into trade and tax policy for the federal Conservative leader, and particularly on the EV industry. He has not, for example, said whether he'd continue the billions of dollars in support the federal government is currently giving the EV battery manufacturing sector, and wasn't asked about during Friday's event.
But he said a Conservative government would introduce a 100 per cent tariff on made-in-China electric vehicles, a 50 per cent tariff on semiconductors and solar cells, and a 25 per cent tariff on other related products and parts. He would also remove rebates for made-in-China EVs. The tariff regime Poilievre is proposing would match the one unveiled by U.S. President Joe Biden earlier this year, a move that led to the Trudeau government's current work on creating a Canadian approach. Public consultations ended on Aug. 1.
Speaking Friday during a campaign-style event at a Hamilton steel plant, Poilievre had harsh words for China, accusing the country's government of stealing western technology and exploiting weak labour and environmental standards. "They're doing this with a goal of crushing our steel, our aluminum and our automotive production and taking our jobs away," he said, echoing comments the federal Liberals and Ford have made about the issue.
But he also accused the Liberals of doing nothing about it. "Trudeau has failed to protect our workers against this economic predation."
A spokesperson for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland called it "incredibly rich" for Poilievre to try to repackage the Liberal government's own position action is necessary to protect Canadian autoworkers and the EV sector from Chinese actions.
"Poilievre is two months late to the conversation and is showing his hypocrisy, given his long anti-union record and continuous efforts to delay the implementation of the government's major investment tax credits, which are essential to securing Canada's auto sector's competitiveness in the 21st century," Katherine Cuplinskas said in an email.
But given that the Liberals haven't actually said what kind of tariff system they would impose, suggesting that Poilievre is merely repackaging something the government has already proposed is simply not true, a spokesperson for the Conservative leader said.
Government consultations on a possible response closed on Aug. 1, and Cuplinskas said it will have more to say "soon" on the issue.
China is the largest manufacturer and exporter of electric vehicles in the world, and has ramped up exports, which jumped by 20 per cent last month, as domestic demand lags. Its critics accuse China of deliberately oversupplying the global market to drive down competition, and there are concerns that cheaper Chinese vehicles will undercut sales of North American products, in turn undermining the billions Ottawa is putting into creating a domestic industry.
The only made-in-China EVs currently sold in Canada are from Tesla, and they are subject to a six-per-cent import tariff.
But despite the threat of much larger tariffs, the massive Chinese EV maker BYD has plans to enter the Canadian market. BYD sold more three million vehicles last year, and its entry-level model has a starting price of about $13,000. The cheapest EVs currently for sale in Canada cost about three times as much.
The European Union has also imposed provisional tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles.
On Friday, China filed a complaint with the World Trade Organization, saying the tariffs violate WTO rules and undermine global co-operation on climate change.
With files from
the Associated Press
CAPTION: Speaking Friday during a campaign-style event at a Hamilton steel plant, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre had harsh words for China, accusing the country's governmentof stealing western technology and exploiting weak labour and environmental standards.Cathie CowardThe Hamilton Spectator
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star With files from the Associated Press

Document 128 of 500

Arbiter says MPP staffer was fired without cause: Mantha was removed from NDP caucus after allegations he sexually harassed now-sacked worker

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/arbiter-says-mpp-staffer-was-fired-without-cause/docview/3091093795/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Arbiter+says+MPP+staffer+was+fired+without+cause%3A+Mantha+was+removed+from+NDP+caucus+after+allegations+he+sexually+harassed+now-sacked+worker&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A constituency assistant fired by the Ontario NDP after being sexually harassed by MPP Michael Mantha was not terminated for just cause, an arbitration decision has found.
Mantha was suspended from caucus in April 2023 and then permanently removed last August, after a workplace investigator found he pressured the woman to "engage in non-consensual sexual interactions with him," required her to regularly hug him, asked her to send sexually explicit photos of herself and to share sexual dreams about him, and pressured her to work from the office so he could be near her and "continue his practice of sexually touching her."
Mantha did not seek to participate in the arbitration proceedings, despite being notified by the NDP, arbitrator Jasbir Parmar said in the decision released Thursday.
Reached by phone Friday afternoon, Mantha told the Star he had not yet seen the arbitration decision, but added "there are many things that I didn't agree with" regarding the workplace investigation findings. He said he did not participate in the arbitration proceedings following advice from lawyers, and that he would review the decision with them before commenting.
At Queen's Park, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Mantha, who now sits as an Independent MPP, should resign from the legislature.
"As leader, I take my responsibility to our staff and their safety very seriously. It's why I took swift action to remove MPP Mantha from my caucus when allegations against him were substantiated by an independent, third-party investigation," Stiles said Friday.
"In light of the arbitration decision being made public, I want to reaffirm the Ontario NDP's commitment to zero tolerance for sexual violence and harassment. Elected officials at all levels must be held to a higher standard. Those who take advantage of the trust and power they have been given are unfit to remain in office. They must be held accountable for their actions."
According to Parmar's decision, the woman first started working for the Algoma-Manitoulin MPP in his Elliot Lake office in 2015. She went on a paid leave of absence after raising concerns to the NDP in August 2022 about an "unsafe work environment," with the Canadian Office and Professional Employees union filing a grievance on her behalf later that year. The NDP hired an external workplace investigator in January 2023 to look into the situation.
In January 2024, just over four months after Mantha was permanently removed from caucus due to the workplace investigation findings, the woman received a letter from the NDP caucus indicating that "given the fact that MPP Mantha no longer held an elected seat within the ONDP Caucus," her employment no longer fell under the collective agreement and had been terminated as of April 1, 2023, Parmar wrote in the arbitration decision.
The union then filed another grievance in January 2024 saying the woman had been unjustly terminated.
Because NDP political aides at Queen's Park and in constituency offices are unionized, Mantha's staff all lost their positions in the union's bargaining unit when he was removed from caucus last year. Sources say the woman in question remained on the NDP payroll months after her unionized job was eliminated.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter With files from Robert Benzie

Document 129 of 500

'Nothing to hide,' in Greenbelt probe, premier insists: RCMP interviews witnesses linked to Ford government in criminal probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nothing-hide-greenbelt-probe-premier-insists/docview/3091092770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Nothing+to+hide%2C%27+in+Greenbelt+probe%2C+premier+insists%3A+RCMP+interviews+witnesses+linked+to+Ford+government+in+criminal+probe&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: RCMP detectives investigating the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal are interviewing witnesses linked to Premier Doug Ford's government.
As first disclosed by the Star, current and former Progressive Conservative aides are sitting down with the Mounties to discuss Ford's controversial 2022 decision to open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt to housing development.
"We have nothing to hide," the premier told reporters in Thunder Bay on Friday, three hours after the Star broke the news.
"Come in and do whatever you have to do ... but I want full co-operation - they know that - because there's nothing to hide there. Let's get on with it," said Ford.
Interviews with members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that probes political crimes and corruption, are being held at the force's Greater Toronto Area detachment.
In a statement Friday, Ford's office confirmed that aides and former staffers were being interviewed.
"We've always said we would co-operate. That co-operation would include the premier and current or former staff conducting interviews as witnesses, which are currently underway," the premier's office said. "Any further questions should be directed to the RCMP."
The Royal Canadian Mounted Police would only "confirm that the investigation is ongoing."
But officials close to Ford said the premier has not yet been approached by the RCMP.
The Star is not identifying anyone who has been contacted by the Mounties because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling - or will tell - detectives.
But sources say the RCMP appears to be focusing on potential witnesses, rather than anyone actually suspected of wrongdoing.
The revelation came on the first anniversary of the auditor general's explosive report into the land swap that ultimately triggered the police investigation.
Ford scrapped his Greenbelt land-swap scheme last Sept. 21 in the wake of an Aug. 9 report by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk that found developers connected to his Progressive Conservatives had been "favoured" in the government's surprise move to allow houses to be built on the environmentally sensitive land.
Lysyk, who told the Star this week she has not been contacted by the Mounties, said the rezoning ballooned the value of the 15 properties in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to $8.28 billion.
Her 97-page report - and a subsequent review by integrity commissioner J. David Wake released on Aug. 30, 2023 - appear to be serving as guidebooks for the RCMP investigation.
Some of the witnesses now being interviewed were named by Wake in his 166-page report.
He interviewed 62 people - most of whom were in the presence of their legal counsel - during the course of his review.
News that the RCMP investigation is heating up comes as Ford, whose Tories have a large lead in public opinion polls, has been considering an early election call next year instead of waiting for the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Last month's Abacus Data tracking poll found his PCs at 44 per cent - their highest level of support in more than two years - with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians July 16-21 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Two of Ford's ministers and two senior political aides resigned last summer after the Lysyk and Wake reports.
With his popularity plummeting last September, the premier reversed course on his Greenbelt scheme.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," Ford said on Sept. 21.
"As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives," he said.
But Stiles said "one year after the auditor general's revelations about Ford's Greenbelt grab, he hasn't learned a thing."
"You deserve a government that tells the truth. That cares about regular people and does right by them. A government that isn't under criminal investigation," the NDP leader said.
Crombie said Friday was "a sad day for the people of Ontario, who deserve and need so much more than a government embroiled in criminal investigation."
"The people of this province need to know exactly how Doug Ford was involved in this scandal. Ontario deserves better," the Liberal leader said.
Schreiner, for his part, maintained "the premier has not learned from the Greenbelt scandal."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford scrapped his Greenbelt land-swap scheme last September in the wake of a report by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk that found developers connected to his Progressive Conservatives had been "favoured" in the government's surprise move to allow houses to be built on the environ- mentally sensitive land.Tara WaltonTHE CANADIAN PRESSfile photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 130 of 500

Thanks Toronto. Love, the cast: As they finished making 'The Umbrella Academy' here in our city, the actors were 'having a blast'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/thanks-toronto-love-cast/docview/3091092768/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Thanks+Toronto.+Love%2C+the+cast%3A+As+they+finished+making+%27The+Umbrella+Academy%27+here+in+our+city%2C+the+actors+were+%27having+a+blast%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On a sunny day in May 2023, the end still seemed far away for "The Umbrella Academy."
Inside a studio in Etobicoke, the main cast members (minus Justin H. Min) were shooting on a set meticulously made to look like a vacant art deco department store.
The inevitable goodbyes would come, of course - the series' fourth and final season debuted Thursday on Netflix - but, at that moment, the actors were having fun.
Viktor Hargreeves (Elliot Page) was lying unconscious at the foot of an out-of-order escalator while his adopted siblings tried to protect him from a monster.
Ritu Arya, who plays Lila, wife to Diego Hargreeves (David Castañeda), chuckled during a shooting break about how the actors were "just looking at air" instead of a monster, to be added later via visual effects.
"And then David had to run through. They're gonna green screen him to do a flip, and then hop onto the escalator and run up (like) a superhero.
"It's going to look really cool after the VFX, but when you're standing there watching it with no music, with no tension, he literally just does this kind of little hop and a little yelp, and someone throws a little debris on his leg and we're all just laughing so hard, like, sorry, but it just looks so funny," she said. "Yeah, we're having a blast."
Indeed, a small group of journalists, watching on monitors, had seen Castañeda run, yell and jump several times, a crew member crouching nearby to throw the debris (the result, in the final edit, of shelving destroyed by the irate monster).
"Did you guys catch Diego's escalator moment?" Robert Sheehan, who plays Klaus Hargreeves, asked us later.
"In the edit that's going to look phenomenal. But in reality it looks like what on earth is he doing?"added Tom Hopper (Luther Hargreeves).
"I feel like Chris Farley," joked Castañeda.
Funny escalator moments aside, the cast were optimistic that the final season will deliver for fans of "The Umbrella Academy," based on a comic-book series of the same name by Gerard Way and Gabriel Bá about a dysfunctional family of super-powered stepsiblings adopted by a billionaire father who is a (literal) alien.
The brothers and one sister, who over three seasons have repeatedly saved the world from apocalypses they inadvertently caused, lost their powers at the end of Season 3.
As Season 4 begins, they're living relatively ordinary but not necessarily happy lives, reuniting at a birthday party for one of Diego and Lila's kids. Then Viktor is kidnapped and the fam is off on another mission. Needless to say, they get their powers back - after a fashion - and there is another apocalypse brewing. Their main antagonists this season are a cult known as the Keepers, led by husband and wife Gene and Jean Thibodeau (guest stars and real-life married couple Nick Offerman and Megan Mullally).
The action is packed into six episodes instead of the usual 10, which the cast seemed to find invigorating.
"Every scene you come in for, there's so much weight to it," said British actor Hopper. "It means something, you know, there's no scenes that are there for no reason."
Added American actor Aidan Gallagher (Number Five), who was just 15 when the show debuted in 2019, "I've had more fun this year than any other just because everything's so tight and also, this year (is) different in a lot of ways, which breathed a lot of life into it. And Five has this really fulfilling emotional arc this year."
Emotional arcs for all the characters have been lurking throughout the show behind the action set-pieces and world-saving hijinks.
"We're in this kind of superhero genre, but the core of it is this deeply emotional story ultimately about these kids who had a really abusive childhood and how that's manifested as adults, and how they're struggling through that and finding themselves all in their own different and unique ways," said Canadian actor Page.
"(From) where their relationships started in Season 1, and then look to now and you go, 'Oh my god, they've all travelled so far individually,'" added Hopper.
They have travelled as a team as well. Over three seasons, we've watched the Hargreeves siblings grow from embittered near strangers into a family, a bond that's emphasized in this final poignant season.
Bonds have formed between the actors too.
"I'm really gonna miss the people," said British actor Arya of her fellow cast members. "I think it's rare to do a job where you're doing it for so long and you really love coming to work, and you love the people you're working with, and you laugh this much and you feel this connected as a family. It's really set a high bar."
"It's bittersweet," added American actor Emmy Raver-Lampman, who plays Allison. "But I also feel really lucky to go into a fourth season knowing that it's the end, so (you don't) take every moment for granted and (you) enjoy the time. A lot of shows don't get that ... and you don't get to say proper goodbyes."
It wasn't just cast and crew members the actors expected to miss; it was Toronto itself, where shooting has been based since "The Umbrella Academy" began.
"I loved Toronto," said Arya. "I feel like it's a second home ... I've spent more time here than any other place other than the U.K."
"It's my preferred Canadian city," added Raver-Lampman. "The second it starts to get warm, it's like overnight the whole city changes and everybody's outside."
"Toronto in May is just heaven, especially if you've endured the sh-ty winter," agreed Irish actor Sheehan.
That raises the not so enjoyable aspect of shooting in Toronto and the GTA. Every single actor, when asked to name the most challenging part of making "The Umbrella Academy," mentioned the cold.
Filming on a back lot in Pickering last winter - standing in for the fictional town of New Grumpson, Maine - was particularly memorable.
It was "essentially just a collection of shipping containers in a field, so there's no indoors, you know?"said Sheehan.
"We were out there for two weeks."
"And it just so happened to be some of the coldest days of the year when we were out there. So the wind chill itself was brutal," added Hopper.
There was also a notable winter shoot outside an East York dry cleaner that lasted until 2 in the morning; fellow actor Min's mouth was so numb he couldn't say his first line, said Hopper.
But, all in all, that was a small inconvenience while making a show that the cast said had boosted their careers, and enabled them to grow as people and as actors.
"What a true gift it's been," said Page. Though the series was ending, "I can't be sad to get to have the amount of seasons we've had to live with the characters. It's something I always wanted the opportunity to do as an actor.
"We're so fortunate that audiences responded the way they did."
The final season of "The
Umbrella Academy" is now streaming on Netflix.
The 'Umbrella Academy'
black book
We asked the "Umbrella Academy" cast for their favourite places to spend time in Toronto. Whether it was spots to walk their dogs, or to get food or coffee, they had a lot to share.
Outdoor spaces
1 High Park, 1873 Bloor St. W.
2 Trinity Bellwoods Park, 790 Queen St. W.
3 Toronto Beaches Boardwalk: "I walk up and down all the time for miles" - Emmy Raver-Lampman
Cultural spaces
4 Art Gallery of Ontario, 317 Dundas St. W.
5 Kensington Market
6 Toronto Public Library Bloor/Gladstone Branch, 1101 Bloor St. W.: "I've been in the libraries quite a bit this season" - Robert Sheehan
Restaurants and bars
7 Amal Toronto, 131 Bloor St. W.
8 Bar Isabel, 797 College St.: "They do great tapas" - Ritu Arya
9 Defina Wood Fired, 1485 Dupont St.
10 Gusto 101, 101 Portland St.
11 Impact Kitchen, various locations
12 Kasa Moto, 115 Yorkville Ave.: "That's great sushi to me" - Tom Hopper
13 La Palma, 849 Dundas St. W.
14 Matty's Pattys, 923A Queen St. W.: "Matty's Pattys is my favourite burger joint" - Emmy Raver-Lampman
15 The Three Speed, 1163 Bloor St. W.
16 Tumi Dumpling House, 2904 Dundas St. W.: "Very nice Chinese dumplings if you're a fan" - Robert Sheehan
17 Venga Cucina, 3076 Dundas St. W.: "Best pizza in Toronto I've come across" - Tom Hopper

A list, provided by Netflix, of places "The Umbrella Academy" shot in Season 4.
Toronto
Linsmore Tavern The bar at 1298 Danforth Ave. stood in for the interior of a bar run by Viktor Hargreeves (Elliot Page) in Nova Scotia.
Mega Cleaners The dry cleaner at 41 Laird Ave. was turned into Sy's One-Hour Dry Cleaning, where the Hargreeves went to rescue a kidnapped Viktor.
Sunnybrook Park Ben (Justin H. Min) and new character Jennifer (Toronto actor Victoria Sawal) take a stroll through the park at 1132 Leslie St., also used as background in other scenes.
Valley Halla Villa A greenhouse on the grounds of the former home of health food magnate Robert Jackson is the site of important conversations between Five (Aidan Gallagher) and Lila (Ritu Arya). (The site isn't public and can't be visited.)
Lower Bay Station Although it wasn't on Netflix's list, Five and Lila spend some time riding the subway. Production designer Andrew M. Stearn said footage of the abandoned TTC station, below Bay station at 1240 Bay St., was blended with a replica built at the studio.
Burlington
Lil' Monkeys Indoor Playground The kids' play space at 3250 Harvester Rd. hosts the birthday party for Diego and Lila's daughter Grace.
Georgetown
Scotsdale Heritage Farm This rural property at 13807 Trafalgar Rd. is the hideout for Keepers Gene and Jean (Nick Offerman and Megan Mullally) and their alpacas.
Brantford
108/110 Colborne St. Members of the Keepers have a showdown with the Umbrella Academy members here, outside a department store that's under renovation.

'We have nothing to hide,' Doug Ford says as RCMP interviews potential witnesses linked to Ontario government in Greenbelt criminal probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-have-nothing-hide-doug-ford-says-as-rcmp/docview/3091195711/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 132 of 500

Pierre Poilievre calls for stiff tariffs, punitive taxes on Chinese-made EVs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-calls-stiff-tariffs-punitive/docview/3091195680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+calls+for+stiff+tariffs%2C+punitive+taxes+on+Chinese-made+EVs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is adding his voice to the chorus of calls for stiff tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, pledging he'd enact a punitive tax regime should his party win the next election.
That pledge could end up a moot point, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government currently working on a policy response aimed at safeguarding Canada's burgeoning EV industry from Chinese competition, a move that would also represent efforts to align Canadian policy with the U.S approach.
Still, Poilievre's decision to weigh in - a moment that marks a rare show of unity with Premier Doug Ford - is a new foray into trade and tax policy for the federal Conservative leader, and particularly on the EV industry.
He has not, for example, said whether he'd continue the billions of dollars in support the federal government is currently giving the EV battery manufacturing sector, and wasn't asked about during Friday's event.
But he said a Conservative government would introduce a 100-per-cent tariff on made-in-China electric vehicles, a 50-per-cent tariff on semiconductors and solar cells, and a 25-per-cent tariff on other related products and parts. He would also remove rebates for made-in-China EVs.
The tariff regime Poilievre is proposing would match the one unveiled by U.S. President Joe Biden earlier this year, a move that led to the Trudeau government's current work on creating a Canadian approach. Public consultations ended on Aug. 1.
Speaking Friday during a campaign-style event at a Hamilton steel plant, Poilievre had harsh words for China, accusing the country's government of stealing Western technology and exploiting weak labour and environmental standards.
"They're doing this with a goal of crushing our steel, our aluminum and our automotive production and taking our jobs away," he said, echoing comments the federal Liberals and Ford have made about the issue.
But he also accused the Liberals of doing nothing about it.
"Trudeau has failed to protect our workers against this economic predation," he said.
A spokesperson for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland called it "incredibly rich" for Poilievre to try to repackage the Liberal government's own position action is necessary to protect Canadian autoworkers and the EV sector from Chinese actions.
"Poilievre is two months late to the conversation and is showing his hypocrisy, given his long anti-union record and continuous efforts to delay the implementation of the government's major investment tax credits, which are essential to securing Canada's auto sector's competitiveness in the 21st century," Katherine Cuplinskas said in an email.
But given that the Liberals haven't actually said what kind of tariff system they would impose, suggesting that Poilievre is merely repackaging something the government has already proposed is simply not true, a spokesperson for the Conservative leader said.
Government consultations on a possible response closed on Aug. 1, and Cuplinskas said it will have more to say "soon" on the issue.
China is the largest manufacturer and exporter of electric vehicles in the world, and has ramped up exports, which jumped by 20 per cent last month, as domestic demand lags.
Its critics accuse China of deliberately oversupplying the global market to drive down competition, and there are concerns that cheaper Chinese vehicles will undercut sales of North American products, in turn undermining the billions Ottawa is putting into creating a domestic industry.
The only made-in-China EVs currently sold in Canada are from Tesla, and they are subject to a six-per-cent import tariff.
But despite the threat of much larger tariffs, the massive Chinese EV maker BYD has plans to enter the Canadian market. BYD sold more three million vehicles last year, and its entry-level model has a starting price of about $13,000. The cheapest EVs currently for sale in Canada cost about three times as much.
The European Union has also imposed provisional tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles.
On Friday, China filed a complaint with the World Trade Organization, saying the tariffs violate WTO rules and undermine global co-operation on climate change.
With files from the Associated Press
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal in June.

Constituency worker was sexually harassed by former NDP MPP, arbiter says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/constituency-worker-was-sexually-harassed-former/docview/3091195657/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Constituency+worker+was+sexually+harassed+by+former+NDP+MPP%2C+arbiter+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A constituency assistant fired by the Ontario NDP after being sexually harassed by MPP Michael Mantha was not terminated for just cause, an arbitration decision has found.
Mantha was suspended from caucus in April 2023 and then permanently removed last August, after a workplace investigator found he pressured the woman to "engage in non-consensual sexual interactions with him," required her to regularly hug him, asked her to send sexually explicit photos of herself and to share sexual dreams about him, and pressured her to work from the office so he could be near her and "continue his practice of sexually touching her."
Mantha did not seek to participate in the arbitration proceedings, despite being notified by the NDP, arbitrator Jasbir Parmar said in the decision released Thursday.
Reached by phone Friday afternoon, Mantha told the Star he had not yet seen the arbitration decision, but added "there are many things that I didn't agree with" regarding the workplace investigation findings. He said he did not participate in the arbitration proceedings following advice from lawyers, and that he would review the decision with them before commenting.
At Queen's Park, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Mantha, who now sits as an Independent MPP, should resign from the legislature.
"As leader, I take my responsibility to our staff and their safety very seriously. It's why I took swift action to remove MPP Mantha from my caucus when allegations against him were substantiated by an independent, third-party investigation," Stiles said Friday.
"In light of the arbitration decision being made public, I want to reaffirm the Ontario NDP's commitment to zero tolerance for sexual violence and harassment. Elected officials at all levels must be held to a higher standard. Those who take advantage of the trust and power they have been given are unfit to remain in office. They must be held accountable for their actions."
According to Parmar's decision, the woman first started working for the Algoma-Manitoulin MPP in his Elliot Lake office in 2015. She went on a paid leave of absence after raising concerns to the NDP in August 2022 about an "unsafe work environment," with the Canadian Office and Professional Employees union filing a grievance on her behalf later that year. The NDP hired an external workplace investigator in January 2023 to look into the situation.
In January 2024, just over four months after Mantha was permanently removed from caucus due to the workplace investigation findings, the woman received a letter from the NDP caucus indicating that "given the fact that MPP Mantha no longer held an elected seat within the ONDP Caucus," her employment no longer fell under the collective agreement and had been terminated as of April 1, 2023, Parmar wrote in the arbitration decision.
The union then filed another grievance in January 2024 saying the woman had been unjustly terminated.
Because NDP political aides at Queen's Park and in constituency offices are unionized, Mantha's staff all lost their positions in the union's bargaining unit when he was removed from caucus last year. Sources say the woman in question remained on the NDP payroll months after her unionized job was eliminated.
Parmar found this week that the woman's employment "was frustrated through no fault of her own" following Mantha's removal from caucus "based on the substantiated findings" that he had sexually harassed and discriminated against her, and had created a toxic workplace.
"I have concluded that the grievor's employment was not terminated for just cause," Parmar wrote, sending the matter back to the NDP and the union to work out a solution.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: MPP Michael Mantha, Algoma-Manitoulin, right, with former Ontario NDP leader Andrea Horwath. An arbitration decision released this week found a worker in Mantha's constituency office was wrongly fired following Mantha's removal from caucus after a workplace investigator found he sexually harassed the woman.

Document 134 of 500

Carolyn Parrish is already failing Mississauga, and playing right into Doug Ford's hands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carolyn-parrish-is-already-failing-mississauga/docview/3090934496/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carolyn+Parrish+is+already+failing+Mississauga%2C+and+playing+right+into+Doug+Ford%27s+hands&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once again, we are drawn to the Potemkin façade that is Mississauga, Canada's seventh largest municipality. A largely forgetful and unremarkable place to many, why does this "city" demand our attention? Two words: Unequivocal failure. But not in the way you think.
"A Place to Grow," Mississauga is not. Buying a house in Mississauga is beyond the reach of most. Our public transit system is the envy of none. Our core identity, an inferiority complex. Did I mention condos are sprouting faster than weeds?
You could blame all of this on the decades of placid neglect known in Mississauga as the Hazel McCallion era. But believe it or not, thanks to Carolyn Parrish's gleeful adoption of Doug Ford's strong mayor powers, things may actually be getting worse in Canada's largest suburb.
Parrish's victory in June's mayoral by-election was heralded as a defining moment in the city's history. As Parrish herself noted, "Today we celebrate not just a victory at the polls but a victory for the future of our city". But that's wrong. If it was a victory for anyone, it was Doug Ford.
In her first few months on the job, Parrish managed to set her city up for failure on perhaps her most important file - affordable housing - while insulating Ford from blame in the process.
Here's how.
On July 1, 2023, the Province of Ontario granted 26 municipalities "Strong Mayor" powers. That same day, then Mississauga Mayor Bonnie Crombie delegated those powers, to hire and fire city officials, to the City Manager.
That seemed like a reasonable and pragmatic approach. And during the mayoral by-election, Parrish promised to follow suit, vowing to "avoid" using the strong mayor powers.
But within a month of her victory, Parrish reversed course. She fired the city manager and had herself appointed the new chair of the city's crucial planning and development committee. The city solicitor, meanwhile, resigned.
In a heartbeat, Parrish had concentrated all power to decide what type of housing gets built, who builds it and where it's built in her office. She also managed to play right into Ford's hands.
Parrish, like several of her Ontario counterparts (Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkins, for example), has fallen into a clumsy trap. Just like the Progressive Conservatives did under Mike Harris in 1996, the Ford government has created legislation- the Strong Mayors, Building Homes Act - that protect it from any criticism over policy failure.
Ford has empowered 26 municipalities to lead his charge to build 1.5 million news housing units in Ontario by 2031. Mississauga is expected to build 120,000. The problem is that Mississauga, like the rest of Ontario, needs more affordable housing, and these legislative changes do not encourage that in any meaningful way.
All the strong mayor powers do is concentrate decision making, and blame, in the offices of the mayors. That goes against the foundational principle of local government in Ontario: that it should be free from politics and partisanship.
But the other, more pressing problem, is that it does nothing to change an incentive structure that is already biased against affordable housing. Unlike private market housing, affordable housing doesn't provide municipal governments with precious development charges to fill their coffers. It also doesn't change the electoral calculus that makes building new affordable units in mature neighbourhoods so politically toxic. All it does is make those problems something the new "strong" mayors have to deal with, not the premier and his team.
Maybe we shouldn't be surprised that Parrish, who as a candidate refused to attend debates, would so brazenly break her own word as mayor. But we should be livid. She is buying into a system that makes local government in Ontario less effective, less efficient and less democratic. A system that does much to buy Doug Ford political cover and just about nothing to get more affordable housing built in a city that desperately needs it.
The city claims you now need a yearly gross income of almost $100,000 to afford a one-bedroom apartment in Mississauga. Let that figure sink in, $100,000. Are we really okay with $100,000 as the new poverty line?
If Parrish's election victory was a "victory for the future of our city", it certainly isn't a victory for those facing housing insecurity. So, we should be asking ourselves, "who is this Mississauga really for?", "who does it serve?", and can this Mayor be taken seriously?
I worry that the answers are already too clear.
CAPTION: New Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish at her June 24 inauguration inside the Living Arts Centre.
CREDIT: Ajay Sharma, contributor

Subject: Local elections; Mayors; Local government; Affordable housing; Special elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

TTC ordered to pay millions after years of delays + meet the bots backing Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-ordered-pay-millions-after-years-delays-meet/docview/3090934229/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+ordered+to+pay+millions+after+years+of+delays+%2B+meet+the+bots+backing+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, August 9 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the TTC's very expensive Pioneer Village station, Conrad Black's real estate wars and the bots backing Pierre Poilievre.
DON'T MISSThe TTC has been ordered to pay millions after years of delays
The TTC must pay the company that built Pioneer Village station almost $60 million after a court found the agency responsible for years of delays on the project, Ben Spurr reports. The station was one of six stops built as part of the Spadina subway extension - which opened two years late and hundreds of millions over budget. Take a look at what went wrong.
Go deeper: Walsh Construction claimed the TTC hadn't finalized its plans for the station when it ordered the company to start building, leading to thousands of revisions and additional costs it wasn't compensated for. The TTC argued Walsh shared responsibility.Ballooning budget: Even before the ruling, Pioneer Village had cost taxpayers far more than expected. Walsh's initial contract was for $166 million; it ended up costing $223 million.Wait, what? Among Walsh's grievances was a $2 million art installation that never ended up installed, after the TTC deemed it inappropriate - something that "should have been obvious to anyone with a scintilla of common sense," the judge wrote.Conrad Black sued his old friend for selling his "ancestral home"
The ex-media baron is suing his old friend Harold Peerenboom for $12 million after he sold Black's "ancestral home," Jacques Gallant reports. Peerenboom bought the Bridle Path mansion in 2016 for $14 million, after the CRA found Black owed $15 million in unpaid taxes. The two allegedly made a verbal contract to split the profits - but when it sold for $36.5 million last year, Black didn't see a dime.
The tea: The plan was for Peerenboom to lease the property back to Black, after the CRA placed liens on the nine-bedroom, 11-bathroom home over unpaid taxes. Black and his family moved out in 2020.Unfriended: The lawsuit is a far cry from how Black described his "old friend" of 50 years in a 2019 National Post column, writing how Peerenboom did him a "favour" by helping him "in residential matters when I was dealing with a pressing tax problem." Who really posted those awkward tweets praising a Pierre Poilievre rally?
If you've been on the website formerly known as Twitter lately, you might have encountered hundreds of awkwardly worded, near-identical posts praising Poilievre's rally last week - what experts call a coordinated bot operation. "This is pretty rudimentary - like, it's just copying and pasting and amplifying messages," an expert tells Alex Boyd. "It's a bit weird to me." Where are these posts coming from?
Word from Conservatives: "The CPC does not pay for bots and has no idea who is behind these accounts," a spokesperson said over email.Word from robots: The hundreds of posts from users who listed their locations as Russia, New York, the U.K. and more all said some variation of how they "just got back" from the rally in a small Ontario town and are "buzzing from the energy."Expert analysis: Given the lack of sophistication, the instigator could be an amateur looking to drum up support or someone testing the limits of what was possible with bots, an expert said. The campaign could also have been made easy to detect by design, intended to sow doubts about the strength of our democracy.WHAT ELSE
