Document 136 of 500

We have bigger problems than Polievre's name calling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-have-bigger-problems-than-polievres-name/docview/3090934160/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+have+bigger+problems+than+Polievre%27s+name+calling&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bigger problems than name calling
Canadians should reject Poilievre's indecency, Aug. 5
I have read the letters to the editor chastising federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre for his political style. Each of the six letters refer to the decency of Canadians. I take issue with each of these letters due to the rise of antisemitism in our Canadian society. This sickness and the lack of action by our political leaders and the apparent blindness and deafness of the Canadian public is far more of a serious threat to our society than the inappropriate name calling in the political arena. Let's get our priorities established regarding decency in the public discourse. Antisemitism is illogical, divisive, indecent and unfair. Is this surge of hate acceptable to my fellow Canadians?
Eric Bornstein, Toronto
Wow - six letters of outrage and indignation - how many more were not published? When gun violence erupts in the U.S., Americans parrot that this is not who they are. Even though Canadians say they are decent, polite and considerate people, the opposite is evident everywhere, even our House of Commons. True, social media abets and amplifies indecency, but dare we deny our ugly side? Both north and south of the border, we must hope that sanity and decency prevail in the coming elections. However, let's not hold our breath to see if it happens. We need to defend democracy by engaging in open, respectful and critical debate - then actually vote! In the process, let's hold the feet of indecent bullies to the fire, and let them prepare for the heat.
Salvatore Amenta, Stouffville, Ont.
There are no emergency coffees
Toronto police investigating viral video of officer allegedly giving middle finger to citizen, Aug. 6
We all know that the police live in a world far above that of humans. When they park in a loading zone to go in for a coffee, they consider it perfectly OK while any other human would get a ticket. When the violation was pointed out to them, they replied with an insulting finger gesture and told the interloper to be on his way. But even worse was when our Premier Doug Ford commented that the offending citizen should mind his own business, was harassing the officers and should fetch a coffee for the cops. I have no problem with police officers parking anywhere they want in an emergency, but was this an emergency coffee? The premier was far out of bounds on this one.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
When is the right time?
Political squabbles and finger pointing abound after the Jasper fire. For some leaders two words get left unsaid, Aug. 3
As contributor Graham Thomson pointed out, many politicians are reluctant to mention climate change, or refuse to even acknowledge its existence, when huge areas are on fire. It is similar to the gun debate in the U.S. where the aftermath of another mass shooting is said to be the wrong time to discuss limits on gun ownership. When, one wonders, would be an appropriate time?
David Steele, Saskatoon, Sask.
It is common to have unclaimed bodies
What happens to the dead if no one comes forward to claim them? In Ontario it's a growing - and costly - problem, Aug. 2
In 45 years of service as a United Church of Canada minister, I have presided at numerous funerals where it was just myself and the funeral directors present. Our common goal was simply dignity in death, something every person, identified or not, deserves. Thank you for presenting the rest of the story, a story which is more common than we might think.
David Shearman, Owen Sound, Ont.
Death can be a dirty business
Canadian funeral directors warn of unauthorized obituaries, Aug. 4
Only a few days after my wife's funeral in Toronto and before her burial in another city, the vultures were circling. I received a package from a real estate firm containing a booklet "Navigating Transitions - A Thoughtful Guide to Probate and Downsizing." I was shocked and appalled at the content and timing of the package. It certainly was not "thoughtful." The firm's agents must be scouring the obituaries in newspapers and funeral homes looking for leads. Under the nonsensical pretence of helping the bereaved, they were clearly fishing for a listing. The expertise of real estate firms is buying and selling properties, not providing guidance on wills and estates. For some macabre reason the agents of this firm seemed to think that downsizing and moving are top of mind to the bereaved, more relevant than laying their loved one to rest or the painful and lengthy process of grieving and adjusting to a new normal. How low can some people go? In essence, firms like this are the equivalent of ambulance chasers. Any marketing strategy that knowingly tries to solicit business from grieving and very fragile people is unethical, unprofessional, immoral, insensitive, crass, callous, offensive and flawed.
Tom Driedger, Toronto
Where are the broadcasting standards?
I am watching avidly the Olympic Games in Paris where human achievement is celebrated. So why is there a commercial for BetRivers, an online casino and sports betting company? It mocks past Olympic events and promotes online games which requires no physical effort. In such advertising, youth are encouraged to use devices to play rather than actually join sports of their choice. Is this what the Olympics should encourage?
Judy Cathcart, Guelph, Ont.
Douglas has good advice for Ford
Take it from a retired judge, Premier Ford - here's what you don't get about our judicial system, Aug. 6
Retired judge Norman Douglas rightly criticizes Premier Doug Ford for threatening to hold judges accountable for their decisions. He notes that Ford does not understand a system designed to presume innocence, in which the particular facts and merits of each case must be weighed. I add that Ford does not understand that in our democracy the judicial and legislative branches are intended to operate independently and in balance. It is no more Ford's job to reconsider judges' verdicts than it is the court's job to pass laws. But perhaps Ford's ignorance of our system is not surprising, given his consistent disregard for higher education. At least we still have beer.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby, Ont.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal on June 19.

Subject: Antisemitism; Canadians; Coffee

Location: United States--US

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 137 of 500

One year later, Ford's tone has shifted: Premier may be ready to move on, but his political opponents aren't and an investigation continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/one-year-later-fords-tone-has-shifted/docview/3090837486/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+year+later%2C+Ford%27s+tone+has+shifted%3A+Premier+may+be+ready+to+move+on%2C+but+his+political+opponents+aren%27t+and+an+investigation+continues&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Contrition may not quite be what it once was.
With Friday marking the first anniversary of the explosive auditor general's report into the Greenbelt land swap, Premier Doug Ford was asked about the lessons learned from a scandal that still looms over his Progressive Conservative government.
Ford shrugged at the reporter's question.
"We made that decision not to move forward with it - and we're going keep moving forward and building homes everywhere other than the Greenbelt," he said Wednesday in Mississauga.
"So that's it."
While the premier conceded his government was still in the process of implementing all 15 recommendations from then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk's report, his tone was a far cry from the plaintive plea when he cancelled the land deal at the end of last summer.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," Ford said on Sept. 21, 2023.
That was when he announced 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land would no longer be removed from the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
"As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives," he said at the time.
It was Lysyk's report - and a separate review by integrity commissioner J. David Wake released on Aug. 30, 2023 - that set in motion events that dog Ford's government to this day.
The RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the branch that investigates corruption and political crimes, has an ongoing probe of the affair.
Lysyk, who retired last September after a 10-year term as the financial watchdog, told the Star this week she has not been contacted by the RCMP.
The reports by her and Wake are widely viewed as potential road maps for police investigators because the independent legislative watchdogs had access to people who do not necessarily have to play ball with the Mounties.
She found developers with ties to Ford's Tories were "favoured" in the process led by then-housing minister Steve Clark's chief of staff, Ryan Amato, and said land they owned that was selected to be opened for housing construction could have soared in value to $8.28 billion as a result.
Amato, who resigned last August, has always denied any wrongdoing and his supporters at Queen's Park - including some past and present PC cabinet ministers - believe he has been unfairly scapegoated.
Clark, who has also insisted he did nothing wrong and has promised to co-operate with the Mounties, stepped down from cabinet last September, languishing as a backbencher for nine months until Ford promoted him to government house leader in the June 6 shuffle.
"He's one of the most experienced and qualified members we have - he knows Queen's Park inside and out. A very credible individual and I support him 1,000 per cent," the unrepentant premier said earlier this week.
Others seen as collateral damage remain politically sidelined.
Kaleed Rasheed resigned as Ford's business minister last September.
Rasheed no longer sits as a Tory MPP after misleading Wake about a 2020 Las Vegas trip with Shakir Rehmatullah, a developer who attended Ford's daughter's wedding and who had a stake in about 23 acres of land removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
The affable Mississauga East-Cooksville Independent MPP maintains he made "an honest mistake" on the date of the Vegas jaunt and is trying to return to the PC fold.
Jae Truesdell, who was also on that trip, quit as Ford's housing adviser after Rasheed's defenestration.
The imbroglio has remained in the headlines - eight weeks ago, the Star and the Narwhal shared the Michener Award, Canada's most prestigious journalism prize, for their work examining the Greenbelt affair.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation found eight of the 15 parcels of land affected had been bought up by developers after Ford's election in 2018 - when the properties were still protected.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Thursday that "people power, investigative journalism and a strong NDP opposition helped thwart Ford's plan to carve up the Greenbelt, but he hasn't learned a thing."
"A year later, the minister who took the fall is back ... and Ford is still making backroom deals that benefit wealthy developers over everyone else," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said, "Doug Ford isn't sorry for trying to give the Greenbelt away to his rich friends, he's only sorry he got caught."
"He even brought his friend, Steve Clark, the Greenbelt fall guy, back," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern Thursday that "the premier has not learned from the Greenbelt scandal."
"He is still refusing to legalize gentle density to build homes people can afford in the communities they love," said Schreiner. "And he is destroying farmland by imposing urban boundary expansions in places like Waterloo region that threaten the land that feeds us."
CAPTION: Those implicated in last year's auditor general and integrity commissioner reports into the Greenbelt land swap have been politically sidelined, including then minister of housing Steve Clark, shown with Premier Doug Ford last August.Cole Burston The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Scandals; Auditors reports; Investigations; Favoritism; Redevelopment; Criticism

Business indexing term: Subject: Auditors reports

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Title: One year later, Ford's tone has shifted:   Premier may be ready to move on, but his political opponents aren't and an investigation continues

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 9, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 138 of 500

Parrish is already failing Mississauga

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parrish-is-already-failing-mississauga/docview/3090837125/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parrish+is+already+failing+Mississauga&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once again, we are drawn to the Potemkin façade that is Mississauga, Canada's seventh-largest municipality. A largely forgetful and unremarkable place to many, why does this "city" demand our attention? Two words: Unequivocal failure. But not in the way you think.
"A Place to Grow," Mississauga is not. Buying a house in Mississauga is beyond the reach of most. Our public transit system is the envy of none. Our core identity, an inferiority complex. Did I mention condos are sprouting faster than weeds?
You could blame all of this on the decades of placid neglect known in Mississauga as the Hazel McCallion era. But believe it or not, thanks to Carolyn Parrish's gleeful adoption of Doug Ford's strong mayor powers, things may actually be getting worse in Canada's largest suburb.
Parrish's victory in June's mayoral byelection was heralded as a defining moment in the city's history. As Parrish herself noted, "Today we celebrate not just a victory at the polls but a victory for the future of our city." But that's wrong. If it was a victory for anyone, it was Doug Ford.
In her first few months on the job, Parrish managed to set her city up for failure on perhaps her most important file - affordable housing - while insulating Ford from blame in the process.
Here's how.
On July 1, 2023, the province of Ontario granted 26 municipalities "Strong Mayor" powers. That same day, then Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie delegated those powers, to hire and fire city officials, to the city manager.
That seemed like a reasonable and pragmatic approach. And during the mayoral byelection, Parrish promised to follow suit, vowing to "avoid" using the strong mayor powers.
But within a month of her victory, Parrish reversed course. She fired the city manager and had herself appointed the new chair of the city's crucial planning and development committee. The city solicitor, meanwhile, resigned.
In a heartbeat, Parrish had concentrated all power to decide what type of housing gets built, who builds it and where it's built in her office. She also managed to play right into Ford's hands.
Parrish, like several of her Ontario counterparts (Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkins, for example), has fallen into a clumsy trap. Just like the Progressive Conservatives did under Mike Harris in 1996, the Ford government has created legislation - the Strong Mayors, Building Homes Act - that protect it from any criticism over policy failure.
Ford has empowered 26 municipalities to lead his charge to build 1.5 million news housing units in Ontario by 2031. Mississauga is expected to build 120,000. The problem is that Mississauga, like the rest of Ontario, needs more affordable housing, and these legislative changes do not encourage that in any meaningful way.
All the strong mayor powers do is concentrate decision making, and blame, in the offices of the mayors. That goes against the foundational principle of local government in Ontario: that it should be free from politics and partisanship.
But the other, more pressing problem, is that it does nothing to change an incentive structure that is already biased against affordable housing. Unlike private-market housing, affordable housing doesn't provide municipal governments with precious development charges to fill their coffers. It also doesn't change the electoral calculus that makes building new affordable units in mature neighbourhoods so politically toxic. All it does is make those problems something the new "strong" mayors have to deal with, not the premier and his team.
Maybe we shouldn't be surprised that Parrish, who as a candidate refused to attend debates, would so brazenly break her own word as mayor. But we should be livid. She is buying into a system that makes local government in Ontario less effective, less efficient and less democratic. A system that does much to buy Doug Ford political cover and just about nothing to get more affordable housing built in a city that desperately needs it.
The city claims you now need a yearly gross income of almost $100,000 to afford a one-bedroom apartment in Mississauga. Let that figure sink in, $100,000. Are we really OK with $100,000 as the new poverty line?
If Parrish's election victory was a "victory for the future of our city," it certainly isn't a victory for those facing housing insecurity. So, we should be asking ourselves: "who is this Mississauga really for?"; "who does it serve?"; and can this mayor be taken seriously?
I worry that the answers are already too clear.
Ajay Sharma is a university lecturer in political science, focusing on local government and the environment.
CAPTION: By adopting Premier Doug Ford's "strong mayor" powers, Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish has insulated the premier from blame on her city's most important file, writes Ajay Sharma.Steve Cornwell Metrolandfile photo

CREDIT: Ajay Sharma contributor;

Subject: Local government; Affordable housing; Economic impact; Public transportation; Political power; Political leadership; Housing prices; Criticism

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing Economic impact Housing prices

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bigger+problems+than+name+calling+Canadians+should+reject+Poilievre+s+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Canadians+should+reject+Poilievre%27s+indecency%2C+Aug.+5+Toronto+police+investigating+viral+video+of+officer+allegedly+giving+middle+finger+to+citizen%2C+Aug.+6+Political+squabbles+and+finger+pointing+abound+after+the+Jasper+fire.+For+some+leaders+two+words+get+left+unsaid%2C+Aug.+3+What+happens+to+the+dead+if+no+one+comes+forward+to+claim+them%3F+In+Ontario+it%27s+a+growing+-+and+costly+-+problem%2C+Aug.+2+Canadian+funeral+directors+warn+of+unauthorized+obituaries%2C+Aug.+4+Take+it+from+a+retired+judge%2C+Premier+Ford+-+here%27s+what+you+don%27t+get+about+our+judicial+system%2C+Aug.+6&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bigger problems than name calling
Canadians should reject Poilievre's indecency, Aug. 5
I have read the letters to the editor chastising federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre for his political style. Each of the six letters refer to the decency of Canadians. I take issue with each of these letters due to the rise of antisemitism in our Canadian society. This sickness and the lack of action by our political leaders and the apparent blindness and deafness of the Canadian public is far more of a serious threat to our society than the inappropriate name calling in the political arena. Let's get our priorities established regarding decency in the public discourse. Antisemitism is illogical, divisive, indecent and unfair. Is this surge of hate acceptable to my fellow Canadians?
Eric Bornstein, Toronto
Wow - six letters of outrage and indignation - how many more were not published? When gun violence erupts in the U.S., Americans parrot that this is not who they are. Even though Canadians say they are decent, polite and considerate people, the opposite is evident everywhere, even our House of Commons. True, social media abets and amplifies indecency, but dare we deny our ugly side? Both north and south of the border, we must hope that sanity and decency prevail in the coming elections. However, let's not hold our breath to see if it happens. We need to defend democracy by engaging in open, respectful and critical debate - then actually vote! In the process, let's hold the feet of indecent bullies to the fire, and let them prepare for the heat.
Salvatore Amenta, Stouffville
There are no emergency coffees
Toronto police investigating viral video of officer allegedly giving middle finger to citizen, Aug. 6
We all know that the police live in a world far above that of humans. When they park in a loading zone to go in for a coffee, they consider it perfectly OK while any other humans would get a ticket. When the violation was pointed out to them, they replied with an insulting finger gesture and told the interloper to be on his way. But even worse was when Premier Doug Ford commented that the offending citizen should mind his own business, was harassing the officers and should fetch a coffee for the cops. I have no problem with police officers parking anywhere they want in an emergency, but was this an emergency coffee? The premier was far out of bounds on this one.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
When is the
right time?
Political squabbles and finger pointing abound after the Jasper fire. For some leaders two words get left unsaid,
Aug. 3
As contributor Graham Thomson pointed out, many politicians are reluctant to mention climate change, or refuse to even acknowledge its existence, when huge areas are on fire. It is similar to the gun debate in the U.S. where the aftermath of another mass shooting is said to be the wrong time to discuss limits on gun ownership. When, one wonders, would be an appropriate time?
David Steele, Saskatoon
Unclaimed bodies are not uncommon
What happens to the dead if no one comes forward to claim them? In Ontario it's a growing - and costly - problem,
Aug. 2
In 45 years of service as a United Church of Canada minister, I have presided at numerous funerals where it was just myself and the funeral directors present. Our common goal was simply dignity in death, something every person, identified or not, deserves. Thank you for presenting the rest of the story, a story which is more common than we might think.
David Shearman,
Owen Sound, Ont.
Death can be a dirty business
Canadian funeral directors warn of unauthorized obituaries, Aug. 4
Only a few days after my wife's funeral in Toronto and before her burial in another city, the vultures were circling. I received a package from a real estate firm containing a booklet "Navigating Transitions - A Thoughtful Guide to Probate and Downsizing." I was shocked and appalled at the content and timing of the package. It certainly was not "thoughtful." The firm's agents must be scouring the obituaries in newspapers and funeral homes looking for leads. Under the nonsensical pretence of helping the bereaved, they were clearly fishing for a listing. The expertise of real estate firms is buying and selling properties, not providing guidance on wills and estates. For some macabre reason, the agents of this firm seemed to think that downsizing and moving are top of mind to the bereaved, more relevant than laying their loved one to rest or the painful and lengthy process of grieving and adjusting to a new normal. How low can some people go? In essence, firms like this are the equivalent of ambulance chasers. Any marketing strategy that knowingly tries to solicit business from grieving and very fragile people is unethical, unprofessional, immoral, insensitive, crass, callous, offensive and flawed.
Tom Driedger, Toronto
Where are the broadcasting standards?
I am watching avidly the Olympic Games in Paris where human achievement is celebrated. So why is there a commercial for BetRivers, an online casino and sports betting company? It mocks past Olympic events and promotes online games, which require no physical effort. In such advertising, youth are encouraged to use devices to play rather than actually join sports of their choice. Is this what the Olympics should encourage?
Judy Cathcart, Guelph
Judge has good advice for Ford
Take it from a retired judge, Premier Ford - here's what you don't get about our judicial system, Aug. 6
Retired judge Norman Douglas rightly criticizes Premier Doug Ford for threatening to hold judges accountable for their decisions. He notes that Ford does not understand a system designed to presume innocence, in which the particular facts and merits of each case must be weighed. I add that Ford does not understand that in our democracy the judicial and legislative branches are intended to operate independently and in balance. It is no more Ford's job to reconsider judges' verdicts than it is the court's job to pass laws. But perhaps Ford's ignorance of our system is not surprising, given his consistent disregard for higher education. At least we still have beer.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby, Ont.

When the Greenbelt scandal broke, Doug Ford was all apologies. What a difference a year makes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-greenbelt-scandal-broke-doug-ford-was-all/docview/3090987636/se-2?accountid=15090

Others seen as collateral damage remain politically sidelined.
Kaleed Rasheed resigned as Ford's business minister last September.
Rasheed no longer sits as a Tory MPP after misleading Wake about a 2020 Las Vegas trip with Shakir Rehmatullah, a developer who attended Ford's daughter's wedding and who had a stake in about 23 acres of land removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
The affable Mississauga East-Cooksville Independent MPP maintains he made "an honest mistake" on the date of the Vegas jaunt and is trying to return to the PC fold.
Jae Truesdell, who was also on that trip, quit as Ford's housing adviser after Rasheed's defenestration.
The imbroglio has remained in the headlines - eight weeks ago, the Star and the Narwhal shared the Michener Award, Canada's most prestigious journalism prize, for their work examining the Greenbelt affair.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation found eight of the 15 parcels of land affected had been bought up by developers after Ford's election in 2018 - when the properties were still protected.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Thursday that "people power, investigative journalism and a strong NDP opposition helped thwart Ford's plan to carve up the Greenbelt, but he hasn't learned a thing."
"A year later, the minister who took the fall is back ... and Ford is still making backroom deals that benefit wealthy developers over everyone else," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said, "Doug Ford isn't sorry for trying to give the Greenbelt away to his rich friends, he's only sorry he got caught."
"He even brought his friend, Steve Clark, the Greenbelt fall guy, back," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern Thursday that "the premier has not learned from the Greenbelt scandal."
"He is still refusing to legalize gentle density to build homes people can afford in the communities they love," said Schreiner.
"And he is destroying farmland by imposing urban boundary expansions in places like Waterloo region that threaten the land that feeds us."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as Steve Clark, then his minister of housing, speaks during a news conference in Mississauga on Aug. 11, 2023.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals

Document 141 of 500

Who really posted those awkward tweets praising a Pierre Poilievre rally? Here's what might be going on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-really-posted-those-awkward-tweets-praising/docview/3090987068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+really+posted+those+awkward+tweets+praising+a+Pierre+Poilievre+rally%3F+Here%27s+what+might+be+going+on&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If online chatter was to be believed, the northern Ontario town of Kirkland Lake - founded on gold, sustained by hockey, population roughly 8,000 - was abuzz last week.
"Just got back from Pierre Poilievre's rally," read one post on X, formerly Twitter. The user, who wrote her bio in Spanish and listed her location as France, continued the emphatic message to her zero followers: "I'm buzzing from the energy! As a northerner, it's refreshing to see a leader who actually listens to our concerns and prioritizes our needs."
A nearly identical message was posted by a user with seven followers who listed their location as Russia - same buzz, same proud northern identity, same praise for the Conservative leader's "palpable" commitment to the region. Then came a message from New York, then another from the United Kingdom. Then another, and another, then dozens more. Between their boilerplate wording and the users' impersonal profiles, the signs seemed to point to a bot campaign, suggesting the posts likely came not from real people but computer programs designed to spread the same message - and fast.
Speculation that the Poilievre camp might have been behind the messages set politicos aflutter. The NDP has written to the Commissioner of Canada Elections asking for an investigation to ensure, among other things, that the Conservative Party of Canada hadn't employed the services of a bot farm. The Liberals called the suspected use of bots "extremely alarming," and urged their opponents to the support the Online Harms Act, which has taken steps toward labelling bot-amplified content. (Elsewhere, a Reddit thread titled "Pierre Poilievre Caught Red Handed Using Foreign Bots and I Can't Stop Laughing" had been upvoted some 3,700 times by Thursday afternoon.)
It's an allegation the Conservatives vehemently deny: "The CPC does not pay for bots and has no idea who is behind these accounts," party spokesperson Sarah Fischer said in an email. "We are seeking the support of actual Canadians, as witnessed by large in-person turnouts at our events."
But at a time when bots are increasingly recognized as a threat that can sway public opinion - and when people are also more likely than ever to recognize them - researchers point out something else about the pro-Poilievre messages: they aren't very good.
"This is pretty rudimentary - like, it's just copying and pasting and amplifying messages," said Fenwick McKelvey, an associate professor in the department of communication studies and co-director of the Applied AI Institute at Concordia University. "The way I would think about doing this is using a large language model to generate messages that are slightly varying in tone," he added, referring to artificial intelligence programs used to create text. "It's a bit weird to me."
There is no dispute the Poilievre event happened - a CTV news report that described the venue as "packed" showed the Conservative leader, dressed in an "Axe the Tax" T-shirt, addressing a standing-room-only crowd. There's nothing illegal about using bots, either.
But the allegation has put a new spotlight on Poilievre, who is known for his online savvy. He's also faced criticism for tactics that include tagging his official YouTube videos with a hashtag used by a misogynistic movement.
Still, the suspected use of bots - which researchers say isn't unique to the right side of the political spectrum - raises questions about who might be paying for them, what they're being used for, and whether those who deploy them are being up front about what they're doing.
Thanks to a lack of transparency from social media platforms, it's almost impossible to know who is behind a bot campaign, says Elizabeth Dubois, an associate professor and University Research Chair in qualitative communication and technology at the University of Ottawa.
But thanks to the overuse of unique, easy-to-search words like "buzzing" and seemingly international attention on an event in a rural outpost, where even the nearest regional airport is over an hour away, this campaign became noticeable. Indeed, it was almost immediately flagged by other users. The lack of sophistication suggests a couple of possibilities for who might be pulling the puppet strings, Dubois says.
The instigator could have been an amateur who was trying to drum up political support, she suggested, or someone who was just testing the limits of what's possible with bots for some future purpose and didn't care if their efforts were seen.
It's also possible this campaign was easy to detect by design. "Sometimes, the point of this is not to actually convince anybody that a bunch of people were at a rally and they were really jazzed about it," Dubois said.
"Sometimes the point of it is to make a conversation messy, to create confusion. That way, it doesn't actually matter that it's a whole bunch of copy-and-pasted stuff. And in fact, you want to be detected because - if that's the motivation behind it - your goal is to make people think, 'I don't know if I can trust my democracy anymore.'"
While it remains unclear who was behind the tweets in question, the general chaos created by bots, which can be relatively cheap and easy to spin up, is the reason why they tend to be useful as a foreign interference strategy, she says.
While bots have been used in Canadian politics as far back as 2012 - Dubois and McKelvey have written about an automated account that tweeted 11,000 times to promote the Coalition Avenir Quebec during the Quebec election that year - they've drawn growing attention for their potential for meddling in electoral processes.
The recent foreign interference inquiry heard about instances of bots completing polls during the 2021 federal election campaign that had been flagged afterwards by staffers. While they'd assumed it was a ploy to collect the $0.50 incentive for completing the survey, they still noted the tactic could be used to influence party or government policy.
There was also a so-called "spamouflage" campaign last summer, in which dozens of MPs from both sides of the political aisle were targeted by posts from new or hijacked social media accounts that claimed their targets had been accused of criminal or ethical violations.
Part of the issue is that social media companies aren't enforcing any rules about how much automation is allowed on their platforms, Dubois says. Studies suggest that networks of bots continue to operate on X despite owner Elon Musk's claims he would crack down on scams on the platform.
But it would also help if political parties would step up and agree to be open and transparent about how they're using new technologies like automation and artificial intelligence, McKelvey says.
At the individual user level, it would also help if we all stopped putting so much stock in what is popular on social media. "As a researcher who has put a lot of time into this, one of the findings is that what is popular on the internet is more complicated than a popularity contest," he said.
"Just because something's trending or a lot of people are tweeting a message - it means nothing."
With files from Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: tweet1tweet2Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal on June 19, 2024.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, August 8 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Canada's U.K. travel advisory, a Toronto police inspector's misconduct verdict and a family seeking accountability from Brantford General Hospital.
DON'T MISSCanada upgraded its U.K. travel advisory amid far-right riots
Ottawa is warning travellers to the U.K. to "exercise a high degree of caution" over the "threat of terrorism" as violent far-right riots escalate across the country, Andy Takagi reports. Cities and towns have been wracked by angry mobs over the past week, ignited by online misinformation over a mass stabbing. Here's what to know, whether you're travelling or just catching up on the situation.
Background: The riots followed a mass stabbing at a dance class in late July, during which three children were killed. A British-born 17-year-old, Axel Rudakubana, was charged with first-degree murder. Social media users falsely claimed the attacker was a Muslim and an immigrant.Last night: Police braced themselves for violence at hundreds of locations - but the streets filled with swells of peaceful, anti-racism counter-protestors instead.The Star's take: The U.K. riots serve as a cautionary tale about the noxious combination of policy failures, misinformation and reckless social media platforms. It would be naive to think the same lawlessness could never happen here.A Toronto cop is guilty of misconduct for interfering in her nephew's crash
A high-ranking Toronto police officer has been found guilty on two counts of professional misconduct after racing to the scene of her nephew's collision - outside her division's jurisdiction - and involving herself in the investigation. Insp. Joyce Schertzer's "inability to act objectively influenced the course of this investigation," the hearing's 63-page decision read. Wendy Gillis dives into the policing scandal - and what happens next.
What happened? Schertzer's nephew, identified in the decision as "Calvin," crashed his pickup truck into a light pole in May 2022. The senior cop rushed to the scene and saw that her nephew was sent home soon after - before Traffic Services could administer a breathalyzer or check his licence.Word from Schertzer: The inspector previously took the stand, saying she involved herself out of sincere concern for Calvin's wellbeing: "I was there as 'Aunt Joyce,'" she testified.Now what? Schertzer has not yet been sentenced; a hearing on her penalty will be held later this year. She could face consequences ranging from a reprimand to demotion to dismissal.He appeared fine in the ambulance - then came the wait
When 73-year-old Len King stepped into the ambulance, he could still walk and talk. But after waiting 33 hours in the packed ER of Brantford General Hospital, the senior became so incapacitated he never went home. His wife told Joanna Frketich she "basically watched him die for 52 days" in hospital before he passed away in January 2023. A year later, his family is still fighting for accountability.
Wait, what? Initially brought to the hospital for a suspected urinary tract infection, the senior was physically restrained and sedated in the ER - at times with an antipsychotic that should be avoided for patients with Parkinson's disease, which King had. He waited more than a day for intravenous fluids and antibiotics that had been ordered hours earlier.Go deeper: Brantford General's ER was singled out by the Ontario Nurses' Association over patient care and staffing concerns, eventually leading to an independent assessment in 2023. More than a year later, the hospital's ambulance off-load times have only gotten worse.WHAT ELSE
Thousands more Canadian dentists have signed onto Ottawa's dental plan. Here's why.Why is Doug Ford asking people not to poop on the beach? Here's what you need to know.Doug Ford says he's "happy as punch" Kamala Harris picked Tim Walz as her running mate.A loaded gun case got tossed after a judge found the suspect had been racially profiled.This Toronto fan flew to Vienna to see Taylor Swift - now a terror threat has seen three Austria shows cancelled.A Toronto kidnapping suspect captured by U.S. Marshals could face the death penalty.Andre De Grasse failed to reach another final in Paris - and this one hurts.Canada's Alysha Newman won bronze in Olympic women's pole vault.Canada made Olympic history in women's beach volleyball. In Kamala Harris picking Tim Walz, 'Americans have just been given a chance to save themselves'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kamala-harris-picking-tim-walz-americans-have/docview/3090618301/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+Kamala+Harris+picking+Tim+Walz%2C+%27Americans+have+just+been+given+a+chance+to+save+themselves%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just this Walz
Doug Ford says he's 'happy as punch' Kamala Harris picked Tim Walz, Aug. 7
In the U.S., Republicans are generally aligned with our Conservatives, while Democrats are aligned with the Liberals. Tim Walz and Kamala Harris are Democrats. So does Premier Doug Ford's reaction mean that he is leaning Liberal? He's a Ford, not a Lincoln.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
I doubt I'm the only Jewish person who is relieved that Kamala Harris chose Tim Walz over Josh Shapiro. Shapiro may be more effective than he is electable, especially during the rise in antisemitism and other forms of irrationality across North America. In Walz, Harris has found a real mensch, a man who is as easy to work with as Donald Trump is the opposite. Walz can out-folksy the populism types, and he's the real deal when it comes to wanting to make life better for working-class and poor families.
Ron Charach, Toronto
In a plot twist worthy of a political thriller, Americans have just been given a chance to save themselves by choosing to do what's right and no longer defaulting to the lesser evil. For the first time in a long time, American democracy appears to be working. Presidential candidate Kamala Harris has chosen Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, a previous social studies teacher, as her Democratic running mate. Consider the symbolism: a woman of colour and a teacher teaming up to challenge the re-election of Donald Trump, the reigning champion of insurrectionist chaos.
Walz brings to the table an educated notion that co-operation, not competition, is humanity's evolutionary secret. This represents the clash of real humanistic values versus the phoney, weird MAGA values of Trumpist populists. This isn't just a choice between red and blue. It's a choice between reality and reality TV.Let the "first Walz" for re-establishing credibility and decency in American democracy begin.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Getting around town
Toronto council sped up construction work on the Gardiner. Why won't it do the same for the TTC? Aug. 6
Columnist Matt Elliot really hit the numbers on the head. Six times as many people ride the subway than drive the Gardiner each day. This does not consider that not everyone on the Gardiner pays taxes into the city. At least transit riders pay fare. Why is the Gardiner getting so much attention and money? On a per-capita basis, it just doesn't make sense.
Speeding up Gardiner construction won't help congestion for very long. Once all lanes are open again, more people will take it until it reaches a new equilibrium of frustration. Also, there is a climate crisis going on. Most of the cars are burning gas and emitting carbon dioxide which is driving climate change. Given these points, we should be investing in more public transit, especially electrified public transit. One quick way to do this is through expanding electric bus service in dedicated lanes.
Roberta Tevlin, Toronto
I have been driving the roads of the GTA since 1965. Yes, I'm that old. As far as I can remember all our roads and highways have always been crazy busy, especially on weekends. We have better roads now with more lanes but there are also more cars, and we have the same old problems from drivers, including lack of courtesy, poor lane discipline, tailgating and distracted driving. In Europe there is a law for "zipper merging" that requires all drivers to stay in a lane that's closing and take turns merging in at the last moment. Simple. We drive a car now with adaptive cruise control that constantly monitors the car ahead of you and adjusts speed to keep the same space between vehicles.
John F. Walmark, Oakville
Too late to think about stormwater
Toronto to explore commercial stormwater charge as part of flood response, July 25
It is a little too late to introduce a stormwater charge. I live in Willowdale where many older homes with large lots (with ample front and back lawns) have been replaced with monster homes that take up the majority of the land. No wonder the water has no place to go but down the sewer. The city should no longer approve these homes and double the property tax on current monster-size homes, instead of charging homeowners with lots that have room to absorb water.
John Cernik, Toronto
Another tax fiasco
City emails show senior staff blunders that led to thousands of homeowners being overbilled, Aug. 5
Toronto's vacant home tax system is a fiasco in the extreme. The concept of badgering hundreds of thousands of Toronto homeowners in order to expose a nominal number of vacant homeowners is impossible to justify. It's akin to an ill-informed person buying and setting a thousand mousetraps in order to catch a single mouse. This system needs to be fundamentally changed or be scrapped.
Robert Woodcock, North York
Keeping construction bins out of bike lanes
City lays bylaw charges against companies that illegally left garbage bin in bike lane where a cyclist died, Aug. 6
I have a suggestion to keep bins out of bike lanes. All bins (or trucks parked overnight) must have a secure air tag attached, with the signal being available 24/7 to both parking authority and construction management. An instant scan by AI software would alert parking authorities and police of offenders so that immediate action to remove offenders could be taken. Such devices would assist managers to better manage assets on their properties and help parking authorities to better control offenders leading to better traffic management and better safety for the public.
Eric Dempster, Thornhill, Ont.
Let cops get coffee
Toronto police investigating viral video of officer allegedly giving middle finger to citizen, Aug. 6
I can't believe the Professional Standards Unit is launching an investigation into this situation. Our police officers report for duty with bulletproof vests to deal with a myriad of problems, emergencies and some very dangerous life-threating situations to protect the citizens of our city. Allow them to pull over to pick up a quick coffee, and even a doughnut for that matter, and park wherever it is most convenient for them at the time.
Marty Fruchtman, Toronto

'Folks, don't be pooping on the beach,' Ford urges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/folks-dont-be-pooping-on-beach-ford-urges/docview/3090200180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Folks%2C+don%27t+be+pooping+on+the+beach%2C%27+Ford+urges&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has a simple plea for the people of Ontario.
"Folks, don't be pooping on the beach. It's as simple as that."
Speaking to reporters Wednesday in Mississauga, Ford made it clear officials "don't have 100 per cent proof" that people are defecating on Wasaga Beach.
Such claims have sprung from a TikTok video that claimed people were using tents as makeshift port-a-potties at Wasaga Beach, the longest freshwater beach in the world, sparking fears of feces buried and hidden in the sand.
The controversy kicked off with a video posted in July by TikTokker Natasha White, who said she doesn't let her kids dig into the sand at Beach Area 1 in Wasaga for fear of them digging up human feces.
The video now has more than a half million views and White has more than 10,000 followers.
"A lot of locals do not use the beach in the summer due to the state of the beach, and I know a lot of people have witnessed people using the beach as an outhouse," White told Simcoe.com's Ian Adams. "The town doesn't want a bad picture painted, which I totally understand, but this is happening everywhere."
The town's mayor, Brian Smith, said in a statement last month that the complaints "lack evidence and promote misinformation."
"The town has received no evidence - from residents, visitors or the Ontario government - to verify that any undesirable, unsanitary behaviour has occurred on the beach areas that make up Wasaga Beach Provincial Park," Smith said.
With files from Robert Benzie and Ian Adams.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie and Ian Adams.

Title: 'Folks, don't be pooping on the beach,' Ford urges

Document 145 of 500

Ford says Walz understands trade priorities: Premier hosted Minnesota governor recently at Queen's Park to discuss cross-border relations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-says-walz-understands-trade-priorities/docview/3090200173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+says+Walz+understands+trade+priorities%3A+Premier+hosted+Minnesota+governor+recently+at+Queen%27s+Park+to+discuss+cross-border+relations&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says he's "happy as punch" U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris chose Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as her Democratic running mate.
"We share a lot in common," Ford told reporters Wednesday in a campaign-style stop in Mississauga where he announced $35 million to expand the G.E. Booth water resource recovery facility.
As the Star reported Tuesday, the premier hosted Walz at Queen's Park two months ago and the pair developed a warm rapport.
"I was happy as punch to see the governor nominated as ... (Harris's) running mate. We tossed the football around a little bit in my office - we gave him a CFL football," the premier said, adding their private meeting focused on the importance of Canada-U.S. trade.
"He understands that. We're stronger together," said Ford, acknowledging the worrying protectionist rhetoric in the run-up to the Nov. 5 U.S. presidential election.
The premier, who has been on a "Buy North American" push with other provincial leaders and U.S. governors, emphasized the importance of Ontario as a trading partner and the need to keep goods and services flowing.
"I explained to him that we're the third largest (U.S.) trading partner in the world - Ontario is. We do approximately $500 billion in (annual) two-way trade," he said.
"We're the number one trading partner to 17 states, number two to 13 other states - and we're the number one customer to those states. We're an economic powerhouse ... in North America," he said.
"We're going to work well together if he gets elected."
Ford has expressed concern about the protectionist talk on the hustings stateside - especially from Republican nominee Donald Trump, the ex-president, and his running mate J.D. Vance.
Because of the looming mandatory review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) in 2026, Canadian politicians are closely watching the American election.
Still, Ford hastened to emphasize he "will work with anyone, I don't care if it's Republican or Democrat."
Ontario's governing Progressive Conservatives were encouraged by Harris's choice Tuesday of the two-term Minnesota governor, who is well-regarded at Queen's Park.
Walz beat out better-known potential running mates, including Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro, Arizona Sen. Mark Kelly and President Joe Biden's transportation secretary, Pete Buttigieg.
Like his Tory, Liberal and NDP predecessors as premier, Ford has made building relationships with Democratic and Republican U.S. governors a priority since winning power in 2018.
But the June 10 meeting with Walz in his office at the legislature went particularly well.
"There was such an excellent rapport and really, really positive statements from the governor on bilateral (Ontario-Minnesota) and Canada-U.S. trade," confided one senior provincial official, who attended the closed-door session.
"They're both very salt-of-the-Earth and 'of the people' so it was a very positive meeting."
As an avid NFL fan, Ford gave Walz, a former high school football coach who once guided his team to a Minnesota state championship, an official Wilson CFL ball.
The two men, born seven-and-a-half months apart in 1964, threw the football together like schoolchildren in the premier's massive second-floor office while aides watched.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Full text: Ford showing
his 'Liberal' side?
Doug Ford says he's 'happy as punch' Kamala Harris picked Tim Walz, Aug. 7

In the U.S., Republicans are generally aligned with our Conservatives, while Democrats are aligned with the Liberals. Tim Walz and Kamala Harris are Democrats. So does Premier Doug Ford's reaction mean that he is leaning Liberal? He's a Ford, not a Lincoln.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
Tim Walz is
the real deal
I doubt I'm the only Jewish person who is relieved that Kamala Harris chose Tim Walz over Josh Shapiro. Shapiro may be more effective than he is electable, especially during the rise in antisemitism and other forms of irrationality across North America. In Walz, Harris has found a real mensch, a man who is as easy to work with as Donald Trump is the opposite. Walz can out-folksy the populism types, and he's the real deal when it comes to wanting to make life better for working-class and poor families.
Ron Charach, Toronto
In a plot twist worthy of a political thriller, Americans have just been given a chance to save themselves by choosing to do what's right and no longer defaulting to the lesser evil. For the first time in a long time, American democracy appears to be working. Presidential candidate Kamala Harris has chosen Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, a previous social studies teacher, as her Democratic running mate. Consider the symbolism: a woman of colour and a teacher teaming up to challenge the re-election of Donald Trump, the reigning champion of insurrectionist chaos.
Walz brings to the table an educated notion that co-operation, not competition, is humanity's evolutionary secret. This represents the clash of real humanistic values versus the phoney, weird MAGA values of Trumpist populists. This isn't just a choice between red and blue. It's a choice between reality and reality TV. Let the "first Walz" for re-establishing credibility and decency in American democracy begin.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Invest more in public transit
Toronto council sped up construction work on the Gardiner. Why won't it do the same for the TTC? Aug. 6

Columnist Matt Elliot really hit the numbers on the head. Six times as many people ride the subway than drive the Gardiner each day. This does not consider that not everyone on the Gardiner pays taxes into the city. At least transit riders pay fare. Why is the Gardiner getting so much attention and money? On a per-capita basis, it just doesn't make sense.
Speeding up Gardiner construction won't help congestion for very long. Once all lanes are open again, more people will take it until it reaches a new equilibrium of frustration. Also, there is a climate crisis going on. Most of the cars are burning gas and emitting carbon dioxide, which is driving climate change. Given these points, we should be investing in more public transit, especially electrified public transit. One quick way to do this is through expanding electric bus service in dedicated lanes.
Roberta Tevlin, Toronto
I have been driving the roads of the GTA since 1965. Yes, I'm that old. As far as I can remember all our roads and highways have always been crazy busy, especially on weekends. We have better roads now with more lanes, but there are also more cars, and we have the same old problems from drivers, including lack of courtesy, poor lane discipline, tailgating and distracted driving.
In Europe there is a law for "zipper merging" that requires all drivers to stay in a lane that's closing and take turns merging in at the last moment. Simple. We drive a car now with adaptive cruise control that constantly monitors the car ahead of you and adjusts speed to keep the same space between vehicles.
John F. Walmark, Oakville
Too late to think about stormwater
Toronto to explore commercial stormwater charge as part of flood response, July 25
It is a little too late to introduce a stormwater charge. I live in Willowdale where many older homes with large lots (with ample front and back lawns) have been replaced with monster homes that take up the majority of the land. No wonder the water has no place to go but down the sewer. The city should no longer approve these homes and double the property tax on current monster-size homes, instead of charging homeowners with lots that have room to absorb water.
John Cernik, Toronto
Tax system needs to be changed
or scrapped
City emails show senior staff blunders that led to thousands of homeowners being overbilled, Aug. 5
Toronto's vacant home tax system is a fiasco in the extreme. The concept of badgering hundreds of thousands of Toronto homeowners in order to expose a nominal number of vacant homeowners is impossible to justify. It's akin to an ill-informed person buying and setting a thousand mousetraps in order to catch a single mouse. This system needs to be fundamentally changed or be scrapped.
Robert Woodcock, North York
How to keep construction bins out of bike lanes
City lays bylaw charges against companies that illegally left garbage bin in bike lane where a cyclist died, Aug. 6

I have a suggestion to keep bins out of bike lanes. All bins (or trucks parked overnight) must have a secure air tag attached, with the signal being available 24/7 to both parking authority and construction management. An instant scan by AI software would alert parking authorities and police of offenders so that immediate action to remove offenders could be taken. Such devices would assist managers to better manage assets on their properties and help parking authorities to better control offenders leading to better traffic management and better safety for the public.
Eric Dempster, Thornhill
Let officers
get their coffee
Toronto police investigating viral video of officer allegedly giving middle finger to citizen, Aug. 6
I can't believe the Professional Standards Unit is launching an investigation into this situation.
Our police officers report for duty with bulletproof vests to deal with a myriad of problems, emergencies and some very dangerous life-threating situations to protect the citizens of our city.
Allow them to pull over to pick up a quick coffee, and even a doughnut for that matter, and park wherever it is most convenient for them at the time.
Marty Fruchtman, Toronto

Document 147 of 500

Why is Premier Doug Ford asking people not to poop on the beach? Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-is-premier-doug-ford-asking-people-not-poop/docview/3090692826/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+Premier+Doug+Ford+asking+people+not+to+poop+on+the+beach%3F+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has a simple plea for the people of Ontario.
"Folks, don't be pooping on the beach. It's as simple as that."
Speaking to reporters Wednesday in Mississauga, Ford made it clear officials "don't have 100 per cent proof" that people are defecating on Wasaga Beach.
Such claims have sprung from a TikTok video that claimed people were using tents as makeshift port-a-potties at Wasaga Beach, the longest freshwater beach in the world, sparking fears of feces buried and hidden in the sand.
The controversy kicked off with a video posted in July by TikTokker Natasha White, who said she doesn't let her kids dig into the sand at Beach Area 1 in Wasaga for fear of them digging up human feces.
The video now has more than a half million views and White has more than 10,000 followers.
"A lot of locals do not use the beach in the summer due to the state of the beach, and I know a lot of people have witnessed people using the beach as an outhouse," White told Simcoe.com's Ian Adams.
"The town doesn't want a bad picture painted, which I totally understand, but this is happening everywhere."
The town's mayor, Brian Smith, said in a statement last month that the complaints "lack evidence and promote misinformation."
"The town has received no evidence - from residents, visitors or the Ontario government - to verify that any undesirable, unsanitary behaviour has occurred on the beach areas that make up Wasaga Beach Provincial Park," Smith said.
He also said Wasaga Beach is "home to ... one of the cleanest, safest and most beautiful beaches in the world."
There are a number of public washrooms "in areas of high use and (they) are very visible to the public from the beachfront" maintained by the town and Ontario Parks, Smith said in his statement.
Ford also stressed that the idea people are defecating on the beach is based on rumours and remains unconfirmed.
"Again, they have no proof that people are pooping on the park, per se," the premier said.
"But, by the way, we've given Wasaga Beach $1 million to build bathrooms and ... hire more people," said Ford. A spokesperson for the Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks clarified that, since 2018, the Ford government has invested "in facility enhancements at Wasaga Beach Provincial Park including the construction of new washroom facilities at Allenwood and New Wasaga Beaches and repairs and upgrades to tractors and beach grooming equipment."
The premier added he'd be "more than happy to talk to the mayor; it's a very popular beach."
With files from Robert Benzie and Ian Adams.
CAPTION: Wasaga Beach's mayor has been adamant that social media reports of beachgoers defecating in the sand are false.

Subject: Parks & recreation areas; Beaches

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-hes-happy-as-punch-kamala-harris/docview/3089869074/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 149 of 500

Removing right to appeal to the OLT further erodes the rights of citizens

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/removing-right-appeal-olt-further-erodes-rights/docview/3089868831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Removing+right+to+appeal+to+the+OLT+further+erodes+the+rights+of+citizens&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Environmental and residents groups call new rules barring OLT third party appeals an attack on citizens rights, Aug 6
I would like to share how the people of West Grey have been impacted by Doug Ford government's actions that further erode the rights of citizens.
Saugeen Preservation Society represents residents in West Grey who are engaged in a fight against THREE separate gravel mines adjacent to each other in an environmentally sensitive, flood prone area straddling the Saugeen River and impacting multiple homes and businesses.
Removing our right to appeal under the Planning Act impedes our ability to fully represent the public interest. It puts us at the mercy of our local council to do the right thing and deny the applicants rezoning applications, so residents can participate at all stages of the Ontario Land Tribunal process.
Removing our right to appeal to the OLT is not governing for the people. It only serves to tip the scales even further in favour of the mismanaged aggregate industry which is already prioritized over the environment and the public's best interest.
Judi Smelko, Saugeen Preservation Society, West Grey
Tim Walz is a climate championHarris picks Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as her running mate in a bid to unite Democrats against Trump, Aug. 6
Thanks for your excellent article on Kamala Harris' pick of Tim Walz for vice-presidential running mate.
I would like to add one very important commitment Walz brings to the VP candidate role - his commitment to climate action.
Walz's administration has prioritized tackling the climate crisis and advancing clean energy. Walz signed legislation to accelerate renewable energy projects and move Minnesota utilities to 100 per cent clean energy generation by 2040. And Walz shows how this can be done while also improving the economy and quality of life for all. Minnesota has seen $1.15 billion in investment thanks to the Inflation Reduction Act and other Biden-Harris climate policies, which have created 2,000 new jobs.
A climate champion in the White House will help bring a livable future for all.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
Toronto's new filmmakers need funding to be able to take risksKeeping Toronto weird - how a group of scrappy indie filmmakers are disrupting the city's movie scene, Aug. 3
I was heartened to read Quinn Henderson's article on Toronto's new filmmaking weirdos.
As a member of the '80s era "Toronto New Wave" (Atom Egoyan, Patricia Rozema, Bruce McDonald, Jeremy Podeswa, Peter Mettler, Ron Mann, John Greyson, Don McKellar, Adrienne Mitchell, Janis Lundman, Camelia Frieberg, and Alexandra Raffe among others) we were weirdos too: fearless, focused and fiercely independent. With help from funding agencies, broadcasters, and film festivals, we were able to take risks, and all of us went on to enjoy robust careers.
I applaud this new generation of filmmakers and look forward to watching their careers develop. It's a shame that the current funding agencies, broadcasters and TIFF don't share their same inspiring attitude.
Colin Brunton, producer: Roadkill, Highway 61, The Rainbow Kid, Cube, Schitt's Creek, Toronto
Why does Canada need 13 health-care systems?Canada has a hospital wait time crisis. Other countries with universal health-care don't. We should follow their lead, Aug 5
The first sentence of this article sums it up: Canada's health-care systems are deteriorating. Indeed, they are! As Emmanuelle Faubert points out, in many studies comparing health-care systems in Western countries, Canada ranks near the bottom, only ahead of the USA.
The first question we need to ask: why do we talk about Canada's health-care systems? Why not Canada's health care system? Both France and the UK have a population of more than 64 million and Germany, nearly 84 million and they function very well with a single health-care system. Why does Canada need 13 health care systems to work (or really not to work)? We waste a huge amount of money on politicians and administrators performing various functions that are duplicated 13 times.
We have the same problem with education, trade, commerce, etc. etc. The size of our country might have been justification for these inefficiencies in 1867 but in 2024 with the means of communication at our disposal, there is no need for our country to be as divided as it is. We must stop invoking our Constitution as an excuse and do something about it even if it means the unpleasant and mammoth task of changing our Constitution. Using our Constitution as an excuse is no better than the Americans using their Constitution as justification to carry guns.
Ms. Faubert also points out the role that the private sector has in hospitals in Europe. She's absolutely correct: the private sector does have a role to play in the delivery of health care. Europeans do make it work to their benefit. Why can't we? At the mere mention of the word 'private' in the same sentence as health care, many Canadians faint. We need to look at Europe to see how it's done and not limit our comparisons to the US.
Claude Gannon, Markham
High Park should be accessible to cars for fairness and equityCases for and against the High Park car ban, Aug 6, 2024
The view expressed is a short-sighted perspective given the evolution of society since High Park was founded in 1873. Yes, cars did not exist when High Park was founded; there was horse and buggy. People did do a lot more walking out of necessity but probably did not go very far since community needs were met locally; they grew their produce, slaughtered their animals. The average life expectancy in Canada in 1900 was 50 years. People did not survive the illnesses which are treatable today and died young due to prevailing morbidities.
Over the intervening 150 years the automobile developed to meet needs other than for visits to High Park Our forefathers saw fit to bring paved roads to the park to facilitate access and the various facilities developed to enhance the functions of the park and the experiences of visitors. Medical advances contributed to our longevity and in 2020 the average live expectancy in Canada is 80 years for men and 84 years for women. Many continue to live beyond those years, into their 90s, often with chronic illnesses and disabilities which were not treatable in 1900.
Walking the distances into, through and out of the park is not feasible for many of the elderly citizens who continue to live active fulfilling lives in society, amongst their families and friends, and deserve to enjoy the pleasures, facilities and opportunities provided by High Park. That is why the park should be accessible to cars.
The young who decry the presence of cars in High Park today will some day get old and appreciate access in their EVs to spend time with their grandchildren at the zoo and Grenadier Café, which we hope will still be standing.
Günes N. Ege, Etobicoke
CAPTION: Suzi Recine, left, and Uzzo Calderaro stand near a planned development in Kleinburg. Their residents group was appealing a development adjacent and abutting the Greenbelt, but that right was taken away from them with the passing of Bill 185.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, August 7 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Kamala Harris' Ontario-friendly running mate, a controversial coach's Olympic eviction and Ottawa's temporary foreign worker crackdown.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford's football friend is Kamala Harris's new running mate
Two months before Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz was chosen as Harris's running mate, the 60-year-old progressive was talking trade and tossing a football around with Ford in his office. It could be good news for Ontario - and the premier's "Buy North America" push against protectionist rhetoric stateside, Robert Benzie reports. Here's what you need to know about the potential future vice president and his Ontario ties.
On the home front: Government insiders say Walz and Ford's impromptu game of catch could have broader trade implications, as the next U.S. administration will preside over the scheduled review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement in 2026.Who is this guy? A veteran, retired teacher and union supporter, Walz has a strong track record of enacting progressive policies. Take a closer look. Vinay Menon's take: Walz is no coastal elite; armed with Midwestern charm and a pragmatic approach, the former football coach appears beloved by Liberals and Conservatives alike.Rana Reider was sent home from Paris
Andre de Grasse's coach was booted from the Paris Olympics after news broke he was being sued by three female athletes for sexual and emotional abuse. It wasn't a complete shock, Bruce Arthur writes - the 54-year-old had a history of allegations and where there's smoke there's fire. But given his reputation, why was Reider at the Olympics at all - and how much did Canada's champion sprinter know of his past?
Rosie DiManno's take: Reider's Olympic plans came crashing down after a bombshell report in The Times detailed the lawsuits against him, alleging sexual and emotional abuse - including "grooming" one of the athletes while she was still a minor.What we know: This wasn't Reider's first controversy. He was put on a 12-month probation by the Center for SafeSport after allegations of sexual misconduct. Take a look at his record.Ottawa is cracking down on abuse of the temporary foreign worker program
Canada is zeroing in on fraud in its ballooning temporary foreign worker program amid surging allegations the program is being abused by some employers. "Bad actors are taking advantage of people and compromising the program for legitimate businesses," Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault said Tuesday, adding that he may refuse to process applications for certain areas and industries. Ghada Alsharif dives into what's being done - and whether it's enough.
Word from Marc Miller: The immigration minister previously accused businesses of being "addicted" to cheap foreign labour instead of hiring Canadian residents - a practice he says drives down wages for everyone.By the numbers: Employers were approved to hire 239,646 temporary foreign workers in 2023 - more than double 2018's 108,988 approved positions.Word from labour advocates: Boissonnault's promises fall short of adequately protecting workers from exploitation, critics charge, as the threat of deportation still hangs over any worker who complains about abusive conditions.WHAT ELSE
This week's global investor panic begs the question: was it an overreaction or is a recession looming?Donald Trump is repeating the baseless claim that Justin Trudeau "could be" the son of Fidel Castro.A woman has been charged with first-degree murder after human remains and chemicals were found at a Toronto home.A man was arrested after a driver struck multiple protesters on the Gardiner Expressway, Toronto police say.Hamas named Yahya Sinwar as its new leader following Israel's assassination of its former leader in Iran.A Nobel laureate will head Bangladesh's interim government after the ouster of its prime minister.Canada's Olympic basketball team disappoints after being "punched in the mouth" and knocked out by France.Canadian hammer thrower Camryn Rogers won gold - and a hug that wraps around her soul.This airline passenger rights group is suing WestJet over its reimbursement policies.Elections Canada fined a man who voted twice in 2019 to "test the electoral system."After 75 years, one of Toronto's most important outlets for jazz might soon be silenced. Is help on the way?Just in time for the Olympics, a new Canadian fencing movie offers intellectual thrills.POV
America's wackiest presidential contender never met a political problem he couldn't make worse.CLOSE-UP
PARIS: Canada's Caeli McKay barely missed the podium yesterday, finishing fourth in women's 10-metre platform diving - a crushing result after a close-fought battle for Olympic bronze. She's still holding her head high, she tells Allan Woods - even if her soul was a little squashed.
