Document 151 of 500

Harris's VP choice could help boost ties: Ford bonded with Minnesota governor during visit to discuss cross-border trade

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/harriss-vp-choice-could-help-boost-ties/docview/3089767767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Harris%27s+VP+choice+could+help+boost+ties%3A+Ford+bonded+with+Minnesota+governor+during+visit+to+discuss+cross-border+trade&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two months ago, Premier Doug Ford was talking trade and tossing a football around his Queen's Park office with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz.
With Walz now the running mate of U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris atop the Democratic ticket, Ford is hopeful that could be good news for Ontario - and for his "Buy North America" push against protectionist rhetoric stateside.
On Tuesday, Harris opted for the two-term Minnesota governor - who for 12 years was a congressman representing a traditionally Republican district bordering the key swing state of Wisconsin - to be her vice-presidential nominee.
Walz edged out high-profile hopefuls such as Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro, Arizona Sen. Mark Kelly and Pete Buttigieg, who is President Joe Biden's transportation secretary.
The premier, who has cultivated relationships with key Democratic and Republican U.S. governors and trade officials over his six years in office, was pleased with Harris's pick - even though he is remaining agnostic on the election.
"There was such an excellent rapport and really, really positive statements from the governor on bilateral (Ontario-Minnesota) and Canada-U.S. trade," said one senior provincial official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the private meeting at Queen's Park on June 10.
"They're both very salt-of-the-earth and 'of the people' so it was a very positive meeting," said the high-ranking insider, who was among about a dozen at the confab dominated by shared concerns over trade.
Ford, a huge NFL fan, presented Walz - a former high school football coach who led his team to the Minnesota state championship in 1999 - with an official Wilson CFL ball.
Behind the closed doors of the premier's capacious second-floor office in the Ontario legislature, the two leaders began throwing the pigskin back and forth to the amusement of their aides.
Insiders said the impromptu game of catch - away from media cameras - symbolized a connection between this province and Walz's state that could have broader trade implications.
That's because the next American administration will preside over the scheduled mandatory review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) in 2026.
Mindful of the protectionist hyperbole on the campaign trail - especially from former president Donald Trump, the Republican nominee, and his running mate JD Vance - Ford has been pushing a "Buy North America" strategy with premiers and U.S. governors.
Provincial sources said Walz, whose state is home to major multinational corporations like 3M and General Mills that have significant operations in Ontario, was simpatico to the premier's pitch.
"As you know, two-way trade between Minnesota and Ontario reached an impressive $5.6 billion (U.S.) in 2023, supporting thousands of jobs on both sides of the border," Ford wrote in a letter to the governor on June 19.
"At a time of increasing global instability, it's never been more important for us to work together to build on this important and growing relationship," the premier wrote.
"As we confront the challenge of global instability, I agree with you that we cannot take our relationship for granted and, as neighbours, we need to stick together," he continued.
"Moving forward, it will be important to consider ways in which we can boost trade between our two jurisdictions as we embrace a 'Buy North America' mindset," added Ford.
"This approach will help provide certainty, stability and prosperity to the hundreds of millions of Americans and Canadian workers that rely on us to maintain an open trading relationship, regardless of political circumstances or external events."
The Progressive Conservative premier noted Ontario can help Minnesota with "critical minerals and clean, affordable energy" to power its businesses.
"I look forward to exploring how we can better secure our North American supply chains across all sectors to become less reliant on countries further afield that cannot be relied on and that do not share our values," he concluded.
Should Harris and Walz win the Nov. 5 U.S. election, the Minnesota governor would follow in the footsteps of Trump's former vice-president, Mike Pence.
In October 2014, Pence, then the governor of Indiana, was promoting his state's relations with Ontario at Queen's Park with then Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne and "boosting bonds with existing partners in Toronto."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Full text: Rights lost in West Grey
Environmental and residents groups call new rules barring OLT third party appeals an attack on citizens rights, Aug. 6
I would like to share how the people of West Grey have been impacted by Doug Ford government's actions that further erode the rights of citizens.
Saugeen Preservation Society represents residents in West Grey who are engaged in a fight against three separate gravel mines adjacent to each other in an environmentally sensitive, flood prone area straddling the Saugeen River and impacting multiple homes and businesses.
Removing our right to appeal under the Planning Act impedes our ability to fully represent the public interest. It puts us at the mercy of our local council to do the right thing and deny the applicants rezoning applications, so residents can participate at all stages of the Ontario Land Tribunal process.
Removing our right to appeal to the OLT is not governing for the people. It only serves to tip the scales even further in favour of the mismanaged aggregate industry which is already prioritized over the environment and the public's best interest.
Judi Smelko, Saugeen Preservation Society, West Grey
Walz is a climate champion
Harris picks Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as her running mate in a bid to unite Democrats against Trump, Aug. 6
Thanks for your excellent article on Kamala Harris's pick of Tim Walz for vice-presidential running mate.
I would like to add one very important commitment Walz brings to the VP candidate role - his commitment to climate action.
Walz's administration has prioritized tackling the climate crisis and advancing clean energy. Walz signed legislation to accelerate renewable energy projects and move Minnesota utilities to 100 per cent clean energy generation by 2040. And Walz shows how this can be done while also improving the economy and quality of life for all.
Minnesota has seen $1.15 billion (U.S.) in investment thanks to the Inflation Reduction Act and other Biden-Harris climate policies, which have created 2,000 new jobs.
A climate champion in the White House will help bring a livable future for all.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
New filmmakers need funding
Keeping Toronto weird - how a group of scrappy indie filmmakers are disrupting the city's movie scene, Aug. 3
I was heartened to read Quinn Henderson's article on Toronto's new filmmaking weirdos.
As a member of the '80s era "Toronto New Wave" (Atom Egoyan, Patricia Rozema, Bruce McDonald, Jeremy Podeswa, Peter Mettler, Ron Mann, John Greyson, Don McKellar, Adrienne Mitchell, Janis Lundman, Camelia Frieberg, and Alexandra Raffe among others) we were weirdos too: fearless, focused and fiercely independent. With help from funding agencies, broadcasters and film festivals, we were able to take risks, and all of us went on to enjoy robust careers.
I applaud this new generation of filmmakers and look forward to watching their careers develop. It's a shame that the current funding agencies, broadcasters and TIFF don't share their same inspiring attitude.
Colin Brunton, producer: Roadkill, Highway 61, The Rainbow Kid, Cube, Schitt's Creek, Toronto
Do we need
13 health-care systems?
Canada has a hospital wait time crisis. Other countries with universal health-care don't. We should follow their lead, Aug. 5
The first sentence of this article sums it up: Canada's health-care systems are deteriorating. Indeed, they are. As Emmanuelle Faubert points out, in many studies comparing health-care systems in western countries, Canada ranks near the bottom, only ahead of the U.S.
The first question we need to ask: why do we talk about Canada's health-care systems? Why not Canada's health-care system? Both France and the U.K. have a population of more than 64 million and Germany, nearly 84 million and they function very well with a single health-care system. Why does Canada need 13 health-care systems to work (or really not to work)? We waste a huge amount of money on politicians and administrators performing various functions that are duplicated 13 times.
We have the same problem with education, trade, commerce, etc. etc. The size of our country might have been justification for these inefficiencies in 1867 but in 2024 with the means of communication at our disposal, there is no need for our country to be as divided as it is. We must stop invoking our Constitution as an excuse and do something about it even if it means the unpleasant and mammoth task of changing our Constitution.
Faubert also points out the role that the private sector has in hospitals in Europe. She's absolutely correct: the private sector does have a role to play in the delivery of health care. Europeans do make it work to their benefit. Why can't we? We need to look at Europe to see how it's done and not limit our comparisons to the U.S.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Cars in park a matter of fairness
Cases for and against the High Park car ban, Aug. 6, 2024
The view expressed is a short-sighted perspective given the evolution of society since High Park was founded in 1873. Yes, cars did not exist when High Park was founded; there was horse and buggy. People did do a lot more walking out of necessity but probably did not go very far since community needs were met locally; they grew their produce, slaughtered their animals. The average life expectancy in Canada in 1900 was 50 years. People did not survive the illnesses that are treatable today and died young due to prevailing morbidities.
Over the intervening 150 years, the automobile developed to meet needs other than for visits to High Park. Our forefathers saw fit to bring paved roads to the park to facilitate access and the various facilities developed to enhance the functions of the park and the experiences of visitors. Medical advances contributed to our longevity and in 2020 the average live expectancy in Canada was 80 years for men and 84 years for women. Many continue to live beyond those years, into their 90s, often with chronic illnesses and disabilities that were not treatable in 1900.
Walking the distances into, through and out of the park is not feasible for many of the elderly citizens who deserve to enjoy the pleasures, facilities and opportunities provided by High Park. That is why the park should be accessible to cars.
The young who decry the presence of cars in High Park today will some day get old and appreciate access in their EVs to spend time with their grandchildren at the zoo and Grenadier Café, which we hope will still be standing.
Günes N. Ege, Etobicoke

Document 153 of 500

Doug Ford's football friend Tim Walz is Kamala Harris's running mate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-football-friend-tim-walz-is-kamala/docview/3089921686/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+football+friend+Tim+Walz+is+Kamala+Harris%27s+running+mate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"As we confront the challenge of global instability, I agree with you that we cannot take our relationship for granted and, as neighbours, we need to stick together," he continued.
"Moving forward, it will be important to consider ways in which we can boost trade between our two jurisdictions as we embrace a 'Buy North America' mindset," added Ford.
"This approach will help provide certainty, stability and prosperity to the hundreds of millions of Americans and Canadian workers that rely on us to maintain an open trading relationship, regardless of political circumstances or external events."
The Progressive Conservative premier noted Ontario can help Minnesota with "critical minerals and clean, affordable energy" to power its businesses.
"I look forward to exploring how we can better secure our North American supply chains across all sectors to become less reliant on countries further afield that cannot be relied on and that do not share our values," he concluded.
Should Harris and Walz win the Nov. 5 U.S. election, the Minnesota governor would follow in the footsteps of Trump's former vice-president, Mike Pence.
In October 2014, Pence, then the governor of Indiana, was promoting his state's relations with Ontario at Queen's Park with then Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne and "boosting bonds with existing partners in Toronto."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz share a moment in the premier's office at Queen's Park in June 2024.

Celebrating Summer McIntosh, Toronto's swimming phenom at the Olympics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/celebrating-summer-mcintosh-torontos-swimming/docview/3089467389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Celebrating+Summer+McIntosh%2C+Toronto%27s+swimming+phenom+at+the+Olympics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Celebrating Summer
How Canadian Olympic star Summer McIntosh is following in her mom Jill McIntosh's wake, Aug. 2
Summer McIntosh is built differently. She is a phenom in the water and becoming a superstar and legend in Canada. I've said it before and I'll say it again, she should be flag bearer at the closing ceremony.
Paul Bacon, Hallandale Beach, Fla.
Just as medals and lofty goals are a family tradition for Summer McIntosh's family, so is modesty. Case in point: I worked with McIntosh's maternal grandmother, Gertie Horstead, for many years and it was only during the 1984 Summer Olympics that she told us her daughter Jill was a participant! I quickly arranged for a telegram to be sent to Jill in Los Angeles wishing her good luck from all of her mother's coworkers. We later arranged for a TV to be set up in the lunchroom so we could watch Jill's butterfly event with bated breath! Now we share in Horstead's happiness at her daughter's 1984 accomplishments and the accomplishments of her granddaughter in 2024.
Hazel Regan, Toronto
Hope for avoiding dementia
Nearly half of dementia cases can be prevented or delayed, a major new study suggests. Here's how, Aug. 1
The results of the new study, suggesting nearly half of dementia cases can be prevented or delayed, is highly exciting. This fine opportunity to prevent or delay the onset of the dreaded dementia could well be the most uplifting news for our aging Canadian population.
The study identified uncorrected eyesight and "bad" cholesterol as two new, major risk factors for dementia. Poor education in early life, social isolation and depression are also considered "the most significant factors." It is heartening to learn that seeing other people, and interacting with them, is the easiest way to keep one's brain active! Geriatric medicine professor Dr. Roger Wong recommends socialization or daily exercise, which helps our brain's ability to march on despite facing cognitive decline. By recognizing the 13 early signs of dementia, we can now plan to defeat or delay dreaded dementia!
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
One big thing missing from this study in risk factors is having a genetic link to others who have had dementia. Two people I personally know who have dementia had mothers who had it, one with very early onset. Anecdotally, it can run in the family, and the research bears this out.
Sandra Hanmer, Hamilton
Ford does not compare
Does anybody remember what the past Conservative governments were like in Ontario, versus what Doug Ford's party is like today? Previous Conservative governments were very frugal with spending taxpayer money, but today, Ford's party is very good at wasting our money. This is not the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario that your parents/grandparents voted for!
Just recently he announced plans to pay off the Beer Store chain up to $225 million to allow other stores to sell beer, wine and alcohol. If he had waited another year or so he would have avoided the costly boondoggle. Adding to that, if this goes through, the private sector will likely charge more money for beer and alcohol than LCBO. Good luck to us with that! The other problem is that it will be much easier for underage people to access alcoholic beverages.
We will remember what you have done, Premier Ford!
Jan Larsen, Bracebridge, Ont.
A vote for a car free High Park
Torontonians maintain a very car-centric attitude, which apparently hasn't changed much since I left the city 50 years ago. Many don't seem to understand that by driving everywhere for everything, including High Park, they worsen the problem. They would alleviate it by walking, cycling or taking transit. Cars are heavy and take up a lot of space, so they cause congestion. Walkers, cyclists or transit riders don't. There really is no excuse why many more can't walk, cycle or take transit more often than they do. Better transit and more, not fewer, cycle tracks would help. By the way, cars did not exist when High Park was founded. Somehow people managed.
Ed Janicki, Victoria, BC
We must accept taxes
Cases for and against the High Park car ban, Aug. 3
A Star letter writer wrote of the "strange yet calm" feeling that he got when walking on the streets in Oslo, Norway where the city has removed 700 parking spaces and replaced them with bike lanes, benches and planters. "Good public transit" picks up the slack. Earlier letters extolled the health care system in Denmark where all residents are guaranteed a local family doctor, and noted the excellent daycare system in Sweden.
In all the praise given to Scandinavia for superior social systems, not a mention is made that taxation makes these systems strong. All these countries have higher income tax levels than Canada. That doesn't count state taxes, sales taxes and other miscellaneous taxes. Canadians cannot hope to have the amenities the Scandinavians have until we get over our visceral hatred of taxes. I'd certainly be willing to pay more tax to get the services we deserve.
Joan Blackwood, Toronto
Trump has always been unglued
Trump's astonishing, embarrassing appearance revealed a candidate who appears to be coming unglued, Aug. 1
It is difficult to know whether Donald Trump is more unglued now than he has been at any point in the recent past. If he was truthful in saying that he did not realize that Kamala Harris was Black the first time he became aware of her then he may have lacked glue for a considerable period. Most of us can accept that individuals can have dual ethnicities and can legitimately claim links to more than one race or nationality. If Trump has become unglued recently, it is not in his perception of other humans but in his willingness to say things which are intrinsically offensive to the audience he is speaking to.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
Cherishing freedom
U.S. and Russia tout prisoner swap as a victory. But perceptions of the deal show stark differences, Aug. 3
Freedom is something to be cherished, just ask Wall Street Journal reporter Evan Gershkovich and former Marine Paul Whelan who had been held in Russian captivity. Both men probably thought that they would never see their families again, but are back on U.S. soil. Americans are seen as bargaining tools to countries hostile to the U.S. and do not belong there under any circumstances. Let us be grateful for the return of these honourable men. On this exceptional day of thanks, the sun appears to be absolutely radiant.
JoAnn Lee Frank, Clearwater, Fla.
CAPTION: Canada's Summer McIntosh celebrates with her gold medal won in the 200m women's individual medley final on Saturday.

Document 155 of 500

Environmental and residents groups call new provincial rules barring OLT third-party appeals an attack on 'citizen rights'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/environmental-residents-groups-call-new/docview/3089467198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Environmental+and+residents+groups+call+new+provincial+rules+barring+OLT+third-party+appeals+an+attack+on+%27citizen+rights%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A provincial law change that curtailed third-party groups' ability to appeal development decisions has left environmental and ratepayer groups saying they've been silenced in a move that puts developer interests ahead of citizen concerns.
Ontario residents can no longer appeal development decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal - a quasi-judicial body designed to adjudicate planning and other land disputes - after the Doug Ford government introduced legislation that removes the ability of third-parties such as ratepayer groups or environmental groups to do so.
The province says the changes to the third-party appeal rights in the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act (Bill 185), which passed in June, will "help communities get quicker planning approvals for housing projects."
But critics say the legislation is another attack on "citizen rights" and the public's participation in the development process and will ultimately lead to poorly planned and less safe communities across the province.
"This is the loss of an important mechanism for the public to make sure that environmental and public health concerns were raised and properly considered in development decisions across the province," said Jacqueline Wilson, counsel for the Canadian Environmental Law Association, who says the organization has been part of local resident appeals for decades.
"It's really a problem to cut out active engaged citizens, who deeply care about their communities, from the equation."
According to the OLT, between April 1, 2022, to March 31, 2023, the Tribunal handled 1,422 cases, totalling 2,161 appeals. The OLT said they don't track the number of appeals made by developers versus individuals or third-party groups. However, since the law came into effect in June, 62 third-party appeals that didn't "meet the transition provisions," which include a hearing date scheduled before April 10, 2024, were dismissed, a spokesperson for the OLT said.
The new legislation places restrictions on the ability of the public to appeal amendments to official plans and zoning bylaws made through development applications. The only groups who can appeal are "public bodies," specified persons who are defined as government, utility boards, and agencies. Landowners and developers can also appeal municipal development decisions they don't agree with.
Justine Teplycky, a spokesperson for Housing Minister Paul Calandra, says the changes to the legislation "provide the appropriate balance between speeding up planning approvals and maintaining an avenue for impacted landowners to voice their concerns."
She says between 2021 and 2023, approximately 67,000 housing units were subject to third-party appeals of official plans and rezoning.
Teplycky says "the public still has opportunities to provide full input in the planning process to their locally elected councils, who are best positioned to make decisions that reflect the needs of their communities."
Environmental lawyer David Donnelly says in his research of third-party appeals, he believes just a handful of appeals in the GTA have hindered or slowed down the process.
Some residents whose cases have been dismissed this past month also disagree with the notion their voices are better heard at the municipal level.
Suzi Recine, with the group Friends to Conserve Kleinburg, who had filed an appeal at the OLT against plans to build about 800 single-detached homes and townhomes on part of the Copper Creek golf club in Vaughan, said the community turned to the OLT once they realized they had no support from the city and there was a "lack of consideration of their concerns."
"This was a way for residents to have a voice. Why are we being silenced?" said Recine. "Bills like Bill 185 ... give more powers to the government and developers, while taking away the public's opportunity to be heard."
The Copper Creek community is owned by both Fieldgate Developments and by TACC Developments, development companies run by Jack Eisenberger and Silvio De Gasperis. The development is being built by Block 55 Northwest Landowners Group Inc., which consists of three landowners, said Alana De Gasperis, a spokesperson for TACC Developments.
Recine says the residents appealed the development to the OLT amidst concerns of overdevelopment of the pristine area, and environmental degradation of an area that borders the Greenbelt and the Humber River watershed.
In a statement, Alana De Gasperis said there have been "numerous opportunities for public input" in addition to statutory public hearings. She also said the TACC and the landowners have worked with staff to preserve trees, create a buffer zone between the new and existing communities, and ensure appropriate environmental protections are in place, including the completion of several studies that have been reviewed by the city and the conservation authority.
She said the appeal, which was previously dismissed by the OLT and heard at Divisional Court, has "delayed the project approximately 3 years."
Recine says her group's case was dismissed by the OLT in July, despite having been given a hearing date prior to the bill's passing.
The Kleinburg group was told on April 10 that their case would be heard in June 2025.
Recine said within hours of their hearing being scheduled, the province introduced Bill 185, which included transition provisions that said any third-party appeal that didn't have a hearing date scheduled prior to April 10, 2024, was immediately dismissed. And even though the law didn't pass until June, this provision was applied retroactively.
"We got our date, and then a short while later, we found out our entire case was dismissed," said Recine. "Where is the fairness to the residents and the community?"
In an email, the province said the law was retroactively applied as that was the day the legislation was introduced, said Teplycky.
Environmental lawyer Donnelly said several groups he is representing have had their cases dismissed as a result of Bill 185.
"It's not just about the rights of one group of residents, it's about the rights of the public to fight for environmental protections for all," he said. "You should care about the fact that developers have all these rights, and citizens have none."
Julie Neves, the chair of Stop Petersburg Sand Pit Inc., says her group's appeal of an aggregate pit in Wilmot, Ont., was dismissed in July as her group was not one of the parties allowed to file an appeal per the new legislation. The group had been opposing an application for an aggregate pit, and had tried to petition their town council to monitor the air quality in the area where six others already operate, to find ways to "clean them up and improve conditions."
When those efforts failed, Neves' group filed an appeal with the OLT. But that appeal route was dismissed before it even began, she said.
"At this point, all our citizen rights have been taken away," said Neves. "There are no more options left ... and the development always gets its way."
Justin Sherwood, a spokesperson with the Building Industry and Land Development Association (BILD), said they support "efforts to increase the speed and efficiency of the approvals process" but says it's too early to know if this move by the province will do just that.
"We understand, and can appreciate, that one of the intentions to limit third party appeals was to eliminate those that were frivolous or vexatious. However, the results and outcome of this are yet to be tested since the legislation was only passed in early June," said Sherwood.
The OLT is the latest iteration of what has been more commonly known as the Ontario Municipal Board and the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal - administrative tribunals that were created to adjudicate planning matters. The OLT was created in 2021, after the government consolidated several different boards including the Conservation Review Board, the Environmental Review Tribunal, the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal and the Mining and Lands Tribunal.
Donnelly said removing third party appeals - or what he calls "citizen appeals" - at the OLT has little to do with NIMBYs (not-in-my-backyard), a term that refers to those who oppose development in their own neighbourhoods. He fears it will instead remove the ability of concerned citizens and environmental groups who have historically used the OLT (and its predecessors) to push cities and developers to make better planning decisions.
"Nobody has made the case that having residents, neighbours, environmentalists, First Nations involved in the planning process is a bad thing. They have always been the check and the balance in the system, and now that's being taken away," Donnelly said.
He cites several cases where appeals led to outcomes in the public interest, including the enactment of the Oak Ridges Moraine Protection Act, which came as the result of a 12-month appeal by a third-party group called Save the Rouge Valley System Inc. to a development on the environmentally sensitive lands. He said other appeals against aggregate quarries and mines have led the projects to be denied, or have a significantly reduced footprint.
CELA lawyer Wilson says that without a place for the public to appeal development decisions, many will have no choice but to turn to the courts by seeking a judicial review of a planning decision, leading to delays and high costs for all parties.
Recine says the Kleinburg group has already spent thousands on legal fees and experts at the OLT. In light of the recent dismissal, she says she's not sure if the group will continue their fight.
"I don't know where this leaves us," she said. "With a dismissal of the entire case, it leaves us without a voice."
CAPTION: A spokesperson for the developers behind the housing planned for Copper Creek say the third-party appeal has delayed the project by three years.Suzi Recine, left, and Uzzo Calderaro stand near a planned development in Kleinburg. Their residents group was appealing a development adjacent and abutting the Greenbelt, but that right was taken away from them with the passing of Bill 185.

Title: Environmental and residents groups call new provincial rules barring OLT third-party appeals an attack on 'citizen rights'

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

The microscopic menace in our air + why Ontario's new OLT rules are being called an attack on 'citizen rights'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/microscopic-menace-our-air-why-ontarios-new-olt/docview/3089467143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+microscopic+menace+in+our+air+%2B+why+Ontario%27s+new+OLT+rules+are+being+called+an+attack+on+%27citizen+rights%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, August 6 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the ultrafine particles in our air, pushback to Ontario Land Tribunal changes and the rise of solar power.
DON'T MISSThese tiny particles are linked to hundreds of deaths in Toronto
Nano-sized particles spewed from vehicle exhaust and industries in Canada's two largest cities are linked to an estimated 1,100 deaths each year - including 600 mortalities in Toronto - a new study discovered. "Where you live determines how much of this exposure you have," a study author tells Moira Welsh. Yet despite the danger, ultrafine air particles have gone unregulated. Take a look at what can be done.
By the numbers: Ultrafine particles are linked to a 7.3 per cent increase in the risk of non-accidental death. Respiratory deaths accounted for the highest increase in mortality, at 17.4 per cent, followed by coronary artery disease at 9.4 per cent.Toxic air: We're still discovering the extent of air pollution's toll on our bodies. A 2023 study found just two hours of exposure to traffic emissions led to these deficiencies in the brain.Meanwhile: New research suggests wildfire smoke could be worse for your cognition than other sources of air pollution, linking it to an increased risk of dementia.Why some call Ontario's new OLT rules an attack on 'citizen rights'
Environmental groups and residents say they've been silenced after a provincial law change banned third parties from appealing developmental decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal, Noor Javed reports. "It's really a problem to cut out active engaged citizens, who deeply care about their communities, from the equation," a lawyer for the Canadian Environmental Law Association said, adding that the move will ultimately lead to poorly planned and less safe communities.
Word from Doug Ford: The province justified the changes by saying they will "help communities get quicker planning approvals for housing projects." Residents can always provide input to their "locally elected councils" instead, a spokesperson for Housing Minister Paul Calandra said.But: Will local officials listen? Some advocates, like the group Friends to Conserve Kleinburg, say they only turned to the OLT after realizing they had no support from the city.They needed new balcony railings - landlords installed solar panels instead
When an aging apartment block on Wilson Avenue needed new balcony railings, landlords decided to ditch the typical glass for specially-designed solar panels. They're tough enough to weather the elements and so effective they don't even need to point at the sun, Marco Chown Oved reports. With solar panels becoming radically cheaper and more powerful, could a similar design soon come to your home?
Energy revolution: Building-integrated photovoltaics are the new frontier - essentially solar panels disguised as marble, wood or steel that can be installed anywhere on a building, not just the roof. They're being built here in Toronto and shipped around the world.Cost analysis: While the panels were more expensive to install than glass, they're still expected to pay for themselves several times over through savings on the power bill, experts say.David Olive's take: If the world is at the "dawn of the solar age," Canada didn't get the memo. Yet even without a federal plan to significantly boost solar power production, scores of community-based solar installations are blooming across the country.WHAT ELSEToronto was under yet another heavy rainfall advisory as parts of the Don Valley Parkway flooded last night.Toronto area home prices dropped in July while real estate listings kept piling up. Here's your real estate forecast.It's official! Kamala Harris is the Democratic presidential nominee - she will face off against Donald Trump this fall.Japan's share benchmark soared nearly 11 per cent a day after massive sell-offs sent global markets crashing.Why do election polls go wrong? Aisling Murphy discovered one reason when she polled for CNN in 2016.Bangladesh's prime minister resigned and fled the country while thousands of protesters stormed her official residence.Canada's Olympic beach volleyball duo toppled the Americans to reach the quarter-finals in a stunning upset.The dirty water in the Seine was just one issue. Here's the other problem Olympic triathletes were facing.The Canadian Olympic Committee pulled the accreditation from sprinter Andre De Grasse's coach.Meghan Markle talked about what she has "overcome" in a new TV interview alongside Harry.Taylor Swift's Eras Tour is coming to Toronto in 100 days. Are you ... ready for it?Are you in need of a U.S. visitor Visa? Watch out for these online scams.POVBumper-to-bumper traffic makes the long-weekend cottage commute a nightmare. It doesn't have to be that way.CLOSE-UP
PARIS: No, Kim Jong Un didn't swing by the boxing match between China and North Korea on Sunday - but professional Kim impersonator Howard X sure did. He tells Allan Woods how he found his calling - and why he decided to crash the Olympics.
Thank you for reading First Up. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca
Sign up here to get this newsletter in your inbox.
CAPTION: A team of researchers from several universities tracked air-pollution levels for a 15-year-period starting in 2001, looking at areas that were populated by 1.5 million adults.Mild flooding of the DVP visible from Dundas Street looking north Monday evening, with cars heading both directions splash each other.Gaze upon this and know: It doesn't have to be this way.Howard X, a professional Kim Jung Un impersonator, appeared at the Paris Olympic boxing match Sunday just as boxers from China and North Korea were fighting for a spot in the women's semifinal match.

Document 157 of 500

Groups say new law takes away 'public's right to be heard': Third parties no longer allowed to appeal development decisions to land tribunal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/groups-say-new-law-takes-away-publics-right-be/docview/3089158837/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Groups+say+new+law+takes+away+%27public%27s+right+to+be+heard%27%3A+Third+parties+no+longer+allowed+to+appeal+development+decisions+to+land+tribunal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A provincial law change that curtailed third-party groups' ability to appeal development decisions has left environmental and ratepayer groups saying they've been silenced in a move that puts developer interests ahead of citizen concerns.
Ontario residents can no longer appeal development decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal - a quasi-judicial body designed to adjudicate planning and other land disputes - after Premier Doug Ford's government introduced legislation that removes the ability of third parties such as ratepayer groups or environmental groups to do so.
The province says the changes to the third-party appeal rights in the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act (Bill 185), which passed in June, will "help communities get quicker planning approvals for housing projects."
But critics say the legislation is another attack on "citizen rights" and the public's participation in the development process and will ultimately lead to poorly planned and less safe communities across the province.
"This is the loss of an important mechanism for the public to make sure that environmental and public health concerns were raised and properly considered in development decisions across the province," said Jacqueline Wilson, counsel for the Canadian Environmental Law Association, who says the organization has been part of local resident appeals for decades.
"It's really a problem to cut out active engaged citizens, who deeply care about their communities, from the equation."
According to the OLT, between April 1, 2022, and March 31, 2023, the tribunal handled 1,422 cases, totalling 2,161 appeals.
The OLT said it doesn't track the number of appeals made by developers versus individuals or third-party groups. However, since the new law came into effect in June, 62 third-party appeals that didn't "meet the transition provisions," which include a hearing date scheduled before April 10, 2024, were dismissed, an OLT spokesperson said.
The new legislation places restrictions on the ability of the public to appeal amendments to official plans and zoning bylaws made through development applications. The only groups who can appeal are "public bodies," specified persons who are defined as government, utility boards and agencies. Landowners and developers can also appeal municipal development decisions they don't agree with.
Justine Teplycky, a spokesperson for Housing Minister Paul Calandra, says the changes to the legislation "provide the appropriate balance between speeding up planning approvals and maintaining an avenue for impacted landowners to voice their concerns."
She says that, between 2021 and 2023, approximately 67,000 housing units were subject to third-party appeals of official plans and rezoning.
Teplycky says "the public still has opportunities to provide full input in the planning process to their locally elected councils, who are best positioned to make decisions that reflect the needs of their communities."
Environmental lawyer David Donnelly says that, in his research of third-party appeals, he believes just a handful in the GTA have hindered or slowed down the process.
Some residents whose cases have been dismissed this past month also disagree with the notion their voices are better heard at the municipal level.
Suzi Recine, with the group Friends to Conserve Kleinburg, who had filed an appeal at the OLT against plans to build about 800 single-detached homes and townhomes on part of the Copper Creek golf club in Vaughan, said the community turned to the OLT once they realized they had no support from the city and there was a "lack of consideration of their concerns."
"This was a way for residents to have a voice. Why are we being silenced?" said Recine. "Bills like Bill 185 ... give more powers to the government and developers, while taking away the public's opportunity to be heard."
The Copper Creek community is owned by both Fieldgate Developments and TACC Developments, companies run by Jack Eisenberger and Silvio De Gasperis. The development is being built by Block 55 Northwest Landowners Group Inc., which consists of three landowners, said Alana De Gasperis, a spokesperson for TACC Developments.
Recine says the residents appealed the development to the OLT amidst concerns of overdevelopment of the pristine area, and environmental degradation of an area that borders the Greenbelt and the Humber River watershed.
In a statement, Alana De Gasperis said there have been "numerous opportunities for public input" in addition to statutory public hearings. She also said the TACC and the landowners have worked with staff to preserve trees, create a buffer zone between the new and existing communities and ensure appropriate environmental protections are in place, including the completion of several studies that have been reviewed by the city and the conservation authority.
She said the appeal, which was previously dismissed by the OLT and heard at Divisional Court, has "delayed the project approximately three years."
Recine says her group's case was dismissed by the OLT in July, despite having been given a hearing date prior to the bill's passing.
The Kleinburg group was told April 10 its case would be heard in June 2025.
Recine said, within hours of their hearing being scheduled, the province introduced Bill 185, which included transition provisions that said any third-party appeal that didn't have a hearing date scheduled prior to April 10, 2024, was immediately dismissed. And even though the law didn't pass until June, this provision was applied retroactively.
"We got our date, and then a short while later we found out our entire case was dismissed," said Recine. "Where is the fairness to the residents and the community?"
In an email, the province said the law was retroactively applied as that was the day the legislation was introduced, said Teplycky.
Environmental lawyer Donnelly said several groups he is representing have had their cases dismissed as a result of Bill 185.
"It's not just about the rights of one group of residents; it's about the rights of the public to fight for environmental protections for all," he said. "You should care about the fact that developers have all these rights, and citizens have none."
Julie Neves, the chair of Stop Petersburg Sand Pit Inc., says her group's appeal of an aggregate pit in Wilmot, Ont., was dismissed in July as her group was not one of the parties allowed to file an appeal per the new legislation. The group had been opposing an application for an aggregate pit, and had tried to petition their town council to monitor the air quality in the area where six others already operate, to find ways to "clean them up and improve conditions."
When those efforts failed, Neves's group filed an appeal with the OLT. But that appeal route was dismissed before it even began, she said.
"At this point, all our citizen rights have been taken away," said Neves. "There are no more options left ... and the development always gets its way."
Justin Sherwood, a spokesperson with the Building Industry and Land Development Association (BILD), said it supports "efforts to increase the speed and efficiency of the approvals process," but says it's too early to know if this move by the province will do just that. "We understand, and can appreciate, that one of the intentions to limit third-party appeals was to eliminate those that were frivolous or vexatious. However, the results and outcome of this are yet to be tested since the legislation was only passed in early June," he said.
The OLT is the latest iteration of what was previously known as the Ontario Municipal Board and the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal - administrative tribunals that were created to adjudicate planning matters. The OLT was created in 2021, after the government consolidated several different boards including the Conservation Review Board, the Environmental Review Tribunal, the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal and the Mining and Lands Tribunal.
Donnelly said removing third-party appeals - or what he calls "citizen appeals" - at the OLT has little to do with NIMBYs (not-in-my-backyard), a term that refers to those who oppose development in their own neighbourhoods.
He fears it will instead remove the ability of concerned citizens and environmental groups who have historically used the OLT (and its predecessors) to push cities and developers to make better planning decisions.
"Nobody has made the case that having residents, neighbours, environmentalists, First Nations involved in the planning process is a bad thing. They have always been the check and the balance in the system, and now that's being taken away," Donnelly said.
He cites several cases where appeals led to outcomes in the public interest, including the enactment of the Oak Ridges Moraine Protection Act, which came as the result of a 12-month appeal by a third-party group called Save the Rouge Valley System Inc. of a development on the environmentally sensitive lands.
He said other appeals against aggregate quarries and mines have led the projects to be denied, or have a significantly reduced footprint.
CELA lawyer Wilson says that, without a place for the public to appeal development decisions, many will have no choice but to turn to the courts by seeking a judicial review of a planning decision, leading to delays and high costs for all parties.
Recine says the Kleinburg group has already spent thousands on legal fees and experts at the OLT. In light of the recent dismissal, she says she's not sure if the group will continue its fight.
"I don't know where this leaves us," she said. "With a dismissal of the entire case, it leaves us without a voice."
CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Full text: Celebrating Summer
Medals, lofty goals are a family tradition, Aug. 2
Summer McIntosh is built differently. She is a phenom in the water and becoming a superstar and legend in Canada. I've said it before and I'll say it again, she should be flag bearer at the closing ceremony.
Paul Bacon,
Hallandale Beach, Fla.
Just as medals and lofty goals are a family tradition for Summer McIntosh's family, so is modesty. Case in point: I worked with McIntosh's maternal grandmother, Gertie Horstead, for many years and it was only during the 1984 Summer Olympics that she told us her daughter Jill was a participant! I quickly arranged for a telegram to be sent to Jill in Los Angeles wishing her good luck from all of her mother's co-workers. We later arranged for a TV to be set up in the lunchroom so we could watch Jill's butterfly event! Now we share in Horstead's happiness at her daughter's 1984 accomplishments and the accomplishments of her granddaughter in 2024.
Hazel Regan, Toronto
Dementia study spurs hope
Study suggests hope against dementia, Aug. 2
The results of the new study, suggesting nearly half of dementia cases can be prevented or delayed, is highly exciting. This fine opportunity to prevent or delay the onset of the dreaded dementia could well be the most uplifting news for our aging Canadian population.
The study identified uncorrected eyesight and "bad" cholesterol as two new, major risk factors for dementia. Poor education in early life, social isolation and depression are also considered "the most significant factors." It is heartening to learn that seeing other people, and interacting with them, is the easiest way to keep one's brain active! Geriatric medicine professor Dr. Roger Wong recommends socialization or daily exercise, which helps our brain's ability to march on despite facing cognitive decline. By recognizing the 13 early signs, we can now plan to defeat or delay dreaded dementia!
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
One big thing missing from this study in risk factors is having a genetic link to others who have had dementia. Two people I personally know who have dementia had mothers who had it, one with very early onset. Anecdotally, it can run in the family, and the research bears this out.
Sandra Hanmer, Hamilton
Ford does not compare
Does anybody remember what the past Conservative governments were like in Ontario, versus what Doug Ford's party is like today? Previous Conservative governments were very frugal with spending taxpayer money, but today, Ford's party is very good at wasting our money. This is not the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario that your parents/grandparents voted for!
Just recently he announced plans to pay off the Beer Store chain up to $225 million to allow other stores to sell beer, wine and alcohol. If he had waited another year or so, he would have avoided the costly boondoggle. Adding to that, if this goes through, the private sector will likely charge more than LCBO. Good luck to us with that! The other problem is that it will be much easier for underage people to access alcoholic beverages.
We will remember what you have done, Premier Ford!
Jan Larsen, Bracebridge, Ont.
Keep High Park free of cars
Torontonians maintain a very car-centric attitude, which apparently hasn't changed much since I left the city 50 years ago. Many don't seem to understand that by driving everywhere for everything, including High Park, they worsen the problem. They would alleviate it by walking, cycling or taking transit. Cars are heavy and take up a lot of space, so they cause congestion. Walkers, cyclists or transit riders don't. There really is no excuse why many more can't walk, cycle or take transit more often than they do. Better transit and more, not fewer, cycle tracks would help. Cars did not exist when High Park was founded. Somehow people managed.
Ed Janicki, Victoria
We must accept taxes
Making the case for, and against, the ban on cars, Reader's letter, Aug. 3
A Star letter writer wrote of the "strange yet calm" feeling that he got when walking on the streets in Oslo, Norway where the city has removed 700 parking spaces and replaced them with bike lanes, benches and planters. "Good public transit" picks up the slack. Earlier letters extolled the health-care system in Denmark where all residents are guaranteed a local family doctor, and noted the excellent daycare system in Sweden. In all the praise given to Scandinavia for superior social systems, not a mention is made that taxation makes these systems strong. All these countries have higher income tax levels than Canada. That doesn't count state taxes, sales taxes and other miscellaneous taxes. Canadians cannot hope to have the amenities the Scandinavians have until we get over our visceral hatred of taxes. I'd certainly be willing to pay more tax to get the services we deserve.
Joan Blackwood, Toronto
Trump's always been unglued
Trump appears to be coming unglued, Opinion, Aug. 2
It is difficult to know whether Donald Trump is more unglued now than he has been at any point in the recent past. If he was truthful in saying that he did not realize that Kamala Harris was Black the first time he became aware of her then he may have lacked glue for a considerable period. Most of us can accept that individuals can have dual ethnicities and can legitimately claim links to more than one race or nationality. If Trump has become unglued recently, it is not in his perception of other humans but in his willingness to say things that are intrinsically offensive to the audience he is speaking to.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
Cherishing freedom
U.S. and Russia tout prisoner swap as a victory. But perceptions of the deal show stark differences, Aug. 3
Freedom is something to be cherished, just ask Wall Street Journal reporter Evan Gershkovich and former marine Paul Whelan who had been held in Russian captivity. Both men probably thought that they would never see their families again, but are back on U.S. soil. Americans are seen as bargaining tools to countries hostile to the U.S. and do not belong there under any circumstances. Let us be grateful for the return of these honourable men.
JoAnn Lee Frank,
Clearwater, Fla.

Document 159 of 500

Canadians should reject Poilievre's indecency

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-should-reject-poilievres-indecency/docview/3088524466/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+should+reject+Poilievre%27s+indecency&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't vote for the indecent
It is time for Canadians to confront Poilievre's assault on decency, Aug. 1
Contributor Christopher Holcroft's call to confront Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's shameless name-calling is well-intentioned but won't stop electoral wins fuelled by emotional partisan impulses. What's needed is political action based on cause and effect: the public should not vote the indecent into office and instead vote the indecent out. Poilievre's name-calling isn't a bug but a major feature of our media-saturated times, where name recognition and outrageous persona win elections. Hurling attention-grabbing insults is the easiest and cheapest way to seize the public's attention and vote. Consider the evidence: Donald Trump's "Crooked Hillary" stuck and boosted his popularity because, in our media-driven attention economy, outrage is currency - it engages and wins hearts and minds. True political power rests with the public where the majority are overwhelmingly decent. If these voters reject indecent behaviour, political indecency will end. This is the most effective way to address the issue.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Poilievre's personal, ad hominem attacks go against the rules of civil debate. Such attacks are the hallmark of bullies. Canada can and must do better than to listen to politicians who do nothing to further Canada's reputation on the world stage and whose remarks do nothing to unify Canadians at home.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
It is heartwarming to know that I am not alone in loathing the political style of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. Let's hope the mere suggestion of expecting more and better from our politicians and other leaders will have the desired effect!
Edward Pickering, Hillier, Ont.
It is beyond time for some critical analysis of the politics of the man wanting to be Canada's next prime minister. I've been very concerned, nay, dismayed, at the lack of analysis of the Conservative Party of Canada's actual policies. The press lets Leader Pierre Poilievre get away with sniping and misleading comment without rebuttal. This is extraordinarily frustrating. I, for one, am hoping more pieces such as this will be published before it is too late for Canada to turn back from the brink.
Ann Peel, Toronto
We teach our children to treat each other with respect, including allowing someone to have a different opinion from ours without name calling, but it is clear Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has not learned this decency in his many years in politics. His criticisms of the prime minister, the Liberal and New Democratic parties include words such as "extremist," "raving wackos" and "lunatics." He is affirming a culture of disinformation, misrepresentation, mockery and demonization. It is a culture of bullying.
Canada is about common decency - the enduring values of civility and caring, honesty and humility. Poilievre does not represent common decency. He is the exact opposite. Poilievre would change Canada from a caring country to one which is divided. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must stay.
Sandra and Jim Hill, Guelph, Ont.
While the majority of Canadians are undoubtedly decent human beings, the problem lies with political leaders who should be establishing the standard by which societies function. When you have leaders like Donald Trump and Pierre Poilievre setting the tone of political discourse for their respective countries, it is easy to understand why we should start to worry about the well-being of civil societies.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
Trump's trash talk
Trump is making his 2024 campaign about Harris' race, whether Republicans want him to or not, Aug. 1
This trash talk by Donald Trump at the National Association of Black Journalists convention is just one more example of a man that is unable to interact meaningfully with strong women, unless it's through vulgar or demeaning outbursts. Women don't factor much in Trump's world. It is clear he chokes when he's challenged by a strong woman. He should write another book titled "The Art of the Choke."
Richard Kadziewicz, Scarborough
Abolish K.C. honour
Doug Ford promised to end the King's Counsel controversy. Now he says no one cares, Aug. 1
I tend to agree with people who say Premier Doug Ford should abolish the K.C. designation. The only K.C we need is Harry Wayne Casey, the lead singer of The Sunshine Band.
Gregory Hoggarth, Stratford, Ont.
The current controversy over the Ford government giving some lawyers the title of King's Counsel reminds me that in the 1980s, when the Peterson government abolished such appointments, then-Treasurer Robert Nixon suggested it could be replaced with a new title, Senior Ontario Barrister (S.O.B.).
Andy Lehrer, Toronto
If, as Premier Doug Ford said, "no one gives two hoots if they have K.C. behind their names," let's rescind the "awards" from last year and eliminate the title for good.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Get rid of deadlines
Premier Doug Ford's office directed Metrolinx to keep Crosstown opening date secret, emails show, Aug. 1
If people in Toronto are fed up with Metrolinx failing to meet deadlines in transit construction, the simplest solution is to get rid of deadlines. We've done this with other things, like the province getting rid of data and oversight (sewage testing, COVID testing in clinics). There are no deadlines in responding to shortages of housing or of physicians or nurses. We can't complain when there are no figures to live up to. There's a pattern here - not particularly intelligent or even grown up - but it seems to work in politics.
James Maloy, Barrie, Ont.
Predictable consequences of the Online News Act
Canadians are encountering fewer legitimate news sources on social media, study finds, Aug. 1
When the government introduced the Online News Act, we knew Meta would stop carrying news on Facebook, Instagram and other platforms, and that this would hurt local journalism and allow misinformation to go unchallenged. A year later, this is exactly what has happened. Ottawa seems to believe that Facebook has committed an act of aggression against Canada. Can the government not accept responsibility for the direct consequences of its own law?
David Arthur, Cambridge, Ont.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks at a news conference in Montreal on July 12.

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Political power; Politics; Prime ministers; Social networks

Document 160 of 500

There is only one proven way to solve road congestion in Toronto and everyone's going to hate it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-is-only-one-proven-way-solve-road/docview/3088524459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+is+only+one+proven+way+to+solve+road+congestion+in+Toronto+and+everyone%27s+going+to+hate+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Few things are as reliable in a Toronto summer as long-weekend traffic. Right up there with death, taxes, and, more recently, Blue Jays-related disappointments.
The traffic snarls begin relatively early on a Friday. By mid-afternoon the radio reports warn of trouble if you are heading north to the cottage, or south toward the border, or in any direction, really. GPS apps display furious red lines stretching as far as one can scroll.
It's a nightmare. It's also one we have chosen.
There is a simple tool to combat traffic congestion that has been proven to be effective. There are real-world examples of where it has been deployed to great and long-lasting success. It's called road pricing. And we seem to be deathly afraid of it.
There has been empirical evidence for the benefits of road pricing for decades now. Putting a cost on automobile travel, whether by tolling certain roads or imposing a fee to drive into, say, the downtown core, encourages would-be drivers to find an alternative: they take public-transit, or they delay that car trip to a non-peak, less costly time. Traffic levels drop and the speed of travel increases. It worked in Singapore in the 1970s and in cities like London and Stockholm in the decades since.
And yet the examples of cities that have actually gone ahead and implemented road pricing remain few. A request from former mayor John Tory to impose tolls on the Don Valley Parkway and Gardiner Expressway was refused by former premier Kathleen Wynne in 2017. Her successor Doug Ford has enthusiastically removed tolls on highways to the city's east and set about expanding existing toll-free highways.
New York Governor Kathy Hochul, meanwhile, abruptly paused a plan to impose a congestion charge on vehicles driving into Manhattan in June, just weeks before it was to go into effect, citing concerns over "unintended consequences." Hochul, like Wynne, is considered a progressive who knows the environmental impacts of traffic congestion. But those unintended consequences are also obvious: angry voters.
Jonas Eliasson, director of travel accessibility at the Swedish Transport Administration, has first-hand experience with the effects of the congestion charge implemented in Stockholm in 2006. Public polling showed two-thirds of voters were against the road-pricing plan before it was introduced in a pilot program. A local politician called it the "most expensive way ever devised to commit political suicide." But after it began, Stockholm traffic levels dropped by 25 per cent, more than double initial estimates. In a subsequent referendum, Stockholm residents voted to adopt the congestion charge permanently.
"What really changed people's minds was when they saw the effects of the charges," Eliasson said in an interview. The improved traffic flow meant that businesses that had feared a loss of customers in downtown areas instead found the opposite was true. Almost two decades later, traffic levels in Stockholm remain below what they were before the program began, even as the city has grown in size.
"I'm actually surprised that (road pricing) hasn't happened in more places than it has so far," Eliasson said. "It's not so much that people don't believe in the effects. It seems to be conflicts of power between different geographic (areas) and different political levels."
Road-pricing plans typically require agreement among multiple governments, often those from competing parties or with differing ideological views.
"And that turns out to be a much harder thing to solve than the more mundane things like how to design the working system on the technological side," Eliasson said.
Consider the case in Toronto. Wynne, who represented a midtown riding, had emphasized the need for new "revenue tools" to help solve Toronto's gridlock when running for the provincial Liberal leadership. Once she became premier, two different government-appointed panels recommended some form of tolling to fund public-transit, but those proposals were never adopted. Even after winning re-election, Wynne shot down city council's request to boost transit funding by tolling highways.
It's worth noting that the provincial NDP also opposed tolling the highways. Andrea Horwath once threatened to bring down Wynne'sgovernment over a small-scale experiment with High-Occupancy Toll lanes on a few stretches of highway. Horwath decried them as "Lexus lanes." Instead of serving as a step toward city-wide road pricing, they remain an anachronism, and a legacy of political cowardice. A worldwide study last year, meanwhile, pegged Toronto's rush-hour traffic as the worst in North America, with an average speed of 18 kilometres per hour.
Todd Litman, the executive director of the Victoria Transport Policy Institute, an independent think-tank, said the answer to gridlock is staring everyone in the face.
"Over the years, transportation economists and planners have pointed out that there really is no other solution to traffic congestion than more efficient pricing," he said in an interview. "So every time somebody said 'No, I don't want road pricing or congestion pricing,' they're actually saying, 'I want traffic congestion.'"
He said that to drivers, traffic congestion is like "what tides are to sailors. That is, you know you are going to experience this thing if you travel at a certain time, and you can either work with it or work against it."
Traffic congestion maintains an equilibrium, Litman said. If it gets bad enough, that discourages some trips. But then if roads are expanded in response, as the Ontario government is now doing, then traffic will soon return to that previous equilibrium, a phenomenon sometimes called induced demand.
"If you widen the roads, you are going to get more peak-period trips, but if you don't then congestion just limits itself," Litman said.
Critics of tolls and congestion charges often accuse them of being regressive policies that disproportionally impact low-income residents, for whom a five-dollar charge would be more keenly felt than someone making six figures. (See, for example, Horwath's use of "Lexus" as a pejorative.)
But, "it is not regressive at all," Litman said. "In almost every city where you're considering putting in road tolls, only a small portion of lower-income people do drive during peak periods. So if it's properly done, and you're using the revenue to improve efficient modes (of transport) like walking, cycling and public transit, no question about it, most low-income people would be better off."
And, again, in places like London and Stockholm where congestion charges have been implemented, the revenues have been put toward improving public-transit options like buses, which run faster and more reliably because traffic has been reduced - clear benefits to those who do not own cars.
Toronto also has its own real-world example of the effectiveness of a tolled road. Highway 407, loathed though it may be because it is privately owned due to the ill-considered sale by the former Mike Harris government, is nevertheless a fast-moving alternative to the perpetually clogged Highway 401. The 407 stands as proof that certain drivers will pay for the privilege of using a highway that is much less likely to leave them stranded in a lengthy traffic jam.
Litman notes that those who argue against road pricing often act as though paying to use a road would be a crippling blow.
"Almost the whole narrative that opponents (use) assumes that we are all struggling cheapskates and that if every motorist had to pay a toll it would harm them terribly and they couldn't possibly afford it," he said. "On the other hand, when's the last time someone paid a few dollars for a litre of water, when you can get almost exactly the same quality of water from the tap?"
There's also research that suggests congestion charges have an upward effect on home prices in the affected area. While that also has a downside related to housing costs, it does demonstrate the benefits of living somewhere where roads have been priced efficiently. Tang Cheng Keat, an assistant professor of economics at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore, found that real estate values inside the congestion-charge zone in London are about three per cent higher than those outside it, even in comparable areas. Traffic levels are lower inside the zone, there are fewer accidents, and air quality is better - all possible reasons why people are willing to pay a premium to live there.
And yet, road pricing remains a difficult sell. Litman pointed to an example close to his home in British Columbia, where the former Liberal government built a new bridge over the Fraser River in Vancouver. The Liberals were pro-business, he noted. "They said, 'We're not a bunch of socialists, we're going to charge people for using that bridge.'" Opposition was so fierce that the opposition NDP - the NDP! - ran successfully on a promise to eliminate the tolls, which they did.
"So those of us that do not use that bridge are now subsidizing those suburban automobile commuters," Litman said, with what sounded like a rueful chuckle, over the phone.
In February, the Toronto region Board of Trade announced a task force of business leaders to consider possible solutions to what it called the city's "congestion crisis." Their report is due back later this year.
History suggests that even if the business group recommends putting a price on roads, the hurdles will still be many. Or, as Litman puts it: "People would rather complain about traffic than do anything about it."
It's something to think about, while heading up to the cottage, or off on the weekend road trip, and hitting the inevitable traffic jams stretching beyond the horizon. It's not just that we could do something about this. It's that we've decided not to, and we're all suffering as a result.
CAPTION: Gaze upon this and know: It doesn't have to be this way.If you love sitting in traffic, you should hate tolls.It doesn't have to be this way.The research is clear: Road tolls would improve traffic in Toronto for everyone.There is a way to get more cars off the streets in Toronto, but you might not like it.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson, Contributor

Subject: Political appointments; Tolls; Voters; Politics; Low income groups; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways

Location: Singapore
