Officer shot chasing wanted man: Off-duty cop was struck in leg while tracking down man who faces attempted murder charge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/officer-shot-chasing-wanted-man/docview/3087933114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Officer+shot+chasing+wanted+man%3A+Off-duty+cop+was+struck+in+leg+while+tracking+down+man+who+faces+attempted+murder+charge&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An off-duty Toronto police officer was shot as he and other officers were arresting a man wanted on a Canada-wide warrant in the city's downtown core late Saturday night.
Police said they were conducting an investigation in the Bathurst Street and King Street West area just before 11:45 p.m. when the off-duty officer was shot in the upper leg.
The officer was transported to hospital with non-life-threatening injuries and was later discharged Sunday morning.
Police added he is currently recovering at home.
Det.-Sgt. Brandon Price from the force's Homicide and Missing Person Unit told reporters Sunday morning at 14 Division that the officer was in the area when he recognized a man wanted on a Canada-wide warrant for attempted murder and numerous other charges.
Price said the officer, who works in 51 Division, "promptly alerted on-duty officers" who then started to approach the man to apprehend him.
This led to a short foot chase by uniformed officers during which the accused, who was believed to be alone at the time, reportedly shot the off-duty officer in the leg while he tried to assist his colleagues, he said.
"The incident demonstrates that our officers, even when off-duty are committed to keeping our city safe, sometimes at great risk to themselves," Price said.
The shooting happened in the southwest corner of the busy intersection as many people were out enjoying the long weekend at the bars and restaurants in the area.
Price said there was no initial interaction between the man and officers, but the suspect started running when he noticed police. He added that there were no shots fired by police during the takedown by on-duty officers.
The 30-year-old-suspect, identified as Javell Jackson from Toronto, was arrested at the scene. He was wanted for the attempted murder of a man in a near-fatal shooting from December 2022 in the area of Church Street and Front Street East.
Police added a firearm was recovered.
"Additionally, he was wanted for unauthorized possession of a firearm, possession of a firearm contrary to a prohibition order, and failure to comply with the release order," Price said.
Jackson will be facing another count of attempted murder and numerous additional firearms charges, Price said.
"There are weapons on the streets, and we need to always be aware of that, and these officers did an amazing job taking a very dangerous individual off our streets," Price said.
The Toronto Police Association (TPA) said in a post on X the officer is 30 years old and has been on the job for six years.
"Despite this event, our member is in good spirits and is well on his way to recovery," TPA said. "We are thankful he's going to be OK and we are proud of his efforts."
TPA president Jon Reid told the Star he became aware of the incident shortly after midnight and he and a fellow TPA director promptly visited the injured officer in hospital.
Reid said the incident could have been "so much worse" and he is glad the officer will "hopefully" be making a full recovery.
He said incidents like this could have a "deep rippling effect" on the injured, the other officers at the scene and family members, adding the association will also be providing mental health resources to all parties involved.
"The criminal element here in the city, obviously, is becoming far more brazen," Reid said when asked about the state of violence in the city.
In June, Toronto police reported there was a nearly 74 per cent increase in "shooting events" across the city in 2024 compared to the same point last year. This number included shootings resulting in injuries or deaths, as well as firearm discharges with no reported injuries.
However, the increase is partly due to 2023 being a statistic outlier after the city saw a significant drop in gun violence rates last year. A total of 168 people were killed or injured in a shooting last year - still a lot of people, but the lowest recorded in a single year since 2015 and down 40 per cent in five years.
Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw said in a statement that the officer's "diligence" and the bravery of all involved officers is "commendable."
Mayor Olivia Chow posted on X on Sunday morning wishing the officer a quick recovery.
"Last night, a Toronto police officer was shot while performing their duties. I wish the officer a quick recovery from their injuries and offer my full support. Violence is never acceptable," Chow said.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said in a statement he is "relieved to hear the officer is alive."
"This terrible shooting is a reminder of the risks our brave women and men in uniform face every day as they work to keep our communities safe, and why it's so important that we all have their backs," Ford said in a post on X. "I'm relieved to hear the officer is alive and receiving treatment and I'm confident those responsible for this terrible act will be held accountable."
CREDIT: Asma Sahebzada and Ilyas Hussein Toronto Star

Full text: Don't vote the indecent into political office
It is time for Canadians to confront Poilievre's assault on decency, Aug. 1
Contributor Christopher Holcroft's call to confront Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's shameless name-calling is well-intentioned but won't stop electoral wins fuelled by emotional partisan impulses. What's needed is political action based on cause and effect: the public should not vote the indecent into office and instead vote the indecent out. Poilievre's name-calling isn't a bug but a major feature of our media-saturated times, where name recognition and outrageous persona win elections. True political power rests with the public where the majority are overwhelmingly decent. If these voters reject indecent behaviour, political indecency will end. This is the most effective way to address the issue.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's personal, ad hominem attacks go against the rules of civil debate. Such attacks are the hallmark of bullies. Canada can and must do better than to listen to politicians who do nothing to further Canada's reputation on the world stage and whose remarks do nothing to unify Canadians at home.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
It is heartwarming to know that I am not alone in loathing the political style of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. Let's hope the mere suggestion of expecting more and better from our politicians and other leaders will have the desired effect.
Edward Pickering, Hillier, Ont.
It is beyond time for some critical analysis of the politics of the man wanting to be Canada's next prime minister. I've been very concerned, nay, dismayed, at the lack of analysis of the Conservative Party of Canada's actual policies. The press lets Pierre Poilievre get away with sniping and misleading comment without rebuttal. This is extraordinarily frustrating. I, for one, am hoping more pieces such as this will be published before it is too late for Canada to turn back from the brink.
Ann Peel, Toronto
We teach our children to treat each other with respect, including allowing someone to have a different opinion from ours without name calling, but it is clear Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has not learned this decency in his many years in politics. His criticisms of the prime minister, the Liberal and New Democratic parties include words such as "extremist," "raving wackos" and "lunatics." He is affirming a culture of disinformation, misrepresentation, mockery and demonization. It is a culture of bullying.
Canada is about common decency - the enduring values of civility and caring, honesty and humility. Poilievre does not represent common decency. He is the exact opposite. Poilievre would change Canada from a caring country to one that is divided.
Sandra and Jim Hill,
Guelph, Ont.
While the majority of Canadians are undoubtedly decent human beings, the problem lies with political leaders who should be establishing the standard by which societies function. When you have leaders like Donald Trump and Pierre Poilievre setting the tone of political discourse for their respective countries, it is easy to understand why we should start to worry about the well-being of civil societies.
Charles Campisi, Oakville

strong women
Trump is making his 2024 campaign about Harris' race, whether Republicans want him to or not, Aug. 1

Women don't factor much in Trump's world. It is clear he chokes when he's challenged by a strong woman.

Scarborough

I tend to agree with people who say Premier Doug Ford should abolish the King's Counsel designation. The only K.C. we need is Harry Wayne Casey, the lead singer of the Sunshine Band.
Gregory Hoggarth, Stratford, Ont.

Andy Lehrer, Toronto

Ditch deadlines
for transit construction
Premier Doug Ford's office directed Metrolinx to keep Crosstown opening date secret, emails show, Aug. 1
If people in Toronto are fed up with Metrolinx failing to meet deadlines in transit construction, the simplest solution is to get rid of deadlines. We've done this with other things, like the province getting rid of data and oversight (sewage testing, COVID testing in clinics). There are no deadlines in responding to shortages of housing or of physicians or nurses. We can't complain when there are no figures to live up to. There's a pattern here - not particularly intelligent or even grown up - but it seems to work in politics.
James Maloy, Barrie
Predictable consequences of Online News Act
Canadians are encountering fewer legitimate news sources on social media, study finds, Aug. 1
When the government introduced the Online News Act, we knew Meta would stop carrying news and that this would hurt local journalism and allow misinformation to go unchallenged. A year later, this is exactly what has happened. Ottawa seems to believe that Facebook has committed an act of aggression against Canada. Can the government not accept responsibility for the direct consequences of its own law?
David Arthur, Cambridge, Ont.

Document 163 of 500

For whom the road tolls: Hate them or not, tolls are the only surefire way to reduce congestion in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whom-road-tolls/docview/3087932796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+whom+the+road+tolls%3A+Hate+them+or+not%2C+tolls+are+the+only+surefire+way+to+reduce+congestion+in+Toronto&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Few things are as reliable in a Toronto summer as long-weekend traffic. Right up there with death, taxes, and, more recently, Blue Jays-related disappointments.
The traffic snarls begin relatively early on a Friday. By mid-afternoon the radio reports warn of trouble if you are heading north to the cottage, or south toward the border, or in any direction, really. GPS apps display furious red lines stretching as far as one can scroll.
It's a nightmare. It's also one we have chosen.
There is a simple tool to combat traffic congestion that has been proven to be effective. There are real-world examples of where it has been deployed to great and long-lasting success. It's called road pricing. And we seem to be deathly afraid of it.
There has been empirical evidence for the benefits of road pricing for decades now. Putting a cost on automobile travel, whether by tolling certain roads or imposing a fee to drive into, say, the downtown core, encourages would-be drivers to find an alternative: they take public-transit, or they delay that car trip to a non-peak, less costly time. Traffic levels drop and the speed of travel increases. It worked in Singapore in the 1970s and in cities like London and Stockholm in the decades since.
And yet the examples of cities that have actually gone ahead and implemented road pricing remain few. A request from former mayor John Tory to impose tolls on the Don Valley Parkway and Gardiner Expressway was refused by former premier Kathleen Wynne in 2017. Her successor Doug Ford has enthusiastically removed tolls on highways to the city's east and set about expanding existing toll-free highways.
New York Gov. Kathy Hochul, meanwhile, abruptly paused a plan to impose a congestion charge on vehicles driving into Manhattan in June, just weeks before it was to go into effect, citing concerns over "unintended consequences." Hochul, like Wynne, is considered a progressive who knows the environmental impacts of traffic congestion. But those unintended consequences are also obvious: angry voters.
Jonas Eliasson, director of travel accessibility at the Swedish Transport Administration, has first-hand experience with the effects of the congestion charge implemented in Stockholm in 2006. Public polling showed two-thirds of voters were against the road-pricing plan before it was introduced in a pilot program. A local politician called it the "most expensive way ever devised to commit political suicide." But after it began, Stockholm traffic levels dropped by 25 per cent, more than double initial estimates. In a subsequent referendum, Stockholm residents voted to adopt the congestion charge permanently.
"What really changed people's minds was when they saw the effects of the charges," Eliasson said in an interview. The improved traffic flow meant that businesses that had feared a loss of customers in downtown areas instead found the opposite was true. Almost two decades later, traffic levels in Stockholm remain below what they were before the program began, even as the city has grown in size.
"I'm actually surprised that (road pricing) hasn't happened in more places than it has so far," Eliasson said. "It's not so much that people don't believe in the effects. It seems to be conflicts of power between different geographic (areas) and different political levels."
Road-pricing plans typically require agreement among multiple governments, often those from competing parties or with differing ideological views.
"And that turns out to be a much harder thing to solve than the more mundane things like how to design the working system on the technological side," Eliasson said.
Consider the case in Toronto. Wynne, who represented a midtown riding, had emphasized the need for new "revenue tools" to help solve Toronto's gridlock when running for the provincial Liberal leadership. Once she became premier, two different government-appointed panels recommended some form of tolling to fund public-transit, but those proposals were never adopted. Even after winning re-election, Wynne shot down city council's request to boost transit funding by tolling highways.
It's worth noting that the provincial NDP also opposed tolling the highways. Andrea Horwath once threatened to bring down Wynne's government over a small-scale experiment with High-Occupancy Toll lanes on a few stretches of highway. Horwath decried them as "Lexus lanes." Instead of serving as a step toward city-wide road pricing, they remain an anachronism, and a legacy of political cowardice. A worldwide study last year, meanwhile, pegged Toronto's rush-hour traffic as the worst in North America, with an average speed of 18 kilometres per hour.
Todd Litman, the executive director of the Victoria Transport Policy Institute, an independent think tank, said the answer to gridlock is staring everyone in the face.
"Over the years, transportation economists and planners have pointed out that there really is no other solution to traffic congestion than more efficient pricing," he said in an interview. "So every time somebody said 'No, I don't want road pricing or congestion pricing,' they're actually saying, 'I want traffic congestion.' "
He said that to drivers, traffic congestion is like "what tides are to sailors. That is, you know you are going to experience this thing if you travel at a certain time, and you can either work with it or work against it."
Traffic congestion maintains an equilibrium, Litman said. If it gets bad enough, that discourages some trips. But then if roads are expanded in response, as the Ontario government is now doing, then traffic will soon return to that previous equilibrium, a phenomenon sometimes called induced demand.
"If you widen the roads, you are going to get more peak-period trips, but if you don't then congestion just limits itself," Litman said.
Critics of tolls and congestion charges often accuse them of being regressive policies that disproportionally impact low-income residents, for whom a five-dollar charge would be more keenly felt than someone making six figures. (See, for example, Horwath's use of "Lexus" as a pejorative.)
But, "it is not regressive at all," Litman said. "In almost every city where you're considering putting in road tolls, only a small portion of lower-income people do drive during peak periods. So if it's properly done, and you're using the revenue to improve efficient modes (of transport) like walking, cycling and public transit, no question about it, most low-income people would be better off."
And, again, in places like London and Stockholm where congestion charges have been implemented, the revenues have been put toward improving public-transit options like buses, which run faster and more reliably because traffic has been reduced - clear benefits to those who do not own cars.
Toronto also has its own real-world example of the effectiveness of a tolled road. Highway 407, loathed though it may be because it is privately owned due to the ill-considered sale by the former Mike Harris government, is nevertheless a fast-moving alternative to the perpetually clogged Highway 401. The 407 stands as proof that certain drivers will pay for the privilege of using a highway that is much less likely to leave them stranded in a lengthy traffic jam.
Litman notes that those who argue against road pricing often act as though paying to use a road would be a crippling blow.
"Almost the whole narrative that opponents (use) assumes that we are all struggling cheapskates and that if every motorist had to pay a toll it would harm them terribly and they couldn't possibly afford it," he said. "On the other hand, when's the last time someone paid a few dollars for a litre of water, when you can get almost exactly the same quality of water from the tap?"
There's also research that suggests congestion charges have an upward effect on home prices in the affected area. While that also has a downside related to housing costs, it does demonstrate the benefits of living somewhere where roads have been priced efficiently. Tang Cheng Keat, an assistant professor of economics at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore, found that real estate values inside the congestion-charge zone in London are about three per cent higher than those outside it, even in comparable areas. Traffic levels are lower inside the zone, there are fewer accidents, and air quality is better - all possible reasons why people are willing to pay a premium to live there.
And yet, road pricing remains a difficult sell. Litman pointed to an example close to his home in British Columbia, where the former Liberal government built a new bridge over the Fraser River in Vancouver. The Liberals were pro-business, he noted. "They said, 'We're not a bunch of socialists, we're going to charge people for using that bridge.' " Opposition was so fierce that the opposition NDP - the NDP! - ran successfully on a promise to eliminate the tolls, which they did.
"So those of us that do not use that bridge are now subsidizing those suburban automobile commuters," Litman said, with what sounded like a rueful chuckle, over the phone.
In February, the Toronto region Board of Trade announced a task force of business leaders to consider possible solutions to what it called the city's "congestion crisis." Their report is due back later this year.
History suggests that even if the business group recommends putting a price on roads, the hurdles will still be many. Or, as Litman puts it: "People would rather complain about traffic than do anything about it."
It's something to think about, while heading up to the cottage, or off on the weekend road trip, and hitting the inevitable traffic jams stretching beyond the horizon. It's not just that we could do something about this. It's that we've decided not to, and we're all suffering as a result.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CAPTION: Highway 407 stands as proof that certain drivers will pay for the privilege of using a highway that is much less likely to leave them stranded in a lengthy traffic jam, Scott Stinson writes.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Document 164 of 500

How Rob and Doug Ford's police helicopter hopes finally took off in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-rob-doug-fords-police-helicopter-hopes/docview/3087695875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Rob+and+Doug+Ford%27s+police+helicopter+hopes+finally+took+off+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Something felt a little retro this week when Premier Doug Ford announced plans to purchase five choppers for police and cover ongoing operating costs.
Helicopters can conjure all kinds of images. Think of the Los Angeles news chopper over the slow-speed police chase of O.J. Simpson and his white Ford Bronco back in 1994, before Simpson was arrested and charged with murdering his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and her friend, Ron Goldman. (That white Bronco is now in a Tennessee crime museum.) Others might equally hearken back to 1980s' TV shows - think "Magnum P.I." or "Airwolf."
Perhaps a little nostalgia is appropriate. These new helicopters are hardly a new idea.
Choppers for coppers was a hot topic 20 years ago, when Ford's late brother Rob was a city councillor, and outspoken proponent for equipping police with an air fleet.
That move ultimately fizzled, but not for lack of trying by Rob Ford and then-Toronto police chief Julian Fantino.
Back then, the Toronto Police Service Board said it didn't see roto-coppers as a priority.
This week, Ford's argument finally won the day, thanks to his brother, the premier.
'We've been dither-dallying'
Two decades ago, Toronto police would call on either the York or Durham forces to loan them their choppers, when needed. That's a practice that continues.
In 2000, Toronto police operated a pair of leased helicopters for six months in what was called (no pun intended) a pilot project.
In a review of that pilot project, former city auditor Jeff Griffiths found the helicopter made police and some citizens feel safer but had mixed results as a law-enforcement tool.
Griffiths' report also said the city would need more than one to patrol the city effectively.
Fantino was undaunted by drawbacks outlined in the auditor's report, which neither recommended nor opposed the helicopters.
The two helicopters used in the pilot project more than 20 years ago were paid for following a fundraising drive dubbed "Choppers for Coppers" with a provincial grant and private donations that Fantino collected from a mixed bag of benefactors, including air, sea and army cadets, major banks, a tobacco company, a brewery, a taekwondo studio and the Toronto Maple Leafs.
York Police also turned to private donors to get their fleet off the ground.
That included a mystery donor who was later revealed to be Charles R. Burnett III, the grandson of Garfield Weston, of the wealthy Weston family, nephew of Galen Weston, head of the family's multibillion-dollar retail, grocery and real estate empire, and nephew-through-marriage of then Ontario Lt.-Gov., Hilary Weston.
Rob Ford chafed in 2004 at a budget meeting that it was high time to finally make a decision.
"There's no sense in waiting for a report," Rob Ford said at the time. "We have to get on to this immediately. We've been dither-dallying for years and years and years."
Rob Ford unsuccessfully pressed on despite comments from Toronto police board chair Alan Heisey that he'd rather add 15 to 20 officers to the force with the money.
The council vote was 27-12 to refer it to the Toronto Police Service Board, where it was quashed.
'Every tool will help'
This week saw Doug Ford pledge $134 million for five new police helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa to combat crimes such as auto theft, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving.
In Ford's plan, two helicopters will be purchased for the Ontario Provincial Police to work with the Ottawa and Toronto police, and three more helicopters will be purchased and operated by Durham, Halton and Peel police.
As Ford announced the establishment of the Greater Toronto Area Joint Air Support Unit (JASU), he said the new helicopter fleet would be just the thing to combat sky-high auto theft rates, that saw Toronto lose more than 12,000 autos to theft last year.
Critics told the Star's Kristin Rushowy that the choppers are helpful but "not the end all, be all."
A security official with Équité Association, the insurance industry investigative arm, said it welcomes helicopters in its fight against auto theft.
"Absolutely," says Bryan Gast, a former OPP officer. "Every tool will help to combat crime, including vehicle crimes."
GTA police were also buoyant at the news.
"Equipped with the latest technology, the helicopters will help keep Toronto safe from violent carjackings, Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw said as he addressed the Toronto Police Service Board on Wednesday.
"Auto thefts, street racing and impaired driving, and will also assist with missing persons and other police operations, at no cost to the city," Demkiw said.
"This is a monumental investment that will provide much-needed support to our officers and help them do the difficult work they do every day to enhance community safety," Peel Regional Police Chief Nishan Duraiappah said.
That means assistance in patrols, suspect and vehicle pursuits, missing person searches and combating street racing and stunt driving, Peel police said.
What other Canadian cities have in the sky
Calgary police have had helicopters for years, and upgraded their fleet in 2020 with two replacement aircraft.
The previous fleet had been in service more than 15 years.
Calgary police say the helicopters allow them to travel from one end of the city to the other in just eight minutes.
Montreal police have often borrowed a chopper from the provincial Sûreté du Quebéc to increase their visibility around popular gathering spots.
This was a particularly big priority for policing the Old Port, the downtown core and Mount Royal between 10 p.m. and 3 a.m. during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, when large gatherings were forbidden.
Some Montreal residents complained that low-flying police choppers were intimidating and irritating, especially for children and pets.
Durham Regional Police Service have had a helicopter, dubbed "Air1," for more than 20 years.
York Regional Police boasts an Air Support Unit with a new state-of-the-art aircraft called "Air2," with what it calls "cutting-edge technology."
York police said the force's old "Air2" proved its worth "time and again," while logging 20 years of service and 20,000 operational hours.
"With the growth of our communities and changing dynamics of policing in Ontario, the York Regional Police Services Board approved a request from the York Regional Police Executive Command Team for the $7.1 million purchase of a replacement aircraft for Air2," York police announced in 2023 as it changed choppers.
"In 2022 alone, Air2 supported 1,373 calls for service, assisted officers on 266 traffic stops and helped make 213 arrests, including for commercial robberies, carjackings, break and enters, impaired driving, street racing and other offences," York police said in a statement. "Additionally, Air2 located 15 missing and vulnerable people."
Amid the debate over effectiveness, one salient point may have been made by former Toronto mayor Mel Lastman, who once said he liked the idea of helicopters because it makes people feel safe.
"I like to feel safe," Lastman said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford on Monday announced $134 million for five new police helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa to combat crimes such as auto theft, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving.In 2000, Const. Nicole Campbell was one of five officers assigned to the Toronto police's six-month helicopter pilot project.York Regional Police's first "Air2" chopper, seen in 2015, logged 20 years of service and 20,000 operational hours. The force has a new state-of-the-art aircraft, also called "Air2."

Subject: Aircraft; Automobile racing; Police pursuit driving; Helicopters; Pilot projects; Robbery; Missing persons

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Ford, Rob

Title: How Rob and Doug Ford's police helicopter hopes finally took off in Toronto

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 4, 2024

Section: news/gta

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 165 of 500

Off-duty Toronto police officer shot downtown after spotting wanted man, assisting colleagues in foot chase

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/off-duty-toronto-police-officer-shot-downtown/docview/3087695811/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Off-duty+Toronto+police+officer+shot+downtown+after+spotting+wanted+man%2C+assisting+colleagues+in+foot+chase&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An off-duty Toronto police officer was shot as he and other officers were arresting a man wanted on a Canada-wide warrant in the city's downtown core late Saturday night.
Police said they were conducting an investigation in the Bathurst Street and King Street West area just before 11:45 p.m. when the off-duty officer was shot in the upper leg.
The officer was transported to hospital with non-life-threatening injuries and was later discharged Sunday morning. Police added he is currently recovering at home.
Det. Sgt. Brandon Price from the force's Homicide and Missing Person Unit told reporters Sunday morning from 14 Division that the officer was in the area when he recognized a man wanted on a Canada-wide warrant for attempted murder and numerous other charges.
Price said the officer, who works in 51 Division, "promptly alerted on-duty officers" who then started to approach the man to apprehend him.
This led to a short foot chase by uniformed officers during which the accused, who was believed to be alone at the time, reportedly shot the off-duty officer in the leg while he tried to assist his colleagues, he said.
"The incident demonstrates that our officers, even when off-duty are committed to keeping our city safe, sometimes at great risk to themselves," Price said.
The shooting happened in the southwest corner of the busy intersection as many people were out enjoying the long weekend at the bars and restaurants in the area.
Price said there was no initial interaction between the man and officers, but the suspect started running when he noticed police. He added that there were no shots fired by police during the takedown by on-duty officers.
The 30-year-old-suspect, identified as Javell Jackson from Toronto, was arrested at the scene. He was wanted for the attempted murder of a man in a near-fatal shooting from December 2022 in the area of Church Street and Front Street East.
Police added a firearm was recovered.
"Additionally, he was wanted for unauthorized possession of a firearm, possession of a firearm contrary to a prohibition order, and failure to comply with the release order," Price said.
Jackson will be facing another count of attempted murder and numerous additional firearms charges.
"There are weapons on the streets, and we need to always be aware of that, and these officers did an amazing job taking a very dangerous individual off our streets," Price said.
The Toronto Police Association (TPA) said in a post on X the officer is 30 years old and has been on the job for six years.
"Despite this event, our member is in good spirits and is well on his way to recovery," TPA said. "We are thankful he's going to be OK and we are proud of his efforts."
TPA President Jon Reid told the Star he became aware of the incident shortly after midnight and he and a fellow TPA director promptly visited the injured officer in hospital.
Reid said the incident could have been "so much worse" and he is glad the officer will "hopefully" be making a full recovery.
He said incidents like this could have a "deep rippling effect" on the injured, the other officers at the scene and family members, adding the association will also be providing mental health resources to all parties involved.
"The criminal element here in the city, obviously, is becoming far more brazen," Reid said when asked about the state of violence in the city.
In June, Toronto police reported there was a nearly 74 per cent increase in "shooting events" across the city in 2024 compared to the same point last year. This number included shootings resulting in injuries or deaths, as well as firearm discharges with no reported injuries.
However, the increase is partly due to 2023 being a statistic outlier after the city saw a significant drop in gun violence rates last year. A total of 168 people were killed or injured in a shooting last year - still a lot of people, but the lowest recorded in a single year since 2015 and down 40 per cent in five years.
Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw said in a statement that the officer's "diligence" and the bravery of all involved officers is "commendable."
Mayor Olivia Chow posted on X on Sunday morning wishing the officer a quick recovery.
"Last night, a Toronto police officer was shot while performing their duties. I wish the officer a quick recovery from their injuries and offer my full support. Violence is never acceptable," Chow said.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said in a statement he is "relieved to hear the officer is alive."
"This terrible shooting is a reminder of the risks our brave women and men in uniform face every day as they work to keep our communities safe, and why it's so important that we all have their backs," Ford said in a post on X. "I'm relieved to hear the officer is alive and receiving treatment and I'm confident those responsible for this terrible act will be held accountable."
CAPTION: Det. Sgt. Brandon Price gives an update to reporters on Sunday morning at 14 Division after an off-duty police officer was shot during an arrest late Saturday night in downtown Toronto.Toronto police investigate the scene of a shooting at King and Bathurst Streetsearly Sunday morning.

Subject: Police

Location: Canada

Title: Off-duty Toronto police officer shot downtown after spotting wanted man, assisting colleagues in foot chase

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 4, 2024

Document 166 of 500

Fords' helicopter hopes finally take off: Both brothers had long advocated for choppers to help police forces combat crime

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-helicopter-hopes-finally-take-off/docview/3087670309/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fords%27+helicopter+hopes+finally+take+off%3A+Both+brothers+had+long+advocated+for+choppers+to+help+police+forces+combat+crime&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 167 of 500

The fires of Jasper are far away, but the climate change risks aren't

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fires-jasper-are-far-away-climate-change-risks/docview/3087557778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+fires+of+Jasper+are+far+away%2C+but+the+climate+change+risks+aren%27t&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The wildfire ravaging Jasper is a monster of our own making: Editorial, July 26
The Star Editorial Board asked how much more of our unparalleled country are we willing to lose before we start taking climate chance seriously. The task before us is as monumental as it is necessary. This week, after Earth recorded its three hottest days ever, Secretary-General of the United Nations António Guterres issued a call to action on extreme heat. Included among the many important measures that he proposed was reducing fossil fuel production and consumption by 30 per cent over the next six years, while tripling renewables and ending deforestation. A fair and just transition for fossil fuel workers is imperative. The goal is to limit global warming to 1.5 C, though every fraction of a degree is important. With each fraction of warming comes more extreme heat, increasing the risk of wildfires like the heartbreaking one that swept through Jasper. The tragedy in Jasper is a warning that we cannot afford to let go unheeded. Canada must do everything in its power to urgently address the climate emergency.
Saverio Colasanto, Powell River, BC
Seeing another Canadian community burn up - Jasper this time, like Lytton or Fort MacMurray before that - is terrifying. You don't have to be a climate scientist to know that climate change is endangering our lives and the people and places we love. Just look at the news. Heat waves are not selective in what they kill.
As a rural person whose home is surrounded by trees in Ontario, none of this feels abstract at all. We've had an unnaturally rainy season this year, with torrential rain and tornado threats. I hope and pray that next year won't be our turn for a killer heat wave and drought.
It isn't just our wildlife in harm's way. Heat waves harm our most vulnerable including seniors, young children and people with pre-existing health conditions. It's not science fiction. Climate change is hurting us right now. The people and places we love are in grave danger. Ask the people of Jasper who have had to evacuate if you're wondering what this is like. Will you and your community be next?
There are things individuals can do, of course, to help slow down climate change, like walking and biking more and driving less or doing less air travel. My family, friends and I do all we can, but more than anything, we need regulations that hold corporations accountable for the fossil fuel pollution they generate. Polluters must be held accountable and pay for the damage they are causing.
Ish Theilheimer, Golden Lake, Ont.
I agree with the The Star's view that wildfires are a monster of our own making and that burning fossil fuels causes more fires and more destruction. Canadians know that oil is a big contributor to our GDP so this is quite a difficult concept for us. Those my age remember the worry that we would run out of oil and the uneasy feeling that we could not live without it. In 2024 that has really changed. We know the world has more oil than we can ever use and that we must use much, much less of it than we do today or we will suffer even more environmental damage. During Parliament's Standing Committee on Environment and Sustainable Development meeting in June, we learned from Ontario MP Adam van Koeverden that Canada's oil has the highest emissions in the world during production. This is even before considering the emissions from leaks of natural gas during distribution and the eventual burning. We must all take steps to reduce our use of gas, oil and propane. We must also reduce the amount we export. Canada must find new, sustainable ways to contribute to GDP in order to protect the future for our children and grandchildren.
Elizabeth Carswell, Sandford, Ont.
Along with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has instituted policies which put us at risk and damage our climate. While Smith has becalmed plans for multiple wind energy projects in Alberta, let's not forget that Ford cancelled 750 renewable energy projects here in Ontario. The use of fossil gas to generate electricity in Ontario has actually increased under Ford. This means additional carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. Ford's government also overruled the Ontario Energy Board's effort to reduce carbon emissions from new housing by legislating that we all must subsidize the connection of these homes to an Enbridge pipeline, rather than have them install a heat pump. Smith is not alone. Ford's policies might be coming from the same book.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
CAPTION: Parks Canada officials say they're making progress on battling the blazes in Jasper National Park.

Subject: Fossil fuels; Emissions; Climate change

Location: Canada

Title: The fires of Jasper are far away, but the climate change risks aren't

We can expect more wildfires: The wildfire ravaging Jasper is a monster of our own making: Editorial, July 26

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-can-expect-more-wildfires/docview/3087526095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+can+expect+more+wildfires%3A+The+wildfire+ravaging+Jasper+is+a+monster+of+our+own+making%3A+Editorial%2C+July+26&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The wildfire ravaging Jasper is a monster of our own making:
Editorial, July 26
The Star editorial board asked how much more of our unparalleled country are we willing to lose before we start taking climate chance seriously. The task before us is as monumental as it is necessary.
This week, after Earth recorded its three hottest days ever, Secretary-General of the United Nations António Guterres issued a call to action on extreme heat. Included among the many important measures that he proposed was reducing fossil fuel production and consumption by 30 per cent over the next six years, while tripling renewables and ending deforestation.
A fair and just transition for fossil fuel workers is imperative. The goal is to limit global warming to 1.5 C, though every fraction of a degree is important. With each fraction of warming comes more extreme heat, increasing the risk of wildfires like the heartbreaking one that swept through Jasper.
The tragedy in Jasper is a warning that we cannot afford to let go unheeded. Canada must do everything in its power to urgently address the climate emergency.
Saverio Colasanto, Powell River, B.C.
Seeing another Canadian community burn up - Jasper this time, like Lytton or Fort MacMurray before that - is terrifying. You don't have to be a climate scientist to know that climate change is endangering our lives and the people and places we love. Just look at the news. Heat waves are not selective in what they kill.
As a rural person whose home is surrounded by trees in Ontario, none of this feels abstract at all. We've had an unnaturally rainy season this year, with torrential rain and tornado threats. I hope and pray that next year won't be our turn for a killer heat wave and drought.
It isn't just our wildlife in harm's way. Heat waves harm our most vulnerable including seniors, young children and people with pre-existing health conditions. It's not science fiction. Climate change is hurting us right now. The people and places we love are in grave danger. Ask the people of Jasper who have had to evacuate if you're wondering what this is like. Will you and your community be next?
There are things individuals can do, of course, to help slow down climate change, like walking and biking more and driving less or doing less air travel. My family, friends and I do all we can, but more than anything, we need regulations that hold corporations accountable for the fossil fuel pollution they generate.
Polluters must be held accountable and pay for the damage they are causing.
Ish Theilheimer, Golden Lake, Ont.
I agree with the Star's view that wildfires are a monster of our own making and that burning fossil fuels causes more fires and more destruction. Canadians know that oil is a big contributor to our GDP so this is quite a difficult concept for us. Those my age remember the worry that we would run out of oil and the uneasy feeling that we could not live without it. In 2024 that has really changed. We know the world has more oil than we can ever use and that we must use much, much less of it than we do today or we will suffer even more environmental damage. During Parliament's Standing Committee on Environment and Sustainable Development meeting in June, we learned from Ontario MP Adam van Koeverden that Canada's oil has the highest emissions in the world during production. This is even before considering the emissions from leaks of natural gas during distribution and the eventual burning.
We must all take steps to reduce our use of gas, oil and propane. We must also reduce the amount we export. Canada must find new, sustainable ways to contribute to GDP in order to protect the future for our children and grandchildren.
Elizabeth Carswell, Sandford, Ont.
Along with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has instituted policies that put us at risk and damage our climate. While Smith has becalmed plans for multiple wind energy projects in Alberta, let's not forget that Ford cancelled 750 renewable energy projects here in Ontario.
The use of fossil gas to generate electricity in Ontario has actually increased under Ford. This means additional carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.
Ford's government also overruled the Ontario Energy Board's effort to reduce carbon emissions from new housing by legislating that we all must subsidize the connection of these homes to an Enbridge pipeline, rather than have them install a heat pump. Smith is not alone. Ford's policies might be coming from the same book.
Allan Baker, Scarborough

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, August 2 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Doug Ford's secrecy directive to Metrolinx, Summer McIntosh's Olympic gold rush and the growing number of unclaimed bodies in Ontario.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford's office ordered Metrolinx to keep quiet on the Crosstown LRT
The transit agency was on the verge of revealing the expected opening date of its long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT last year when the premier's office abruptly ordered it kept secret, the Star has learned. Ford's office tells Ben Spurr it didn't want the schedule released because it had doubts it would be met. But critics say Ford's response highlights an alarming lack of transparency around the $13 billion, problem-plagued project.
The fallout: Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster was forced to hold a now-infamous press conference where he told perplexed reporters that despite his promise to reveal a potential opening date by the end of summer, he had changed his mind - of his own volition.Word from Mike Schreiner: "We've spent a lot of money to build this and we as people of this province, as the citizens, as the taxpayers, deserve to know when we can use it," the Green Party Leader said.Background: The 19-kilometre LRT has been under construction since 2011, originally slated to open by 2020. But it's since been derailed by a parade of delays and legal disputes.Summer McIntosh wins her second gold and makes Olympic history
The teenaged Toronto swimming phenom is making so much history you'll need to start keeping a list. After dominating the 200-metre butterfly yesterday - scoring the second-fastest time ever and setting an Olympic record - McIntosh became the first Canadian woman to win two individual golds at a Summer Olympics. And she's still hungry for more. "It's been a pretty amazing Olympics for me," she tells Bruce Arthur. "But, yeah, I'm not done yet."
Family ties: The McIntosh family has been dominating competitive swimming for decades. Summer's mom, Jill, was an Olympian herself, finishing ninth in the 200-metre butterfly. Four decades later, her daughter would beat her time by more than 10 seconds.Gold rush: Canada continues to rake in the hardware. Take a look at Team Canada's growing medal count.What happens to the dead if no one claims them? It's a growing problem
The remains of thousands lie waiting in Ontario's cemeteries, in meagre graves identified not by name but by number. Their ranks are growing by the day, Maria Iqbal reports, and we're running out of places to bury them. Industry insiders say public funds for burying the unclaimed aren't keeping up with the soaring cost of land and operations, leading to hiked-up burial prices. Here's why so many go unclaimed.
By the numbers: From 2021 to 2023, 2,908 bodies went unclaimed in Ontario. The majority, about 2,500, were aged 50 and older.What's happening? In more than half the cases, the family of the deceased were located - but they refused to claim the body, usually citing financial restrictions, Ontario's chief coroner said.Price of death: It's little wonder - the cost of dying is skyrocketing across Canada, with the average cost of a burial ranging from $5,000 to $10,000. Here's why it's so expensive to die in Canada - and what you can do about it.WHAT ELSE
"I didn't do any of it." Now he says no one cares.Would tax breaks for Ontario developers really make housing more affordable? Document 170 of 500

Ford says no one cares about King's Counsel: Premier vowed to 'fix process' around patronage award

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-says-no-one-cares-about-kings-counsel/docview/3087189065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+says+no+one+cares+about+King%27s+Counsel%3A+Premier+vowed+to+%27fix+process%27+around+patronage+award&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You could call it the abdication of the King's Counsel patronage award for lawyers.
One year after promising "to fix the process" surrounding his controversial reinstatement of the ceremonial legal honorific, a defensive Premier Doug Ford conceded Thursday the scheme is effectively on the back burner.
"People don't give two hoots if they have K.C. behind their names," said Ford, who criticized the Star for pursuing "a nothing story."
"The average person, they're worried about health care that we're announcing today. They're very worried about affordability and the economy and crime," he said at Lakeridge Health Oshawa, where he announced there would eventually be a new hospital in Whitby.
"Someone who does work for the government, they get (a) K.C., they aren't getting any more money, they aren't doing anything, it's just something behind their name."
While the title indeed carries no special privileges aside from allowing lawyers to put the initials "K.C." next to their name and wear silk robes to court, the Progressive Conservatives' quiet reviving of the moniker on the eve of Canada Day last year sparked an outcry.
That's because former Liberal premier David Peterson eliminated what was then known as the Queen's Counsel in 1985 because previous Tory governments had "corrupted" the designation.
Peterson, now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company, said past PC premiers had treated it as a patronage award for loyalists.
"It lost all meaning, and so any principled approach would have been to stop them," the former Grit premier said last July.
"I just think it's unbelievable that they would reinstitute this," said Peterson, a K.C. himself dating back to the early 1980s.
On the Friday afternoon before last year's July 1 long weekend, Attorney General Doug Downey announced 91 lawyers - including Tory ministers, MPPs, PC donors and party loyalists - were receiving the award. There were no calls for nomination, selection criteria or information about the appointment process prior to Downey's June 30 press release.
But no new K.C. recipients have been named in the 13 months since.
Stung by the fallout, Ford pledged last July "to implement" a nomination protocol.
"Should there have been probably ... another process, yes," the premier said at the time.
However, there is little evidence anything has happened behind the scenes to rectify that.
"There are no updates at this time," Downey's office said in a terse statement Tuesday.
The Star's Jacques Gallant reported last summer that the elected head of the Law Society at the time, Jacqueline Horvat, declined the government's offer of a K.C. due to the lack of selection criteria. However, several Law Society board members accepted the honorific.
Last November, the Advocates' Society, a national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, wrote Downey to say the main criterion for the designation should be professional excellence, not political connections.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford said Thursday that the scheme to reform the awarding of the ceremonial legal honorific is effectively on the back burner.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 171 of 500

Ontario won't adopt new federal riding boundaries: Ford confirms split from past practices for election maps

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-wont-adopt-new-federal-riding-boundaries/docview/3087186999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+won%27t+adopt+new+federal+riding+boundaries%3A+Ford+confirms+split+from+past+practices+for+election+maps&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says Ontario is breaking with tradition and not adopting federal electoral boundary changes for the next provincial election, meaning there will be 124 ridings contested.
"No," said Ford on Thursday when asked whether Queen's Park would follow Ottawa's lead and increase the number of constituencies in the province.
His surprise move also means Toronto city council boundaries won't be redrawn either, so there will remain 25 councillors - instead of being cut to 24 - after the 2026 municipal election.
"Why change something that works? Just because the feds want to do it, jerry-rig the ridings," said Ford, alleging, without providing evidence, that the independent federal boundary commission gerrymanders electoral districts.
"It's no secret people do that, governments do that, I'm not doing that," he told reporters at an unrelated press conference at Lakeridge Health Oshawa.
"The less politicians, the better it is," said the premier, whose cabinet has 36 ministers, by far the largest in Ontario history. "I'm going to leave the boundaries alone and people will decide if they want to move forward with our government on prosperity and health care and on the economy - or if they want to go backwards and look for the other guys. They'll have that choice."
Ford's unexpected comments followed a Star report this week that Elections Ontario was awaiting instructions from the legislature on how to proceed on redistribution.
The uncertainty came against the backdrop of Ford considering an early election call next spring, which would cost taxpayers $150 million, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
With the exception of Northern Ontario, the province's current electoral ridings are based on the Elections Canada map.
Federal boundaries will change for an election expected in October 2025, jumping from 338 electoral districts to 343 across Canada.
That includes boosting Ontario's 121 federal ridings to 122 with the addition of three seats in Brampton, Durham and Milton and the elimination of one in Toronto and another in the north due to demographic changes.
Polls suggest that provincial redistribution would have favoured Ford's Tories, who won 83 of 124 seats in the 2022 election.
The premier's decree clears the air for Elections Ontario, which had warned "Ontario does not have legislation in place for a scheduled review of electoral districts and boundaries."
"Provincial electoral district boundaries in Ontario will therefore remain unchanged until new legislation has passed," the independent arm's length agency told the Star last week.
"The legislature has a choice in how to respond to the new federal electoral boundaries. It could, for example, establish its own commission to determine what the new electoral boundaries would be, it could adopt the new federal electoral boundaries in part or in their entirety, or it could choose to retain the existing 124 electoral boundaries."
Under Ontario's 2017 Representation Act, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals, there are now "111 southern electoral districts whose names and boundaries are identical to those of the federal electoral districts."
Wynne - after a report from the non-partisan Far North Electoral Boundaries Commission - kept 13 Northern Ontario ridings instead of the 10 federal constituencies at the time in order to improve Indigenous and northern representation.
Ford's intervention ensures that Toronto will not lose the constituency of Don Valley East at Queen's Park. Federally, it's being absorbed into Don Valley West and Don Valley North, with the rest merged with Scarborough Centre to create a renamed Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East.
It also means that city council boundaries won't be amended for the 2026 municipal election, sparing Ward 16 (Don Valley East) represented by Coun. Jon Burnside.
But fast-growing Brampton, Durham and Milton won't get the additional provincial representation they will receive federally.
The federal riding of Brampton North has been divided into Brampton North-Caledon, including portions of Dufferin Caledon, and Brampton-Chinguacousy Park, which takes part of Brampton Centre.
And the federal constituency of Durham has been split into two new ridings called York-Durham and Bowmanville-Oshawa North, while Milton has been divvied up into Burlington North-Milton West and Milton East-Halton Hills South, including a piece of Wellington-Halton Hills.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford rejected the new federal electoral map that adds one riding to the GTA and removes one from the city.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Provincial elections; Election districts; Local elections; Boundaries; Political representation; Federal elections-Canada

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Title: Ontario won't adopt new federal riding boundaries:   Ford confirms split from past practices for election maps

Document 172 of 500

Ford's office stopped release of LRT opening date: Metrolinx was told not to share estimate for Crosstown line, internal emails show

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-office-stopped-release-lrt-opening-date/docview/3087186984/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+office+stopped+release+of+LRT+opening+date%3A+Metrolinx+was+told+not+to+share+estimate+for+Crosstown+line%2C+internal+emails+show&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Metrolinx was on the verge of revealing the expected opening date of the long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown last year, but Premier Doug Ford's office ordered it be kept secret, the Star has learned.
According to emails obtained through a freedom of information request, the provincial transit agency supported releasing the estimated completion date in summer 2023, and was actively planning to do so when Ford's office shut it down.
Instead, later that month, Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster held a now-infamous press conference in which he declared he had a good idea of when the line would be done, but had decided not to announce it. The agency has yet to publish a timeline for when the LRT line will carry its first passengers.
Ford's office says the government didn't want to release the schedule because it had doubts it would be met. But opposition critics said the emails are evidence of a troubling lack of transparency around the $13-billion, problem-plagued light rail project, and accused the Ford government of exerting improper influence over Metrolinx, the arm's-length agency that oversees transit in the GTA.
The premier's office "is refusing to be honest with the people of Ontario," said Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
"We've spent a lot of money to build this, and we as people of this province, as the citizens, as the taxpayers, deserve to know when we can use it," he argued, adding that Metrolinx has a responsibility to "communicate in an honest and transparent way with the people of Ontario, and the premier is clearly interfering in that, I'm assuming for political reasons."
New Democrat MPP Jill Andrew said constituents in her Toronto-St. Paul's riding who have been dealing with Crosstown construction for more than a decade have "been asking for a date" but not getting the information they need.
"So to learn that the premier consciously was suppressing information from the public about this transit line is pretty disappointing," she said.
In a statement, Ford's spokesperson Grace Lee said the premier's office has "had very little confidence in the opening dates previously provided to the government." She said none of the previous schedules has turned out to be accurate, including the one referenced in the emails. (The Star couldn't confirm the accuracy of the date, because it's redacted in the documents released through the access request.)
"We remain committed to being open and transparent about the issues that have been the cause of the delays of this project and will continue to do so. We will inform the public when we are absolutely confident about an opening date," Lee said.
The 19-kilometre Crosstown has been under construction since 2011. It was originally supposed to open by 2020, but has been stymied by multiple delays and legal disputes between Metrolinx and Crosslinx Transit Solutions (CTS), the consortium building the line.
The project's completion appeared to be within sight last August, when Verster told media that by the end of that summer he would announce a "range of dates" for when it could open.
The emails show that on Aug. 26, 2023, Metrolinx's vice-president for strategic communications emailed staff in the agency and the Ministry of Transportation, seeking approval for a statement that included the "likely" opening date. The vice-president said the statement would be released under Verster's name the following week.
But it wasn't, and on Sept. 1, Michael Beaton, chief of staff to then transportation minister Caroline Mulroney, wrote to Verster's chief of staff, Steven Hobbs, and reported that earlier that day the premier's office had "given direction to continue to hold communicating the public opening date." Beaton wrote that the order was "consistent with" direction Ford's deputy chief of staff Ivana Yelich had given the previous winter.
According to the email, Ford's office was concerned that announcing the date could cause headaches if CTS was unable to meet it. But Beaton said his "team" disagreed, and wanted to publicize the date to "hold CTS accountable." Continuing to withhold the new schedule "is not the preference" of the ministry or Mulroney's office, he wrote.
Hobbs wrote back, agreeing that releasing the new schedule, which had been independently assessed by two consultant firms, Kroll and Mott MacDonald, would put down "a marker by which we measure CTS's success." He was also worried that failing to disclose a date would open Metrolinx to questions about why it still didn't have even a rough idea of when the line could open.
Ford shuffled his cabinet on Sept. 4, replacing Mulroney with Prabmeet Sarkaria. Beaton left the government that month.
On Sept. 27, Verster delivered his highly anticipated update on the Crosstown, and told perplexed reporters that despite his promise to reveal a potential opening date by the end of summer, he had changed his mind. The CEO, who last year made a salary of almost $840,000, described the reversal as his decision, explaining that he believed there were still too many technical issues and defects being discovered on the line.
Although media reports have suggested the LRT could be ready by the end of this year, the agency and premier's office wouldn't confirm that Thursday, or give a date for when the completion will be announced.
On Thursday, Sarkaria's press secretary also denied Beaton's assertions in the emails that the ministry had been on board with releasing the date.
"Neither the minister nor the minister's office supported any proposal to repeatedly release an inaccurate opening date. As we've said before, we will inform the public once we have an opening schedule we're confident in," Dakota Brasier said in a statement.
Asked about the emails and Verster's announcement, Metrolinx spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks reiterated the agency's commitment to announcing the date three months before the line is ready, and promised to share updates until that time.
"As stated previously by our CEO Phil Verster, it is important that we have confidence that all risks and issues affecting the safety and reliability of the (Crosstown) have been rectified before the project opens," she said.
With files from Robert Benzie

Document 173 of 500

Ford government to break with tradition and not adopt federal boundary changes for next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-break-with-tradition-not-adopt/docview/3087402215/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+break+with+tradition+and+not+adopt+federal+boundary+changes+for+next+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says Ontario is breaking with tradition and not adopting federal electoral boundary changes for the next provincial election, meaning there will be 124 ridings contested.
"No," said Ford on Thursday when asked whether Queen's Park would follow Ottawa's lead and increase the number of constituencies in the province.
His surprise move also means Toronto city council boundaries won't be redrawn either, so there will remain 25 councillors - instead of being cut to 24 - after the 2026 municipal election.
"Why change something that works? Just because the feds want to do it, jerry-rig the ridings," said Ford, alleging, without providing evidence, that the independent federal boundary commission gerrymanders electoral districts.
"It's no secret people do that, governments do that, I'm not doing that," he told reporters at an unrelated press conference at Lakeridge Health Oshawa.
"The less politicians, the better it is," said the premier, whose cabinet has 36 ministers, by far the largest in Ontario history.
"I'm going to leave the boundaries alone and people will decide if they want to move forward with our government on prosperity and health care and on the economy - or if they want to go backwards and look for the other guys. They'll have that choice."
Ford's unexpected comments followed a Star report earlier this week that Elections Ontario was awaiting instructions from the legislature on how to proceed on redistribution.
The uncertainty came against the backdrop of Ford considering an early election call next spring, which would cost taxpayers $150 million, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
With the exception of northern Ontario, the province's electoral ridings are based on the Elections Canada map.
Federal boundaries will change for an election expected in October 2025, jumping from 338 electoral districts to 343 across Canada.
That includes boosting Ontario's 121 federal ridings to 122 with the addition of three seats in Brampton, Durham and Milton and the elimination of one in Toronto and another in the north due to demographic changes.
Polls suggest that provincial redistribution would have favoured Ford's Tories, who won 83 of 124 seats in the 2022 election.
The premier's decree clears the air for Elections Ontario, which had warned "Ontario does not have legislation in place for a scheduled review of electoral districts and boundaries."
"Provincial electoral district boundaries in Ontario will therefore remain unchanged until new legislation has passed," the independent arm's length agency told the Star last week.
"The legislature has a choice in how to respond to the new federal electoral boundaries. It could, for example, establish its own commission to determine what the new electoral boundaries would be, it could adopt the new federal electoral boundaries in part or in their entirety, or it could choose to retain the existing 124 electoral boundaries."
Under Ontario's 2017 Representation Act, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals, there are now "111 southern electoral districts whose names and boundaries are identical to those of the federal electoral districts."
Wynne - after a report from the non-partisan Far North Electoral Boundaries Commission - kept 13 northern Ontario ridings instead of the 10 federal constituencies at the time in order to improve Indigenous and northern representation.
There were 124 ridings up for grabs in the 2018 and 2022 elections, up from 107 in 2014, 2011 and 2007.
Ford's intervention ensures that Toronto will not lose the constituency of Don Valley East at Queen's Park. Federally, it's being absorbed into Don Valley West and Don Valley North with the rest merged with Scarborough Centre to create a renamed Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East.
It also means that city council boundaries won't be amended for the 2026 municipal election, sparing Ward 16 (Don Valley East) represented by Coun. Jon Burnside.
But fast-growing Brampton, Durham and Milton won't get the additional provincial representation they will receive federally.
The federal riding of Brampton North has been divided into Brampton North-Caledon, including portions of Dufferin Caledon, and Brampton-Chinguacousy Park, which takes part of Brampton Centre.
And the federal constituency of Durham has been split into two new ridings called York-Durham and Bowmanville-Oshawa North while Milton has been divvied up into Burlington North-Milton West and Milton East-Halton Hills South, including a piece of Wellington-Halton Hills.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford takes speaks during a press conference about police helicopter funding in Mississauga on July 29, 2024.

Document 174 of 500

Doug Ford promised to end the King's Counsel controversy. Now he says no one cares

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promised-end-kings-counsel-controversy/docview/3087401858/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promised+to+end+the+King%27s+Counsel+controversy.+Now+he+says+no+one+cares&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You could call it the abdication of the King's Counsel patronage award for lawyers.
One year after promising "to fix the process" surrounding his controversial reinstatement of the ceremonial legal honorific, a defensive Premier Doug Ford conceded Thursday the scheme is effectively on the backburner.
"People don't give two hoots if they have K.C. behind their names," said Ford, who criticized the Star for pursuing "a nothing story."
"The average person, they're worried about health care that we're announcing today. They're very worried about affordability and the economy and crime," he said at Lakeridge Health Oshawa, where he announced there would eventually be a new hospital in Whitby.
"Someone who does work for the government, they get (a) K.C., they aren't getting any more money, they aren't doing anything, it's just something behind their name."
While the title indeed carries no special privileges aside from allowing lawyers to put the initials "K.C." next to their name and wear silk robes to court, the Progressive Conservatives' quiet reviving of the moniker on the eve of Canada Day last year sparked an outcry.
That's because former Liberal premier David Peterson eliminated what was then known as the Queen's Counsel in 1985 because previous Tory governments had "corrupted" the designation.
Peterson, now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company, said past PC premiers had treated it as a patronage award for loyalists.
"It lost all meaning, and so any principled approach would have been to stop them," the former Grit premier said last July.
"I just think it's unbelievable that they would reinstitute this," said Peterson, a K.C. himself dating back to the early 1980s.
On the Friday afternoon before last year's July 1 long weekend, Attorney General Doug Downey announced 91 lawyers - including Tory ministers, MPPs, PC donors and party loyalists - were receiving the award.
There were no calls for nomination, selection criteria or information about the appointment process prior to Downey's June 30 press release.
But no new K.C. recipients have been named in the 13 months since.
Stung by the fallout, Ford pledged last July "to implement" a nomination protocol.
"Should there have been probably ... another process, yes," the premier said at the time.
However, there is little evidence anything has happened behind the scenes to rectify that.
"There are no updates at this time," Downey's office said in a terse statement Tuesday.
The Star's Jacques Gallant reported last summer that the elected head of the Law Society at the time, Jacqueline Horvat, declined the government's offer of a K.C. due to the lack of selection criteria. However, several Law Society board members accepted the honorific.
Last November, the Advocates' Society, a national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, wrote Downey to say the main criterion for the designation should be professional excellence, not political connections.
"If the designation is permitted to fall into such disrepute again, public confidence in the legal profession will be undermined," wrote then-president Dominique Hussey, chief executive officer of the prestigious law firm Bennett Jones.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Nominations; Patronage; Attorneys

Title: Doug Ford promised to end the King's Counsel controversy. Now he says no one cares
