Document 175 of 500

Premier Doug Ford's office directed Metrolinx to keep Crosstown opening date secret, emails show

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premier-doug-fords-office-directed-metrolinx-keep/docview/3087186422/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Doug+Ford%27s+office+directed+Metrolinx+to+keep+Crosstown+opening+date+secret%2C+emails+show&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Metrolinx was on the verge of revealing the expected opening date of the long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown last year, but Premier Doug Ford's office ordered it be kept secret, the Star has learned.
According to emails obtained through a freedom of information request, the provincial transit agency supported releasing the estimated completion date in the summer of 2023, and was actively planning to do so when Ford's office shut it down.
Instead, later that month Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster held a now-infamous press conference in which he declared he had a good idea of when the line would be done, but had decided not to announce it. The agency has yet to publish a timeline for when the LRT will take its first passengers.
Ford's office says the government didn't want to release the schedule because it had doubts it would be met.
But opposition critics said the emails are evidence of a troubling lack of transparency around the $13-billion, problem-plagued light rail project, and accused the Ford government of exerting improper influence over Metrolinx, the arms-length agency that oversees transit in the GTA.
The premier's office "is refusing to be honest with the people of Ontario," said Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
"We've spent a lot of money to build this and we as people of this province, as the citizens, as the taxpayers, deserve to know when we can use it," he argued, adding that Metrolinx has a responsibility to "communicate in an honest and transparent way with the people of Ontario, and the premier is clearly interfering in that, I'm assuming for political reasons."
NDP MPP Jill Andrew said constituents in her Toronto-St. Paul's riding who have been dealing with Crosstown construction for more than a decade have "been asking for a date" but not getting the information they need.
"So to learn that the premier consciously was suppressing information from the public about this transit line is pretty disappointing," she said.
In a statement, Ford's spokesperson Grace Lee said the premier's office has "had very little confidence in the opening dates previously provided to the government." She said none of the previous schedules has turned out to be accurate, including the one referenced in the emails. (The Star couldn't confirm the accuracy of the date, because it's redacted in the documents released through the access request.)
"We remain committed to being open and transparent about the issues that have been the cause of the delays of this project and will continue to do so. We will inform the public when we are absolutely confident about an opening date," Lee said.
The 19-kilometre Crosstown has been under construction since 2011. It was originally supposed to open by 2020, but has been stymied by multiple delays and legal disputes between Metrolinx and Crosslinx Transit Solutions (CTS), the consortium building the line.
The project's completion appeared to be within sight last August, when Verster told media that by the end of that summer he would announce a "range of dates" for when it could open.
The emails show that on Aug. 26, 2023, Metrolinx's vice-president for strategic communications emailed staff in the agency and Ministry of Transportation, seeking approval for a statement that included the "likely" opening date. The vice-president said the statement would be released under Verster's name the following week.
But it wasn't, and on Sept. 1, Michael Beaton, chief of staff to then-transportation minister Caroline Mulroney, wrote to Verster's chief of staff, Steven Hobbs, and reported that earlier that day the premier's office had "given direction to continue to hold communicating the public opening date." Beaton wrote that the order was "consistent with" direction Ford's deputy chief of staff Ivana Yelich had given the previous winter.
According to the email, Ford's office was concerned that announcing the date could cause headaches if CTS was unable to meet it. But Beaton said his "team" disagreed, and wanted to publicize the date to "hold CTS accountable." Continuing to withhold the new schedule "is not the preference" of the ministry or Mulroney's office, he wrote.
Hobbs wrote back, agreeing that releasing the new schedule, which had been independently assessed by two consultant firms, Kroll and Mott MacDonald, would put down "a marker by which we measure CTS' success." He was also worried that failing to disclose a date would open Metrolinx to questions about why it still didn't have even a rough idea of when the line could open.
Ford shuffled his cabinet on Sept. 4, replacing Mulroney with Prabmeet Sarkaria. Beaton left the government that month.
On Sept. 27, Verster delivered his highly anticipated update on the Crosstown, and told perplexed reporters that despite his promise to reveal a potential opening date by the end of summer, he had changed his mind. The CEO, who last year made a salary of almost $840,000, described the reversal as his decision, explaining that he believed there were still too many technical issues and defects being discovered on the line.
Although media reports have suggested the LRT could be ready by the end of this year, the agency and premier's office wouldn't confirm that Thursday, or give a date for when the completion will be announced.
On Thursday, Sarkaria's press secretary also denied Beaton's assertions in the emails that the ministry had been on board with releasing the date.
"Neither the minister nor the minister's office supported any proposal to repeatedly release an inaccurate opening date. As we've said before, we will inform the public once we have an opening schedule we're confident in," Dakota Brasier said in a statement.
Asked about the emails and Verster's announcement, Metrolinx spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks reiterated the agency's commitment to announcing the date three months before the line is ready, and promised to share updates until that time.
"As stated previously by our CEO Phil Verster, it is important that we have confidence that all risks and issues affecting the safety and reliability of the (Crosstown) have been rectified before the project opens," she said.

We will inform the public when we are absolutely confident about an opening date," said Premier Doug Ford's spokesperson Grace Lee.On Sept. 1, Michael Beaton, chief of staff to then-transportation minister Caroline Mulroney, pictured in this file photo, wrote to Metrolinx CEO's chief of staff that continuing to withhold the new schedule "is not the preference" of the ministry or Mulroney's office.Emails obtained by the Star through a freedom of information request show Premier Doug Ford's office ordered Metrolinx to withhold the scheduled opening date for the Crosstown LRT.

Document 176 of 500

They - and their hair - were massive in the '80s. On a new tour opening for Billy Idol, can these Toronto rockers make a comeback?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/they-their-hair-were-massive-80s-on-new-tour/docview/3086940544/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=They+-+and+their+hair+-+were+massive+in+the+%2780s.+On+a+new+tour+opening+for+Billy+Idol%2C+can+these+Toronto+rockers+make+a+comeback%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You have your Halley's Comet. You have your solar eclipses. But an even rarer phenomenon - a national tour pairing two rock acts who played together 40 years ago - will be visible in Toronto when Billy Idol and Platinum Blonde perform at Scotiabank Arena on August 9.
Back in January 1984, when music videos reigned supreme, the sneering, spiky British punk Billy Idol, then riding high on his Rebel Yell tour, invited Toronto's ascendant glam rockers Platinum Blonde to open for him in Ontario, including a Massey Hall appearance.
Four decades later, with many hits, kilometres and history behind them, the bands began a cross-Canada tour in Vancouver on July 30.
So how does Platinum Blonde bassist and singer Mark Holmes feel about this full circle moment?
"When (promoter) Live Nation talked to me about it, they wanted to hearken back to those times, because we were both fresh and green and it was exciting," said Holmes, who has piloted the fashionable group since its inception in 1979. "And now, after all this time, we get to do it again. It's almost like a gift."
In '84, he recalled, "we were really catching on. It was more of a doubleheader (billing). We were both on the rise, pretty much neck and neck."
He's not kidding: Canadians quickly took to the Blondes, which in addition to Holmes - a native of Mansfield, England - featured Sergio Galli on guitar and Chris Steffler on drums. Aided by colourful videos and punchy, hook-laden songs such as "Not in Love" and "Doesn't Really Matter," they ruled the airwaves.
Their debut album, "Standing in the Dark," released in early '84, ended up selling several hundred thousand copies. A free concert at Nathan Phillips Square on May 25 of that same year, expected to draw 2,500 people, attracted a crowd estimated at between 25,000 to 40,000, resulting in more than 100 people being treated for various ailments and front-page coverage in the Toronto Star.
Guitarist Kenny MacLean joined the band a year later, in time for their sophomore album, "Alien Shores," which yielded the No. 1 single "Crying Over You" and sold more than 500,000 copies.
"We had teenage girls screaming like the Beatles, and every time we stopped a song, we had to cover our ears, they were so loud," Holmes recalled during a recent interview conducted on his 64th birthday.
"Fans loved us. The industry really hated us. We were rock stars: crazy kids doing crazy stuff, getting drunk and getting into fights."
They had no handbook, so Holmes and crew played up their celebrity stature.
"You feel like it's your path," he said. "Because everything is happening for the first time, you don't know what's going to be happening the next time."
Some misunderstanding
Despite the hysteria, Holmes maintained that Platinum Blonde may have been one of the world's most misunderstood bands.
"Did anybody know what we were singing about?" he asked rhetorically. "We were singing about fallout from nuclear war. We were singing about acid rain. We were singing about f--ked-up psychological things, like 'Not in Love,' which is about denial.
"We weren't singing, 'I love you, baby,' and I'm f--king proud about that. I wanted to be remembered for being socially conscious."
"Doesn't Really Matter," for example, was inspired by the time Holmes was travelling to an airport but didn't make it because the Irish Republican Army had blown up an entrance to the highway.
"That was our first hit, and all these teenage girls were screaming at us," he said. "Listen to that first record, it's dark. 'Standing in the Dark' - that's how I'd like to be remembered.
"Will people remember us that way? No, because people still remember the hair."
As much as fans may remember those magnificent, massive coifs, they haven't forgotten the music: Sony Music Canada recently announced that "Alien Shores" has sold 860,000 units.
Platinum Blonde's winning streak ended with 1987's "Contact," an album that reimagined the group as a funk band (with new drummer Sascha Tukatsch) - a direction that Holmes conceded may have been a bit premature in the wake of the "Alien Shores" triumph.
"The '80s were a changing, volatile time," Holmes admitted. "Holy cow: every six months there was a new sound, a new vibe." When the supergroup Power Station released their debut album with the of-the-moment gated drum sound, Platinum Blonde enlisted their producer Bernard Edwards to work on "Contact."
"That music was so great and of course we got to play with a real funk-rock legend," Holmes said.
"But this is what I learned: when you're in a band, you've heard your own songs and played them so much and they (may not even be) released yet, but you find yourself musically moving on to different things.
"You have to slow yourself down. You've got to think, 'If we want to take our fans with us, we've got to bring this in a little bit more with our classic sound.'
"During that 'Contact' record, there were a lot of influences, because now, all of a sudden, we're big stars - big money, everybody's got things on the line. 'Contact' put wedges between band members."
Holmes admitted that drugs also played a part in the internal strife.
"It absolutely was in the band," he said. "It was a coke-fueled industry, and I can say that now without having people say to me, 'Oh, you'll never work again.'"
A new beginning
Though they didn't tour "Contact," they regrouped as the Blondes for the album "Yeah Yeah Yeah," but after 1989 didn't play together live for 10 years. They reconvened for the occasional gig between 2000 and 2002, and then Holmes put Platinum Blonde on hold to co-found Toronto's Mod Club, where he was also known as DJ MRK.
While he was spinning, his old bandmate Kenny MacLean kept in touch.
"He would call me when I had found a lot of success as a DJ and with remixes," Holmes recalled. "That's where my life was: I had no time to talk or rehearse with the band. I'm trying to stay atop of the DJ world. You talk about pressure? That's pressure: always being the guy who is trying to come up with the new thing. It's an interesting world and I became fully involved in it.
"One day, I just decided to soften up. Kenny had called me every six months to say, 'Let's write some songs and do a tour with the band.' I was leading a double life: my DJ fans didn't know I was in Platinum Blonde. All they knew was MRK is this big English DJ. They had no clue of any of my past.
"I didn't want that knowledge to cross over to a band, because I don't know what that would have done to my rep. All my fans were in their early 20s."
But Holmes finally relented and agreed to reunite Platinum Blonde on stage, joining MacLean at the Mod Club during the release party for one of the musician's solo albums to sing an old hit or two.
"You should have seen his face," Holmes said. "He was so happy."
That was on Nov. 22, 2008 - the last time Holmes saw MacLean alive.
"Kenny was so happy, and I was so happy to see him so happy. I was invigorated, and I thought, we're going to do this! This is going to be awesome! We had the after-party upstairs in what we call the Orange Room and then he went home that night.
"When he was brushing his teeth, it was over." On Nov. 24, MacLean was found dead of natural causes.
Holmes said he decided to honour MacLean's memory by reuniting with Sergio Galli and recording 2012's "Now & Never."
"I couldn't say that was the end," said Holmes. "We did a record, released it and toured it, and it was a success. Every moment I played, Kenny was with us. It's still heartbreaking."
After having his band feted at Canada's Walk of Fame last September, Holmes - also co-owner of the West End club Jean Darlene Piano Room - said Platinum Blonde is back and prepping new music, some of which will be heard at Scotiabank Arena.
They're also yearning for another hit.
"When you're a creative artist and all you do is write and produce, that never goes away," he said. "Even with Platinum Blonde, we'll never lose that feeling of wanting to put out another hit - and, who knows, maybe one day you get a solid hit.
In 2010, Platinum Blonde got a boost when Toronto electro act Crystal Castles, with help from the Cure's Robert Smith, covered "Not in Love." "When we released our song 'Beautiful,'" Holmes said, "we got hits in places we'd never had before. That was in 2012.
"So, it's always there, if you want it. The key component to being a functioning, creative musician and songwriter is to never ever, under any circumstances, grow up. You grow up, your art is done. It's over."

Document 177 of 500

Forced rehab wrong path, Jones says: Health minister takes issue with Poilievre after he said he was 'open' to involuntary treatment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/forced-rehab-wrong-path-jones-says/docview/3086771751/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Forced+rehab+wrong+path%2C+Jones+says%3A+Health+minister+takes+issue+with+Poilievre+after+he+said+he+was+%27open%27+to+involuntary+treatment&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones is taking issue with federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's talk of forcing people with drug addictions into treatment as the country grapples with a deadly opioid crisis.
In another sign of tensions between the provincial and federal Tories, with polls suggesting Poilievre is poised to become Canada's next prime minister, Jones said mandatory rehab is the wrong path.
"I have concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes that we want," Jones said Wednesday at Mount Sinai Hospital. "But having said that, when we see the opportunity and the need for intervention, and people are willing to take on those treatments to make a difference, that's when we can show them our government is committed."
Jones had been asked about recent remarks from Poilievre, who mulled the concept of involuntary treatment last week during a campaign-style stop in London, Ont.
"I don't know if you can take someone off the street that has not committed a prison offence and successfully rehabilitate them. If we can, I'm open to it, but I'd need to see more evidence at this point," he said.
"I need to study it more. I need to understand how it would work. I want everybody who's on drugs to be in treatment and rehab to get off drugs. What I haven't been able to figure out is, if someone doesn't want to be rehabilitated, can you require them to be?"
Poilievre's remarks were made at a news conference in a public park where he criticized a safe opioids supply program at a health centre a few blocks away.
He blamed safe supply strategies for fuelling the addictions crisis and said a Conservative federal government would cut any federal funding for them.
Jones said the province, where Premier Doug Ford has expressed reservations about safe consumption sites, has a new addictions plan coming in which "treatment is a very large portion."
While Poilievre has railed against safe supply programs as "drug dens," proponents say providing users with prescription opioids free of dangerous and potent add-ons in street opioids reduces overdose deaths. But in London, for example, police said some of the safe supply is being diverted to the streets.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government froze approvals of supervised drug consumption sites last fall to conduct a review after 44-year-old mother of two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was stuck and killed by a stray bullet.
She was walking near Queen Street and Carlaw Avenue in east end Toronto when she was caught in the crossfire of a shooting near a consumption site.
The premier has made no secret of his differences with Poilievre.
In a thinly veiled shot at the federal Conservative leader last month, Ford signalled he will "fight like a pit bull" for Ontario's interests if Ottawa acts against them after the next federal election expected in fall 2025.
The wording was telling because Ford has worked closely with the Trudeau government on issues such as health care and providing billions of dollars in joint support for electric vehicle battery plants to create next-generation auto industry jobs.
Sources have told the Star's Robert Benzie that Ford is considering an early election next year to get to the polls before the expected federal vote, fearing a Poilievre government would cut transfer payments to the provinces and cut the cord on Trudeau's electric vehicle strategy.
Ford is also concerned some of his MPPs and cabinet ministers could jump ship to Poilievre's team, following in the footsteps of Parm Gill, the minister of red tape reduction who resigned this year to run federally.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 178 of 500

Reaching for the rock: They - and their hair - were huge in the '80s. Now, with an upcoming arena show headlined by Billy Idol, can Toronto's Platinum Blonde make a comeback?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reaching-rock/docview/3086771455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reaching+for+the+rock%3A+They+-+and+their+hair+-+were+huge+in+the+%2780s.+Now%2C+with+an+upcoming+arena+show+headlined+by+Billy+Idol%2C+can+Toronto%27s+Platinum+Blonde+make+a+comeback%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You have your Halley's Comet. You have your solar eclipses. But an even rarer phenomenon - a national tour pairing two rock acts who played together 40 years ago - will be visible in Toronto when Billy Idol and Platinum Blonde perform at Scotiabank Arena on Aug. 9.
Back in January 1984, when music videos reigned supreme, the sneering, spiky British punk Billy Idol, then riding high on his Rebel Yell tour, invited Toronto's ascendant glam rockers Platinum Blonde to open for him in Ontario, including a Massey Hall appearance.
Four decades later, with many hits, kilometres and history behind them, the bands began a cross-Canada tour in Vancouver on Tuesday.
So how does Platinum Blonde bassist and singer Mark Holmes feel about this full circle moment?
"When (promoter) Live Nation talked to me about it, they wanted to hearken back to those times, because we were both fresh and green and it was exciting," said Holmes, who has piloted the fashionable group since its inception in 1979. "And now, after all this time, we get to do it again. It's almost like a gift."
In '84, he recalled, "we were really catching on. It was more of a doubleheader (billing). We were both on the rise, pretty much neck and neck."
He's not kidding: Canadians quickly took to the Blondes, which in addition to Holmes - a native of Mansfield, England - featured Sergio Galli on guitar and Chris Steffler on drums. Aided by colourful videos and punchy, hook-laden songs such as "Not in Love" and "Doesn't Really Matter," they ruled the airwaves.
Their debut album, "Standing in the Dark," released in early '84, ended up selling several hundred thousand copies. A free concert at Nathan Phillips Square on May 25 of that same year, expected to draw 2,500 people, attracted a crowd estimated at between 25,000 and 40,000, resulting in more than 100 people being treated for various ailments and front-page coverage in the Toronto Star.
Guitarist Kenny MacLean joined the band a year later, in time for their sophomore album, "Alien Shores," which yielded the No. 1 single "Crying Over You" and sold more than 500,000 copies.
"We had teenage girls screaming like the Beatles, and every time we stopped a song we had to cover our ears, they were so loud," Holmes recalled during a recent interview conducted on his 64th birthday.
"Fans loved us. The industry really hated us. We were rock stars: crazy kids doing crazy stuff, getting drunk and getting into fights."
They had no handbook, so Holmes and crew played up their celebrity stature.
"You feel like it's your path," he said. "Because everything is happening for the first time, you don't know what's going to be happening the next time."
Some misunderstanding
Despite the hysteria, Holmes maintained that Platinum Blonde may have been one of the world's most misunderstood bands.
"Did anybody know what we were singing about?" he asked rhetorically. "We were singing about fallout from nuclear war. We were singing about acid rain. We were singing about f--ked up psychological things, like 'Not in Love,' which is about denial.
"We weren't singing, 'I love you, baby,' and I'm f--king proud about that. I wanted to be remembered for being socially conscious."
"Doesn't Really Matter," for example, was inspired by the time Holmes was travelling to an airport but didn't make it because the Irish Republican Army had blown up an entrance to the highway.
"That was our first hit and all these teenage girls were screaming at us," he said. "Listen to that first record, it's dark. 'Standing in the Dark' - that's how I'd like to be remembered.
"Will people remember us that way? No, because people still remember the hair."
As much as fans may remember those magnificent, massive coifs, they haven't forgotten the music: Sony Music Canada recently announced that "Alien Shores" has sold 860,000 units.
Platinum Blonde's winning streak ended with 1987's "Contact," an album that reimagined the group as a funk band (with new drummer Sascha Tukatsch) - a direction that Holmes conceded may have been a bit premature in the wake of the "Alien Shores" triumph.
"The '80s were a changing, volatile time," Holmes said. "Holy cow: every six months there was a new sound, a new vibe." When the supergroup Power Station released their debut album with the of-the-moment gated drum sound, Platinum Blonde enlisted their producer Bernard Edwards to work on "Contact."
"That music was so great and of course we got to play with a real funk-rock legend," Holmes said.
"But this is what I learned: when you're in a band, you've heard your own songs and played them so much and they (may not even be) released yet, but you find yourself musically moving on to different things.
"You have to slow yourself down. You've got to think, 'If we want to take our fans with us, we've got to bring this in a little bit more with our classic sound.'
"During that 'Contact' record, there were a lot of influences because now, all of a sudden, we're big stars: big money, everybody's got things on the line. 'Contact' put wedges between band members."
Holmes admitted that drugs also played a part in the internal strife.
"It absolutely was in the band," he said. "It was a coke-fuelled industry and I can say that now without having people say to me, 'Oh, you'll never work again.'"
A new beginning
Although they didn't tour "Contact," they regrouped as the Blondes for the album "Yeah Yeah Yeah," but after 1989 didn't play together live for 10 years. They reconvened for the occasional gig between 2000 and 2002, and then Holmes put Platinum Blonde on hold to co-found Toronto's Mod Club, where he was also known as DJ MRK.
While he was spinning, his old bandmate Kenny MacLean kept in touch.
"He would call me when I had found a lot of success as a DJ and with remixes," Holmes recalled. "That's where my life was: I had no time to talk or rehearse with the band. I'm trying to stay atop of the DJ world. You talk about pressure? That's pressure: always being the guy who is trying to come up with the new thing. It's an interesting world and I became fully involved in it.
"One day, I just decided to soften up. Kenny had called me every six months to say, 'Let's write some songs and do a tour with the band.' I was leading a double life: my DJ fans didn't know I was in Platinum Blonde. All they knew was MRK is this big English DJ. They had no clue of any of my past.
"I didn't want that knowledge to cross over to a band, because I don't know what that would have done to my rep. All my fans were in their early 20s."
But Holmes finally relented and agreed to reunite Platinum Blonde onstage, joining MacLean at the Mod Club during the release party for one of the musician's solo albums to sing an old hit or two.
"You should have seen his face," Holmes said. "He was so happy."
That was on Nov. 22, 2008, the last time Holmes saw MacLean alive.
"Kenny was so happy and I was so happy to see him so happy. I was invigorated and I thought, we're going to do this! This is going to be awesome! We had the after-party upstairs in what we call the Orange Room and then he went home that night.
"When he was brushing his teeth, it was over." On Nov. 24, MacLean was found dead of natural causes.
Holmes said he decided to honour MacLean's memory by reuniting with Sergio Galli and recording 2012's "Now & Never."
"I couldn't say that was the end," said Holmes. "We did a record, released it and toured it, and it was a success. Every moment I played, Kenny was with us. It's still heartbreaking."
After having his band feted at Canada's Walk of Fame last September, Holmes - also co-owner of the Jean Darlene Piano Room - said Platinum Blonde is back and prepping new music, some of which will be heard at Scotiabank Arena.
They're also yearning for another hit.
"When you're a creative artist and all you do is write and produce, that never goes away," he said.
"Even with Platinum Blonde, we'll never lose that feeling of wanting to put out another hit and, who knows, maybe one day you get a solid hit."
In 2010, Platinum Blonde got a boost when Toronto electro act Crystal Castles, with help from the Cure's Robert Smith, covered "Not in Love." "When we released our song 'Beautiful,' we got hits in places we'd never had before. That was in 2012," Holmes said.
"So it's always there, if you want it. The key component to being a functioning, creative musician and songwriter is to never ever, under any circumstances, grow up. You grow up, your art is done. It's over."
CAPTION: Platinum Blonde's Chris Steffler, Mark Holmes and Sergio Galli in their high-hair heyday.A Platinum Blonde concert at Toronto City Hall in May 1984 drew tens of thousands.Mark Holmes performed with Platinum Blonde at Peterborough's Del Crary Park in August 2016.Dimo SafariFrank LennonToronto Star file photoClifford SkarstedtPeterborough Examinerfile photo
CREDIT: Nick Krewen Special to the Star

Ontario health minister's concerns about Pierre Poilievre's interest in forced drug rehab reveals latest tension between the two parties

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-health-ministers-concerns-about-pierre/docview/3086993168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+health+minister%27s+concerns+about+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+interest+in+forced+drug+rehab+reveals+latest+tension+between+the+two+parties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 180 of 500

Drones, not helicopters, are a preferred option to aid the car theft crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/drones-not-helicopters-are-preferred-option-aid/docview/3086359182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Drones%2C+not+helicopters%2C+are+a+preferred+option+to+aid+the+car+theft+crisis&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Helicopters won't help
Worried about car theft? Ford government says five new helicopters will help, July 29
Premier Doug Ford is doing an end run around the people of Toronto and Ottawa by imposing police helicopters without community consultation. Toronto residents said a loud "no" to police helicopters repeatedly 20 years ago. The province is now foisting them on us through the back door, supposedly as a tool to address car theft. The result will be increased noise and surveillance of our neighbourhoods, with a likely disproportionate impact on low income and racialized communities.
Police helicopters do not prevent crime. City of Toronto Auditor Jeffrey Griffiths' 2001 evaluation of the Toronto police helicopter pilot program found the helicopter was of no use in high-speed car chases and did not improve outcomes in crime situations. Helicopters reveal a destructive and expensive trend toward militarization, the opposite of community policing. Drones would be a cheaper and much less intrusive policing tool in our city, especially for use in car theft cases.
Helen Armstrong and Tony Rapoport, Stop the Choppers.ca
Eyes in the sky can help deter crime, but those eyes don't have to be aboard an expensive helicopter. How many drones would $134 million buy?
Bruce McCallum, Toronto
It will cost $134 million for five new police surveillance helicopters? Unbelievable. What does this achieve? Surveillance is already everywhere. This money should go into community programs, public housing or health care. The social needs are endless. Our tax money should be put to our needs. Come on, Premier Ford. Spend the millions elsewhere.
Frances Sedgwick, Toronto
The province is spending $134 million to help solve the auto theft epidemic. Whether or not that is money wisely spent is for another day. In the meantime, one could ask why the taxpayers are the only ones bearing the cost for this (and other police measures) while the car companies and insurance companies go merrily along.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
We can rejig crosswalks
After yet another death, should Toronto replace these dangerous pedestrian crossovers? July 30
I wholeheartedly agree with Matt Elliott's column regarding the dangers of the yellow-light "stop if you feel like it" pedestrian crossover signals in the city. However, there is a better (and probably cheaper) solution than converting them to full-out traffic lights. When I visited Los Angeles in 2009, I observed crossovers that were both safe and efficient in terms of preventing unnecessary idling after the pedestrians are finished crossing. The crosswalk has a flashing yellow light at all times and then when the button is pushed by a pedestrian the flashing light turns red, effectively making the crosswalk a temporary stop sign. This means all cars must come to a full stop but then can proceed when it is safe to do so, just like at a stop sign. Toronto should seriously consider this option. Los Angeles may not be the best role model for most aspects of traffic management, but they certainly got this one right.
Rebecca Tomasone, Toronto
Crossovers promote a false sense of security for pedestrians. Yes, crosswalks are a shared responsibility between pedestrians and drivers but the pedestrian bears the additional burden of being perfectly in the right in case a driver pays no attention or chooses not to acknowledge the rules. A crosswalk is safe to step into only when the cars have come to a full stop and stay there for a period of time.
John Smeeton, Etobicoke
Move over, Australia
What we learned from Canada's win over Australia, as a Paris Olympics quarterfinal berth nears, July 30
Many consider Australians to be the top athletic nation in the world as they excel in so many sports. For a lesser-populated country, they compete above their weight class, and are surprisingly competent in traditional North American sports such as baseball and even ice hockey to some extent. One must admire them for that. To see our Canadian men's basketball team and our women's rugby 7 team finally better the Aussies gave great delight to those of us that are Olympics-watchers. Well done to both of Canada's teams!
Mike Brown, Burlington
High Park works as is
The ban on cars in High Park has left many park users unhappy. A year later, the city is making some changes, July 28
The same people are often quoted in articles about the anger residents have over the High Park car ban. Is this really representative of how most people in Toronto feel? If that were the case, why don't people complain about accessibility of Toronto Islands, Moore Park Ravine or Trinity Bellwoods? It would seem ludicrous to suggest putting roads and parking in those parks. The compromise of weekend closures and reduced parking on weekdays is working. I'm in the park frequently, at different times of the day and there are always a few parking spots available by the dog park and many in the restaurant's parking lot, so it seems to be enough to meet needs of those who require it. At the same time, the park now functions as intended - as a park where people can enjoy nature, feel at peace and safe, and hear birds chirping instead of horns honking.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
It mystifies me why High Park is a location where cyclists want to do high speed racing on what they think is a track. In fact, their track is a road with a speed limit. Other parks like Centennial or Downsview have wide open spaces where a cycling track could be created that doesn't involve other park users like seniors, families, sport teams and so forth. I get the impression that seniors and patrons with disabilities are regarded as low priority for accessing the park, but they are taxpayers supporting parks too. The legacy of High Park from the Howard family stipulated that it would be a place of relaxation for all. How sad that the city has perverted this sentiment. As well, let's remember that there is dedicated space for cyclists along Lake Shore, just south of High Park.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Matlow vs. Swift
Toronto to rename downtown streets in superstar's honour, July 26
Congratulations to Coun. Josh Matlow, the only Toronto city councillor mature enough to resist renaming a downtown strip after Taylor Swift. In the ever more dangerous and overcrowded mess that is Canada's largest city, there are greater priorities than fawning over an American teeny bopper idol.
W. Selby Martin, Bracebridge, Ont.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford takes speaks during a press conference about police helicopter funding in Mississauga on Monday.

Full text: Helicopters won't help police
Worried about car theft? Ford government says five new helicopters will help, July 29
Premier Doug Ford is doing an end run around the people of Toronto and Ottawa by imposing police helicopters without community consultation. Toronto residents said a loud "no" to police helicopters repeatedly 20 years ago. The province is now foisting them on us through the back door, supposedly as a tool to address car theft. The result will be increased noise and surveillance of our neighbourhoods, with a likely disproportionate impact on low income and racialized communities.
Helicopters do not prevent crime. City of Toronto auditor Jeffrey Griffiths' 2001 evaluation of the Toronto police helicopter pilot program found the helicopter was of no use in high-speed car chases and did not improve outcomes in crime situations. Helicopters reveal a destructive and expensive trend toward militarization, the opposite of community policing. Drones would be a cheaper and much less intrusive policing tool in our city, especially for use in car theft cases.
Helen Armstrong and Tony Rapoport, Stop the Choppers.ca
The province is spending $134 million to help solve the auto theft epidemic. Whether or not that is money wisely spent is for another day. In the meantime, one could ask why the taxpayers are the only ones bearing the cost for this (and other police measures) while the car companies and insurance companies go merrily along.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
There are much better uses for that $134M
It will cost $134 million for five new police surveillance helicopters? Unbelievable. What does this achieve? Surveillance is already everywhere. This money should go into community programs, public housing or health care. The social needs are endless. Our tax money should be put to our needs. Come on, Premier Ford. Spend the millions elsewhere.
Frances Sedgwick, Toronto
Eyes in the sky can help deter crime, but those eyes don't have to be aboard an expensive helicopter. How many drones would $134 million buy?
Bruce McCallum, Toronto

This means all cars must come to a full stop but then can proceed when it is safe to do so, just like at a stop sign. Toronto should seriously consider this option. Los Angeles may not be the best role model for most aspects of traffic management, but they certainly got this one right.

Crossovers promote a false sense of security for pedestrians. Yes, crosswalks are a shared responsibility between pedestrians and drivers but the pedestrian bears the additional burden of being perfectly in the right in case a driver pays no attention or chooses not to acknowledge the rules. A crosswalk is safe to step into only when the cars have come to a full stop and stay there for a period of time.
John Smeeton, Etobicoke
Australians a good measuring stick for Canada
What we learned from Canada's win over Australia, as a Paris Olympics quarterfinal berth nears, July 30
Australia is considered among the world's top athletic nations as they excel in so many sports. To see our Canadian men's basketball team and our women's rugby team finally better the Aussies gave great delight to those of us that are Olympics-watchers. Well done to both of Canada's teams!
Mike Brown, Burlington

Document 182 of 500

TTC warns of longer travel times: Subways ordered to reduce speed through 34 'slow zones' needing repairs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ttc-warns-longer-travel-times/docview/3086242356/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+warns+of+longer+travel+times%3A+Subways+ordered+to+reduce+speed+through+34+%27slow+zones%27+needing+repairs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto subway users are facing weeks of longer travel times, thanks to reduced speed zones the TTC has imposed across large portions of its network.
The transit agency says it has ordered trains to operate at slower speeds on 34 sections of Lines 1 and 2 as a "safety precaution," after a geometric survey conducted in May identified multiple points where track required repairs.
The reduced speeds allow "trains to run safely during service hours while minimizing disruptions to customers," the agency said on its website. They also "protect workers who may be required at track level to conduct routine or emergency inspections."
Trains normally travel at speeds of between 40 to 60 km/h, but through the affected sections they will slow to between 15 and 25 km/h. The majority of the zones are on Line 1, including the bottom of the busy downtown "U" between St. Andrew, Union and King stations. They will add an average of about 15 minutes to a trip between Vaughan and Finch on Line 1, while trip times on Line 2 between Kennedy and Coxwell will be about three minutes longer.
The agency said the problems it has identified are "minor track issues" such as rail gauge or wear being outside TTC standards. It plans to perform the required maintenance overnight when the system stops running, and during scheduled weekend closures between now and September.
Shelagh Pizey-Allen, executive director of the transit advocacy group TTCriders, said it's encouraging that the TTC is proactively monitoring its network for potential problems, but it's troubling so much track is in bad shape. She said the high number of slow zones raises questions about whether the agency - which this year reported a state-of-good-repair shortfall of $2.4 billion a year across its entire system - is adequately maintaining the subway.
Those concerns had already been stoked by incidents such as the hydraulic fluid leak in May that forced a partial shut down of Line 2 for 12 hours, and the derailment of the Scarborough RT last year.
"I think we'd all rather be safe than sorry, but we also expect the subway to be fast, and riders I think deserve answers about what's going on. Why are there so many (slow zones)?" Pizey-Allen said.
"We can't accept this as the new normal for a rapid transit line."
The TTC subway is responsible for about one million boardings every weekday. This isn't the first time customers have suffered slow zones. The agency implemented 23 of them in January.
The city is planning to upgrade Line 2 by replacing its fleet, which will start reaching the end of its service life in 2026. As part of the "new deal" Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford struck last November, the city and provincial governments each pledged $758 million toward the $2.3-billion purchase of 55 new trains.
Toronto is still waiting on a commitment from the federal government for the rest of the money. While Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals have yet to pledge a specific amount, this month they announced a 10-year, $30-billion public transit fund. That money isn't expected to flow until 2026.
The TTC says it needs the funding by early next year to place the train order, otherwise it will have to spend millions to extend the life of its existing fleet.
With files from Mark Ramzy
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief With files from Mark Ramzy

TTC warns of longer travel times as subways ordered to reduce speed through 34 'slow zones'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-warns-longer-travel-times-as-subways-ordered/docview/3086425130/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+warns+of+longer+travel+times+as+subways+ordered+to+reduce+speed+through+34+%27slow+zones%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto subway users are facing weeks of longer travel times, thanks to reduced speed zones the TTC has imposed across large portions of its network.
The transit agency says it has ordered trains to operate at slower speeds on 34 sections of Lines 1 and 2 as a "safety precaution," after a geometric survey conducted in May identified multiple points where track required repairs.

Trains normally travel at speeds of between 40 to 60 km/h, but through the affected sections they will slow to between 15 and 25 km/h.
The majority of the zones are on Line 1, including the bottom of the busy downtown "U" between St. Andrew, Union and King stations. They will add an average of about 15 minutes to a trip between Vaughan and Finch on Line 1, while trip times on Line 2 between Kennedy and Coxwell will be about three minutes longer.
The agency said the problems it has identified are "minor track issues" such as rail gauge or wear being outside TTC standards. It plans to perform the required maintenance overnight when the system stops running, and during scheduled weekend closures between now and September.
Shelagh Pizey-Allen, executive director of the transit advocacy group TTCriders, said it's encouraging that the TTC is proactively monitoring its network for potential problems, but it's troubling so much track is in bad shape. She said the high number of slow zones raises questions about whether the agency - which earlier this year reported a state-of-good-repair shortfall of $2.4 billion a year across its entire system - is adequately maintaining the subway.
Those concerns had already been stoked by incidents such as the hydraulic fluid leak in May that forced a partial shut down of Line 2 for 12 hours, and the derailment of the Scarborough RT last year.
"I think we'd all rather be safe than sorry, but we also expect the subway to be fast, and riders I think deserve answers about what's going on. Why are there so many (slow zones)?" Pizey-Allen said.
"We can't accept this as the new normal for a rapid transit line."
The TTC subway is responsible for about one million boardings every weekday.
This isn't the first time customers have suffered through slow zones. The agency implemented 23 of them in January.
The City is planning to upgrade Line 2 by replacing its fleet, which will start reaching the end of its service life in 2026. As part of the "new deal" Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford struck last November, the city and provincial governments each pledged $758 million toward the $2.3-billion purchase of 55 new trains.

The TTC says it needs the funding by early next year to place the train order, otherwise it will have to spend millions to extend the life of its existing fleet.
However, the TTC has already applied to the fund, and Minister of Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Sean Fraser told the Star this month that while the money won't be available for another two years, agreements for the first round could be awarded in 2024.
Funding for the Line 2 trains could "be locked in much sooner than 2026 and then allow them to deal with the expenses (as) they come up, knowing that the money's going to be there," he said.
With files from Mark Ramzy
CAPTION: This map showed the reduced speed zones along the TTC's Line 1 and 2.Toronto subway trains will be running at reduced speeds through 34 "slow zones" as a safety precaution, TTC says.

Document 184 of 500

Canada's on an Olympic gold rush + for-profit health care creeping across Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-on-olympic-gold-rush-profit-health-care/docview/3086044939/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+on+an+Olympic+gold+rush+%2B+for-profit+health+care+creeping+across+Canada&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, July 30 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Team Canada raking in the Olympic hardware, for-profit health care creeping across the nation and a probe into Canada's airline industry.
DON'T MISSJudoka Christa Deguchi clinched Canada's first gold at the Paris Olympics
Meet Christa Deguchi, the 28-year-old phenom who gripped, locked and ankle-hooked her way to Canada's very first gold in judo, Rosie DiManno writes - the country's first gold at the Paris Olympics, too. That's despite a hissing and hollering French crowd, who heckled as she came out on top against France's Sarah-Léonie Cysique in the semifinals. But Deguchi was unswayed, confronting and toppling the reigning world champion, Korea's Mi-Mi Huh, in the shadow of the Eiffel Tower yesterday. Here's what she had to say following her historic win.
Moments later: Toronto teen Summer McIntosh dominated the women's 400 individual medley, scoring her first Olympic gold at just 17 years old. It's like she was made for this, Bruce Arthur writes.What we know: Canada is already raking in the hardware and the Olympics are just getting started. Check out our running tally of Team Canada's gold, silver and bronze wins so far.Meanwhile on the pitch: Canada's women's soccer team weathered a drone-spying scandal, the loss of their head coach and a harsh six-point penalty from FIFA - and still came out on top against France over the weekend to secure their survival in the Games.As for-profit health care expands across Canada, doctors are fighting back
As for-profit practices gradually seep into Canada's public health care system, a group of doctors from across the nation are stepping up to fight back. The Canadian Medical Association is calling on governments to ensure medically necessary care is based on need, not the ability to pay. "Health care should not privilege those who can afford to pay for services," one doctor tells Allan Woods. Privatization is not inevitable; look at the doctors' recommendations and what they're fighting for.
Context: Private clinics and providers are flourishing amid a national health care crisis that's left some with no option but to pay out of their pockets.Armine Yalnizyan's take: In Norway, they have a term for the privatization of care: the tapeworm economy. Like a parasite robbing a body of its nutrients and destroying its organs, the pursuit of profit in health care devours public funding, erodes quality of care and degrades working conditions - and it's coming to Ontario.ICYMI: In a controversial move to cut wait times, Ontario expanded the powers of certain private clinics to provide CT scans, MRIs, and hip and knee replacements. But critics fear it'll only make Ontario's health care issues worse.The Competition Bureau launched a new probe into Canada's airline industry - and this time it's got teeth
Amid a rising tide of traveller complaints about the cost and quality of services, Canada's Competition Bureau is taking a hard look at the nation's airline industry, Josh Rubin reports. This time, following changes to the federal Competition Act, they've come armed with new powers - like the ability to demand documents from companies. "The only two entities who benefit from the current situation are Air Canada and WestJet, not the travelling public," said Flair Airlines' interim CEO. But will the bureau actually use their new powers?
Go deeper: The probe will focus on three key issues in the industry: the state of competition, barriers to entry and "impediments to Canadians making informed choices" on air travel.Why it matters: The bureau's new abilities could result in a much clearer, more detailed view of anti-competitive behaviour. "This looks like it's going to be a serious market study," one expert said. "I don't know how aggressive they'll be in using those powers, but we'll see."Speak up: Do you have information or experience the bureau should know about? The study is taking online submissions until Aug. 31.WHAT ELSE
As some MPs say they want to see her gone, Chrystia Freeland insists she has enough support to stay on as finance minister.Asylum seekers in Canada end up separated from their families for years. Document 185 of 500

Province offers cash for police choppers: Helicopters can help avoid deadly chases, experts say, but may not deter auto thieves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-offers-cash-police-choppers/docview/3085841303/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+offers+cash+for+police+choppers%3A+Helicopters+can+help+avoid+deadly+chases%2C+experts+say%2C+but+may+not+deter+auto+thieves&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario wants some help from above to catch auto thieves.
With the province pledging $134 million for five new police helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa, experts say such a move isn't the be-all and end-all - and there's little research in Canada as to whether they actually deter theft.
But choppers can be a tool to help police track stolen cars or criminals without having to resort to dangerous high-speed chases.
Helicopters are used by police forces in major cities around the world, and are "a highly valuable asset to put at the disposal of law enforcement," said Christian Leuprecht, a professor at both Queen's University and Royal Military College of Canada in Kingston.
With a growing population in the Greater Toronto Area, and growing traffic gridlock, having eyes in the sky will "prove effective and efficient," said Leuprecht, an expert in intelligence and defence.
It will also save lives, he said, pointing to recent high-speed chases, "because with the availability of a helicopter, you have options that you simply don't have if all you have to rely on are assets on the ground."
"I mean, every day, you can think of an incident in Toronto where it would have been helpful to have air assets."
On Monday, Premier Doug Ford announced the funding, which is expected to help combat auto thefts, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving.
The province's spring budget provided funding to lease four helicopters, but this new money will see Ontario instead purchase five choppers and cover ongoing operating costs.
This "will be a massive boost for community safety," Ford said.
Two helicopters will be purchased for the Ontario Provincial Police to work with the Ottawa and Toronto police, and three helicopters will be bought and operated by Durham, Halton and Peel police.
"In Ontario, a car is stolen every 14 minutes - this is completely unacceptable," said Solicitor General Michael Kerzner.
Some of the funds for the new helicopters were announced in the Ford government's March budget, and are in addition to funding for the OPP's auto theft team.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie blamed the government for inaction on auto theft, saying "Doug Ford has been premier for over six years. In that time, he has failed to tackle auto thefts and keep Ontario families safe."
Western University Prof. Laura Huey, a criminologist who focuses on policing, said the public does not want police to engage in high-speed chases - which can lead to deadly outcomes, including one recent incident in Durham that killed four, including a baby and his grandparents - but citizens also want something to be done about the massive car-theft problem.
"So there's only two other solutions, and one is fairly new and untried," Huey said.
"Those solutions are helicopters and drones."
While "New York is experimenting with drones," she said, "the issue is the same: people that don't want the cops to chase" also don't like the loss of privacy that comes with drones.
"Then that leaves you with one solution, which is helicopters," which she said are better "relative to us doing nothing."
Currently in the GTA, two helicopters are in use and shared with other police forces.
Mike Arntfield, a criminologist, author and professor at Western University, said there isn't a lot of peer-reviewed Canadian research on the usefulness of helicopters, apart from a study more than two decades ago on the use of a police helicopter in London, where he was an officer at the time.
Other, older studies also found that helicopters aided with "successful apprehensions of suspects with minimal risks to the public." They also give police "superior tactical advantage," he said.
Given they have been used by various police forces in North America for 80 years, "decades of best practices have been established, and they allow for real-time communications between the observer or the pilot on board, and ground units," which unmanned drones can't provide.
Wilfrid Laurier University Prof. Scott Blandford, the school's policing and public safety program co-ordinator, was also a London officer during the helicopter project.
He said operations and maintenance funding are key, given how costly helicopters can be.
Helicopters, he added, are "good, but they're not the end-all, be-all. They are a tool - they are just another resource that can be used," but have the advantage of avoiding traffic gridlock.
"The availability of the helicopter at any given time is a key factor in how effective they are," as is co-ordination with ground units, he said. "Once they are in the air, and they're aware of a hot spot, they can get to it much quicker. They then become a surveillance (tool), but that's all they are. They're not an interdiction.
"It's not going to suddenly end street racing," he said, especially in the age of social media where those events spring up spontaneously and are over quickly.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada says that, in Ontario, the Greater Toronto Area holds almost all of the top spots when it comes to auto theft claims, with huge increases in recent years.
Toronto is at the top, followed by Brampton, Mississauga, Vaughan and Markham, Ottawa, Oakville, Richmond Hill, Hamilton and London.
Liam McGuinty, the bureau's vice-president of strategy, said there's been a real focus on combating auto theft, but Ottawa must also play a larger role in enacting its national action plan. Auto manufacturers need to modernize their vehicle anti-theft technologies.
"We've got to put more emphasis on preventing cars from being stolen," said McGuinty. "And our view, and many others', is that regulating a standard that auto manufacturers have to live up to is the best and most effective way to do that" and has proved successful in the past.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Automobile racing; Automobiles; Theft; Helicopters; Robbery; Funding

Title: Province offers cash for police choppers:   Helicopters can help avoid deadly chases, experts say, but may not deter auto thieves

Document 186 of 500

Elections Ontario awaits direction on riding map: Province must decide if it will follow Ottawa in redrawing boundaries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/elections-ontario-awaits-direction-on-riding-map/docview/3085840878/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Elections+Ontario+awaits+direction+on+riding+map%3A+Province+must+decide+if+it+will+follow+Ottawa+in+redrawing+boundaries&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is not yet known how many ridings will be up for grabs in the next Ontario election.
Even though Premier Doug Ford is preparing for an early campaign next year, Elections Ontario is still awaiting direction from the legislature on whether there will be 124 constituencies contested as there were in the 2022 and 2018 votes.
"No decision has been made at this time," Attorney General Doug Downey's office said Monday when asked about any changes to the provincial electoral map.
Current public-opinion polls suggest that redrawing the electoral boundaries could be politically advantageous to Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives, who won 83 of the 124 seats in the last election.
Ontario's provincial ridings are largely based on those of Elections Canada - except for some northern constituencies.
Federal boundaries are changing for a national election expected in October 2025 from 338 electoral districts to 343 across the country.
That includes increasing Ontario's 121 federal ridings to 122 by adding three seats in Brampton, Milton and Durham while losing Don Valley East in Toronto and one in northern Ontario due to population changes.
The federal riding of Brampton North has been split into Brampton North-Caledon, which includes part of Dufferin Caledon, and Brampton-Chinguacousy Park, taking in sections of Brampton Centre.
Similarly, the federal riding of Durham has been divided into two new constituencies called York-Durham and Bowmanville-Oshawa North.
The federal riding of Milton has been carved into Burlington North-Milton West and Milton East-Halton Hills South, which includes a chunk of Wellington-Halton Hills.
In Toronto, sections of Don Valley East were absorbed into Don Valley West and Don Valley North and the rest merged with Scarborough Centre to create a renamed Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East.
Because federal and provincial riding boundaries are identical in southern Ontario, Ottawa's changes are being closely watched at Queen's Park, where Ford is considering a vote a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 election.
But Elections Ontario is hamstrung until MPPs act.
"While the last provincial redistribution followed federal redistribution, Ontario does not have legislation in place for a scheduled review of electoral districts and boundaries," Elections Ontario said in a statement to the Star. "Provincial electoral district boundaries in Ontario will therefore remain unchanged until new legislation has passed.
"The legislature has a choice in how to respond to the new federal electoral boundaries," Elections Ontario added.
"It could, for example, establish its own commission to determine what the new electoral boundaries would be, it could adopt the new federal electoral boundaries in part or in their entirety, or it could choose to retain the existing 124 electoral boundaries."
Under the provincial Representation Act, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals in 2017, there are currently "111 southern electoral districts whose names and boundaries are identical to those of the federal electoral districts."
Concerned about the lack of Indigenous MPPs in the legislature, Wynne's government - bolstered by a report from the non-partisan Far North Electoral Boundaries Commission - ensured there were 13 northern ridings instead of the 10 federal constituencies at the time.
Two-term MPP New Democrat Sol Mamakwa (Kiiwetinong) is the only Indigenous member of the legislature.
Buoyed by recent public and internal polls, Tory insiders believe they could win the rejigged Brampton and Durham seats - though they concede a victory in the May 2 Milton byelection would not necessarily translate into picking up the additional riding there due to demographics.
The disappearance of the riding of Don Valley East, currently held by Liberal Adil Shamji, could be a setback to the Grits.
Don Valley East was one of only seven ridings the Liberals won in the 2018 election and one of eight in the 2022 campaign.
However, a new provincial Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East riding could pit Shamji against Tory MPP David Smith (Scarborough Centre) with boundaries more favourable to the Liberals.
CAPTION: Ontario's provincial ridings are largely based on those of Elections Canada, and with federal boundaries being changed for an election expected in October 2025, Ontario must choose whether it will follow suit for a provincial election expected next year.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Toronto Star Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Election districts; Provincial elections; Legislatures; Elections

People: Ford, Doug

Ford government announces $134M for police helicopters to help combat auto theft, carjackings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-announces-134m-police-helicopters/docview/3085668953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+announces+%24134M+for+police+helicopters+to+help+combat+auto+theft%2C+carjackings&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario wants some help from above to help catch auto thieves.
Premier Doug Ford is in Mississauga Monday morning alongside Solicitor General Michael Kerzner to announce $134 million for five new helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa for not just thefts but carjackings, street racing and impaired driving.
The province's spring budget provided funding for four helicopters, and this funding will allow for the purchase, not lease, of five - the additional chopper going to Ottawa - as well as to cover ongoing operating costs.
"Our government is making the necessary investments to ensure that police services have the tools and resources they need to crack down on auto theft and car thieves," Ford said in a written statement.
Two H135 helicopters will be purchased for the Ontario Provincial Police to use for air surveillance in Ottawa and Toronto police, and three helicopters will be purchased and operated by Durham, Halton and Peel police.
"Criminal networks have become more sophisticated, but so have we," said Kerzner of the state-of-the-art aircraft.
"In Ontario, a car is stolen every 14 minutes - this is completely unacceptable. These helicopters will be a critical tool in helping police stay one step ahead of the criminals who are stealing cars and committing crimes on our streets."
Some of the funds for the new helicopters were announced in the Ford government's March budget, and are in addition $51 million for the OPP's auto theft team.
Some 90,000 vehicles stolen annually in Canada, and the Insurance Bureau of Canada says that in Ontario, Toronto has the highest number of auto theft claims - up 561 per cent in five years - followed by Brampton, Mississauga, Vaughan and Markham.
The Ford government has also said that it wants car thieves to lose their driver's licence for life, though critics said such a move would not deter criminals.
Currently in the GTA, two helicopters are in use and shared with other police forces.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Fordis in Mississauga to announce $134 million for five new helicopters.

Move on from the drone and support women's soccer at the Olympics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/move-on-drone-support-womens-soccer-at-olympics/docview/3085668879/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Move+on+from+the+drone+and+support+women%27s+soccer+at+the+Olympics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What was there to be gained with a drone?
Canada attempted to use drone at Copa America, CEO says, July 26
Except for the illegal use of a drone, I can't see what the fuss is all about. In soccer, what is there to gain in spying on an opposing team? How could one steal secrets? I can only imagine seeing players kick a ball around for 90 minutes and trying to score in sports' biggest net. I'm not saying that soccer is boring but I am saying that spying on another team probably has very little advantage or would not change anything. The best results can be achieved by excellent physical stamina and skills training. Anything else is luck. Let's lay off trying to make criminals out of foolhardy coaches who believe spying makes any difference.
John Cousins, Bowmanville, Ont.
One more time, Canada wants to overreact to a faux pas by its coaches. As if the Olympics are squeaky clean! Analyst Joseph Lombardi flew a drone over a New Zealand practice. So what? Dirtier practices have come up in Olympics history. Why should Canada not take every advantage, as other countries do? This smells like 1988, when Ben Johnson was singled out while other competitors were drug-enhanced. There is pathetic talk of kicking Canada's women's team out of the Olympics. Nonsense. Our soccer players trained for thousands of hours and played their games while being constantly underfunded. Let's get real and let the women settle this on the field.
Mike Faye, Toronto
Smith's crocodile tears
We are losing the prettiest places in Canada. And still, we will not change: Mallick, July 26
Premier Danielle Smith got emotional when speaking of the destruction in Jasper, one of Canada's most beautiful and iconic places. Columnist Heather Mallick is so right to question the premier's sincerity as she pushes for more drilling, processing, burning and selling of climate-disrupting fossil fuels.
Liz Addison, Toronto
I was struck by the press conference when Alberta Premier Danielle Smith was tearful. In a maudlin way, she recounted the tale of the horrific situation in Jasper, without mentioning her part in continuing to pump oil and give permits for exploration of new wells. For shame, Ms. Smith.
Melvin Tonken, Toronto
I have been following the Star's coverage of wildfires in Canada and climate change with much interest. As columnist Heather Mallick points out, Alberta and Premier Danielle Smith have a serious case of cognitive dissonance. Much ink is spilled debating what natural weather disasters can be attributed to climate change. Man despoiling his own nest, as it were.
Decades ago we were warned that too much carbon in the atmosphere from burning fossil fuels would lead to runaway climate change. Now, our effort to control emissions is dwarfed by the emissions from mega wildfires and thawing permafrost releasing methane, an even more potent greenhouse gas. We may have already passed a tipping point. Our future will be bleak indeed.
Kurt Crist, Consecon, Ont.
Canada is on fire and Jasper has just been decimated by flames. The devastation is felt by us all. I found it quite appalling that Alberta Premier Danielle Smith had the audacity to shed tears during her press conference regarding Jasper. She has fiercely supported and increased the oil and gas industry, while bringing in new restrictions on renewable power projects. We need leaders that will make the right decisions today so we have a sustainable world for the next generations to come.
Susan Mayrand, Toronto
Trudeau and the Titanic
With no sign of Justin Trudeau leaving, some Liberal MPs want major changes in his cabinet, July 25
A note to Liberal dissidents: Shuffling the deck chairs wouldn't have done much to change the fate of the Titanic. On the other hand, a different captain might have altered course in time to avoid disaster.
Ron Freedman, Toronto
Democrats would have accepted anyone
Kamala Harris instantly ignited the Democrats. Will a leadership change do the same for the federal Liberals? July 23
The speed at which Kamala Harris has been welcomed with open arms by a such a large contingent and variety of Democrats is impressive. However, this has surely been magnified by Donald Trump being the competition. If she was up against a less polarizing Republican, her acceptance might not have been accomplished so swiftly. The short time frame before a choice has to be made and fear of Donald Trump winning another term made a perfect storm for Harris to become the Democrats' presidential candidate.
Ann Sullivan, Peterborough
Trump's dismissive labels
For years now I've listened to Donald Trump's campaign of disrespect with disgust as he refers to his opponents only by their first names, often mispronounced and usually prefaced by a pejorative label. He is particularly fond of targeting women who he finds threatening. Perhaps it's time for a little "tit for tat." How about "Disaster Donald" or "Demented Donnie?"
Michael Bines, Toronto
Making his mark
Province pushing for above ground garage at Ontario Place due to cost, July 26
After initially agreeing to provide underground parking for this foreign-owned private spa, the Ford government's potential change in plans may result in a new massive structure dominating our waterfront skyline. I suggest that a fitting legacy for Doug Ford is to name this structure after him. How about "Doug Ford's Park-a-Rama?"
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Does it really matter if the parking lot is above ground or below? I just need to know if it will last longer than the Science Centre building.
Brian Mellor, Midland, Ont.
What about a stamp for Alex?
Canada Post to honour acclaimed director Norman Jewison with commemorative stamp, July 18
Filmmaker Norman Jewison deserves such an honour for being a talented and well-known Canadian. But, what about a Canadian stamp for Alex Trebek? He was born in Sudbury, Ont. and went to the University of Ottawa. He had a significant broadcasting career with the CBC, including the game show "Reach for the Top." He may have become a naturalized American citizen, but this does not erase his contributions as a Canadian. If the U.S. Postal Service can recognize him, why not Canada Post? What more do they need?
Diana Pronay, Mississauga
CAPTION: Men's national team head coach Jesse Marsch learned of the drone use after the fact, according to Soccer Canada CEO Kevin Blue.
