Document 201 of 500

'Ford Fest' back as premier considers an early election: Progressive Conservative party uses event to build brand and raise money

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-fest-back-as-premier-considers-early/docview/3085073288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Ford+Fest%27+back+as+premier+considers+an+early+election%3A+Progressive+Conservative+party+uses+event+to+build+brand+and+raise+money&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cementing a recent byelection victory, Premier Doug Ford is drumming up supporters for another "Ford Fest" - this time in Milton.
Robocalls from the Progressive Conservative party went out to potential voters this week for the Aug. 16 event - as well as to at least one Ontario Provincial Police detachment, located in the west GTA.
"It's not like they're targeting the stations," said Grace Lee, Ford's director of communications.
"The calls are going to numbers they have, that someone had put in as the preferred contact."
In the calls, an enthusiastic-sounding Ford touts the Friday night get-together, promising a multicultural atmosphere in the riding won by Zee Hamid, a former Liberal who switched to the Progressive Conservatives for the May 2 vote.
"Join me and our entire caucus and cabinet ... free hamburgers, hot dogs, Halal burgers and veggie burgers and free T-shirts and bottles for the first 500 guests," Ford says.
"There'll be live music, free carnival rides for the kids and fun for the entire family."
Ford steers potential attendees to the party website, where they are invited to make a donation to the Progressive Conservative party after registering.
The PCs use Ford Fests to build their brand, raise money for their war chest and identify potential voters, something that is taking on increased urgency this summer after Ford prompted speculation about an early provincial election next year.
At a news conference in May to announce a controversial speeding up of the timetable to put beer, wine, cider and mixed drinks into convenience stores after Labour Day, the premier would not pledge to abide by the next scheduled general election date in June 2026.
"I never rule out anything, but my main focus is to continue moving the province forward and really focusing on the things that people care really care about," he told the Star's Kristin Rushowy at the annual premier's conference last week in Halifax.
An election in the first half of 2025 would get Ford's Tories ahead of an expected October 2025 federal vote, with public opinion polls now favouring federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre over Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
In a veiled shot at Poilievre in the Halifax interview, Ford said he will fight "like a pit bull" for Ontario if any federal government acts against its interests.
Ford has worked closely with Trudeau's Liberal government on a number of issues, such as supports for electric vehicle battery factories now under construction.
While Ford's Tories have a healthy lead in the polls over the Official Opposition New Democrats under Marit Stiles and the Liberals under their new leader, Bonnie Crombie, an RCMP criminal investigation is underway into "irregularities" in the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
In the latest Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, Ford's PCs hit their highest level of support in two years at 44 per cent, with the Liberals at 26 per cent, the NDP at 19 per cent, and the Greens at seven per cent.
The survey of 1,000 Ontarians from July 16 to 21 used online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points 19 times out of 20.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 202 of 500

Why political parties could take a page from corporate succession

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-political-parties-could-take-page-corporate/docview/3085072884/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+political+parties+could+take+a+page+from+corporate+succession&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine that you have a choice of joining one of three high-profile corporate boards. The organizations are very different but share a common challenge: each has to decide whether to keep or change its CEO.
Company No. 1 has a photogenic CEO with a progressive brand. He turned the business around and led it to spectacular success in 2015. By 2019, though, the business had ceded substantial market share to its largest competitor. Since 2021, the company has fallen 20 points behind, and analysts' forecasts for 2025 are even worse. Would you let the CEO go?
Company No. 2 had a bombastic CEO who left unwillingly in 2021 after a controversial four-year term. Since then, the courts have found him to be a sexual abuser and fraudster, and he faces even more serious charges. He regularly lies and demeans competitors, colleagues and international partners. While he is popular among the company's rank-and-file, his former executives believe he is unfit for office. Would you rehire him?
Company No. 3 has a competent, decent 81-year-old CEO showing signs of physical and possibly cognitive decline. Many key managers have lost confidence in his ability to serve. While most respect him too much to speak out, some are becoming vocal about their concerns. Would you negotiate a succession plan?
In business, the path to such decisions is clear - if the company had good governance. But in politics, the parties led by Justin Trudeau, Donald Trump and - until recently - Joe Biden have all faced a daunting dilemma: Removing a leader against their will is incredibly difficult.
Why does this matter to citizens, businesses, workers and communities? In turbulent times, we need effective leaders who can make smart economic, social and environmental decisions. To get such leadership, we need to solve a problem: Those who govern us are often terrible at governing themselves.
The challenge involves both structure and culture. Mass-market leadership selection processes are superficially democratic but increasingly distorted by third-party spending, disinformation and demagoguery. Transparency and accountability mechanisms are weak. And the tribal culture of politics leaves even party grandees fearful of speaking their minds lest they be ejected from their clans.
Corporate governance, in contrast, has improved significantly in recent decades, driven by collaboration between business, investors, academia and regulators. After high-profile episodes of corporate malfeasance, we now have widely accepted standards for many governance processes. These include fiduciary responsibilities, codes of conduct, conflict resolution, independent directors, leadership selection and succession, risk management, whistleblower policies and disclosure rules.
Major democracies urgently need their political parties to improve their governance. Political leaders should be more accountable to their caucuses, and their governing boards - as long as the bar to force a leadership change is suitably high.
Unlike zero-sum battles such as electoral redistribution, rival parties share a common problem: they are increasingly vulnerable to making poor - sometimes disastrous - leadership decisions. It's not surprising that new political talent is becoming harder to find, and that parties find themselves at risk of being taken over by rival factions.
While change may seem far-fetched today, pundits once thought the same about the Reform Act, 2014, championed by Conservative MP Michael Chong. While the act gave Canadian MPs greater independence, including the power to review their leaders, the governing Liberals chose not to give their MPs this authority.
This issue that should transcend partisanship, because addressing governance would benefit all parties - and all citizens. This is an ideal role for party elders who no longer hold elected office or staff roles, in partnership with schools of politics and public policy, and independent volunteer activists who are free to speak their own minds. The movement could start as a coalition of the willing; it would gather momentum through intraparty elections and leadership races, and culminate in legislation.
To return to my opening question, most people wouldn't join any board with poor governance. That's why the challenge falls to our parties: the change must begin from within.
Daniel Tisch is the CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce,
a former senior policy adviser in the federal government, and a certified corporate director.
CAPTION: In politics, the parties led by Justin Trudeau, Donald Trump and Joe Biden face a daunting dilemma, writes Daniel Tisch: Removing a leader against their will is incredibly difficult.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Daniel Tisch Contributor Daniel Tisch is the CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce, a former senior policy adviser in the federal government, and a certified corporate director.

Subject: Political activism; Political leadership; Political parties; Leadership; Succession planning; Accountability

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Succession planning

People: Trump, Donald J; Biden, Joseph R Jr; Trudeau, Justin

Document 203 of 500

How politically savvy are you?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-politically-savvy-are-you/docview/3085072810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+politically+savvy+are+you%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This summer's political machinations are more than making up for any lack of excitement at the box office with new plot twists - JD Vance is in! Joe Biden is out! - dropping every week and a continually expanding universe of characters. Will the K-Hive get in formation? Did Republicans really hold up "Mass Deportation Now" signs at their convention? Could you pick the governor of North Carolina, a potential vice-presidential contender, out of a police lineup? The answers to none of the above - and more - can be found in our quiz below.
1. Kamala Harris will want to avoid comparisons to Selena Meyer, the famously shallow vice-president played by Julia Louis Dreyfus in the HBO satire "Veep." What meaningless campaign slogan from that series did Australian Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull use in his successful 2016 re-election campaign?
a) Now more than ever
b) Continuity and change
c) I alone can fix it, the thing that I broke
d) The devils you know
2.The title of political journalist Paul Wells' 2013 book "The Longer I'm Prime Minister" is the first half of a quip spoken by Stephen Harper. How does the line end?
a) "... the longer I'm prime minister."
b) "... the less you'll recognize this country."
c) ".... the happier we'll be."
d) With a whimper.
3.When voters are in a particularly bad mood, who or what do they want to throw out?
a) The clowns
b) The baby with the bathwater
c) The bums
d) The opening pitch
4.Joe Biden, who dropped out the 2024 presidential campaign last Sunday, has a recurring fondness for the word malarkey. Which of these is NOT a way the one-term U.S. pesident has deployed the term?
a) A campaign bus called the "No Malarkey Express"
b) A debate rebuttal that he'd "never heard so much malarkey" in his life
c) Cans of water labeled "Zero Malarkey," sold on his official website under the name "Dark Brandon's Secret Sauce" with the following description: "The secret to a good debate performance? Staying hydrated. Get yourself the same performance enhancers Joe Biden took before going on stage."
d) Co-chairing a Demalarking Government Task Force in his first term as vice president.
5.Which 1997 movie featured an embattled incumbent president who successfully deploys the slogan "Why change horses in midstream"?
a) "Good Will Hunting"
b) "Wag the Dog"
c) "Air Force One"
d) "The Sweet Hereafter"
6.Which idiom describes an election strategy deployed by an unpopular government aiming to minimize the scale of its loss?
a) Pull the goalie
b) Save the furniture
c) Hang the DJ
d) Flip a coin
7.The 1980 election campaign that returned Pierre Trudeau to 24 Sussex deployed a textbook "hide the leader" strategy. According to a Toronto Star analysis of the Liberal win, what did Trudeau's principal secretary Jim Coutts successfully protect his boss from?
a) The public
b) The press
c) Himself
d) All of the above
8.The Second City's 1994 comedy revue was named after the federal Progressive Conservative Party's crushing defeat the previous year. What was the show called?
a) "True Grit"
b) "Tory, Party of Two"
c) "They Blue It"
d) "GST'd off"
9.One of the following lines is from Donald Trump's "The Art of the Deal." The other three are from JD Vance's "Hillbilly Elegy". Which one is Trump's?
a) To this day, I find the very notion of pyjamas an unnecessary elite indulgence, like caviar or electric ice cube makers."
b) I don't believe in epiphanies.
c) To me it's very simple: if you're going to be thinking anyway, you might as well think big.
d) Recently, an acquaintance used the word "confabulate" in a sentence, and I just wanted to scream.
Benjamin Errett is the author, most recently, of "Elements of Taste: Understanding What We Like and Why."
1. b) The phrase was "the most concise example we could come up with of a politician saying a word and its opposite very quickly," showrunner Armando Ianucci told the Hollywood Reporter. "The aim is to have it sound snappy and confident, as if it makes sense. In fact, it means nothing. The two words cancel each other out."
2. a) As spoken by the 22nd prime minister of Canada, Wells takes the line to mean that "every day he stayed in office, (Harper) would make the decisions that only a prime minister can make, knowing few would be noticed, almost none would be contested, and that together they would add up." As a rare bonus for a politician's statement, this one is actually true.
3. c) "Throw the bums out" is political shorthand for an anti-incumbent mood, no matter which party is in power or which bums are eagerly awaiting the chance to be thrown in. The expression has been traced back to jeers shouted by baseball and boxing fans, with the first political use credited to William Jennings Bryan for his 1924 observation that "We have improved greatly since the Democratic Party decided to throw the bums out and invite the women in."
4. d) The idea of a demalarking government task force is perhaps an example of malarkey, but it is not a real example of Joe Biden's malarky use. When Biden used the term during the 2012 vice-presidential debates, it seemed old school and authentic. As the name of his campaign bus during his 2020 run, it was an anachronism that somehow worked. As an expression on novelty beverage cans, it wasn't so much the pause that refreshes as the refresh that paused. (Sadly, as of this week, Dark Brandon's Secret Sauce is no longer available for sale.)
5. b) "Wag the Dog". The Barry Levinson satire features a U.S. President who starts a war in the Balkans to distract voters from a sex scandal. The expression dates back to Abraham Lincoln, who humbly acknowledged during his 1864 re-election campaign that his party had "concluded that it is not best to swap horses while crossing the river, and have further concluded that I am not so poor a horse that they might not make a botch of it in trying to swap."
6. b) This expression is used in both English and French to describe a reallocation of resources in hopes of a dignified defeat. To extend the metaphor, all the seats that can be carried out of the house should be moved to higher ground in anticipation of a tidal wave. The trick, of course, is getting the party faithful excited about a search-and-rescue mission.
7. d) As reporter Andrew Szende wrote in his campaign postmortem, Justin Trudeau's father "was not to be exposed to any debates, interviews, hotline shows, or stress during the campaign." The candidate's sleep regimen was strictly guarded and he rarely deviated from his stump speech. The strategy resulted in a majority government.
8. b) The resulting caucus of Jean Charest and Elsie Wayne could in fact comfortably fit around a small cafe table, though technically the Progressive Conservatives lost official party status.
9. c) According to a 2016 CBS News investigation, "The Art of The Deal" has sold 1.1 million copies since it was published in 1987. According to a July 20 Marketwatch report, "Hillbilly Elegy" has sold 1.6 million copies since it came out in 2016. If you're going to be selling a book anyway, you might as well sell 500,000 more copies than your running mate and boss.
CAPTION: Former Australian Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull rode a slogan straight out of satire to election victory in 2016.Andrew Taylor the associated press file photo
CREDIT: Benjamin Errett CONTRIBUTOR Benjamin Errett is the author, most recently, of "Elements of Taste: Understanding What We Like and Why.";

'Ford Fest' is back as Doug Ford considers an early election

Document 205 of 500

Court dismisses challenge to Ford government's Ontario Place revamp

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/court-dismisses-challenge-ford-governments/docview/3085131525/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Court+dismisses+challenge+to+Ford+government%27s+Ontario+Place+revamp&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Place construction can resume after a court rejected a bid to halt the Ford government's controversial revamp of the waterfront site.
The Superior Court on Friday dismissed Ontario Place Protectors' legal challenge to the Rebuilding Ontario Place Act. Work on the project - which includes a towering privately owned spa resort, an expanded Budweiser Stage concert venue and a relocated Ontario Science Centre - was halted earlier this month pending the court decision.
Justice Lisa Brownstone ruled that the act, which gives the province powers to fast-track redevelopment at the waterfront site and exempt it from environmental assessment, is constitutional.
Ontario Place Protectors, a coalition of residents and organizations that include numerous architectural interests, had argued that the act improperly erodes judicial authority by preventing all remedies under all statutes when it comes to Ontario Place.
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives hailed their court victory.
"Our government is pleased with the decision and eager to move forward with this important project," Attorney General Doug Downey's office said in a statement Friday afternoon.
"The court dismissed Ontario Place Protector's application. The court also declined to grant the injunctive relief sought by the applicant," Downey's office noted.
"As this matter is in the appeal period it would be inappropriate to comment further."
Eric Gillespie, lawyer for Ontario Place Protectors, noted the court ruled it's possible for the government to go too far in sweeping away regulatory and judicial barriers to its plan, but found it had not done so in this case. He questioned the logic, saying that Ford's government could not have gone further than eliminating all remedies under Ontario statutes.
"The court doesn't directly address the precedent being set here," Gillespie said in an interview. "The obvious question is what's next" on the province's list of fast-tracked projects.
"If you can do this at Ontario Place," he said, "it doesn't appear there's any reason you can't do this to the entire Greenbelt, to build the next 417 Highway, to build the next airport, to put in the next landfill, to put in the next nuclear plant.
"In many people's view, simply giving the government carte blanche to do as it pleases and eliminate all the Ontario laws that might apply to future projects ... it wouldn't be surprising if this decision does become the subject of an appeal."
His group could try to overturn the decision at the Ontario Court of Appeal, Gillespie said. However, the ruling also leaves open the possibility of a group of Ontarians more directly affected by changes planned for the theme park trying the same kind of challenge.
CAPTION: A judge on Friday ruled that the provincial act that allows the Ford government to fast-track the redevelopment of Ontario Place is constitutional.

Subject: Court decisions; Redevelopment

Document 206 of 500

Province pushing for above ground garage at Ontario Place due to cost, says head of CNE

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-pushing-above-ground-garage-at-ontario/docview/3084749609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+pushing+for+above+ground+garage+at+Ontario+Place+due+to+cost%2C+says+head+of+CNE&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The ministry in charge of Ontario Place redevelopment seems determined to build a new parking lot for spa customers and other visitors above ground, citing potentially massive costs of burying it near Lake Ontario, says the head of the agency that runs the Ex.
Darrell Brown, CEO of the Canadian National Exhibition Association (CNEA), said a senior Infrastructure Ontario official told him in a meeting this month, about the prospect of the controversial garage being built at Exhibition Place rather than Ontario Place that "it's terribly expensive to build underground parking" and that the provincial government "doesn't have the money for this."
The official cited the recent rise in construction costs, Brown said, suggesting a previous estimate of $125,000 per underground stall has doubled and offering $800 million as the possible final price tag of parking to service the planned Therme spa, an expanded Budweiser Stage and a relocated Ontario Science Centre.
Asked about the meeting and $800 million estimate - much higher than previous figures, and too high according to one parking industry expert - Infrastructure Ontario said in a statement only that the Ontario government and city officials are exploring the possibility of putting the parking structure at city-owned Exhibition Place and "discussions are still at an early stage."
When the ministry official asked if the CNEA could help with financing, Brown said he responded that his organization, a not-for-profit running Toronto's annual three-week fair, is not in a position to do that.
The meeting followed months of rumours that Premier Doug Ford's government suffered sticker shock at the cost of original plans for below-grade parking, said Brown, who strongly opposes an above-ground lot at the home of the Ex.
"Exhibition Place is supposed to be a meeting place, an event location, and an above-ground parkade does nothing in terms of programming various events and community activities," he said. "It also takes away from us programmable space where we are generating revenue through things like the children's midway or parking or use by vendors.
"Premier Ford has talked about wanting to have a legacy at Ontario Place but destroying the legacy of the place next to it doesn't make a lot of sense."
Ford's government originally suggested a multi-level underground lot at Ontario Place to accommodate motorists visiting a planned new spa resort and expanded Budweiser Stage concert venue. Last fall, as part of the new financial deal for Toronto, Ford agreed to put the lot at neighbouring Exhibition Place, subject to negotiations between city and provincial representatives. Visitors to a relocated Ontario Science Centre would also use the lot.
On Monday, Ford added a new possibility - likely the most controversial - suggesting his government might build a towering parkade at Ontario Place - something advocacy group Ontario Place for All said would be a "blight" on the treasured waterfront. Ford cited the costs of building underground, while also suggesting it might be built on the city site on the other side of Lake Shore Boulevard West.
Under terms of Therme's 95-year lease and development deal, the province must provide parking within 650 metres of the spa resort's entrance.
The CNEA, a key Exhibition Place tenant, has long proposed building underground for parking, but also space to do marshalling and back-of-house activities to support major events, such as the tech-focused Collision trade show, held on the surface above. One version of the CNEA proposal ends at Lake Shore Boulevard, while another goes under the road to link with Ontario Place, giving motorists a weather-shielded way to park at Exhibition Place and get to the new attractions on Ontario Place's West Island.
City and provincial officials are just starting to negotiate what a garage at Exhibition Place would look like. The city's guiding principles, as decided by the Exhibition Place board chaired by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, state that the parking solution must "be completely underground to ensure highest and best use of land."
In a statement Tuesday, Malik said the province must front not only construction costs if it is built at Exhibition Place but also "long-term costs such as the impact on Exhibition Place and CNE operations."
Brown said he hopes the city sticks to its opposition to an above-ground structure. "One question is whether the city is really willing to stick to foundational principles or was that a statement to appease the community," he said. "We'll continue to push."
Malik told the Star on Thursday that the Exhibition Place board knows there is a "viable underground option.
"If the province values investments in our waterfront, they won't build a massive above-ground parking lot on it," she said. Below-grade parking will "enhance visitor experience, and improve public realm and programmable space."
Jeremiah Vanderlaan, a Guelph-based engineer who helps build parkades, called the $800 million estimated cost of building underground near the lake way too high. Even on landfill with a high water table due to the lake, stalls should not cost more than $100,000 to build, said Vanderlaan.
But he also argues that building above ground at Ontario Place or Exhibition Place would give visitors a better user experience at a fraction of the cost. His company, Kiwi Newton, wrote a blog explaining how a 2,700-spot above-ground parkade could be built for Ontario Place for only $81 million.
It would be brighter, safer and less "dark and dingy" than underground garages, he said. An above-ground parkade would block views but its appearance could be improved with solar panels, greenery and other features, Vanderlaan added.
"I understand the underground argument, out of sight, out of mind, but what are they willing to pay for that?"
CAPTION: As part of the "new deal" between the city and province, the new parking garage for the Ontario Place megaspa is supposed to be built on nearby Exhibition Place grounds. Recent statements from the Ford government have cast doubt on that plan.

Subject: Construction costs; Resorts & spas; Fairs & exhibitions; Automobile drivers

Jasper wildfires devour beloved mountain town + why Trudeau's MPs want a deep cabinet overhaul

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jasper-wildfires-devour-beloved-mountain-town-why/docview/3084749224/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, July 26 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a devastating wildfire that tore through Jasper, Liberal MPs hoping for a major cabinet overhaul and an international student challenging Canada's work permit laws.
DON'T MISSBeloved mountain town of Jasper is burning
Possibly up to half the structures in the iconic mountain town of Jasper, Alta., may have been reduced to cinders in an ongoing, devastating wildfire, Premier Danielle Smith updated yesterday afternoon - although park officials say they're still assessing damages. Some 25,000 residents, including some emergency services, have already evacuated town, Andy Takagi and Raisa Patel report. "The destruction and loss that many of you are facing and feeling is beyond description and comprehension," Jasper Mayor Richard Ireland wrote to residents yesterday. "We are all in this together."
In pictures: Shocking photos out of Jasper show vast plumes of smoke billowing from razed town blocks and the skeletons of homes and cars. Take a look inside the unfolding calamity.Heather Mallick's take: Canadians are horrified by Jasper's tragedy, but still we will not change - even as our prettiest places go up in smoke. What's next? Banff? Lake Louise?The Star's take: The horrific wildfires are a reminder that we take the beauty of this country for granted at our peril. Climate change is making wildfire season longer, deadlier and more destructive; Jasper's destruction is a monster of our own making.With no sign of Justin Trudeau leaving, some Liberal MPs want to overhaul his cabinet
A tiny cabinet change last week did nothing to appease those in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's caucus who believe a major overhaul of key government ministers could help save the Liberal party from electoral defeat. "Clearly, what's happening is not working," one anonymous Liberal MP told the Star's Alex Ballingall and Raisa Patel. As Trudeau stands unwavering amid growing calls for his resignation, some MPs are hoping for deep, meaningful changes elsewhere.
What we know: Of the many MPs that spoke with the Star, four are naming Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland as having to go. Others welcomed the potential addition of former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney to party ranks.ICYMI: Rumblings of discontent within Trudeau's caucus have only grown after the party lost the Liberal stronghold of Toronto-St. Paul's to the Tories in a fateful byelection.Wait, what? Even if Trudeau were to give up on re-election, a recent poll suggests his successors might not fare much better - and may instead drive more voters away from the party than toward it ahead of the next election, slated for October 2025.This international student with undiagnosed mental disorders took 9 years to get a degree and was refused a work permit
Growing up in Nigeria, Izaka Jefferson Eugene-Akhere was bullied for his weight. No one recognized his mental disorders, which fed into his binge-eating and binge-watching TV habits, Nicholas Keung reports. In 2012, an 18-year-old Eugene-Akhere hoped to start fresh in Canada. But his depression and anxiety continued to haunt him; after having his studies suspended twice by York University over poor attendance and grades, Eugene-Akhere finally graduated in 2022 - only to have his postgraduation work permit refused last November. He's now challenging Canada's work permit rules - here's why.
Background: To qualify for the work permit, international students must maintain full-time student status each semester - except for the final term, or unless they had taken an approved leave of no more than 150 days - from an authorized institution.More: Eugene-Akhere claims the full-time requirement discriminates against disabled students, violating their equality rights under the Charter. "We're challenging the constitutionality of the policy itself," his lawyer told the Star.WHAT ELSE
The COC has removed Olympic women's soccer coach Bev Priestman amid the drone spying scandal.Canada's soccer spying scandal didn't start with New Zealand. The Olympic champions' legacy is in ruins.Loblaw and George Weston must pay half a billion dollars for a bread price-fixing scheme in the "largest antitrust settlement in history.""One way or another, someone needs to pay." Toronto to explore "rain tax" for industrial and commercial properties as part of its flood response.The province is pushing for an above-ground garage at Ontario Place to cut costs, the head of CNE says.The LCBO strike seems to have given Doug Ford's Tories a boost - but his government's poor approval rating is a warning sign, survey shows.He was dramatically pulled over. The cop drew his gun. Was it misconduct? That depends on whom you ask.Trudeau minister shoots down "false" claims from Republican senators about Canada's immigration program for Palestinians.Toronto's condo market is officially in "recessionary territory" as investors can no longer afford to prop it up, a report says.A two-bedroom Toronto co-ownership sold for just over $400,000. Sound like a good starter home? Here's why it might not be.Stars are lining up to support Kamala Harris. Here's why celebrity endorsements are crucial to U.S. politics.Prince Harry is back on TV - here's everything he said about his family's royal rift.POV
Let's all hope the repackaging of Kamala Harris works.CLOSE-UP

Document 208 of 500

Doug Ford is correct to commit advertising dollars to local media but he's wrong to use tax dollars to promote his party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-correct-commit-advertising-dollars/docview/3084749211/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+correct+to+commit+advertising+dollars+to+local+media+but+he%27s+wrong+to+use+tax+dollars+to+promote+his+party&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government's recent decision to direct its four largest agencies to spend 25 per cent of their advertising dollars on Ontario-based news publishers is a welcome move to support the province's struggling media industry.In an era of declining print advertising revenues and the rise of digital giants siphoning away ad dollars, local news outlets across Canada are fighting for survival. Premier Doug Ford deserves credit for recognizing the vital role journalism plays in our communities and taking concrete action to help.However, while the advertising directive is laudable, it risks being overshadowed by the government's troubling record on its own advertising practices. Shortly after taking office, the Progressive Conservatives broke their election promise to restore the Auditor General's oversight powers over government advertising, which the previous Liberal government had gutted.Under the original rules, the Auditor General could reject government ads if their primary purpose was to foster a positive impression of the governing party. The Liberals watered this down in 2015 and despite railing against the changes while in opposition, the Ford government has chosen to leave them in place.The result is that Ontarians have been subjected to a barrage of publicly funded ad campaigns that are a thinly veiled partisan promotion of the government - from attacks on the federal carbon tax, to pre-election ads touting Ontario's economic progress, to the recent $8 million "It's Happening Here" campaign.While feel-good ads trumpeting a government's accomplishments are hardly new, there's a key difference when they are funded by taxpayers rather than political parties. Without meaningful oversight, the line between necessary public information and partisan ads can quickly blur.If Ford truly wants to be a champion of Ontario's news industry, he should couple his advertising commitment with a return to more stringent rules around the government's own advertising. His government was elected on a promise to restore the Auditor General's powers and it's not too late to follow through.As the famous media theorist Marshall McLuhan noted, the medium is the message. In this case, Ford's chosen advertising medium of local news outlets sends a positive message about his government's priorities. But the integrity of that message risks being undermined if his government continues to resist reasonable limits on its own ability to use public money for partisan ends.By restoring the Auditor General's oversight and reining in publicly funded partisan ads, Ford would send a powerful signal that his commitment to Ontario's news industry is principled. A government that truly values a strong, independent press should welcome reasonable checks and balances on its own advertising, not resist them.As he looks to boost Ontario's media through his new advertising policy, Ford should also seize the opportunity to elevate his government's own communications standards. By matching his financial support with a real commitment to non-partisan, accountable public outreach, he can leave a lasting legacy of strengthening two essential pillars of our democracy - a robust local news sector and a transparent, trustworthy government.
CAPTION: "Under the original rules, the Auditor General could reject government ads if their primary purpose was to foster a positive impression of the governing party. The Liberals watered this down in 2015 and despite railing against the changes while in opposition, the Ford government has chosen to leave them in place," writes Éric Blais.
CREDIT: Éric Blais Contributor

Subject: Advertising; Newspaper industry

LCBO strike seems to have given Doug Ford's Tories a boost, but government's poor approval rating is a warning sign, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-strike-seems-have-given-doug-fords-tories/docview/3084749083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+strike+seems+to+have+given+Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+a+boost%2C+but+government%27s+poor+approval+rating+is+a+warning+sign%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives will drink to that.
The first strike in LCBO history appears to have boosted the popularity of Ford's Tories, according to the latest Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star.
Abacus found the PCs at 44 per cent - their highest level of support in more than two years - with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
"At the end, Ford won that fight - unlike previous labour conflicts," Abacus president David Coletto said Thursday.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from July 16 through Sunday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The job action by more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) members, which closed 680 liquor stores for 18 days, officially ended Monday and LCBO outlets reopened the following morning.
Coletto noted "the Conservatives have opened up a bigger lead than we saw before the strike."
In last month's Abacus poll, the Tories were at 41 per cent to 25 per cent for the Liberals, 22 per cent for the NDP and eight per cent for the Greens.
"It appears as if people didn't understand why OPSEU was on strike. It's not like an education workers' strike where people intuitively understand that schools will be closed and students and parents affected," said Coletto, whose findings echoed those in the government's own Campaign Research polling obtained by the Star on July 16.
"So Ford will leave the strike undeterred and probably more empowered to move ahead with his preferences on how alcohol is sold in Ontario," he said.
That's a reference to the premier's controversial decision to give $225 million to The Beer Store, a foreign-owned private company, to expedite liberalization of booze sales to 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets.
OPSEU argued that losing the LCBO's monopoly on selling premixed ready-to-drink beverages - such as Dillon's gin cocktails and Mike's Hard Lemonade - would hurt the Crown retailer, which retains exclusive rights to the sale of spirits.
But Coletto said the work stoppage "came at politically the right time for Ford because it allowed him to demonstrate why liberalization was good."
"The Tories are in a position of strength - the opposition doesn't feel like it's gaining any momentum," he said.
While Ford has been considering an early election next year - instead of waiting for the scheduled June 2026 vote - Coletto cautioned that the Tories shouldn't be putting the champagne in ice just yet.
The PC government has a 33 per cent approval rating and a 44 per cent disapproval rating with 20 per cent neither approving nor disapproving and four per cent unsure.
"That approval rating isn't great," the pollster said, emphasizing that may be "the one uncertainty" making the Tories nervous about a snap vote.
"I would caution that these numbers look very similar to what Justin Trudeau saw before his early election call in 2021," said Coletto, noting that gambit did not pan out as well as the prime minister had hoped.
"So if (Ford) gets into a situation where people are asking 'why are we having this (early) election,' that could pose problems."
In June's poll, Abacus found 44 per cent of Ontarians would be "unhappy" or "very angry" if Ford called an early $150-million election.
Regarding views of the four party leaders, 34 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 43 per cent had a negative impression with 21 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say. That's a net impression rating for him of minus nine per cent.
Crombie was 25 per cent positive, 31 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 15 per cent unsure for a minus six per cent net rating.
Stiles also had a 25 per cent positive with a 26 per cent negative, a 30 per cent neutral and 18 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus one per cent.
Schreiner had a 21 per cent positive, 20 per cent negative, 36 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus one per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford carries a case of beer during a photo-op at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto, on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Beer; Strikes; Liberalization

Business indexing term: Subject: Strikes Liberalization

Title: LCBO strike seems to have given Doug Ford's Tories a boost, but government's poor approval rating is a warning sign, survey shows

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jul 26, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 210 of 500

Are Chinese-made EVs 'spy machines'?: Experts raise alarm that vehicles could pose an 'invisible' security threat to Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-chinese-made-evs-spy-machines/docview/3084675550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+Chinese-made+EVs+%27spy+machines%27%3F%3A+Experts+raise+alarm+that+vehicles+could+pose+an+%27invisible%27+security+threat+to+Canada&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is gearing up to slap hefty tariffs on "artificially cheap" Chinese electric vehicles amid mounting concerns the cars also pose a security threat.
With Canada widely expected to impose levies on China-made EVs next month, industry expert Michael Dunne warned "a connected car is a kind of invisible threat because it is hiding in plain sight - right there in our driveway."
Mitra Mirhassani, an electrical and computer engineering professor at the University of Windsor, said Thursday the EVs pose far greater risks than Chinese-made smartphones or laptops.
"EVs are connecting to our power grids," said Mirhassani, a co-founder of the university's SHIELD Automotive Cybersecurity Centre.
"Your cellphone is ... protected by different networks and firewalls. EV networks are not that secure yet. They haven't reached that maturity of a cellphone network, the IT network," she said.
The warnings come after Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland confirmed last month the federal government is studying "the national security aspect ... which is heightened when we're talking about imports of very high-tech vehicles like EVs."
Freeland's comments echoed those of Sir Richard Dearlove, the former head of Britain's MI6 intelligence service, who told CNBC this year that consumers "should be very concerned" about the vehicles.
"There's no question an inexpensive Chinese car has devices under the bonnet, which should concern us. The car could be switched off by the manufacturer," said Dearlove in April.
"How many cars would it take to completely block the circulation of traffic in a major Western city? Malware can be put into the computer in the car and the collection of data right down to bugging the car internally is a possibility," the former spymaster said.
Mirhassani said that lax cybersecurity protocols could be the "hidden cost" of cheap Chinese EVs - unlike those manufactured elsewhere in Asia, North America and Europe.
While the trade implications of affordable Chinese EVs on Canadian roads are well known, Dunne - a former president of GM Indonesia and director of JD Power and Associates in China - said the potential for spyware and malware in the cars is as worrying.
"Some people may think this sounds alarmist, but that's a powerful super computer parked in front of your house," said the San Diego-based CEO of Dunne Insights, a firm with expertise in global EV markets and battery supply chains, and the author of "American Wheels, Chinese Roads: The Story of General Motors in China."
With their cameras, the Chinese cars could in theory be repurposed for covert surveillance, collecting data on drivers and their surroundings and even weaponized for co-ordinated cyberattacks.
"These things could be used as mobile spy machines," he said, pointing out that the vehicles could disrupt 5G cell service or Wi-Fi networks.
Unlike Chinese-made smartphones, laptops and internet-enabled appliances that have been sold in Canada for years, inexpensive mass-market EVs from China are not yet available here.
Concerns over the vehicles come against the backdrop of heightened awareness of foreign interference in Canadian politics by hostile states.
China has always denied it interferes in Canada's domestic affairs.
But the Chinese government understands the potential danger posed by "spy cars" - that's why Beijing has in the past strictly limited where U.S.-engineered Tesla EVs are allowed to go in China.
"The Chinese themselves are acutely aware of the risks. They are restricting where Teslas can drive in China," he said, noting the American-designed EVs were until recently barred from many Chinese highways, military installations, airports, prisons and other infrastructure deemed sensitive.
Even Chinese-made Tesla EVs were impacted until the company promised all data generated from its vehicles would be stored locally in China, meeting Beijing's security requirements.
"There are already 400 million cameras inside China tracking everyone's movements so they don't want (the U.S.) spying on them (with camera-laden American EVs)," said Dunne.
Dan Gittleman, managing partner at SanBoca Insights, an automotive software consultant that advises manufacturers, said the U.S. Commerce Department is considering new restrictions on the EV software imported from China.
"They're looking at barring certain types of software and hardware in any kind of vehicle in addition to the 100 per cent (U.S.) tariff on Chinese EVs," Gittleman said in interview from France.
"But we're playing catch-up," he said.
Canada currently has a six per cent tariff on Chinese EVs - but Ottawa gives those who buy Tesla and Volvos made in China the same $5,000 rebate as it does for purchasers of North American, Korean and European vehicles.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who has been holding consultations with stakeholders that conclude next week, is expected to soon match U.S. President Joe Biden's stiff tariffs on Chinese EVs.
"We're watching closely what the Americans and other allies have done," Trudeau said in June.
It's been more than two months since Biden boosted American levies on the China-built cars from 25 per cent to 100 per cent.
Premier Doug Ford has been urging Trudeau to do the same in order to protect Ontario's burgeoning EV industry from being swamped by inexpensive imports.
"I'm calling on the federal government to immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports, including at least a 100 per cent tariff on Chinese electric vehicles," Ford said June 21.
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles," the premier said at the time.
Dunne said "the 100 per cent tariffs are a showstopper for now, but look for the Chinese to do workarounds" by building their cars in other countries. He noted that Volvo, a Swedish-based firm wholly owned by China's Geely Holding since 2010, is shifting production of its EX30 SUV to Europe to avoid the 100 per cent duties. American-owned Tesla is expected to supply the Canadian market with more U.S.-made vehicles instead of from its Shanghai factory.
"The tariffs are a good first step, but China has a 10-year head start on (the rest of the world) on EVs ... so we need to sustain that urgency. We are the underdogs."
Trudeau and Ford have earmarked tens of billions of dollars in production subsidies and tax breaks for Stellantis, Volkswagen, Honda and other major manufacturers to invest in Ontario's EV sector.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on an EV strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy, said the Chinese threat is real.
"We know the Chinese don't play by the rules - there's evidence of election interference, covert police operations here and the arbitrary detention of the Michaels," he said, referring the three-year incarceration of Canadians Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor and ongoing concern about Chinese meddling in domestic affairs.
"So to believe that they're going to play by the rules after they've deployed an incredible amount of state-owned technology on Canadian roads is naive."
CAPTION: A Sealion car by Chinese electric vehicle manufacturer BYD at the Bangkok International Motor Show in March. With Canada expected to impose levies on China-made EVs next month, one expert said the vehicles pose far greater risks than Chinese-made smartphones or laptops.LILLIAN SUWANRUMPHA AFP via Getty images file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Electric vehicles; Tariffs; Cybersecurity; Automobiles; Malware; Information ethics; National security; Espionage

Document 211 of 500

LCBO strike gave Ford's Tories a boost: Poll suggests Ontarians didn't understand reason for work stoppage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-strike-gave-fords-tories-boost/docview/3084675469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+strike+gave+Ford%27s+Tories+a+boost%3A+Poll+suggests+Ontarians+didn%27t+understand+reason+for+work+stoppage&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives will drink to that.
The first strike in LCBO history appears to have boosted the popularity of Ford's Tories, according to the latest Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star.
Abacus found the PCs at 44 per cent - their highest level of support in more than two years - with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
"At the end, Ford won that fight - unlike previous labour conflicts," Abacus president David Coletto said Thursday.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from July 16 through Sunday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform.
While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The job action by more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) members, which closed 680 liquor stores for 18 days, officially ended Monday and LCBO outlets reopened the following morning.
Coletto noted "the Conservatives have opened up a bigger lead than we saw before the strike."
In last month's Abacus poll, the Tories were at 41 per cent to 25 per cent for the Liberals, 22 per cent for the NDP and eight per cent for the Greens.
"It appears as if people didn't understand why OPSEU was on strike. It's not like an education workers' strike where people intuitively understand that schools will be closed and students and parents affected," said Coletto, whose findings echoed those in the government's own Campaign Research polling obtained by the Star on July 16.
"So Ford will leave the strike undeterred and probably more empowered to move ahead with his preferences on how alcohol is sold in Ontario," he said.
That's a reference to the premier's controversial decision to give $225 million to the Beer Store, a foreign-owned private company, to expedite liberalization of booze sales to 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets.
OPSEU argued that losing the LCBO's monopoly on selling premixed ready-to-drink beverages - such as Dillon's gin cocktails and Mike's Hard Lemonade - would hurt the Crown retailer, which retains exclusive rights to the sale of spirits.
But Coletto said the work stoppage "came at politically the right time for Ford because it allowed him to demonstrate why liberalization was good."
"The Tories are in a position of strength - the opposition doesn't feel like it's gaining any momentum," he said.
While Ford has been considering an early election next year - instead of waiting for the scheduled June 2026 vote - Coletto cautioned that the Tories shouldn't be putting the champagne in ice just yet.
The PC government has a 33 per cent approval rating and a 44 per cent disapproval rating with 20 per cent neither approving nor disapproving and four per cent unsure.
"That approval rating isn't great," the pollster said, emphasizing that may be "the one uncertainty" making the Tories nervous about a snap vote.
"I would caution that these numbers look very similar to what Justin Trudeau saw before his early election call in 2021," said Coletto, noting that gambit did not pan out as well as the prime minister had hoped.
"So if (Ford) gets into a situation where people are asking 'why are we having this (early) election,' that could pose problems."
In June's poll, Abacus found 44 per cent of Ontarians would be "unhappy" or "very angry" if Ford called an early $150-million election.
Regarding views of the four party leaders, 34 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 43 per cent had a negative impression with 21 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say. That's a net impression rating for him of minus nine per cent.
Crombie was 25 per cent positive, 31 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 15 per cent unsure for a minus six per cent net rating.
Stiles also had a 25 per cent positive with a 26 per cent negative, a 30 per cent neutral and 18 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus one per cent.
Schreiner had a 21 per cent positive, 20 per cent negative, 36 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus one per cent.
CAPTION: Abacus president David Coletto said the strike came at the right time politically for Premier Doug Ford because it allowed him to show why liberalizing alcohol sales was good.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Strikes; Liberalization; Polls & surveys; Liquor stores; Liquor industry

Document 212 of 500

Province pushes above-ground parkade to serve Ontario Place: Below-grade plan far too expensive, official says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-pushes-above-ground-parkade-serve/docview/3084675455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+pushes+above-ground+parkade+to+serve+Ontario+Place%3A+Below-grade+plan+far+too+expensive%2C+official+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The ministry in charge of Ontario Place redevelopment seems determined to build a new parking lot for spa customers and other visitors above ground, citing potentially massive costs of burying it near Lake Ontario, says the head of the agency that runs the Ex.
Darrell Brown, CEO of the Canadian National Exhibition Association (CNEA), said a senior Infrastructure Ontario official told him in a meeting this month, about the prospect of the controversial garage being built at Exhibition Place rather than Ontario Place that "it's terribly expensive to build underground parking" and that the provincial government "doesn't have the money for this."
The official cited the recent rise in construction costs, Brown said, suggesting a previous estimate of $125,000 per underground stall has doubled and offering $800 million as the possible final price tag of parking to serve the planned Therme spa, an expanded Budweiser Stage and a relocated Ontario Science Centre.
Asked about the meeting and $800-million estimate - much higher than previous figures, and too high according to one parking industry expert - Infrastructure Ontario said in a statement only that the Ontario government and city officials are exploring the possibility of putting the parking structure at city-owned Exhibition Place and "discussions are still at an early stage."
When the ministry official asked if the CNEA could help with financing, Brown said he responded that his organization, a not-for-profit running Toronto's annual three-week fair, is not in a position to do that.
The meeting followed months of rumours that Premier Doug Ford's government suffered sticker shock at the cost of original plans for below-grade parking, said Brown, who strongly opposes an above-ground lot at the home of the Ex.
"Exhibition Place is supposed to be a meeting place, an event location, and an above-ground parkade does nothing in terms of programming various events and community activities," he said. "It also takes away from us programmable space where we are generating revenue through things like the children's midway or parking or use by vendors.
"Premier Ford has talked about wanting to have a legacy at Ontario Place but destroying the legacy of the place next to it doesn't make a lot of sense."
Ford's government originally suggested a multi-level underground lot at Ontario Place to accommodate motorists visiting a planned new spa resort and expanded Budweiser Stage concert venue. Last fall, as part of the new financial deal for Toronto, Ford agreed to put the lot at neighbouring Exhibition Place, subject to negotiations between city and provincial representatives. Visitors to a relocated Ontario Science Centre would also use the lot.
On Monday, Ford added a new possibility - likely the most controversial - suggesting his government might build a towering parkade at Ontario Place - something advocacy group Ontario Place for All said would be a "blight" on the treasured waterfront. Ford cited the costs of building underground, while also suggesting it might be built on the city site on the other side of Lake Shore Boulevard West.
Under terms of Therme's 95-year lease and development deal, the province must provide parking within 650 metres of the spa resort's entrance.
The CNEA, a key Exhibition Place tenant, has long proposed building underground for parking, but also space to do marshalling and back-of-house activities to support major events, such as the tech-focused Collision trade show, held on the surface above. One version of the CNEA proposal ends at Lake Shore Boulevard, while another goes under the road to link with Ontario Place, giving motorists a weather-shielded way to park at Exhibition Place and get to the new attractions on Ontario Place's West Island.
City and provincial officials are just starting to negotiate what a garage at Exhibition Place would look like. The city's guiding principles, as decided by the Exhibition Place board chaired by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, state that the parking solution must "be completely underground to ensure highest and best use of land."
In a statement Tuesday, Malik said the province must front not only construction costs if it is built at Exhibition Place but also "long-term costs such as the impact on Exhibition Place and CNE operations."
Malik told the Star on Thursday that the Exhibition Place board knows there is a "viable underground option.
"If the province values investments in our waterfront, they won't build a massive above-ground parking lot on it," she said.
Jeremiah Vanderlaan, a Guelph-based engineer who helps build parkades, called the $800 million estimated cost of building underground near the lake way too high. Even on landfill with a high water table due to the lake, stalls should not cost more than $100,000 to build, said Vanderlaan.
But he also argues that building above ground at Ontario Place or Exhibition Place would give visitors a better user experience at a fraction of the cost. His company, Kiwi Newton, wrote a blog explaining how a 2,700-spot above-ground parkade could be built for Ontario Place for only $81 million.
It would be brighter, safer and less "dark and dingy" than underground garages, he said. An above-ground parkade would block views but its appearance could be improved with solar panels, greenery and other features, Vanderlaan added.
"I understand the underground argument, out of sight, out of mind, but what are they willing to pay for that?"
CAPTION: Faced with an $800-million estimate to build an underground parking garage at Ontario Place for the planned Therme spa, an expanded Budweiser Stage and a relocated Ontario Science Centre, provincial officials are now proposing an above- ground parkade at Exhibition Place.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 213 of 500

OPP deal makes officers highest paid cops in province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opp-deal-makes-officers-highest-paid-cops/docview/3084675044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=OPP+deal+makes+officers+highest+paid+cops+in+province&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Provincial Police officers are now the highest paid in the province, their union says, after they ratified a four-year deal last week.
The contract covers 2023 to 2026 and the officers will see raises of 4.75 per cent retroactive to the first year of the deal, followed by an increase of 4.5 per cent in the second year and 2.75 per cent raises in each of the last two years of the deal.
By the end of the deal, a first-class constable will earn a salary of $123,194, said the Ontario Provincial Police Association, the union that represents about 10,000 officers and civilian employees.
"We're very happy with the deal," said John Cerasuolo, the association's president. "We had fallen behind our provincial policing partners to 32nd in the province and it's become very, very competitive over the last few years in the policing profession to attract and retain people."
The force went to arbitration for its last contract in 2019 shortly before Premier Doug Ford's government passed a law, known as Bill 124, which capped wages for public sector workers. OPP officers were caught up in that new law, which did not apply to municipal workers.
That meant other police forces across the province were not subject to the wage cap.Unions challenged Bill 124 in court in 2022, arguing it was unconstitutional.
First a lower court agreed, which was followed this year by a ruling from the Court of Appeal that also found the law unconstitutional.
Shortly after that decision, the province chose not to try to appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada and instead decided to repeal the law entirely.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Title: OPP deal makes officers highest paid cops in province

Document 214 of 500

Are Chinese-made electric vehicles 'spy machines' that pose an 'invisible threat' to Canada? Experts are raising the alarm

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-chinese-made-electric-vehicles-spy-machines/docview/3084675526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+Chinese-made+electric+vehicles+%27spy+machines%27+that+pose+an+%27invisible+threat%27+to+Canada%3F+Experts+are+raising+the+alarm&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is gearing up to slap hefty tariffs on "artificially cheap" Chinese electric vehicles amid mounting concerns the cars also pose a security threat.
With Canada widely expected to impose levies on China-made EVs next month, industry expert Michael Dunne warned "a connected car is a kind of invisible threat because it is hiding in plain sight - right there in our driveway."

"Your cellphone is ... protected by different networks and firewalls. EV networks are not that secure yet. They haven't reached that maturity of a cellphone network, the IT network," she said.
The warnings come after Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland confirmed last month the federal government is studying "the national security aspect ... which is heightened when we're talking about imports of very high-tech vehicles like EVs."
Freeland's comments echoed those of Sir Richard Dearlove, the former head of Britain's MI6 intelligence service, who told CNBC earlier this year that consumers "should be very concerned" about the vehicles.
"There's no question an inexpensive Chinese car has devices under the bonnet, which should concern us. The car could be switched off by the manufacturer," said Dearlove in April.
"How many cars would it take to completely block the circulation of traffic in a major Western city? Malware can be put into the computer in the car and the collection of data right down to bugging the car internally is a possibility," the former spymaster said.
Mirhassani said that lax cybersecurity protocols could be the "hidden cost" of cheap Chinese EVs - unlike those manufactured elsewhere in Asia, North America and Europe.
While the trade implications of affordable Chinese EVs on Canadian roads are well known, Dunne - a former president of GM Indonesia and director of JD Power and Associates in China - said the potential for spyware and malware in the cars is as worrying.
"Some people may think this sounds alarmist, but that's a powerful super computer parked in front of your house," said the San Diego-based CEO of Dunne Insights, a firm with expertise in global EV markets and battery supply chains, and the author of American Wheels, Chinese Roads: The Story of General Motors in China.
With their cameras, the Chinese cars could in theory be repurposed for covert surveillance, collecting data on drivers and their surroundings and even weaponized for co-ordinated cyberattacks.
"These things could be used as mobile spy machines," he said, pointing out that the vehicles could disrupt 5G cell service or Wi-Fi networks.
Unlike Chinese-made smartphones, laptops and Internet-enabled appliances that have been sold in Canada for years, inexpensive mass-market EVs from China are not yet available here.
Concerns over the vehicles come against the backdrop of heightened awareness of foreign interference in Canadian politics by hostile states.
China has always denied it interferes in Canada's domestic affairs.
But the Chinese government understands the potential danger posed by "spy cars" - that's why Beijing has in the past strictly limited where U.S.-engineered Tesla EVs are allowed to go in China.
"The Chinese themselves are acutely aware of the risks. They are restricting where Teslas can drive in China," he said, noting the American-designed EVs were until recently barred from many Chinese highways, military installations, airports, prisons and other infrastructure deemed sensitive.
Even Chinese-made Tesla EVs were impacted until the company promised all data generated from its vehicles would be stored locally in China, meeting Beijing's security requirements.
"There are already 400 million cameras inside China tracking everyone's movements so they don't want (the U.S.) spying on them (with camera-laden American EVs)," said Dunne.
Dan Gittleman, managing partner at SanBoca Insights, an automotive software consultant that advises manufacturers, said the U.S. Commerce Department is considering new restrictions on the EV software imported from China.
"They're looking at barring certain types of software and hardware in any kind of vehicle in addition to the 100 per cent (U.S.) tariff on Chinese EVs," Gittleman said in interview from France.
"But we're playing catch-up," he said.
Canada currently has a six per cent tariff on Chinese EVs - but Ottawa gives those who buy Tesla and Volvos made in China the same $5,000 rebate as it does for purchasers of North American, Korean and European vehicles.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who has been holding consultations with stakeholders that conclude next week, is expected to soon match U.S. President Joe Biden's stiff tariffs on Chinese EVs.
"We're watching closely what the Americans and other allies have done," Trudeau said in June.
It's been more than two months since Biden boosted American levies on the China-built cars from 25 per cent to 100 per cent.
Premier Doug Ford has been urging Trudeau to do the same in order to protect Ontario's burgeoning EV industry from being swamped by inexpensive imports.
"I'm calling on the federal government to immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports, including at least a 100 per cent tariff on Chinese electric vehicles," Ford said June 21.
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles," the premier said at the time.
Dunne said "the 100 per cent tariffs are a showstopper for now, but look for the Chinese to do workarounds" by building their cars in other countries.
He noted that Volvo, a Swedish-based firm wholly owned by China's Geely Holding since 2010, is shifting production of its EX30 SUV to Europe to avoid the 100 per cent duties. American-owned Tesla is expected to supply the Canadian market with more U.S.-made vehicles instead of from its Shanghai factory.
"The tariffs are a good first step, but China has a 10-year head start on (the rest of the world) on EVs ... so we need to sustain that urgency. We are the underdogs."
Trudeau and Ford have earmarked tens of billions of dollars in production subsidies and tax breaks for Stellantis, Volkswagen, Honda and other major manufacturers to invest in Ontario's EV sector.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on an EV strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy, said the Chinese threat is real.
"We know the Chinese don't play by the rules - there's evidence of election interference, covert police operations here and the arbitrary detention of the Michaels," he said, referring the three-year incarceration of Canadians Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor and ongoing concern about Chinese meddling in domestic affairs.
"So to believe that they're going to play by the rules after they've deployed an incredible amount of state-owned technology on Canadian roads is naïve."
CAPTION: A Sealion car by Chinese electric vehicle manufacturer BYD is seen at the Bangkok International Motor Show in Nonthaburi on March 27, 2024.

Subject: Software; Smartphones; Electric vehicles; Roads & highways; Tariffs; Cybersecurity; Automobiles; Malware; Portable computers; Prime ministers
