Document 215 of 500

MPP Sarah Jama's threat of defamation lawsuit against Doug Ford expires

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mpp-sarah-jamas-threat-defamation-lawsuit-against/docview/3084675108/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+Sarah+Jama%27s+threat+of+defamation+lawsuit+against+Doug+Ford+expires&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A threatened $2.5-million libel lawsuit against Premier Doug Ford has fizzled after Independent MPP Sarah Jama and her lawyer failed to serve papers before a mid-July deadline.
An eight-page statement of claim from Jama (Hamilton Centre) was filed with Ontario Superior Court of Justice and dated Jan. 11.
But no further action was taken in the lawsuit, which alleged Ford defamed the former New Democrat MPP in the wake of a statement she made following the surprise Oct. 7 attack on Israel by Hamas and subsequent Israeli retaliation in Gaza.
"The deadline expired and it was never served," Ford lawyer Gavin Tighe said in an email to the Star.
"We offered from the outset, in writing, to accept service. Under the rules, a statement of claim 'shall' be served within six months of issuance," he added.
"That period has now lapsed."
Jama and her lawyer, Stephen Ellis, did not respond Thursday to phone calls and emails seeking comment on the status of the lawsuit.
Ford accused Jama - then a member of the NDP caucus - of "a long and well-documented history of antisemitism" and of "publicly supporting the rape and murder of innocent Jewish people" in an Oct. 11, 2023 statement posted on X, formerly Twitter.
It was a day after she issued a statement on her MPP letterhead calling for an immediate ceasefire in the Israel-Hamas war.
That statement focused on the plight of Palestinians under Israeli occupation and did not mention or condemn the Israeli casualties in the surprise attack by Hamas.
In the statement, Jama described the Palestinian situation as an "apartheid in real time through the continued violation of human rights in Gaza ... for 75 years, violence and retaliation rooted in settler colonialism have taken the lives of far too many innocent people. I call for an immediate ceasefire and de-escalation."
Hours after Ford's statement on X and at the previous urgings of New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles for a retraction of her original statement, Jama issued a clarification and apology.
It stated: "To be clear, I unequivocally condemn terrorism by Hamas on thousands of Israeli civilians. I also believe that Israel's bombardment and siege on civilians in Gaza ... is wrong.
"I understand the pain that many Jewish and Israeli Canadians, including my own constituents, must be feeling. I apologize," her statement continued.
In the statement of claim, Jama called Ford's accusations "false."
Jama also apologized to the Jewish community on the eve of her March 2023 byelection win, when she acknowledged making "harmful" comments about Israel and a "poor choice of words" in a video circulating online.
Stiles ejected Jama from the NDP caucus last Oct. 23 for insubordination on the same day she was censured by a majority vote of Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the legislature, a move that prevents Jama from asking questions of Ford and his cabinet ministers or otherwise speaking in the chamber.
Jama repeatedly declined to comment on her ejection and censure as she left the legislature that day.
In May, a court rejected Jama's push for a judicial review of the censure, saying it had "no jurisdiction to review matters that fall within parliamentary privilege."
Jama called that court decision "disappointing."
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on May 6, 2024.

Document 216 of 500

Loblaws customers' complaints over PC Optimum accounts spark privacy commissioner investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/loblaws-customers-complaints-over-pc-optimum/docview/3084394756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Loblaws+customers%27+complaints+over+PC+Optimum+accounts+spark+privacy+commissioner+investigation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) says it has opened an investigation after receiving "several" complaints against Loblaws from customers alleging that they have not been able to delete their PC Optimum accounts. One of Canada's largest retail loyalty programs, PC Optimum has more than 16 million active annual users, while more than $1 billion worth of points have been redeemed by customers since the program's inception, according to Loblaws' 2023 annual report.The OPC said it could not provide further details about the complaints as the investigation is ongoing. Loblaws spokesperson Catherine Thomas told the Star the company has a process in place to close PC Optimum accounts as requested, "generally within a few weeks and often much sooner than that."During the process, customers will receive an email acknowledging their request, and one confirming the closure. When calls and/or online requests for our customer service team are higher than normal, it may take a little longer for them to respond," she said in an email. "We apologize to anyone who felt the timeline was unreasonable."The move by the privacy commissioner comes after a few rough months on the public relations front for Loblaw Companies Ltd., the nation's largest retailer.In March, the Competition Bureau of Canada opened an investigation into the parent companies of both Loblaws and Sobeys for alleged anti-competitive conduct surrounding leasing agreements that supposedly restrict retail grocery competition from other potential tenants. In June, the bureau said at that time "there is no conclusion of wrongdoing."Then in May, Loblaw stores became the target of a boycott organized by a Reddit group intent on forcing the grocery giant to lower food prices at a time when many Canadians were feeling the pinch of high inflation. PC Optimum was formed in 2018 when Loblaw brought the Shoppers Optimum and PC Plus programs together. The now-popular unified program allows customers to earn and redeem points at nearly 2,500 Loblaw-owned stores across the country, including Shoppers Drug Mart, Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Independent, Zehrs, and Loblaws.When it comes to security of personal information online, it is understandable that people who request their accounts and personal data be deleted "would be disappointed and frankly confused by an organization that wasn't able to do that in a very timely fashion," said Charles Finlay, executive director of the Rogers Cybersecure Catalyst, a hub for innovation and collaboration in cybersecurity at Toronto Metropolitan University. "It is important because of course the more that your private data is held by more entities, the more exposed you are to having that personal information released or exploited in the event of a cybersecurity attack against an entity that holds your data."David Young, a Toronto-based privacy lawyer, says that under federal privacy law, organizations are generally required to obtain consent for collection, use and disclosure of personal information and that individuals can withdraw consent at any time, with some exceptions. "That exists under our current law today and ... if these individuals had a complaint with Loblaws, that would be the basis under the law which that complaint will be analyzed by the privacy commissioner," Young said. He added that current legislation, the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, or PIPEDA, does not have an express right for customers to force organizations to dispose ofpersonal information, known as "the right to be forgotten," but such a right is contained in the current draft of the new Consumer Privacy Protection Act before Parliament.With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Loblaw chairman Galen Weston, who is also chairman and chief executive of George Weston, apologized on behalf of the companies and says "this behaviour should never have happened."

Document 217 of 500

Doug Ford is kicking in millions to speed up the Gardiner? Hold the champagne

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-kicking-millions-speed-up-gardiner/docview/3084394677/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+kicking+in+millions+to+speed+up+the+Gardiner%3F+Hold+the+champagne&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a good chance that someone you love is stuck in traffic on the Gardiner Expressway at this very moment. If you're able to get a message through to them, you can impart some good news: the construction slowing them down should be clear by spring 2026. So they should be home in time for the World Cup.
If that sounds more like a complaint than a reason for celebration, note that it was long planned to be worse. The new projected construction finish is a full year earlier than anticipated, after the provincial government stepped in with $73 million to accelerate construction by moving to round-the-clock shift work and adding more equipment and workers, as announced at city hall Wednesday morning by Mayor Olivia Chow and provincial Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
Two years from now is not soon enough, of course. And this is a situation where a fix literally cannot come soon enough, if the aim is to spare Torontonians agony and frustration and millions of dollars in lost productivity. The current construction situation has seen travel times increase by 250 per cent, as there's a kind of perfect storm of major rebuilding on both the east and west sections of the Gardiner, even while many of the most viable alternative routes are also snarled by long-term roadwork.
All those hours lost. All those commercial deliveries delayed. All that gas burned. For two more years, yet. Sort of dampens the celebration.
Still, a year earlier than planned - roughly a one-third reduction from the wait time we expected before Wednesday - is something to appreciate. And many Ontarians will see it as coming at a bargain cost, relative to the going rate for speeding things up by one year. It wasn't too long ago that this same Doug Ford government announced, to great fanfare, it was spending an amount many estimate will be about $1 billion to get beer and wine into corner stores 12 months ahead of schedule.
Of course, the new timeline for the widespread booze sales is far quicker: we're less than two months away from grabbing a bottle at the Circle K. And maybe there's some sense to that order of operations. Anyone looking to drown their sorrows over commute times will appreciate being able to walk to the nearest corner to pick up some plonk, because driving to a specialized store - like driving anywhere in Toronto - has become an unwelcome adventure, full of hazards and trials and delays.
In the TV show "Fallout," one character explains that the postapocalyptic wasteland they live in has its own Golden Rule: "Thou shalt get sidetracked by bullsh-- every g-dd-mn time." I can only imagine the writers of that show borrowed that line from the daily mutterings of Toronto commuters.
Unfortunately, that same rule seems to apply around here to big infrastructure projects. April 2026 is the plan. But the reality is hard to project at this point.
But this is where we've come to in Toronto after years of saving money by proudly, defiantly refusing to build or maintain anything. Our governments are now spending hand over fist to get subways and LRTs and roadways constructed. They hold press conferences at a furious rate to celebrate their largesse. And yet the dubious immediate payoff is that the problems those projects are designed to solve get worse in the immediate term.
In a city of immigrants, the appropriate attitude of forbearance may come naturally. Sitting in traffic, instead of leaning on the horn or cursing aloud, just whisper to yourself, as generations who have come here seeking a better life have: "I am suffering this in hopes that my children will not have to."
Today, we're $73 million closer to a brighter, faster-moving future for our children. That we are, finally, might be seen as a further dividend of the Olivia Chow-Doug Ford "new deal for Toronto."
As part of that deal, the province takes over responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway, which has the benefit of freeing up $200 million or more per year in the city's budget. My colleague Matt Elliott wrote this week that the city's accounts are already seeing significant benefit from that deal. And we then add this $73 million to the pot.
Because Ford must also have a sudden awareness that he and his gang will own the thing now, and all that road rage people are building up sitting in traffic will soon be directed at the provincial government. When you're looking to get re-elected - maybe one year ahead of schedule, naturally - the bam-BOOZE-lement routine can only take you so far. And in Toronto right now, however far it takes you, you can expect the journey to be long and frustrating.
CAPTION: The $73 million the Ford government on Wednesday gave to speed up Gardiner repairs means they should be done a year earlier than originally planned.

Subject: Traffic

Title: Doug Ford is kicking in millions to speed up the Gardiner? Hold the champagne

Team Canada embroiled in drone spying scandal + Why Canada's interest rate cuts won't affect the real estate market

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, July 25 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a spying scandal rocking Canada's Olympic soccer team, real estate unmoved by the latest interest rate cuts and a deadly "gun battle" in Scarborough.
DON'T MISSCanada Soccer staffer sent home with a suspended prison sentence after allegedly drone spying on New Zealand's practice
Joseph Lombardi, an unaccredited analyst with Canada Soccer, was arrested in France earlier this week at the Paris Olympics after New Zealand women's soccer players spotted a drone zooming over and filming their practice, Mark Colley reports. "Winning the right way is the only way for us," David Shoemaker, CEO of the Canadian Olympic Committee, told reporters. "This is not how Team Canada planned to start these Games."
The consequences: After accepting his eight-month suspended prison sentence, Lombardi and assistant coach Jasmine Mander, an assistant coach acting in a "supervisory capacity" to Lombardi, were sent home immediately. Head coach Bev Priestman, who maintains she knew nothing of the incident, will sit out Canada's opening match against New Zealand Thursday.Bruce Arthur's take: Canada got caught cheating at the Olympics, and decided to take as little responsibility as possible. It's hard to imagine the detail-oriented Priestman was entirely ignorant of the spying going on inside her own team. She has to be fired.Word from New Zealand: Players on New Zealand's team say the off-field scandal "lit a fire in our bellies" ahead of their opening match with Canada Thursday.The Bank of Canada cut interest rates again - but it'll have little effect on the real estate market, experts say
While a welcome reprieve for homeowners and potential buyers, the Bank of Canada's second consecutive key interest rate cut yesterday morning won't impact the real estate market in any "significant way," experts predict. At least a full percentage point drop is needed to stimulate the market, Clarrie Feinstein reports, and the quarter percentage point decrease just doesn't cut it. "Rates are still comparatively high and many potential buyers are waiting for further rate decreases to increase their buying power before they make a move," one expert says. Yet, slowly but surely, analysts believe the market will make its comeback.
Wait, what? Royal LePage is still holding to its forecast of a 10 per cent price jump by the end of this year compared to the fourth quarter of 2023, an increase that could soon see Toronto home prices overtaking Vancouver's.Bad news for: Sellers caught between a rock and a hard place. People like Jeff Steinman, who has dropped his condo's price by $50,000 but is still getting no bites - despite its prime location and three months on the market. "I'm frustrated and confused about why it hasn't sold." Meanwhile: Housing starts were down drastically in June, with reports showing a 60 per cent decline over the same period last year, reflecting the high interest rates and the "macroeconomic environment" catching up with developers."A war zone." Two dead, two seriously injured after what police call a "gun battle" in Scarborough
A man wearing a bright red shirt and shorts burst through the yellow police tape encircling the scene of a grisly gunfight yesterday. "Where is he? Where is he?" he shouted, running through the Scarborough plaza littered with blood and bullet casings. The grieving man later identified himself as Darryl McCann, father of Braydon McCann, one of two people killed in what police are calling a "gun battle." Braydon had just turned 22 on Monday; "this kid's heart is a heart of gold," Darryl told the Star's Calvi Leon, Ilyas Hussein and Nathan Bawaan. Take a look inside the deadly incident.
Background: Gunfire erupted early Wednesday morning in the parking lot of a plaza at Ellesmere and Birkdale Roads. On arrival, police found four people with gunshot wounds; a man died at the scene and a women died in hospital. Two men were rushed to a trauma centre with serious injuries.Word from witnesses: Nichole Gonsalves, who lived in the apartment building next door, was up watching TV when he heard the gunfire that sounded like a "war zone." He came out to a chaotic scene swarmed with emergency vehicles and first responders: "As a parent, I was pretty terrified," he said.WHAT ELSE

Document 219 of 500

Federal documents reveal what happened behind the scenes with Toronto's request to decriminalize drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/federal-documents-reveal-what-happened-behind/docview/3084394479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+documents+reveal+what+happened+behind+the+scenes+with+Toronto%27s+request+to+decriminalize+drugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Health Canada put dozens of questions to Toronto about the practical implications of the city's plans for drug decriminalization, newly released documents reveal, a plan ultimately quashed in the midst of a political firestorm earlier this year.
Records from Health Canada, obtained by the Star under Access to Information law, explain what was happening behind the scenes in the months before the Liberal government rejected the city's application, a move that followed weeks of political pressure during which there were few details about what was going on with Toronto's request.
Against the backdrop of a toxic drug supply killing hundreds of people a year, Toronto had applied for an exemption under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act in 2022 to decriminalize drugs. Exemptions allow for the personal possession of otherwise illegal drugs, an approach that public health advocates say reduces the harms associated with criminalizing people for personal drug use.
Toronto's proposal, sent by Toronto Public Health (TPH), came after B.C. was granted an exemption and launched a three-year pilot project in 2023.
The documents show Health Canada came back within months of Toronto's application with questions including: asking for more information on how potential risks would be mitigated, why no thresholds were set for possession amounts and about the choice not to place age restrictions on the policy.
Toronto updated its application, answered the questions, provided a letter of support from the police and detailed plans on risk mitigation, communications and monitoring and evaluation for the program.
It wasn't enough to pass muster with Health Canada and the federal agency went back to the city.
"Given the very broad request by TPH (i.e. the inclusion of all youth, all substances in the [Controlled Drugs and Substances Act] and no maximum possession amount), Health Canada continues its assessment of the request and levers available to them in this context," the department wrote in a May 12, 2023 letter.
The two sides continued talking, but email exchanges show in the fall of 2023, Toronto told Health Canada it wanted to pivot its focus onto a broader mental health and addictions strategy, and said talks on the drug decriminalization application could resume in 2024.
By then, B.C.'s program was underway and was facing challenges. It began with a more limited scope than Toronto was seeking - B.C. only asked for certain illicit drugs to be included, placed limits on how much a person could possess, and set age limits.
Then, B.C. scaled back the program even further in the fall of 2023 by putting more controls over where the drugs could be used. But stories began emerging about drug use in hospitals placing people at risk, and police also began voicing concerns that their hands were too tightly tied to address public safety concerns around drug use, even though they did broadly support the approach.
Critics of not just the policy - but also the B.C. and federal government more broadly - seized on the issue as a political pressure point, and began demanding Ottawa and the province reverse course.
In April, B.C. asked Ottawa to further curtail its exemption, and during the ensuing political debate, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre argued Ottawa must not just agree with B.C.'s request but also reject Toronto's application.
Confusion then followed about the status of Toronto's request - was it just sitting on Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks' desk unread? Back with the city? Caught in the bureaucratic machine? The Liberal government refused to be clear about it, with Saks referring to the application as dormant, and Health Canada saying they were waiting on information from Toronto, while the city said it had answered Ottawa's concerns.
Meanwhile, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau inferred it didn't matter because if the provincial government wasn't onside, Ottawa wouldn't be either. By this point, Ontario Premier Doug Ford had come out against the idea.
Who Health Canada was corresponding with directly in Toronto is redacted from the records obtained by the Star.
But the records show that as the political hyperbole around Toronto's application hit a fever pitch in early May, a senior Health Canada official met a Toronto official in person, and then followed up with an email.
"Further to that conversation and public statements made, I'm hoping we could find a few minutes to connect in the next day or so," Jennifer Saxe, an associate assistant deputy minister at Health Canada, wrote to a Toronto counterpart on May 13.
On May 17, Saks announced Toronto's application was denied.
CAPTION: Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks rejected Toronto's request to decriminalize the possession of controlled drugs, citing concerns about public safety.

Document 220 of 500

Gardiner gets a fast track: Province kicks in $73 million to speed up expressway repairs, but expect gridlock to remain until 2026

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gardiner-gets-fast-track/docview/3084198533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gardiner+gets+a+fast+track%3A+Province+kicks+in+%2473+million+to+speed+up+expressway+repairs%2C+but+expect+gridlock+to+remain+until+2026&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Traffic-clogging construction on the Gardiner Expressway could end at least one year earlier than originally planned, thanks to a funding injection from the provincial government.
Drivers will still be suffering through gridlock for another two years or so.
But at a news conference at city hall on Wednesday, Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria joined Mayor Olivia Chow to announce Queen's Park would spend up to $73 million to shave at least 12 months off the project, which since it began in April has been causing maddening bottlenecks on the highway and some downtown roads.
The new funding moves up the estimated date of completion from April 2027 to April 2026. The province says it's providing the money - which will pay for measures like additional workers, equipment and modified construction techniques - on the condition crews are allowed to work 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
The funding will also support decongestion efforts like modifying the Jameson on-ramp, opening a left-turn lane on Spadina Avenue from eastbound Lake Shore Boulevard and "relaxing" overnight lane closures.
Sarkaria said he knows first-hand how frustrating the construction has been for the 140,000 drivers who use the Gardiner every day. The MPP for Brampton South said it's added 40 minutes to his commute to Queen's Park.
He predicted accelerating the work would benefit the province's economy by $273 million by "getting drivers and goods out of gridlock a year faster than planned."
Premier Doug Ford's government "will continue to work with the city in accelerating this critical project as we work to make life easier and more convenient for drivers in every corner of our province," he said.
Chow said she understands road work is "painful," which is why the city has been consulting with experts on how to bring forward the completion date for the Gardiner repairs. Wednesday's announcement "cuts out a whole year long of congestion," she said, adding "we're excited and very grateful for this partnership."
The project has reduced the Gardiner from three to two lanes in each direction between Strachan Avenue and Dufferin Street, part of a wider plan to repair the 60-year-old partly elevated highway.
This phase was expected to cost $300 million, and requires replacing 700 metres of concrete deck and girders and restoring the highway's substructure.
Although the work is necessary to extend the life of the highway, according to one study it has increased travel times on the Gardiner by 250 per cent during morning rush hour.
Chow has been under pressure from critics on council to speed up work on the expressway, which last year the province agreed to take ownership of.
Although council voted unanimously Wednesday to approve the acceleration plan, Brad Bradford (Ward 19, Beaches-East York) argued it would have been cheaper to have streamlined the process from the outset using a competitive bidding process, instead of making changes "midstream."
Speeding up the work required negotiating new terms with Grascan Construction, which was awarded the Gardiner contract in October 2023.
Bradford called the project "a total disaster."
"Frankly, we've had a real lack of leadership," he said, asserting that getting the project finished sooner should have been one of Chow's priorities when she took office last July.
But the original budget for it was approved last year under former mayor John Tory, countered Coun. Paula Fletcher (Ward 14, Toronto-Danforth). "I think we should step back and try not to politicize this with mudslinging," she said.
City staff reported that their original plan for this phase, which went to tender in 2022, was based on considerations like noise restrictions, and "the financial challenges facing the city at the time."
Until now, excessive noise has been restricted on most of the project between 11 p.m. and 7 a.m. City staff say they will monitor volume levels of the overnight work allowed under the acceleration plan using real-time sensors, and address concerns quickly.
According to the report that went to council Wednesday, under the new plan, Grascan has proposed finishing most of the reconstruction by April 15, 2026, weeks before Toronto is set to host the FIFA World Cup. After the tournament, the company would return to the site for two weekends of median work and final paving.
Despite the minister and mayor's pronouncements, however, the report warns that achieving the pre-World Cup timeline is "contingent on many factors, including weather, constraints, resources and approvals."
If the work isn't done in time, Grascan would switch to a "fallback target" and reopen the expressway around the World Cup, then return to complete the job before the contract's milestone date of April 2027.
Sarkaria, whose ministry has struggled to keep major projects like the Eglinton Crosstown LRT on schedule, said there will be financial consequences to the contractor for missing the new Gardiner deadlines.
"If those timelines are not met, they're not going to get the incentivization payments to complete this project," he warned.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Subject: Councils; Funding; Roads & highways; Economic impact; Urban development; Traffic congestion

Document 221 of 500

Proceed with caution on Ford's cash infusion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/proceed-with-caution-on-fords-cash-infusion/docview/3084195008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Proceed+with+caution+on+Ford%27s+cash+infusion&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a good chance that someone you love is stuck in traffic on the Gardiner Expressway at this very moment. If you're able to get a message through to them, you can impart some good news: the construction slowing them down should be clear by spring 2026. So they should be home in time for the World Cup.
If that sounds more like a complaint than a reason for celebration, note that it was long planned to be worse.
The new projected construction finish is a full year earlier than anticipated after the provincial government stepped in with $73 million to accelerate construction by moving to round-the-clock shift work and adding more equipment and workers, as announced at city hall Wednesday morning by Mayor Olivia Chow and provincial Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
Two years from now is not soon enough, of course. And this is a situation where a fix literally cannot come soon enough if the aim is to spare Torontonians agony and frustration and millions of dollars in lost productivity.
The current construction situation has seen travel times increase by 250 per cent, as there's a kind of perfect storm of major rebuilding on both the east and west sections of the Gardiner, even while many of the most viable alternative routes are also snarled by long-term roadwork.
All those hours lost. All those commercial deliveries delayed. All that gas burned. For two more years, yet. Sort of dampens the celebration.
Still, a year earlier than planned - roughly a one-third reduction from the wait time we expected before Wednesday - is something to appreciate. And many Ontarians will see it as coming at a bargain cost, relative to the going rate for speeding things up by one year.
It wasn't too long ago that this same Doug Ford government announced, to great fanfare, that it was spending an amount many estimate will be about $1 billion to get beer and wine into corner stores 12 months ahead of schedule.
Of course, the new timeline for the widespread booze sales is far quicker: we're less than two months away from grabbing a bottle at the Circle K. And maybe there's some sense to that order of operations.
Anyone looking to drown their sorrows over commute times will appreciate being able to walk to the nearest store to pick up some plonk because driving to a specialized store - like driving anywhere in Toronto - has become an unwelcome adventure, full of hazards and trials and delays.
In the TV show "Fallout," one character explains that the postapocalyptic wasteland they live in has its own Golden Rule: "Thou shalt get sidetracked by bulls--t every goddamn time."
I can only imagine the writers of that show borrowed that line from the daily mutterings of Toronto commuters. Unfortunately, that same rule seems to apply around here to big infrastructure projects. April 2026 is the plan. But the reality is hard to project at this point.
But this is where we've come to in Toronto after years of saving money by proudly, defiantly refusing to build or maintain anything.
Our governments are now spending hand over fist to get subways and LRTs and roadways constructed. They hold press conferences at a furious rate to celebrate their largesse. And yet the dubious immediate payoff is that the problems those projects are designed to solve get worse in the immediate term.
In a city of immigrants, the appropriate attitude of forbearance may come naturally.
Sitting in traffic, instead of leaning on the horn or cursing aloud, just whisper to yourself, as generations who have come here seeking a better life have: "I am suffering this in hopes that my children will not have to."
Today, we're $73 million closer to a brighter, faster-moving future for our children. That we are, finally, might be seen as a further dividend of the Olivia Chow-Doug Ford "new deal for Toronto."
As part of that deal, the province takes over responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway, which has the benefit of freeing up $200 million or more per year in the city's budget.
My colleague Matt Elliott wrote this week that the city's accounts are already seeing significant benefit from that deal. And we then add this $73 million to the pot.
Because Ford must also have a sudden awareness that he and his gang will own the thing now, and all that road rage people are building up sitting in traffic will soon be directed at the provincial government.
When you're looking to get re-elected - maybe one year ahead of schedule, naturally - the bam-BOOZE-lement routine can only take you so far. And in Toronto right now, however far it takes you, you can expect your journey to be a long and frustrating one.
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Subject: Construction costs; Public transportation; Urban development; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways

Document 222 of 500

PC Optimum under investigation: Complaints from Loblaws customers spark privacy commissioner's probe into loyalty program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pc-optimum-under-investigation/docview/3084194899/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PC+Optimum+under+investigation%3A+Complaints+from+Loblaws+customers+spark+privacy+commissioner%27s+probe+into+loyalty+program&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) says it has opened an investigation after receiving "several" complaints against Loblaws from customers alleging that they have not been able to delete their PC Optimum accounts.
One of Canada's largest retail loyalty programs, PC Optimum has more than 16 million active annual users, while more than $1 billion worth of points have been redeemed by customers since the program's inception, according to Loblaws' 2023 annual report.
The OPC said it could not provide further details about the complaints, as the investigation is ongoing.
Loblaws spokesperson Catherine Thomas told the Star the company has a process in place to close PC Optimum accounts as requested, "generally within a few weeks and often much sooner than that.
"During the process, customers will receive an email acknowledging their request, and one confirming the closure. When calls and/or online requests for our customer service team are higher than normal, it may take a little longer for them to respond," she said in an email. "We apologize to anyone who felt the timeline was unreasonable."
The move by the privacy commissioner comes after a few rough months on the public relations front for Loblaw Companies Ltd., the nation's largest retailer.
In March, the Competition Bureau of Canada opened an investigation into the parent companies of both Loblaws and Sobeys for alleged anti-competitive conduct surrounding leasing agreements that supposedly restrict retail grocery competition from other potential tenants. In June, the bureau said at that time "there is no conclusion of wrongdoing."
Then in May, Loblaw stores became the target of a boycott organized by a Reddit group intent on forcing the grocery giant to lower food prices at a time when many Canadians were feeling the pinch of high inflation.
PC Optimum was formed in 2018 when Loblaw brought the Shoppers Optimum and PC Plus programs together. The now-popular unified program allows customers to earn and redeem points at nearly 2,500 Loblaw-owned stores across the country, including Shoppers Drug Mart, Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Independent, Zehrs and Loblaws.
When it comes to security of personal information online, it is understandable that people who request their accounts and personal data be deleted "would be disappointed and frankly confused by an organization that wasn't able to do that in a very timely fashion," said Charles Finlay, executive director of the Rogers Cybersecure Catalyst, a hub for innovation and collaboration in cybersecurity at Toronto Metropolitan University.
"It is important because of course the more that your private data is held by more entities, the more exposed you are to having that personal information released or exploited in the event of a cybersecurity attack against an entity that holds your data."
David Young, a Toronto-based privacy lawyer, says that under federal privacy law, organizations are generally required to obtain consent for collection, use and disclosure of personal information and that individuals can withdraw consent at any time, with some exceptions.
"That exists under our current law today and ... if these individuals had a complaint with Loblaws, that would be the basis under the law which that complaint will be analyzed by the privacy commissioner," Young said.
With files from
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

Document 223 of 500

Is the King's Counsel dead - again? One year on from patronage scandal, Ford government silent on future of controversial lawyers' title

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-kings-counsel-dead-again-one-year-on-patronage/docview/3084008092/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+the+King%27s+Counsel+dead+-+again%3F+One+year+on+from+patronage+scandal%2C+Ford+government+silent+on+future+of+controversial+lawyers%27+title&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is the King's Counsel designation for lawyers - revived by the provincial government last year, only to spark a patronage scandal - once again dead?It's been just over a year since Premier Doug Ford promised to "fix" a secretive process that saw the awarding of the K.C. title to dozens of Tory cabinet ministers, MPPs, political staff, donors and other party loyalists.Amid public outcry, Attorney General Doug Downey's office said last July that, going forward, there would be a public application process to receive the designation, which does little else but grant lawyers the privilege of adding the letters K.C. next to their names.Despite legal organizations providing Downey with suggestions, there is still no sign of a new process, or even any public indication that the government remains interested in handing out the designation.Downey's office did not return the Star's repeated requests for comment this month."It would be nice if the government for once could admit that they made a mistake and acted without all the information," said Ottawa criminal defence lawyer Michael Spratt."Or, if you think that the K.C. program is such a vital and important core function that you need to be involved in," Spratt said of the government, "maybe follow through with what you're going to do and tell the truth about what your intentions are."In 1985, the Liberal government of David Peterson stopped handing out the designation - when it was known as Queen's Counsel during the reign of Queen Elizabeth II - because it had become "corrupted" and "lost all meaning," Peterson told the Star last year. (Peterson, a K.C. himself, is the chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.)The backlash was swift after the Ford government announced the first 91 recipients last year on the Friday afternoon of the Canada Day long weekend. While many individuals were closely linked to the Tories - including Downey himself and his predecessor, Caroline Mulroney - new K.C.s also included senior bureaucrats and the head of the Ontario Human Rights Commission.Further controversy followed after the Star revealed that Mulroney, who was Transportation Minister at the time, was called to the Ontario bar just three days before the government's announcement, in order to qualify for her K.C. Until that point, Mulroney had only ever been licensed to practise in New York, even during her time as Ontario's attorney general.She was called to the bar thanks to an amendment the Tories made to the Barristers Act that allowed for former provincial attorneys general to bypass the usual licensing requirements of the legal regulator, the Law Society of Ontario, including examinations. The elected head of the Law Society at the time, Jacqueline Horvat, told the Star that she declined the government's offer of a K.C. due to the lack of selection criteria. (Several Law Society board members did, however, accept the offer.)The issue dogged Ford for days, as he tried to distance himself and declare that he did not see a list of recipients. He finally stated that the government would "fix" the process."I still think there are legitimate questions to be asked about the whole idea of the government being involved in designating a class of 'super-lawyers,'" said University of Ottawa law professor Amy Salyzyn, who specializes in legal ethics."But, if the government decides that it must go down this road, having public and non-partisan criteria and processes are clearly in the public interest."There has been no shortage of suggestions on how to improve the process.The Advocates' Society, the national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, told Downey in a letter last November that the primary criterion for the designation should be professional excellence, and that the government should explicitly state that a lawyer's political affiliation will play no role in whether they get a K.C.A public nomination process should involve an independent, non-partisan advisory committee to screen applications, the society said, noting that similar committees in other provinces include judges, lawyers and sometimes members of the public.The society referred in their letter to the patronage concerns that led to the Liberals' decision to stop handing out the title in the 1980s. "If the designation is permitted to fall into such disrepute again, public confidence in the legal profession will be undermined," wrote then-president Dominique Hussey.The Federation of Ontario Law Associations also pushed for a depoliticized process, and suggested to Downey that priority should be given to lawyers "who have applied their skills in the course of reputable civic engagement," with a special emphasis on volunteer work.The federation also proposed that lawyers in certain positions automatically receive the title, including MPPs, MPs, heads of tribunals and senior judges.The federation's chair told the Star that he's surprised at the lack of clarity from the government one year later, having heard nothing aside from an acknowledgement that the federation's proposals were received."Maybe they looked at everything they received and said 'there isn't really support in the legal community for this, and maybe we should move on,'" federation chair Douglas Judson told the Star."But I'd be surprised they wouldn't just come out and say that."
CAPTION: Ontario Attorney General Doug Downey is seen here in an Oct. 24, 2023, file photo.

Subject: Patronage; Attorneys general; Scandals

Document 224 of 500

Province to give Toronto $73 million to speed up Gardiner construction - with one condition

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-give-toronto-73-million-speed-up/docview/3084008040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+give+Toronto+%2473+million+to+speed+up+Gardiner+construction+-+with+one+condition&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Traffic-clogging construction on the Gardiner Expressway could end at least one year earlier than originally planned, thanks to a funding injection from the provincial government.
Drivers will still have to endure the gridlock for another two years or so. But at a press conference at city hall on Wednesday, Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria joined Mayor Olivia Chow to announce Queen's Park would spend up to $73 million to shave at least 12 months off the project, which since it began in April has been causing maddening bottlenecks on the highway and some downtown roads.
The estimated completion date for the work had been pegged for April 2027. The new funding shortens that timeline to April 2026. The province says it is providing the money - which will pay for measures like additional workers, equipment and modified construction techniques - on the condition crews are allowed to work up to 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
Sarkaria said he knows firsthand how frustrating the construction has been for the 140,000 drivers who use the Gardiner every day. The MPP for Brampton South said it's added 40 minutes to his commute to Queen's Park.
He predicted accelerating the work would benefit the province's economy by $273 million by "getting drivers and goods out of gridlock a year faster than planned."
Premier Doug Ford's government "will continue to work with the city in accelerating this critical project as we work to make life easier and more convenient for drivers in every corner of our province," he said.
Chow said she understands road work is "painful," which is why the city has been consulting with experts on how to bring forward the completion date for the Gardiner repairs. Wednesday's announcement "cuts out a whole year long of congestion," she said, adding "we're excited and very grateful for this partnership."
In addition to speeding up construction, the funding agreement will support decongestion efforts like modifying the Jameson on-ramp, opening a left-turn lane on Spadina Avenue from eastbound Lake Shore Boulevard, and "relaxing" overnight lane closures.
Although overnight construction will be allowed under the acceleration plan, Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, who is local councillor for the area, said "the commitment has been made to ensure that folks are able to get a good night's rest."
"We'll stay on top of ensuring that any noise issues are addressed quickly," said Malik (Ward 10, Spadina-Fort York).
Until now, there have been noise restrictions in place on the project between 11 p.m. and 7 a.m., with exceptions for the section of highway above the Exhibition GO Station and TTC streetcar loop, which can only be worked on at night to minimize impact on transit service.
For the past three months, the Gardiner has been reduced from three to two lanes in each direction between Strachan Avenue and Dufferin Street for major rehabilitation work, part of a wider project to repair the 60-year-old partly elevated highway.
This phase was expected to cost $300 million, and requires replacing 700 metres of concrete deck and girders, restoring the highway's substructure and installing new lighting.
Although the work is necessary to extend the life of the highway, it's been a major headache for drivers. According to one study, travel times on the Gardiner have increased by 250 per cent during morning rush hour.
Chow has been under pressure from councillors and the public to speed up work on the expressway, which last year the province agreed to take ownership of. She said earlier this month she had been meeting with Sarkaria to hash out a plan.
The city awarded the contract for the work to Grascan Construction in 2023, and accelerating it required negotiating new terms with the company.
Sarkaria, whose ministry has struggled to keep major projects like the Eglinton Crosstown LRT on schedule, said there will be financial penalties to the contractor for missing the new Gardiner deadlines.
"If those timelines are not met, they're not going to get the incentivization payments to complete this project," he said.
According to a report that went before council Wednesday morning, under the new plan, Grascan has proposed finishing most of the reconstruction by April 15, 2026, weeks before Toronto is set to host the FIFA World Cup. After the tournament, the company would return to the site for two weekends of median work and final paving.
Despite the minister and mayor's pronouncements however, the report warns that achieving the pre-World Cup timeline is "is contingent on many factors, including weather, constraints, resources and approvals."
If the work isn't done in time, Grascan would switch to a "fallback target" and reopen the expressway around the World Cup, then return to complete the job by the contract's milestone date of April 2027.
CAPTION: The restoration of the Gardiner Expressway between Dufferin and Strachan continues with the highway reduced to two lanes each way in Toronto.

Document 225 of 500

Doug Ford adds to confusion surrounding plans for Ontario Place's contentious parking garage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-adds-confusion-surrounding-plans/docview/3083998331/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+adds+to+confusion+surrounding+plans+for+Ontario+Place%27s+contentious+parking+garage&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As city and provincial officials start to negotiate the fate of one of the most controversial parts of the Ontario Place remake, the premier is suggesting a contentious new option for creating thousands of parking spots at the treasured waterfront site.
Doug Ford's government originally suggested a multi-level underground lot at Ontario Place to accommodate motorists visiting a planned new spa resort and expanded Budweiser Stage concert venue.
Last fall, as part of the new financial deal for Toronto, Ford agreed to put the lot at neighbouring city-owned Exhibition Place, subject to negotiations between city and provincial representatives. Visitors to a relocated Ontario Science Centre would also use the lot.
Asked Monday if he is rethinking an underground lot, given the recent massive flooding of Lake Shore Boulevard that separates Ontario Place and Exhibition Place, Ford seemed to float what would be the most controversial option yet - an above-ground lot near the Ontario Place waterfront.
"We haven't confirmed the three underground parking (levels) because it costs so much to build underground," Ford said. "We'd like to build as much on top without prohibiting the view," he told reporters. "Stay tuned on the parking lot. We're working with the city as well - maybe we'll put it on the other side."
Ford's comments injected further uncertainty into a key element of the redevelopment plan that has been shrouded in secrecy. Even with early construction underway at the site, the location, size, cost and form of the parking facility remain undecided.
The idea of a towering new parkade on the provincially owned site is being panned by Ontario Place for All, an activist group fighting Ford's plans to grant Austrian spa company Therme a 95-year lease. The group argues the shuttered amusement park should remain fully public and as natural as possible, including Lake Ontario views.
"An overground parking lot would be an absolute blight at Ontario Place," said the group's co-chair Norman Di Pasquale.
"Imagine a five-storey lot, maybe with the Science Centre on top? You're not going to be able to see the pods or the Cinesphere at all," he said, referring to the site's landmark buildings. "We oppose any new parking structure there."
The premier's newly expressed aversion to the cost of burying a parkade near the waterfront could be a sticking point in the city-provincial talks on the parking lot that are just getting underway as part of the "new deal" agreement announced by Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow last November.
Two sources with knowledge of the talks, but not authorized to speak, said provincial officials seem to want the big provincially funded parkade - if it's built at Exhibition Place - to be above ground.
But the city's guiding principles, as decided by the Exhibition Place board chaired by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, state that the parking solution must "be completely underground to ensure highest and best use of land."
In a statement, Malik said the province must front not only construction costs if it is built at Exhibition Place but also "long-term costs such as the impact on Exhibition Place and CNE operations."
Any new garage must also not hurt the city's revenue generation from existing parking lots or Toronto's ambitious target to become a carbon-neutral city by 2040, in part by encouraging alternatives to driving.
Ford's office, asked for an update on plans for the lot, said only that city-provincial discussions are at a "preliminary" stage and no decisions have been made.
A spokesperson for Chow, who agreed to stop fighting Ford's Ontario Place redevelopment plan as part of the new deal, and who welcomed the proposal to put the parkade at Exhibition Place, said: "There are many options and outcomes for the Ontario Place garage. Talks are ongoing between city staff, the province and our office."
The parking plans have been shrouded in mystery since Ford announced the surprise redevelopment in 2021. Early talk of the lot being built deep underground on a man-made island for private-sector tenants, potentially costing taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars, triggered fierce criticism.
Ontario's auditor general pierced provincial secrecy on the plans last December, revealing in a report that the province's agreement with Therme requires Ontario to provide the firm with dedicated parking spots within 650 metres of the spa entrance or face a financial penalty.
The spa, concert venue and Science Centre are expected to require 2,700 parking spots, or 2,000 more than currently exist at Ontario Place. Ontario's infrastructure ministry pegged the cost of building the new spots underground at $307 million.
The auditor general found that the requirement to build new parking was one reason behind the Ford government's belated decision to move the Science Centre to the waterfront from its existing Don Mills site, "in order to dispel public/stakeholder concerns relating to cost and impact on the environment."
Ford defended the relocation on Monday, saying: "We're building a brand spanking new state-of-the-art (facility), more exhibit space in the right location - not in a sleepy little neighbourhood in the suburbs that no one goes to.
"(Downtown) is where all the action is - where the soccer is, and the stadium and LiveNation, the Exhibition - so it's just common sense, a no-brainer."
It's Ford's comments that lack brains, the co-chair of Save Ontario Science Centre told the Star in an email.
Jason Ash said that more than 50 million Ontarians who have visited the interactive science museum since it opened in Flemingdon Park in 1969 "wholeheartedly disagree" with the premier.
Ash called the neighbourhood "one of the most densely-populated neighbourhoods in Toronto, and in the country," with an additional 27,000 homes planned or under construction.
"Inclusive of Mr. Ford's two subway expansions, Flemingdon Park is attracting a lot of investment for a sleepy little neighbourhood in the suburbs that no one goes to."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Even with construction underway at Ontario Place, the location, size, cost and form of its new parking lot remain undecided.

What's going on with Ontario Place's massive parking lot? + GTA home sales continue to spiral

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+going+on+with+Ontario+Place%27s+massive+parking+lot%3F+%2B+GTA+home+sales+continue+to+spiral&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, July 24 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a massive parking lot at the new Ontario Place, plummeting GTA home sales and the final moments before a deadly triple shooting.
DON'T MISSWhat's going on with Ontario Place's massive parking lot? Doug Ford suggests a controversial new option
Even as construction begins at the Ontario Place remake, one of its most controversial features remains shrouded in secrecy - where will its thousands of visitors park at the prestigious waterfront site? Premier Doug Ford previously suggested a multi-level underground lot, but floated a far more controversial above-ground parkade near the waterfront to reporters on Monday, David Rider reports. Critics are already panning the concept as "an absolute blight at Ontario Place." When asked for an update, Ford's office only said no decisions had been made.
Go deeper: Insiders tell the Star provincial authorities seem to want the taxpayer-funded parkade above ground but Toronto's guiding principles state the parking solution must "be completely underground to ensure highest and best use of land."Meanwhile: Construction on some of the controversial Ontario Place rebuild, which would see new additions of a $350 million waterpark and spa on Toronto's West Island, has been temporarily paused after a group hoping to preserve the site won a legal hearing to challenge the redevelopment.David Olive's take: Power brokers like Ford live to make big statements, which are often vanity projects. As his state-sponsored vandalism of the West Island proceeds, we should stop and assess before committing to megaprojects we may live to regret.Sales of new GTA homes were already at a record low last year. Now, they're more than 50 per cent lower
Toronto's housing market has continued to plummet, with year-to-date sales of new homes dropping to less than half of the previous record-low seen in 2023, a new report finds. Just over 1,100 new homes were sold last month - almost 60 per cent less than the 10-year average, Manuela Vega reports. "What we're seeing today in terms of sales is a direct reflection of the interest rates impacting the ability for a homeowner to carry those costs," experts say. At the same time, building costs are skyrocketing.
Bad news for: Condo owners trapped amidst a "catastrophic" 57 per cent drop in sales, hitting lows the new condominium market hasn't seen in 27 years.Silver lining: The GTA's luxury housing market has remained "surprisingly active" in defiance of high interest rates and a stagnating economy, a report this morning finds, although properties over $1 million are still struggling to sell.What else: Ontario's cottage market has remained "very quiet" despite a Bank of Canada rate cut in June. Unless rates drop further in the BoC's update this morning, experts say prices could soon plummet by as much as 10 per cent."The voices are still in my head." Anguished witness recounts the final moments before deadly triple shooting
For the past month, Shahrokh Biniaz has been haunted by the final moments before the bloodshed inside his office building. The sound of the first gunshot. The victims pleading for their lives. The call to 911 and the final spree of bullets that left three people - including two of his long-time colleagues and close friends - dead. "I think about it all the time," Biniaz tells the Star's Calvi Leon. In an exclusive interview, the lone witness to the triple shooting is making public his account of what transpired. Here's what he saw.
Context: On a warm June afternoon, an angry client - Alan Kats, 46 - barged into the North York office and confronted businessman Arash Missaghi, 54, and his associate, Samira Yousefi, 44. Biniaz heard his friends begging for their lives before fleeing the building to phone 911.Moments later: Biniaz rushed through the backdoor and called police. Almost immediately, an officer arrived, who radioed colleagues before entering the building alone. Three more cruisers arrived on scene, believed to be visible to the gunman, before the fatal gunshots rang out, he said.Wait, what? However, police dispute Biniaz's recollection of events, saying there were no reports of the solo officer entering the building alone nor of any gunshots after the additional officers arrived.WHAT ELSE

Document 227 of 500

Migrant workers are suffering in the unprecedented Ontario heat. Doug Ford needs to do more to protect them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/migrant-workers-are-suffering-unprecedented/docview/3083998250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Migrant+workers+are+suffering+in+the+unprecedented+Ontario+heat.+Doug+Ford+needs+to+do+more+to+protect+them&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Harris far from 'unpopular'
Kamala Harris smashes fundraising record with stunning $81 million haul over 24 hours, July 22
While it's heartening that the Kamala Harris campaign fundraised the largest 24-hour sum reported by either side in the 2024 campaign, it is a shame that the American electoral playing field requires so much money that candidates in both major parties are beholden to elite donors. That being said, it is poetic justice that Donald Trump, who has treated women and people of colour badly all his life, now has to face off with a mixed-race female prosecutor.
Ron Charach, Toronto
The letter writer who referred to Kamala Harris as "unpopular" should check the facts. Within 24 hours of her being named as the Democratic choice to replace Joe Biden, a record amount of $81 million was collected in 24 hours. As well, almost 30,000 new volunteers signed up to assist her campaign to defeat Donald Trump. This doesn't sound unpopular to me.
Gary Slippoy, Toronto
You know who would be an awesome vice-president pick for Kamala Harris? Liz Cheney. It would be a unity ticket. It worked for Abraham Lincoln, a Republican, who picked Andrew Johnson of the Democratic Party. Together they formed the National Union Party.
Glen Loucks, Toronto
Harris should follow Biden's lead
History will look very kindly on Joe Biden's presidency. The first half of his term were years of great accomplishment. As Ronald Reagan's policies bred the now-defunct Reagan Republicans for over 30 years, the Democratic Party would be well served to closely follow Biden's agenda. Kamala Harris and the party must have an economic policy that builds from the bottom-up and middle-out, is forward looking in job creation, unflinching in support of organized labour and assigns fair taxation on the highest income earners and large corporations. Expand health care coverage, reduce drug costs and introduce social programmes such as child care and parental leave. Reinstate and protect women's reproductive health rights. Continue to advance gun controls and an automatic weapons ban. Reintroduce the Donald Trump-blocked border control legislation and launch real immigration reform. Adopt a recently-announced plan to reform a dysfunctional Supreme Court. These are all popular, positive and winning strategies, favoured by a large majority of our American neighbours. If well-communicated and followed, Democrats will find success this November and for years to come.
Mike Brown, Burlington
Highlighting Harris' background isn't the right strategy
After all the trouble that the Democrats took to convince Joe Biden to step down, they seem to be intent on losing to Donald Trump anyway. They are endorsing a strategy focusing on Kamala Harris as a former prosecutor who will turn the public away from electing a felon. Trump has been under attack by prosecutors for over two years now for many criminal charges. That was a nothing burger, as the public is quite used to the idea that he has kept up practices like hiding income and evading taxes. They have heard all about the other crimes, and Trump is still leading. The fact that Harris is a former prosecutor won't be her ticket to victory.
There are plenty of other things she can focus on. She could talk about the cap on insulin costs. She could talk about the near-full employment levels reached under Democrats. She could talk about the importance of Ukraine. She could talk about women's rights and Roe v. Wade. She could promise a tough policy on immigration before Trump can articulate one. She could talk about the $15 minimum wage. She could talk about how the Supreme Court would support even more redistricting to deny voting rights for Black Americans. She could talk about how a Trump presidency would start undoing all of these achievements and giving more tax breaks to the rich.
Venkat Krishnan, Ajax, Ont.
Migrant workers need same protections as Canadians and permanent residents
Apples are treated better than some migrant farm workers, July 20
As Ontario adapts to higher summer temperatures, migrant farm workers are particularly vulnerable to heat waves. While here on temporary work permits, many are afraid to speak up for better working conditions, as their employer can easily replace them. Migrant farm workers have closed work permits, tying them to their employer. If they have poor working conditions or poor housing, they cannot change jobs.
The article states that Premier Doug Ford expects employers to create their own standards for migrant workers and is failing to mandate safe work conditions. We rely heavily on migrant workers to grow our food. Most of us live near farmland, migrant workers live and work near us.
All of us are struggling with the heat waves this summer. Luckily, many of us who work outside are allowed to take breaks and sit in the shade thanks to the labour laws that protect Ontario residents. The same protections and rights as Canadians and permanent residents who work in Ontario must be given to migrant farm workers.
Julia Deveau, Oxford County, Ella Haley, Brant County, Ashley Perry, Brantford, Lesley Pinto, Hamilton, Ouin Arndt, Brant County, Richard Tunstall, Brant County and Linda Abbas, Hamilton
Breast cancer screening should happen sooner
Shannen Doherty, 'Beverly Hills, 90210' star, dies at 53, July 15
Thank you for pointing out that actor Shannen Doherty died from breast cancer at age 53 after she was originally diagnosed in her 40s. Canadian women, health care providers and scientists are currently engaged in a fight to have breast cancer screening available to women in their 40s. The Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care (CTFPH) wants to recommend that screening start at age 50, which is at odds with the U.S. guidelines and with a majority of provincial recommendations. Modelling data shows that cancers arising in women in their 40s are responsible for 25 per cent of the years of life lost to breast cancer. I believe the CTFPH needs to be dismantled. Canadian women deserve to have guidelines that focus on saving lives and encourages them to take action that might save their own.
Robin Alter, Toronto
CAPTION: Vice President Kamala Harris arrives at her campaign headquarters in Wilmington, Del., on Monday.

Document 228 of 500

LCBO stores may be short on some items: Shortages due to strike not expected to last, expert says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-stores-may-be-short-on-some-items/docview/3083836326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+stores+may+be+short+on+some+items%3A+Shortages+due+to+strike+not+expected+to+last%2C+expert+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a cheery "welcome back, everybody," the doors opened and the first strike in LCBO history was finally, irrevocably over.
As smiling staff greeted eager customers at the LCBO's College and Bathurst location for the first time in more than two weeks Tuesday morning, it was hard to tell who was happier.
"Good to see you guys again," one customer exclaimed, to happy nods from staff. "We're glad to be back," came the reply.
"We're a little low on a few things right now. We're supposed to be getting a delivery today at some point," another staffer explained apologetically to one restaurant manager looking for their usual brands of tequila.
Some popular products - Jameson Irish whiskey, Tequila Tromba - were nowhere to be seen in a store that was busier than usual for a Tuesday morning.
At the LCBO's flagship store on Queens Quay, several rows of bottles were put on the shelf backwards, as a way, said staff, of indicating that the product normally on that shelf was out of stock.
"Why are some of those price things upside down," another customer asked.
"That means that's out of stock too, at least for now," came the reply.
On Sunday, 9,000 LCBO workers voted to ratify a tentative agreement finalized early Saturday between the provincial liquor monopoly and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
The strike began July 5, with OPSEU raising concerns over the provincial government's plans to expand alcohol retailing.
Now that it's over, customers might notice a few things missing at first, but likely not for too long, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson.
"I think we'll see them back on track pretty quickly," said Hutcheson, managing director at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group. "I was actually pretty surprised how little stuff was missing at the store I went too."
Meanwhile, OPSEU's chief negotiator slammed Ontario Premier Doug Ford Tuesday, saying the premier was wrong when he said the LCBO doesn't make money from its retailing operations.
"It's clear that Doug Ford has no idea where LCBO revenues come from," OPSEU's Colleen MacLeod said. "He claimed that the LCBO doesn't make money through retail sales, and repeatedly said that "we make money on wholesaling."
MacLeod said its retail operations account for 79.4 per cent of the LCBO's revenues, citing the agency's 2023 annual report.
MacLeod also disputed the premier's suggestion on Monday that the strike wasn't necessary.
"LCBO workers are proud of what they achieved in this contract, which wouldn't have been possible without the strike," MacLeod said.
"This strike should have never ever happened and we could have easily sat around the table," the premier said Monday.
The new, three-year contract gives LCBO workers a total of eight per cent in raises over three years and guarantees that 1,000 casual employees will get permanent part-time employment. It also provides better access to benefits for some casual part-time employees and guarantees "market expansion" will not trigger the closure of retail stores for the term of the collective agreement.
The agreement does not impact the province's move to allow convenience, grocery and big-box stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails.
It also lays out letters of agreement that include capping the number of LCBO convenience stores at 400. A non-binding, joint committee of union and management employees will "work to determine the best implementation of the marketplace plans," according to the terms of the tentative deal.
While the broad terms of the deal were agreed to Friday afternoon, a last-minute dispute over a return-to-work protocol almost derailed it, resulting in more back and forth between the two sides Friday night.
With files from Megan Ogilvie, Kristin Rushowy and Robert
Benzie
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star With files from Megan Ogilvie, Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie
