Document 229 of 500

Premier casts doubt on parking lot plans: Ford's suggestion structure may be built above ground draws criticism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-casts-doubt-on-parking-lot-plans/docview/3083836306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+casts+doubt+on+parking+lot+plans%3A+Ford%27s+suggestion+structure+may+be+built+above+ground+draws+criticism&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As city and provincial officials start to negotiate the fate of one of the most controversial parts of the Ontario Place remake, the premier is suggesting a contentious new option for creating thousands of parking spots at the treasured waterfront site.
Doug Ford's government originally suggested a multi-level underground lot at Ontario Place to accommodate motorists visiting a planned new spa resort and expanded Budweiser Stage concert venue.
Last fall, as part of the new financial deal for Toronto, Ford agreed to put the lot at neighbouring city-owned Exhibition Place, subject to negotiations between city and provincial representatives. Visitors to a relocated Ontario Science Centre would also use the lot.
Asked Monday if he is rethinking an underground lot, given the recent massive flooding of Lake Shore Boulevard that separates Ontario Place and Exhibition Place, Ford seemed to float what would be the most controversial option yet - an above-ground lot near the Ontario Place waterfront.
"We haven't confirmed the three underground parking (levels) because it costs so much to build underground," Ford said. "We'd like to build as much on top without prohibiting the view," he told reporters. "Stay tuned on the parking lot. We're working with the city as well - maybe we'll put it on the other side."
Ford's comments injected further uncertainty into a key element of the redevelopment plan that has been shrouded in secrecy. Even with early construction underway at the site, the location, size, cost and form of the parking facility remain undecided.
The idea of a towering new parkade on the provincially owned site is being panned by Ontario Place for All, an activist group fighting Ford's plans to grant Austrian spa company Therme a 95-year lease. The group argues the shuttered amusement park should remain fully public and as natural as possible, including Lake Ontario views.
"An overground parking lot would be an absolute blight at Ontario Place," said the group's co-chair Norman Di Pasquale.
"Imagine a five-storey lot, maybe with the Science Centre on top? You're not going to be able to see the pods or the Cinesphere at all," he said, referring to the site's landmark buildings.
"We oppose any new parking structure there."
The premier's newly expressed aversion to the cost of burying a parkade near the waterfront could be a sticking point in the city-provincial talks on the parking lot that are just getting underway as part of the "new deal" agreement announced by Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow last November.
Two sources with knowledge of the talks, but not authorized to speak, said provincial officials seem to want the big provincially funded parkade - if it's built at Exhibition Place - to be above ground.
But the city's guiding principles, as decided by the Exhibition Place board chaired by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, state that the parking solution must "be completely underground to ensure highest and best use of land."
In a statement, Malik said the province must front not only construction costs if it is built at Exhibition Place but also "long-term costs such as the impact on Exhibition Place and CNE operations."
Any new garage must also not hurt the city's revenue generation from existing parking lots or Toronto's ambitious target to become a carbon-neutral city by 2040, in part by encouraging alternatives to driving.
Ford's office, asked for an update on plans for the lot, said only that city-provincial discussions are at a "preliminary" stage and no decisions have been made.
A spokesperson for Chow, who agreed to stop fighting Ford's Ontario Place redevelopment plan as part of the new deal, and who welcomed the proposal to put the parkade at Exhibition Place, said: "There are many options and outcomes for the Ontario Place garage. Talks are ongoing between city staff, the province and our office."
The parking plans have been shrouded in mystery since Ford announced the surprise redevelopment in 2021.
Early talk of the lot being built deep underground on a man-made island for private-sector tenants, potentially costing taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars, triggered fierce criticism.
Ontario's auditor general pierced provincial secrecy on the plans last December, revealing in a report that the province's agreement with Therme requires Ontario to provide the firm with dedicated parking spots within 650 metres of the spa entrance or face a financial penalty.
The spa, concert venue and Science Centre are expected to require 2,700 parking spots, or 2,000 more than currently exist at Ontario Place. Ontario's infrastructure ministry pegged the cost of building the new spots underground at $307 million.
The auditor general found that the requirement to build new parking was one reason behind the Ford government's belated decision to move the Science Centre to the waterfront from its existing Don Mills site, "in order to dispel public/stakeholder concerns relating to cost and impact on the environment."
Ford defended the relocation on Monday, saying: "We're building a brand spanking new state-of-the-art (facility), more exhibit space in the right location - not in a sleepy little neighbourhood in the suburbs that no one goes to.
"(Downtown) is where all the action is - where the soccer is, and the stadium and LiveNation, the Exhibition - so it's just common sense, a no-brainer."
It's Ford's comments that lack brains, the co-chair of Save Ontario Science Centre told the Star in an email.
Jason Ash said that more than 50 million Ontarians who have visited the interactive science museum since it opened in Flemingdon Park in 1969 "wholeheartedly disagree" with the premier.
Ash called the neighbourhood "one of the most densely populated neighbourhoods in Toronto, and in the country," with an additional 27,000 homes planned or under construction.
"Inclusive of Mr. Ford's two subway expansions, Flemingdon Park is attracting a lot of investment for a sleepy little neighbourhood in the suburbs that no one goes to."
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter With files from Robert Benzie

Letters: Kamala Harris smashes fundraising record with stunning $81 million haul over 24 hours, July 22 Migrant workers demand protection, July 11 '90210' star advocated for cancer awareness, July 15

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3083835992/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Harris is far
from 'unpopular'
Kamala Harris smashes fundraising record with stunning $81 million haul over 24 hours, July 22
The letter writer who referred to Kamala Harris as "unpopular" should check the facts. Within 24 hours of her being named as the Democratic choice to replace Joe Biden, a record amount of $81 million (U.S.) was collected in 24 hours, and almost 30,000 new volunteers signed up to assist her campaign to defeat Donald Trump.
This doesn't sound unpopular to me.
Gary Slippoy, Toronto
While it's heartening that the Kamala Harris campaign fundraised the largest 24-hour sum reported by either side in the 2024 campaign, it is a shame that the American electoral playing field requires so much money that candidates in both major parties are beholden to elite donors. That being said, it is poetic justice that Donald Trump, who has treated women and people of colour badly all his life, now has to face off with a mixed-race female prosecutor.
Ron Charach, Toronto
You know who would be an awesome vice-president pick for Kamala Harris? Liz Cheney. It would be a unity ticket. It worked for Abraham Lincoln, a Republican, who picked Andrew Johnson of the Democratic Party.
Together they formed the National Union Party.
Glen Loucks, Toronto
Taking note
of Biden's lead
History will look very kindly on Joe Biden's presidency. The first half of his term were years of great accomplishment. As Ronald Reagan's policies bred the now-defunct Reagan Republicans for over 30 years, the Democratic Party would be well served to closely follow Biden's agenda.
Kamala Harris and the party must have an economic policy that builds from the bottom-up and middle-out, is forward-looking in job creation, unflinching in support of organized labour and assigns fair taxation on the highest income earners and large corporations. Expand health-care coverage, reduce drug costs and introduce social programs such as child care and parental leave. Reinstate and protect women's reproductive health rights. Continue to advance gun controls and an automatic weapons ban. Reintroduce the Donald Trump-blocked border control legislation and launch real immigration reform. Adopt a recently announced plan to reform a dysfunctional Supreme Court.
These are all popular, positive and winning strategies, favoured by a large majority of our American neighbours. If well-communicated and followed, Democrats will find success this November and for years to come.
Mike Brown, Burlington
Harris needs more than just her experience
After all the trouble that the Democrats took to convince Joe Biden to step down, they seem to be intent on losing to Donald Trump anyway. They are endorsing a strategy focusing on Kamala Harris as a former prosecutor who will turn the public away from electing a felon. Trump has been under attack by prosecutors for more than two years now for many criminal charges.
That was a nothing burger, as the public is quite used to the idea that he has kept up practices like hiding income and evading taxes. They have heard all about the other crimes and Trump is still leading. The fact that Harris is a former prosecutor won't be her ticket to victory.
There are plenty of other things she can focus on.
She could talk about the cap on insulin costs.
She could talk about the importance of Ukraine.
She could talk about women's rights and Roe v. Wade.
She could promise a tough policy on immigration before Trump can articulate one.
She could talk about the $15 minimum wage.
She could talk about how the Supreme Court would support even more redistricting to deny voting rights for Black Americans.
She could talk about how a Trump presidency would start undoing all of these achievements and giving more tax breaks to the rich.
Venkat Krishnan, Ajax
All workers need same protections
Migrant workers demand protection, July 11
As Ontario adapts to higher summer temperatures, migrant farm workers are particularly vulnerable to heat waves.
While here on temporary work permits, many are afraid to speak up for better working conditions, as their employer can easily replace them. Migrant farm workers have closed work permits, tying them to their employer. If they have poor working conditions or poor housing, they cannot change jobs.
The article states that Premier Doug Ford expects employers to create their own standards for migrant workers and is failing to mandate safe work conditions.
We rely heavily on migrant workers to grow our food. Most of us live near farmland and migrant workers live and work near us.
All of us are struggling with the heat waves this summer. Luckily, many of us who work outside are allowed to take breaks and sit in the shade, thanks to the labour laws that protect Ontario residents.
The same protections and rights as Canadians and permanent residents who work in Ontario must be given to migrant farm workers.
Julia Deveau, Oxford County
Ella Haley, Brant County
Ashley Perry, Brantford
Lesley Pinto, Hamilton
Ouin Arndt, Brant County
Richard Tunstall, Brant County
Linda Abbas, Hamilton
Breast cancer screening should happen sooner
'90210' star advocated for cancer awareness, July 15
Thank you for pointing out that actor Shannen Doherty died from breast cancer at age 53 after she was originally diagnosed in her 40s.
Canadian women, health-care providers and scientists are currently engaged in a fight to have breast cancer screening available to women in their 40s.
The Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care wants to recommend that screening start at age 50, which is at odds with the U.S. guidelines and with a majority of provincial recommendations.
Modelling data shows that cancers arising in women in their 40s are responsible for 25 per cent of the years of life lost to breast cancer.
I believe the task force needs to be dismantled. Canadian women deserve to have guidelines that focus on saving lives and encourage them to take action that might save their own.
Robin Alter, Toronto

Document 231 of 500

Premier urges BoC to cut interest rate: Ford wants figure slashed down to 3% to help build homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-urges-boc-cut-interest-rate/docview/3083835954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+urges+BoC+to+cut+interest+rate%3A+Ford+wants+figure+slashed+down+to+3%25+to+help+build+homes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid concerns about Ontario's housing crisis, Premier Doug Ford is stepping up calls for the Bank of Canada to cut its key lending rate Wednesday.
"People in Ontario and across Canada need help," Ford said on social media Tuesday.
"The best way to boost the economy and give people stuck with sky-high mortgage rates some relief is for the Bank of Canada to cut interest rates again tomorrow. They need to get this done," the premier said.
Ford's latest intervention comes as the Bank of Canada is widely expected to lower its key overnight lending rate for the second consecutive time Wednesday as the economy appears to be slowing.
Analysts believe the central bank will cut the rate to 4.5 per cent from 4.75 per cent.
But the premier, who has been publicly lobbying Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem for months to slash the cost of borrowing, said rates must be even lower to jump-start housing construction.
"I can't help myself with this but the Bank of Canada, they need to continue cutting these rates," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
Blasting the bank as an "antiquated group ... living back in the 1970s," the premier said drastic action must be taken.
"Folks, you want to build homes? We get the Bank of Canada to knock the interest rate down to about three per cent, you'll see them pop up like mushrooms everywhere," he said.
That would lead to developers borrowing additional money to build homes and more buyers getting mortgages to afford them, Ford said.
Prior to last month's cut, the Bank of Canada raised rates 10 times - from 0.25 per cent in March 2022 to five per cent in July 2023.
"I encourage the governor of the Bank of Canada (to) get his act together and start lowering interest rates at a lot faster pace - just like you did when you raised the interest rates 10 times (at a) record speed," the premier said.
"It's easy to raise things but we need to start moving on that," he said.
The bank's rationale for increasing rates was to slow the economy to reduce inflation - because making it more expensive to borrow leads to less spending, driving down prices.
In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, inflation skyrocketed to 8.1 per cent in June 2022, well above the central bank's target of two per cent.
According to Statistics Canada, inflation was at 2.7 per cent last month, down from 2.9 per cent in April.
Because Ford is pondering an early election next spring - one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - he is under pressure to demonstrate some success in tackling Ontario's housing crisis.
But his goal to build 1.5 million new homes by 2031 appears to be falling short, with annual housing starts far below the average levels needed to achieve that.
The $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, which is now under investigation by the RCMP, has also hindered the province's efforts to get more homes built.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 232 of 500

Doug Ford urges Bank of Canada to cut interest rate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-urges-bank-canada-cut-interest-rate/docview/3084061516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+urges+Bank+of+Canada+to+cut+interest+rate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid concerns about Ontario's housing crisis, Premier Doug Ford is stepping up calls for the Bank of Canada to cut its key lending rate Wednesday.
"People in Ontario and across Canada need help," Ford said on social media Tuesday.
"The best way to boost the economy and give people stuck with sky-high mortgage rates some relief is for the Bank of Canada to cut interest rates again tomorrow. They need to get this done," the premier said.
Ford's latest intervention comes as the Bank of Canada is widely expected to lower its key overnight lending rate for the second consecutive time Wednesday as the economy appears to be slowing.
Analysts believe the central bank will cut the rate to 4.5 per cent from 4.75 per cent.
But the premier, who has been publicly lobbying Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem for months to slash the cost of borrowing, said rates must be even lower to jump-start housing construction.
"I can't help myself with this but the Bank of Canada, they need to continue cutting these rates," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
Blasting the bank as an "antiquated group ... living back in the 1970s," the premier said drastic action must be taken.
"Folks, you want to build homes? We get the Bank of Canada to knock the interest rate down to about three per cent, you'll see them pop up like mushrooms everywhere," he said.
That would lead to developers borrowing additional money to build homes and more buyers getting mortgages to afford them, said Ford.
Prior to last month's cut, the Bank of Canada raised rates 10 times - from 0.25 per cent in March 2022 to five per cent in July 2023.
"I encourage the governor of the Bank of Canada (to) get his act together and start lowering interest rates at a lot faster pace - just like you did when you raised the interest rates 10 times (at a) record speed," the premier said.
"It's easy to raise things but we need to start moving on that," he said.
The bank's rationale for increasing rates was to slow the economy to reduce inflation - because making it more expensive to borrow leads to less spending, driving down prices.
In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, inflation skyrocketed to 8.1 per cent in June 2022, well above the central bank's target of two per cent.
According to Statistics Canada, inflation was at 2.7 per cent last month, down from 2.9 per cent in April.
Because Ford is pondering an early election next spring - one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - he is under pressure to demonstrate some success in tackling Ontario's housing crisis.
But his goal to build 1.5 million new homes by 2031 appears to be falling short, with annual housing starts far below the average levels needed to achieve that.
The $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, which is now under investigation by the RCMP, has also hindered the province's efforts to get more homes built.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Document 233 of 500

A year ago Toronto's budget was a dumpster fire. Now the city's running a surprise surplus. What happened?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/year-ago-torontos-budget-was-dumpster-fire-now/docview/3083665980/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+year+ago+Toronto%27s+budget+was+a+dumpster+fire.+Now+the+city%27s+running+a+surprise+surplus.+What+happened%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sometimes money does change everything.
A year ago, Toronto city council and the just-elected Mayor Olivia Chow received a report showing that the operating budget for Canada's largest city was, well, a dumpster fire. Based on accounting for the first four months of 2023, the city was on track for a year-end budget deficit of $893 million.
Avoiding the deficit - necessary since Toronto, like other Ontario municipalities, is legally required to maintain a balanced operating budget - would mean using all the emergency reserve funds squirreled away during the pandemic by delaying and deferring projects. But that Band-Aid fix could only be used once, and would still leave Chow with a need to come up with a strategy to continue to pay for those costs and new costs caused by inflation and growth in her 2024 budget. The total problem amounted to about $1.8 billion. Alarm bells were sounded. Panic buttons were pushed.
A year later, though, a new budget report - accounting for the first four months of 2024 - sounds no alarms and pushes no buttons. Instead, the report, to be debated at this week's council meeting, Though challenges with Toronto's finances remain, the operating budget dumpster fire is out.
What changed? Two things. The "new deal" Chow negotiated with Premier Doug Ford definitely helped a bunch, contributing $300 million to TTC costs and $200 million for shelter costs this year. City hall also found about $400 million worth of sustainable budget savings and employed budget strategies to push $400 million to future years. But much of the other money needed to escape Toronto's budget hole came from a more tried-and-true method: Chow just raised taxes.
Her unprecedented 9.5 per cent residential property tax increase certainly wasn't the most popular thing she's ever done. Polls showed it took a bite out of her approval rating. And her toughest critics on council argued it was an unnecessary tax grab. But there's no doubt, looking at the numbers, that it was the prudent financial move.
The tax increase not only helped the city avoid the risk of falling into deficit but also allowed for some solid investments in better service. This year, for instance, seasonal park washrooms and some pools actually opened early.
The tax increase has also given city hall some ability to withstand some bad budget beats. The overall projected surplus, for example, comes despite the police being on track to overrun their budget by $21 million. That's "mainly due to increased premium pay spending to respond to major events and public safety initiatives," the report says. The fire department is also heading toward a $13-million overrun due to overtime costs.
The parks department, meanwhile, has had to deal with a $5 million unexpected expense to add more staff on Toronto Island ferries because of new federal safety requirements. And the transportation department is speeding toward a $25-million overrun as it has decided to accelerate road work and expand the traffic agent program to deal with high levels of congestion.
That city hall is still looking at a year-end surplus despite these setbacks and challenges is remarkable.
The numbers also help justify what will likely be a second big move on taxes in Chow's coming budget for 2025.
In fact, this year's prebudget script appears to be mostly the same as last year. Last summer, reports out of city hall started credibly making the case that the city's operating budget was not sustainable, and a major property tax increase was needed to offer decent public service. This year, reports have started making the case that the city's capital budget - the money to repair and build infrastructure - is in crisis. The gap is $26 billion over 10 years.
And that's just what's needed to prevent further decline from the status quo - a status quo that is already demonstrating Toronto's infrastructure can't handle things like an increased frequency of major storms as the climate changes.
The answer to that problem isn't likely to be more property taxes. Instead, expect Chow to start talking again about new taxes.
The flooding last week renewed some to call for a stormwater charge that would collect revenue from properties with big parking lots or other hard surfaces that contribute to flooding problems.
That's a worthy option - and certainly not one that should be dismissed as a "rain tax" - but Chow and her budget chief, Coun. Shelley Carroll, have so far seemed to favour a commercial parking levy that would collect a fee for each parking space on large properties like shopping malls. Both a parking levy and a stormwater charge would have the effect of making property owners with large parking lots either pay up or convert their parking spaces to other uses.
More importantly, though, a levy could raise significant cash to pay for things like transit, housing and, yes, flood protection. Preliminary estimates peg the amount at between $100 million and $150 million annually. Based on those numbers, Carroll has requested a report on an implementation plan for 2025, in advance of Chow's next budget.
Chow's critics will inevitably suggest any new municipal levy is unnecessary and will just amount to a tax grab. But the mayor's record so far makes the case for thinking differently. So do the numbers. And so does the rain.
CAPTION: The "new deal" Mayor Olivia Chow signed with Premier Doug Ford obviously helped fill in a great deal of the city's budget shortfall, but so did Chow's other moves.

Subject: Fires; Councils; Cost control; Floods; Stormwater management; Budget deficits; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost control Budget deficits Property taxes Tax increases

Document 234 of 500

LCBO stores reopen 18 days after workers walked off the job as stores anticipate deliveries to restock

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-stores-reopen-18-days-after-workers-walked/docview/3083665975/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+stores+reopen+18+days+after+workers+walked+off+the+job+as+stores+anticipate+deliveries+to+restock&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a cheery "welcome back, everybody," the doors opened and the first strike in LCBO history was finally, irrevocably over.
As smiling staff greeted eager customers at the LCBO's College and Bathurst location for the first time in more than two weeks Tuesday morning, it was hard to tell who was happier.
"Good to see you guys again," one customer exclaimed, to happy nods from staff. "We're glad to be back," came the reply.
"We're a little low on a few things right now. We're supposed to be getting a delivery today at some point," another staffer explained apologetically to one restaurant manager looking for their usual brands of tequila.
Some popular products - Jameson Irish whiskey, Tequila Tromba - were nowhere to be seen in a store that was busier than usual for a Tuesday morning.
At the LCBO's flagship store on Queens Quay, several rows of bottles were put on the shelf backwards, as a way, said staff, of indicating that the product normally on that shelf was out of stock.
"Why are some of those price things upside down," another customer asked.
"That means that's out of stock too, at least for now," came the reply.
Sunday, 9,000 LCBO workers voted to ratify a tentative agreement finalized early Saturday between the provincial liquor monopoly and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
The strike began July 5, with OPSEU raising concerns over the provincial government's plans to expand alcohol retailing.
Now that it's over, customers might notice a few things missing at first, but likely not for too long, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson.
"I think we'll see them back on track pretty quickly," said Hutcheson, managing director at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group. "I was actually pretty surprised how little stuff was missing at the store I went too."
Meanwhile, OPSEU's chief negotiator slammed Ontario Premier Doug Ford Tuesday, saying the premier was wrong when he said the LCBO doesn't make money from its retailing operations.
"It's clear that Doug Ford has no idea where LCBO revenues come from," OPSEU's Colleen MacLeod said. "He claimed that the LCBO doesn't make money through retail sales, and repeatedly said that "we make money on wholesaling."
MacLeod said its retail operations account for 79.4 per cent of the LCBO's revenues, citing the agency's 2023 annual report.
MacLeod also disputed the premier's suggestion on Monday that the strike wasn't necessary.
"LCBO workers are proud of what they achieved in this contract, which wouldn't have been possible without the strike," MacLeod said.
"This strike should have never ever happened and we could have easily sat around the table," the premier said Monday.
The new, three-year contract gives LCBO workers a total of eight per cent in raises over three years and guarantees that 1,000 casual employees will get permanent part-time employment. It also provides better access to benefits for some casual part-time employees and guarantees "market expansion" will not trigger the closure of retail stores for the term of the collective agreement.
The agreement does not impact the province's move to allow convenience, grocery and big-box stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails.
It also lays out letters of agreement that include capping the number of LCBO convenience stores at 400. A non-binding, joint committee of union and management employees will "work to determine the best implementation of the marketplace plans," according to the terms of the tentative deal.
While the broad terms of the deal were agreed to Friday afternoon, a last-minute dispute over a return-to-work protocol almost derailed it, resulting in more back and forth between the two sides Friday night.
With files from Megan Ogilvie, Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Customers head into a LCBO location in Toronto on Tuesday as Ontario's liquor stores reopened following a strike that saw their doors close in early July.A LCBO employee opens the doors to a store for customers in Toronto on Tuesday as Ontario's liquor stores reopen following a strike that saw their doors close in early July.

Five candidates register for byelection: Hopefuls have until Sept. 19 to sign up for race to fill former councillor Jaye Robinson's seat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/five-candidates-register-byelection/docview/3083464623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+candidates+register+for+byelection%3A+Hopefuls+have+until+Sept.+19+to+sign+up+for+race+to+fill+former+councillor+Jaye+Robinson%27s+seat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prospective candidates to fill former councillor Jaye Robinson's seat in Don Valley West started tossing their hats in the ring on Monday as registrations opened for the fall byelection.
Among the five candidates who registered at city hall to run in the Nov. 4 byelection was Evan Sambasivam, 28, a mental health advocate who grew up in the ward and who ran unsuccessfully as a councillor in 2022 in Eglinton-Lawrence.
Sambasivam called Don Valley West "one of the heart beats of Toronto," but said there are minimal community amenities, doctors, medical spaces and community spaces in the ward.
He said he's wanted to join city council after losing a long-time friend to homelessness. "I will always vote for more housing where it needs to be," he said on Monday, adding that he would be an "ardent defender" of the Ontario Science Centre, which is in the bordering ward.
Also registering Monday was Dhruv Jain, 41, director of transit policy for TTC chair Jamaal Myers. Jain lives in Don Valley West with his family and previously worked in local MPP Stephanie Bowman's office, after several years working for the Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario.
Jain noted key issues in the ward include fixing aging infrastructure, adding more family-sized homes and building a family-oriented neighbourhood with safer streets and transit. He said he's committed to "working across political lines to build a city that works for every single family," which also includes revitalizing green spaces and building parks.
Jain was accompanied Monday by councillors Myers and Dianne Saxe, who sit on the TTC board and endorsed Jain's candidacy. Myers called him an "amazing" friend who was "instrumental" in preventing a TTC strike and Saxe called him a "solutions-oriented" person who "does his homework."
Anthony Furey, 40, a former Toronto Sun columnist and former editorial director of the right-wing media arm for True North Centre for Public Policy, also registered on Monday.
Furey, who came in fourth in last year's mayoral byelection and who has faced criticism for some of his political views, particularly around Muslims, called himself a "pragmatic guy" on Monday, one who's interested in advocating for "common sense" solutions.
"I'm not going to get captured by the circus, the sideshows and so forth at city hall," he said, referencing what he later called "pet projects" like renaming Yonge-Dundas Square. "I'm going to stay true to the people."
Furey, who lives in Beaches-East York, said key issues include declining quality of life indicators, out-of-control traffic, the increase in car thefts and the city ignoring "the basics" like its state of good repair. He also plans to work with police to address drivers using "side roads and non-core roads as freeways."
Habiba Desai, 31, who ran as an independent candidate in Scarborough-Guildwood in the 2023 provincial election and ran in that same year's mayoral byelection, lives in Toronto-Danforth.
She said among the top issues in the ward are youth gun violence, domestic violence and transit mobility. She also said she is "completely against" safe injection sites, but that mental health services should be widely available and that she'd vote to "shut down" homeless shelters but that housing should be free.
By the end of the day, Sheena Sharp, a one-time Green Party candidate, had also registered.
Mayor Olivia Chow said she won't be endorsing any candidates for the byelection.
Candidates have until Sept. 19 to register for the byelection.
CAPTION: Nominations opened Monday for the Nov. 4 byelection to fill the Don Valley West council seat.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Document 236 of 500

Ford blames union for LCBO strike: Premier says job action could have been avoided via talks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-blames-union-lcbo-strike/docview/3083464561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+blames+union+for+LCBO+strike%3A+Premier+says+job+action+could+have+been+avoided+via+talks&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As liquor stores reopen Tuesday after an 18-day strike, Premier Doug Ford says the first job action in LCBO history could have been avoided.
"We learned many things with this strike - people were still able to get their alcohol ... don't mess around with people's booze in Ontario, because they aren't too happy," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
"This strike should have never ever happened and we could have easily sat around the table," the premier said, blaming Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) brass for the stoppage.
More than 9,000 unionized workers hit the bricks on July 5, shuttering 680 Liquor Control Board of Ontario outlets and forcing consumers to buy from other retailers, including the 450 grocery stores licensed to sell beer and wine by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015.
It was Ford's decision two months ago to spend $225 million to widen Wynne's liberalization to 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets - and allow the expanded sale of ready-to-drink beverages - that spurred OPSEU to strike the LCBO for the first time since its creation in 1927.
The union felt that losing the LCBO's monopoly on selling premixed drinks, such as Mike's Hard Lemonade and Dillon's gin cocktails, would undermine the future of the Crown retailer - even though it will still enjoy exclusive rights to spirit sales.
After a settlement that included eight per cent raises over three years, giving full-time jobs to 1,000 part-time employees and guarantees the LCBO would retain its existing retail footprint and cap the number of private agency stores, OPSEU president JP Hornick insisted "this fight is far from over."
"Ontarians are more aware than ever that Ford's plan isn't for them, it's for big box CEOs. We have no doubt that Ford's webs of corporate buddies and corrupt backroom deals will continue to be exposed," Hornick said Sunday.
The premier, who gives out his cellphone number to the public, maintained he "was able to connect with over 500 people" in the union through the more than two-week strike.
"I can tell you 98 per cent were super, super nice, they were understanding and all they kept saying is, 'I just want to go back to work,'" said Ford. "You get the odd nasty call but that goes with anything, but the vast majority were super people and they just want to earn a paycheque," he said.
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team, said her members "won" because they "stood strong" and "talked to their communities" about what was at stake.
"In my 27 years at the LCBO, the employer has continuously casualized the workforce, so that people wouldn't get guaranteed hours, benefits or any hope of permanent work. Permanent part-time nearly went extinct," she said. "I am beyond proud that we fought back and won these permanent jobs."
Beer and wine and - as of this week - ready-to-drink beverages are available in the 450 licensed supermarkets with the thousands of other retailers expected to be stocking booze by Oct. 31.
Internal Progressive Conservative government polling obtained by the Star last week concluded there was significant support for liberalized alcohol sales even though 54 per cent backed the OPSEU strike while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
The Campaign Research survey suggested the job action had little impact on the public with about three-quarters of respondents - 73 per cent - saying it was not "inconvenient" while 28 per cent said it was.
Some 69 per cent backed Wynne's liberalization and 57 per cent supported Ford's expansion while 61 per cent favoured more availability of premixed drinks.
CAPTION: A frustrated customer stands at the front door of a Brampton LCBO Monday that was mistakenly listed as open on Google. All LCBO stores will open Tuesday after a settlement that included eight per cent raises over three years and full-time positions for 1,000 part-time employees.Nick LachanceToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

How is Toronto now running a surplus?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-is-toronto-now-running-surplus/docview/3083464469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+is+Toronto+now+running+a+surplus%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sometimes money does change everything.
A year ago, Toronto city council and the just-elected Mayor Olivia Chow received a report showing that the operating budget for Canada's largest city was, well, a dumpster fire. Based on accounting for the first four months of 2023, the city was on track for a year-end budget deficit of $893 million.
Avoiding the deficit - necessary since Toronto, like other Ontario municipalities, is legally required to maintain a balanced operating budget - would mean using all the emergency reserve funds squirreled away during the pandemic by delaying and deferring projects. But that Band-Aid fix could only be used once, and would still leave Chow with a need to come up with a strategy to continue to pay for those costs and new costs caused by inflation and growth in her 2024 budget. The total problem amounted to about $1.8 billion. Alarm bells were sounded. Panic buttons were pushed.
A year later, though, a new budget report - accounting for the first four months of 2024 - sounds no alarms and pushes no buttons. Instead, the report, to be debated at this week's council meeting, though challenges with Toronto's finances remain, the operating budget dumpster fire is out.
What changed? Two things. The "new deal" Chow negotiated with Premier Doug Ford definitely helped a bunch, contributing $300 million to TTC costs and $200 million for shelter costs this year. City hall also found about $400 million worth of sustainable budget savings and employed budget strategies to push $400 million to future years. But much of the other money needed to escape Toronto's budget hole came from a more tried-and-true method: Chow just raised taxes.
Her unprecedented 9.5 per cent residential property tax increase certainly wasn't the most popular thing she's ever done. Polls showed it took a bite out of her approval rating. And her toughest critics on council argued it was an unnecessary tax grab. But there's no doubt, looking at the numbers, that it was the prudent financial move.
The tax increase not only helped the city avoid the risk of falling into deficit but also allowed for some solid investments in better service. This year, for instance, seasonal park washrooms and some pools actually opened early.
The tax increase has also given city hall some ability to withstand some bad budget beats. The overall projected surplus, for example, comes despite the police being on track to overrun its budget by $21 million. That's "mainly due to increased premium pay spending to respond to major events and public safety initiatives," the report says. The fire department is also heading toward a $13-million overrun due to overtime costs.
The parks department has had to deal with a $5-million unexpected expense to add more staff on Toronto Island ferries because of new federal safety requirements. And the transportation department is speeding toward a $25-million overrun as it has decided to accelerate road work and expand the traffic agent program to deal with high levels of congestion.
That city hall is still looking at a year-end surplus despite these setbacks and challenges is remarkable.
The numbers also help justify what will likely be a second big move on taxes in Chow's coming budget for 2025.
In fact, this year's prebudget script appears to be mostly the same as last year. Last summer, reports out of city hall started credibly making the case that the city's operating budget was not sustainable, and a major property tax increase was needed to offer decent public service. This year, reports have started making the case that the city's capital budget - the money to repair and build infrastructure - is in crisis. The gap is $26 billion over 10 years.
And that's just what's needed to prevent further decline from the status quo - a status quo that is already demonstrating Toronto's infrastructure can't handle things like an increased frequency of major storms as the climate changes.
The answer to that problem isn't likely to be more property taxes. Instead, expect Chow to start talking again about new taxes.
The flooding last week renewed some to call for a stormwater charge that would collect revenue from properties with big parking lots or other hard surfaces that contribute to flooding problems.
That's a worthy option - and certainly not one that should be dismissed as a "rain tax" - but Chow and her budget chief, Coun. Shelley Carroll, have so far seemed to favour a commercial parking levy that would collect a fee for each parking space on large properties like shopping malls. Both a parking levy and a stormwater charge would have the effect of making property owners with large parking lots either pay up or convert their parking spaces to other uses.
More importantly, though, a levy could raise significant cash to pay for things like transit, housing and, yes, flood protection. Preliminary estimates peg the amount at between $100 million and $150 million annually. Based on those numbers, Carroll has requested a report on an implementation plan for 2025, in advance of Chow's next budget.
Chow's critics will inevitably suggest any new municipal revenue source is unnecessary and will just amount to a tax grab. But the mayor's record so far makes the case for thinking different. So do the numbers. And so does the rain.
CAPTION: The "new deal" Mayor Olivia Chow signed with Premier Doug Ford filled in much of the city's budget shortfall, Matt Elliott writes, as well as her unprecedented 9.5 per cent residential property tax increase.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Document 238 of 500

'This law has nothing to do with housing': Critics question use of new strong-mayor powers to hire, fire city staff

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-law-has-nothing-do-with-housing/docview/3083464137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+law+has+nothing+to+do+with+housing%27%3A+Critics+question+use+of+new+strong-mayor+powers+to+hire%2C+fire+city+staff&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tool Doug Ford's government created to give mayors the power to build housing faster is being used in some Ontario municipalities to make unilateral staffing decisions that have little to do with the construction of new homes and more to do with the consolidation of power at a local level, critics say.
Although so-called "strong-mayor" powers were enacted in 2022, a flurry of a half-dozen decisions by newly elected Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish just days after she was sworn in - including the dismissal of the city manager and the hiring of other senior staff - has raised concerns about their use in municipalities and their impact on how cities operate across the province.
"This law has nothing to do with housing, but has everything to do with concentrating power in one person," said Caledon Coun. David Sheen, who has been monitoring the use of strong-mayor powers in his town. "It's hard to believe that was the province's intention."
After testing out the tool in Toronto and Ottawa in 2022, the province gave some 49 municipalities enhanced strong-mayor powers in 2023 with the aim of advancing such provincial priorities as housing and the construction of roads and transit.
The powers allow mayors to veto or bypass the rest of the local council while making decisions about the hiring or firing of senior staff, setting up or dissolving committees, setting the budget and vetoing any decision by council.
Dozens of orders have been enacted by mayors since, with the bulk of these being administrative issues.
In a few cases, such as pushing for fourplexes in Mississauga or approving a development in Hamilton, the strong-mayor powers have been used to advance housing initiatives.
But it's the use of the orders to hire and fire senior level staff with no consultation or rationale - particularly the city's top role of chief administrative officer (CAO), who is to provide expert professional advice to council and serve as a conduit between council and staff - that has caused the most concern among both municipal officials and experts.
"The province rolled these powers out in order to accelerate the building of more housing, but not all of the powers - such as appointments - are restricted to housing," said Martin Horak, a professor and associate director for the Centre for Urban Policy and Local Governance at Western University. "The implications will be much broader than housing."
Some critics say the ability of mayors to have sole control over the hiring and firing of senior city staff is blurring the lines between politics and a bureaucracy that has always been viewed as independent and neutral.
Horak believes it could be used by some mayors to build a more "executive-centred style government at the local level focused on just one person."
"This could become very problematic for local democracy," he said.
Some GTA mayors have opted not to use the powers, while others have delegated the hiring of city staff to the CAO.
A day after she became mayor, Parrish used the strong-mayor powers to pass a bylaw allowing her to "hire and dismiss certain city officials." The next day, she dismissed CAO Shari Lichterman and appointed a new person to the city's top job.
In an interview, Lichterman said the mayor met with her the day after being sworn into office, and told her that she was being dismissed "without cause."
"I was surprised and disappointed as I loved the job and I was hoping to talk to her about her agenda and how we could work together," said Lichterman, who had been in the position for only a year.
"I was surprised, but with the strong-mayor powers, it's a risk ... and it's a risk we all worry about in these positions."
Mississauga councillors, who asked for anonymity for fear of reprisals, said they were disappointed Parrish made this crucial decision without their input. In previous years, the hiring of senior staff had been done through an extensive selection process involving all members of council, they said.
A week after she enacted a bylaw giving herself hiring and firing powers over senior staff, Parrish gave some of those hiring powers back to the CAO.
In an email, Parrish said that former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie had "transferred all strong-mayor powers to the CAO as she was involved in a leadership campaign and eventually on a leave of absence."
Those strong-mayor powers "were transferred to me as the newly elected mayor and I made a change," Parrish wrote, adding that she didn't want to dwell on "personnel matters."
Parrish, who said during the election that she would "avoid" using the strong-mayor powers, now says she has no issue using them "during my term of council when they can assist in enabling more, better and more affordable housing."
The province did not answer questions about whether it was tracking the use of the strong-mayor powers, or monitoring decisions enacted through it.
But the province is "already seeing strong mayors proposing bylaws to help advance provincial priorities such as building more homes," according to Justine Teplycky, the director of communications for Housing Minister Paul Calandra.
Teplycky added that "implementation of the strong-mayor framework is a local matter and up to the mayor, council and municipality."
But municipalities are scrambling to understand and implement the legislation without real guidance from the province.
This month, city of Windsor staff held a training session for councillors on the strong-mayor powers so they could better understand how they work. Some councillors were left with more questions than answers.
"There's not a lot of detail or guidance behind it, so it's subject to interpretation," said a staff member during the session.
Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens made headlines last fall when he dismissed the city's top engineer and lawyer using the new powers. Since then, Dilkens has used the strong-mayor powers 36 times, including 12 times for hiring and promoting to a variety of positions, including the director of corporate security and manager of environmental services.
Windsor is now facing a lawsuit for wrongful dismissal from one of the people fired last year.
Windsor Coun. Kieran McKenzie said that while most of those personnel decisions by the mayor were done in consultation with council, he believes the legislation has had little impact on housing decisions in the city. He said the legislation consolidates power "into a single individual" and thinks it should be repealed.
"There are too many unintended consequences of this legislation," he said, adding that the province has put in "few guardrails" to ensure the powers aren't abused.
In an email, Dilkens confirmed the wrongful dismissal suit was ongoing, but did not answer questions about his use of strong-mayor powers.
Mississauga's Lichterman said while she doesn't expect the province to rescind the legislation, she said the province should amend it to clarify how CAOs should operate, given this new dynamic.
"The job of a CAO still is to answer to all of the council, not just one individual," she said. "On the other hand, one individual can hire and fire you.
"So how do we balance that loyalty and who is your boss in that situation?"
It's a dynamic that has also been felt in Caledon since Mayor Annette Groves appointed a new CAO last August.
Since then, the city has seen significant staff turnover, including the dismissal of at least six people and the resignation of another 12, according to Sheen, who has been tracking the revolving door at the town since the strong-mayor legislation was enacted.
Groves appointed Nathan Hyde in August 2023, with no consultation with fellow councillors, who were also denied access to the details in his new contract.
Sheen said it took him months to obtain the document through access to information, and he was shocked at some of the details - in particular, the fact that the new CAO's annual performance appraisal would be done by just one person.
"Your performance will be reviewed annually by the mayor, and your performance appraisal will be delivered by the mayor. Your performance is not subject to review by any member of council that is not the mayor," the contract, seen by the Star, stated.
Sheen is also concerned about the severance Hyde is entitled to if he is dismissed, which could be up to four years of severance pay if he is fired after 35 months of service. Prior to that, he would be entitled to three years of pay.
In an email, Groves said she has used the strong-mayor powers 10 times since they were introduced in 2023.
She defended her decision to appoint Hyde to the role of CAO. She said she felt the town needed a "change in senior leadership to address internal issues and build strong policies and structures to prepare for aggressive population growth."
She said two of the 10 orders she issued were directly related to advancing the province's housing priorities.
And she said she plans to continue using them.
"I will use them, if warranted and when appropriate, to serve the best short- and long-term interests of our community," she said.
But Sheen said the way the law is being applied is impacting the way municipal government operates and the ability of council to do its job effectively.
"Strong-mayor power legislation clearly gives mayors the authority to hire and fire the CAO - that's the law of the land," said Sheen.
"But the law doesn't fundamentally change the relationship between the CAO and municipal council, but that is what we are seeing happen now."
CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Subject: Wrongful discharge; Legislation; Mayors; Councils; Decision making; Municipalities; Workforce planning; Housing developments; Criticism; Reforms; Political power

Ford pledges $260M for skills training: Province boosting fund to help employers fill nearly 300,000 open jobs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pledges-260m-skills-training/docview/3083464028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pledges+%24260M+for+skills+training%3A+Province+boosting+fund+to+help+employers+fill+nearly+300%2C000+open+jobs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hindered by a shortage of skilled labour, Premier Doug Ford is earmarking as much as $260 million for employers and unions to train workers.
Ford said Monday he was boosting the province's "skills development fund" in order to help fill some of the nearly 300,000 available jobs in Ontario.
"By continuing to ... make these important investments, we are also tackling Ontario's labour shortage and bringing back good-paying jobs in manufacturing and the skilled trades," the premier told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
Ford said starting next Monday, municipalities, hospitals, private companies, labour unions, community organizations, Indigenous groups and training providers can apply to tap in to the funding to tackle their employment needs.
This latest cash infusion brings to $1.4 billion the amount Queen's Park has spent on skills training subsidies since 2021.
In those three years, the government said the program has helped train nearly 600,000 people, including 36,000 health care and personal support workers, some 66,000 in construction and more than 92,000 in manufacturing.
Ford said the money is "helping connect workers here in Kitchener and across the province to better jobs and bigger paycheques."
Karen Renkema, Ontario vice-president of the Progressive Contractors Association of Canada, said the funding "has been a game changer for the construction industry."
"The funds have enabled innovative training partnerships, pathways and programs that have served to attract and support many new participants into the skilled trades," said Renkema.
One of the many challenges Ford's government has had trying to get housing built in Ontario is the dearth of skilled tradespeople.
The premier has promised to have 1.5 million new homes built by 2031, but housing starts are falling well short of his targets.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Will U.S. bombshell make Trudeau change election game plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-u-s-bombshell-make-trudeau-change-election/docview/3084295308/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+U.S.+bombshell+make+Trudeau+change+election+game+plan%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau kept it simple with his statement on Joe Biden's decision to bow out of the U.S. presidential race, calling him a "true friend" and thanking him for the many years they've worked together.
Canada's prime minister has had much in common with the beleaguered U.S. president over the past month - mounting cries to step down and clear the field for someone in a better position to fight back against the threats to his continued power.
For Biden, the whispers turned into a shout. Trudeau, for now, is still hanging in, not facing anything close to the Democratic revolt that made Biden's exit inevitable and inescapable.
Trudeau has now seen three U.S. presidents come and go since he took office in 2015: Barack Obama, Donald Trump and, with Sunday's announcement, Biden. With so much still up in the air in the whirlwind that is U.S. politics these days, neither Trudeau nor his government knows who the fourth president will be, once the November vote is over.
The plan still remains, though - prepare for any outcome. That's how "Team Canada," as they like to call themselves, were dealing with the U.S. before Sunday's bombshell and how they will be dealing with it in the days to come.
Trudeau has made no secret of the fact that Biden and the Democrats are better allies for Canada - whether that's because of their common cause on climate change, abortion rights or progressive politics in general. So if Biden's departure makes a Democratic victory more possible, that's obviously good news for the Trudeau Liberals.
But Canada can't afford to see a Trump return to the White House as beyond the realm of possibility either, especially after watching the Republicans basking in unity and expectation of November triumph after their convention last week.
Trudeau has in fact spoken to Biden and Donald Trump in the past two weeks. He and Biden were together at the NATO summit in Washington during the first week of July, while Trudeau phoned Trump last weekend after the former president was shot at a rally in Pennsylvania on July 13.
Biden's perilous political future was very much an issue at the NATO summit, but it reportedly wasn't part of the conversations between Trudeau and Biden in Washington.
As for the chat between Trump and Trudeau - the first they've had since Trump ceased to be president in 2021 - sources say it was friendly and upbeat. The two men connected thanks to a conversation between Foreign Minister Mélanie Joly and South Carolina Sen. Lindsey Graham, who was with Trump in New Jersey as he recovered from the gunshot.
The Joly-Graham link is a glimpse into just how much Canada has been keeping its network alive with Republicans and Democrats as the presidential race heats up.
Canada has had good relations with the Biden team - Biden's campaign chief, Jen O'Malley Dillon, much in the news these past few weeks, gave advice to Trudeau when he was running to be prime minister and spoke at his first Liberal convention in 2014.
But as Canada's ambassador to the U.S., Kirsten Hillman is constantly saying, the Canada-U.S. relationship is bigger than a simple leader-to-leader one.
Trudeau's team did take note when Trump's pick for vice-president, JD Vance, slammed North American free trade in his speech at the Republican convention last week. But they also noted how Trump said NAFTA had been replaced with a much better deal - one of his own making, of course.
"I got rid of NAFTA, the worst trade deal ever made, and replaced it with USMCA, which is, they say, the best trade deal ever made," Trump told his Republican troops.
Trudeau and his government will be taking some comfort from those remarks, in the hope that Trump won't try to tear up a deal he sees as a success. But the fact remains that the U.S. is in a protectionist mood, whether November produces a Republican or Democratic victory.
Biden, up until the minute he said he was leaving on Sunday, insisted he could not walk away from a fight that put the country's democracy at stake. Trudeau and his Liberals have been casting the next election battle against the Conservatives as an existential one for Canada too, presenting leader Pierre Poilievre as a Trump-in-waiting for this country.
There is, for now - again - no indication that Trudeau is in the same position as Biden found himself, acknowledging that battle was too big for him and all the baggage he was bringing to the looming race.
All Trudeau is saying now is that Biden is a friend, not an example.
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Document 241 of 500

What the decline of condo sales may mean

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-decline-condo-sales-may-mean/docview/3084293417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+the+decline+of+condo+sales+may+mean&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are two ways of interpreting the continuing decline of new condo sales - a trend that somehow achieved uncharted lows, according to information on second-quarter activity released last week by Urbanation.
The firm, which tracks the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area (GTHA) housing market, reported that just 1,688 condos sold between April and June, a 66 per cent decline from the same period in 2023, marking the lowest sales rate in a generation (except for the first few months of the pandemic) - and all this despite overwhelming evidence of a housing affordability crisis.
One interpretation, advanced by many economists, is that the GTHA condo market hasn't yet responded to the Bank of Canada's June 25 basis point rate cut and its subsequent signalling that more relief is on the way. As a Leger survey conducted this spring for Royal LePage found, 41 per cent of respondents needed to see a cut of at least 50 or even more than 100 basis points in order to make the math on a purchase work.
Indeed, you can think of that June cut as the moment when your alarm clock first goes off. You groggily hit snooze, but you're now a wee bit awake. The second and perhaps third alarm will get buyers out of bed and signing presale agreements, or so goes the theory of orthodox market economics.
The second interpretation veers off in a different direction and takes a run at the apparently inviolable laws of supply and demand, which work seamlessly when one is selling lawn mowers or summer dresses but not so well when it comes to housing.
A small clarification here: the market signals travelling between builder and buyer were far clearer in the days when subdivisions of single-family homes dominated, primarily because the buyers were also end-users.
Yet due to decades of financialization, the condo sector no longer works that way. The buyers sending the market signals to developers are often investors; in the presale market, they're outright speculators. What has been most in demand by investors were one- or one-plus bedroom apartments, and so that's what got built. Whether GTHA residents looking for housing mainly want to live in one or one-plus bedroom apartments is an entirely different question.
It's as if a shoe wholesaler had said to one of its suppliers, "Just send ladies' small sizes. Trust me. I'm certain that's what my retailers need." Thing is, the retailers' own customers don't just want ladies small, nor can they afford to pay premium prices, and therein lies the problem.
The other riddle in these statistics has to do with what's different about the GTHA, whose housing market isn't behaving like those in other large Canadian cities. Key macroeconomic factors - labour shortages, interest rates, inflation, supply chain issues, building material costs - apply across the country (and beyond it), not just in one region.
So, what accounts for the anchor weighing down the GTHA sector? Land use policies are an easy target, but Premier Doug Ford's government, as well as more proactive municipalities, have spent the past few years removing regulatory and administrative roadblocks to development in an effort to stoke the supply of new housing.
One other structural explanation ultimately involves prices: the median cost of GTHA condos and homes today - ranging from $640,000 to $1.3 million, according to the Toronto Regional Real Estate Board (TRREB) - far exceeds the carrying capacity of most families. A gross income of almost $140,000 is needed today to support a half-million-dollar mortgage, whereas the median Toronto household income in 2021 was just $84,000. The timeline for saving enough for a stress-tested down-payment is now measured in decades for the GTA.
The takeaway isn't difficult to grasp: many people have little choice but to stay out of the market entirely and instead rely on equally unaffordable rentals, many of which are inadequately proportioned condos owned by investors.
The TRREB's own second-quarter evaluation tried to strike an optimistic tone, noting that prices on all the main categories of dwellings had fallen by a couple of percentage points in the past few months. What Urbanation's latest sales data all but shouts is that the much-vaunted equilibrium between supply and demand is still nowhere to be seen, housing crisis be damned.
John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor. He is a contributor for the Star.

Doug Ford says the 18-day LCBO strike could have been avoided

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-18-day-lcbo-strike-could-have-been/docview/3083726982/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+the+18-day+LCBO+strike+could+have+been+avoided&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As liquor stores reopen Tuesday after an 18-day strike, Premier Doug Ford says the first job action in LCBO history could have been avoided.
"We learned many things with this strike - people were still able to get their alcohol ... don't mess around with people's booze in Ontario, because they aren't too happy," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
"This strike should have never ever happened and we could have easily sat around the table," the premier said, blaming Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) brass for the stoppage.
More than 9,000 unionized workers hit the bricks on July 5, shuttering 680 Liquor Control Board of Ontario outlets and forcing consumers to buy from other retailers, including the 450 grocery stores licensed to sell beer and wine by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015.
It was Ford's decision two months ago to spend $225 million to widen Wynne's liberalization to 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets - and allow the expanded sale of ready-to-drink beverages - that spurred OPSEU to strike the LCBO for the first time since its creation in 1927.
The union felt that losing the LCBO's monopoly on selling premixed drinks, such as Mike's Hard Lemonade and Dillon's gin cocktails, would undermine the future of the Crown retailer - even though it will still enjoy exclusive rights to spirit sales.
After a settlement that included eight per cent raises over three years, giving full-time jobs to 1,000 part-time employees and guarantees the LCBO would retain its existing retail footprint and cap the number of private agency stores, OPSEU president JP Hornick insisted "this fight is far from over."
"Ontarians are more aware than ever that Ford's plan isn't for them, it's for big box CEOs. We have no doubt that Ford's webs of corporate buddies and corrupt backroom deals will continue to be exposed," Hornick said Sunday.
The premier, who gives out his cellphone number to the public, maintained he "was able to connect with over 500 people" in the union through the more than two-week strike.
"I can tell you 98 per cent were super, super nice, they were understanding and all they kept saying is, 'I just want to go back to work,'" said Ford.
"You get the odd nasty call but that goes with anything, but the vast majority were super people and they just want to earn a paycheque," he said.
"I've always said I differentiate between labour and labour leadership. I support the frontline labour folks that work their backs off and I differentiate between public- and private-sector unions. I love the private sector and I get along well with some public-sector unions..."
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team, said her members "won" because they "stood strong" and "talked to their communities" about what was at stake.
"We went on strike to protect good jobs and public revenues and to win more permanent jobs with benefits and guaranteed hours," said MacLeod.
"In my 27 years at the LCBO, the employer has continuously casualized the workforce, so that people wouldn't get guaranteed hours, benefits or any hope of permanent work. Permanent part-time nearly went extinct," she said.
"I am beyond proud that we fought back and won these permanent jobs - it will improve the lives of workers and their families for many years to come."
Beer and wine and - as of this week - ready-to-drink beverages are available in the 450 licensed supermarkets with the thousands of other retailers expected to be stocking booze by Oct. 31.
Internal Progressive Conservative government polling obtained by the Star last week concluded there was significant support for liberalized alcohol sales even though 54 per cent backed the OPSEU strike while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
The Campaign Research survey suggested the job action had little impact on the public with about three-quarter of respondents - 73 per cent - saying it was not "inconvenient" while 28 per cent said it was.
Some 69 per cent backed Wynne's liberalization and 57 per cent supported Ford's expansion while 61 per cent favoured more availability of premixed drinks.
Campaign Research surveyed 1,995 Ontarians July 12-13 using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 18 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and 10 per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
That's similar to last month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, which had the PCs at 41 per cent, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford walks past reporters after arriving late for a meeting between Canada's premiers and Indigenous leaders at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.
