Document 265 of 500

I'm a lifelong Toronto renter. I live in constant fear of being forced onto the street

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/im-lifelong-toronto-renter-i-live-constant-fear/docview/3082292033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I%27m+a+lifelong+Toronto+renter.+I+live+in+constant+fear+of+being+forced+onto+the+street&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've lived in apartments in Toronto my whole life. I've been in my current apartment for nine years - and I've watched in horror as apartments around me doubled, then tripled in price. For the past five years, I've lived with the terrifying knowledge that if I had to move tomorrow, there would be nowhere for me to go. As a struggling creative and small business owner, even prices in Etobicoke and Scarborough are beyond my reach.
I also have chronic health issues that could worsen at any moment, rendering me unable to work and unable to afford even my current, rent-controlled apartment. I live in constant fear that something will change, either with my apartment or my health, and force me onto the street.
So, I was excited to see Toronto city council's initiative to create 65,000 affordable rental units. But I was also nervous that these affordable apartments wouldn't actually provide housing to our most vulnerable populations. Affordable is, after all, a relative term. If we define "affordable" based on the average prices of Toronto rentals - $2,392 for a one bedroom and $3,166 for a two bedroom - we can argue that $1,500 for a one bedroom and $2,500 for a two bedroom is affordable.
The monthly rent for a studio apartment built under city council's initiative will cost $1,088. A one bedroom will be $1,378. A two bedroom will cost $1,992. A three bedroom will be $2,190. Not as high as the average Toronto rental, but it still seems to be defined by the market.
I define affordable differently. In my mind, to be affordable, a rental unit must cost no more than 30 per cent of before-tax income of our poorest populations - disabled folks, people on disability benefits, and minimum wage workers.
By this generally accepted standard, the city council's "affordable units" aren't affordable at all. The average person on disability receives $1,133 a month. Even a studio apartment using the current guidelines would cost 90 per cent of their monthly income.
Things are even worse for folks on Ontario Works income support program. A single person on OW only receives $390 a month for shelter and $343 a month for their basic needs. Even combining those two amounts wouldn't provide enough for a studio apartment using the city's current definition of affordable.
In short, if you can't work - or if you lose your job and don't find a new one before EI runs out - you can't afford one of these "affordable" units.
Things are a little better for minimum-wage workers, making $2,440 a month (before taxes). A studio apartment will be more than 30 per cent of their income, but they'll technically be able to afford it. They might even be able to afford a one bedroom if they're smart about budgeting. What they won't be able to do is rent a two or three bedroom. In other words, single parents working minimum wage won't be able to claim these units and provide adequate housing for their children.
The current Ontario government has no intention of raising disability, Ontario Works, or minimum wage. If we vote Premier Doug Ford's conservatives out next election, we might see minimum wage increase, but Liberal governments have proven equally reluctant to raise disability benefits or Ontario Works. So, it's up to Toronto city council to provide housing these people can actually afford.
There are some rent-geared-to-income apartments - 6,500 of them - included in the city's plan. That is not enough. Not when the city's subsidized housing list is 10 years long and you have to jump through hoops to even get on the list.
A housing initiative meant to really uplift the most vulnerable among us would include closer to 20,000 rent-geared-to-income units and a definition of affordability that accounts for our lowest-income residents for the rest.
I want - no, need - to believe there's a future in Toronto for disabled people, for people struggling to find work, for people working minimum wage. I need to believe there's a future here for me, that I'll be able to stay in this city where I've grown up and spent my entire life even when I become unable to work.
This housing plan doesn't create it. Toronto city council needs to do better. We need to push them until they do.
CAPTION: Toronto city council's initiative to create 65,000 affordable rental units is welcome. But these 'affordable units' aren't affordable at all. The average person on disability receives $1,133 a month, writes Dianna Gunn. Even a studio apartment using the current guidelines would cost 90 per cent of their monthly income.
CREDIT: Dianna Gunn, Contributor

Subject: Minimum wage; Councils; Rentals; Apartments; Disability; Cities

Business indexing term: Subject: Minimum wage

Ford optimistic as LCBO talks resume

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-optimistic-as-lcbo-talks-resume/docview/3082121592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+optimistic+as+LCBO+talks+resume&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not just the booze talking.
Talks are continuing between the Liquor Control Board of Ontario and the union representing more than 9,000 workers who have been off the job for two weeks.
Contract bargaining resumed Wednesday for the first time since July 4, leaving Premier Doug Ford optimistic a deal can be reached to get employees back on the job and 680 LCBO stores reopened.
Ford said he was "quite excited" that the Ontario Public Service Employees Union had returned to the negotiating table.
"I've talked to hundreds and hundreds of front-line workers, they just want to get back to work," the premier told reporters at the annual Council of the Federation meeting in Halifax.
"Hopefully we can get this done. We've offered benefits, we've offered a (seven per cent) pay increase (over three years and 400) part-time folks full-time opportunities," he said, praising the striking OPSEU members who have contacted him for being "so polite."
"They told me their stories (and it) hits you in the heart."
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team that represents the striking workers, insisted "we will not back down from our core demands to protect good jobs and public revenues."
"We are showing up today in good faith that a fair deal can finally be reached. What we're putting forward are ideas that are good for workers and good for Ontario," said MacLeod.
The first strike in LCBO history came after Ford announced the province would pay the Beer Store $225 million to move up the Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded liberalization of alcohol sales.
Under the premier's changes, beer, wine and "ready-to-drink" beverages can be sold at 8,500 licenced supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Liquor; Strikes

Business indexing term: Subject: Strikes

Few Ontarians affected by job action, poll finds: Nearly half of respondents support union's demands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/few-ontarians-affected-job-action-poll-finds/docview/3082121558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Few+Ontarians+affected+by+job+action%2C+poll+finds%3A+Nearly+half+of+respondents+support+union%27s+demands&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new poll suggests just 15 per cent of Ontarians are personally affected by the LCBO's ongoing strike, as the provincial government expands access to alcohol at other retailers.
That's despite nearly 90 per cent of respondents in a new Leger poll saying they are aware the job action is happening.
Fewer than one-third of Ontarians say they want the provincial government to intervene to end the nearly two-week strike at Ontario's main liquor retailer, while about half are supportive of the striking union's demands.
The Leger poll of 601 residents can't be assigned a margin of error because online surveys are not considered truly random samples.
Approximately 10,000 workers at the LCBO walked off the job July 5 after negotiations broke down.
The union representing the workers said the sides were headed back to the bargaining table Wednesday.
The poll conducted last weekend asked whether the government should use binding arbitration or legislation to ensure that LCBO stores open back up as soon as possible.
Twenty-nine per cent of respondents supported such a move, while 44 per cent opposed it.
The poll also asked if respondents support the union's stated goals, including wage increases and more permanent positions.
Just under half, 49 per cent, answered in the affirmative, while 25 per cent said they were not supportive.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union has said the main issue is the province's alcohol expansion plans, which would see ready-to-drink cocktails sold outside LCBO stores - a move it maintains poses an existential threat to the LCBO and could lead to major job losses.
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the union's LCBO bargaining unit, has said the plan would "mean thousands of lost jobs, fewer hours for the 70 per cent of LCBO retail workers who are casual and struggling to make ends meet and hundreds of millions in dollars of lost public revenues drained from health care, education and infrastructure."
The LCBO, a Crown corporation, nets the province $2.5 billion a year.
On Monday, the Ontario government sped up its expansion plan. The 450 stores across Ontario already licensed to sell beer, wine and ciders will be able to start ordering coolers and seltzers on Thursday and sell them as soon as they arrive.
Stephanie Ross, an associate professor in the school of labour studies at McMaster University, said Premier Doug Ford doesn't have a great reputation when it comes to labour, given the high-profile disputes in recent years with health-care and education workers.
And he's faced accusations of making policy moves that benefit friends in the private sector, a criticism that's been levied against him in the LCBO dispute.
"There is a base of support for the union's message here, both in terms of the working conditions that they're trying to fight to improve, and in terms of the role that the LCBO plays in funding public services in the province," she said.
But the public may not be as sympathetic to LCBO workers as it has been to some others, like in the Metro grocery workers' strike last year, she said - a relatively straightforward fight by low-paid workers struggling to afford food against the industry being partially blamed for food prices.
That means the LCBO union has its work cut out to try and convince the public of its cause, said Ross.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The Ontario Public Service Employees Union says the province's alcohol expansion plan poses an existential threat to the LCBO and could lead to major job losses.Steve RussellToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Anja Karadeglija and Rosa Saba The Canadian Press

Document 268 of 500

Ford vows to take on feds: Change of power in Ottawa wouldn't alter his priorities, premier insists

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-vows-take-on-feds/docview/3082121399/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+vows+to+take+on+feds%3A+Change+of+power+in+Ottawa+wouldn%27t+alter+his+priorities%2C+premier+insists&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford insists it does not matter whether the Liberals or the Conservatives win the next federal election.
But in a veiled shot at Pierre Poilievre's federal Tories, Ford - who has worked closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals - says regardless of who is elected, he will fight "like a pit bull" for the province if Ottawa acts against its interests.
Speaking to the Star in an exclusive interview at the annual meeting of Canada's 13 premiers, held this week in Nova Scotia, Ford also said calling an Ontario election in 2025 - a year before the scheduled vote in June 2026 - is not something he's thinking about now.
"It's not on my mind," he said. "I never rule out anything, but my main focus is to continue moving the province forward and really focusing on the things that people really care about - and that's the economy that drives everything else, health care," as well addressing increasing concerns about crime.
An early election call "is probably the last thing on my mind right now. And we'll see when we get closer."
The Star reported in May that Ford is considering moving up the Ontario vote in order to go to the polls before an expected October 2025 federal election.
That's because he is concerned about budget cuts and policy changes a future Poilievre government might impose.
"I just want to make sure on our federal election that Ontario has a strong voice," Ford told the Star.
"No matter what party it is - and I don't care what party does win - if it's not in the best interest of Ontario, and the federal government does something to hurt Ontario, I'm going after them like a pit bull."
Asked Wednesday about the prospect of a Poilievre government, the premier said, "I can't have concerns about him - I've never sat down and really negotiated anything with him."
The premier said he is "focusing on dealing with the government that's in there right now, and I'll always focus on that. But if for any reason if that changes, my No. 1 priority is Ontario."
Ford is the most powerful Conservative politician in Canada after winning back-to-back majority governments in a province his federal counterparts need if they want to form a government. But he's had a rocky relationship with Poilievre's team, some of whom privately maintain the premier isn't really a conservative.
Just last March, Ford was visibly displeased by an attack on his then education minister Stephen Lecce by newly elected federal Tory MP Jamil Jivani - whom the premier had personally appointed as his communities opportunities advocate in 2019.
In his victory speech after winning the byelection in Durham, Jivani railed about "the liberal elites who run the Ontario Ministry of Education in this province."
That prompted Ford to retort that when Jivani "showed up - no one even knew who he was - he came in my office, I gave him an incredible opportunity" five years ago.
"To be very frank - and go to root cause - he wouldn't even be where he is if I didn't give him that opportunity," Ford said at the time. "Maybe he should come in, I'll give him a couple of lessons on how to speak to ... his constituents and that."
Ford takes the helm of the Council of the Federation - comprising the 10 provincial and three territorial leaders - on Aug. 1, and said they are united in advocating their needs to Ottawa.
"I've always said to the other premiers, the smaller provinces - we have a duty to help our smaller provinces, to make ourselves a lot stronger as a country," Ford said. "They appreciate that. I've always taken that approach."
Whether the Liberals should call a federal election before October 2025 "is up to the people, it's up to the federal government," Ford also said. "It all depends on what they decide, but either way, I'm going to focus No. 1 on Ontario and support our other premiers, the territories and the provinces.
"They look at Ontario as a leader and we'll always lead."
Ford flew to Halifax on Monday with wife Karla, but said she had to rush home after massive downpours in the Toronto area on Tuesday that flooded highways, roads and homes also poured into their Etobicoke property.
"We got the videos - there was four feet of water piling through the downstairs, and a couple of feet of water" in the basement, he said, adding his brother went in there with pumps to help clear it out.
"This is one of the million different stories - everyone had a lot of damage," he said.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Provinces; Roads & highways

Location: Canada

Document 269 of 500

How will Ford handle his leadership role?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-will-ford-handle-his-leadership-role/docview/3082121375/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+will+Ford+handle+his+leadership+role%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Listening to Doug Ford and his fellow premiers shedding a few tears in Halifax this week, you'd think the world - and the country - were coming to an end.
With predictable passion, they rattled off a list of perennial perils:
Federal interventionism is high. American protectionism is nigh. Asylum-seekers are on the rise. Equalization is inequitable.
Feasting on seafood, washed down with sour grapes while sharing a few private laughs, the premiers used their annual conference to toast one another for prudent stewardship of their own provinces. While raising the alarm about everyone else:
With a Donald Trump presidency looming, Canadians face renewed risk. With Justin Trudeau's prime ministerial reign lagging, Canadians face prolonged risk.
Climate change is in the air, but most of the premiers called for the federal carbon levy to be buried. Federal meddling in provincial jurisdiction is intolerable, yet the premiers cheerfully waded into Ottawa's bailiwick by demanding more defence spending and cuts to interest rates (by the independent Bank of Canada).
Ottawa-bashing (and hectoring) is apparently part of every premier's job description. But what if it's your turn to serve as leader of the pack - not merely as one premier among 13, but the one who represents them all?
What then? What tone might you strike, what topics would you like?
For Ford, that challenge is now at hand. For the next year, he will hold the gavel as chair of the Council of the Federation (as this parley of provinces and territories is called).
Next July in Ontario, the premier will convene his counterparts to frame a national agenda for collaboration in the Confederation. Can Ford rise to the challenge?
To be sure, there's no certainty he will still be premier, given his private (now public) fantasy of springing an early election on Ontario next spring. Even if one assumes he wins re-election - his Progressive Conservatives enjoy a commanding lead in the polls - there's no guarantee Trudeau will still be prime minister, leaving him a sitting duck for quacking premiers.
As Ford mused in an interview with my colleague Kristin Rushowy, he knows little of Pierre Poilievre, the federal opposition leader whose Conservatives are far ahead in the polls. If Poilievre wins power before the next annual summit in Ontario, there's no assurance Ford or his fellow premiers would be any kinder toward the new prime minister.
But situational politics, like situational ethics, is a sometime thing. Ford has always aspired to have a national voice, so if he wants to make his mark as a leader among premiers, he needs to have something more enduring to say.
At the podium in Halifax on Wednesday, Ford couldn't resist taking a shot at the federal government for the allegedly costly way it delivers social programs for low-income Canadians, which leaves the premiers "pissed." But as a populist, Ford knows better than to think Canadians care for these traditional jurisdictional squabbles.
Give him credit for trying to be constructive. Ford travelled to Halifax armed with a proposal to speed up approvals for life-saving drugs, and placed it squarely on the agenda for the next year.
All that said, the fastest way to get the green light for new medicines would be through a national pharmacare program that integrates the approval, procurement, pricing and distribution processes across all boundaries - bureaucratic and geographic. Ford has been hostile to pharmacare since taking power, targeting the embryonic OHIP+ program developed by the previous Liberal government to deliver universal coverage to people up to age 25 or 65 and older (the program now helps only those young people without private benefits while seniors still get free drugs).
A little policy coherence on this growing health-care contradiction - a free, universal medicare program without free medicines for all - would make Ford sound more convincing on the national stage as he takes on the challenge of being chair. Pharmacare is no longer about ideology, it's about efficiency and equity.
Where Ford might truly make his mark, however, is not so much on national issues but bilateral problems. If Trump wins the November election and the Republicans are ascendant, the premier will have his work cut out for him; even if the Democrats somehow hold on, protectionist impulses will surge.
Ford is planning a trip to Washington in the new year, after the January inauguration. He will have limited influence as a premier - even one who speaks for all premiers - but he is well placed to lobby on our behalf.
Perhaps it takes a populist to understand a populist. More than that, it takes someone who has lived in America to comprehend the lived American experience (Ford lived in Chicago for many years running a branch of the family's printing business).
As an expatriate Canadian, Ford understood how inward-looking the American outlook can be. Now as an incoming chair of the premiers' platform, he needs to be rise above the inward-looking impulses among the provinces.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks Wednesday at the final news conference of this week's Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax. Ford will take over as leader of the group next month.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 270 of 500

As a renter in Toronto, I live in fear of being forced out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/as-renter-toronto-i-live-fear-being-forced-out/docview/3082121363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+a+renter+in+Toronto%2C+I+live+in+fear+of+being+forced+out&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've lived in apartments in Toronto my whole life. I've been in my current apartment for nine years - and I've watched in horror as apartments around me doubled, then tripled in price. For the past five years, I've lived with the terrifying knowledge that if I had to move tomorrow, there would be nowhere for me to go. As a struggling creative and small business owner, even prices in Etobicoke and Scarborough are beyond my reach.
I also have chronic health issues that could worsen at any moment, rendering me unable to work and unable to afford even my current, rent-controlled apartment. I live in constant fear that something will change, either with my apartment or my health, and force me onto the street.
So, I was excited to see Toronto city council's initiative to create 65,000 affordable rental units. But I was also nervous that these affordable apartments wouldn't actually provide housing to our most vulnerable populations. Affordable is, after all, a relative term. If we define "affordable" based on the average prices of Toronto rentals - $2,392 for a one bedroom and $3,166 for a two bedroom - we can argue that $1,500 for a one bedroom and $2,500 for a two bedroom is affordable.
The monthly rent for a studio apartment built under city council's initiative will cost $1,088. A one bedroom will be $1,378. A two bedroom will cost $1,992. A three bedroom will be $2,190. Not as high as the average Toronto rental, but it still seems to be defined by the market.
I define affordable differently. In my mind, to be affordable, a rental unit must cost no more than 30 per cent of before-tax income of our poorest populations - disabled folks, people on disability benefits and minimum wage workers.
By this generally accepted standard, the city council's "affordable units" aren't affordable at all. The average person on disability receives $1,133 a month. Even a studio apartment using the current guidelines would cost 90 per cent of their monthly income.
Things are even worse for folks on Ontario Works income support program, under which a single person only receives $390 a month for shelter and $343 a month for their basic needs. Even combining those two amounts wouldn't provide enough for a studio apartment using the city's current definition of affordable.
In short, if you can't work - or if you lose your job and don't find a new one before EI runs out - you can't afford one of these "affordable" units.
Things are a little better for minimum-wage workers, making $2,440 a month (before taxes). A studio apartment will be more than 30 per cent of their income, but they'll technically be able to afford it. They might even be able to afford a one bedroom if they're smart about budgeting. What they won't be able to do is rent a two or three bedroom. In other words, single parents working minimum wage won't be able to claim these units and provide adequate housing for their children.
The current Ontario government has no intention of raising disability, Ontario Works, or minimum wage. If we vote Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives out next election, we might see a minimum wage increase, but Liberal governments have proven equally reluctant to raise disability benefits or Ontario Works. So, it's up to Toronto's city council to provide housing these people can actually afford.
There are some rent-geared-to-income apartments - 6,500 of them - included in the city's plan. That is not enough. Not when the city's subsidized housing list is 10 years long and you have to jump through hoops to even get on the list.
A housing initiative meant to really uplift the most vulnerable among us would include closer to 20,000 rent-geared-to-income units and a definition of affordability that accounts for our lowest-income residents for the rest.
I want - no, need - to believe there's a future in Toronto for disabled people, for people struggling to find work, for people working minimum wage. I need to believe there's a future here for me, that I'll be able to stay in this city where I've grown up and spent my entire life even when I become unable to work.
This housing plan doesn't create it. Toronto city council needs to do better. We need to push them until they do.
Dianna Gunn is a Toronto freelance writer and fantasy and horror author.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Dianna Gunn Contributor;

Subject: Minimum wage; Councils; Rentals; Apartments; Affordable housing; Urban development; Disability; Rental services; Income inequality; Cities; Initiatives; Public policy

Document 271 of 500

Provinces say newcomers need help: Ford calls on Ottawa to help premiers cover costs of providing support for asylum-seekers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-say-newcomers-need-help/docview/3082121269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provinces+say+newcomers+need+help%3A+Ford+calls+on+Ottawa+to+help+premiers+cover+costs+of+providing+support+for+asylum-seekers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is asking the federal government to pay up and cover the costs of supporting and housing asylum-seekers, as Quebec's premier said his province is willing to give up some funding so that other provinces can take in larger numbers.
Speaking at the final news conference at the Council of the Federation's annual summer meeting - this year hosted by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston - Premier Doug Ford said Ontario spends $1 billion a year and that asylum-seekers are often left in limbo by the federal government.
"When it comes to asylum-seekers, Quebec and Ontario bear the brunt of it," Ford told reporters. "We're just asking the federal government to give our fair share. We've spent over a billion dollars in Ontario every single year."
Ford said he talks to newcomers "and they want to work. They want a better life, and it's taking way too long to get their working visa from the federal government."
The premiers' demand follows Mayor Olivia Chow's long-standing request to Ottawa for additional help for her cash-strapped city.
While the federal government finally came forward with $97 million for Toronto last year to help refugees, there are still asylum-seekers lined outside of the Peter Street homeless intake centre.
That was part of a one-time infusion of $362.4 million for refugee resettlement across Canada.
City hall says there are still more than 4,000 asylum-seekers in shelters each night, which nearly matches the number of homeless people in them.
Ford said families are often stuck "staying in a hotel like they do on the airport strip up in Toronto ... or a couple of kids down playing in a parking lot in Scarborough" when they "want a better life. They want to work like everyone else ... but we have to make sure we have shelter, we have homes for them. We really need the support of the federal government."
Quebec Premier François Legault said "it's not fair" his province receives $750 million for asylum-seekers, while other provinces "receive nothing. We would prefer to receive less in the future and have less asylum-seekers."
Alberta is also taking in proportionally more newcomers, said Premier Danielle Smith, noting some 70,000 Ukrainian evacuees have recently settled in her province.
"Aside from a small amount of money at the beginning, there are hotels that need to be funded, there are schools that need to be funded" as well as health-care needs and other programming, she said.
"We want to do our part, but ... when provinces are shouldering larger burdens," Ottawa needs to step up, Smith added.
The 13 provincial and territorial leaders held talks on a number of issues over the past three days, including health care, federal equalization payments and relations with the U.S.
Looming large is the outcome of the U.S. presidential election and the impact that could have on trade relations.
Earlier Wednesday, Ford noted that when tariffs were placed on Canadian goods by the Trump administration, talks were held with U.S. trade representatives and the matter was resolved within a few weeks.
"We won't hesitate if they put tariffs on any of our products," Ford said when asked about tough talk coming from presidential candidates regarding trade.
"We do $500 billion of trade in Ontario alone with the U.S. ... We're the number one customer to 17 states and number two to 11," Ford said, adding other provinces have strong trading ties as well.
"We are an economic powerhouse," the premier added.
Prince Edward Island Premier Dennis King said no matter the outcome of the election, there will "be good discussions and (we'll) continue the good relationship" with the U.S.
Ford takes over as chair of the premiers' group Aug. 1, and said U.S. relations will be key.
"Hopefully we'll plan a trip as the new administration, whoever it is, gets sworn in" after the November election, Ford said. "We'll have a conversation ... and (stress) it's not just 'buy America,' it's about 'buy North America.'
"At the end of the day, it's Canada, the U.S. and you can put Mexico in there, against the rest of the world, no matter if it's South America, Asia, Europe," Ford told reporters.
He also said federal government duplication in some programs - notably in the recent student nutrition program - overlaps with provincial efforts and costs more, wasting taxpayer dollars.
"And you know what happens?" Ford said, "The premiers get pissed. It's as simple as that. They just jump in there and think they know better what we know, what we deal with on a daily basis."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Canada's premiers gather for a group photo this week during the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau With files from Robert Benzie

Document 272 of 500

Doug Ford tells the Star he won't rule out an early election and vows to defend Ontario 'like a pit bull' against Ottawa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-tells-star-he-wont-rule-out-early/docview/3082348437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+tells+the+Star+he+won%27t+rule+out+an+early+election+and+vows+to+defend+Ontario+%27like+a+pit+bull%27+against+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-Premier Doug Ford insists it does not matter whether the Liberals or the Conservatives win the next federal election.
But in a veiled shot at Pierre Poilievre's federal Tories, Ford - who has worked closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals - says regardless of who is elected, he will fight "like a pit bull" for the province if Ottawa acts against its interests.
Speaking to the Star in an exclusive interview at the annual meeting of Canada's 13 premiers, held this week in Nova Scotia, Ford also said calling an Ontario election in 2025 - a year before the June 2026 scheduled vote - is not something he's thinking about now.
"It's not on my mind," he said. "I never rule out anything, but my main focus is to continue moving the province forward and really focusing on the things that people really care about - and that's the economy that drives everything else, health care," as well addressing increasing concerns about crime.
An early election call "is probably the last thing on my mind right now. And we'll see when we get closer."
The Star reported in May that Ford is considering moving up the Ontario vote in order to go to the polls before an expected October 2025 federal election.
That's because he is concerned about budget cuts and policy changes a future Poilievre government might impose.
"I just want to make sure on our federal election that Ontario has a strong voice," Ford told the Star.
"No matter what party it is - and I don't care what party does win - if it's not in the best interest of Ontario, and the federal government does something to hurt Ontario, I'm going after them like a pit bull."
Asked Wednesday about the prospect of a Poilievre government, the premier said, "I can't have concerns about him - I've never sat down and really negotiated anything with him."
The premier said he is "focusing on dealing with the government that's in there right now, and I'll always focus on that. But if for any reason if that changes, my number one priority is Ontario."
Ford is the most powerful Conservative politician in Canada after winning back-to-back majority governments in a province his federal counterparts need if they want to form government. But he's had a rocky relationship with Poilievre's team, some of whom privately maintain the premier isn't really a conservative.
Just last March, Ford was visibly displeased by an attack on his then-education minister Stephen Lecce by newly elected federal Tory MP Jamil Jivani - who the premier had personally appointed as his communities opportunities advocate in 2019.
In his victory speech after winning the byelection in Durham, Jivani railed about "the liberal elites who run the Ontario Ministry of Education in this province."
That prompted Ford to retort that when Jivani "showed up - no one even knew who he was - he came in my office, I gave him an incredible opportunity" five years ago.
"To be very frank - and go to root cause - he wouldn't even be where he is if I didn't give him that opportunity," Ford said at the time.
"Maybe he should come in, I'll give him a couple of lessons on how to speak to ... his constituents and that."
Ford takes the helm of the Council of the Federation - comprising the 10 provincial and three territorial leaders - on Aug. 1, and said they are united in advocating their needs to Ottawa.
"I've always said to the other premiers, the smaller provinces - we have a duty to help our smaller provinces, to make ourselves a lot stronger as a country," Ford said. "They appreciate that. I've always taken that approach."
Whether the Liberals should call a federal election before October 2025 "is up to the people, it's up to the federal government," Ford also said. "It all depends on what they decide, but either way, I'm going to focus number one on Ontario and support our other premiers, the territories and the provinces.
"They look at Ontario as a leader and we'll always lead."
Ford flew to Halifax on Monday with wife Karla, but said she had to rush home after massive downpours in the Toronto area on Tuesday that flooded highways, roads and homes also poured into their Etobicoke property.
"We got the videos - there was four feet of water piling through the downstairs, and a couple of feet of water" in the basement, he said, adding his brother went in there with pumps to help clear it out.
"This is one of the million different stories - everyone had a lot of damage," he said.

Document 273 of 500

Doug Ford optimistic as contract talks resume in LCBO strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-optimistic-as-contract-talks-resume/docview/3081756667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+optimistic+as+contract+talks+resume+in+LCBO+strike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An optimistic Premier Doug Ford is hopeful progress will be made in talks aimed at ending the 13-day strike by Liquor Control Board of Ontario workers.
As contract negotiations between the LCBO and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union resumed Wednesday for the first time since July 4, Ford said he was "quite excited about that."
"I've talked to hundreds and hundreds of frontline workers, they just want to get back to work. The people of Ontario want to work, LCBO wants to work," the premier told reporters at the annual Council of the Federation meeting in Halifax.
"Hopefully we can get this done. We've offered benefits, we've offered a (seven per cent) pay increase (over three years and 400) part-time folks full-time opportunities," he said, praising the striking OPSEU members who have contacted him for being "so polite."
"They told me their stories (and it) hits you in the heart."
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team that represents the more than 9,000 strikers who have shut down 680 LCBO outlets, insisted "we will not back down from our core demands to protect good jobs and public revenues."
"We are showing up today in good faith that a fair deal can finally be reached. What we're putting forward are ideas that are good for workers and good for Ontario," said MacLeod.
The first strike in LCBO history came six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to speed up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded liberalization of alcohol sales.
Under the premier's changes, beer, wine and "ready-to-drink" beverages can be sold at 8,500 licensed supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
While the LCBO currently enjoys a monopoly on most premixed alcoholic drinks - the fastest-growing booze category - Ford said "that ship has sailed" and such products will be more widely available.
The 450 supermarkets licensed to sell beer and wine by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 can begin ordering ready-to-drink booze - like Bacardi Breezer and Mike's Hard Lemonade - as of Thursday with cans and bottle expected on grocery shelves next week.
Ford emphasized the LCBO would retain its exclusive rights for the sale of spirits, such as whisky, vodka, gin, rum and tequila, and it will control the wholesale distribution of wine and ready-to-drink products to all retailers.
But OPSEU fears "the Ford government's expanded alcohol-everywhere scheme" will lead to reduced public revenues and inevitable job losses for the Crown asset.
Union president JP Hornick said "today is about the concrete gains that must be achieved at the bargaining table, directly with this employer, to end the strike that Ford forced by interfering."
Internal Progressive Conservative government polling obtained by the Star found overwhelmingly support for more liberalized alcohol sales even though 54 per cent back OPSEU's "decision to go strike" while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
The Campaign Research survey suggested the job action is so far having little impact on the public.
About three-quarter of respondents - 73 per cent - said the job action was not "inconvenient" while 28 per cent said it was.
There is 91 per cent awareness of the strike with only nine per cent unaware.
With 69 per cent backing Wynne's decision to begin liberalization and 57 per cent support for Ford's expansion of sales - and 61 per cent favouring more availability of premixed drinks - the Tories believe public opinion is with them.
Campaign Research surveyed 1,995 Ontarians last Friday and Saturday using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
According to Campaign Research, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 18 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and 10 per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
That's similar to the findings in last month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, which found the PCs at 41 per cent, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford holds a flat of beer cans during a photo-op at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto.

Document 274 of 500

One way for progressives to beat Pierre Poilievre: Create a new party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-way-progressives-beat-pierre-poilievre-create/docview/3081756664/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+way+for+progressives+to+beat+Pierre+Poilievre%3A+Create+a+new+party&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If ever there was a time for a "big tent" left wing party in Canada, now is it.
Not a merger of the Liberals and NDP, mind you. This misses the point that Canadians are dissatisfied with current leadership.
Canada already has a de facto merger of the two parties anyways. But in trying to be all things to all people, this merged entity has alienated many Canadian progressives. The Liberals have governed as though they owe nothing to the party keeping them in power, while the NDP has asked for far too little in exchange for too much. Though the limited dental and pharmaceutical care agreements are worthwhile, they would be insufficient for a party with an unencumbered progressive agenda.
What we need is a new political party comprising the disaffected progressive members of all federal parties. With a clear progressive vision for the future, such a party would pose a more formidable challenge to the Conservatives than either the Liberals or the NDP can do today. And recent examples from around the world demonstrate that 'big tent' progressive efforts have considerable public appeal.
Many political experts were surprised by the recent electoral victory of France's New Popular Front, which goes to show how left-wing coalitions can form governments even when underestimated. Recent electoral successes in the United Kingdom, Mexico and India also underscore the appeal of progressive leadership in this era of economic uncertainty.
The New Popular Front ran on a sensible progressive platform: taxing the rich, free school lunches, building a million new homes, investing in France's infrastructure. That they further committed to recognizing Palestine didn't hurt either, given the widespread and growing sense of helplessness felt by many witnessing the horrors committed against the people of Gaza.
What is needed most in Canada is a rearticulation of shared progressive values - for the public, not the political class - as the foundation for such a movement. An open door, big tent, progressive party where shared values and goals serve as the main attraction, rather than the personality cults of career politicians, would be a welcome and refreshing change of pace.
A concerted effort at rebuilding the social safety net by significant increases in taxation on the highest earners and corporations, in addition to major investments to decarbonize the energy and transport grids and buttress against climate change, would be crucial focus areas to improve quality of life and stimulate the economy. A rejuvenation of our democracy through electoral, Senate, and constitutional reforms, could make Canada a more participatory and representative democracy. A stronger federal democracy would have the mandate needed to fight back against encroaching privatization of healthcare, and disinvestment in education, committed by conservative governments ideologically opposed to the values of social democracy.
If these sound like radical proposals, recent world events show they're also salable in this moment.
Where France's right-wing defined itself in opposition to everything from immigration to "le wokeisme," France's left was open, inclusive, and accepting.
Canada has had seven elections in the last 20 years, and the highest participation level reached was in 2015, when 68.3 per cent of eligible voters cast ballots. That was the election Trudeau promised would be the last using "first past the post" and arguably one won on a platform of progressive promises. Despite Trudeau's comfortable majority at the time, many of those promises were abandoned.
Over the past 20 years anywhere from 32 to 42 per cent of the electorate hasn't voted, and it's this "silent plurality" - combined with disaffected Liberal, NDP, Green, Bloc, and even Communist Party supporters - that could sweep a new progressive alternative to power in Canada.
Creating a new party isn't easy-nothing worthwhile ever is. There will be resistance from those inside the political bubble who will argue this is splitting the vote. But the progressive vote is already split several ways, and the entrenched leadership of extant political parties are resisting cooperation to their detriment.
Canadians do not have to accept Pierre Poilievre as an inevitability, just as the French and British didn't have to accept as inevitable a government led by Le Pen or Farage.
A new progressive big tent could be a powerful antidote to the toxicity on the Canadian right.
CAPTION: Participants gesture toward a giant banner that reads as "France is the fabric of migration" during an election night rally following the first results of the second round of France's legislative election, at Place de la Republique in Paris on Sunday. A loose alliance of French left-wing parties thrown together for snap elections was on course to become the biggest parliamentary bloc and beat the far right, according to shock projected results.

A historic storm and flooding in Toronto + a 15-year-old fatally struck in Brampton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/historic-storm-flooding-toronto-15-year-old/docview/3081756564/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+historic+storm+and+flooding+in+Toronto+%2B+a+15-year-old+fatally+struck+in+Brampton&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, July 17 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the downpour and flooding in Toronto that left the city drenched and in the dark, and remembering a 15-year-old fatally struck in Brampton.
DON'T MISS
How a downpour walloped Toronto: "There was a storm on top of a storm"
Torontonians across the city were pelted with torrential rain on Tuesday, while subsequent flooding left commuters stranded at stations and drivers needing rescue on the DVP. The unexpected downpour left thousands across the downtown area without power for hours, Omar Mosleh, Calvi Leon and I report. Businesses were shuttered, cars were trapped in traffic and pedestrians were soaked. Here's how Toronto dealt with a historic day for rainfall.
How much rain? Toronto usually receives 74mm of rain throughout July. The city, on Tuesday, was showered with 98mm of rain - more than a month's worth of rainfall in a day.Dark and stormy: Over 127,000 people were without power in the afternoon, with outages slowly fixed through till midnight. Toronto Hydro told the Star's Mark Colley that suspected flooding at a transmission station led to the massive outage.Soaked through: Across the city, Torontonians grappled with the weather in their special ways. Some marveled, sending video of the pooling water to family back home. Others took shelter in Tim Hortons. None were dry.Toronto was simply "not designed to handle" this much rain, city warns
Toronto's infrastructure was "not designed to handle this," the city's top civil servant told reporters on Tuesday as rain leaked through the ceiling at City Hall. The flooding from the rain shut down Union Station and a major highway, prompting emergency services to scramble to 1,700 calls across the city. Tuesday's storm was just shy of the 2013 single-day rainfall record that caused $1 billion in damages. The flooding won't be fixed with a bucket and some towels, here's what Toronto needs before the next storm hits.
Mississauga, Brampton and Halton: It didn't just rain in Toronto, extreme flooding in Mississauga overflowed its creeks and rivers, tipping water into parks and green spaces. Emergency services across Peel and Halton Regions attended to reports of flooding in basements, amidst power outages.Early warning: As extreme storms happen more often and become more severe thanks to climate change, you might be checking the city's flood map to see if you're safe - but that won't give you the full picture. Check here for Toronto's secret flood map.To the gills: A strike, a flood, a power outage. Bars and businesses across Toronto struggled to cope with the perfect storm of stressors. Amidst an LCBO strike that has left them lacking spirits, the rain and flooding couldn't have come at a worse time.15-year-old fatally struck on sidewalk after crash "had so many plans"
Maddy Rousell, 15, was supposed to learn to drive with her dad last Saturday but she was killed by an allegedly impaired driver in Brampton just after 8 p.m. on Friday, walking on her way home. Her father's last words to her while dropping her off at a friend's house a few hours earlier: "I love you. Be careful." She responded, "OK. I love you, too." Here's how Maddy's friends, family and loved ones remember her.
About Maddy: She was a curious teen who enjoyed math and despised gym, according to her mother. She loved fuzzy peach candy. Her sister, Brittnye, said she had a fearlessness that let her conquer even the most intimidating rides at Canada's Wonderland.Context: A 36-year-old Mississauga man is charged with refusing to comply with a demand for the roadside screening device and dangerous operation of a vehicle causing death.WHAT ELSE
The Trump shooting has set off a flood of conspiracy theories. But you might be surprised where some are coming from.A moment of clarity in a broken, flooded Toronto.Toronto saw a sharp drop in housing starts in June as high interest rates catch up to developers.Inflation falls to 2.7 per cent in June, increasing the odds of a Bank of Canada interest rate cut.These new Canadian auto theft numbers may surprise you.University of Windsor criticized for a deal that ended its pro-Palestinian encampment.Internal poll for Doug Ford finds strong support for the booze liberalization plan - and striking LCBO workers.LCBO and OPSEU to resume bargaining talks on Wednesday morning.An increased Canada Child Benefit will go out this Friday. Here's how much more you could get.The Raptors' future might be here, if they can develop it.Live chat: Join us to discuss the secret in Alice Munro's house on July 24.The last time I saw the Red Hot Chili Peppers, I was a teenage superfan. Last night's show at Bud Stage left me ice cold.POV
There's a reason this Republican convention feels like The Eras Tour for Donald Trump fans.
CLOSE-UP
DON VALLEY PARKWAY: When do you abandon your car on a highway? Maybe when it starts to float. Drivers stuck in the middle of the road as water filled up to their waists needed were by emergency services on Tuesday. Toronto Fire responded to over a thousand calls yesterday, and were sent to nearly 500 incidents, from elevator rescues to water calls. Here's how the rescue on the DVP unfolded.
Thank you for reading First Up. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca
Sign up here to get this newsletter in your inbox.

Document 276 of 500

'North America first': Doug Ford has a message for America as premiers discuss U.S. relations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/north-america-first-doug-ford-has-message-as/docview/3081756462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27North+America+first%27%3A+Doug+Ford+has+a+message+for+America+as+premiers+discuss+U.S.+relations&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX - Ontario is asking the federal government to pay up and cover the costs of supporting and housing asylum-seekers, as Quebec's premier said his province is willing to give up some funding so that other provinces can take in larger numbers.
Speaking at the final news conference at the Council of the Federation's annual summer meeting - this year hosted by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston - Premier Doug Ford said Ontario spends $1 billion a year and that asylum-seekers are often left in limbo by the federal government.
"When it comes to asylum-seekers, Quebec and Ontario bear the brunt of it," Ford told reporters. "We're just asking the federal government to give our fair share. We've spent over a billion dollars in Ontario every single year."
Ford said he talks to newcomers "and they want to work. They want a better life, and it's taking way too long to get their working visa from the federal government."
The premiers' demand follows Mayor Olivia Chow's long-standing request to Ottawa for additional help for her cash-strapped city.
While the federal government finally came forward with $97 million for Toronto last year to help refugees, there are still asylum-seekers lined outside of the Peter Street homeless intake centre.
That was part of a one-time infusion of $362.4 million for refugee resettlement across Canada.
City hall says there are still more than 4,000 refugees and asylum-seekers in shelters each night, which nearly matches the number of homeless people in them.
Ford said families are often stuck "staying in a hotel like they do on the airport strip up in Toronto ... or a couple of kids down playing in a parking lot in Scarborough" when they "want a better life. They want to work like everyone else ... but we have to make sure we have shelter, we have homes for them. We really need the support of the federal government."
Quebec Premier François Legault said "it's not fair" that his province receives $750 million for asylum-seekers, while other provinces "receive nothing. We would prefer to receive less in the future and have less asylum-seekers."
Alberta is also taking in proportionally more newcomers, said Premier Danielle Smith, noting some 70,000 Ukrainian evacuees have recently settled in her province.
"Aside from a small amount of money at the beginning, there are hotels that need to be funded, there are schools that need to be funded" as well as health-care needs and other programming, she said.
"We want to do our part, but ... when provinces are shouldering larger burdens" Ottawa needs to step up, Smith added.
The 13 provincial and territorial leaders held talks on a number of issues over the past three days, including health care, federal equalization payments and relations with the U.S.
Looming large is the outcome of the U.S. presidential election and the impact that could have on trade relations.
Earlier Wednesday, Ford noted that when tariffs were placed on Canadian goods by the Trump administration, talks were held with U.S. trade representatives and the matter was resolved within a few weeks.
"We won't hesitate if they put tariffs on any of our products," Ford said when asked about tough talk coming from presidential candidates regarding trade.
"We do $500 billion of trade in Ontario alone with the U.S. ... We're the number-one customer to 17 states and number two to 11" Ford said, adding other provinces have strong trading ties as well.
"We are an economic powerhouse," the premier added.
Prince Edward Island Premier Dennis King said no matter the outcome of the election, there will "be good discussions and (we'll) continue the good relationship" with the U.S.
Ford takes over as chair of the premiers' group Aug. 1, and said U.S. relations will be key.
"Hopefully we'll plan a trip as the new administration, whoever it is, gets sworn in" after the November election, Ford said. "We'll have a conversation ... and (stress) it's not just 'buy America,' it's about 'buy North America.'
"At the end of the day, it's Canada, the U.S. and you can put Mexico in there, against the rest of the world, no matter if it's South America, Asia, Europe," Ford told reporters.
Ford also said federal government duplication in some programs - notably in the recent student nutrition program - overlaps with provincial efforts and costs more, wasting taxpayer dollars.
"And you know what happens?" Ford said, "The premiers get pissed. It's as simple as that. They just jump in there and think they know better what we know, what we deal with on a daily basis."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Canada's premiers gather for a group photo during the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on Monday.

New party is one way to stop Poilievre: 'Big tent' for progressives could reshape politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-party-is-one-way-stop-poilievre/docview/3081564580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+party+is+one+way+to+stop+Poilievre%3A+%27Big+tent%27+for+progressives+could+reshape+politics&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If ever there was a time for a "big tent" left wing party in Canada, now is it.
Not a merger of the Liberals and NDP, mind you. This misses the point that Canadians are dissatisfied with current leadership.
Canada already has a de facto merger of the two parties anyways. But in trying to be all things to all people, this merged entity has alienated many Canadian progressives. The Liberals have governed as though they owe nothing to the party keeping them in power, while the NDP has asked for far too little in exchange for too much. Though the limited dental and pharmaceutical care agreements are worthwhile, they would be insufficient for a party with an unencumbered progressive agenda.
What we need is a new political party comprising the disaffected progressive members of all federal parties. With a clear progressive vision for the future, such a party would pose a more formidable challenge to the Conservatives than either the Liberals or the NDP can do today. And recent examples from around the world demonstrate that 'big tent' progressive efforts have considerable public appeal.
Many political experts were surprised by the recent electoral victory of France's New Popular Front, which goes to show how left-wing coalitions can form governments even when underestimated. Recent electoral successes in the United Kingdom, Mexico and India also underscore the appeal of progressive leadership in this era of economic uncertainty.
The New Popular Front ran on a sensible progressive platform: taxing the rich, free school lunches, building a million new homes, investing in France's infrastructure. That they further committed to recognizing Palestine didn't hurt either, given the widespread and growing sense of helplessness felt by many witnessing the horrors committed against the people of Gaza.
What is needed most in Canada is a rearticulation of shared progressive values - for the public, not the political class - as the foundation for such a movement. An open door, big tent, progressive party where shared values and goals serve as the main attraction, rather than the personality cults of career politicians, would be a welcome and refreshing change of pace.
A concerted effort at rebuilding the social safety net by significant increases in taxation on the highest earners and corporations, in addition to major investments to decarbonize the energy and transport grids and buttress against climate change, would be crucial focus areas to improve quality of life and stimulate the economy. A rejuvenation of our democracy through electoral, Senate, and constitutional reforms, could make Canada a more participatory and representative democracy. A stronger federal democracy would have the mandate needed to fight back against encroaching privatization of health care, and disinvestment in education, committed by conservative governments ideologically opposed to the values of social democracy.
If these sound like radical proposals, recent world events show they're also saleable in this moment.
Where France's right-wing defined itself in opposition to everything from immigration to "le wokeisme," France's left was open, inclusive, and accepting.
Canada has had seven elections in the last 20 years, and the highest participation level reached was in 2015, when 68.3 per cent of eligible voters cast ballots. That was the election Trudeau promised would be the last using "first past the post" and arguably one won on a platform of progressive promises. Despite Trudeau's comfortable majority at the time, many of those promises were abandoned.
Over the past 20 years anywhere from 32 to 42 per cent of the electorate hasn't voted, and it's this "silent plurality" - combined with disaffected Liberal, NDP, Green, Bloc, and even Communist Party supporters - that could sweep a new progressive alternative to power in Canada.
Creating a new party isn't easy - nothing worthwhile ever is. There will be resistance from those inside the political bubble who will argue this is splitting the vote. But the progressive vote is already split several ways, and the entrenched leadership of extant political parties are resisting co-operation to their detriment.
Canadians do not have to accept Pierre Poilievre as an inevitability, just as the French and British didn't have to accept a government led by Marine Le Pen or Nigel Farage.
A new progressive big tent could be a powerful antidote to the toxicity on the Canadian right.
Taylor C. Noakes is an independent journalist and public
historian from Montreal.
He is a freelance contributor for the Star.

Document 278 of 500

U.S. trade rhetoric a worry for provinces: Ford pushes 'buy North America' mantra amid protectionist talk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/u-s-trade-rhetoric-worry-provinces/docview/3081564519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+trade+rhetoric+a+worry+for+provinces%3A+Ford+pushes+%27buy+North+America%27+mantra+amid+protectionist+talk&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With tough protectionist trade talk coming from U.S. presidential candidates, Premier Doug Ford says it's time to change the message to "buy North America."
"We are facing a lot of challenges in the country, as a new president will be elected (this November) - that was a big conversation," Ford said on Tuesday after emerging from a day of discussions among all 13 provincial and territorial leaders at the annual Council of the Federation meeting.
Amid "Buy America and "America first" rhetoric and policies, Ford said "we don't want to see the 10 per cent tariffs at all. I can speak for Ontario - we always believe in 'buy North America.'"
He said when it comes to promoting his province, "I'm first to admit, I'm probably the worst culprit when I told governors 'buy Ontario, buy Ontario' - but I think we are changing the language with the U.S. governors now."
The annual meeting, held this summer in Nova Scotia, saw the premiers discuss trade, housing, energy and emergency preparedness on Tuesday, among other topics including the upcoming U.S. election.
Some questions were raised about a Donald Trump victory and the impact it might have on asylum-seekers here, given the likelihood he would tighten up U.S. borders, as well as the need to boost relationships with governors in the states bordering Canada.
Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, who is chair of the federation until handing over the reins to Ford on Aug. 1, told reporters that economic ties to the U.S. are crucial.
"There's a significant amount of trade that happens, and Canada is a trusted trading partner to the United States, so the discussion is around how do we maintain that relationship," Houston said.
He said that Ford's "buy North America" mantra takes both large and small provinces into account, and "while he, of course, is a promoter of Ontario, he was quick to say that maybe it is a broader focus that we need."
Houston said premiers may need to, "as necessary, remind our friends in the United States that Canada is a friend and a neighbour, and it's a big country with a lot to offer."
Ontario is the No. 1 trade partner to 17 states, and NO. 2 to 11 others, "so it plays a very important role in the economy," Ford said.
Earlier Tuesday, the three territorial leaders called the federal government's commitment to boost defence spending a "generational opportunity" for Canada's North.
"If we look at the infrastructure gap that you see between the north and the south that continues to grow, and the commitment to get to that two per cent (of GDP on defence spending) is an incredible opportunity," said Nunavut Premier P.J. Akeeagok.
"We're at this point where it's an incredible generational opportunity ... We need healthy communities for us to bring the security and sovereignty" in the Arctic, he said.
Last week, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced the country's defence spending is expected to hit the NATO target of two per cent of GDP by 2032.
Northern premiers have long argued that in order to bolster Arctic security and sovereignty, the federal government must help make the area more attractive so that people want to live there, through improvements to infrastructure such as roads and broadband internet.
Two years ago, the three northern premiers said Russia's invasion of Ukraine was a wake-up call that Canada needs to be better prepared.
"We need healthy communities to bring security and sovereignty," Akeeagok told reporters Tuesday morning.
Akeeagok said the northern premiers are also pleased to see Canada's defence plans will be focused on the north, at a time of growing threats from Russia and China.
While having been assured there is no imminent danger, Northwest Territories Premier R.J. Simpson said "we need to be prepared as we move forward."
Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai spoke of a security incident last year when Canadian and U.S. fighter jets shot down what was believed to be an unmanned Chinese spy balloon flying over the territory.
"That gave us a lot of experience watching how the military mobilized and how communication was done," Pillai said.
The federal government's new defence strategy, he added, "is not only going to be incredibly important to the nation, but also from an economic perspective," and local populations must be consulted.
Meanwhile, Houston said given the floods in the Toronto area on Tuesday, the premiers' discussions of emergency preparedness and response - and the need for more resources - was timely.
"We all see what's happening in Toronto with the floods there - we were thinking of them," he told reporters. "There are a number of premiers around the table today that are battling forest fires back at home, and, of course, here in this province we had tragic flash flooding, just last week ... (It was) a very good discussion that certainly takes on additional meaning and importance at times like this."
CAPTION: Northern premiers, from left, Yukon's Ranj Pillai, Northwest Territories' R.J. Simpson, and Nunavut's P.J. Akeeagok speak to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on Tuesday. They called Ottawa's commitment to boost defence spending a "generational opportunity" for Canada's North.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Business indexing term: Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP

Document 279 of 500

LCBO, OPSEU back to bargaining table: Negotiations are set to resume Wednesday as work stoppage approaches two-week mark

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-opseu-back-bargaining-table/docview/3081564478/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO%2C+OPSEU+back+to+bargaining+table%3A+Negotiations+are+set+to+resume+Wednesday+as+work+stoppage+approaches+two-week+mark&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Talks aimed at ending an almost two-week-old LCBO strike were to resume Wednesday morning.
"We are going to the table in a position of power," the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) said in a memo to members Tuesday night.
"The pressure we have built has caused panic reactions from Doug Ford," OPSEU said of the premier who has been urging them to return to bargaining since they hit the bricks July 5.
The move came after the LCBO launched an advertising blitz aimed at ending the 11-day strike.
"I've talked to hundreds and hundreds of front-line people - all they want to do is get back to work," Ford told the Star in Halifax, where he is attending the annual Council of the Federation meeting of the premiers.
"They are frustrated with the way their labour leader is right now - they want to get back. That's all they want," he said.
Ford said striking Liquor Control Board of Ontario workers have told him they are hurting financially while on picket lines and not earning their usual salaries.
"It breaks my heart when I speak to these people," the premier said. "It's very tough to pay the bills and they just want to get back to work ... I hear their stories and they are struggling."
In a surprise salvo at OPSEU, Ford announced Monday "ready-to-drink" cocktails will be sold at 450 licensed supermarkets as early as next week.
Expanding the sale of the pre-mixed drinks - such as Bacardi Breezer and Mike's Hard Lemonade - has been a key concern of the union, which fears the loss of the LCBO's monopoly on such beverages will undermine the Crown corporation.
Under Ford's scheme, the products could be sold at as many as 8,500 convenience stores, supermarkets and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
The LCBO would remain the only retailer allowed to sell spirits such as whisky, rum, gin, vodka and tequila.
But OPSEU's bargaining team said "morale remains high on the (picket) lines" despite what Ford says.
"Seventy per cent of LCBO workers are precarious - they have been facing financial struggles since before the strike began," the team said in a statement.
"But workers know that they are fighting for good jobs in every community, and to protect public revenues."
OPSEU president JP Hornick said "the premier needs to stop interfering with our members' right to bargain and stop escalating his destruction of public alcohol sales."
"Our members saw that their employer was directed never to bargain with them at the table," said Hornick.
"They were handed an insulting offer that made it clear their employer did not have a mandate to address workers' core demands: protecting jobs and public revenues."
On Tuesday, the LCBO took out full-page newspaper ads and 30-second broadcast spots explaining its side of a labour dispute that began July 5.
"This strike did not need to happen," says LCBO president and chief executive George Soleas in the ads.
"In fact, the LCBO worked hard to avoid it," says Soleas.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Liquor; Employment; Bargaining; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Employment Bargaining Supermarkets
