Document 280 of 500

Ontarians back liberalized booze sales: Survey commissioned by PCs shows 57% back expansion while 54% support LCBO strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarians-back-liberalized-booze-sales/docview/3081564459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+back+liberalized+booze+sales%3A+Survey+commissioned+by+PCs+shows+57%25+back+expansion+while+54%25+support+LCBO+strike&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians overwhelmingly support more liberalized alcohol sales even though a majority back the LCBO strikers, according to internal government polling obtained by the Star.
The poll commissioned by Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives found that 54 per cent are in support of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union's "decision to go strike" while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
But the Campaign Research survey suggested the job action that has closed 680 Liquor Control Board of Ontario stores since July 5 is so far having little impact on the public or on the Tories' popularity.
Only about one-quarter of respondents - 28 per cent - said the job action was "inconvenient" while 73 per cent said it wasn't.
That's despite the fact 91 per cent were aware of the work stoppage with only nine per cent unaware.
Some 69 per cent said they agreed with former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne's 2015 decision to allow beer and wine to be sold in 450 grocery stores, while only 23 per cent opposed it and eight per cent didn't know.
More than half - 57 per cent - backed Ford expanding booze sales to other supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets, with 35 per cent opposed and eight per cent unsure.
And 61 per cent support the premier's push to sell "ready-to-drink" cocktails - currently only available at the LCBO - at 8,500 private retailers, with 27 per cent opposed and 12 per cent unsure.
A senior government official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the ongoing labour dispute, said the poll underscores why Ford is moving forward with opening up the market while ensuring the LCBO remains a key player.
"He actually deeply cares about the workers (and) the LCBO ... having a special and important place in our alcohol ecosystem," said the high-ranking insider.
Campaign Research principal Nick Kouvalis said Tuesday that "the public clearly wants liberalization to continue."
Campaign Research surveyed 1,995 Ontarians on Friday and Saturday using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
"The union clearly believes that they're having an impact politically, but the ballot test (response) suggests they're not," said Kouvalis.
According to Campaign Research, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent in public support to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 18 per cent for Marit Stiles' New Democrats and 10 per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
That echoes the findings in last month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, which had the Tories at 41 per cent, the Grits at 25 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent support.
"It doesn't look like there's any negative impact for the Ford government," said Kouvalis, noting "there's Ford voters who support the union's decision to go on strike for better wages, benefits and job security."
The LCBO has offered the workers seven per cent raises over three years and promised to make 400 casual employees full-time.
But OPSEU leaders say Ford's move to allow pre-mixed beverage sales in other stores will undermine the Crown booze giant and inevitably lead to jobs losses.
The poll found 41 per cent believe the premier opening up the market "poses an existential threat to the LCBO that will lead to major jobs losses," while 40 per cent say it was something he "promised in the election so that people in Ontario would have more choices and more convenience" and 18 per cent were unsure.
About one-third - 34 per cent - said "Ford's plan is to privatize the LCBO," while 44 per cent said he's "allowing the LCBO to become the sole distributor to all restaurants, bars, grocery stores, convenience stores and big-box stores in Ontario" and 22 per cent were unsure.
CAPTION: The Campaign Research survey suggests the LCBO strike is so far having little impact on the public or on the popularity of Premier Doug Ford's government.Darren CalabreseThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Liquor; Convenience stores; Strikes; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Strikes Grocery stores

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Liquor Control Board-Ontario Canada; NAICS: 445320, 926150

Title: Ontarians back liberalized booze sales:   Survey commissioned by PCs shows 57% back expansion while 54% support LCBO strike

Document 282 of 500

Drake shares video of apparent flooding in Bridle Path mansion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/drake-shares-video-apparent-flooding-bridle-path/docview/3081564579/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Drake+shares+video+of+apparent+flooding+in+Bridle+Path+mansion&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Drake shared a video of flooding in what appears to be his Bridle Path mansion after Toronto saw severe rainfall on Tuesday.
"This better be Espresso Martini," the hip hop megastar wrote on his Instagram story.
Murky brown water can be seen at ankle level in the video, while an unknown person is holding doors closed as the water pours into what looks to be a change room.
Drake is holding a broom as he trudges through the flood.
The mansion was originally greenlit by the city in 2016, after the property was purchased by a numbered company based in Halifax for $6.7 million in September 2015.
Drake wasn't the only well-known figure sharing in the mess of Tuesday's storm.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford also reported flooding in the basement of his Etobicoke home.
"My house got flooded out - the basement is all flooded right now I was told," said Ford.
Toronto saw severe rainfall and recorded roughly 98 mm of rain, making it the fifth wettest day in history. The record is held at 126 mm on July 8, 2013.
Much of the downtown core saw intense flooding with portions of the Don Valley Parkway closing and service being disrupted for many commuters at Union Station and the subway.
With files from The Canadian Press

Document 283 of 500

Fewer than 4% of grocery stores have applied for Ontario's new alcohol license - here's why many don't want one

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fewer-than-4-grocery-stores-have-applied-ontarios/docview/3081242407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fewer+than+4%25+of+grocery+stores+have+applied+for+Ontario%27s+new+alcohol+license+-+here%27s+why+many+don%27t+want+one&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Convenience stores are snapping up Ontario's new alcohol-retailing licences in droves. Grocery stores? Not so much.
According to official data, fewer than four per cent of the province's 2,000 grocery stores have applied for new licences allowing them to sell beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails beginning Oct. 31.
Grocery industry insiders say the biggest reason is a requirement that any stores selling alcohol under the new licenses also have to accept bottle and can returns for recycling.
"Food safety is the number one concern. They don't want empty beer bottles with cigarette butts being dragged into their stores," said Gary Sands, vice president of the Canadian Federation of Independent Grocers.
Just 53 grocery stores across the province had been issued new alcohol-retailing licenses as of July 14 (out of 75 applications), according to the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario. More than 3,000 convenience stores - or roughly 40 per cent of the total - had been given licences (out of 3,587 which applied). Convenience stores won't be required to accept empties when their licences come into force Sept. 5.
The Retail Council of Canada, which represents large grocery chains including Loblaws, Sobeys and Metro, and big box chains like Costco and Walmart, says the cost of installing recycling infrastructure makes the alcohol licenses unappealing.
"The primary concern is the plan to force consumers to return their empty alcohol containers to grocery stores. Operating return-to-retail systems are expensive, take up valuable floor and backroom space, and create health and safety concerns," said RCC spokesperson Michelle Wasylyshen. "Grocery stores were not built for this purpose and adaptation of stores will cost hundreds of millions of dollars - costs which Ontario consumers would ultimately bear."
A spokesperson for Ontario finance minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the government has already tried to be flexible with recycling requirements, including exemptions for smaller grocery stores and convenience stores.
"The (size) requirement was determined to exempt smaller retailers from accepting empties ... while continuing to require returns for large grocery stores, who have more square footage for storage and sorting," said Bethlenfalvy spokesperson Colin Blanchar.
Under the new rules for grocery stores - and big-box stores like Walmart - starting Oct. 31, any store 4,000 square feet or larger needs to have its own recycling set up to handle empties. Until Jan. 1, 2026, there's an exemption for stores that are within five kilometres of a Beer Store outlet.
Monday, the Ontario government announced that roughly 450 grocery stores which already have licences to sell beer and wine under the old rules will be allowed to sell ready-to-drink cocktails and cases of beer starting Thursday, as opposed to Aug. 1 - a shot across the bow of striking LCBO workers.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union has argued that allowing RTDs - the fastest-growing alcohol segment - to be sold at other retailers would hurt the LCBO's revenue. Premier Doug Ford, says the union's insistence on allowing the LCBO to keep a monopoly on RTD sales is a futile effort, arguing "that ship has sailed."
Those 450 licenses were issued under the Master Framework Agreement (MFA), which expires at the end of 2025.
As part of an agreement with The Beer Store and its owners, the provincial government is paying $225 million to get an early jump on wide-open alcohol retailing that would otherwise have had to wait until the beginning of 2026, after the official end of the MFA.
While convenience stores have been more inclined to apply for the alcohol licenses, it's not a no-brainer for them, either, said Anne Kothawala, CEO of the Convenience Industry Council of Canada.
"I'm not surprised a lot of our members have applied, because this is something we've been asking for for a while. But there are still a lot of details they're concerned about, including how they'll get it delivered, and how much that will cost."
CAPTION: In May, Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy announced the sale of beer and wines at corner stores.

Subject: Retail stores; Licenses; Alcohol; Liquor; Beer; Convenience stores; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores Convenience stores Grocery stores

Title: Fewer than 4% of grocery stores have applied for Ontario's new alcohol license - here's why many don't want one

Document 284 of 500

Doug Ford appeals to LCBO union to return to bargaining

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-appeals-lcbo-union-return-bargaining/docview/3081242405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+appeals+to+LCBO+union+to+return+to+bargaining&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-As the LCBO launches an advertising blitz aimed at ending an 11-day strike, Premier Doug Ford is urging the Ontario Public Service Employees Union to return to bargaining.
"I've talked to hundreds and hundreds of front-line people - all they want to do is get back to work," Ford told the Star in Halifax, where he is attending the annual Council of the Federation meeting of the premiers.
"They are frustrated with the way their labour leader is right now - they want to get back. That's all they want," he said.
Ford said striking Liquor Control Board of Ontario workers have told him they are hurting financially while on picket lines and not earning their usual salaries.
"It breaks my heart when I speak to these people. It's very tough to pay the bills and they just want to get back to work ... I hear their stories and they are struggling."
In a surprise salvo at OPSEU, Ford announced Monday that "ready-to-drink" cocktails will be sold at 450 licensed supermarkets as early as next week.
Expanding the sale of the pre-mixed drinks - such as Bacardi Breezer and Mike's Hard Lemonade - has been a key concern of the union, which fears the loss of the LCBO's monopoly on such beverages will undermine the Crown corporation.
Under Ford's scheme, the products could be sold at as many as 8,500 convenience stores, supermarkets and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
The LCBO would remain the only retailer allowed to sell spirits such as whisky, rum, gin, vodka and tequila.
But OPSEU's bargaining team said "morale remains high on the (picket) lines" despite what Ford says.
"Seventy per cent of LCBO workers are precarious - they have been facing financial struggles since before the strike began," the team said in a statement.
"But workers know that they are fighting for good jobs in every community, and to protect public revenues."
OPSEU president JP Hornick said "the premier needs to stop interfering with our members' right to bargain and stop escalating his destruction of public alcohol sales."
"Our members saw that their employer was directed never to bargain with them at the table," said Hornick.
"They were handed an insulting offer that made it clear their employer did not have a mandate to address workers' core demands: protecting jobs and public revenues."
On Tuesday, the LCBO took out full-page newspaper ads and 30-second broadcast spots explaining its side of a labour dispute that began July 5.
"This strike did not need to happen," says LCBO president and CEO George Soleas in the ads.
"In fact, the LCBO worked hard to avoid it," says Soleas.
At the same time, OPSEU has a new digital ad depicting "a relatable billionaire grocery CEO" - who looks like Loblaw's Galen Weston - sneeringly claiming he needs the profits from expanded booze sales in supermarkets.
"I worked really hard to be born into money - don't I deserve more of yours?" says the actor playing the Weston-esque character.
"What's a billionaire got to do to get some extra zeroes around here? Stop funding public services, start funding me."
The LCBO is offering seven-per-cent raises over three years to the more than 9,000 OPSEU members who work at 680 LCBO stores and in its distribution warehouses.
As well, the LCBO has promised to give full-time jobs to 400 casual employees.
It's the first time in the 97-year history of the booze giant that workers have hit the bricks.
The job action came just six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to speed up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded alcohol sales.
Currently, the LCBO has a monopoly on most "ready-to-drink" beverages, which is the fastest growing alcohol category.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have blamed Ford's rushed alcohol liberalization push for the strike.
On Monday, the Tourism Industry Association of Ontario implored the government to end the dispute, which is hindering the operations of bars and restaurants.
The Ontario Restaurant Hotel and Motel Association also warned "the strike is already impacting licensed operations."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters upon arriving at a meeting at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on Monday.

Improving Canada's drug approval process will be delicate balance: Despite small market, there are ways to help patients get timely access, experts say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/improving-canadas-drug-approval-process-will-be/docview/3080953417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Improving+Canada%27s+drug+approval+process+will+be+delicate+balance%3A+Despite+small+market%2C+there+are+ways+to+help+patients+get+timely+access%2C+experts+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is a much smaller market than the U.S. or U.K. and that affects how fast drugs are submitted for approval, but there are ways for the federal and provincial governments to help move the process along so patients get faster access to them, say experts.
Talk of the delay getting drugs to patients is top of mind for Premier Doug Ford, who arrived in Nova Scotia on Monday as the annual meeting of Canada's 13 provincial and territorial leaders began.
Ford, who takes the helm of the Council of the Federation Aug. 1, has said he will raise the issue and make it a focus for the coming year.
"Canada currently ranks last in the G7 in the time it takes to approve and provide patients access to innovative and often life-saving medicines," Ford said in a recent letter to the pan-Canadian Pharmaceutical Alliance, which was founded by the country's premiers.
"This needs to change," Ford wrote. "We owe it to Canadians to do everything we can to give them the same timely access to life-changing treatments as patients in the rest of the world."
University of Toronto Prof. Mina Tadrous, who researches pharmaceutical policies and drug effectiveness, said Canada does lag behind when comparisons are made to the American market from when they first get drugs, but "there are some very important caveats you need to consider ... The first is that the decision to submit to the regulator, Health Canada or the (American) FDA, is not made by governments, it's made by companies."
They choose when to apply for a drug to enter a market, and with Canada sitting as the ninth largest, "companies will always choose to go with the larger U.S., Europe and then Japan markets first," said Tadrous, who is with U of T's Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy.
"Doug Ford isn't going to be able to make us on par with the size of the American market, just given our population size compared to the U.S., so a lot of this is kind of outside of the control of government," added Matthew Herder, director of the Health Justice Institute at Dalhousie University.
Tadrous noted that in the U.S., once the FDA (Food and Drug Administration) approves a drug, it's allowed to enter the market. Here, a similar process occurs with Health Canada but differs after that stage. With so much government spending on drugs, it must go through a health technology assessment, and the provinces then negotiate the price together. That process is currently going through improvements, he added.
That process "is slower because (drug companies) want to get listed on the public formulary," he said. "If (governments) resource that a little more, that might go a bit faster," he said.
He noted there are already special access programs for innovative drugs - especially for rare cancers - to get them to patients quickly, with co-operation and additional resources for the reviews, something Ford has pointed to as a way to get priority approval for more medications.
Improved co-operation and simultaneous assessments between provincial governments helped during the COVID pandemic to get vaccines and medications to market faster, noted Jennifer Lake, a professor in U of T's pharmacy faculty.
Herder, who is also a professor of pharmacology at Dalhousie, said what could make a difference is all premiers getting onside with a system of national pharmacare, eliminating the time it takes for each province to decide what is covered.
"If the premiers want to get together (Tuesday) in Halifax and really have a serious discussion, they should be talking about how they can support pharmacare instead of opposing it," he said, as some provinces have criticized it as federal overreach into an area of provincial jurisdiction.
Cancer groups have said patients are desperate to get access to the latest treatments, and are urging the provinces to take action.
"Today, people with blood cancer are offered the best of science and the worst of bureaucracy. Despite tremendous advances in research and an incredible number of revolutionary therapies approved in Canada, our crumbling health-care system denies most patients access to these life-saving treatments," said Jessy Ranger of Myeloma Canada and Christina Sit of the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society of Canada, in a joint statement to the Star.
"The danger they face is just one example of what awaits Canadians impacted by cancer if our governments fail to take concerted action and urgently address the issues which sicken our health-care systems."
Tadrous cautioned, however, that there is "a very important nuance of risk and balance ... We want to make sure that it's well studied in safety, and it's not rushed."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, shown Monday in Halifax for the annual meeting of the Council of the Federation, says leaders "owe it to Canadians" to do everything possible to give them the same timely access to life-changing treatments as patients around the world.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 286 of 500

'Ready-to-drink' cocktails can hit shelves Thursday: Ford's hastening of privatization blasted by LCBO workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ready-drink-cocktails-can-hit-shelves-thursday/docview/3080953337/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Ready-to-drink%27+cocktails+can+hit+shelves+Thursday%3A+Ford%27s+hastening+of+privatization+blasted+by+LCBO+workers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a booze-fuelled brawl.
Premier Doug Ford is rushing the sale of "ready-to-drink" cocktails to 450 supermarkets in a surprise gambit denounced by striking LCBO workers as a provocation.
"This is an important milestone for grocery retailers and consumers alike as we continue our work modernizing Ontario's alcohol marketplace," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday.
"As the next step in the government's plan to give people in Ontario more choice and convenience, licensed grocery stores will be able to order ready-to-drink beverages and large beer pack sizes starting Thursday ... and begin selling them immediately upon arrival," said Bethlenfalvy.
"This new timeline accelerates the first phase of the government's plan to expand alcohol sales to grocery, convenience and big-box stores by allowing the 450 grocery stores that are currently licensed to sell beer, cider or wine to sell them once they arrive in store, rather than Aug. 1."
That means Ontarians should be able to purchase the ready-to-drink beverages - such as Bacardi Breeze and Mike's Hard Lemonade - in supermarkets as early as next week.
For the time being, such drinks would only be available in the 450 stores licensed to sell six-packs of beer and wine by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals in 2015.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents the more than 9,000 workers whose strike has closed 680 LCBO stores since July 5, blasted the Progressive Conservative premier.
"Doug Ford is only focused on pushing his harmful agenda - he can't be trusted," said Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team.
"Ford's picking a fight with workers and the people of Ontario who are in this fight with us," said MacLeod. "It's why we're fighting for a fair collective agreement at the table and for better policies in the streets," she said, adding "it's on Ford to end this strike, after all - he forced it to justify his rushed alcohol everywhere scheme."
Liquor Control Board of Ontario chief executive George Soleas on Monday blamed the 10-day liquor store strike on OPSEU for hitting the bricks before hearing out the Crown-owned retailer.
"The offer stands and we remain prepared to negotiate collective bargaining issues," said Soleas.
But OPSEU president JP Hornick said "it is absolutely not true" the union walked away from the table.
"The mediator, to my understanding, determined that we were at an impasse and called off talks," said Hornick noting the union has not heard directly from the LCBO or the mediator about a resumption of bargaining.
The labour dispute has shuttered the LCBO's 680 stores for the first time in the 97-year history of the government booze monopoly.
Soleas said OPSEU told the LCBO "no deal was possible unless the government changed its current policy on ready-to-drink beverages in grocery and conveniences stores, and revoked regulations passed by the government and legislature of Ontario."
He emphasized the LCBO does "not set government policy and/or alcohol regulation in Ontario."
The strike came six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to expedite the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025, deadline for expanded liberalization of alcohol sales.
Currently, the LCBO has a monopoly on most "ready-to-drink" alcoholic beverages.
On Sunday, the LCBO scrapped plans to re-open 32 stores for limited hours later this week.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Grocers slow to apply for licences: Can, bottle returns and recycling cited as major reason

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grocers-slow-apply-licences/docview/3080952821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grocers+slow+to+apply+for+licences%3A+Can%2C+bottle+returns+and+recycling+cited+as+major+reason&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Convenience stores are snapping up Ontario's new alcohol-retailing licences in droves. Grocery stores? Not so much.
According to official data, fewer than four per cent of the province's 2,000 grocery stores have applied for new licences allowing them to sell beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails beginning Oct. 31.
Grocery industry insiders say the biggest reason is a requirement that any stores selling alcohol under the new licences also have to accept bottle and can returns for recycling.
"Food safety is the number one concern. They don't want empty beer bottles with cigarette butts being dragged into their stores," said Gary Sands, vice-president of the Canadian Federation of Independent Grocers.
Just 53 grocery stores across the province had been issued new alcohol-retailing licences as of July 14 (out of 75 applications), according to the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario. More than 3,000 convenience stores - or roughly 40 per cent of the total - had been given licences (out of 3,587 which applied). Convenience stores won't be required to accept empties when their licences come into force Sept. 5.
The Retail Council of Canada, which represents large grocery chains including Loblaws, Sobeys and Metro, and big box chains like Costco and Walmart, says the cost of installing recycling infrastructure makes the alcohol licences unappealing.
"The primary concern is the plan to force consumers to return their empty alcohol containers to grocery stores. Operating return-to-retail systems are expensive, take up valuable floor and backroom space, and create health and safety concerns," said RCC spokesperson Michelle Wasylyshen. "Grocery stores were not built for this purpose and adaptation of stores will cost hundreds of millions of dollars - costs which Ontario consumers would ultimately bear."
A spokesperson for Ontario finance minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the government has already tried to be flexible with recycling requirements, including exemptions for smaller grocery stores and convenience stores.
"The (size) requirement was determined to exempt smaller retailers from accepting empties ... while continuing to require returns for large grocery stores, who have more square footage for storage and sorting," said Bethlenfalvy spokesperson Colin Blanchar.
Under the new rules for grocery stores - and big-box stores like Walmart - starting Oct. 31, any store 4,000 square feet or larger needs to have its own recycling set up to handle empties.
Until Jan. 1, 2026, there's an exemption for stores that are within five kilometres of a Beer Store outlet.
On Monday, the Ontario government announced that roughly 450 grocery stores which already have licences to sell beer and wine under the old rules will be allowed to sell ready-to-drink cocktails and cases of beer starting Thursday, as opposed to Aug. 1 - a shot across the bow of striking LCBO workers.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union has argued that allowing RTDs - the fastest-growing alcohol segment - to be sold at other retailers would hurt the LCBO's revenue. Premier Doug Ford says the union's insistence on allowing the LCBO to keep a monopoly on RTD sales is a futile effort, arguing "that ship has sailed."
Those 450 licences were issued under the master framework agreement (MFA), which expires at the end of 2025.
As part of an agreement with The Beer Store and its owners, the provincial government is paying $225 million to get an early jump on wide-open alcohol retailing that would otherwise have had to wait until the beginning of 2026, after the official end of the MFA.
While convenience stores have been more inclined to apply for the alcohol licences, it's not a no-brainer for them, either, said Anne Kothawala, CEO of the Convenience Industry Council of Canada.
"I'm not surprised a lot of our members have applied, because this is something we've been asking for for a while. But there are still a lot of details they're concerned about, including how they'll get it delivered, and how much that will cost."
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star

Report warns city couldn't afford to run Science Centre: Best hope is a deal with province, councillor says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/report-warns-city-couldnt-afford-run-science/docview/3080952753/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Report+warns+city+couldn%27t+afford+to+run+Science+Centre%3A+Best+hope+is+a+deal+with+province%2C+councillor+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Toronto can't force the provincial government to keep the Ontario Science Centre open, and the cash-strapped municipality can't afford to run the shuttered attraction on its own.
That's the conclusion of a new city staff report headed to Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee on Tuesday.
The document was published Monday in response to a council request for options to protect the 55-year-old institution, which Premier Doug Ford's government abruptly closed last month, citing a damaged roof and other infrastructure failures.
The facility is owned by the province but sits on property at 770 Don Mills Rd. belonging to the city and the Toronto and Region Conservation Authority. The report confirmed that while the 99-year lease Ontario signed with the other two parties in 1965 restricts the province to operating a museum at the site, "it does not obligate the operation of one."
Staff also determined the feasibility of Toronto taking over the Science Centre and reopening it "appears to be very low," even without taking into account the potential cost to the city of repairing the structure. The province has said those repairs would cost at least $500 million, although advocates dispute that figure.
"The city's options are extremely limited, approaching zero," said Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16, Don Valley East), whose ward includes the Science Centre.
According to the report, early termination of the lease requires consent from all three parties, which would require negotiations. The lease also states that if the province lets the buildings fall into disrepair, the TRCA and city may be able to either take them over, or compel the province to demolish them at its own expense. Tearing them down is expected to cost $25 million.
Burnside said the city's best hope of prolonging the Science Centre's life is negotiating an agreement with the Ford government to repurpose it, possibly with public or private funding for "expanded learning opportunities." But that would require "a vision that the province can get behind ... And I haven't seen that from anybody yet," he said.
The report found that even with grants of more than $24 million and revenues of almost $14 million, the Science Centre lost $784,000 last year. Staff determined Toronto would have to find more than $20 million a year to operate the facility on its own.
They predicted costs would only increase in coming years due to the centre's "declining brand," closures for repairs, and competition from the relocated Science Centre the province plans to open at Ontario Place.
"On balance of these factors, staff's preliminary analysis is that it would not be sustainable for the City alone to assume operations of the (Science Centre) under current conditions," the report concluded.
Although last month's closure was sudden, the province had previously announced plans to move the Science Centre to Ontario Place as part of its controversial plans to redevelop the waterfront park.
Under the terms of the new deal Chow reached with Ford in November, the province agreed to hold talks with Toronto about maintaining "community-oriented science programming" at the Don Mills site after the Science Centre moved. But with the fate of the buildings uncertain, it's not clear how that will be possible.
According the report, the potential for reactivating the Don Mills site "is not well understood," and whether the city "could or should participate in any future operations" there should only be made after considering "additional research, consultation, financial analysis and broader new deal considerations."
Chow has said the province "can't just walk away from the Science Centre." On Monday, her spokesperson wouldn't say what the mayor believes the city can do to preserve the site, but said Chow "looks forward to examining the options."
"The mayor knows it is a special place for many children and their families, and has a unique role in the city in helping to create a love of science and learning," the spokesperson said.
A spokesperson for the Ontario Ministry of Infrastructure said "no decisions have been made on the future" of the property. But she said the province remains committed to discussing "future partnership opportunities" with Toronto, "including for science-based programming and education."
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Trudeau government's carbon rebates went out Monday - but one major bank still isn't using their official name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-carbon-rebates-went-out/docview/3081422239/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+carbon+rebates+went+out+Monday+-+but+one+major+bank+still+isn%27t+using+their+official+name&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All but one of Canada's major banks have adopted clear labels for the government's quarterly carbon rebate payments after a lengthy battle with Ottawa concluded with changes to a law that now forces them to comply.
On Monday, eligible Canadians received their quarterly rebates, which the federal government sends out to offset increased costs of heat, fuel and other goods due to its carbon pricing plan.
They are the first rebate payments to reach Canadians since the Trudeau government amended the Financial Administration Act to compel banks to label the payments in a way specified by Ottawa.
The change is among a host of tweaks, including a rebrand of the rebates first reported by the Star, introduced by the Trudeau government this year, that aimed to address points of contention and communication mishaps with the politically controversial carbon pricing regime.
The Liberals insist the Canada Carbon Rebates return more money to most households than the carbon levy costs them, while preserving an incentive across the economy to slash greenhouse gas emissions that are causing the climate crisis. Meanwhile, opposition politicians have blamed the so-called "carbon tax" for juicing recent spikes in inflation. Consumer and industrial carbon pricing is expected to account for about one-third of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030.
Ottawa's decision to force the hands of banks came after the Star reported some were "being less than helpful" in response to government requests to label the payments "CDACarbonRebate" in English and "CarboneRemiseCA" in French, citing character limits, bilingual requirements and "various purported technical reasons," according to a senior government official.
"Most frustrating," the official said at the time, is that "one of the big banks also says they aren't willing to make these changes at all," although they did not specify which bank.
Now only CIBC, which labels the rebate payments as "Deposit Canada" on its customers' accounts, has yet to make the change.
CIBC spokesperson Tom Wallis told the Star the bank is "on track to implement a labelling change by later this summer." Because CIBC has committed to comply by the next rebate payment in October, a government official who spoke to the Star on condition of anonymity said they don't expect the federal government to pursue any enforcement actions, but will track the situation closely.
The Liberals hope the bank labelling will increase awareness of the rebates. In Ontario, those eligible receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19.
"There are still many Canadians who are receiving the benefits of the Canada Carbon Rebate but, until now, may not yet know it," Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said in a statement to the Star. "Getting the banks to properly label the payments will go a long way for Canadians to see for themselves that the Canada Carbon Rebate leaves them better off, while they are helping to fight climate change."
That government-wide push has also included more aggressive promotion of the rebates, including on social media and in MPs' communications with constituents.
A video shared by Environment Canada on Monday says disinformation is "one of the biggest challenges" facing carbon pricing. The government source pointed to a flyer distributed recently by Calgary Conservative MP Greg McLean, which claimed "Canada's carbon tax will cost Alberta families an additional $2,943 in 2024-25."
That number, a spokesperson for the Parliamentary Budget Officer told the Star, includes both the direct costs and indirect economic impacts of the price on carbon, but does not include the quarterly rebates Canadians receive. The real number should be $911 on average, although low-income Albertans who pollute less would ultimate see a gain. With only the direct costs of the price on pollution accounted for, the average Albertan would receive $559 more than they pay, the spokesperson said.
In a statement, Conservative critic for finance Jasraj Singh Hallan accused the Liberals of lying about the costs of carbon pricing.
"The Liberals are desperate to keep raising their carbon tax, but if they really believe it isn't hurting Canadians who are struggling to heat their homes and feed their families, they should call a carbon tax election and let Canadians decide."
With files from Alex Ballingall
CAPTION: Federal Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault speaks at a news conference in St. John's on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Costs; Emissions; Carbon; Public officials; Climate change; Tax elections; Environmental tax

Doug Ford wants Canada to approve drugs faster. Is that possible?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-canada-approve-drugs-faster-is/docview/3081422121/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+Canada+to+approve+drugs+faster.+Is+that+possible%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-Canada is a much smaller market than the U.S. or U.K. and that affects how fast drugs are submitted for approval, but there are ways for the federal and provincial governments to help move the process along so patients get faster access to them, say experts.
Talk of the delay getting drugs to patients is top of mind for Premier Doug Ford, who arrived in Nova Scotia on Monday as the annual meeting of Canada's 13 provincial and territorial leaders began.
Ford, who takes the helm of the Council of the Federation Aug. 1, has said he will raise the issue and make it a focus for the coming year.
"Canada currently ranks last in the G7 in the time it takes to approve and provide patients access to innovative and often life-saving medicines," Ford said in a recent letter to the pan-Canadian Pharmaceutical Alliance, which was founded by the country's premiers.
"This needs to change," Ford wrote. "We owe it to Canadians to do everything we can to give them the same timely access to life-changing treatments as patients in the rest of the world."
University of Toronto Prof. Mina Tadrous, who researches pharmaceutical policies and drug effectiveness, said Canada does lag behind when comparisons are made to the American market from when they first get drugs, but "there are some very important caveats you need to consider ... The first is that the decision to submit to the regulator, Health Canada or the (American) FDA, is not made by governments, it's made by companies."
They choose when to apply for a drug to enter a market, and with Canada sitting as the ninth largest, "companies will always choose to go with the larger U.S., Europe and then Japan markets first," said Tadrous, who is with U of T's Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy.
"Doug Ford isn't going to be able to make us on par with the size of the American market, just given our population size compared to the U.S., so a lot of this is kind of outside of the control of government," added Matthew Herder, director of the Health Justice Institute at Dalhousie University.
Tadrous noted that in the U.S., once the FDA (Food and Drug Administration) approves a drug, it's allowed to enter the market. Here, a similar process occurs with Health Canada but differs after that stage. With so much government spending on drugs, it must go through a health technology assessment, and the provinces then negotiate the price together. That process is currently going through improvements, he added.
That process "is slower because (drug companies) want to get listed on the public formulary," he said. "If (governments) resource that a little more, that might go a bit faster," he said.
He noted there are already special access programs for innovative drugs - especially for rare cancers - to get them to patients quickly, with co-operation and additional resources for the reviews, something Ford has pointed to as a way to get priority approval for more medications.
Improved co-operation and simultaneous assessments between provincial governments helped during the COVID pandemic to get vaccines and medications to market faster, noted Jennifer Lake, a professor in U of T's pharmacy faculty.
Herder, who is also a professor of pharmacology at Dalhousie, said what could make a difference is all premiers getting onside with a system of national pharmacare, eliminating the time it takes for each province to decide what is covered.
"If the premiers want to get together (Tuesday) in Halifax and really have a serious discussion, they should be talking about how they can support pharmacare instead of opposing it," he said, as some provinces have criticized it as federal overreach into an area of provincial jurisdiction.
Cancer groups have said patients are desperate to get access to the latest treatments, and are urging the provinces to take action.
"Today, people with blood cancer are offered the best of science and the worst of bureaucracy. Despite tremendous advances in research and an incredible number of revolutionary therapies approved in Canada, our crumbling health-care system denies most patients access to these life-saving treatments," said Jessy Ranger of Myeloma Canada and Christina Sit of the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society of Canada, in a joint statement to the Star.
"The danger they face is just one example of what awaits Canadians impacted by cancer if our governments fail to take concerted action and urgently address the issues which sicken our health-care systems."
Tadrous cautioned, however, that there is "a very important nuance of risk and balance ... We want to make sure that it's well studied in safety, and it's not rushed."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters upon arriving late for a meeting between Canada's premiers and Indigenous leaders at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Document 291 of 500

Canada Carbon Rebate will land in bank accounts Monday. Here's how much you can get

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-carbon-rebate-will-land-bank-accounts/docview/3080596070/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Carbon+Rebate+will+land+in+bank+accounts+Monday.+Here%27s+how+much+you+can+get&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After an adjustment in April, the Canada Carbon Rebate (renamed from the Climate Action Incentive Payment) will go out Monday, providing some financial relief for some Canadians.
Most Ontarians will receive at least $140 per quarter from the carbon price rebate, up from $122 last year thanks to a boost to the rebate in April. The rebate is delivered every three months through deposits as a direct refund for the Liberal government's carbon levy - a program, often referred to by the opposition Conservatives as a "carbon tax," that has two components.
The scheme includes a pricing system for carbon-emitting heavy industries and a fuel charge on gasoline and home heating.
Here's what you need to know about the program and whether you're eligible.
What is the carbon tax rebate?
The credit is meant to help individuals and families offset the extra costs from the federal government's pollution pricing, according to the Canada Revenue Agency. In a review of the program, the parliamentary budget officer found in July 2023 that most Canadians in 2021 got more from the Canada Carbon Rebate (CCR) than they paid through the carbon levy. But, the budget watchdog, in a more expansive analysis, found that Canadians would see a "net loss" in the coming years from the carbon pricing scheme and rebate.
The Chiefs of Ontario and the Attawapiskat First Nation called for a judicial review of the carbon levy in November over their concerns about the disproportionate impact of the pricing scheme.
The government made some changes to the carbon pricing system in late October 2023, doubling the top-up to the rebate for rural Canadians and temporarily pausing the fuel charge for heating oil.
In Ontario, the carbon pricing program is administered at a federal level, with the federal government returning 90 per cent proceeds from those charges to eligible residents.
The CCR has gained attention not only for its rebrand - which the government implemented in an attempt to make its contentious climate policy more palatable - but also for resistance from provincial leaders. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe, in February, said his province would not submit the money it owed for the carbon price on natural gas, leading to the federal government responding by threatening to deny payment of the rebate to Saskatchewan residents.
Several premiers across the country, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, called on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to pause the carbon price increase on April 1.
Trudeau did not oblige, accusing conservative premiers of "(making) political hay" out of the carbon price and challenging them to develop an alternative policy to reduce emissions.
Who is eligible for the carbon tax rebate?
Residents of Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario were eligible when the program first rolled out and the program was expanded in July 2023 to include Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and New Brunswick.
To receive the benefit, you must be a resident of a CCR-eligible province on the first day of the payment month. You must also be at least 19 years old. If you are under that age, you must either have (or previously had) a spouse or common-law partner, or are (or previously were) a parent and live (or previously lived) with your child.
How much will I receive in carbon tax rebate credits?
Ontarians eligible for the rebate will receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19. A family of four in Ontario, according to the federal government, would receive $280.
The payments are universal and not dependent on income levels. Here's how it breaks down by province, for a family of four, per quarter:
Alberta: $450Manitoba: $300New Brunswick: $190Newfoundland and Labrador: $298Nova Scotia: $206Ontario: $280Prince Edward Island: $220Saskatchewan: $376
The CCR also includes a 20 per cent supplemental bump for residents in small and rural communities, which includes all eligible P. E. I residents, according to the government. However, some rural communities have spoken out about the rules demarcating rural towns, saying the government has unfairly looped them in with nearby cities.
Recipients can also expect quarterly payments in October and in January and April of 2025.
How do I receive the carbon tax payment?
Eligible CCR recipients should see the credit in the form of a cheque or direct deposit automatically, without needing to apply. Canadians who file their taxes electronically before March 15 should receive their payment on the quarterly schedule, according to the government's website. Those filing later can expect their CCR payments six to eight weeks after their tax return is assessed.
If you have a spouse or common-law partner, the person whose tax return was assessed first will receive the total credit for the family. It doesn't matter which person receives the rebate as the total amount will be the same.
People with tax debt will see the credit automatically subtracted from the amount they owe.
The Canada Revenue Agency noted it may take up to 10 working days to receive a CCR payment.
CAPTION: The Canada Carbon Rebate is a direct refund for the carbon levy, previously referred to as a "carbon tax rebate."

Should Justin Trudeau stay or go? + grappling with the death of a father of seven

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/should-justin-trudeau-stay-go-grappling-with/docview/3080596068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+Justin+Trudeau+stay+or+go%3F+%2B+grappling+with+the+death+of+a+father+of+seven&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, July 15 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
DON'T MISSJustin Trudeau is reckoning with the decision of his political life. Here's why few know what he will do
Calls for Justin Trudeau to step down as leader of the Liberal Party have been mounting, and speculation has swirled on whether he could take on a strongly-polling Pierre Poilievre in an election. And, it's not like Trudeau can go to his cabinet for advice - the decision to leave is a solitary one. But, after nine years in government and a loss last month in a key byelection, the question keeps coming up - is it time for someone else? Here's Ottawa Bureau Chief Tonda MacCharles' deep-dive on the question hanging over the PM's head.
From one source: "You cannot for one second - unless there are people very, very, very close to you - hint that you're going, because you still have to command respect and be able to get people to follow you."On the one hand: Just one Liberal MP - a former cabinet minister - has called for Trudeau's resignation, following the party's loss in Toronto-St. Paul's, a long-held Grit seat.On the other: A Liberal insider told the Star that Trudeau and the party need to "double down" on their current direction, and work to win back the faith of voters."Why didn't anyone go and help him?" The killing of a father of seven at a TTC station leaves his family grasping for answers
On the evening of June 25, Matthew Rumble Sr. was stabbed and killed at Jane Station in broad daylight, after an altercation between him and another man on the bus. He was supposed to take his son, 18-year-old Matthew Rumble Jr., to the gym for the first time. He's survived by his wife, Stella, their six children and one other child. "How did this happen? With so many people on the bus, nobody called for help?" Stella asked, the same questions running through her mind every day. Here's the story of Matthew Rumble Sr.'s life, and the loved ones he leaves behind.
By the numbers: In recent years, riding the TTC has felt like a roll of the dice, after a wave of violent and sometimes random high-profile incidents. However, last summer, the TTC said the rate of offenses had decreased by 36 per cent.From the union: "This is a real loss on so many levels ... innocent people riding transit should not have to be worried about their safety," the head of the TTC's union said.About Rumble Sr.: He worked as a chef at the restaurant Insomnia, loved taking his daughters to parks, movie theatres and Canada's Wonderland. But most of all Stella remembers his ability to make people smile.Toronto is losing the war on rats. Here's how it got so bad and what the city should be doing
In the summer of 2018, rats were running the streets, skittering across busy thoroughfares in broad daylight, swarming backyards, and tearing into waste bins and garbage bags. With residents at their limit, city council voted to create a rat management strategy - which promptly died out in 2019. Since then, the city has been in rat-induced limbo, leaving residents to rely on brooms, home remedies and private pest control companies. Year after year, Toronto's rat problem has grown, but it doesn't have to be that way, Amy Dempsey Raven writes in part two of the Rat Detective series.
How to win the rat war: You go to rat school. In part one, Amy Dempsey Raven flew out to New York City for the Rat Academy, a city-run program for all things pest control held in the heart of America's rattiest city.Who runs Toronto? Toronto has already lost its century-long war against raccoons - the bigger, cuter trash-eaters that roam the city. We're a city plagued (and sometimes gifted) with wildlife abound, often with little strategy to manage human-animal interactions.Except for: the pigeons. We have them on birth control, at least. Document 293 of 500

Alcohol is to Doug Ford as gold was to Midas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alcohol-is-doug-ford-as-gold-was-midas/docview/3080595643/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alcohol+is+to+Doug+Ford+as+gold+was+to+Midas&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alcohol is to Ontario Premier Doug Ford what gold was to Midas, what artisanal coffee is to millennials, what Ivanka Trump is to Donald Trump. It's an essential material, the core around which life swirls.
Now I don't understand the concept of craving something that is readily available to the point of forcing. We can obtain booze at home, at other people's homes, at the LCBO, at other stores, in restaurants and bars and all day at the airport. We can drink it in parks.
Same with coffee. Ubiquitous. Ivanka Trump and her husbandette moved to Florida to be closer to her father. Reach out and there it is.
That I have long COVID and wine now tastes like room-temperature bear urine is immaterial. (Why is the bear in a room though?) Ford makes sure alcohol is as reachable for me as it is for alcoholics desperately trying to avoid booze-triggers.
When the LCBO strike began, Ford even published what looked like a road map to perdition, guiding Ontario booze hounds to Wine Rack stores and such, the kind of comedy gold we remember from his brother Rob Ford's days as mayor. Not so long ago, how we longed for a similar map of pharmacies stocked with COVID vaccines.
But now I wonder. Perhaps I couldn't see the forest for the beer jokes. Maybe it's not that Ford, who says he doesn't drink, doesn't like the booze so much as he detests the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents 9,000 striking LCBO workers and donates to his opponents.
Similarly, the existence of The Beer Store offended Ford so he paid it at least $225 million to let beer be sold in a wider world.
But why? Because The Beer Store was ugly, soul-destroying and industrial? Nah. Ford doesn't care about the esthetics of getting hammered. Because he has a principled opposition to monopolies? No, Ford has no principles, not as such. But he has preferences.
He likes privatization. Small-minded Conservatives like privatized highways, private retail and outsourced jobs.
If there had been a buyer wanting to run the Ontario Science Centre in Don Mills as a going concern, attached to, yes, a condo development, maybe he would have given it to them as long as they built a new roof. I wait agog.
A new Disneyfied science centre at Ontario Place is what Ford envisions. It's his idea of family fun.
It would be a place to store the kids while prosperous parents spend the day at that private Austrian spa, drinking small-batch alcohols, soaking, gambling, and contracting a urinary tract infection from poorly cleaned tubs. No, a nail fungus. Trichophyton rubrum will be our word du jour.
I have a stock tank at home but right now it's full of zucchini plants. So there I'd sit at Therme, staring out at Lake Ontario and sloppily singing old Blue Rodeo songs in a privatized tank, thinking about Ford.
What does our premier want? Better yet, what do we want in a premier? A Keir Starmer, I think. Someone who'd put an end to all the nonsense on Day One, as the new British prime minister did. No one will be sent to Rwanda. It's over, he said.
We want a premier who prioritizes health care, education, housing and transit along with a green plan for climate change. What we have is a strange man obsessed with booze, online gambling, and raising speed limits while giving good Ontario land to real estate developers building great stupid swathes of oversized homes for buyers who'll be sunk in regret by 2032.
We want a sensible person. I'm tired of writing columns about peripheral Ford entertainments. I want a government based on thought and substance. I won't get it of course but this is what I want.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford moves a flat of beer cans during a photo-op at Cool Beer Brewing Company on July 10.

Subject: Alcohol; Beer

People: Trump, Ivanka

Title: Alcohol is to Doug Ford as gold was to Midas

Document 294 of 500

Somali community centre plan in Etobicoke park facing uncertainty after Doug Ford sides with upset residents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/somali-community-centre-plan-etobicoke-park/docview/3080588118/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Somali+community+centre+plan+in+Etobicoke+park+facing+uncertainty+after+Doug+Ford+sides+with+upset+residents&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A city-backed plan to build a recreation centre for Toronto's Somali community is facing an uncertain future, after fierce backlash from residents who have enlisted Premier Doug Ford in their fight to stop the project coming to their neighbourhood.
The group behind the proposal to construct a Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation (SCCR) in Etobicoke's Buttonwood Park says it would be the culmination of a decades-long effort to carve out space for a Black community that has endured discrimination and lacks access to public amenities.
But nearby residents say they weren't consulted on the plan, which would take up precious green space in their neighbourhood near Eglinton Avenue and Royal York Road. They have a powerful ally in the premier, who along with Etobicoke Centre representatives Coun. Stephen Holyday and MPP Kinga Surma, attended a recent meeting of residents opposed to the plan.
The proposal is on the agenda for Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee on Tuesday, but as a result of the outcry the committee may vote to delay consideration of it for months. With political opposition mounting, it looks increasingly likely the centre won't be built at Buttonwood, and could be moved to a new site.
Zakaria Abdulle, chair of the SCCR, said the non-profit is committed to "finalizing the best location" based on community input.
But he argued it is "absolutely critical for civic leaders to support ... Black communities by investing in their needs and creating opportunities for social and economic advancement."
He said Toronto's Somali population has suffered from a lack of funding for social infrastructure, and the recreation centre would create "high quality opportunities for Somali Canadians and surrounding neighbourhoods."
Although the facility would have programming geared to the Somali population, it would be open to the general public for at least half its operating hours. The goal is to "bring communities together, create a space for cultural exchange and serve as a benefit to all Torontonians," Abdulle said.
But many in Buttonwood Hill don't see the project as beneficial. As of Friday, there was a "Save Buttonwood Park" sign on the lawn of nearly every home in neighbourhood.
Christina Manulak, president of the Buttonwood Hill Residents Association, said she and her neighbours have been "working day and night" against the plan. She argued that the City "made a huge mistake by not telling the community" about it before recommending last month that council approve a lease with SCCR.
The centre would be built on about three acres of the 10.6-acre park. According to Manulak, that would be a serious loss to a community that "desperately" needs to maintain green space. That need that will only grow when thousands of people move into condos under construction on Eglinton Avenue.
Manulak said her association understands the Somali community's need for a recreation facility, but the city should "go back to the drawing board and find a place that really makes sense."
City council first voted in 2022 to work with the SCCR on a culture and recreation centre, and in April of this year voted to move ahead with the plan.
Under the terms of the proposed agreement, SCCR would pay the city a nominal rent, and be responsible for the costs to build, maintain, and operate the centre, which could include amenities like recreation space, basketball court, child care spots, a library and exhibition space to showcase Somali culture. SCCR is hoping to secure federal and provincial funding.
However, Buttonwood Park wasn't revealed as the preferred location until June, just days before a recommendation to approve an initial 30-year land lease with SCCR was set to go to a meeting of Chow's executive.
Residents taken off guard by the proposal voiced their anger at the June 18 meeting, and the mayor - who voted for the plan in April - agreed that "somehow the process got messed up, and the community didn't understand or know that it was happening." Her committee deferred consideration of the plan at least until this month.
Arianne Robinson, the mayor's press secretary, wouldn't say whether Chow plans to defer the report again on Tuesday. Coun. Amber Morley (Ward 3, Etobicoke-Lakeshore) has submitted a motion that would punt the issue to an executive meeting in October to allow for public consultation. But Robinson signalled Chow, who namechecked the Somali community throughout her 2023 election campaign, is open to moving the project.
"The mayor is invested in working with the community and local residents to find the right location," she said in an email.
A spokesperson for Ford said he has "heard from many residents ... who have strongly expressed that they want Buttonwood Park to remain a park enjoyed by families." She said Ford is co-operating with the city, SCCR, and the Toronto District School Board to identify a new site in Rexdale, in the premier's Etobicoke North riding.
At least one resident who spoke at last month's executive appeared to link their concerns about the Somali centre to the unfounded fear that it would attract crime to the Edenbridge-Humber Valley neighbourhood, which according to census data is wealthier and less diverse than Toronto as a whole.
Opponents of the SCCR plan are adamant that residents' objections aren't rooted in a desire to to keep members of a racialized community out of their neighbourhood.
"We've made it clear we don't care what type of cultural centre it would be. It's absolutely not about that," said Manulak.
Coun. Holyday said he doesn't believe his constituents are motivated by prejudice. Rather they feel "they've been left in the margins, or City Hall hasn't been listening."
CAPTION: From left, Zakaria Abdulle, Abdifatah Hussein, Halla Ahmed, Deqa Egal, Rihana Mohamed and Hamdi Ali are working to establish a Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation in Toronto, which they say will provide important opportunities for the Somali community and other residents.Christina Manulak, president of the Buttonwood Hill Residents Association, said she and her neighbours have been "working day and night" against the plan.The group behind the proposalto constructa Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation(SCCR) in Etobicoke's Buttonwood Park says it would be the culmination of a decades-long effort to carve out space for a Black community that has endured discrimination and lacks access to public amenities.As of Friday, there was a "Save Buttonwood Park" sign on the lawn of nearly every home in the Buttonwood Hill neighbourhood.Toronto, ON - July 14: A red-tailed hawk soars over Buttonwood Park on a warm Sunday morning. Dozens of lawn signs asking city council to save Buttonwood Park, proposed location of a new Somali Community Centre, fill the neighbourhood surrounding the park. PD Nick Lachance/Toronto Star Nick Lachance/Toronto StarToronto, ON - July 14: A man sits in Buttonwood Park on a warm Sunday morning. Dozens of lawn signs asking city council to save Buttonwood Park, proposed location of a new Somali Community Centre, fill the neighbourhood surrounding the park.
