Document 295 of 500

We rely on LCBO stores for funding services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-rely-on-lcbo-stores-funding-services/docview/3080588015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+rely+on+LCBO+stores+for+funding+services&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We rely on LCBO sales
Ford's zealous desire to privatize alcohol sales will be costly for Ontario taxpayers: McQuaig, July 11
Columnist Linda McQuaig brings up a point rarely spoken about: the cost to our public economy and essential services when the provincial Conservative government moves to privatize part of our services. Rarely do we hear that the LCBO is a profitable publicly-owned company that pours millions of dollars in profit back into our economy. These dollars help pay for our health and long-term-care, education and environmental protection. Loss of LCBO sales means loss of jobs. Rarely do we discuss that by eliminating union jobs we are adding to the financial stress many Ontarians are feeling. When we turn our backs on well-paid union employment and look to minimum wage and insecure jobs at local convenience and grocery stores, we are leading a push to the bottom for the majority of us in Ontario. McQuaig's opinion piece should be read and considered by all of us who care about the kind of society we want to live in.
Fran Bazos, Newmarket
One wonders why we haven't heard about much about what Ontario has to gain with off-loading wine, beer and beverage drinks to up to private outlets. What sort of net profits will the government realize with this off-loading? Will it realize a net gain once all those LCBO jobs are gone?
Renate Manthei, Hamilton, Ont.
Restaurants and the LCBO
Pressure mounting on Doug Ford to resolve LCBO strike, July 12
The July 12 letter that said restaurants should have just stocked up on booze before the strike misses the point. Restaurants like all businesses work on cash flow. Stocking up with a month's supply is not so easy.
Mark Carriere, Mississauga
Trudeau talked but said nothing
Trudeau announces when Canada expects to hit military spending commitments to NATO, July 11
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has done it again, making big announcements but providing no details. This time at the NATO summit he said Canada will meet the commitment of spending two per cent of GDP to the alliance by 2032. Really? The PM didn't say just how that 2032 target will be met. Trudeau also bragged earlier about buying up to a dozen submarines that can go under ice. By when? Not sure. How much? Can't say. Oh well. I'm sure most Canadians have realized by now how Trudeau is all sizzle and no steak.
Dan Nyznik, Keene, Ont.
I find it strange that our Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's commitment was announced at the NATO meeting instead of at the House of Commons when the last budget was being announced. The prime minister and the minister of defence appear like two school boys caught playing hooky from school. They seem willing to say anything to escape the wrath of the NATO principal. I am encouraged that the NATO countries that border on the Arctic and North Atlantic Ocean are going to co-operate in the defence of these waters with new icebreakers. I wait with much interest for the details on how the government is going to pay for these new ships, who is going to build them and how we are going to entice our unemployed youth to join the forces to participate.
Ken Stock, Port Hope, Ont.
Create housing that works at Downsview
Five ways Downsview's massive redevelopment will change the face of the city, July 11
The Downsview redevelopment plan is a perfect opportunity to create housing that really works for people. That will happen only if every housing unit in the complex is built using universal design (UD). UD is a series of accessibility features that make the unit suitable for anyone of any age or ability. The units will not look institutional, and if accessibility is incorporated at the design stage, the units are not significantly more expensive to build. It's fine to build for different income levels, but housing that's affordable isn't much use if a person can't get inside it and can't physically use it. Let accessibility be one of the social justice principles guiding this promising new neighbourhood.
Carol Damioli, Accessible Housing Network, Toronto
Dental work thanks to the Liberal-NDP agreement
Thanks to the Trudeau Liberals and the Singh NDP agreement, I had my teeth cleaned by a capable hygienist yesterday for the first time in many years. Two decades of part-time/casual employment with no benefits (intermingled with some years of full-time employment providing enough benefits to cover one visit to the dentist per year) was one reason I rarely see a dentist as I am now retired and on a fixed income. Meanwhile, Premier Doug Ford's rubber-stamping rolling back rental controls increased my spending elsewhere, so I couldn't pay for dental work, while neglecting to adjust the Ontario Seniors Dental Care Program for inflation. Everyone but the Conservatives seems to know that Canada's burgeoning poverty-class needs health care as opposed to a prolonged spiralling downward into the misery of poor physical and mental health. It isn't so much that the Conservatives suffer from ignorance of the issues, it is that they care nothing about people and social policy. They care everything about power.
Todd Benson, Welland, Ont.
Gratitude for 'Stiff & Sons'
2024 Toronto Fringe Festival: A complete guide with reviews and our critics' picks, July 7
What a lot of fresh theatre air during Fringe 2024! We saw quite a few excellent shows and yes, there were some that should not be in this festival. One show that does not need a review is "Stiff and Sons," written by Aidan Gouveia and directed by Daniel Reale. Why does it not need a review? Because the audience was the jury and they gave it golden status! We laughed, sniggered, felt awkward, cringed and guffawed while these actors took us on a joyride not to be forgotten. Playing over-the-top and in-the-moment takes superhuman focus, and the ensemble excelled! And yes! It was dark comedy par excellence! Physicality was superb and in many cases almost dancelike! The pace never dropped and the suspense stayed alive throughout. How did they do that? They acted using energy from one another. You could feel it! Creative use of space impressed. Voice work and diction on point. Thank you!
Esther Bredenkamp, Toronto
CAPTION: A truck is allowed to leave the LCBO warehouse facility in Whitby, Ont. as workers demonstrate outside.

Document 296 of 500

Doug Ford fast-tracks 'ready-to-drink' cocktails to grocery stores as LCBO strike continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-fast-tracks-ready-drink-cocktails/docview/3080587926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+fast-tracks+%27ready-to-drink%27+cocktails+to+grocery+stores+as+LCBO+strike+continues&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a booze-fueled brawl.
Premier Doug Ford is rushing the sale of "ready-to-drink" cocktails to 450 supermarkets in a surprise gambit denounced by striking LCBO workers as a provocation.
"This is an important milestone for grocery retailers and consumers alike as we continue our work modernizing Ontario's alcohol marketplace," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday.
"As the next step in the government's plan to give people in Ontario more choice and convenience, licensed grocery stores will be able to order ready-to-drink beverages and large beer pack sizes starting Thursday ... and begin selling them immediately upon arrival," said Bethlenfalvy.
"This new timeline accelerates the first phase of the government's plan to expand alcohol sales to grocery, convenience and big-box stores by allowing the 450 grocery stores that are currently licensed to sell beer, cider or wine to sell them once they arrive in store, rather than Aug. 1," he said.
That means Ontarians should be able to purchase the ready-to-drink beverages - such as Bacardi Breeze and Mike's Hard Lemonade - in supermarkets as early as next week.
For the time being, such drinks would only be available in the 450 stores licensed to sell six-packs of beer and wine by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals in 2015.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents the more than 9,000 workers whose strike has closed 680 LCBO stores since July 5, blasted the Progressive Conservative premier.
"Doug Ford is only focused on pushing his harmful agenda - he can't be trusted," said Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team.
"Ford's picking a fight with workers and the people of Ontario who are in this fight with us," said MacLeod.
"It's why we're fighting for a fair collective agreement at the table and for better policies in the streets," she said, adding "it's on Ford to end this strike, after all - he forced it to justify his rushed alcohol everywhere scheme."
Liquor Control Board of Ontario chief executive George Soleas on Monday blamed the 10-day liquor store strike on OPSEU for hitting the bricks before hearing out the Crown-owned retailer.
"The offer stands and we remain prepared to negotiate collective bargaining issues," said Soleas.
But OPSEU president JP Hornick, said "it is absolutely not true" the union walked away from the table.
"The mediator, to my understanding, determined that we were at an impasse and called off talks," said Hornick noting the union has not heard directly from the LCBO or the mediator about a resumption of bargaining.
The labour dispute has shuttered the LCBO's 680 stores for the first time in the 97-year history of the government booze monopoly.
Soleas said OPSEU told the LCBO "no deal was possible unless the government changed its current policy on ready-to-drink beverages in grocery and conveniences stores, and revoked regulations passed by the government and legislature of Ontario."
He emphasized the LCBO does "not set government policy and/or alcohol regulation in Ontario."
The strike came six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to expedite the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded liberalization of alcohol sales.
Under his changes, beer, wine and premixed beverages will be sold at 8,500 licensed supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
Currently, the LCBO has a monopoly on most "ready-to-drink" alcoholic beverages, which is the fastest growing booze category.
Ford told the union last Wednesday that "if they want to negotiate over (ready-to-drink beverages), the deal's off ... that ship has sailed - it's halfway across Lake Ontario."
But the premier said the LCBO would remain a public asset and retain its exclusive rights for the sale of spirits, such as whisky, vodka, gin, rum and tequila, and it would control the wholesale distribution of wine and ready-to-drink products to all private retailers.
Last week, Ford said the LCBO had made a fair offer to OPSEU, including seven per cent raises over three years and a pledge to move 400 casual employees into full-time jobs.
Soleas said Monday that "the union has never responded to the LCBO's proposal."
"The offer stands and we remain prepared to negotiate collective bargaining issues," he said.
On Sunday, the LCBO scrapped plans to re-open 32 stores for limited hours later this week.
Instead, it will focus resources to online shopping on its website.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have blamed Ford's booze liberalization push for the job action.
CAPTION: LCBO workers picket at the Queens Quay flagship store location in Toronto.

Subject: Licenses; Alcohol; Liquor; Beverages; Beer; Convenience stores; Strikes; Collective bargaining; Mediators; Employment; Liberalization; Supermarkets; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Strikes Collective bargaining Employment Liberalization Supermarkets Grocery stores

Title: Doug Ford fast-tracks 'ready-to-drink' cocktails to grocery stores as LCBO strike continues

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 297 of 500

Ford backs opponents of Somali centre: Residents say they weren't consulted on Buttonwood Park plan to give space to community

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-backs-opponents-somali-centre/docview/3080194149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+backs+opponents+of+Somali+centre%3A+Residents+say+they+weren%27t+consulted+on+Buttonwood+Park+plan+to+give+space+to+community&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

SCCR is hoping to secure federal and provincial funding.
However, Buttonwood Park wasn't revealed as the preferred location until June, just days before a recommendation to approve an initial 30-year land lease with SCCR was set to go to a meeting of Chow's executive.
Residents taken off guard by the proposal voiced their anger at the June 18 meeting, and the mayor - who voted for the plan in April - agreed that "somehow the process got messed up, and the community didn't understand or know that it was happening." Her committee deferred consideration of the plan at least until this month.
Arianne Robinson, the mayor's press secretary, wouldn't say whether Chow plans to defer the report again on Tuesday.
Coun. Amber Morley (Ward 3, Etobicoke-Lakeshore) has submitted a motion to punt the issue to an executive meeting in October to allow for public consultation.
But Robinson signalled that Chow, who namechecked the Somali community throughout her 2023 election campaign, is open to moving the project.
A spokesperson for Ford said he has "heard from many residents ... who have strongly expressed that they want Buttonwood Park to remain a park enjoyed by families." She said Ford is co-operating with the city, SCCR, and the Toronto District School Board to identify a new site in Rexdale, in the premier's Etobicoke North riding.
At least one resident who spoke at last month's executive appeared to link their concerns about the Somali centre to the unfounded fear that it would attract crime to the Edenbridge-Humber Valley neighbourhood, which according to census data is wealthier and less diverse than Toronto as a whole.
Opponents of the SCCR plan are adamant that residents' objections aren't rooted in a desire to to keep members of a racialized community out of their neighbourhood.
"We've made it clear we don't care what type of cultural centre it would be. It's absolutely not about that," said Manulak.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Document 298 of 500

LCBO walks back plan to open stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-walks-back-plan-open-stores/docview/3080187999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+walks+back+plan+to+open+stores&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The LCBO will no longer be opening its stores until the strike is resolved, reversing its original plan to open 32 select locations for limited hours starting on Friday, the company said in a statement Sunday afternoon.
The LCBO said it will instead reallocate its personnel to other parts of its operations as it continues to serve retail customers through online shopping on its website. Currently, management staff are running the operations, with orders being fulfilled within a week, according to an LCBO spokesperson.
"This pivot means that we will be able to improve how we serve Ontario bars and restaurants to help increase product selection, availability, and expedited delivery," the company wrote. More details will be shared directly with the customers, it said.
This is the second time the LCBO has backed out of opening stores during the strike. Last Wednesday, the company was supposed to open five stores to exclusively serve licensees, including bars, restaurants, nightclubs and lounges. But it has since scrapped that plan, citing threats by the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) to picket the locations.
"Workers saw this announcement coming," JP Hornick, president of OPSEU, told the Star on Sunday. "Without the LCBO workers, the LCBO doesn't work."
The strike began just after midnight on July 5, with more than 9,000 workers hitting the picket lines, forcing the closure of nearly 700 stores. Since then, pressure has been mounting on Premier Doug Ford to resolve the strike, while OPSEU is said to have walked away from the bargaining table.
"We are prepared to go back to the table as soon as it is clear to us that (Premier) Ford and the LCBO are prepared to bargain in good faith a future for the LCBO that will protect good jobs and public revenues and create more stable and permanent jobs," said Hornick.
LCBO workers "miss their regulars and want to get back to work," Hornick added. "It's on Ford to end this strike."
The LCBO said in a statement it is committed to reaching an agreement with OPSEU, and encourages the union to consider its "fair offer."
OPSEU's main concern relates to Ford's plan to sell beer, wine and ready-to-drink beverages at supermarkets, convenience and big-box stores. The Progressive Conservative government is spending anywhere between $225 million and $1 billion to expedite liberalized alcohol sales by Oct. 31.
This, OPSEU argues, threatens job security for LCBO employees as it believes it will lose significant business to large private owners of convenience and grocery stores.
"LCBO workers are on strike for the future of the LCBO, the $2.5 billion in revenues it generates each year and for the thousands of jobs on the line," OPSEU chair Colleen MacLeod said in a statement on Friday.
Ford, however, has said the province won't budge when it comes to the expansion.
Meanwhile, some grocery stores are running out of liquor, as buyers stock up in a frenzy. Bars and restaurants have also been struggling to snatch customer favourites since the strike, with industry insiders criticizing the LCBO for its unsuccessful contingency plan.
With files from Kristin Rushowy, Robert Benzie and Josh Rubin.
CREDIT: Ana Pereira staff Reporter With files from Kristin Rushowy, Robert Benzie and Josh Rubin.

Document 299 of 500

East-end rally held to protest closure: 200 residents, politicians unite against move

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/east-end-rally-held-protest-closure/docview/3080187960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=East-end+rally+held+to+protest+closure%3A+200+residents%2C+politicians+unite+against+move&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A crowd of about 200 community members, joined by local and provincial politicians, attended a rally Sunday afternoon in an east-end park to protest last month's sudden closure of the Ontario Science Centre and its planned relocation.
Many waved placards critical of Premier Doug Ford for closing the nearby Don Mills Road facility after engineers found structural concerns with its crumbling roof and other urgently needed repairs, and chanted "Shame" and "Fix the roof."
Floyd Ruskin of Save Ontario's Science Centre, a grassroots group that organized the event in Ferrand Drive Park, took aim at the premier for calling it "decrepit" and saying it would cost half a billion dollars to fix.
"Does it need work? Of course it does," said Ruskin of the 55-year-old building. "With a 15-year history of deferred maintenance, the bill runs up. But it's certainly not $500 million ... That's a scare tactic ... There's no reason why those doors can't be opened today."
Among the speakers was Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who urged people to make their voices heard at city hall. She said a staff report will be made public Monday examining the Ontario government's obligations under its lease with the city. While the Ontario government owns the centre, the lands it's built on are leased from the city and the Toronto and Region Conservation Authority for a 99-year term, expiring in 2064.
Chow said the report will explore repair costs, operating costs and what role, if any, the city can play - she noted the city is financially strapped.
"Come to the executive committee meeting and talk about your dreams, your imagination and ... what role the city can play," she said, adding the next meeting is Tuesday. "Count on the city as one of your allies."
"Under the new deal, the city and the province said that we would explore a partnership to make sure there is local, science-based programming right at the Science Centre. So what does that look like? We don't know. But this is the time to quickly think about what that looks like so that we can, at least, have a conversation with the province."
Chow later told reporters, "We need a Science Centre. And we cannot have it closed for too long because children deserve much better."
NDP MPP Doly Begum said, "For us folks in Scarborough, we don't actually have a lot of fantastic places like the Science Centre."
"This is a prized possession," said the representative for Scarborough Southwest, urging the public to continue fighting to keep it at its current location, noting the premier has reversed course on other decisions. As she spoke, she was flanked by NDP colleagues Kristyn Wong-Tam (Toronto Centre) and Bhutila Karpoche (Parkdale-High Park).
After the rally, community members marched to the Science Centre, waving signs and chanting, as passing cars honked in support.
In an interview with the Star, Hafiza, 11, said class trips to the centre inspired her to want to become an astronaut or scientist when she grows up.
"It's really interesting to me that in the universe there's lots to explore still," said Hafiza, with a handmade poster that read, "This is not your Science Centre. It is Ontario's."
Vitoria Paterson, who had a membership to the centre, said visits there were a highlight for her daughters, aged four and seven.
"We love going there," she told the Star.
"When I broke the news that they had shut the centre, effective immediately, they both almost burst into tears."
The province closed the Science Centre on June 21 and has released two engineering reports. The initial one by Rimkus Consulting Group, which prompted the closure, said while the centre is safe now, there's a risk that a buildup of snow or rain could cause the collapse of roof panels built with reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete. A peer review by VanBoxmeer & Stranges Engineering Ltd. confirmed the overall findings. At a presentation last week, government infrastructure officials showed photos of deteriorating concrete, boiler insulation failure, and problems with the heat and cooling system.
The province says closing the centre is the best option due to repair costs. And, it was already slated in 2028 to relocate to Ontario Place, which is being redeveloped into a waterfront park that includes a privately owned spa. A search is now underway for an interim location.
With files from
the Star archives
CREDIT: Isabel Teotonio Toronto Star With files from the Star archives

Document 300 of 500

Booze is to Ford as gold was to Midas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/booze-is-ford-as-gold-was-midas/docview/3080187536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Booze+is+to+Ford+as+gold+was+to+Midas&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alcohol is to Ontario Premier Doug Ford what gold was to Midas, what artisanal coffee is to millennials, what Ivanka Trump is to Donald Trump. It's an essential material, the core around which life swirls.
Now I don't understand the concept of craving something that is readily available to the point of forcing.
We can obtain booze at home, at other people's homes, at the LCBO, at other stores, in restaurants and bars and all day at the airport. We can drink it in parks.
Same with coffee. Ubiquitous. Ivanka Trump and her husbandette moved to Florida to be closer to her father. Reach out and there it is.
That I have long COVID and wine now tastes like room-temperature bear urine is immaterial. (Why is the bear in a room though?) Ford makes sure alcohol is as reachable for me as it is for alcoholics desperately trying to avoid booze-triggers.
When the LCBO strike began, Ford even published what looked like a road map to perdition, guiding Ontario booze hounds to Wine Rack stores and such, the kind of comedy gold we remember from his brother Rob Ford's days as mayor. Not so long ago, how we longed for a similar map of pharmacies stocked with COVID vaccines.
But now I wonder. Perhaps I couldn't see the forest for the beer jokes. Maybe it's not that Ford, who says he doesn't drink, doesn't like the booze so much as he detests the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, which represents 9,000 striking LCBO workers and donates to his opponents.
Similarly, the existence of the Beer Store offended Ford, so he paid it at least $225 million to let beer be sold in a wider world.
But why? Because the Beer Store was ugly, soul-destroying and industrial? Nah. Ford doesn't care about the esthetics of getting hammered. Because he has a principled opposition to monopolies? No, Ford has no principles, not as such. But he has preferences.
He likes privatization. Small-minded Conservatives like privatized highways, private retail and outsourced jobs.
If there had been a buyer wanting to run the Ontario Science Centre in Don Mills as a going concern, attached to, yes, a condo development, maybe he would have given it to them as long as they built a new roof. I wait agog.
A new Disney-fied science centre at Ontario Place is what Ford envisions. It's his idea of family fun.
It would be a place to store the kids while prosperous parents spend the day at that private Austrian spa, drinking small-batch alcohols, soaking, gambling and contracting a urinary tract infection from poorly cleaned tubs. No, a nail fungus. Trichophyton rubrum will be our word du jour.
I have a stock tank at home but right now it's full of zucchini plants. So there I'd sit at Therme, staring out at Lake Ontario and sloppily singing old Blue Rodeo songs in a privatized tank, thinking about Ford.
What does our premier want? Better yet, what do we want in a premier? A Keir Starmer, I think. Someone who'd put an end to all the nonsense on Day One, as the new British prime minister did. No one will be sent to Rwanda. It's over, he said.
We want a premier who prioritizes health care, education, housing and transit along with a green plan for climate change. What we have is a strange man obsessed with booze, online gambling and raising speed limits while giving good Ontario land to real estate developers building great stupid swathes of oversized homes for buyers who'll be sunk in regret by 2032.
We want a sensible person. I'm tired of writing columns about peripheral Ford entertainments. I want a government based on thought and substance. I won't get it of course but this is what I want.
HEATHER MALLICK IS A TORONTO-BASED COLUMNIST COVERING CURRENT AFFAIRS FOR THE STAR. FOLLOW HER ON X: @HEATHERMALLICK
CAPTION: Ontarians want a premier who prioritizes health care, education, housing and transit along with a green plan for climate change, Heather Mallick writes, but what we have is Doug Ford's obsession with booze.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star

CREDIT: Heather Mallick Toronto Star OPINION HEATHER MALLICK IS A TORONTO-BASED COLUMNIST COVERING CURRENT AFFAIRS FOR THE STAR. FOLLOW HER ON X: @HEATHERMALLICK;

Document 301 of 500

Ford pushes for faster access to medications: Energy, trade also on agenda at premiers summit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pushes-faster-access-medications/docview/3080187512/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pushes+for+faster+access+to+medications%3A+Energy%2C+trade+also+on+agenda+at+premiers+summit&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Getting medications approved and into patients' hands can take about two years in Canada - when other countries approve them in half that time, or less - and it's a concern Doug Ford wants to make a key issue at this year's meeting of premiers.
The Ontario premier arrives Monday morning for the annual gathering of all 10 provincial and three territorial leaders, hosted this summer by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, the current chair of the Council of the Federation. Ford takes over as head of the federation in August, and will host next summer's meeting in Ontario.
"To ensure Canada maintains its advantage as a leader in health-care innovation, we also need to get new publicly funded medicines to people as fast as possible," a Ford spokesperson said Saturday.
"Currently, patients in Canada wait almost two years to access life-saving breakthrough medicines, a year longer than in other developed countries, placing us last in the G7. As Premier Ford takes over as chair of the Council of the Federation, one of his focuses will be doing everything possible to give Canadian patients the same timely access to life-saving treatments as patients in the rest of the world."
The 13 premiers will meet with Indigenous leaders on Monday before holding a number of closed-door sessions on a range of topics over the following two days, including energy resources and independence, Arctic security and sovereignty - amid worries of ongoing Russian aggression - as well as the U.S. election and what to expect from a Biden or Trump administration, especially in terms of protectionist trade policies.
They are also expected to address "revitalizing co-operative federalism in Canada," which Houston recently wrote to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau about on behalf of all premiers, asking him to "work with us in a true partnership."
"Canadians are facing immense and complex challenges that require all orders of government to work together," Houston said in his July 11 letter.
"To revive the collaborative spirit of the federation, we urge the federal government to refrain from unilateral actions in areas of provincial and territorial jurisdiction, particularly in health care, education and housing. In areas of shared responsibility, timely outreach and engagement will ensure that our respective programs, funding and policies complement one another and are effective in each jurisdiction across Canada."
Houston said "when all governments work together, we can find the best solutions for the challenges we face. This meeting will allow premiers to engage on the issues that matter most to Canadians."
Ford's spokesperson also said "in the face of global uncertainty, it's never been more important for Ontario and Canada to have a secure and affordable homegrown energy supply that people and businesses can rely on."
Ford "is keen to discuss the future of clean, reliable nuclear energy, including small modular reactors, and how provinces can work together to make it easier to build energy infrastructure across borders."
Newly appointed Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce recently held talks with U.S. Department of Energy officials and has since reached out to U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer Granholm for a meeting, in part to highlight the province's technology as well as exporting nuclear energy to the States.
Canada's three northern premiers are hoping to highlight their unique needs on housing and infrastructure, as well as broader security issues.
"The NWT has benefited greatly from the provincial-territorial relationships at the (Council of the Federation) table," said Agata Gutkowska, press secretary for Northwest Territories Premier R.J. Simpson.
"With the system of consensus government, to the co-management of lands and resources legislation with Indigenous governments, the N.W.T. is a unique jurisdiction in Canada."
Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai is looking for better collaboration with the federal government and what the provinces and territories can do as a group to foster that, given their "strong and productive" relations, as well as looking at keeping Arctic sovereignty "top of mind for all Canadians."
That's also a major concern for Nunavut Premier P.J. Akeeagok, who has been able to move new housing into high gear but says more needs to be done to encourage people to live in remote communities - including investments in things like hydro or fibre optics - which is part of the broader plan to boost security in the North.
That issue received unanimous support from all 13 premiers at their meeting two summers ago, just months after Russia first invaded Ukraine.
"Arctic sovereignty and security are priorities for Nunavut at this year's national premiers conference," Akeeagok said in a statement to the Star.
"Our territory wants to see investments in multi-use infrastructure such as deep-sea ports that can address Canada's security needs while revitalizing Nunavut's economic potential. These are nation-building projects that address reconciliation while serving local Arctic communities."
Last summer, when the premiers met in Winnipeg, Ford's main focus was getting Ottawa to work with provinces to help big infrastructure projects move along faster, in particular to reduce delays and duplication, especially on environmental assessments.
In March, the province and federal government ended their spat over Ottawa's insistence on environmental assessments on Highway 413 that Ontario officials said was causing construction delays.
The two levels of government instead signed a memorandum of understanding to create a collaborative working group to look at addressing environmental impacts. That came after a Supreme Court decision that found Ottawa had "overstepped" its authority on the provincial highway project, a key campaign promise of Ford's.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3080187487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford%27s+push+to+privatize+booze+will+be+costly%2C+McQuaig%2C+July+11+Pressure+mounting+on+Ford+to+resolve+LCBO+disruption%2C+July+13+PM+bristles+at+defence+spending+criticism%2C+July+12+Runway+cleared+for+major+development%2C+July+11+The+best+of+the+Toronto+Fringe+Festival%2C+July+11&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario relies on LCBO revenue
Ford's push to privatize booze will be costly, McQuaig, July 11
Columnist Linda McQuaig brings up a point rarely spoken about: the cost to our public economy and essential services when the provincial Conservative government moves to privatize part of our services.
Rarely do we hear that the LCBO is a profitable publicly owned company that pours millions of dollars back into our economy. These dollars help pay for our health and long-term care, education and environmental protection. Loss of LCBO sales means loss of jobs.
Rarely do we discuss that by eliminating union jobs we are adding to the financial stress many Ontarians are feeling. When we turn our backs on well-paid union employment and look to minimum wage and insecure jobs at local convenience and grocery stores, we are leading a push to the bottom for the majority of Ontarians.
McQuaig's piece should be read and considered by all of us who care about the kind of society we want to live in.
Fran Bazos, Newmarket
One wonders why we haven't heard about much about what Ontario has to gain with off-loading wine, beer and beverage drinks to private outlets. What sort of net profits will the government realize with this off-loading? Will it realize a net gain once all those LCBO jobs are gone?
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
Restaurants
and the LCBO
Pressure mounting on Ford to resolve LCBO disruption, July 13
The July 12 letter that said restaurants should have just stocked up on booze before the strike misses the point. Restaurants, like all businesses, work on cash flow. Stocking up with a month's supply is not so easy.
Mark Carriere, Mississauga
Trudeau talked but said nothing
PM bristles at defence spending criticism, July 12
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has done it again, making big announcements but providing no details. This time at the NATO summit he said Canada will meet the commitment of spending two per cent of GDP to the alliance by 2032.
Really? The PM didn't say just how that 2032 target will be met. Trudeau also bragged earlier about buying up to a dozen submarines that can go under ice. By when? Not sure. How much? Can't say. Oh well. I'm sure most Canadians have realized by now how Trudeau is all sizzle and no steak.
Dan Nyznik, Keene, Ont.
I find it strange that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's commitment was announced at the NATO meeting instead of in the House of Commons when the last budget was being announced. The prime minister and the minister of defence appear like two school boys caught playing hooky from school. They seem willing to say anything to escape the wrath of the NATO principal. I am encouraged that the NATO countries that border on the Arctic and North Atlantic Ocean are going to co-operate in the defence of these waters with new icebreakers. I wait with much interest for the details on how the government is going to pay for these new ships, who is going to build them and how we are going to entice our unemployed youth to join the forces to participate.
Ken Stock, Port Hope, Ont.
Federal dental plan made world of difference
Thanks to the Trudeau Liberals and the Singh NDP agreement, I had my teeth cleaned by a capable hygienist recently for the first time in many years. Two decades of part-time/casual employment with no benefits (intermingled with some years of full-time employment providing enough benefits to cover one visit to the dentist per year) was one reason I rarely see a dentist as I am now retired and on a fixed income.
Meanwhile, Premier Doug Ford's rubber-stamping rolling back rental controls increased my spending elsewhere, so I couldn't pay for dental work, while neglecting to adjust the Ontario Seniors Dental Care Program for inflation.
Everyone but the Conservatives seem to know that Canada's burgeoning poverty class needs health care as opposed to a prolonged spiralling downward into the misery of poor physical and mental health. It isn't so much that the Conservatives suffer from ignorance of the issues, it is that they care nothing about people and social policy. They care everything about power.
Todd Benson, Welland, Ont.
Create housing that works at Downsview
Runway cleared for major development, July 11
The Downsview redevelopment plan is a perfect opportunity to create housing that really works for people. That will happen only if every housing unit in the complex is built using universal design, which is a series of accessibility features that make the unit suitable for anyone of any age or ability.
The units will not look institutional, and if accessibility is incorporated at the design stage, the units are not significantly more expensive to build. It's fine to build for different income levels, but housing that's affordable isn't much use if a person can't get inside it and can't physically use it. Let accessibility be one of the social justice principles guiding this promising new neighbourhood.
Carol Damioli, Accessible Housing Network, Toronto
Fringe Festival shows were outstanding
The best of the Toronto Fringe Festival, July 11
What a lot of fresh theatre air during Fringe 2024. We saw quite a few excellent shows and yes, there were some that should not be in this festival.
One show that does not need a review is "Stiff and Sons," written by Aidan Gouveia and directed by Daniel Reale. Why does it not need a review? Because the audience was the jury and they gave it golden status. We laughed, sniggered, felt awkward, cringed and guffawed while these actors took us on a joyride not to be forgotten. Playing over-the-top and in-the-moment takes superhuman focus, and the ensemble excelled.
And yes, it was dark comedy par excellence. Physicality was superb and in many cases almost dancelike. The pace never dropped and the suspense stayed alive throughout. How did they do that? They acted using energy from one another. You could feel it. Creative use of space impressed. Voice work and diction on point. Thank you!
Esther Bredenkamp, Toronto

Document 303 of 500

Doug Ford to focus on getting medications approved faster at annual premiers' conference

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-focus-on-getting-medications-approved/docview/3080841321/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+to+focus+on+getting+medications+approved+faster+at+annual+premiers%27+conference&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX - Getting medications approved and into patients' hands can take about two years in Canada - when other countries approve in half that time, or less - and it's a concern Doug Ford wants to make a key issue at this year's meeting of premiers.
The Ontario premier arrives Monday morning for the annual gathering of all 10 provincial and three territorial leaders, hosted this summer by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, the current chair of the Council of the Federation. Ford takes over as head of the federation in August, and will host next summer's meeting in Ontario.
"To ensure Canada maintains its advantage as a leader in health care innovation, we also need to get new publicly funded medicines to people as fast as possible," a Ford spokesperson said Saturday.
"Currently, patients in Canada wait almost two years to access life-saving breakthrough medicines, a year longer than in other developed countries, placing us last in the G7. As Premier Ford takes over as chair of the Council of the Federation, one of his focuses will be doing everything possible to give Canadian patients the same timely access to life-saving treatments as patients in the rest of the world."
The 13 premiers will meet with Indigenous leaders on Monday before holding a number of closed-door sessions on a range of topics over the following two days, including energy resources and independence, arctic security and sovereignty - amid worries of ongoing Russian aggression - as well as the U.S. election and what to expect from a Biden or Trump administration, especially in terms of protectionist trade policies.
They are also expected to address "revitalizing co-operative federalism in Canada," which Houston recently wrote to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau about on behalf of all premiers, asking him to "work with us in a true partnership."
"Canadians are facing immense and complex challenges that require all orders of government to work together," Houston said in his July 11 letter.
"To revive the collaborative spirit of the federation, we urge the federal government to refrain from unilateral actions in areas of provincial and territorial jurisdiction, particularly in health care, education and housing. In areas of shared responsibility, timely outreach and engagement will ensure that our respective programs, funding and policies complement one another and are effective in each jurisdiction across Canada."
Houston said "when all governments work together, we can find the best solutions for the challenges we face. This meeting will allow premiers to engage on the issues that matter most to Canadians."
Ford's spokesperson also said "in the face of global uncertainty, it's never been more important for Ontario and Canada to have a secure and affordable homegrown energy supply that people and businesses can rely on."
Ford "is keen to discuss the future of clean, reliable nuclear energy, including small modular reactors, and how provinces can work together to make it easier to build energy infrastructure across borders."
Newly appointed Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce recently held talks with U.S. Department of Energy officials and has since reached out to U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer Granholm for a meeting, in part to highlight the province's technology as well as exporting nuclear energy to the states.
The country's three northern premiers are hoping to highlight their unique needs on housing and infrastructure, as well as broader security issues.
"The NWT has benefitted greatly from the provincial-territorial relationships at the CoF table," said Agata Gutkowska, press secretary for Northwest Territories Premier R.J. Simpson.
"With the system of consensus government to the co-management of lands and resources legislation with Indigenous governments the NWT is a unique jurisdiction in Canada."
Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai is looking for better collaboration with the federal government and what the provinces and territories can do as a group to foster that given their "strong and productive" relations, as well as looking at keeping Arctic sovereignty "top of mind for all Canadians."
That's also a major concern for Nunavut Premier P.J. Akeeagok, who has been able to move new housing into high gear but more needs to be done to encourage people to live in remote communities - including investments in things like hydro or even fibre optics - which is part of the broader plan to boost security in the north.
That issue received unanimous support from all 13 premiers at their meeting two summers ago, just months after Russia first invaded Ukraine.
"Arctic sovereignty and security are priorities for Nunavut at this year's national premiers' conference," Akeeagok said in a statement to the Star.
"Our territory wants to see investments in multi-use infrastructure such as deep sea ports that can address Canada's security needs while revitalizing Nunavut's economic potential. These are nation-building projects that address reconciliation while serving local Arctic communities."
Last summer, when the premiers met in Winnipeg, Ford's main focus was getting Ottawa to work with provinces to help big infrastructure projects move along faster, in particular to reduce delays and duplication, especially on environmental assessments.
In March, the province and federal government ended their spat over Ottawa's insistence on environmental assessments on Highway 413 that Ontario officials said was causing construction delays.
The two levels of government instead signed a memorandum of understanding to create a collaborative working group to look at addressing environmental impacts. That came after a Supreme Court decision that found Ottawa had "overstepped" its authority on the provincial highway project, a key campaign promise of Ford's.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.Halifax waterfront: Artwork swiped from a city pub was restored after the owners used social media to appeal for its return.Halifax's boardwalk starts at Casino Nova Scotia and ends at a farmers market, passing marinas and other sights.

Document 304 of 500

LCBO walks back plan to open dozens of stores on Friday as strike continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-walks-back-plan-open-dozens-stores-on-friday/docview/3080841284/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+walks+back+plan+to+open+dozens+of+stores+on+Friday+as+strike+continues&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The LCBO will no longer be opening its stores until the strike is resolved, reversing its original plan to open 32 select locations for limited hours starting on Friday, the company said in a statement Sunday afternoon.
The LCBO said it will instead reallocate its personnel to other parts of its operations as it continues to serve retail customers through online shopping on its website. Currently, management staff are running the operations, with orders being fulfilled within a week, according to an LCBO spokesperson.
"This pivot means that we will be able to improve how we serve Ontario bars and restaurants to help increase product selection, availability, and expedited delivery," the company wrote. More details will be shared directly with the customers, it said.
This is the second time the LCBO has backed out of opening stores during the strike. Last Wednesday, the company was supposed to open five stores to exclusively serve licensees, including bars, restaurants, nightclubs and lounges. But it has since scrapped that plan, citing threats by the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) to picket the locations.
"Workers saw this announcement coming," JP Hornick, president of OPSEU, told the Star on Sunday. "Without the LCBO workers, the LCBO doesn't work."
The strike began just after midnight on July 5, with more than 9,000 workers hitting the picket lines, forcing the closure of nearly 700 stores. Since then, pressure has been mounting on Premier Doug Ford to resolve the strike, while OPSEU is said to have walked away from the bargaining table.
"We are prepared to go back to the table as soon as it is clear to us that (Premier) Ford and the LCBO are prepared to bargain in good faith a future for the LCBO that will protect good jobs and public revenues and create more stable and permanent jobs," said Hornick.
LCBO workers "miss their regulars and want to get back to work," Hornick added. "It's on Ford to end this strike."
The LCBO said in a statement it is committed to reaching an agreement with OPSEU, and encourages the union to consider its "fair offer."
OPSEU's main concern relates to Ford's plan to sell beer, wine and ready-to-drink beverages at supermarkets, convenience and big-box stores. The Progressive Conservative government is spending anywhere between $225 million and $1 billion to expedite liberalized alcohol sales by Oct. 31.
This, OPSEU argues, threatens job security for LCBO employees as it believes it will lose significant business to large private owners of convenience and grocery stores.
"LCBO workers are on strike for the future of the LCBO, the $2.5 billion in revenues it generates each year and for the thousands of jobs on the line," OPSEU chair Colleen MacLeod said in a statement on Friday.
Ford, however, has said the province won't budge when it comes to the expansion. "If they want to negotiate over (ready-to-drink beverages), the deal's off ... that ship has sailed - it's halfway across Lake Ontario."
Meanwhile, some grocery stores are running out of liquor, as buyers stock up in a frenzy. Bars and restaurants have also been struggling to snatch customer favourites since the strike, with industry insiders criticizing the LCBO for its unsuccessful contingency plan.
With files from Kristin Rushowy, Robert Benzie and Josh Rubin.
CAPTION: LCBO workers picket at the Yonge and Wellesley location in Toronto on July 9.

Document 305 of 500

Justin Trudeau is reckoning with the decision of his political life. Here's why few know what he will do

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-reckoning-with-decision-his/docview/3079952831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+reckoning+with+the+decision+of+his+political+life.+Here%27s+why+few+know+what+he+will+do&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is a moment of reckoning for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
The question of whether to stay or go, or to deliver some other kind of dramatic change the Liberal caucus is clamouring for, is one of the most sensitive and solitary exercises a prime minister can undertake.
Sometimes the moment arrives when bitter internal rivalries push a leader out, as happened to Jean Chrétien. Sometimes it comes on the night of a general election defeat, as it did for Paul Martin and Stephen Harper.
Sometimes it is imposed on a prime minister by devastating poll results, as it was for Brian Mulroney, or as in Trudeau's case now, by dint of a brutal byelection loss that telegraphs future trouble for his party.
However it arrives, it is a decision laden with political and policy implications. At the same time, it is deeply personal and is contemplated in privacy if not secrecy, in large part due to the nature of the job.
Most cabinet ministers would not be in the loop, and sources who spoke to the Star said Trudeau has not shown them his cards.
That makes sense, said several sources, including current and former government officials, all of whom spoke to the Star on condition they not be identified in order to speak frankly.
One said it is "very difficult to have candid conversations with anyone" in cabinet or caucus about it, not just because he might want the element of surprise, but because the prime minister would rightly fear immediate leaks.
If Trudeau doesn't choose a major cabinet shuffle or staff shakeup at the Prime Minister's Office or Liberal party headquarters, and opts instead for his own departure, his intention would likely only be known to a couple of people, most suggested.
Unlike the choice to run for party leadership, when a politician must consult widely, cajole support, and call on friends to commit their energies, ideas and money to campaigning, another source said, "Leaving is a solitary act."
"You cannot for one second - unless there are people very, very, very close to you - hint that you're going, because you still have to command respect and be able to get people to follow you.
"You are there until the minute you decide you're not. There's no (Mont-)Tremblant-type of exercise that's going to go on, where 15 people are going to sit around a hot tub and decide whether or not he's going to stay or go."
That's a reference to Trudeau's memoir, "Common Ground," in which he describes a three-day retreat with a couple of dozen friends and advisers. They puzzled through the future of the Liberal party and whether Trudeau should dive into the leadership contest to replace Michael Ignatieff, the leader who had dragged down Liberals to third place in the House of Commons.
Trudeau wrote that he learned while travelling internationally with his father, prime minister Pierre Trudeau, to make big decisions after listening to many voices and opinions.
But Trudeau's own leadership style since he took the top office hasn't always followed that path.
Current and former caucus members describe Trudeau as someone with whom they do not have a close relationship.
Three former cabinet ministers have written books that go farther, and call Trudeau's approach to government overly centralized, and reliant on only a few trusted advisers. Jody Wilson-Raybould, Bill Morneau and Marc Garneau portray Trudeau as a distant, aloof prime minister with whom they never had the access or the input they expected.
Others disagree. They say Trudeau is open to challenge and debate, yet ultimately is one to take decisions, certain in his own judgment.
A lot of people think they know Justin Trudeau.
Few really do.
Trudeau himself has described a kind of personal defence mechanism acquired over a lifetime in the public eye as the son of a prime minister.
He is swayed by neither his most adamant haters nor admirers.
An introvert despite the enthusiastic grip-and-grin impression he leaves as he glad-hands his way through a crowd, Trudeau has a broad circle of people he knows and has relied upon over the years.
But he has only a small circle of people he remains close to and trusts.
By their own accounts, once someone leaves his inner circle, they're out. It's not that Trudeau dumps them. Rather, they become distanced or removed from his day-to-day world, and are no longer among those he turns to for advice.
And now, for all Trudeau has talked about "engaging" with Liberal colleagues and caucus members following last month's defeat by the Conservatives in Toronto-St. Paul's, most do not know what he is thinking as he mulls the way forward.
After nine years in government, a yearlong plunge in public opinion polls, and the humbling loss of a long-held Liberal riding in downtown Toronto, some who consider themselves his friends wonder where his head is at.
Publicly, Trudeau says only that he believes now is the time for the Liberal government to double down on delivering services that improve the lives of Canadians hit hard by inflation and high interest rates, and that's where his focus lies. Trudeau frames his determination to push through his progressive agenda as an antidote to the loss of public faith in leaders and institutions, to democratic disengagement, and as a counterweight to the rise of the populist right - as he sees it.
That strikes some Liberals as hubris.
One said it seems like Trudeau thinks there isn't anyone else who can do that job, but there always is, the source said, and "there has to be. That's the nature of democracy. Someone else always has to be" able to do the job.
Another Liberal who supports Trudeau described grappling with the dilemma of "what's the best option for him, what's the best option for the party, what's the best option for the country."
"I still think he's the best option for the country, and he still represents the best option for the party," that person said. However it is not clear to him if staying on "is the right call" for the prime minister personally or if Trudeau should leave now with his head high and a strong policy legacy intact.
Trudeau can be a risk-taker. He took a big risk in calling a snap election, mid-pandemic, in 2021.
But since the byelection loss that triggered angst in Liberal ranks, he has not taken the risk of convening a national caucus meeting, as many are calling for, to let members of Parliament vent or let MPs in on his thinking.
To some, that makes tactical sense in that it keeps nervous MPs away from the national media.
To others, it is strategically the wrong thing to do. They say it makes Trudeau seem unwilling or unable to take the chance that he can't hold caucus together.
Still, Trudeau likes to surprise, as when he announced he would decriminalize cannabis, required all Liberal candidates to support abortion rights, bought a pipeline, and ditched electoral reform.
He is unafraid of upending Liberal orthodoxies - ousting senators from the Liberal caucus, creating a Senate appointment process intended to remove patronage, or dropping fees for party membership to expand his base.
At the NATO summit in Washington, he announced Canada would meet its defence spending promise to the alliance by 2032, a declaration he has avoided making for nearly nine years.
By his own description, Trudeau likes to be underestimated - as he was in his political curtain-raiser boxing match with a young Conservative senator.
Now in his 13th year of elected office, has he ruled anything out? Probably not.
Trudeau once said he never viewed himself as a lifelong politician, and wasn't his father who spent nearly 20 years in parliament. Near the end of his first term, then still with a majority government, the prime minister privately expressed that whenever he left politics, he would have no regrets.
He has even mused about taking up some kind of teaching role in a post-political career.
But at age 52, is Justin Trudeau about to give up what is probably the best job he'll ever have?
Text me if you know.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives for a meeting at the 2024 North Atlantic Treaty Organization summit in Washington on July 11, 2024.

Subject: Political leadership; Prime ministers

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Title: Justin Trudeau is reckoning with the decision of his political life. Here's why few know what he will do

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jul 14, 2024

Document 306 of 500

PM facing decision of his political life: Trudeau weighing next moves in secrecy, with even cabinet ministers unsure what he's thinking

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-facing-decision-his-political-life/docview/3079877298/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+facing+decision+of+his+political+life%3A+Trudeau+weighing+next+moves+in+secrecy%2C+with+even+cabinet+ministers+unsure+what+he%27s+thinking&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

They puzzled through the future of the Liberal party and whether Trudeau should dive into the leadership contest to replace Michael Ignatieff, the leader who had dragged down Liberals to third place in the House of Commons.
Trudeau wrote that he learned while travelling internationally with his father, prime minister Pierre Trudeau, to make big decisions after listening to many voices and opinions.
But Trudeau's own leadership style since he took the top office hasn't always followed that path.
Current and former caucus members describe Trudeau as someone with whom they do not have a close relationship.
Three former cabinet ministers have written books that go farther, and call Trudeau's approach to government overly centralized, and reliant on only a few trusted advisers. Jody Wilson-Raybould, Bill Morneau and Marc Garneau portray Trudeau as a distant, aloof prime minister with whom they never had the access or the input they expected.
Others disagree. They say Trudeau is open to challenge and debate, yet ultimately is one to take decisions, certain in his own judgment.
A lot of people think they know Justin Trudeau.
Few really do.
Trudeau himself has described a kind of personal defence mechanism acquired over a lifetime in the public eye as the son of a prime minister.
He is swayed by neither his most adamant haters nor admirers.
An introvert despite the enthusiastic grip-and-grin impression he leaves as he glad-hands his way through a crowd, Trudeau has a broad circle of people he knows and has relied upon over the years.
But he has only a small circle of people he remains close to and trusts.
By their own accounts, once someone leaves his inner circle, they're out. It's not that Trudeau dumps them.
Rather, they become distanced or removed from his day-to-day world, and are no longer among those he turns to for advice.
And now, for all Trudeau has talked about "engaging" with Liberal colleagues and caucus members following last month's defeat by the Conservatives in Toronto-St. Paul's, most do not know what he is thinking as he mulls the way forward.
After nine years in government, a yearlong plunge in public opinion polls, and the humbling loss of a long-held Liberal riding in downtown Toronto, some who consider themselves his friends wonder where his head is at.
Publicly, Trudeau says only that he believes now is the time for the Liberal government to double down on delivering services that improve the lives of Canadians hit hard by inflation and high interest rates, and that's where his focus lies. Trudeau frames his determination to push through his progressive agenda as an antidote to the loss of public faith in leaders and institutions, to democratic disengagement, and as a counterweight to the rise of the populist right - as he sees it.
That strikes some Liberals as hubris.
One said it seems like Trudeau thinks there isn't anyone else who can do that job, but there always is, the source said, and "there has to be. That's the nature of democracy. Someone else always has to be" able to do the job.
Another Liberal who supports Trudeau described grappling with the dilemma of "what's the best option for him, what's the best option for the party, what's the best option for the country."
"I still think he's the best option for the country, and he still represents the best option for the party," that person said.
However it is not clear to him if staying on "is the right call" for the prime minister personally or if Trudeau should leave now with his head high and a strong policy legacy intact.
Trudeau can be a risk-taker. He took a big risk in calling a snap election, mid-pandemic, in 2021.
But since the byelection loss that triggered angst in Liberal ranks, he has not taken the risk of convening a national caucus meeting, as many are calling for, to let members of Parliament vent or let MPs in on his thinking.
To some, that makes tactical sense in that it keeps nervous MPs away from the national media.
To others, it is strategically the wrong thing to do. They say it makes Trudeau seem unwilling or unable to take the chance that he can't hold caucus together.
Still, Trudeau likes to surprise, as when he announced he would decriminalize cannabis, required all Liberal candidates to support abortion rights, bought a pipeline, and ditched electoral reform.
He is unafraid of upending Liberal orthodoxies - ousting senators from the Liberal caucus, creating a Senate appointment process intended to remove patronage, or dropping fees for party membership to expand his base.
At the NATO summit in Washington, he announced Canada would meet its defence spending promise to the alliance by 2032, a declaration he has avoided making for nearly nine years.
By his own description, Trudeau likes to be underestimated - as he was in his political curtain-raiser boxing match with a young Conservative senator.
Now in his 13th year of elected office, has he ruled anything out? Probably not.
Trudeau once said he never viewed himself as a lifelong politician, and wasn't his father who spent nearly 20 years in parliament. Near the end of his first term, then still with a majority government, the prime minister privately expressed that whenever he left politics, he would have no regrets.
He has even mused about taking up some kind of teaching role in a post-political career.
But at age 52, is Justin Trudeau about to give up what is probably the best job he'll ever have?
Text me if you know.
CAPTION: If Prime Minister Justin Trudeau opts for his own departure, his intention would likely only be known to a couple of people, sources say. "Leaving is a solitary act," one source said. "You cannot for one second - unless there are people very, very, very close to you - hint that you're going, because you still have to command respect and be able to get people to follow you."Kent NishimuraGetty Images

Document 307 of 500

Liberals, NDP should look to France

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-ndp-should-look-france/docview/3079876551/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals%2C+NDP+should+look+to+France&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's a unifying thought for a politically divided Canada: Take a page from an even more politically polarized France.
I don't mean import its political culture, which is increasingly intolerant and ungovernable. I'm thinking of the French penchant for improvisation in their hour of desperation.
Facing a high-stakes parliamentary election, politicians from across the spectrum came together this month to gang up against France's far-right proto-fascists. Could Canada's rival progressive parties ever come together in the same way for a similar purpose?
Don't discount the idea of a better way of counting ballots.
After the first round of voting in France, Marine Le Pen's National Rally - an anti-immigrant, nakedly Islamophobic movement cloaked in antisemitism - emerged with a stunning lead that put her on a path to power. But in the runoff voting that followed this week, rival parties of the centre and left co-ordinated their campaigns in an unprecedented common front.
Competing parties bowed out of the tightest races to avoid splitting the centre-left vote, rallying around the candidate best-placed to overtake the far-right leader in key ridings. Even if far from ideal, the muddled outcome is far better than the alternative of unravelling French domestic and foreign policy.
What lessons can be applied to Canadian politics? Under our existing system, known as first past the post (FPTP), the victor needs only a plurality of the ballots cast to edge out everyone else - typically without a majority of more than 50 per cent.
Many will pine, predictably, for proportional representation (PR) to choose a government that is more mathematically representative of the popular vote - with smaller one-issue parties winning a bigger share of seats on the fringe, further splintering Parliament. All that said and argued, Canada's political reality reminds us of the elusiveness of electoral reform.
PR isn't coming anytime soon - it keeps failing in one provincial referendum after another. It fizzled out federally because the rival parties were too suffused by self-interest to reach a compromise.
Conservatives prefer the thumping majorities of FPTP; Greens and New Democrats dream of holding the balance of power in perpetuity with their PR vote share assured; Liberals, as ever, are somewhere in the middle. But the French experience offers an example of how to practise politics differently, in two different ways.
First, I've long argued (and fantasized) that Liberals and New Democrats might unite against a united right, rather than dragging each other down while the Tories rise to the top. More recently, the two parties have worked (and voted) together in the current minority Parliament to bring forward historic legislation on pharmacare and dental care.
Why work at cross-purposes at campaign time? Short of formal unification, why not informal collaboration to avoid splitting the progressive vote in ridings where the Conservatives can come up the middle? Instead of praying for people to vote strategically, why don't the progressive parties campaign strategically by agreeing not to run against each other in winnable ridings?
There is a second way to apply the French result, by adopting the ranked ballot system already used in Australia.
The virtue of this voting system is that it allows for true majority rule, riding by riding.
The conventional ranked ballot is part of our history, we just don't recognize its traditional use in political conventions: Remember that none of our mainstream political parties relies on just a single ballot to choose its chief in a leadership race (or they'd win with only a paltry plurality); leadership conventions typically hold several ballots, with the last-place candidate eliminated in each round and their votes reallocated, until the winner achieves a majority of more than 50 per cent.
Think of the ranked ballot as an electronic runoff, where your second and third choices are reallocated if your candidate trails the pack. The outcome better reflects the electoral consensus - not just who you like most, but who you dislike least.
That's essentially what happened in the French elections. Under the French legislative system, votes are conducted in two stages: after the first round, any candidate with less than 12.5 per cent support is eliminated and voters cast their second ballot a week later, choosing from among the remaining rivals; in France, party strategists looked at the results of the first round and pared the pack to avoid splitting the vote among too many progressives.
Uniting the left isn't a perfect solution, but as the French like to say, the perfect is the enemy of the good. The ranked ballot isn't without fault, but as the British say of democracy itself, it's better than the alternatives.
CAPTION: Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives stand to benefit as Justin Trudeau's Liberals and Jagmeet Singh's NDP split the left-of-centre vote in the next election.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Political conventions; Electoral reform; Referendums; Elections; Political parties
