Canada must withdraw from NATO. Don't talk about spending, talk about peace

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-must-withdraw-nato-dont-talk-about/docview/3079114192/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+must+withdraw+from+NATO.+Don%27t+talk+about+spending%2C+talk+about+peace&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau and NATO
Under pressure, Justin Trudeau announces when Canada expects to hit military spending commitments to NATO, July 11
There has been a lot of discussion lately about Canada's contribution to NATO, always quoting a percentage of GDP. But this is meaningless to the average person. Let's talk dollars. Canada has the seventh largest economy by GDP in the world, not including India and China. Canada made the seventh largest contribution in dollars to NATO in 2023 with over $29.5 billion. Only the U.S., Germany, United Kingdom, France, Italy and Poland contributed more money than Canada. This was about $10 billion more than the next highest contribution. My point is that contributions from Canada, Germany, France and Italy, all less than two per cent, are already contributing substantial funds to NATO every year, far greater than almost any other country regardless of the percentage of their GDP. No one should think that Canada is not making a significant contribution to NATO. It's time to talk dollars and not percentages.
Stephen Carson, Toronto
Seeing all the questioning of Canada's commitment to NATO makes one wonder what has happened to the grand ideas that fill so much of Canada's history. Take Nobel Peace Prize winning Canadian Lester B. Pearson, with his brilliant idea of a United Nations peacekeeping force. Out-of-the-box thinking at the highest level. It is past time we started thinking grand thoughts again. Canada must withdraw from NATO! That's right, get out of NATO! This great land must become the peacekeeper to the world again by becoming the country that carries out all United Nations peacekeeping initiatives. Why? Because we are damned good at it and it will bring great pride to Canadians. A Canadian military comprising people from every country in this world will have an advantage that no one else can match. We can do this!
Roger Hilderley, Stratford, Ont.
So, the Business Council of Canada is concerned about "diplomatic isolation" should Canada not meet NATO's two-per-cent annual expenditure target. It's good to see that someone's finally thinking in terms of diplomacy. How can there be a minimum military spending threshold without there also being a base level investment in international relations? We live in a Manichaean world where the rhetoric of "we're good, they're evil" predominates, with little analysis of history or the causal factors which lead to conflict. Perhaps there would be less need to spend on artillery, tanks and drones if we were better equipped to partake in constructive dialogue with our so-called adversaries.
Joe Davidson, Toronto
LCBOs are safer than convenience stores
LCBO urges OPSEU to return to the bargaining table ASAP, July 11
I was recently at an LCBO store where I witnessed the aftermath of a robbery. There were broken bottles on the floor as I entered. The presumed culprit was being escorted out of the store by two police officers and one security guard. He was not going quietly. There was quite a struggle to get him out and detained. Once gone, an LCBO employee asked, "How are Doug Ford's corner stores going to deal with situations like this?" Before booze hits convenience stores, what provisions will be in place to ensure the safety of Ontarians? Will there be a security guard in every corner store in the province? It seems that LCBOs are a popular target for thieves as it is. With more booze options at corner stores, won't Ford create a greater and easier target? What about jobs and revenue loses? If it works, don't fix it.
G. W. Ride, Toronto
I think that expanding alcohol sales is a terrible idea and will end very badly with more crime. I absolutely support the LCBO strike. They are right. There are many important issues to be tackled.
Anne Grguric, Grand Valley, Ont.
Restaurants should have been prepared
Bars and restaurants already struggling to order favourites as LCBO strike continues, July 10
Am I missing something? Everyone knew, weeks ago, that a strike was looming at LCBO. Surely any intelligent manager of a bar or restaurant would therefore stock up on sufficient alcohol products to last at least a month. It is inventory and does not go bad.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington, Ont.
Issues with the building? Look around
Ford says second report confirms problems with Ontario Science Centre, July 10
Premier Doug Ford has declared the Ontario Science Centre "is absolutely just a total mess from top to bottom." Of course, this begs the question of which government allowed the building to deteriorate to such a degree. Since Ford has been in power for more than six years, he and his government clearly bear much of the responsibility for its neglect. Had he not been so focused on promoting beer sales in corner stores, unreadable license plates and trying to cut into Ontario's Greenbelt, he might have spared a few dollars and some energy on maintaining this building. The Ontario Science Centre is an architectural jewel in a unique Toronto ravine setting, designed by internationally-renowned architect Raymond Moriyama. Instead of celebrating its neglect, why is Ford not championing its heritage value? In fact, the larger question should be his ulterior plans for this stunning building. This building should have heritage status, and if not, why not?
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Monsters are not exclusively men
One imagines William Shakespeare could have written a good tragedy involving Alice Munro's family. The sad truth is that Munro was a great writer but a flawed human being. Perhaps there isn't much mystery in that.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
How could she? What a betrayal of the most fundamental duty of a parent to one's child! And, what's worse, "to position herself as the victim." I will never again pick up an Alice Munro book or stop myself from cringing when I hear her name. I think every library and bookstore should trash every single one of her books! God bless you, Andrea Skinner, and may you live a peaceful life.
Rita Maio, Woodbridge, Ont.
Life is fleeting
I'm deeply saddened by the loss of Benji Gregory, the talented actor who brought joy to many as character Brian Tanner on the beloved sitcom "Alf." At just 46 years old, Gregory's passing is a tragic reminder of how fleeting life can be. My thoughts are with his family, friends and fans.
Paul Bacon, Hallandale Beach, Fla.

Pressure mounting on Doug Ford to resolve LCBO strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pressure-mounting-on-doug-ford-resolve-lcbo/docview/3079113987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pressure+mounting+on+Doug+Ford+to+resolve+LCBO+strike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the first LCBO strike in history enters its second week, pressure is mounting on Premier Doug Ford to resolve the labour disruption.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Friday said it's time for Ford to part ways with Liquor Control Board of Ontario chair Carmine Nigro, his close friend.
At the same time, the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, which represents the 9,000 striking workers who have shuttered 680 LCBO stores since July 5, said Crown-owned booze giant needs to "stop the bullying and start bargaining."
"On Thursday, July 4, the LCBO put forward an insulting offer and told us not to come back unless we were willing to give up on our core demands for a better future," said Colleen MacLeod, chair of OPSEU's liquor board employees division and bargaining team.
MacLeod reiterated the union's fear that Ford's plan to sell beer, wine and premixed beverages - like White Claw and Mike's Hard Lemonade - at supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets "would threaten thousands of jobs" at the LCBO.
"How much revenue will be lost by the plan to expand alcohol sales, including ready to drinks?" said the union leader, noting the provincial chain brings in $2.5 billion to the treasury.
"The LCBO has said they want us back at the table, but only if we follow their rules and drop ready-to-drink cocktails ... (which) are the fastest growing market in the alcohol industry," she said Thursday night.
"To us, seeing those products go into 8,500 new private retail locations like gas stations and convenience chains means less hours of work, fewer jobs, and lower public revenues."
But Ford warned Wednesday "if they want to negotiate over (ready-to-drink beverages), the deal's off ... that ship has sailed - it's halfway across Lake Ontario."
The premier said the LCBO would make 400 casual employees full-time and give all workers seven per cent raises over three years.
It will remain a public entity with a monopoly on the sale of spirits and on wholesale distribution to all retailers.
But he insisted it is full-steam ahead with his plan, which will cost between $225 million and $1 billion, to speed up liberalized beer, wine and premixed cocktail sales by Oct. 31.
Crombie blamed the strike in Ford and his associates.
"It has become clear that the LCBO board is no longer working in the best interests of the people of Ontario, and is instead focused on advancing Doug Ford's personal political agenda," the Liberal leader said.
"It's no wonder why. Doug Ford handpicked the board of the LCBO, stacking it with (Progressive) Conservative insiders," she said.
"Mr. Nigro is a close personal friend of Doug Ford. He has donated tens of thousands of dollars to the PCs, and was previously a vice-president of the party," said Crombie.
"Ford's handpicked cronies can't be trusted on this billion-dollar 'boozedoggle,' which will benefit wealthy corporations, at the expense of Ontario families. It is just another example of Doug Ford's cycle of cronyism, helping his rich friends get richer."
The premier reappointed Nigro, a trusted member of his inner circle and president and CEO of CRAFT Development Corporation, to a second three-year term as LCBO czar in 2022.
On Thursday, the LCBO said it was "at the table ready for active negotiations to restart today."
But no talks are scheduled - despite calls from rank-and-file members for their resumption.
OPSEU members walked off the job last Friday, closing the LCBO for the first time in its 97-year history.
The job action came six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to move up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded liberalization, which began under former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford moves a flat of beer cans during a photo-op at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on Wednesday.

Ontario Place work put on pause + Pierre Poilievre's speech falls flat, critics say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-place-work-put-on-pause-pierre-poilievres/docview/3079113579/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Place+work+put+on+pause+%2B+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+speech+falls+flat%2C+critics+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, July 12 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on paused work at Ontario Place, criticisms of Pierre Poilievre at the Assembly of First Nations and new details about the Alice Munro revelations.
DON'T MISSWork at Ontario Place has been stopped pending a court hearing
Construction work on the controversial redevelopment of Ontario Place has been paused until July 19. Ontario Place Protectors, a group hoping to preserve the site, won a legal hearing to challenge the project on constitutional grounds. The group hopes for a declaration that legislation to expedite the project breaches the doctrine of public trust. "Ontario Place doesn't belong to the current government, it belongs to the people of Ontario and future generations," said the lawyer representing Ontario Place Protectors. Here's what they'll be arguing in court.
ICYMI: Austria-based company Therme was awarded a 95-year lease from the province to develop a $350-million private water park and spa on the West Island at Ontario Place. Earlier this year, the Ford government approved an enhanced zoning order to fast-track its development.If at first you don't succeed... An Ontario court rejected another legal challenge last month. Ontario Place for All had requested an environmental assessment of the West Island, but the Ford government passed legislation to exempt it from one.Meanwhile: The province released the second engineering report meant to support the decision to shutter the Ontario Science Centre, which is set to relocate to the redeveloped Ontario Place. However, the report, critics say, doesn't recommend that the iconic Toronto museum be closed.Pierre Poilievre sharply criticized after speech to the Assembly of First Nations
Speaking to more than 300 First Nation chiefs and representatives gathered at Montreal, Pierre Poilievre was met with anger and disappointment by some leaders. At his first speech to the Assembly of First Nations, some said the Conservative leader failed to mention policies around missing and murdered Indigenous women and girls, inherent Indigenous rights, the climate crisis, residential school survivors and the proposed $47.8-billion child welfare settlement. Here's what those chiefs and representatives had to say about the man who could be prime minister one day.
History lesson: There are some long-standing grievances held against Poilievre and his time as a cabinet minister in the government of Stephen Harper. That government oversaw spending cuts and resource extraction on Indigenous lands. In 2008, Poilievre said that Indigenous people needed to learn "the values of hard work and independence and self-reliance."Across the aisle: At the same time, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was putting out fires after a Globe story from Thursday that alleged the PM's senior advisers were questioning Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's effectiveness. He said he has "full confidence" in Freeland.Alice Munro told an OPP detective that her daughter was lying about being molested by her stepdad
A former OPP detective described to the Star the moment that famed author Alice Munro was told her husband, Gerry Fremlin, was being charged with sexually abusing her youngest daughter. Munro went into a rage, went "sideways, totally against her daughter, and all pro for him," Sam Lazarevich told reporters Betsy Powell and Deborah Dundas. He used statements from Andrea Skinner, Munro's daughter, and self-incriminating letters written by Fremlin, to charge Fremlin with the equivalent of a modern sexual assault charge. Here's how Lazarevich remembers the confrontation.
Context: Last week, the Star published Skinner's account of her stepfather's sexual abuse towards her, which began when she was nine years old. After she disclosed her abuse to her mother, Fremlin responded with letters admitting to sexual touching but claiming Skinner had initiated it.Timeline: The revelations that Munro, a Nobel Prize-winning author and Canadian icon, knew about her daughter's sexual abuse and did not act, came just months after she died in May. It's more common than you think.WHAT ELSE

Document 323 of 500

Science Centre leaders defend closure: New report echoes engineers' findings on need for repairs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/science-centre-leaders-defend-closure/docview/3078937483/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Science+Centre+leaders+defend+closure%3A+New+report+echoes+engineers%27+findings+on+need+for+repairs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shuttering the beloved Ontario Science Centre has been "emotional" - but leaders believe closing the museum was the best option because it is no longer safe due to "failing infrastructure, including critical roof issues, building systems and an inaccessible pedestrian bridge."
The centre's top brass delivered that verdict Thursday as the province released a second engineering report on the state of the facility, echoing concerns from an initial review that detailed the building's crumbling roof, among other urgently needed repairs.
But critics were quick to point out that the second report, while confirming serious issues in some areas, also did not call for the immediate shutdown that happened three weeks ago.
"It is unfortunate that we are in this circumstance - it is terrible," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said of the closure and "decommissioning" of the three Science Centre buildings. "None of us wanted to make a decision like this."
Given the risks cited in the reports, the estimated $500-million cost and length of time to make repairs - when the centre is already moving to Ontario Place in 2028 - it was the only choice for Premier Doug Ford's government, she said.
However, Jason Ash, co-chair of Save Ontario's Science Centre, took issue with that, as neither report saw the need to "require or recommend the immediate and permanent closure" of the facility.
"Independent experts continue to publicly state the building is safe to reopen, and that the repairs it needs are normal and expected for a 55-year-old building," said Ash, maintaining the Science Centre requested funding for a number of critical projects in recent years and had been denied.
"Now, Infrastructure Ontario wants to abandon the building," he said.
"Make no mistake, for whatever reason, Premier Ford wants the Ontario Science Centre demolished."
In a letter released with the report, board chair John Carmichael and CEO Paul Kortenaar said "we have been incredibly touched by the outpouring of love and support that we have received for the Ontario Science Centre since we closed our doors at 770 Don Mills Rd. on June 21, 2024 - much sooner than we anticipated.
"It has been an emotional time for all of us," they wrote, adding "the health and safety of our employees and visitors is always our top priority."
Surma said Thursday that atop the annual $25 million in provincial funding, Queen's Park spent $7 million for "emergency repairs" last year at the museum, which sits on land leased from the city of Toronto.
The original engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group said that while the Science Centre building is safe for now, rain or snow buildup could hasten damage to the roof and there is a risk of collapse in some areas that were constructed with reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete (RAAC). That prompted a decision to close down the site, so that staff would have time to remove its exhibitions by the end of October.
Making the Don Mills facility viable "would continue to require the immediate closure of the building and an immediate investment of approximately $70 million in urgent capital repair and an additional $20 million to $40 million for immediate roof repairs," the government said.
A peer review by VanBoxmeer & Stranges Engineering Ltd. (VB&S) said while some formatting of graphs and tables in the original Rimkus report was "confusing," the overall findings were sound.
"Based on our review of the documents, and our random sampling visual review of the condition of the RAAC roof panel structures, we find that evaluation of the risks identified by Rimkus are reasonable," said the VB&S review, which was released Thursday.
"Furthermore, the recommendations made to mitigate these risks through immediate remediation of critical and high-risk panels and their eventual replacement - to coincide with a roofing assembly replacement - are appropriate to safely extend the useful lifespan of the building structure," the firm said.
"We conclude that it is a concise and comprehensive narrative that adequately addresses the risks associated with RAAC elements in existing buildings."
The government warns the crumbling aerated concrete roof is only part of the problem. It says there is also the "risk of disrupting asbestos, which must be contained," as well as other concerns with the centre's Great Hall and auditorium that have not been previously addressed.
In a presentation by the Ministry of Infrastructure, photos taken Monday showed deteriorating concrete, boiler insulation failure, and problems that have left one building without any working heat or cooling system.
Ford has called it "an old, decrepit building," insisting "it just doesn't make sense to throw good money" in since it's moving to the waterfront in four years.
Since its shutdown, officials have been scrambling to find a temporary home for the science exhibits to open by January 2026.
Critics argue it would cost much less to replace the aerated concrete in the existing Science Centre than to move it to the waterfront, and that repairs could be done in phases while the building remained open to the public.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford "chose to let it fall into disrepair."
Local Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said both engineering reports clearly state the building can be salvaged.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said while "there is no doubt that the current Science Centre needs significant repairs - the only roadblock is that the premier does not see the value of the site."
It remains unclear what city hall's plans are for the sprawling property.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 324 of 500

Work stopped at Ontario Place pending hearing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/work-stopped-at-ontario-place-pending-hearing/docview/3078937419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Work+stopped+at+Ontario+Place+pending+hearing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Work on redeveloping Ontario Place into a spa and waterpark has been paused until July 19, after a group hoping to preserve the site won a legal hearing to challenge the project on constitutional grounds.
Ontario Place Protectors is seeking a declaration that the Rebuilding Ontario Place Act - passed by the Doug Ford government in an effort to expedite the redevelopment - is unconstitutional, breaching the doctrine of public trust.
"Ontario Place doesn't belong to the current government, it belongs to the people of Ontario and future generations," said Eric Gillespie, the lawyer representing Ontario Place Protectors, a broad coalition of residents and organizations, including numerous architectural interests.
"The government appears to be breaching the trust that is owed to the people of Ontario," Gillespie said.
The application, which will be heard in Ontario Superior Court, includes affidavits from organizations including the Architectural Conservancy of Ontario and the Toronto Society of Architects, among others.
The Austria-based company Therme is building a $350-million water park and spa on the West Island at Ontario Place. Work began June 23.
Ontario Place Protectors said the Ontario government agreed "not to cause or permit any permanent destruction of any trees, shrubs or buildings at Ontario Place," until after the one-day hearing is held.
But the news seemed to take provincial officials by surprise.
At Queen's Park, Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma told reporters she could not even say whether the legal action had temporarily stopped work at Queen's Park.
"There is an action in place before the courts and so as you know I cannot comment," said Surma.
"The hearing is taking place though on July 19. We look forward to July 19."
Asked if a loss in court could "endanger the entire redevelopment of Ontario Place," the minister would only say: "I cannot speculate."
The government was not able to provide further comment in time for this story's deadline.
Gillespie said Ontario Place Protectors has been working closely with Ontario Place for All, which lost a legal challenge at Ontario Divisional Court in June, requesting that an environmental assessment of the site's West Island, where the spa is being built, be conducted.
Gillespie said the new law will also be challenged on the grounds that it improperly erodes judicial authority by preventing all remedies under all statutes when it comes to Ontario Place.
"That appears to take away powers that are central to judges' abilities to operate our court system," Gillespie said.
Gillespie said that if the application is successful, the court will declare the legislation unconstitutional and Ontario Place Protectors will ask that work on the project not resume.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Francine Kopun Senior Writer With files from Robert Benzie

Subject: Architecture; Day spas; Redevelopment

Document 325 of 500

LCBO urges union to quickly return to the bargaining table: OPSEU members share frustrations over lack of movement in talks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-urges-union-quickly-return-bargaining-table/docview/3078936804/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+urges+union+to+quickly+return+to+the+bargaining+table%3A+OPSEU+members+share+frustrations+over+lack+of+movement+in+talks&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We need to talk.
That's the message from the LCBO to more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) members whose strike has shuttered 680 liquor stores for a week.
"We are at the table ready for active negotiations to restart today," the Liquor Control Board of Ontario said in a statement Thursday.
The LCBO's move came the day after Premier Doug Ford personally intervened in the labour dispute, imploring OPSEU to resume bargaining.
It also followed a heated virtual town hall meeting Wednesday night, where OPSEU members peppered union brass with questions about why they weren't negotiating right now.
"How come we don't have any bargaining meetings? They said they are willing to compromise on some stuff - some of us are eager to run our stores again," said an employee identified only as "Brian."
"I can speak for 90 per cent of our picket line who are pushing forward daily but growing frustrated at the lack of bargaining," said another unidentified union member.
"Some of the proposals put forward by the company were not terrible. Is it not possible to go back to the table and negotiate on what they have offered?" said LCBO employee "Jennifer."
The LCBO's statement Thursday, which castigated OPSEU for not continuing talks, noted it has "made a very fair offer that included wage increases, improved access to benefits, and provided for additional job security for workers."
"Instead of responding to this offer or sitting down to talk about it, OPSEU held a press conference to announce it was going on strike over the issue of ready-to-drink beverages," it continued, referring to premixed cocktails sold exclusively at the LCBO that will soon be available at licensed convenience stores, supermarkets and big-box outlets.
"Two days ago, the president of OPSEU appeared to indicate that ready-to-drink beverages was no longer the union's top issue, saying instead job security was its chief concern."
In an interview with the Star late Wednesday, OPSEU president JP Hornick said Ford "would be welcome to join us (at the negotiating table) when we get there.
"I'm even happier they're prepared to present an offer that provides job security for our members ... this fight has been about two things for the entire time: protecting jobs in our communities and ... protecting the public revenue," said Hornick.
But the union leader said it was "a lie" for Ford, who said he has spoken to "hundreds" of strikers over the past days, to claim OPSEU hadn't shared the LCBO's final offer with members.
"We never walked away from the table," said Hornick.
On Wednesday, Ford - whose government is spending between $225 million and $1 billion to expedite booze liberalization for beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails by Oct. 31 - urged the parties to restart talks.
"These folks don't want to be sitting out in the 100-degree weather or the pouring rain, they want to work, they want to get a deal," he said at Cool Beer Brewing Co. in Toronto.
"We should be at the table - that strike should never, ever have happened. We didn't walk away from the table, OPSEU walked away from the table," said Ford, pointing out the LCBO's most recent offer would give full-time jobs to 400 casual employees and give all workers seven per cent raises over three years.
However Colleen MacLeod, chair of the union's liquor board employees division and bargaining team, accused the premier of "lying about the LCBO's offer."
Ford has said while sales will be expanded to more than 8,000 other private retailers in the coming months, the LCBO will maintain a monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, gin and vodka, and serve as the wholesale distributor.
"We're not privatizing the LCBO, we aren't selling the LCBO - we're not putting spirits into the retail stores, the big box stores," the premier said.
The OPSEU members hit the bricks last Friday, closing the LCBO for the first time in its 97-year history.
Six weeks ago, Ford announced the scheme to pay The Beer Store $225 million to expedite the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025, deadline for expanded alcohol liberalization, which began under former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government a decade ago.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who backs the strikers, has warned Ford's plan could actually cost taxpayers $1 billion.
CAPTION: More than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union members went on strike last Friday, closing the LCBO for the first time in its 97-year history.Nick LachanceToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Full text: Canada
and NATO
Under pressure, Justin Trudeau announces when Canada expects to hit military spending commitments to NATO, July 11
There has been a lot of discussion lately about Canada's contribution to NATO, always quoting a percentage of GDP. But this is meaningless to the average person. Let's talk dollars. Canada has the seventh largest economy by GDP in the world, not including India and China. Canada made the seventh largest contribution in dollars to NATO in 2023 at more than $29.5 billion. Only the U.S., Germany, United Kingdom, France, Italy and Poland contributed more money than Canada. This was about $10 billion more than the next highest contribution. My point is that contributions from Canada, Germany, France and Italy, all less than two per cent, are already contributing substantial funds to NATO every year, far greater than almost any other country regardless of the percentage of their GDP. No one should think that Canada is not making a significant contribution to NATO.
Stephen Carson, Toronto
Seeing all the questioning of Canada's commitment to NATO makes one wonder what has happened to the grand ideas that fill so much of Canada's history. Take Nobel Peace Prize-winning Canadian Lester B. Pearson, with his brilliant idea of a United Nations peacekeeping force. It is past time we started thinking grand thoughts again. Canada must withdraw from NATO. This great land must become the peacekeeper to the world again by becoming the country that carries out all United Nations peacekeeping initiatives. Why? Because we are damned good at it and it will bring great pride to Canadians. A Canadian military comprising people from every country in this world will have an advantage that no one else can match. We can do this.
Roger Hilderley, Stratford, Ont.
So, the Business Council of Canada is concerned about "diplomatic isolation" should Canada not meet NATO's two per cent annual expenditure target. It's good to see that someone's finally thinking in terms of diplomacy. How can there be a minimum military spending threshold without there also being a base level investment in international relations? We live in a Manichaean world where the rhetoric of "we're good, they're evil" predominates, with little analysis of history or the causal factors that lead to conflict. Perhaps there would be less need to spend on artillery, tanks and drones if we were better equipped to partake in constructive dialogue with our so-called adversaries.
Joe Davidson, Toronto
LCBOs are
safer than
corner stores
LCBO urges OPSEU to return to the bargaining table ASAP, July 11
I was recently at an LCBO store where I witnessed the aftermath of a robbery. There were broken bottles on the floor as I entered. The presumed culprit was being escorted out of the store by two police officers and one security guard. He was not going quietly. There was quite a struggle to get him out and detained. Once gone, an LCBO employee asked, "How are Doug Ford's corner stores going to deal with situations like this?" Before booze hits convenience stores, what provisions will be in place to ensure the safety of Ontarians? Will there be a security guard in every corner store in the province? It seems that LCBOs are a popular target for thieves as it is. With more booze options at corner stores, won't Ford create a greater and easier target? What about jobs and revenue loses? If it works, don't fix it.
G. W. Ride, Toronto
I think that expanding alcohol sales is a terrible idea and will end very badly with more crime. I absolutely support the LCBO strike. They are right. There are many important issues to be tackled.
Anne Grguric, Grand Valley, Ont.
Issues with
the building?
Look around
Ford says second report confirms problems with Ontario Science Centre, July 10
Premier Doug Ford has declared the Ontario Science Centre "is absolutely just a total mess from top to bottom." Of course, this begs the question of which government allowed the building to deteriorate to such a degree. Since Ford has been in power for more than six years, he and his government clearly bear much of the responsibility for its neglect. Had he not been so focused on promoting beer sales in corner stores, unreadable licence plates and trying to cut into Ontario's Greenbelt, he might have spared a few dollars and some energy on maintaining this building. The Ontario Science Centre is an architectural jewel in a unique Toronto ravine setting, designed by internationally renowned architect Raymond Moriyama. Instead of celebrating its neglect, why is Ford not championing its heritage value? In fact, the larger question should be his ulterior plans for this stunning building. This building should have heritage status, and if not, why not?
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Restaurants should have
been prepared
Bars and restaurants already struggling to order favourites as LCBO strike continues,
July 10
Am I missing something? Everyone knew, weeks ago, that a strike was looming at LCBO. Surely any intelligent manager of a bar or restaurant would therefore stock up on sufficient alcohol products to last at least a month. It is inventory and does not go bad.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
The sad truth is that Munro was a flawed human
One imagines William Shakespeare could have written a good tragedy involving Alice Munro's family. The sad truth is that Munro was a great writer but a flawed human being. Perhaps there isn't much mystery in that.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
How could she? What a betrayal of the most fundamental duty of a parent to one's child. And, what's worse, "to position herself as the victim." I will never again pick up an Alice Munro book or stop myself from cringing when I hear her name. I think every library and bookstore should trash every single one of her books. God bless you, Andrea Skinner, and may you live a peaceful life.
Rita Maio, Woodbridge, Ont.
Life is fleeting
I'm deeply saddened by the loss of Benji Gregory, the talented actor who brought joy to many as Brian Tanner on the beloved sitcom "Alf." At just 46 years old, Gregory's passing is a tragic reminder of how fleeting life can be. My thoughts are with his family and friends.
Paul Bacon,
Hallandale Beach, Fla.

Document 327 of 500

Canada risks falling behind on renewable energy: Despite abundant hydro power, country lagging on new wind and solar construction, report says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-risks-falling-behind-on-renewable-energy/docview/3078935067/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+risks+falling+behind+on+renewable+energy%3A+Despite+abundant+hydro+power%2C+country+lagging+on+new+wind+and+solar+construction%2C+report+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thanks to abundant hydro power, Canada has the second highest share of renewable energy in the G20.
But according to a new report, our country risks squandering that advantage as other countries have embarked on a wind and solar building spree.
The moratorium on renewable energy in Alberta, and the priority given to nuclear in Ontario, have contributed to Canada dropping to 14th place in new renewable power construction, said the report, put out by British clean energy think tank Ember.
Worse than falling behind, Canada is moving in the wrong direction, the report found.
Unlike most G20 countries, which saw drops in power sector emissions, Canada's electricity emissions grew by two per cent from 2022 to 2023 - though the report chalked this up to "temporary conditions" like a dry summer that reduced the amount of hydro available and nuclear refurbishments, which contributed to an increase in natural gas and coal generation.
"Canada is very lucky to already have a substantial amount of our electricity be non-emitting through hydro and other resources," said Jason Wang, acting director of the electricity program at the Pembina Institute. "We are off to a great start, but we do need to fill those gaps."
"Many of Canada's provinces don't have a plan for meeting net zero electricity generation by 2035, which is the main milestone that can help the rest of the economy decarbonize," he added. "Renewables are the most cost-effective ways to decarbonize the grid."
Currently, Canada's electricity is 66 per cent renewable because it's generated by wind, solar and hydro. If you include nuclear, which doesn't produce carbon emissions but isn't considered renewable because it creates radioactive waste, Canada's power is 80 per cent "non-emitting."
But with only seven per cent of our electricity coming from wind and solar - the two technologies that have plunged in price and are being built rapidly around the world - Canada trails the global average of 13 per cent, and is lagging far behind some northern European countries like the U.K. (33 per cent), Germany (39 per cent) and the Netherlands (40 per cent). Even rainy Denmark was able to generate more than 90 per cent of its electricity from the wind and sun this May.
At last year's COP28 climate summit in Dubai, most of the world's governments pledged to triple renewable power by 2030. This near-term goal is widely considered a minimum first step necessary to tackle climate change by building a clean economy no longer reliant on fossil fuels.
Early results are encouraging. Renewables were built at the fastest rate ever in 2023, with a 14 per cent increase in global generation capacity, according to the International Renewable Energy Agency.
China is driving this clean energy revolution, leading the world in both manufacturing and installation of solar energy.
The Rocky Mountain Institute, a clean energy think tank based in Colorado, called China "the world's first electrostate."
"In 2023, China added more solar capacity in a single year than the United States has in 30 years," the group wrote in a recent report.
But Chinese growth is unique because it's fueled by massive government subsidies and a voracious appetite for energy in the world's second largest economy. A better role model for countries like Canada is Brazil, which also relies heavily on hydro. Like Canada, the South American country started building natural gas plants to supplement its network of dams. But unlike Canada, it recently pivoted to renewables and has been building solar and wind power at an aggressive pace.
"Brazil is a renewables superpower," said Ember analyst Kostantsa Rangelova. "Brazil had a head start in the renewables race thanks to its strong hydro base, but it has continued to sprint to the finish line with wind and solar. The rest of the G20 can follow Brazil's successful model and lead the global transition to a sustainable energy future."
While power generation will always rely heavily on government policy, the economics of wind and solar give renewables a huge advantage over other sources of energy, leading many experts to conclude their dominance is inevitable.
In a recent editorial, the Economist magazine called the exponential growth of solar so rapid "it makes it hard for people to get their heads round what is going on."
Installed solar capacity doubles about every three years, the magazine wrote, which has set it on a trajectory of a tenfold increase each decade.
"When it was a tenth of its current size ten years ago, solar power was still seen as marginal even by experts who knew how fast it had grown. The next ten-fold increase will be equivalent to multiplying the world's entire fleet of nuclear reactors by eight in less than the time it typically takes to build just a single one of them," the editorial said.
"Solar cells will in all likelihood be the single biggest source of electrical power on the planet by the mid-2030s. By the 2040s they may be the largest source not just of electricity but of all energy ... This will not stop climate change, but could slow it a lot faster."
Even in the United States, where clean energy has become a political football, economics has driven a construction spree, and wind power surpassed coal generation for the first time in April.
Last month, Alberta phased out coal, more than five years ahead of its scheduled 2030 retirement. Only 10 years ago, coal produced 60 per cent of the province's electricity. Alberta now leads the nation in wind and solar construction.
Ontario's electricity situation, by contrast, shows how a head start can lead to complacency. Nuclear (53 per cent) and hydro (25 per cent) provide most of the province's power without producing emissions. Between 2009 and 2018, Ontario built more wind and solar than any other province. When Premier Doug Ford was elected in 2018, he spent $231 million to cancel 800 renewable energy projects already under contract. Ontario hasn't built any wind or solar since.
Last year, Queen's Park embarked on an electricity construction blitz, announcing the first new nuclear reactors in more than 30 years and new gas plants to meet short-term needs.
In December, Ford launched a round of procurement of renewable energy.
The federal government is currently in the process of finalizing its Clean Electricity Regulations, which would require most electricity to be emissions-free by 2035.
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Think tanks; Nuclear reactors; Wind power; Electricity; Emissions; Clean technology; Renewable resources; Alternative energy sources; Climate change; Coal; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Renewable resources

Location: United States--US; Canada; China; Brazil

Company / organization: Name: Group of Twenty; NAICS: 926110

Title: Canada risks falling behind on renewable energy:   Despite abundant hydro power, country lagging on new wind and solar construction, report says

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jul 12, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Language of publication: English

There's a building bonanza in renewable power. But Canada is missing out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-building-bonanza-renewable-power-canada-is/docview/3079422546/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 329 of 500

Work at Ontario Place is stopped pending a court hearing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/work-at-ontario-place-is-stopped-pending-court/docview/3078935128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Work+at+Ontario+Place+is+stopped+pending+a+court+hearing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Work on redeveloping Ontario Place into a spa and waterpark has been paused until July 19, after a group hoping to preserve the site won a legal hearing to challenge the project on constitutional grounds.
Ontario Place Protectors is seeking a declaration that the Rebuilding Ontario Place Act - passed by the Doug Ford government in an effort to expedite the redevelopment - is unconstitutional, breaching the doctrine of public trust.
"Ontario Place doesn't belong to the current government, it belongs to the people of Ontario and future generations," said Eric Gillespie, the lawyer representing Ontario Place Protectors, a broad coalition of residents and organizations, including numerous architectural interests.
"The government appears to be breaching the trust that is owed to the people of Ontario," Gillespie said.
The application, which will be heard in Ontario Superior Court, includes affidavits from organizations including the Architectural Conservancy of Ontario and the Toronto Society of Architects, among others.
The Austria-based company Therme is building a $350-million water park and spa on the West Island at Ontario Place. Work began June 23.
Ontario Place Protectors said the Ontario government agreed "not to cause or permit any permanent destruction of any trees, shrubs or buildings at Ontario Place," until after the one-day hearing is held.
But the news seemed to take provincial officials by surprise.
At Queen's Park, Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma told reporters she could not even say whether the legal action had temporarily stopped work at Queen's Park.
"There is an action in place before the courts and so as you know I cannot comment," said Surma. "The hearing is taking place though on July 19. We look forward to July 19."
Asked if a loss in court could "endanger the entire redevelopment of Ontario Place," the minister would only say: "I cannot speculate."
The government was not able to provide further comment in time for this story's deadline.
Gillespie said Ontario Place Protectors has been working closely with Ontario Place for All, which lost a legal challenge at Ontario Divisional Court in June, requesting that an environmental assessment of the site's West Island, where the spa is being built, be conducted.
Gillespie said the new law will also be challenged on the grounds that it improperly erodes judicial authority by preventing all remedies under all statutes when it comes to Ontario Place.
"That appears to take away powers that are central to judges' abilities to operate our court system," Gillespie said.
Gillespie said that if the application is successful, the court will declare the legislation unconstitutional and Ontario Place Protectors will ask that work on the project not resume.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: A woman runs towards Trillium Park past an entrance to the Ontario Place construction zone last month.

Document 330 of 500

Doug Ford's critics give him too much credit for privatization

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-critics-give-him-too-much-credit/docview/3078680732/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+critics+give+him+too+much+credit+for+privatization&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After six years of Doug Ford in power, here are six privatized provincial things that will never be the same:
ServiceOntario outlets are moving into big chain stores.The LCBO is under pressure from private beer and wine outlets.Private health-care clinics are thriving.The old Ontario Place is never coming back and the Ontario Science Centre can't be saved.Private construction firms have turned public infrastructure into big business.Ontario Hydro isn't the public utility it once was.
Six sins committed by the premier. How did he get away with remaking Ontario in his own right-wing image?
Answer: He didn't - he's just doing more or less what previous premiers did before him.
That's the paradox of politics in this province. While the six changes listed above largely predate Ford's turn in power, most people and pundits talk as if he has orchestrated a revolutionary U-turn in Ontario's political culture and economic outlook.
Thanks to his pugnacious persona - brash and in your face, populist and provocative - people assume the worst and blame him for all of it. If you listen to opposition and union leaders, you'd think that Ford's agenda of privatization is unprecedented in the province's history.
In fact, the premier's less bombastic Liberal predecessors - Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne - got the ball rolling and the beer flowing in many of these areas. That's politics - and that's perception.
To be sure, much of the premier's agenda is controversial. To be clear, many of the things he says are nonsensical - from liberating the Greenbelt to axing the carbon levy.
But in 2024, it's surely time for an honest conversation about privatization - where it helps, where it hurts. The least we could do is have an informed debate, rather than misinformed diatribes and misleading dogma.
The biggest and boldest liberation of beer and wine in Ontario's history came under Wynne's Liberal government, when she phased in supermarket sales that cut the (privately held) Beer Store down to size. Ford's incessant boasts about bringing beer to corner stores are a pale echo of Wynne's willingness, a decade ago, to stare down the big breweries (plus their union allies and the NDP) by easing the stranglehold of The Beer Store's unholy duopoly with the LCBO.
On the picket line, OPSEU is framing its strike as pushback against privatization. But Ford's Tories aren't about to wind down the LCBO behemoth, with some 680 stores; just look at how Quebec's publicly owned SAQ coexists with private distribution in corner stores.
When Wynne's Liberals attempted the partial privatization of our Hydro One transmission utility - which deals mostly in boring fixed assets such as wires and poles - while still safeguarding the government's control as the largest single shareholder, New Democrats rallied public opinion against her by conflating it with dismantling the old Ontario Hydro. Never mind that Ontario Hydro was carved up decades ago.
Equally with Ontario Place, people are blaming Ford for finishing it off as a public enterprise. Yet it was McGuinty's Liberals who shuttered the site more than a decade ago because attendance and revenues had dropped to unjustifiable levels.
Ever wonder why the water slides of Ontario Place lost ballast? They couldn't compete with the wild roller-coaster rides of Canada's Wonderland - a private entity that invested big money in supersized attractions.
Belatedly, Ford has embraced a long-standing recommendation from public servants to relocate the outdated Ontario Science Centre to a reborn Ontario Place, alongside a water park rebranded as a Therme "spa." Predictably, that has raised the hackles of the hair-shirt brigade, but what business does government have in the waterpark business?
It's a similar story of perception versus reality - and history - with ServiceOntario, which helps people renew driver's licences and OHIP cards. When the media reacted with astonishment that some outlets would be relocated to Staples stores, I happened to be shopping at my local Canadian Tire location where - shock - a ServiceOntario branch, already privately owned, has been located for years. Same with public-private partnerships for building new hospitals, which the Liberals long relied on.
None of this is to argue that privatization and devolution are good or bad on ideological grounds. The point is to consider it on efficiency and equity grounds.
The debate gets more complicated with private health-care clinics. Opposition politicians and health-care unions love to scaremonger about people being asked for credit cards instead of OHIP cards, but private delivery of publicly financed health care (by self-employed doctors in stand-alone clinics or labs) is as old as medicare itself.
It matters little, from a public policy perspective, whether you buy stamps or renew a driver's licence from a government building, a Staples or a Canadian Tire. It matters a great deal whether people are getting health care from the right provider without being out of pocket.
But after all these years (not just the last six under Ford), isn't it time for an intellectually honest conversation about the perils of privatization, versus the exaggeration about experimentation?
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Privatization; Strikes; Health care; Politics; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Strikes Breweries

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Title: Doug Ford's critics give him too much credit for privatization

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jul 11, 2024

Section: politics

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3078680732

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-for ds-critics-give-him-too-much-credit/docview/3078680732/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 331 of 500

Inside the chaos at Pickering city council + a second Ontario Science Centre report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/inside-chaos-at-pickering-city-council-second/docview/3078680715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inside+the+chaos+at+Pickering+city+council+%2B+a+second+Ontario+Science+Centre+report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, July 11 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a city council under siege by alt-right activists, Doug Ford previewing a second Science Centre report and a troubling trend in the condo market.
DON'T MISSWhen alt-right activists targeted Pickering, the city was unprepared for the chaos that ensued
The "Freedom Convoy" of 2022 was just a proof of concept for what has become a contentious battle in city councils, school boards and public libraries across Canada. Local institutions have become battlegrounds for alt-right activists villainizing school-based sex education, equity initiatives, 15-minute cities and other policies. It's been an ongoing problem in Pickering, where city councillor Lisa Robinson has opposed Pride events, denounced Black History Month and repeatedly sidelined council business. Her defenders, meanwhile, have suggested the mayor should be publicly hanged and two councillors say they've been followed home. Rachel Mendleson and R.J. Johnston paint the picture of the growing alt-right movements in local communities.
Context: In April, the Pickering council sought help from the Ontario Human Rights Council, asking the provincial body for tools to address Robinson's conduct after she denounced Black History Month.Word from a Pickering councillor: "The democratic process I believe in ... is being hijacked," said Dave Pickles, who has been a councillor for more than two decades.From the mayor: "We're trying to run a city," Pickering Mayor Kevin Ashe told the Star. "We really don't have the time, energy or resources to deal with conspiracy theorists. And I think that's what this is."Doug Ford says a second report confirms problems with Ontario Science Centre
A second report on the now-closed Ontario Science Centre shows just how unsafe the building is, Premier Doug Ford said at a press conference Wednesday. "It just doesn't make sense to throw good money into an old decrepit building," he said, adding that "you'll fall off your chair" after seeing the contents of the report. The premier is sticking by his sudden shuttering of the Science Centre last month, citing a crumbling roof with aerated concrete that posed a risk to visitors. Opponents have argued that repairing the roof would not be as costly as the Ford government has claimed. Here's what else Ford is saying about the Ontario Science Centre.
Look out for: The second engineering report is set to be released today, with Ford foreshadowing that it's "not just the roof" that prompted the decision to shut the Science Centre down.ICYMI: The Ontario Science Centre's closure didn't come as a surprise to many who had worked there before - the museum had been underfunded for years, lacking money for repairs and new exhibits.Edward Keenan's take: The Ford government wasn't forced to close the iconic Toronto museum, it chose to. The closure sets up the museum's move to Ontario Place in 2028, albeit in a smaller location. Meanwhile, the same provincial government has shied away from spending on booze in corner stores or the Ontario Line (which includes a Science Centre stop).Toronto condo sales are tanking, but asking prices aren't budging - here's why
If you're looking to buy a condo in Toronto, news that condo sales are on the decline might sound like a good thing. Think again. Prices have barely budged, thanks to sellers that are over-leveraged and unwilling to take the financial hit of listing low. That just feeds into a vicious loop in Toronto's downtown, where small, investor-owned units aren't being rented out, nor are they appealing to buyers. So, how does the condo market move on from this stalemate? Clarrie Feinstein explains why condo prices have remained stubbornly high.
By the numbers: Condo sales fell by an enormous 29 per cent year-over-year in June, while prices only fell by 0.9 per cent, according to the Toronto Regional Real Estate Board.Timing is everything: "If you haven't sold in the first 35 to 40 days, the market is telling you they don't agree with where you've priced your property," one realty sales representative said.WHAT ELSE

Candles laid out, a eulogy and a moment of silence show just how much the city's history is intertwined with the adorable, if not frustrating, backyard trash pandas. Here's what Conrad's memorial says about Toronto's love-hate relationship with the critters that roam our alleyways.

Document 332 of 500

'If the apples can get sunburn, what about us?' Migrant farm workers demand protection from dangerous heat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-apples-can-get-sunburn-what-about-us-migrant/docview/3078680713/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27If+the+apples+can+get+sunburn%2C+what+about+us%3F%27+Migrant+farm+workers+demand+protection+from+dangerous+heat&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He has harvested tons of fruits and vegetables in Ontario farms over the years - apples, carrots, tomatoes and many others that would end up on Canadians' dining table.
The migrant farm worker from Trinidad and Tobago is proud of what he does because the job allows him to provide for his family back home. That's why he's been returning here annually for the last 12 years.
However, amid the recent brutal heat dome, he and other workers, struggling in temperatures between 38 and 45 C, were upset when their boss made them stay out to remove all the bins of apples from the fields so the fruit wouldn't get sunburn.
"If the apples can get sunburn, what about us who are there working in bare heat with no shelter?" questioned a worker at the farm in Haldimand Norfolk in southwestern Ontario. The Star is not naming him for fear of work reprisals.
But he said no one dared to disobey the order or raise a stink for fear that they would not be called back to work in Canada next year.
Advocates are familiar with these complaints from migrant farm workers and have demanded the Ontario government put in heat protection regulations for the tens of thousands of agricultural workers employed in the province.
They were hopeful that changes would come soon when they were told last year that the province was committed to enacting such rules to better protect farm workers, whom the labour minister at the time, Monte McNaughton, called "heroes."
But since then, there has been radio silence, said Taneeta Doma, staff lawyer at Windsor's Migrant Farmworkers Clinic, which, along with Justicia for Migrant Workers, wrote an open letter to the province this week to reiterate the demand.
"Even when folks are fainting or having other types of reactions to the heat, they're not being given more breaks to just chill," said Doma. "There's nowhere to even go into the shade if you're in the fields. If they're trying to stay hydrated, they don't have proper access to washrooms.
"That's more than exploitation and degradation. They're not being treated like human beings."
Citing an American research study on occupational heat-related mortality, Doma said agriculture had more than 35 times the risk of heat-related death compared to most industries, followed by construction, which had 13 times the risk. The two sectors that accounted for 58 per cent of all cases.
On Wednesday, talking to reporters, Premier Doug Ford said employers are required to ensure they have proper facilities to keep workers cool but "that's going to be up to the employer and the employees."
In a statement to the Star, the Ministry of Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development said the province takes worker safety very seriously and all workers have a right to be safe in their workplaces from any potential hazards including heat stress.
"Under the Occupational Health and Safety Act, employers and supervisors have a duty to take every precaution reasonable in the circumstances for the protection of a worker, including protection in hot environments," the ministry said in an email.
"Specific to heat stress, the employer should establish a heat stress control plan for the summer to manage job tasks in high temperatures and humidity."
Doma said it's true that workers can complain to the labour ministry if their employers are being not reasonable or accommodating, but it doesn't work in reality because of the power employers hold over the workers, especially against migrants with precarious status in Canada.
"We're calling for specific regulations with respect to heat and with respect to the cold and air quality," she said. "It needs to be spelled out for these employers what exactly they need to be doing, because putting it in employers' hands is not going to work."
She said officials must legislate the trigger temperatures, proper breaks, hours of work, as well as access to shade and water - and invest in enforcing the rules.
Currently, both British Columbia and Quebec in their occupational and safety laws stipulate heat stress assessment and exposure controls, and so do some U.S. states such as Washington, California, Minnesota, Wisconsin and Colorado. This month, President Joe Biden's administration just proposed new rules to protect workers exposed to extreme heat.
The farm worker from Trinidad and Tobago hopes Ontario will follow suit.
"Enough isn't being done for us," he said. "A lot of workers that are on farms work in conditions that a normal Canadian citizen will never do."
CAPTION: Migrant farm workers pick apples in Ontario's Norfolk County.Taneeta Doma, staff lawyer at the Migrant Farmworkers Clinic in Windsor.

Subject: Migrant workers; Heat; Employers; Farmworkers; Farms; Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

Business indexing term: Subject: Migrant workers Employers Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

Document 333 of 500

Failing infrastructure, damaged roof: Here's what a second report on the Ontario Science Centre found

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/failing-infrastructure-damaged-roof-heres-what/docview/3078680710/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Failing+infrastructure%2C+damaged+roof%3A+Here%27s+what+a+second+report+on+the+Ontario+Science+Centre+found&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shuttering the "beloved" Ontario Science Centre has been "emotional" - but leaders believe closing the museum was the best option because it is no longer safe due to "failing infrastructure, including critical roof issues, building systems and an inaccessible pedestrian bridge."
The centre's top brass delivered that verdict Thursday as the province released a second engineering report on the state of the facility, echoing concerns from an initial review that detailed the building's crumbling roof, among other urgently needed repairs.
But critics were quick to point out that the second report, while confirming serious issues in some areas, also did not call for the immediate shutdown that happened three weeks ago.
"It is unfortunate that we are in this circumstance - it is terrible," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said of the closure and "decommissioning" of the three science centre buildings. "None of us wanted to make a decision like this."
Given the risks cited in the reports, the estimated $500-million cost and length of time to do repairs - when the centre is already moving to Ontario Place in 2028 - it was the only choice for Premier Doug Ford's government, she said.
However, Jason Ash, co-chair of Save Ontario's Science Centre, took issue with that, as neither report saw the need to "require or recommend the immediate and permanent closure" of the facility.
"Independent experts continue to publicly state the building is safe to reopen, and that the repairs it needs are normal and expected for a 55-year-old building," said Ash, maintaining the science centre requested funding for a number of critical projects in recent years and had been denied.
"Now, Infrastructure Ontario wants to abandon the building," he said. "Make no mistake, for whatever reason, Premier Ford wants the Ontario Science Centre demolished."
In a letter released with the report, board chair John Carmichael and CEO Paul Kortenaar said "we have been incredibly touched by the outpouring of love and support that we have received for the Ontario Science Centre since we closed our doors at 770 Don Mills Road on June 21, 2024 - much sooner than we anticipated.
"It has been an emotional time for all of us," they wrote, adding "the health and safety of our employees and visitors is always our top priority."
Surma said Thursday that atop the annual $25 million in provincial funding, Queen's Park spent $7 million for "emergency repairs" last year at the museum, which sits on land leased from the city of Toronto.
The original engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group said that while the science centre building is safe for now, rain or snow buildup could hasten damage to the roof and there is a risk of collapse in some areas that were constructed with reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete (RAAC). That prompted a decision to close down the site, so that staff would have time to remove its exhibitions by the end of October.
Making the Don Mills facility viable "would continue to require the immediate closure of the building and an immediate investment of approximately $70 million in urgent capital repair and an additional $20-40 million for immediate roof repairs," the government said.
A peer review by VanBoxmeer & Stranges Engineering Ltd. (VB&S) said while some formatting of graphs and tables in the original Rimkus report was "confusing," the overall findings were sound.
"Based on our review of the documents, and our random sampling visual review of the condition of the RAAC roof panel structures, we find that evaluation of the risks identified by Rimkus are reasonable," said the VB&S review, which was released Thursday.
"Furthermore, the recommendations made to mitigate these risks through immediate remediation of critical and high-risk panels and their eventual replacement - to coincide with a roofing assembly replacement - are appropriate to safely extend the useful lifespan of the building structure," the firm said.
"We conclude that it is a concise and comprehensive narrative that adequately addresses the risks associated with RAAC elements in existing buildings."
The government warns the crumbling aerated concrete roof is only part of the problem. It says there is also the "risk of disrupting asbestos, which must be contained," as well as other concerns with the centre's Great Hall and auditorium that have not been previously addressed.
In a presentation by the Ministry of Infrastructure, photos taken Monday showed deteriorating concrete, boiler insulation failure, and problems that have left one building without any working heat or cooling system.
Ford has called it "an old, decrepit building," insisting "it just doesn't make sense to throw good money" in since it's moving to the waterfront in four years.
Since its shutdown, officials have been scrambling to find a temporary home for the science exhibits to open by January 2026.
Some 366 of Ontario's 4,500 schools contain some aerated concrete, but boards have been monitoring their condition since last fall when the Ministry of Education asked that they all be inspected in light of a decision in the United Kingdom to shut down more than 100 schools last fall due to risk of roof collapse.
Infrastructure officials said the science centre's problem are more grave than any Ontario school because boards of education have been monitoring and repairing them in recent years.
Critics argue it would cost much less to replace the aerated concrete in the existing science centre than to move it to the waterfront, and that repairs could be done in phases while the building remained open to the public.
"The government has a responsibility to protect public safety," Surma said, noting that "is one of the reasons why the government made the decision to build a brand new science centre, for the people of Ontario, so that they can have a science centre for the next 50 years."
Carmichael said it wasn't just the roof, but "a multiplicity of issues" that led to the board voting unanimously for the abrupt shut down on June 21.
"We had certainly hoped that this building would be sustainable to get us to a new building," said the chair, who spoke fondly of taking his children and grandchildren to the science centre.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford "chose to let it fall into disrepair."
Local Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said both engineering reports clearly state the building can be salvaged.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said while "there is no doubt that the current science centre needs significant repairs - the only roadblock is that the premier does not see the value of the site."
It remains unclear what city hall's plans are for the sprawling property.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed last month after a report showed some concrete roofing panels were deteriorating and needed to be replaced.

Subject: Engineering; Science; School closures; Aerated concrete
