Document 345 of 500

Ford's push to privatize booze will be costly

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-push-privatize-booze-will-be-costly/docview/3078297824/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+push+to+privatize+booze+will+be+costly&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are a lot of things that can be hard to get in Ontario - a job, a home, an ambulance when you need it. But one thing that's rarely difficult to get in Ontario is a drink.
Given how easy it's been to purchase alcohol in the province, it's odd and a little infuriating that Premier Doug Ford is putting great effort - and considerable taxpayer money - into making alcohol even more widely available.
Meanwhile, when it comes to things that actually do matter in our lives - like access to excellent health care and education - the premier is always scrimping, seriously underfunding these vital public services.
The Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO), a crown corporation, has been doing a fine job selling alcohol - not exactly a risky enterprise requiring a lot of innovation - through its 677 outlets across the province.
And since it is publicly owned, its healthy annual profit - $2.5 billion in 2023 - goes into the public treasury, where it pays for things like health care and education.
Ontarians have long seemed satisfied with this reasonable arrangement.
But business interests and the pro-business media have long been opposed. In an editorial this week, The Globe and Mail objected to the very existence of the LCBO, insisting that governments should raise revenue through taxes, not through competing with the private sector.
Yet the Globe is quick to denounce any tax increase (certainly any tax increase that impacts corporations or rich people). Indeed, given the business community's hostility to taxes, it would be quite a challenge to raise taxes enough to replace the $2.5 billion in revenue the government receives each year from the LCBO.
Furthermore, it's doubtful that Ontarians would want to pay higher taxes so that more profits from alcohol sales could go to highly profitable grocery store chains.
While the government isn't shutting down the LCBO (at least not yet), its plan to move alcohol sales increasingly to private retail stores will be costly for Ontario taxpayers.
To begin with, Ford recklessly threw away $225 million (to cancel the province's contract with the Beer Store a year early, in order to begin its increased privatization plan this summer).
Once all the LCBO's lost revenue is factored in, the full cost to the public treasury of this privatization will likely be higher - possibly close to a billion dollars.
This needless waste of public money seems to be driven, at least in part, by Ford's hostility to public sector unions. Certainly, selling ever more alcohol in retail stores poses a threat to the relatively well-paying unionized jobs at the LCBO, which is why LCBO workers are on strike for the first time.
Ford's keenness to undermine public sector unions makes a mockery of his claim to be concerned about the affordability crisis facing Ontarians. Nothing contributes more to the affordability crisis than low-paying jobs.
If he were truly interested in ensuring Ontarians can afford a decent living standard, he'd support unionized jobs, not try to replace them with low-paying jobs in convenience and grocery stores.
Like so much this premier does, the basic animating force appears to be a zealous desire to privatize, to hand over ever more of our province to private interests, to further cannibalize Ontario's strong tradition of public services and public enterprises that have served the province well.
Ford is following the path of former Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris, whose needless privatizations produced some disasters for Ontario.
Harris's privatization of Highway 407 has cost us billions, his water-testing privatization was a factor in seven water-contamination deaths in Walkerton, and his privatization of long-term-care homes worsened the COVID crisis, with death rates four times higher in private homes than in public ones.
I'd gladly forego the joy of being able to pick up a ready-made cocktail at Loblaws so that hundreds of millions of dollars could be redirected to our ever-closing emergency wards.
Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist
and a freelance contributing columnist for The Star.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's keenness to undermine public sector unions makes a mockery of his claim to be concerned about the affordability crisis facing Ontarians, writes Linda McQuaig. Nothing contributes more to this crisis than low-paying jobs.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star
CREDIT: Linda McQuaig OPINION Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelance contributing columnist for The Star.;

Document 346 of 500

'You'll fall off your chair': Doug Ford says second report confirms problems with Ontario Science Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/youll-fall-off-your-chair-doug-ford-says-second/docview/3078771189/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27You%27ll+fall+off+your+chair%27%3A+Doug+Ford+says+second+report+confirms+problems+with+Ontario+Science+Centre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says "you'll fall off your chair" at the contents of a second engineering report to be released Friday that outlines a number of concerns about the Ontario Science Centre.
"At the end of the day, this is not a safe building. It's my responsibility as premier to make sure people are safe," Ford said at an unrelated announcement in Etobicoke on Wednesday morning.
"And when I have two engineering reports back to back - and I was briefed for over an hour, just about every issue you can possibly think of - it just doesn't make sense to throw good money into an old decrepit building."
While he recognizes people are passionate and have fond memories of visiting the facility, "once the report comes out (Friday) ... you'll fall off your chair when you see all the issues we have down there - not just the roof," Ford said.
On June 21, the Ministry of Infrastructure announced the board of the science centre had voted to immediately close the facility on Don Mills Road in light of an engineering report that detailed concerns about reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete, known as RAAC, used in the roof that could lead to a collapse.
The science centre, which opened in 1969, is already set to be moved to the Ontario Place waterfront redevelopment site - where it could reopen by 2028 - but the abrupt shutdown has left officials scrambling to find an interim location they hope to open by January 2026.
Private offers to cover the cost of the repairs have been made. Opposition critics at Queen's Park have said it would cost much less to replace the aerated concrete in the existing building than moving the centre to the waterfront, and it could be done in phases while remaining open to the public.
Even an architect from the firm that designed the building has said it could safely operate while repairs are made, given that almost all of the exhibition space is unaffected by structural concerns.
Jason Ash, co-chair of Save Ontario's Science Centre, has lamented that "there will be nothing for anybody to visit for 18 months ... they closed it without having anything prepared. It just speaks to how they seem to operate in general."
The interim site will be a scaled-back version, with a request for proposals seeking a space of 50,000 to 100,000 square feet - about a fifth to one-tenth the size of the Don Mills facility.
The initial engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group cited aerated concrete used in some parts of the roof as a concern.
In the United Kingdom, more than 100 schools were closed last fall just before the start of classes due to the risk of sudden collapse in buildings made of the same material.
Some 366 of Ontario's 4,500 schools contain some aerated concrete, but boards have been monitoring their condition since last fall, when the Ministry of Education asked that they all be inspected in light of what was happening in the United Kingdom.
Aerated concrete that was used to build floors, walls, eaves and roofs from the 1960s into the 1980s is now believed to have a lifespan of about 30 years. The Education Ministry told school boards that given pre-1980 aerated concrete parts are "now past their expected service life ... it is recommended that consideration is given to their replacement."
Board officials have said they've repaired or are closely monitoring any areas of concerns in their schools.
"We know that there are schools built during that time period," said Cathy Abraham, immediate past president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association. "And if they found something, action would have been taken, because keeping our schools as safe facilities" is the priority.
The Rimkus report said while the science centre building is safe for now, rain or snow buildup could hasten roof damage, so the decision was made to close down the Don Mills site to give staff time to remove exhibitions by the end of October.
Ford said Wednesday that the science centre "wasn't made to last 55 years."
He noted that summer camps had been moved to nearby schools.
"I'm not going to put those kids at risk. God forbid there's a heavy rainfall," he said.
"I'm not chancing it," he added. "I'm just not going to chance kids or people to be in there ... that place is absolutely just a total mess from top to bottom, the front, the back, to every single building."
Ford noted the province has no plans to build on the property, which is owned by the City of Toronto. "Anything they decide to do - outside of building another science centre - we'll be there to support them," he said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to media at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.The Ontario Science Centre closed last month after a report showed some concrete roofing panels were deteriorating and needed to be replaced.

Document 347 of 500

Doug Ford slams union over LCBO strike, says workers 'want a deal'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-slams-union-over-lcbo-strike-says/docview/3077975276/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+slams+union+over+LCBO+strike%2C+says+workers+%27want+a+deal%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is going on the offensive as Ontario has been plunged into the first LCBO strike in history.
Injecting himself into the labour dispute with the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), Ford said he had spoken to "hundreds" of LCBO workers since the job action began last Friday.
"These folks don't want to be sitting out in the 100-degree weather or the pouring rain, they want to work, they want to get a deal," the premier told reporters at Etobicoke's Cool Beer Brewing Co. on Evans Avenue.
"We should be at the table - that strike should never, ever have happened. We didn't walk away from the table, OPSEU walked away from the table," he said, noting the LCBO's last offer would give full-time jobs to 400 casual employees and give all workers seven per cent raises over three years.
But Colleen MacLeod, chair of the union's liquor board employees division and bargaining team, said "Ford is lying about the LCBO's offer."
"We didn't walk away from the table - the LCBO told us not to come back unless we were willing to give up on our core demands that included job security and growing the LCBO to meet demand and improve convenience," said MacLeod.
"We would rather be back at work, or at least back at the bargaining table, working to find a deal. That's why we were thrilled to hear that Doug Ford wants us back at the table with the LCBO - and that they are prepared to present an offer that provides job security for our members. Doug Ford is welcome to join those talks."
OPSEU president JP Hornick said the strike is an existential battle against the Progressive Conservatives' "privatization" agenda.
Hornick warned expanding the sale of ready-to-drink beverages, such as Mike's Hard Lemonade and White Claw, to convenience stores, big box outlets and supermarkets as part of the Tories' liberalization of booze sales would undermine the future of the LCBO.
Ford countered that "if they want to negotiate over (ready-to-drink beverages), the deal's off ... that ship has sailed - it's halfway across Lake Ontario."
He reiterated the LCBO, which will continue to sell the premixed cocktails and seltzers, would also maintain its long-held monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, gin and vodka.
"We're not privatizing the LCBO, we aren't selling the LCBO - we're not putting spirits into the retail stores, the big box stores," said Ford.
He and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy held their press conference Wednesday morning in Etobicoke against the backdrop of a six-day strike that has closed 680 Liquor Control Board of Ontario stores.
With restaurateurs and bar owners concerned about dwindling supplies, Ford scrambled to remind Ontarians there are still thousands of places open to buy alcohol.
"We ... gave them the opportunity to sell directly to the public, which has been very beneficial to the bars and restaurant - I want to thank them for their patience and we will get through this," he said, stressing "you can order online anywhere in Ontario by the way" at lcbo.com.
More than 9,000 OPSEU members hit the bricks Friday, closing the LCBO for the first time in its 97-year existence.
In a letter to LCBO chair Carmine Nigro on Tuesday, Bethlenfalvy emphasized "while some take a more short-sighted view that opposes change, as a best-in-class wholesaler and retailer of alcohol, LCBO will continue to make meaningful financial contributions to provincial coffers."
The PC government is spending anywhere between $225 million and $1 billion to expedite liberalized alcohol sales by Oct. 31.
Six weeks ago, the premier announced taxpayers would give the foreign-owned Beer Store $225 million to move up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline to allow the sale of beer, wine and premixed cocktails in variety stores, big box outlets and additional supermarkets.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who rallied with Hornick and OPSEU strikers at the LCBO's Queens Quay store Tuesday, insisted the price tag could skyrocket to $1 billion.
Ford launched an interactive online map on Monday to steer consumers to the hundreds of breweries, wineries, wine shops and distilleries, 449 licensed supermarkets, 422 Beer Store outlets and 389 rural LCBO agency stores that remain open.
Under his changes, the LCBO would be the wholesale distributor for all retailers.
But distribution problems due to the strike have businesses worried they won't be able to get products for their restaurants and bars.
Fearful of pickets, the LCBO scrapped a proposal to open five stores as of Wednesday to supply restaurateurs and publicans.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford moves a flat of beer cans during a news conference at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.Premier Doug Ford shakes hands with Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy at an announcement in May.Premier Doug Ford speaks to media at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto, July 10, 2024.

Document 348 of 500

Critics warned that Olivia Chow would be an 'unmitigated disaster' as mayor. Here's how her first year in power went

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/critics-warned-that-olivia-chow-would-be/docview/3077975267/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Critics+warned+that+Olivia+Chow+would+be+an+%27unmitigated+disaster%27+as+mayor.+Here%27s+how+her+first+year+in+power+went&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Olivia Chow wanted a reminder of how much needs fixing at city hall, she got it last month.
During a late spring storm, rain started streaming through the roof outside the mayor's office, forcing staff to cordon off the hallway.
"Oh, literally buckets of water coming down," Chow said. "I thought, 'oh my God.'"
Although it sounds like a too-good-to-be-true metaphor, Chow, who on Friday will mark one year in office, has spent the past 12 months trying to patch up municipal government. While the scale of the problems she inherited were well known - a $1.8-billion budget shortfall and a crushing housing crisis among them - Chow has defied expectations.
Rather than the left-wing ideologue her election opponents tried to paint her as, the veteran NDPer has shown herself a practical leader, finding common ground with politicians of all stripes, and mostly delivering incremental, rather than radical change.
The approach has won her praise from some who don't share her politics, and allowed her to push policy through a council whose members are mostly to the right of her. But at times it has also alienated her progressive supporters.
And for all the credit she's received for her handling of major files, Chow has sometimes been tripped up by smaller issues, which to critics is evidence her office doesn't have a firm handle on council business.
Chow insists she and her team remain focused on her progressive vision, and are committed to creating a "more caring, affordable and safer city."
"I don't think I'm tacking (to the centre)," she told the Star during a wide-ranging interview about her year in power, pointing to her record of freezing TTC fares, and boosting funding to prevent evictions. "That's pretty progressive."
The Star spoke to Chow, council members and experts about her first year. Here are the highs, lows and in-betweens.
Doing a deal
In November, Chow and Premier Doug Ford shocked longtime political observers by announcing the province had agreed to take ownership of the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway as part of a "new deal" between Toronto and Queen's Park.
The accord is expected to free up as much as $7.6 billion in capital spending for the city through the highway upload and provincial commitments for new subway cars and housing funds, while Toronto is also to receive $1.2 billion from Queen's Park for transit and shelter operations over three years.
"I don't know if it's her greatest achievement, but I think it's the one that has taken the most people by surprise," said Alejandra Bravo (Ward 9, Davenport), one of Chow's closest allies. She said the "massive" savings have given the city financial breathing room and "underwritten" all of Chow's other policies.
The deal also showed Chow could shelve political differences and work with a premier who had warned voters she would be "an unmitigated disaster."
The highway upload was a "huge, huge achievement," said Amanda Galbraith, former mayor John Tory's communications director. Galbraith said that Tory got the ball rolling on the highway decision, but Chow took it over the line. "I give her really high marks for that," she said.
As part of the agreement, Chow agreed to drop the city's opposition to the mega-spa at Ontario Place. While the mayor defended the climbdown as merely acknowledging the province had the legal authority to pursue the plan, her retreat was deflating to progressive voters who saw it as abdication of her campaign promise to "stand up for Ontario Place."
Historic tax hike
In February, Chow got her first budget through council, funded by a 9.5 per cent property tax increase that was Toronto's largest since amalgamation. The mayor argued the historic hike was necessary to replenish municipal coffers left bare by previous administrations, and despite the headline-grabbing tax figure the move ultimately faced little council opposition.
"We finally had an honest conversation and confronted the fact ... the city's finances were in shambles," said Coun. Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park).
But on the eve of the budget debate, Chow gave in to a concerted campaign from the police chief for an additional $12.6 million. The concession frustrated progressives who hoped Chow would hold the line on the ever-expanding police budget, and divert more money to social causes of crime.
Chow told the Star this week the move reflected the will of council, and ultimately represents a small addition to the force's $1.2-billion budget.
But Jin Huh, executive director of Social Planning Toronto, said Chow's move was "a real big disappointment." Huh said Chow may have been seeking a compromise, but, "for many of us, she went past that line."
Help for housing
In her first year, Chow has increased the city's target for new rental homes to 65,000, adjusted the definition of affordable housing to better reflect what residents can pay, secured council approval for increasing density on major streets and spending $350 million to unlock the construction of almost 6,000 new affordable units. She's also allocated $100 million over three years to a program that supports non-profits to convert market housing to affordable units.
Mark Richardson, founder of advocacy group HousingNowTO, said Chow has "changed the discussion" around housing by introducing policies to help renters. That's crucial when growing numbers of nurses, librarians, teachers and others vital to Toronto's success can't afford to buy homes here. But he said it's still early days and Chow should be judged by whether her plans actually lead to more units being built.
Fixing the basics
Chow said she's started to address discontent over the state of Toronto's public amenities by deploying 1,000 new litter bins, filling 150,000 potholes, and opening wading pools two weeks earlier.
This spring, the rollout of the CaféTO program also went more smoothly than in previous years, and the city has extended hours at libraries (she has yet to fulfil her promise to have all 100 branches open seven days a week).
Chow acknowledged she still has work to do to tackle common complaints like traffic congestion, but said the improvements to basic services so far are meaningful for residents who rely on libraries for school or work, or need outdoor space to take their kids in the summer.
"All of those so-called small things are not that small," she said. "It impacts people's lives."
A botched tax rollout
In April, city hall was flooded with complaints after more than 150,000 households received tax bills for homes the municipality had incorrectly deemed unoccupied. The mayor blamed the fiasco on the "deeply flawed design" of the vacant home tax, which Tory introduced in 2022, and said she moved quickly to fix it.
Yet Chow had made the tax a key part of her agenda. Last fall, she led council in tripling its rate, which she said would discourage owners from leaving properties vacant during the housing crisis, while boosting city revenues.
Coun. Stephen Holyday (Ward 2, Etobicoke Centre), a conservative critic of Chow's, said she can't dodge responsibility for the botched rollout. She's still "the head of council and the person that has to take charge of these issues," he said.
Renaming Yonge-Dundas
Chow may have thought she was brokering a noble compromise in December when she backed a plan to abandon renaming all of Dundas Street over its namesake's purported ties to slavery, and instead rechristen just Yonge-Dundas Square and a handful of other civic assets.
Rather than ending the controversy, council's vote to rename the plaza "Sankofa Square" has proved deeply unpopular, according to opinion polls, and continued to spark heated debates at city hall.
Chow and other proponents maintain it's a long overdue gesture to carve out public space for Black Torontonians, and point out the name was recommended by an advisory group of Black and Indigenous leaders. But critics charge the mayor didn't do enough consultation on the change.
Coun. Brad Bradford (Ward 19, Beaches-East York), who ran against Chow last year, said the effort the mayor invested in the renaming shows she isn't focused enough on issues that matter most to residents, like the cost of living, congestion and crime.
"Frankly, I hear more about those problems than I do anything else," he said. "And the mayor hasn't had a really good response."
Unforced errors
Chow's fumbling of her initial response to the Israel-Gaza conflict, her abrupt reversal on a plan to cap the number of ride-hailing licences and a botched attempt by her TTC chair to suspend the transit agency's CEO stand out as low points of her first year. To critics, such errors are avoidable, and show Chow's team is mismanaging the basics.
Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16, Don Valley East), who praised Chow earlier in her term for reaching across the aisle to work with councillors, said he's concerned her demanding schedule of community events may be distracting her from business at city hall.
"I'm not sure the mayor's eye has been on the ball as much as it needs to be," Burnside said.
Chow rejected that assertion, and said her days are packed with briefings, meetings, and negotiations. She said she would pit her knowledge of city business against any other council member.
"I challenge any of them to debate me on any issues," she said. "Just because I am outside don't assume I don't know the files. Maybe it's because I have more energy."
With files from Alyshah Hasham.
CAPTION: Olivia Chow, who marks one year as mayor of Toronto on July 12, has surprised many by finding common ground with politicians of all political stripes."I don't think I'm tacking (to the centre)," said Chow of her incremental approach.

Document 349 of 500

'This is chaos': Bars and restaurants already struggling to order favourites as LCBO strike continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-chaos-bars-restaurants-already-struggling/docview/3077975051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+chaos%27%3A+Bars+and+restaurants+already+struggling+to+order+favourites+as+LCBO+strike+continues&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just five days into the first strike in LCBO history, bar and restaurant owners are struggling to get their hands on customer favourites, and temporary workarounds are already starting to look shaky.
"It's not even close to what we'd normally be buying, but at least it's something," said Sarah Brewer, after managing to buy two 750 ml bottles of Absolut vodka for the two restaurants and bars owned by her husband.
Usually, she said, the Mugshot Tavern and The Alpine go through six 1.14-litre bottles of Absolut a week.
The wholesale ordering system for licensees launched this spring requires that customers order a case at a time, which isn't feasible for small, independent places, especially when they're ordering liquor. Another workaround - a plan to open five stores for in-person shopping by licensees - was scrapped by the LCBO late Monday, after the Ontario Public Service Employees Union said it would picket the stores.
Instead, the LCBO has now allowed small bar and restaurant customers to order via the same online ordering site open to individual consumers.
"It only allowed me to buy two of the Absolut, and it's going to take five to seven business days to get here. Don't get me wrong, I'm grateful for any help they can give us, but it's not ideal," said Brewer, who also noticed some other favourites were out of stock entirely.
"There wasn't any Jameson showing up at all, and that's one of our more popular whiskies," said Brewer.
The strike by the 9,000 workers is a disaster in the making for a bar and restaurant industry that's still struggling to recover from the financial hit of the COVID-19 pandemic, said Restaurants Canada CEO Kelly Higginson.
"The contingency plans are not working, and they didn't work right out of the gate. It's chaos," said Higginson.
The strike comes in the middle of a patio season that's even more crucial than usual, Higginson added. On top of increased debt load from COVID, customers having less disposable income and cost increases, restaurant and bar owners also had to deal with the usual slowdown in spring and winter.
"It's a must season. We need a good summer," said Higginson.
For bigger restaurants and bars which have the money and storage space to buy by the case, the LCBO's wholesale website has been equally frustrating, Higginson said.
"They're struggling to get alcohol. The LCBO said there were going to be 168 different (products) available for wholesale, but in fact at this point there are maybe 30," said Higginson.
At whisky bar The Caledonian, husband and wife team David and Donna Wolff tried to get as much shopping done in stores before the strike began.
"I had a feeling it was going to be a mess, so the Wednesday before the strike I went to three different stores and bought $7,000 worth of booze. And I'm glad I did," said David Wolff.
While some of the rarer, pricier whiskies among The Caledonian's 600-bottle collection don't sell out quickly at the bar, the more standard options are likely to run out soon, he added.
"We sell more Ardbeg 10 than any other place in North America. So we'll notice it when it's gone," David Wolff said.
The LCBO is also temporarily pausing orders from suppliers to help make the system run smoother, the agency said in a statement Tuesday night.
"Given the impacts of OPSEU's strike on LCBO operations, we had to pause carrier partner pickups to help reduce congestion in our warehouses and across our supply chain," the statement said.
"Consignment orders are being reviewed individually to optimize product flow," the statement added.
In an email to members obtained by the Star, Drinks Ontario, which represents import agents and producers, said it was unclear how widespread the impact of the shipping pause would be.
"Drinks Ontario is in ongoing communication with LCBO policy and strike contingency teams to find out additional information, including whether this applies to all products worldwide and to all LCBO channels," Drinks Ontario said.
While Ontario Premier Doug Ford has touted the availability of beer, wine and spirits produced in this province, Higginson said it's not a viable option for most bars and restaurants - even if they wanted to buy local.
"It's not realistic to be able to supply our entire industry with what we need. ... The volume just isn't available. The availability isn't there."
CAPTION: On Day 5 of their first-ever strike, LCBO workers walk the picket line at the Yonge and Wellesley location.

A recap of Olivia Chow's first year as mayor + new rules for study permits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/recap-olivia-chows-first-year-as-mayor-new-rules/docview/3077975050/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+recap+of+Olivia+Chow%27s+first+year+as+mayor+%2B+new+rules+for+study+permits&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, July 10 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Olivia Chow's first year as mayor, new study permit rules for Canada's post-secondary schools and Canada getting called out for NATO spending.
DON'T MISSCritics warned she'd be an "unmitigated disaster" - here's how Olivia Chow's first year in power went
Friday will mark Mayor Olivia Chow's first full year in power. After inheriting a $1.8 billion budget shortfall and a housing crisis, the mayor that many opponents had once tagged as a radical leftist has mostly delivered slow, incremental change. From major deals with the province to give the city some financial breathing room, to botched policy rollouts, Chow has overseen a great deal of progress from the city's seat of power. While some critics say she's been too focussed on community events, Chow told the Star she could take them all on in a debate "on any issue." Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia recap of the highs and lows of Chow's time as mayor.
A big deal: In November, Chow and Premier Doug Ford reached a deal to upload the Gardiner and the Don Valley Parkway, opening up billions in capital for the city. In return, Chow acquiesced to the Ford government's controversial Ontario Place redevelopment.Take a hike: Chow also introduced a major hike to Toronto's vacant home tax in October, a move meant to boost the city's housing supply and boost city coffers by $55 million.Cap, or no cap? The mayor stumbled her way through a cap on Uber licenses in December, calling a surprise vote to limit the number of ride-share drivers before reversing her policy months later.Ottawa will stop processing study permits for colleges and universities that fail to track international students
Don't know what your international students are up to? Then kiss your study permits goodbye. The federal government's proposed new plan is to suspend processing of study permits for colleges and universities that fail to keep track of international students. The move is yet another policy meant to keep international student enrolment in check. Under the proposed new rules, schools will be required to report to the feds whether a student is attending school and complying with all study permit requirements. Here's what else you need to know about the new rules.
By the numbers: Study permit holders in Canada have skyrocketed in recent years. In 2023, the country welcomed more than one million study permit holders, compared to just over 350,000 in 2015.Context: The federal government slammed the brakes on the country's international program in January, introducing a cap on study permits with the aim of reducing the number issued by 35 per cent of 2023 levels.Canada has been called out for failing to hit NATO spending target
Canada is "riding America's coattails" when it comes to NATO spending - at least that's what U.S. House Speaker Mike Johnson reportedly said when calling out Canada's contribution to the military alliance. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was called out at a NATO summit in Washington for his lack of a plan to bump military spending up to NATO's two-per-cent-of-GDP target. But Ottawa is trying to work within "fiscal guardrails" it has set for itself as the government tries to rein in spending. Here's what failure to meet NATO spending targets could mean for Canada.
ICYMI: A record number of NATO allies are hitting the alliance's spending target - amidst the war in Ukraine and American pressure. Of NATO's 32 member nations, 23 have reached the two-per-cent goal.Bottom of the list: Canada remains one of the lowest spenders, doling out an equivalent to 1.37 per cent of the country's GDP to the military.Getting there: Since 2015, Ottawa has significantly increased its defence budget and is expected to hit defence spending of 1.76 per cent of GDP by 2029.WHAT ELSE
Western University is reconsidering its ties to Alice Munro following her daughter's revelations.The mayor of the town where Alice Munro lived will "consider" amending a monument honouring her.How did what happened to Alice Munro's daughter stay quiet so long? Start with our uniquely Canadian devotion to silence."A sexually mature young woman." A Toronto lawyer has been reprimanded over his argument that a 14-year-old girl "not a child."A plan to "significantly" speed construction on the Gardiner is in the works, says city manager.Knowing right from wrong will be the next challenge for the Leafs, Raptors and Blue Jays.Bars and restaurants are already struggling to order their favourites as the LCBO strike continues.The LCBO won't be left behind when booze sales are expanded, the Ford government says.Ontario is reporting five people have been hospitalized with listeriosis, as Silk and Great Value beverages are recalled across Canada.Hurricane Beryl's remnants could bring heavy rain to Toronto starting Tuesday night.The TTC will close a major portion of the Line 2 subway this weekend.The president of Hot Docs, Canada's largest documentary film festival, has stepped down.POV
Olivia Chow proved she can work well with others. But can she work for Toronto?
CLOSE-UP
TORONTO: It was a rough and frustrating night for Canadian soccer fans. With a chance to move onto the Copa America finals, it was none other than superstar Lionel Messi that put the nail into team Canada's coffin. After their 2-0 defeat, Canada will go on to play one more game for the third-place title, but all eyes are on 2026 to see just how far this Canadian team can go at the World Cup.

Document 351 of 500

LCBO strike should open up Doug Ford's eyes, but he's looking away

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-strike-should-open-up-doug-fords-eyes-hes/docview/3077975046/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+strike+should+open+up+Doug+Ford%27s+eyes%2C+but+he%27s+looking+away&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Essential service?
Doug Ford reveals new road map around the LCBO strike, July 8
My daughter worked part-time at the LCBO during the COVID-19 pandemic. At that time, the LCBO was considered an essential service (which I did not agree with) and remained open most of the time, putting staff at risk of contracting the virus. So why are workers not legislated back to work if they were deemed essential then? Why not now? I do not agree that the LCBO is essential but again, why was it during COVID-19?
Nancy Lucas, Toronto
Ontario loses revenue with booze in corner stores
I find it hard to comprehend why stores other than LCBO outlets should be allowed to sell alcohol. People want health care and education, and the LCBO profits help fund those things as a revenue stream for the province. We whine about lack of health services but seem too stupid to realize that sales go to the store, not the province. Do large stores such as Loblaws and Metro really need the money from alcohol?
Barbara Tallis, Toronto
The LCBO strike is another big blunder of Premier Doug Ford. For starters, why would Ontario want to compete with itself? Taking business away from a well-managed, secure, and efficient LCBO does not make sense. Putting beer, wine and mixed drinks in the hands of private store operators is very concerning. The many concerns of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) are justified. Allowingalcohol to be sold in other markets will ruin a strong organization that has worked well for many years. Don't fix it if it ain't broke!
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
One of Premier Doug Ford's poorly-thought-out decisions that may prove disastrous in the future is the weakening of the mandate of the LCBO by allowing beer, wine and mixed cocktails into corner stores. I think we can all agree that the contributions made by the LCBO toward our health care and education systems are invaluable. In fact, I believe that the LCBO is the goose that lays the golden eggs. So, what smart person that owns such a goose would wilfully try to weaken it? It is all too obvious that Premier Doug Ford does not know how to play chess and is incapable of properly foreseeing the next move. Who in their right mind would want to weaken that asset for little return? Someone intent on the short term goal of reelection at any cost. Wake up, premier.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge
Why is alcohol retail more important than addressing homelessness? The priorities of this government are skewed. Revenue for health care and education are much more important. Premier Doug Ford does not have my vote.
Ted Lister, Hamilton
You can still get booze online
I sympathize with LCBO workers. In essence they are government employees, but far too many of them are apparently being used as part-time workers. Still, I can't help but feel this recent strike action is a misbegotten approach. You can still order online, with now free delivery. Unless you're in dire need of a quick fix or are computer-less, why would anyone ever again need an LCBO store?
Jack Drury, Toronto
So, LCBO workers are striking to preserve their monopoly. They actually expect the public's sympathy and support?
David Pankhurst, Toronto
Great new festival
I was driving by the airport when the radio announcer listed the wonderful festivals in Toronto this weekend. Then an idea for a new festival hit me ... The Taste of Gridlock Festival! It starts Jan. 1 and goes to Dec. 31 every year! It features fender benders and road rage. Don't expect political protests blocking traffic. They were already stopped! There is no need to advertise because millions of Torontonians join in The Taste of Gridlock Festival every day!
John Cowles, Oakville
Toronto ain't so bad
Why hundreds of thousands of people are leaving the city for other parts of Canada, June 29
My wife and I began married life almost 50 years ago, living in the Toronto Annex neighbourhood and loving it. We moved to Thornhill when our first child was born over 45 years ago, raised our three children there and stayed long after they had all flown the nest. All the while, we felt a bit like fish out of water. We made the decision to sell our house and move down to the St. Lawrence Market neighbourhood in December of last year. What a wonderful change! It's diverse, vibrant, walkable, accessible and so full of life; everything we felt we had been missing all these years. I get that people have been leaving Toronto for all sorts of reasons. We decided to buck that trend and are thrilled to have, once again, become proud downtown Torontonians!
Michael Bines, Toronto
MLSE franchise management should pay attention
Playoff failures, rising ticket prices, frustrated fans. How did we get here? An inside look at Toronto's miserable sports scene, July 6
I sincerely hope that the senior management of both the Maple Leafs and Blue Jays are paying attention to the excellent series being run in the Star. This well-researched and well-written series clearly points out the reasons for lack of success on the ice and on the field. It is time the team ownership stepped in and terminated the employment of Brendan Shanahan, Brad Treliving, Mark Shapiro and Ross Atkins. It is clear they do not have the competence to produce winning teams so it is time for them to be replaced.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
I have little interest in baseball and even less for basketball, but I have been a hockey fan and a Maple Leaf nut for all my life. Most often when a team does poorly the coach gets fired even though he is usually not the real culprit. A coach is given a group of players to coach to the best of their ability. Who gives them these players? The general manager. If these players do not have the skills of other players in the league, then of course they will not win. But who gets blamed?
It is my opinion that the Leafs made a big mistake when they hired Brad Treliving as general manager. They should have chosen Mark Hunter since he had already worked as assistant GM. Since he left the organization, his choices of players to draft and trade for have made the London Knights an Ontario Hockey Association powerhouse for almost 20 years. There would also be a good chance he would have been able to get brother Dale Hunter to come coach. They would have worked their magic with the Leafs.
The problem lies with who hired the GM, the upper brass and owners. Who fires the boss?
Robert Drummond, Exeter, Ont.
CAPTION: On day five of their historic, first-ever strike, LCBO workers picket at the Yonge and Wellesley location in Toronto.

Document 352 of 500

Olivia Chow proved she can work well with others. But can she work for Toronto?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chow-proved-she-can-work-well-with-others/docview/3077974667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow+proved+she+can+work+well+with+others.+But+can+she+work+for+Toronto%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Has it been one year already? At the same time, has it only been a year?
Olivia Chow was sworn in as mayor of Toronto on July 12, 2023 after a whirlwind campaign in which she presented the most hopeful and optimistic take on what the city could do next.
How's that going?
In many obvious ways, her performance in the job has certainly lived up to her smiling confidence. People said she had divisive partisan baggage and wouldn't be able to break out of the NDP bubble. She said she could, and would work with everyone. I admit I wondered.
Weeks before the election, a political insider said to me, "Can you imagine her trying to work with Doug Ford?" It was difficult. In the days after the election, plenty of avid city hall watchers trying to predict the governing council alliances thought Jennifer McKelvie, Mike Colle and Paul Ainslie would likely be steadfast opponents of the new mayor and wondered if she could get a governing majority.
As for council, those three mentioned above have been reliable allies (two are deputy mayors!) and the only real consistent opposition to the Hallelujah chorus of Team Chow has come from campaign opponent Brad Bradford and longtime conservative outlier Stephen Holyday. She has not lost a vote (though she had to backtrack on some things, notably restrictions she tried to rush through on ride-sharing services).
The biggest mark of success on both the council harmony and policy agenda fronts was likely the 9.5 per cent property tax increase in her first budget. Two mayors over three terms had promised and delivered low property taxes as their number one priority, and they had created the deep-seeded impression that it was politically impossible to do otherwise.
I don't think the largest tax increase in megacity history was popular. But it did not spur a revolution, in the streets or on the council floor. The meeting where it passed was less contentious than past Ford-Tory meetings over cuts to the TTC and community recreation programs. The vote in favour was 18-8.
As for the premier, he and Chow appear to be besties. When I asked Chow late last month for a "This Matters" podcast about her biggest success so far, Chow cited the "new deal" arrangement with the province that delivered about $9 billion in funding and budget room for the city. "You're talking about billions of dollars that, for me, matters," she said, "It really helped the finances of the city of Toronto and to free up that money for us to do many other things, like investing in public transit."
And while her relationship with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has been somewhat less chummy, she did get the housing and shelter dollars she was asking for last year. Now, onto the city's urgent request for money to keep the Bloor-Danforth subway line running.
That's among a long list of items where success will only be judged in the future, including housing affordability. Chow has certainly pushed through ambitious new targets for housing, secured some funding for projects, and introduced protections for tenants and dollars to kick-start community and co-op rentals.
But one year in, the price of a place to live remains about as nauseatingly expensive as it was when she took office. She's recently been talking, including in our podcast interview, about the challenge of moving people around the city on the roads and on transit. Chow cited the example of fixing the King Street Transit Corridor as a model. It did work there quickly. Across the rest of the city, we're still waiting in traffic.
In that sense, not just on housing and congestion, it can seem that a lot hasn't changed. As I've written more often than I'd like, a lot of the city can feel like a grind, so much so that it has been driving residents out, and causing many of the rest of us to fall less in love with our city and lose hope for better.
Chow's promise as a candidate for mayor might have been summed up as "good vibes ahead." She's still smiling and projecting optimism, but I think it's fair to say many Torontonians still feel like they're grinding it out.
Before her election, the mood of the city felt a bit blah-de-blah: "Do we have to? Why bother?"
I don't think her campaign exactly whipped up a frenzy of "Yes we can!" confidence. But it did seem to get people nodding and saying, "Well, it's worth a shot I guess."
It strikes me that many of us are still in that mode a year later. Chow has shown she can work with people to implement her agenda. What we need to see now, as these decisions get implemented, is that her agenda can make our lives in the city substantially better.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow has proven she can work well with others, including Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.

Subject: Mayors; Councils; Success; Prime ministers; Tax increases

Full text: Essential service?
Doug Ford reveals new road map around the LCBO strike, July 8
My daughter worked part-time at the LCBO during the COVID-19 pandemic. At that time, the LCBO was considered an essential service (which I did not agree with) and remained open most of the time, putting staff at risk of contracting the virus. So why are workers not legislated back to work if they were deemed essential then? Why not now? I do not agree that the LCBO is essential but again, why was it during COVID-19?
Nancy Lucas, Toronto
Booze in corner stores costs us
I find it hard to comprehend why stores other than LCBO outlets should be allowed to sell alcohol. People want health care and education, and the LCBO profits help fund those things as a revenue stream for the province. We whine about lack of health services but seem too stupid to realize that sales go to the store, not the province. Do large stores such as Loblaws and Metro really need the money from alcohol?
Barbara Tallis, Toronto
The LCBO strike is another big blunder of Premier Doug Ford. For starters, why would Ontario want to compete with itself? Taking business away from a well-managed, secure and efficient LCBO does not make sense. Putting beer, wine and mixed drinks in the hands of private store operators is very concerning. The many concerns of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union are justified. Allowing alcohol to be sold in other markets will ruin a strong organization that has worked well for many years. Don't fix it if it ain't broke!
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
One of Premier Doug Ford's poorly thought-out decisions that may prove disastrous in the future is the weakening of the mandate of the LCBO by allowing beer, wine and mixed cocktails into corner stores. I think we can all agree that the contributions made by the LCBO toward our health care and education systems are invaluable. In fact, I believe that the LCBO is the goose that lays the golden eggs. So, what smart person who owns such a goose would wilfully try to weaken it? It is all too obvious that Ford does not know how to play chess and is incapable of properly foreseeing the next move. Who in their right mind would want to weaken that asset for little return? Someone intent on the short term goal of re-election at any cost. Wake up, premier.
David Ottenbrite,
Cambridge, Ont.
Why is alcohol retail more important than addressing homelessness? The priorities of this government are skewed. Revenue for health care and education are much more important. Premier Doug Ford does not have my vote.
Ted Lister, Hamilton
You can still get alcohol online
I sympathize with LCBO workers. In essence they are government employees, but far too many of them are apparently being used as part-time workers. Still, I can't help but feel this recent strike action is a misbegotten approach. You can still order online, with now free delivery. Unless you're in dire need of a quick fix or are computer-less, why would anyone ever again need an LCBO store?
Jack Drury, Toronto
So, LCBO workers are striking to preserve their monopoly. They actually expect the public's sympathy and support?
David Pankhurst, Toronto
MLSE should pay attention
Playoff failures, rising ticket prices, frustrated fans. How did we get here? An inside look at Toronto's miserable sports scene, July 6
I sincerely hope that the senior management of both the Maple Leafs and Blue Jays are paying attention to the excellent series being run in the Star. This well-researched and well-written series clearly points out the reasons for lack of success on the ice and on the field. It is time the team ownership stepped in and terminated the employment of Brendan Shanahan, Brad Treliving, Mark Shapiro and Ross Atkins. It is clear they do not have the competence to produce winning teams so it is time for them to be replaced.

I have little interest in baseball and even less for basketball, but I have been a hockey fan and a Maple Leaf nut for all my life. Who gives them these players? The general manager. If these players do not have the skills of other players in the league, then of course they will not win. But who gets blamed?
It is my opinion that the Leafs made a big mistake when they hired Brad Treliving as general manager. They should have chosen Mark Hunter since he had already worked as assistant GM. Since he left the organization, his choices of players to draft and trade for have made the London Knights an Ontario Hockey Association powerhouse for almost 20 years. There would also be a good chance he would have been able to get brother Dale Hunter to come coach. They would have worked their magic with the Leafs.

Robert Drummond, Exeter, Ont.
Toronto isn't really all that bad
Why hundreds of thousands of people are leaving the city for other parts of Canada, June 29
My wife and I began married life almost 50 years ago, living in Toronto's Annex neighbourhood and loving it. We moved to Thornhill when our first child was born, more than 45 years ago, raised our three children there and stayed long after they had all flown the nest. All the while, we felt a bit like fish out of water. We made the decision to sell our house and move down to the St. Lawrence Market neighbourhood in December of last year. What a wonderful change! We decided to buck that trend and are thrilled to have, once again, become proud downtown Torontonians!

Document 354 of 500

Incremental gains, not radical change: Mayor Olivia Chow defies expectations after one year in office

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/incremental-gains-not-radical-change/docview/3077690825/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Incremental+gains%2C+not+radical+change%3A+Mayor+Olivia+Chow+defies+expectations+after+one+year+in+office&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Olivia Chow wanted a reminder of how much needs fixing at city hall, she got it last month.
During a late spring storm, rain started streaming through the roof outside the mayor's office, forcing staff to cordon off the hallway.
"Oh, literally buckets of water coming down," Chow said. "I thought, 'oh my God.'"
Although it sounds like a too-good-to-be-true metaphor, Chow, who on Friday will mark one year in office, has spent the past 12 months trying to patch up municipal government.
While the scale of the problems she inherited were well known - a $1.8-billion budget shortfall and a crushing housing crisis among them - Chow has defied expectations.
Rather than the left-wing ideologue her election opponents tried to paint her as, the veteran NDPer has shown herself a practical leader, finding common ground with politicians of all stripes, and mostly delivering incremental, rather than radical change.
The approach has won her praise from some who don't share her politics, and allowed her to push policy through a council whose members are mostly to the right of her.But at times it has also alienated her progressive supporters.
And for all the credit she's received for her handling of major files, Chow has sometimes been tripped up by smaller issues, which to critics is evidence her office doesn't have a firm handle on council business.
Chow insists she and her team remain focused on her progressive vision, and are committed to creating a "more caring, affordable and safer city."
"I don't think I'm tacking (to the centre)," she told the Star during a wide-ranging interview about her year in power, pointing to her record of freezing TTC fares and boosting funding to prevent evictions. "That's pretty progressive."
The Star spoke to Chow, council members and experts about her first year. Here are the highs, lows and in-betweens.
Doing a deal
In November, Chow and Premier Doug Ford shocked longtime political observers by announcing the province had agreed to take ownership of the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway as part of a "new deal" between Toronto and Queen's Park.
The accord is expected to free up as much as $7.6 billion in capital spending for the city through the highway upload and provincial commitments for new subway cars and housing funds, while Toronto is also to receive $1.2 billion from Queen's Park for transit and shelter operations over three years.
"I don't know if it's her greatest achievement, but I think it's the one that has taken the most people by surprise," said Alejandra Bravo (Ward 9, Davenport), one of Chow's closest allies.
She said the "massive" savings have given the city financial breathing room and "underwritten" all of Chow's other policies.
The deal also showed Chow could shelve political differences and work with a premier who had warned voters she would be "an unmitigated disaster."
The highway upload was a "huge, huge achievement," said Amanda Galbraith, former mayor John Tory's communications director. Galbraith said that Tory got the ball rolling on the highway decision, but Chow took it over the line. "I give her really high marks for that," she said.
As part of the agreement, Chow agreed to drop the city's oppo-sition to the mega-spa at Ontario Place.
While the mayor defended the climbdown as merely acknowledging the province had the legal authority to pursue the plan, her retreat was deflating to progressive voters who saw it as abdication of her campaign promise to "stand up for Ontario Place."
Historic tax hike
In February, Chow got her first budget through council, funded by a 9.5 per cent property tax increase that was Toronto's largest since amalgamation. The mayor argued the historic hike was necessary to replenish municipal coffers left bare by previous administrations, and despite the headline-grabbing tax figure the move ultimately faced little council opposition.
"We finally had an honest conversation and confronted the fact ... the city's finances were in shambles," said Coun. Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park).
But on the eve of the budget debate, Chow gave in to a concerted campaign from the police chief for an additional $12.6 million. The concession frustrated progressives who hoped Chow would hold the line on the ever-expanding police budget, and divert more money to social causes of crime.
Chow told the Star this week the move reflected the will of council, and ultimately represents a small addition to the force's $1.2-billion budget.
But Jin Huh, executive director of Social Planning Toronto, said Chow's move was "a real big disappointment." Huh said Chow may have been seeking a compromise, but, "for many of us, she went past that line."
Help for housing
In her first year, Chow has increased the city's target for new rental homes to 65,000, adjusted the definition of affordable housing to better reflect what residents can pay, secured council approval for increasing density on major streets and spending $350 million to unlock the construction of almost 6,000 new affordable units. She's also allocated $100 million over three years to a program that supports non-profits to convert market housing to affordable units.
Mark Richardson, founder of advocacy group HousingNowTO, said Chow has "changed the discussion" around housing by introducing policies to help renters. That's crucial when growing numbers of nurses, librarians, teachers and others vital to Toronto's success can't afford to buy homes here. But he said it's still early days and Chow should be judged by whether her plans actually lead to more units being built.
Fixing the basics
Chow said she's started to address discontent over the state of Toronto's public amenities by deploying 1,000 new litter bins, filling 150,000 potholes, and opening wading pools two weeks earlier.
This spring, the rollout of the CaféTO program also went more smoothly than in previous years, and the city has extended hours at libraries (she has yet to fulfil her promise to have all 100 branches open seven days a week).
Chow acknowledged she still has work to do to tackle common complaints like traffic congestion, but said the improvements to basic services so far are meaningful for residents who rely on libraries for school or work, or need outdoor space to take their kids in the summer.
"All of those so-called small things are not that small," she said. "It impacts people's lives."
A botched tax rollout
In April, city hall was flooded with complaints after more than 150,000 households received tax bills for homes the municipality had incorrectly deemed unoccupied. The mayor blamed the fiasco on the "deeply flawed design" of the vacant home tax, which Tory introduced in 2022, and said she moved quickly to fix it.
Yet Chow had made the tax a key part of her agenda. Last fall, she led council in tripling its rate, which she said would discourage owners from leaving properties vacant during the housing crisis, while boosting city revenues.
Coun. Stephen Holyday (Ward 2, Etobicoke Centre), a conservative critic of Chow's, said she can't dodge responsibility for the botched rollout. She's still "the head of council and the person that has to take charge of these issues," he said.
Renaming Yonge-Dundas
Chow may have thought she was brokering a noble compromise in Decemberwhen she backed a plan to abandon renaming all of Dundas Street over its namesake's purported ties to slavery, and instead rechristen just Yonge-Dundas Square and a handful of other civic assets.
Rather than ending the controversy, council's vote to rename the plaza "Sankofa Square" has proved deeply unpopular, according to opinion polls, and continued to spark heated debates at city hall.
Chow and other proponents maintain it's a long overdue gesture to carve out public space for Black Torontonians, and point out the name was recommended by an advisory group of Black and Indigenous leaders. But critics charge the mayor didn't do enough consultation on the change.
Coun. Brad Bradford (Ward 19, Beaches-East York), who ran against Chow last year, said the effort the mayor invested in the renaming shows she isn't focused enough on issues that matter most to residents, like the cost of living, congestion and crime.
Unforced errors
Chow's fumbling of her initial response to the Israel-Gaza conflict, her abrupt reversal on a plan to cap the number of ride-hailing licences and a botched attempt by her TTC chair to suspend the transit agency's CEO stand out as low points of her first year. To critics, such errors are avoidable, and show Chow's team is mismanaging the basics.
Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16, Don Valley East), who praised Chow earlier in her term for reaching across the aisle to work with councillors, said he's concerned her demanding schedule of community events may be distracting her from business at city hall.
"I'm not sure the mayor's eye has been on the ball as much as it needs to be," Burnside said.
Chow rejected that assertion, and said her days are packed with briefings, meetings, and negotiations. She said she would pit her knowledge of city business against any other council member.
"Just because I am outside don't assume I don't know the files. Maybe it's because I have more energy."
With files from Alyshah Hasham
CREDIT: Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star With files from Alyshah Hasham

Subject: Capital expenditures; Councils; Budgets; Voters; Affordable housing; Critics; Tax increases

Title: Incremental gains, not radical change:   Mayor Olivia Chow defies expectations after one year in office

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Document 355 of 500

PCs attempt to assure LCBO it has a future: Union says province's letter to agency doesn't guarantee safety for workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-attempt-assure-lcbo-has-future/docview/3077690556/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+attempt+to+assure+LCBO+it+has+a+future%3A+Union+says+province%27s+letter+to+agency+doesn%27t+guarantee+safety+for+workers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hit by the first strike in LCBO history, Queen's Park is assuring the Crown liquor monopoly it will have a central role in future booze liberalization.
With more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) members on picket lines since Friday, Premier Doug Ford's government is underscoring the importance of the Liquor Control Board of Ontario.
In a letter to board chair Carmine Nigro, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy stressed "while some take a more short-sighted view that opposes change, as a best in class wholesaler and retailer of alcohol, LCBO will continue to make meaningful financial contributions to provincial coffers."
But OPSEU president JP Hornick, who maintains the strike is a fight against the premier's privatization agenda, dismissed Bethlenfalvy's missive as "weasel words."
"There's nothing in there that actually guarantees a single job or what the future of the LCBO looks like," Hornick told reporters Tuesday at the LCBO's Queens Quay flagship store.
Earlier in the day, Bethlenfalvy insisted "you can provide more choice while still generating revenue to invest in front-line government services (and the) LCBO will continue to innovate and compete in this new marketplace as a valuable public asset."
Workers hit the bricks as the Progressive Conservative government is spending anywhere between $225 million and $1 billion to expedite liberalized alcohol sales by Oct. 31.
Six weeks ago, Ford announced the province would pay the Beer Store $225 million to move up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline to allow the sale of beer, wine and premixed cocktails in corner stores, big box outlets and more supermarkets.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who joined Hornick at the OPSEU picket line Tuesday afternoon, warned the price tag could balloon to $1 billion due to other clauses buried in the contract.
"Doug Ford (is) too busy spending his summer at the cottage flipping burgers and putting up interactive maps," said Crombie, deriding the premier's latest campaign-style promotional video.
With about 680 LCBO outlets closed across the province, Ford on Monday launched the online map to help consumers find the hundreds of breweries, wineries, wine shops and distilleries, 449 licensed supermarkets, 422 Beer Store outlets and 389 rural LCBO agency stores that are open.
Under his reforms, the LCBO, which brings in more than $2 billion annually, would retain its monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, gin and vodka and be the wholesale distributor for all retailers.
Signalling more money could be generated, Bethlenfalvy's office shared data showing Ontario lags behind most other provinces when it comes to per-capital revenue from alcohol sales at $159 per person.
Newfoundland leads at $358 per person annually compared with British Columbia ($218) and Quebec ($161), the provinces that already us the hybrid pri-vate-public model Ontario is adopting.
Alberta, which has the most privatized system in the country, collects $178 per person each year in booze revenue.
Emphasizing its key place in the more open marketplace, Bethlenfalvy has instructed Nigro, a close Ford associate and past chair of Ontario Place, to champion local domestic producers "while being mindful of Ontario's trade obligations."
To that end, the treasurer has asked the LCBO to appoint "an internal/external champion" to lead the board's push to boost Ontario products and improve outreach to the domestic beer, wine, cider and spirits industry and measure the progress of those efforts.
Bethlenfalvy also wants Nigro to "provide prominent merchandising space, signage, and promotional opportunities for small and local Ontario producers that reflect consumer trends and demands."
As well, he would like to see the LCBO "expand de-alcoholized and non-alcoholic beverage offerings from small and local Ontario alcohol producers to support consumer trends" and - when stores eventually reopen - use locally sourced paper bags for customers if possible.
Concerned about picketers, the LCBO abandoned a proposal to open five stores Wednesday to supply restaurateurs and publicans who wanted to purchase products in person.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 356 of 500

May won't step down, but says Trudeau should

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/may-wont-step-down-says-trudeau-should/docview/3077690545/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=May+won%27t+step+down%2C+but+says+Trudeau+should&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Green Party Leader Elizabeth May says it's "obvious" Justin Trudeau should "pass the torch," and if she were in the Liberal party she'd be pulling him aside saying, "prime minister, with all due respect, don't you think it's time?"
Following a press conference Tuesday, where May's unofficial co-leader, Jonathan Pedneault, resigned, the long-time head of the Green Party was confronted by questions about her own leadership. But she dismissed a complaint she isn't making room for the next generation of Green leadership. She suggests she'll pick her heir, and will guide the party into the next campaign - her fifth in 15 years.
"(Trudeau) is hated in ways that I think are unfair," May told the Star in a long interview Tuesday, comparing the prime minister to U.S. President Joe Biden, whom she also thinks should step aside. "It's different in that there's no sense of Justin Trudeau having cognitive difficulties, but I mean, there's a moment where you realize - and it was some time ago - that it would be better for the Liberal party if there was a different leader who didn't attract so many negatives."
May thinks the hatred against Trudeau is un-Canadian, but believes he has fuelled cynicism with a string of broken promises - from electoral reform to purchasing a pipeline and increasing carbon emissions.
"He's broken his word so many times, on so many issues, that the trust is gone," she says. "When you break a promise ... there's consequences, and he's experiencing the consequences, but he wants to not believe his own eyes."
Some Grits, she says, believe Trudeau is a great campaigner and that no one will fare better than him. His feat in 2015, catapulting the Liberals from third place to first, was impressive, May notes. Perhaps the prime minister believes he is that good a campaigner, but, she says, somebody who likes him needs to tell him: "This is time for you to protect what you've accomplished in legacy terms and make sure there's a different communicator, a different face, a different voice, that people can believe going into the next election."
If May sees any parallels between Trudeau's hold on the Liberal party and her own with the federal Greens, she makes no mention of it. She's led the party for so long (from 2006 to 2019, and again from 2022 until now), that in many people's minds, she is the Green Party.
Tuesday, Pedneault quit following a federal council meeting Monday. The move came as a surprise to some.
In 2022, Pedneault contacted May for advice on his leadership bid. Weeks later, she proposed they run together on a co-leader ticket. She encouraged members to rank Pedneault - a young bilingual queer Black man - first, making him the de facto leader as the Greens' constitution allows for only one leader. But the majority of the approximately 8,000 voters ranked May number one. The plan was to have party members endorse a co-leadership model constitutional change, but 19 months after the May-Pedneault victory, a meeting still hasn't been held.
At their joint press conference announcing Pedneault's departure, May cited a provincial byelection in Ontario last year as the reason the meeting was postponed. It was delayed again in June, apparently because of the federal byelection in Toronto-St.Paul's. The Star's reporting suggests the co-leadership appointment model was not without controversy with party members.
May says she was frustrated by the delay and that Pedneault was, too.
The 34-year-old human rights activist refused to elaborate Tuesday as to why he was leaving, citing "personal reasons," but a source with knowledge of the situation suggested changes could have been made to ensure he remained.
Alex Tyrrell, leader of the Quebec Greens, blamed May for Pedneault's resignation. In a statement, he suggested she hogged the spotlight and didn't live up to commitments she'd made to Pedneault. Tyrrell called on May to step down, saying she "has done everything she can for the Green Party of Canada," that the party would "make no further gains under her leadership," and it was time she "pass the torch" to the next generation of Green leaders.
May called Tyrrell's allegations "patently untrue."
She says despite Pedneault's resignation, she's still committed to the co-leadership model but a meeting likely won't be held until after three provincial elections this fall. She told the Star she's unwilling to resign and run on a co-leadership ticket with a new partner, even if the members agree to the constitutional change.
Instead, she suggests she'll pick her co-leader and possibly have the membership approve her choice of an "heir apparent."
Leadership races are expensive and she doesn't think it's wise to step down when a snap election could be called at any time. Plus, she says, the party just had a leadership race.
"The one thing I'm sure of, is they wanted me to be the leader. That part, I get."
CAPTION: Green Party Leader Elizabeth May told the Star that she thinks the hatred against Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is un-Canadian, but believes he has fuelled cynicism with a string of broken promises.Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION
