Document 357 of 500

Deputy resigns, ending bid to become co-leader: Pedneault cites 'personal reasons' for decision that will leave May as sole head of federal Greens

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/deputy-resigns-ending-bid-become-co-leader/docview/3077690537/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Deputy+resigns%2C+ending+bid+to+become+co-leader%3A+Pedneault+cites+%27personal+reasons%27+for+decision+that+will+leave+May+as+sole+head+of+federal+Greens&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jonathan Pedneault has dropped his bid to co-lead the federal Green Party after internal debate and numerous delays impeded those plans, leaving Elizabeth May as the party's sole leader.
Citing "personal reasons," the longtime human-rights activist announced his resignation as the party's deputy leader at a news conference with May in Ottawa on Tuesday, after the party's federal council held an emergency meeting Monday night to discuss the party's leadership.
Pedneault said it had been the "honour of a lifetime" to serve as the Greens' deputy leader, and pushed back against the suggestion the delays in confirming him as co-leader had anything to do with his resignation.
"I'm not going to discuss the reasons as to why I'm leaving today," he told reporters. "That is for me."
It was the latest twist in a debate over the Green Party's leadership that some members say threatens the stability the party has seen since November 2022, when the former leader May returned with a proposal to co-lead with Pedneault after a turbulent period marked by dysfunction within the party.
Pedneault's resignation, May said in an emotional tribute, is "heartbreaking."
"There's no point to say more now - just to say that Jonathan's decision is his decision," she said. "I respect him and love him. I couldn't have asked for a better partner, a better sounding board, a better voice for so many core issues that face Canadian society."
The pair had campaigned to overhaul the party's constitution - which does not allow for co-leadership - but internal debate over that proposal led to delays that frustrated both. Pedneault was never officially named co-leader.
His exit leaves unanswered questions about how or even if the party will embrace a co-leader model. The Star previously reported that Pedneault had been frustrated with delays in arriving at that decision.
But May painted the delays as necessary for "important" discussions, and insisted there is no pushback against the idea, even if it has taken longer than expected to come to fruition.
Those delays - and Pedneault's departure - are concerning some party members, who fear a return to dysfunction after a period of relative calm.
"If we can't get through this, then I don't know what the future is going to look like for us," said Kyle Hutton, CEO of the party's Burlington electoral district association.
However, Hutton believes the party remains in a better position that it was a few years ago when internal conflict almost tore it apart.
May appeared confident the party will continue on the path toward having co-leaders - a move she said could lead to success like that of Green parties in Europe - before the next federal election, but said it would be up to members to decide.
"I remain committed to the path that the Green Party is on currently for constitutional reform to constitutionally address the issue of co-leadership and why it is a preferable course," she said.
"My own timeline would have been more aggressive from the moment when the leadership race ended."
A change to the party's constitution that would permit co-leadership was set to be put to a vote, but there was dissension behind the scenes, the Star reported in May. Some party members argued that moving to co-leadership would require a new leadership election, while others said they had never agreed to co-leadership to begin with. Amid questions about what co-leadership should look like, the official change was delayed several times.
Pedneault characterized a vote on co-leadership as a "confidence vote on my involvement," internal messages viewed by the Star showed. He told the Star he would work with members to reach consensus, but that another leadership election would not be a good use of party resources in the year before a federal election. What the party needed, he said, was stability.
Most recently, the vote on co-leadership was set for June 23, but the party delayed it once again - this time for at least 60 days - citing the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection.
In an email to party members obtained by the Star, Green Party president Natalie Odd said the delay also gave the party time to gauge members' interest in exploring co-leadership, including through engagement sessions and surveys.
May has repeatedly said she doesn't believe co-leaders should be elected separately, suggesting on Tuesday she would hand-pick her co-leader and allow members to decide.
May, who recently turned 70, is one of two Green MPs, alongside Ontario MP Mike Morrice. Morrice does not have any plans to run for co-leader of the party, a spokesperson told the Star.
CAPTION: Jonathan Pedneault hugs Green Party Leader Elizabeth May Tuesday after announcing his resignation as deputy leader in Ottawa. May said in an emotional tribute that his decision to step down and end his bid to co-lead the party is "heart- breaking."Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 358 of 500

Can Chow deliver for Toronto?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-chow-deliver-toronto/docview/3077690421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Chow+deliver+for+Toronto%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Has it been one year already? At the same time, has it only been a year?
Olivia Chow was sworn in as mayor of Toronto on July 12, 2023, after a whirlwind campaign in which she presented the most hopeful and optimistic take on what the city could do next. How's that going?
In many obvious ways, her performance in the job has certainly lived up to her smiling confidence. People said she had divisive partisan baggage and wouldn't be able to break out of the NDP bubble. She said she could, and would work with everyone. I admit I wondered.
Weeks before the election, a political insider said to me, "Can you imagine her trying to work with Doug Ford?" It was difficult. In the days after the election, plenty of avid city hall watchers trying to predict the governing council alliances thought Jennifer McKelvie, Mike Colle and Paul Ainslie would likely be steadfast opponents of the new mayor and wondered if she could get a governing majority.
As for council, those three mentioned above have been reliable allies (two are deputy mayors!) and the only real consistent opposition to the Hallelujah chorus of Team Chow has come from campaign opponent Brad Bradford and longtime conservative outlier Stephen Holyday. She has not lost a vote (though she had to backtrack on some things, notably restrictions she tried to rush through on ride-sharing services).
The biggest mark of success on both the council harmony and policy agenda fronts was likely the 9.5 per cent property tax increase in her first budget. Two mayors over three terms had promised and delivered low property taxes as their number one priority, and they had created the deep-seeded impression that it was politically impossible to do otherwise.
I don't think the largest tax increase in megacity history was popular. But it did not spur a revolution, in the streets or on the council floor. The meeting where it passed was less contentious than past FordTory meetings over cuts to the TTC and community recreation programs. The vote in favour was 18-8.
As for the premier, he and Chow appear to be besties. When I asked Chow last month for a "This Matters" podcast about her biggest success so far, Chow cited the "new deal" arrangement with the province that delivered about $9 billion in funding and budget room for the city. "You're talking about billions of dollars that, for me, matters," she said, "It really helped the finances of the city of Toronto and to free up that money for us to do many other things, like investing in public transit."
And while her relationship with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has been somewhat less chummy, she did get the housing and shelter dollars she was asking for last year.
Now, onto the city's urgent request for money to keep the Bloor-Danforth subway line running.
That's among a long list of items where success will only be judged in the future, including housing affordability. Chow has certainly pushed through ambitious new targets for housing, secured some funding for projects, and introduced protections for tenants and dollars to kick-start community and co-op rentals.
But one year in, the price of a place to live remains about as nauseatingly expensive as it was when she took office. She's recently been talking, including in our podcast interview, about the challenge of moving people around the city on the roads and on transit. Chow cited the example of fixing the King Street Transit Corridor as a model. It did work there quickly. Across the rest of the city, we're still waiting in traffic.
In that sense, not just on housing and congestion, it can seem that a lot hasn't changed. As I've written more often than I'd like, a lot of the city can feel like a grind, so much so that it has been driving residents out, and causing many of the rest of us to fall less in love with our city and lose hope for better.
Chow's promise as a candidate for mayor might have been summed up as "good vibes ahead." She's still smiling and projecting optimism, but I think it's fair to say many Torontonians still feel like they're grinding it out.
Before her election, the mood of the city felt a bit blah-de-blah: "Do we have to? Why bother?"
I don't think her campaign exactly whipped up a frenzy of "Yes we can!" confidence. But it did seem to get people nodding and saying, "Well, it's worth a shot I guess."
It strikes me that many of us are still in that mode a year later. Chow has shown she can work with people to implement her agenda. What we need to see now, as these decisions get implemented, is that her agenda can make our lives in the city substantially better.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow has proven she can work well with others, including Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, to implement her agenda, Edward Keenan writes. Now she needs to show that her agenda can make life better for Torontonians.Arlyn McAdorey The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Document 359 of 500

'Chaos' as bars run low on booze: Wholesale bulk ordering system not feasible for small pubs, restaurants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chaos-as-bars-run-low-on-booze/docview/3077690401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Chaos%27+as+bars+run+low+on+booze%3A+Wholesale+bulk+ordering+system+not+feasible+for+small+pubs%2C+restaurants&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just five days into the first strike in LCBO history, bar and restaurant owners are struggling to get their hands on customer favourites, and temporary workarounds are already starting to look shaky.
"It's not even close to what we'd normally be buying, but at least it's something," said Sarah Brewer, after buying just two 750 ml bottles of Absolut vodka for the businesses she owns with her husband.
Usually, she said, the Mugshot Tavern and The Alpine go through six 1.14-litre bottles a week.
The wholesale ordering system for licensees launched this spring requires that customers order a case at a time, which isn't feasible for small, independent places, especially when they're ordering liquor. Another workaround - a plan to open five stores for in-person shopping by licensees - was scrapped by the LCBO late Monday, after the Ontario Public Service Employees Union said it would picket stores.
Instead, the LCBO has now allowed small bar and restaurant customers to order via the online site open to individual consumers.
"It only allowed me to buy two of the Absolut, and it's going to take five to seven business days to get here. Don't get me wrong, I'm grateful for any help they can give us, but it's not ideal," said Brewer, who also noticed some other favourites were out of stock entirely.
"There wasn't any Jameson showing up at all, and that's one of our more popular whiskies," said Brewer.
The strike by the 9,000 workers is a disaster in the making for a bar and restaurant industry that's still struggling to recover from the financial hit of the COVID-19 pandemic, said Restaurants Canada CEO Kelly Higginson.
"The contingency plans are not working, and they didn't work right out of the gate. It's chaos," said Higginson.
The strike comes in the middle of a patio season that's even more crucial than usual, Higginson added. On top of increased debt load from COVID, customers having less disposable income and cost increases, restaurant and bar owners also had to deal with the usual slowdown in spring and winter.
"It's a must season. We need a good summer," said Higginson.
For bigger restaurants and bars which have the money and storage space to buy by the case, the LCBO's wholesale website has been equally frustrating, Higginson said.
"They're struggling to get alcohol. The LCBO said there were going to be 168 different (products) available for wholesale, but in fact at this point there are maybe 30," said Higginson.
At whisky bar The Caledonian, husband and wife team David and Donna Wolff tried to get as much shopping done in stores before the strike began.
"I had a feeling it was going to be a mess, so the Wednesday before the strike I went to three different stores and bought $7,000 worth of booze. And I'm glad I did," said David Wolff.
While some of the rarer, pricier whiskies among The Caledonian's 600-bottle collection don't sell out quickly at the bar, the more standard options are likely to run out soon, he added.
"We sell more Ardbeg 10 than any other place in North America. So we'll notice it when it's gone," David Wolff said.
The LCBO is also pausing orders from suppliers to help make the system run smoother, the agency said in a statement Tuesday night.
"Given the impacts of OPSEU's strike on LCBO operations, we had to pause carrier partner pickups to help reduce congestion in our warehouses and across our supply chain," the statement said.
"Consignment orders are being reviewed individually to optimize product flow," the statement added.
In an email to members obtained by the Star, Drinks Ontario, which represents import agents and producers, said it was unclear how widespread the impact of the shipping pause would be.
"Drinks Ontario is in ongoing communication with LCBO policy and strike contingency teams to find out additional information, including whether this applies to all products worldwide and to all LCBO channels," Drinks Ontario said.
While Ontario Premier Doug Ford has touted the availability of beer, wine and spirits produced in this province, Higginson said it's not a viable option for most bars and restaurants - even if they wanted to buy local.
"It's not realistic to be able to supply our entire industry with what we need. ... The volume just isn't available. The availability isn't there."
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star

Document 360 of 500

It's 'obvious' Justin Trudeau should step down, Elizabeth May says - so why won't she listen to her own critics?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/obvious-justin-trudeau-should-step-down-elizabeth/docview/3078100069/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+%27obvious%27+Justin+Trudeau+should+step+down%2C+Elizabeth+May+says+-+so+why+won%27t+she+listen+to+her+own+critics%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Green Party Leader Elizabeth May says it's "obvious" Justin Trudeau should "pass the torch," and if she were in the Liberal party she'd be pulling him aside saying, "prime minister, with all due respect, don't you think it's time?"
Following a press conference Tuesday, where May's unofficial co-leader, Jonathan Pedneault, resigned, the long-time head of the Green Party was confronted by questions about her own leadership. But she dismissed a complaint she isn't making room for the next generation of Green leadership. She suggests she'll pick her heir, and will guide the party into the next campaign - her fifth in 15 years.
"(Trudeau) is hated in ways that I think are unfair," May told the Star in a long interview Tuesday, comparing the prime minister to U.S. President Joe Biden, whom she also thinks should step aside. "It's different in that there's no sense of Justin Trudeau having cognitive difficulties, but I mean, there's a moment where you realize - and it was some time ago - that it would be better for the Liberal party if there was a different leader who didn't attract so many negatives."
May thinks the hatred against Trudeau is un-Canadian, but believes he has fuelled cynicism with a string of broken promises - from electoral reform to purchasing a pipeline and increasing carbon emissions. "He's broken his word so many times, on so many issues, that the trust is gone," she says. "When you break a promise ... there's consequences, and he's experiencing the consequences, but he wants to not believe his own eyes."
Some Grits, she says, believe Trudeau is a great campaigner and that no one will fare better than him. His feat in 2015, catapulting the Liberals from third place to first, was impressive, May notes. Perhaps the prime minister believes he is that good a campaigner, but, she says, somebody who likes him needs to tell him: "'This is time for you to protect what you've accomplished in legacy terms and make sure there's a different communicator, a different face, a different voice, that people can believe going into the next election.'"
If May sees any parallels between Trudeau's hold on the Liberal party and her own with the federal Greens, she makes no mention of it. She's led the party for so long (from 2006 to 2019, and again from 2022 until now), that in many people's minds, she is the Green Party.
Tuesday, Pedneault quit following a federal council meeting Monday. The move came as a surprise to some.
In 2022, Pedneault contacted May for advice on his leadership bid. Weeks later, she proposed they run together on a co-leader ticket. She encouraged members to rank Pedneault - a young bilingual queer Black man - first, making him the de facto leader as the Greens' constitution allows for only one leader. But the majority of the approximately 8,000 voters ranked May number one. The plan was to have party members endorse a co-leadership model constitutional change, but 19 months after the May-Pedneault victory, a meeting still hasn't been held.
At their joint press conference announcing Pedneault's departure, May cited a provincial byelection in Ontario last year as the reason the meeting was postponed. It was delayed again in June, apparently because of the federal byelection in Toronto-St.Paul's. The Star's reporting suggests the co-leadership appointment model was not without controversy with party members.
May says she was frustrated by the delay and that Pedneault was, too.
The 34-year-old human rights activist refused to elaborate Tuesday as to why he was leaving, citing "personal reasons," but a source with knowledge of the situation suggested changes could have been made to ensure he remained.
Alex Tyrrell, leader of the Quebec Greens, blamed May for Pedneault's resignation. In a statement, he suggested she hogged the spotlight and didn't live up to commitments she'd made to Pedneault. Tyrrell called on May to step down, saying she "has done everything she can for the Green Party of Canada," that the party would "make no further gains under her leadership," and it was time she "pass the torch" to the next generation of Green leaders.
May called Tyrrell's allegations "patently untrue."
She says despite Pedneault's resignation, she's still committed to the co-leadership model but a meeting likely won't be held until after three provincial elections this fall. She told the Star she's unwilling to resign and run on a co-leadership ticket with a new partner, even if the members agree to the constitutional change. Instead, she suggests she'll pick her co-leader and possibly have the membership approve her choice of an "heir apparent."
Leadership races are expensive and she doesn't think it's wise to step down when a snap election could be called at any time. Plus, she says, the party just had a leadership race.
"The one thing I'm sure of, is they wanted me to be the leader. That part, I get."
CAPTION: Jonathan Pedneault, who ran alongside Green Party Leader Elizabeth May in the hopes of one day becoming the party's co-leader, announced he is stepping away from his current role as her deputy. Asked whether there were internal complaints around establishing him as a co-leader, they both defended the grassroots process. (July 9, 2024 / The Canadian Press)Green party co-leader Elizabeth May speaks during a press conference at the National Press Theatre in Ottawa on July 9, 2024.

Document 361 of 500

LCBO won't be left behind when booze sales are expanded, Ford government says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-wont-be-left-behind-when-booze-sales-are/docview/3078098426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+won%27t+be+left+behind+when+booze+sales+are+expanded%2C+Ford+government+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hit by the first strike in LCBO history, Queen's Park is assuring the Crown liquor monopoly it will have a central role in future booze liberalization.
With more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) members on picket lines since Friday, Premier Doug Ford's government is underscoring the importance of the Liquor Control Board of Ontario.
In a letter to board chair Carmine Nigro, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy stressed "while some take a more short-sighted view that opposes change, as a best in class wholesaler and retailer of alcohol, LCBO will continue to make meaningful financial contributions to provincial coffers."
But OPSEU president JP Hornick, who maintains the strike is a fight against the premier's privatization agenda, dismissed Bethlenfalvy's missive as "weasel words."
"There's nothing in there that actually guarantees a single job or what the future of the LCBO looks like," Hornick told reporters Tuesday at the LCBO's Queens Quay flagship store.
Earlier in the day, Bethlenfalvy insisted "you can provide more choice while still generating revenue to invest in front-line government services (and the) LCBO will continue to innovate and compete in this new marketplace as a valuable public asset."
Workers hit the bricks as the Progressive Conservative government is spending anywhere between $225 million and $1 billion to expedite liberalized alcohol sales by Oct. 31.
Six weeks ago, Ford announced the province would pay The Beer Store $225 million to move up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline to allow the sale of beer, wine and premixed cocktails in corner stores, big box outlets and more supermarkets.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who joined Hornick at the OPSEU picket line Tuesday afternoon, warned the price tag could balloon to $1 billion due to other clauses buried in the contract.
"Doug Ford (is) too busy spending his summer at the cottage flipping burgers and putting up interactive maps," said Crombie, deriding the premier's latest campaign-style promotional video.
With about 680 LCBO outlets closed across the province, Ford on Monday launched the online map to help consumers find the hundreds of breweries, wineries, wine shops and distilleries, 449 licensed supermarkets, 422 Beer Store outlets and 389 rural LCBO agency stores that are open.
Under his reforms, the LCBO, which brings in more than $2 billion annually, would retain its monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, gin and vodka and be the wholesale distributor for all retailers.
Signalling more money could be generated, Bethlenfalvy's office shared data showing Ontario lags behind most other provinces when it comes to per-capital revenue from alcohol sales at $159 per person.
Newfoundland leads at $358 per person annually compared with British Columbia ($218) and Quebec ($161), the provinces that already us the hybrid private-public model Ontario is adopting.
Alberta, which has the most privatized system in the country, collects $178 per person each year in booze revenues.
Emphasizing its key place in the more open marketplace, Bethlenfalvy has instructed Nigro, a close Ford associate and past chair of Ontario Place, to champion local domestic producers "while being mindful of Ontario's trade obligations."
To that end, the treasurer has asked the LCBO to appoint "an internal/external champion" to lead the board's push to boost Ontario products and improve outreach to the domestic beer, wine, cider and spirits industry and measure the progress of those efforts.
Bethlenfalvy also wants Nigro to "provide prominent merchandising space, signage, and promotional opportunities for small and local Ontario producers that reflect consumer trends and demands."
As well, he would like to see the LCBO "expand de-alcoholized and non-alcoholic beverage offerings from small and local Ontario alcohol producers to support consumer trends" and - when stores eventually reopen - use locally sourced paper bags for customers if possible.
Concerned about picketers, the LCBO abandoned a proposal to open five stores Wednesday to supply restaurateurs and publicans who wanted to purchase products in person.
CAPTION: The union representing Ontario liquor store workers has ramped up pressure in a five-day-old strike, with no talks planned to hammer out a deal. Thousands of workers at the Liquor Control Board of Ontario walked off the job. (July 9, 2024 / The Canadian Pess)Premier Doug Ford shakes hands with Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy at an announcement in May.

Document 362 of 500

Deputy leader stepping down from bid to co-lead federal Green Party after internal debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/deputy-leader-stepping-down-bid-co-lead-federal/docview/3077142191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Deputy+leader+stepping+down+from+bid+to+co-lead+federal+Green+Party+after+internal+debate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Jonathan Pedneault is stepping down from his bid to co-lead the federal Green Party, citing "personal reasons," after internal debate and numerous delays impeded those plans, leaving the party's leadership back in the sole hands of Elizabeth May.
Pedneault, a longtime human-rights activist, announced his resignation as the party's deputy leader on Tuesday in a press conference with May in Ottawa, after the party's federal council held an emergency meeting to discuss how to move forward Monday night.
He said it was the "honour of a lifetime" to serve as deputy leader, and pushed back against the notion the delays had anything to do with his resignation.
"I'm not going to discuss the reasons as to why I'm leaving today," he told reporters. "That is for me."
The Green Party has operated under a de-facto co-leadership since November 2022 when May won on a ticket to co-lead with Pedneault in hopes of reviving the party. That followed a tumultuous period that included allegations of racism, antisemitism, misogyny and poor leadership, and led to the ouster of Annamie Paul, the first Black leader of a federal political party.
In that election, members did not vote to change the party's rules to allow co-leadership, meaning May officially remained the sole leader and Pedneault was the deputy leader. Although the pair campaigned on a co-leadership bid, the party's constitution does not allow co-leaders. A potential change to the constitution to permit it was set to be put to a separate vote for the party's grassroots.
But behind the scenes, some party members dissented, the Star reported in May. Some argued that moving to co-leadership would require a new election, while others said members never agreed to co-leadership to begin with. Amid questions about what co-leadership should look like, the official change was delayed several times.
May and Pedneault asserted it was clear from their election platform that the two intended to co-lead. Internal communications obtained by the Star showed Pedneault was frustrated by the delays. He told the Star another election would not be a good use of party resources in the year before a federal election. What the party needed, he said, was stability.
Most recently, that vote was set to occur in a meeting on June 23, but the party delayed it once again - this time for at least 60 days - citing the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection. Christian Cullis, the party's candidate, received 1,057 votes, roughly three per cent.
In an email to party members obtained by the Star, Green Party president Natalie Odd said the delay also gave the party time to gauge members' interest in exploring co-leadership, including through engagement sessions and surveys.
May, who recently turned 70 and has led the party for all but three years since 2006, is one of two Green MPs, alongside Ontario MP Mike Morrice. Pedneault does not hold a seat in Parliament.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: Green Party Leader Elizabeth May speaks as deputy leader Jonathan Pedneault looks on during a news conference about their expectations for the federal budget, in Ottawa on April 15, 2024.

Doug Ford's new map to get around the LCBO strike + unplugging the Port Lands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-new-map-get-around-lcbo-strike/docview/3077141715/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, July 9 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the LCBO strike, progress in the Port Lands and a massive Ticketmaster breach.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford revealed a new road map around the LCBO strike
Looking for alcohol during the LCBO strike? Premier Doug Ford unveiled a new searchable map on Monday marked with breweries, wineries, distilleries and licensed supermarkets across the province. Ford said the map was made "to connect people across the province to local Ontario-made products." But it comes as the province is locked in a stalemate with LCBO workers that has shuttered more than 600 stores. Here's what you need to know about the ongoing strike.
More: The head of the union representing the 9,000 workers on strike said they will stake on the picket lines to combat the Ford government's "privatisation of alcohol sales."How we got here: In late May, Ford announced that beer and wine would be available in Ontario corner stores thanks to a $225-million payout to the Beer Store, liberalizing the booze sales market in the province.Dry summer: It's only day five of the strike, but the union said they can "weather a strike as long as necessary.Toronto's new river valley has been finally been connected to Lake Ontario
The Don River is one step closer to being set free. The west plug in the newly rebuilt river valley was removed piece by piece over the past week to officially connect the area with Lake Ontario through the Polson Slip - a major milestone in the $1.4 billion Port Lands flood protection project. But workers are still weeks away from completely removing the north plug separating the Don River from the new river valley and another to the south along the shipping channel. Here's a peek at the future of Toronto's waterfront.
Step-by-step: The project is meant to protect the Port Lands from flooding while re-naturalizing the mouth of the Don River. The project is one of the largest infrastructure undertakings in North America.History lesson: The Don River used to flow into one of the largest marshes on Lake Ontario, but in the late 1800s the Don was separated and later filled to create a port industrial district.Getting creative: The massive undertaking has tasked the team behind the Port Lands project with some creative problem solving. Here are some of the innovative tools the team used to get to this point.Users' personal data may have been stolen in a Ticketmaster security breach
Gone to a concert recently? You might want to check your Ticketmaster account. The ticket seller emailed customers to notify them that a breach occurred between April 2 and May 18 - and everything from personal contact info to encrypted credit card information could have been stolen. "We are fully committed to protecting your information, and deeply regret that this incident occurred," the company said in a statement. Here's what you need to know about the breach.
Just in case: Ticketmaster is offering identity monitoring with TransUnion, which will look out for personal data on the dark web, and recommends watching your accounts and account statements.Context: Live Nation, Ticketmaster's parent company announced in June that a massive data breach had occurred. Some media reports say a hacking group took responsibility for the hack, and was seeking $500,000 for the millions of people whose names, addresses and card information was accessed.WHAT ELSE

NEW JERSEY: It's game time! Canada has the chance to advance to the Copa America finals but they'll have to make it through a powerhouse team from Argentina, led by soccer superstar Lionel Messi. Canada last played Argentina 19 days ago, in the Copa America opener where Argentina won 2-0. Since then, Canada's players and coach are adamant they are a new team ready to take on Argentina again. Document 364 of 500

City hall spends a staggering amount of time talking about housing. Maybe it's time to cut the chatter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-hall-spends-staggering-amount-time-talking/docview/3077141345/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+hall+spends+a+staggering+amount+of+time+talking+about+housing.+Maybe+it%27s+time+to+cut+the+chatter&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This might sound almost like a parody of government bureaucracy, so I feel a duty to assure you right off the top that this column is not a gag.
Here's the news: having spent the last few months holding a series of public consultations about how to improve the public consultation process, Toronto city hall's planning and housing committee will consider a report this week that recommends more public consultations about public consultations.
"Staff will continue to consult stakeholders on potential policy amendments related to pre-application consultation and community consultation meetings and report back" next spring, concludes the report set to be debated Thursday.
Geez, why rush into things? Why not first hold some consultations about whether to continue to consult about consultations?
OK, now I'm joking. But seriously, this is emblematic of one of the big barriers with getting stuff done at the local level. City hall is straight-up obsessed with consulting the public before things happen.
A lot of the time, it's an understandable obsession because municipal government decisions tend to have a direct impact on the day-to-day lives of residents. If you're going to add a stop sign, install some new playground equipment in a park, or change zoning to allow for a skyscraper, it makes sense to want to get input from people who live nearby.
But it's also an obsession that comes with a cost, both in terms of time and actual, you know, money. The report set to be considered by committee this week indicates the city ran more than 650 public consultation processes last year. Of those, 325 were related to individual development applications. Those development application consultations are the major focus of this report, as recent housing-focused legislation from Premier Doug Ford's government has tried to place limits on development consultations as part of an effort to speed up construction of new units.
Ford's housing legislation has generally been a hash of contradictory half-measures but it's easy to see why there's been a consistent focus on reining in the municipal consultation process. At Toronto city hall, the report says, "staff from all divisions engaged in development review reported spending a cumulative 190 hours on logistics (e.g., scheduling, venue booking, co-ordinating a presentation, preparing materials, etc.) for each community consultation meeting in support of a development application."
Read that again: 190 hours. That means each meeting, which generally runs for a few hours, takes about eight days of staff time to organize. Calculating a specific cost for all that time is challenging because there are a variety of roles involved all making different salaries, but even a lowball estimate puts the cost at thousands of dollars per meeting.
And if I multiply those 190 hours by the reported 325 consultations held last year, I get the truly staggering figure of 61,750 hours spent delivering development-related consultations.
When I ran that number by a city spokesperson, they told me to pump the brakes a little. "The effort estimates are best understood as 'typical'," Deborah Blackstone, senior communications advisor,explained in an email. "So, a direct calculation of 190 hours [multiplied by] 325 meetings likely overstates staff level of effort." Blackstone also added that online consultations - which the city is doing more of lately - generally require less preparation time.
Duly noted, but even if you discount that figure by a few thousand hours, we're still stuck with the conclusion that the city's planning department spends a huge amount of time holding public consultations.
Maybe spending this kind of time and resources would be defensible if the public was left feeling genuinely consulted after attending these public consultations, but a survey run by the city found 62 per cent aren't satisfied with the opportunities to provide feedback, and 73 per cent aren't confident their feedback is even being considered.
To its credit, the consultations-on-consultations report set to be considered this week offers some obvious ideas to fix things, like improving the website that lists information about development applications, redesigning the signs notifying residents of new developments, and posting recordings of all consultations to YouTube.
There are also some novel ideas, like dispensing with individual meetings for each development and instead holding monthly meetings in each ward where people can weigh in on any active development proposals.
More than anything, though, city hall might need to embrace just holding fewer public consultations. Reserve specific public hearings for issues where there is a real opportunity for the public to influence a decision, instead of the current practice of holding a meeting for people to chime in on something that's already a foregone conclusion.
Some frequent meeting-goers may take that as a personal slight. But there are a lot of other people in the city whose most ardent criticism of city hall isn't specific quibbles about any one project, but rather that the government spends far too much time talking about issues before getting anything done. For them, the answer might be a little less consultation - and a little more action.
CAPTION: Each public consultation meeting on a housing development in the city takes roughly eight days of staff time to organize.

City's public consultations demand a lot

Some frequent meeting-goers may take that as a personal slight. But there are a lot of other people in the city whose most ardent criticism of city hall isn't specific quibbles about any one project, but rather that the government spends far too much time talking about issues before getting anything done. For them, the answer might be a little less consultation - and a little more action.

Document 366 of 500

Ford touts online map of booze purveyors: As LCBO strike continues, premier releases promo clip

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-touts-online-map-booze-purveyors/docview/3076928193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+touts+online+map+of+booze+purveyors%3A+As+LCBO+strike+continues%2C+premier+releases+promo+clip&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has a new road map to navigate the strike that has closed hundreds of LCBO stores across the province.
Ford on Monday unveiled a new searchable online map, "where to buy alcoholic beverages," designed to help consumers find their favourite beer, wine, cider and spirits.
"Even though LCBO workers are on strike, there's still plenty of options for you to buy beer, wine, cider, coolers - and even spirits," the premier said in a 90-second promotional video that begins with him flipping burgers on a gas barbecue surrounded by cans of Ontario craft brews.
"We've created this handy new map that shows what stores are open and what they sell," Ford, who does not drink alcohol or eat red meat, said as he demonstrates the map on a MacBook laptop.
"Say you want to buy some Ontario craft beer, just zoom in on where you are, click the filter for beer - and look at all the fantastic options that pop up," he said.
In a statement, Ford said the goal is "to connect people across the province to local Ontario-made products and support the hundreds of Ontario businesses and thousands of Ontario workers who make these products and serve customers each and every day."
"It also supports our plan to offer consumers the kind of choice and convenience available to other Canadians when purchasing alcoholic beverages, starting later this summer," he added.
"I hope people across the province will take this chance to shop local and responsibly enjoy some great Ontario-made products."
His move comes after more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union hit the bricks after midnight Friday in the first strike in the history of the Liquor Control Board of Ontario.
According to OPSEU president JP Hornick, the workers are striking because of Ford's "plan to try and expand privatization of alcohol sales in such a way to reduce the meaningfulness of the LCBO and the work that we do."
"That puts every Ontarian at risk," Hornick told about 80 pickets outside the LCBO store near Bay and Bloor Streets on Saturday.
There are about 680 LCBO outlets across the province that are currently shuttered.
Ford's map includes hundreds of Ontario breweries, wineries, wine shops and distilleries as well as 449 licensed supermarkets, 422 Beer Store outlets and 389 rural LCBO agency stores in cottage country and other remote areas.
In May, the premier announced the Progressive Conservative government would pay the foreign-owned Beer Store $225 million to expedite the liberalization of booze sales more than a year ahead of the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025, deadline.
That means beer, wine and premixed cocktails in corner stores by Labour Day with the fully liberalized system in place around Oct. 31.
Under the changes, the Crown-owned LCBO would maintain its monopoly on spirits sales such as whisky, gin, vodka and rum.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has warned Ford's decision could actually cost taxpayers $1 billion and said it contributed to the LCBO strike.
"Doug Ford's billion-dollar 'boozedoggle' is now failing 10,000 hardworking employees at the LCBO by putting their jobs at risk," Crombie said Friday.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "nobody wants a dry summer," but blamed the Tories for bungling the booze file.
"The LCBO brings in billions to our community every year that then goes on to fund services like health care and education. Ford's privatization experiments are going to cost the province good jobs and billions in revenue for public services," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the "chaos in the beverage industry is entirely the Ford government's doing - and now Ontarians are the ones paying for it."
While Ford's government changed licensing rules during the COVID-19 pandemic to allow more than 19,000 bars and restaurants to sell bottles and cans to go, those pubs and eateries are excluded from the interactive map.
Nor does it feature popular independent wine shops like Grape Witches on Dundas Street West or Paris Paris on Ossington Avenue that emerged during and after the pandemic.
Specialty boutiques like Eataly in the Manulife Centre, which boasts one of the largest selections of Italian wines in Canada, are also not on the government map.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 367 of 500

LCBO workers face an uphill battle

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-workers-face-uphill-battle/docview/3076926839/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+face+an+uphill+battle&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In case you hadn't noticed, most of the LCBO is closed for business.
In midsummer, in the middle of a heat wave, some 680 stores are shuttered by a strike - have been since last week.
What's a parched drinker to do? Where's a busy party planner to go?
Don't panic. But spare a thought, if not a drop, for the future of the LCBO - and its workers.
As Doug Ford boasted this week, there is no shortage of alternative locations to get what you need in this post-Prohibition province. In a video released Monday, the teetotalling premier clutches a can of craft beer after opening his laptop to show you precisely how to search an online map for alcohol sales near you.
Provocative, to be sure. But that's Ontario's new reality.
In summers past, the stakes were so much higher. The media whipped Ontarians into a frenzy of panic-buying at the mere mention of a possible strike.
Front page headlines counted down the days to the bargaining deadline. Drinkers lined up around the block to stock up in case of a shutdown.
Until recently, the LCBO held a virtual monopoly on wine and spirits sales. Which is why the government of the day paid higher wages to avoid leaving Ontarians high and dry.
Times change. Now, people can get high on legal cannabis, visit 389 rural LCBO agency stores, check out what's left of the Beer Store, shop for beer and wine at hundreds of supermarkets, try thousands of local bars or restaurants for takeout booze, or order online.
Times keep changing. Within months, pre-mixed coolers are coming, and beer will pop up on even more shelves in thousands of convenience stores.
Which is why the Ontario Public Service Employees Union has gone on strike. Not just for more money, but more job security for its members - and less availability for drinkers.
At the very time the LCBO is losing market share, and its workers are losing bargaining leverage, OPSEU has walked off the job in hopes of saving jobs. Union president JP Hornick argues the premier looms like a ghost at the table, diluting the LCBO's clout by allowing more booze to be sold by more competitors across the province.
There is some truth to that analysis. But trying to persuade Ontarians they should go back in time, to a time of fewer outlets, fewer choices and fewer hard coolers?
That's a hard sell. Will Ontarians buy it?
Hornick has made the fight both political and personal as OPSEU tries to win the "hearts and minds" of drinkers with established tastes. The union leader blames Ford for weakening the LCBO through a phantom privatization, thus undermining the multibillion-dollar revenue stream that underwrites hospitals and social services.
I argued long ago for beer (and wine) in supermarkets, on the grounds that the privately owned Beer Store and the government-owned LCBO were in an unholy duopoly. I never bought into Ford's crusade for convenience stores, but he has won two elections and never made a secret of his obsessions as he positions himself for a third campaign.
While I criticized the Beer Store for dilapidated facilities and declining service, I've always defended the LCBO as an imperfect but improvable government enterprise with economies of scale and knowledgeable staff. But it's hard to argue, in a time of retail revolutions, that you can put the toothpaste back in the tube.
We no longer trek to central government post offices now that pharmacy kiosks stay open late, and we no longer believe internet gambling can be stopped. The Beer Store was an anachronism and the LCBO cannot be frozen in time.
One person's privatization is another drinker's decentralization. One public sector union's fight to keep its membership numbers high is another private sector union's opportunity to sign up more supermarket workers selling booze (corner stores will remain a side show).
It's hard to fathom the people of Ontario rallying in support of the union's top demand - banning pre-mixed cocktails in supermarkets. Just as it's hard to imagine them rioting over the closure of LCBO stores throughout the summer, as they might have decades ago.
OPSEU boasts that it can dig into a deep strike fund and is digging in its heels. But the government is pointing the way to alternative outlets.
Many of the LCBO's newer staff have to work long and hard to get benefits and decent shifts - the myth of overpaid stock clerks is increasingly outdated. But like retail workers everywhere, they will find it a hard battle to fight and win.
WestJet's vital maintenance workers brought the airline to its knees by striking hard on the Canada Day holiday weekend - maximizing their bargaining leverage. Days later, LCBO staff are having far less impact (much like the TVO workers who went off the air for months last year).
That's the reality of labour relations - it's not just about justice, but who has the power. And the power of persuasion.
CAPTION: While the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, which represents LCBO workers, is trying to protect jobs, the strike appears to be trying to persuade Ontarians they should go back to fewer outlets and fewer choices, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Privatization; Online gambling; Beer; Liquor; Convenience stores; Strikes; Bargaining; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Convenience stores Strikes Bargaining Supermarkets

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Ontario Public Service Employees Union; NAICS: 813930; Name: Liquor Control Board-Ontario Canada; NAICS: 445320, 926150

Title: LCBO workers face an uphill battle

Doug Ford reveals new road map around the LCBO strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-reveals-new-road-map-around-lcbo-strike/docview/3077414098/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+reveals+new+road+map+around+the+LCBO+strike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has a new road map to navigate the strike that has closed hundreds of LCBO stores across the province.

"It also supports our plan to offer consumers the kind of choice and convenience available to other Canadians when purchasing alcoholic beverages, starting later this summer," he added."
"I hope people across the province will take this chance to shop local and responsibly enjoy some great Ontario-made products!"
His move comes after more than 9,000 Ontario Public Service Employees Union hit the bricks after midnight Friday in the first strike in the history of the Liquor Control Board of Ontario.
According to OPSEU president JP Hornick, the workers are striking because of Ford's "plan to try and expand privatization of alcohol sales in such a way to reduce the meaningfulness of the LCBO and the work that we do."
"That puts every Ontarian at risk," Hornick told about 80 pickets outside the LCBO store near Bay and Bloor Streets on Saturday.
There are about 680 LCBO outlets across the province that are currently shuttered.
Ford's map includes hundreds of Ontario breweries, wineries, wine shops and distilleries as well as 449 licensed supermarkets, 422 Beer Store outlets and 389 rural LCBO agency stores in cottage country and other remote areas.
In May, the premier announced the Progressive Conservative government would pay the foreign-owned Beer Store $225 million to expedite the liberalization of booze sales more than a year ahead of the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline.
That means beer, wine and premixed cocktails in corner stores by Labour Day with the fully liberalized system in place around Oct. 31.
Under the changes, the Crown-owned LCBO would maintain its monopoly on spirits sales such as whisky, gin, vodka and rum.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has warned Ford's decision could actually cost taxpayers $1 billion and said it contributed to the LCBO strike.
"Doug Ford's billion-dollar 'boozedoggle' is now failing 10,000 hardworking employees at the LCBO by putting their jobs at risk," Crombie said Friday.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "nobody wants a dry summer," but blamed the Tories for bungling the booze file.
"The LCBO brings in billions to our community every year that then goes on to fund services like health care and education. Ford's privatization experiments are going to cost the province good jobs and billions in revenue for public services," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the "chaos in the beverage industry is entirely the Ford government's doing - and now Ontarians are the ones paying for it."
While Ford's government changed licensing rules during the COVID-19 pandemic to allow more than 19,000 bars and restaurants to sell bottles and cans to go, those pubs and eateries are excluded from the interactive map.
Nor does it feature popular independent wine shops like Grape Witches on Dundas Street West or Paris Paris on Ossington Avenue that emerged during and after the pandemic.
Specialty boutiques like Eataly in the Manulife Centre, which boasts one of the largest selections of Italian wines in Canada, are also not on the government map.
The Tories insisted it would continue to be updated "as alcohol sales are expanded to more licensed grocery and convenience stores. As LCBO retail stores reopen, these will also be added to the map."
"Ontario can rest assured there are more options to purchase than ever before, thanks to the government's bold plan to modernize alcohol sales," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a convenience store in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Alcohol; Privatization; Beer; Liquor; Convenience stores; Alcoholic beverages; Pandemics; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Convenience stores Alcoholic beverages Breweries

Title: Doug Ford reveals new road map around the LCBO strike

Document 369 of 500

LCBO workers are on strike. Has anyone noticed?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-workers-are-on-strike-has-anyone-noticed/docview/3077410792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+are+on+strike.+Has+anyone+noticed%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In case you hadn't noticed, most of the LCBO is closed for business.
In midsummer, in the middle of a heat wave, some 680 stores are shuttered by a strike - have been since last week.
What's a parched drinker to do? Where's a busy party planner to go?
Don't panic. But spare a thought, if not a drop, for the future of the LCBO - and its workers.
As Doug Ford boasted this week, there is no shortage of alternative locations to get what you need in this post-Prohibition province. In a video released Monday, the teetotalling premier clutches a can of craft beer after opening his laptop to show you precisely how to search an online map for alcohol sales near you.
Provocative, to be sure. But that's Ontario's new reality.
In summers past, the stakes were so much higher. The media whipped Ontarians into a frenzy of panic-buying at the mere mention of a possible strike.
Front page headlines counted down the days to the bargaining deadline. Drinkers lined up around the block to stock up in case of a shutdown.
Until recently, the LCBO held a virtual monopoly on wine and spirits sales. Which is why the government of the day paid higher wages to avoid leaving Ontarians high and dry.
Times change. Now, people can get high on legal cannabis, visit 389 rural LCBO agency stores, check out what's left of the Beer Store, shop for beer and wine at hundreds of supermarkets, try thousands of local bars or restaurants for takeout booze, or order online.
Times keep changing. Within months, pre-mixed coolers are coming, and beer will pop up on even more shelves in thousands of convenience stores.
Which is why the Ontario Public Service Employees Union has gone on strike. Not just for more money, but more job security for its members - and less availability for drinkers.
At the very time the LCBO is losing market share, and its workers are losing bargaining leverage, OPSEU has walked off the job in hopes of saving jobs. Union president JP Hornick argues the premier looms like a ghost at the table, diluting the LCBO's clout by allowing more booze to be sold by more competitors across the province.
There is some truth to that analysis. But trying to persuade Ontarians they should go back in time, to a time of fewer outlets, fewer choices and fewer hard coolers?
That's a hard sell. Will Ontarians buy it?
Hornick has made the fight both political and personal as OPSEU tries to win the "hearts and minds" of drinkers with established tastes. The union leader blames Ford for weakening the LCBO through a phantom privatization, thus undermining the multibillion-dollar revenue stream that underwrites hospitals and social services.
I argued long ago for beer (and wine) in supermarkets, on the grounds that the privately owned Beer Store and the government-owned LCBO were in an unholy duopoly. I never bought into Ford's crusade for convenience stores, but he has won two elections and never made a secret of his obsessions as he positions himself for a third campaign.
While I criticized the Beer Store for dilapidated facilities and declining service, I've always defended the LCBO as an imperfect but improvable government enterprise with economies of scale and knowledgeable staff. But it's hard to argue, in a time of retail revolutions, that you can put the toothpaste back in the tube.
We no longer trek to central government post offices now that pharmacy kiosks stay open late, and we no longer believe internet gambling can be stopped. The Beer Store was an anachronism and the LCBO cannot be frozen in time.
One person's privatization is another drinker's decentralization. One public sector union's fight to keep its membership numbers high is another private sector union's opportunity to sign up more supermarket workers selling booze (corner stores will remain a side show).
It's hard to fathom the people of Ontario rallying in support of the union's top demand - banning pre-mixed cocktails in supermarkets. Just as it's hard to imagine them rioting over the closure of LCBO stores throughout the summer, as they might have decades ago.
OPSEU boasts that it can dig into a deep strike fund and is digging in its heels. But the government is pointing the way to alternative outlets.
Many of the LCBO's newer staff have to work long and hard to get benefits and decent shifts - the myth of overpaid stock clerks is increasingly outdated. But like retail workers everywhere, they will find it a hard battle to fight and win.
WestJet's vital maintenance workers brought the airline to its knees by striking hard on the Canada Day holiday weekend - maximizing their bargaining leverage. Days later, LCBO staff are having far less impact (much like the TVO workers who went off the air for months last year).
That's the reality of labour relations - it's not just about justice, but who has the power. And the power of persuasion.
CAPTION: Striking LCBO workers picket in downtown Toronto on July 6, 2024.

Subject: Privatization; Online gambling; Liquor; Beer; Convenience stores; Strikes; Supermarkets; Bargaining

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Convenience stores Strikes Supermarkets Bargaining

Title: LCBO workers are on strike. Has anyone noticed?

Publication date: Jul 8, 2024

Document 370 of 500

Queer rights are in danger in Canada. Pierre Poilievre owns some of the blame

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/queer-rights-are-danger-canada-pierre-poilievre/docview/3076779262/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Queer+rights+are+in+danger+in+Canada.+Pierre+Poilievre+owns+some+of+the+blame&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, Ipsos has been asking individuals around the world for their opinions on Queer people. And for years, the pollster has found, those opinions have steadily improved. People had been growing more and more supportive of the LGBTQ community with each passing year, especially in Canada.
In 2011, more than six-in-ten Canadians said people should be open about their sexual orientation and gender identity; even more voiced support for anti-discrimination laws; more, still, endorsed Queer couples adopting children.
But this year, those trends suddenly reversed.
Ipsos's 2024 survey found a 12 point drop in the number of Canadians who support Queer people being open and visible in society. That puts us on par with Poland and the United States. Support has plummeted across the board for Queer people holding hands in public, for anti-discrimination protections, and for healthcare for trans people.
The protest and revelry of Pride month is over. Now we need to take stock of why support for our community has plunged. To me, it's no great mystery: Conservative politicians in this country, and around the world, have been attacking the LGBTQ community and they've radicalized their supporters in the process. We know what happens if this doesn't stop. We've seen it before. Our rights get repealed. Anti-Queer violence climbs. Our hard-won progress bleeds away.
Playing politics with trans rights Anti-Queer rhetoric has gotten very bad, very fast.
When he first ran for the White House, Donald Trump was downright liberal on LGBTQ issues. Today, he bloviates about "left-wing gender insanity," wants to ban all transgender healthcare, and surrounds himself with militant activists who smear all Queer people as "groomers."
Here in Canada, some conservatives have waded into the same waters. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has imposed a ban on gender-affirming healthcare for younger teens, over the objections of many parents. Saskatchewan and New Brunswick premiers Scott Moe and Blaine Higgs have forbidden students from using their preferred pronouns or given names without parental permission.
Last year, members of the Conservative Party of Canada adopted policy positions meant to "protect our kids" by forbidding trans healthcare for minors and dictating that certain spaces be sex-specific, forcing trans people in the wrong bathrooms, for example.
For a minute, Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre seemed to resist going down this road. Last year, I quizzed a high-ranking Conservative Member of Parliament about Poilievre's intentions. Poilievre was never going to drape himself in a trans flag, the MP assured me, but he had no plans to embrace transphobic policies.
"They know (doing that would) fuel hatred," that MP told me. They did it anyway.
Earlier this year, I asked Poilievre his position on banning healthcare for trans youth. He didn't answer. Instead, he accused me of "spreading disinformation," and of refusing to discuss the actual policies in question. (His handlers snatched the microphone away before I could respond.) He ranted that "Justin Trudeau has spread hatred against parents."
A day later, Poilievre went even further than Smith, calling for a total ban on puberty blockers and hormones for those under 18. "I think we should protect children," Poilievre told reporters. From whom, he didn't say.
Deciphering Poilievre's tactics is not difficult. The Conservative Party, under its previous leader, Erin O'Toole, had tried to cement itself as solidly pro-LGBTQ, even helping to speed through a ban on conversion therapy. Looking back, conservative organizers I've spoken to now believe that was a mistake. They think it alienated a rump of their core supporters, pushing them into the arms of Maxime Bernier. Winning them back, Poilievre's team thinks, means parroting their concerns.
History repeats itselfTrans people have seen this pattern before. "We go through these cycles of reappearing in media that go back to the beginning of newspapers," Morgan M. Page, an excellent Queer historian, told me recently. "There's never been a 10 year period in the history of newspapers in the English language where trans people have not become a focus of attention."
You can go back at least to the 1920s, when Berlin doctor Magnus Hirschfeld was doing pioneering work on sexuality and genders. His motto, "through science to justice," led to the founding of an institute for the study of gender identity and some of the earliest gender confirmation procedures.
But amid economic turmoil and strife, the backlash began. The Nazis targeted Hirschfeld, alleging he was part of a Jewish conspiracy to encourage degeneracy. Hitler Youth raided his institute and burned his research in the street. But it was the conservative Catholic establishment, pressed by the far-right, that declared the nascent LGBTQ community "dangerous" and sent police to crack down. Hirschfeld died in exile.
This same cycle recurred again and again throughout the 20th century. When some of the earliest Queer organizing secured a key anti-discrimination ordinance in Miami in the 1970s, a former orange juice pitchwoman named Anita Bryant fought to have it repealed. Crying "save our children," she rallied the people to vote it down by a two-to-one margin.
It happened in Canada, too. In the 1990s, Ontario had been a world leader in gender-affirming care - 'sex change operations,' as they were called then. But a newly-elected Mike Harris abruptly delisted the procedures from the provincial health plan. "I came out in the 10 year period when it was not covered," Page said. "That was a very tricky time."
It would take until 2008 for Premier Dalton McGuinty to reverse the change and cover the surgeries again. Back then, a 28-year-old Pierre Poilievre was already serving his second term in Parliament. He went on the warpath, vowing to lobby his government to ensure that "federal health transfers should go only to vital health care treatments and not to the McGuinty sex-change program."
Today, Poilievre is just rehashing the same tired tactics.
A vortex of conspiracy and rageFor decades, the LGBTQ community has made progress by lauding supportive politicians, cajoling the skeptical, and admonishing the moral crusaders and charlatans. Doing that requires a real diversity of tactics: Rioting, protesting, lobbying, and running for office.
But today, as Page puts it, there is an online "vortex" of conspiratorial thought targeting Queer people. Now the LGBTQ movement doesn't just need to convince, we need to pull people out of the whirlpool. Poilievre and his ilk are making that much harder to do.
Politicians have enabled this toxicity by actively misrepresenting the issue, claiming children are being hurt or indoctrinated by health professionals. Major medical associations in Canada and the United States continue to support this healthcare and oppose the kind of bans Smith has imposed (and Poilievre supports.) There is ample study and debate happening amongst the experts, some politicians just don't care.
In recent years in Canada, we have seen a marked rise in what political scientists call "affective polarization." Our divides are increasingly not driven by genuine policy concerns or honest debate, but by rabid partisanship and culture wars. Poilievre has helped turn opposing Queer rights into a partisan merit badge. It has increasingly become something his supporters wear, with pride, as a token of group identity.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has enabled this same process from the other side. By constantly smearing the Conservative Party as anti-LGBTQ - even when politicians like O'Toole were desperately trying to moderate - he drew a line in the sand. Far from helping the Queer community, Trudeau convinced many Conservatives they had nothing to gain by embracing Queer rights.
Conservatives may be driving the bandwagon, but this backlash is bigger than any one party or politician. This culture war has now unleashed irate parents who are flooding school boards and city council meetings, alleging a Queer plot to indoctrinate children. They have been sucked into the vortex, and now want books banned and Queer teachers drummed out of schools.
Some Conservatives will try and spin this trend away. They oscillate between blaming Trudeau's "wokeness" and deflecting responsibility for the slide in LGBTQ support onto new immigrants. One Queer Tory, however, told me that the Conservatives know full well what they're doing. But, "no one will contradict the leader explicitly or flirt with the line that may be considered contradicting," they said. So the Queer people in the party sit idly by as their leader sings Anita Byrant karaoke.
We often talk about the history of the LGBTQ community as a very straight line, from obscurity to acceptance. In fact, it has been a twisting and curving road, a history of progress and retreat. Right now, in Canada, the backlash is on.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has helped fuel a backlash against Queer rights in Canada, according to Star columnist Justin Ling.Toronto Star contributing columnist Justin Ling.

Subject: Bans; Conservatism; Indoctrination; Political parties; LGBTQ community; Conspiracy; Partisanship; Gender identity; Politicians; Gender-affirming care

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Ipsos; NAICS: 541910

Title: Queer rights are in danger in Canada. Pierre Poilievre owns some of the blame

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jul 8, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3076779262

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/queer-rights-are-danger-canada-pierre-poilievre/docview/3076779262/se-2?accountid=15090
