Document 372 of 500

Do Canadians want more or less government in their lives?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/do-canadians-want-more-less-government-their/docview/3076421803/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Do+Canadians+want+more+or+less+government+in+their+lives%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Susan Delacourt: Just before the long Canada Day weekend, Matt, you wrote a piece that has stayed with me, even if it wasn't exactly a rallying, hooray-for-Canada tale. You wrote that you're worried about people giving up on this country because many things, big or small, seem to be too hard for governments to do competently. For those who haven't read the piece already (and really, they should) do you want to give us a quick recap of why you felt the need to write it?
Matt Gurney: I mean, I mainly write because if I don't write I go crazy and make everyone around me miserable. Writing just makes some of the readers miserable, which is (for me) a huge upgrade. But the column I wrote was a product of a few things. And I admit they're mostly anecdotal. I know the quip that the plural of anecdote is not "data." But when I get whacked over the head repeatedly with something, I start to pay attention. And over the last few years, I've come to suspect that people are leaving Canada, for economic or sometimes (though less often) political reasons, or, interestingly, to access health care they can't reliably get at home. To be clear, I don't think this is a huge number of people, and I 100 per cent understand that for many, Canada is still the promised land. But I think that some of those with means are either leaving outright (this was something my colleague Jen Gerson wrote about recently) or are at least coming up with a Plan B - a backup location where they have a "vacation" property, a bank account, a health plan and maybe residency rights. I stress again that I don't think this is a huge number of people. But I think it might be a lopsided amount of money and talent, both of which Canada badly needs. And for what it's worth, I was reading about something similar just a few days ago right here in the Toronto Star.
SD: It is undeniably true that all kinds of government services to citizens are stretched, especially coming out of the pandemic. But it got me thinking about the whole notion of activist government and the weird place we find ourselves in North America. If polls are correct, a lot of Canadians and Americans are flirting with hard-right conservatism. At the same time, maybe as a product of the pandemic, the public seems to want more activist government. They want fixers in power. I wonder how this can be reconciled with politics that used to be all about getting the government out of people's lives?
MG: I don't know how hard-right Canadian conservatives will get. Will they be hard-right enough to fund the military, end supply management and open up domestic markets to foreign competition? Or will they mainly just tweet meanly? In any case, I sort of view the problem from the opposite perspective: I think a problem more progressive-leaning governments have, including very much our incumbent federal government, is that they want full value for their proposals. "We are proposing to do X, aren't we good people for doing X?" The problem is, me and about 41 million of my buddies have noticed what you very charitably described as stretched government services. I've put it more bluntly: things are literally and visibly falling apart. And every time a government proposes to do a new thing, I'm not bowled over with gratitude, I'm suspicious they're offering me something new to distract me from the old stuff that's breaking.
SD: I too would be grateful, if only out of exasperation with talking points, if all politicians stopped telling me the government has my back. But clichés are built around ideas that are commonplace. There is an assumption now that the public needs to be assured constantly that government is there to meet an ever-expanding list of citizens' needs. It's something that Pierre Poilievre, if he does become prime minister, is going to have to grapple with, as you point out with those examples of military spending and supply management. Poilievre grew up in the old Reform Party, when then leader Preston Manning was repeating the old joke about the scariest words in the English language: "I'm from the government and I'm here to help." I'm not sure that joke would get the same laughs now.
MG: I'm not the first person to note this, so give me no credit. But what we need - desperately - is a government that's focused on competency in service delivery and policy execution. I don't think anyone suggests we have that today. So a political party that was based around the notion of "Make Government Competent Again" would, I think, be popular. And that's non-partisan. That could mean more taxes to better fund a big government. It could mean lower taxes to sustain a smaller one. I don't care! Just make me an offer. But we won't get that. We'll get social media wedges and performative grandstanding. And I think we'll keep getting what we started talking about earlier: a slow trickle of at least money and business activity out of Canada, into jurisdictions where you can buy with cash what governments in Canada can no longer provide.
SD: Competence, one assumes, should be the low bar. But I guess what I'm saying is that I think citizens have to do some hard thinking too about wants and needs. We're in a housing mess now in part because successive governments of all stripes got out of that business and now everyone wants the government back in. It would be great to have an election debate that got voters assessing just how much they want activist government.
MG: You're right that competence should be the low bar. But it isn't. Even talking about it feels like a journey into "if wishes were horses" territory. I'd love a debate about what kind of government we want. I'd love a whole election about that. But everything I've seen in recent years tells me what we'll get is this: a variety of options all basically offering us the same dysfunction, but in a variety of "Don't you just hate those other bastards?" flavours. No one votes for competency. We vote to punish those that annoy or terrify us.
CAPTION: There is no denying that all kinds of government services, including health care, are stretched and appear to falling apart, Susan Delacourt and Matt Gurney write.hat WEBhat PRINT

'This is our line in the sand': Job action enters its second day with workers and union leaders taking to downtown streets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-is-our-line-sand/docview/3076368357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+our+line+in+the+sand%27%3A+Job+action+enters+its+second+day+with+workers+and+union+leaders+taking+to+downtown+streets&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO workers took to picket lines for the second day of a province-wide strike on Saturday.
"This is our line in the sand and we are making history," said Ontario Public Service Employees Union president JP Hornick at a news conference and rally Saturday afternoon. They were supported by a crowd of approximately 80 people gathered at the picket line at the corner of Bay and Bloor Streets.
At midnight on Friday, more than 9,000 LCBO employees hit picket lines after contract negotiations broke down, demanding, among other things, job security in light of the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.
Last year, Premier Doug Ford announced that the province would allow the sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocers by 2026, starting this September.
This is the first time LCBO staff have gone on strike in the crown corporation's more than 100-year existence.
"We are here today because of the Ford government's plan to try and expand privatization of alcohol sales in such a way to reduce the meaningfulness of the LCBO and the work that we do. That puts every Ontarian at risk," Hornick said to the crowd, who responded with applause.
The first step of the expansion plan is set to start in less than a month, when grocery stores that already sell beer and wine will be able to sell ready-to-drink cocktails. Convenience stores can start selling beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails on Sept. 5. The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario said Friday it has already issued 2,813 convenience store licences.
OPSEU is also advocating for wage increases and more full-time positions, saying 70 per cent of members currently hold part-time and casual roles.
The LCBO said about half of casual workers are guaranteed at least 1,000 working hours a year. It said its most recent proposal responded to a number of the workers' demands but the union did not make a counter-offer.
"We remain hopeful that we can quickly reach an agreement that is fair to our employees, while enabling the LCBO's continued success in a changing marketplace," the LCBO said in an emailed statement Saturday.
According to OPSEU, the LCBO brings $2.5 billion that goes toward funding health care, education and infrastructure. The board also provides good jobs province-wide and urged people to shop for their alcohol only at the public outlet.
"For every drink that you buy, that is outside of an LCBO you are reducing good jobs in your communities. The cost of convenience should not be here shutting down or schools falling apart," said Hornick.
The rally began at 12:45 p.m. and shortly after, there was a brief downpour of rain.
"Ford can send the rain, but we'll still be here, am I right?" NDP Leader Marit Stiles said when she took the mic at a press conference held at the rally. The crowd responded with a loud "Yes!"
"I'm here to stand with the workers, and let them know that I think most Ontarians are standing with them, (and) understand why they're out right now and want Doug Ford to do the right thing, go back to the table and get a deal," Stiles said in an interview with the Star.
LCBO staff are striking for better working conditions and as an effort to maintain the public service that the LCBO offers by putting revenues into health care and education, Stiles said.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy issued a statement on Friday saying the government is "more committed than ever" to its plan.
"We are particularly disappointed that OPSEU is opposed to giving people in Ontario the choice and convenience of buying ready-made drinks, like coolers and seltzers, in grocery and convenience stores," he wrote.
Following Stiles and Hornick, three other union leaders delivered messages of support
"We will be at your distribution centres. We're going to have people on the ground, we're going to stop those trucks from going in and we're going to slow the process down every step of the way. Transit workers have your back," said Amalgamated Transit Union president John Di Nino.
"We will be loud and we will be clear and we will not let (Ford) get away with this," said Jason MacLean, Secretary-Treasurer of the National Union of Public and General Employees spoke at the press conference.
"When you come for OPSEU, you come for all of us," said Andria Babbington, President of the Toronto & York Region Labour Council.
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: LCBO workers picketed and held a rally in downtown Toronto on Saturday. More than 9,000 employees have been on strike since Friday.Steve RussellToronto Star
CREDIT: Morgan Sevareid-Bocknek Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

Document 374 of 500

Flight from justice: After the bloody murder came a panicked getaway across Ontario and a pursuit over three European countries, ending in capture. In the second of three parts, the Hamilton Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly retraces the fugitives' steps

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/flight-justice/docview/3076368333/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Flight+from+justice%3A+After+the+bloody+murder+came+a+panicked+getaway+across+Ontario+and+a+pursuit+over+three+European+countries%2C+ending+in+capture.+In+the+second+of+three+parts%2C+the+Hamilton+Spectator%27s+Nicole+O%27Reilly+retraces+the+fugitives%27+steps&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tyler Pratt and Jordyn Romano expected to meet their friends for a routine real-estate inspection in Hamilton, and a dinner together afterward at a nice restaurant in Oakville.
Their friends, Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li, planned something more sinister.
When Pratt and Romano arrived in their usual white Range Rover, Karafa and Li were already there in a car Pratt and Romano didn't recognize, a white Audi.
The two vehicles parked facing each other at the back of an industrial building at 347 Arvin Ave. in Stoney Creek.
Where's the realtor? Pratt asked.
They were expecting a man named Michael to show them around, but he was nowhere to be seen. What's more, they were locked out of the building.
Li was sent to look for a lockbox; she didn't find one - that's because no one had actually made plans to view the property.
Romano, who was 13 weeks pregnant and cold, returned to their Range Rover to warm up. Li was directed to join her while the guys talked.
Romano was dressed up, in a tan sweater and thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots. She believed the foursome was going out for dinner at the restaurant Soto Soto in Oakville afterward. It was strange, she thought, that Li - usually impeccably dressed - was in sweats. She "showed up in rags," Romano said.
Romano and Pratt had known Karafa about four months, since they started investing in one of his businesses. Over the preceding months, they met often. Pratt and Karafa spoke almost daily. Romano believed the two couples had become good friends.
Indeed, Pratt and Romano bought Karafa a diamond chain as a gift for his birthday a week earlier. And two days earlier, Karafa messaged them about taking a trip to Italy in June. Li spoke to Romano about her upcoming baby shower.
But inside the car that day, something seemed off.
Li was "nervously texting," Romano said. "She comes in the car and couldn't even look at me."
When Li got out of the car, Romano slid over into the driver's seat.
Treachery revealed
Her window was down when she looked up and froze. Karafa, his face shaking, was pointing a gun at her.
"Get out of here, Jo," is all Romano could remember him saying before she was shot. Panicked, she reversed her car and crashed into something. She heard Pratt saying, "What the f-k, bro," before more gunshots.
More than an hour later, about 7:20 p.m., a passing motorist - Annette Bright - found Romano crawling on the road in front of 347 Arvin Ave. Police later found Pratt's body lying on the cold ground behind the property.
Both cars, damaged in the chaos, were gone. The Audi was found abandoned nearby parked at Green Road and South Service Road.
Li and Karafa fled in Romano's Range Rover. Karafa - who was prohibited from driving after being convicted of impaired driving causing death years earlier - had to hold the door closed as they drove on the highway.
They dumped the vehicle near Mimico GO Station in Etobicoke. Li says Karafa later told her he also dumped the gun somewhere near there, but it was never found.
Karafa arranged for a guy he met through a Kijiji ad, named Mohamad Morad, to buy both vehicles for $2,000, strip them for parts and ship them out of the country. But police found the vehicles first.
As Romano fought for her life, Karafa and Li took a taxi back to their condo on Erskine Avenue, near Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue in Toronto.
Before the shooting, they had switched cellphone SIM cards on one of their phones with Phi Chinh - an unsuspecting friend of Karafa's - and then switched them back later that evening.
When data from cell towers was inspected later, it appeared as if Li and Karafa stayed home, while Chinh travelled to the shooting scene. Indeed, Chinh was later arrested for murder. When he explained to police what actually happened, he was let go.
Back at their condo, Karafa and Li tried to get some rest, but early the next morning they saw an article about the shooting in The Hamilton Spectator: one male was dead and one female critically injured.
Now they had a problem. Romano could identify them.
They thought their careful planning would allow them to continue their lives without paying Pratt and Romano back. Pratt and Romano had invested nearly $500,000 in a business of Karafa's selling medical supplies in Europe. Karafa had promised big profits but the couple had not been repaid a penny.
Now, with a witness, they had to run.
On the lam
Surveillance video shows Karafa, carrying a garbage bag, and Li, carrying a UPS envelope, leaving their condo shortly before 3 a.m. on March 1. They drove Li's Mercedes north to Muskoka, stopping at the Glen Orchard General Store near Port Carling, which also includes a gas station, restaurant, tackle shop and other properties off Butterfly Lake.
When the store's general manager, Charlene Smith, arrived at work around 6:30 a.m. on March 1, she noticed the vehicle in the parking lot. She opened the store at 7 a.m. and, about an hour later, saw an attractive young couple come in to use the washroom.
They bought some drinks and left in a taxi.
Chris Whittingham, who owns the Glen Orchard store, didn't think much of the abandoned Mercedes until his daughter found a garbage bag in the recycling bin filled with 40 nine-millimetre bullets, a blond wig, clothing and other suspicious items. He called police.
Ontario Provincial Police documented the evidence found in the bag, including a pair of Li's pants with blood drops and a phone charger with blood on it. The key fob to an Audi was also found in the bag.
Inside the abandoned Mercedes, police found more clothing and items, including the UPS folder that contained documents from the life-insurance scheme. Before the shooting, Romano and Pratt believed Li was finalizing a $1.5-million life-insurance policy that would help them transfer some of the money they were owed from Europe; Li later admitted she never intended to file the application and only pretended to go along with it to buy Karafa more time.
By the time police found the evidence in Muskoka, Li and Karafa were long gone.
Sandy Sethi, the taxi driver, was going to drop them off at the bus station in Barrie, but the couple changed their plans and she drove them to Toronto. She dropped them off at a subway station, earning $400 for the trip.
Li and Karafa then travelled to Union Station, where their movements could be traced on surveillance video buying tickets and then boarding a train to Montreal. In Montreal, they sat silently on a shuttle bus that took them to Montreal-Trudeau International Airport. They arrived at 4:40 p.m., and checked in for a flight to Germany and a connecting flight to Prague.
Li carried nothing. Karafa had a bag filled with cash: between $16,000 and $17,000. Because of this, Li said they were stopped at the gate until she showed them her banking records for the money she had withdrawn to buy the Audi from Karafa's friend the day before.
The prosecution would later argue the money was likely Pratt's, which he brought back from Vancouver the day of the shooting. That money was never recovered.
In messages between Karafa and Li just two days before the shooting, Karafa said, "I hope these retards have money."
By March 4, Hamilton police had announced warrants for Karafa and Li for first-degree murder and attempted murder. Later that month, they said the fugitives had fled to eastern Europe and were now wanted on international warrants.
Jim Callender, then a detective sergeant with Hamilton police and the case manager in charge of the investigation, vowed they would find them. It was a promise Callender, now an inspector, and his team, including Det. David Tsuchida, who was the lead detective, made good on several months later.
An international pursuit
What happened between the time Li and Karafa landed in Prague and were arrested in Budapest, Hungary, months later has been the subject of speculation. The details that are known largely come from Li, who testified about their run from the law.
Karafa was an entrepreneur with multiple businesses. Most were in Europe. Karafa's business partner, Martin Macek, was based in Prague.
After landing there, they took an Uber to Macek's condo and then spent a few days at an Airbnb.
Business records obtained by The Spectator show their partnership in at least one business was dissolved a few days after Karafa's arrival. In 2021, Macek declined to speak with The Spectator and said he found out about the murder only after Karafa left Prague.
After a few days in Prague, Li and Karafa took a train to Slovakia, where his dad picked them up and drove to their family home, Li said. It was just Karafa's parents and their cat in the house.
Karafa and Li were both exhausted; they showered and fell asleep.
But Li awoke around midnight when her phone rang. It was one of her sisters telling her warrants for their arrests had been reported in the media.
Li woke her husband, who woke his dad.
The Karafa family home would be the first place authorities would look for the fugitive couple, so they would have to leave.
Karafa's father didn't want to lose his son again to the justice system in Canada - Karafa had already served a prison sentence for the drunk-driving death.
In 2021, there were strict pandemic lockdown rules in Eastern Europe. And, as a Chinese woman, Li said she stood out. She was made to crouch in the back of a truck while Karafa's dad drove them to a friend's house.
Li said she called her mom, Hong Wei 'Winnie' Liao, who hysterically yelled at her daughter while Li sobbed. Her mom was telling her to come home.
It was then that Liao, a high-profile business person with political ties, released a statement in Chinese-language media.
In the statement, which the Spectator translated from Mandarin, the family said they were "shocked, disturbed and puzzled by Lucy's involvement in the tragic incident" and offered condolences to the victims and their families.
Addressing Li directly, they said they were sure she would not be involved in such an incident voluntarily and would not do "such a ridiculous thing."
"We can hardly imagine the shock and anxiety you experienced after the incident."
The family implored Li to co-operate.
Li's mom arranged for her to speak with a lawyer on the phone while they were in Slovakia. Karafa told her he would not turn himself in.
That night, she awoke to hear Karafa speaking Slovak in the kitchen with men who claimed to be Europol police officers. The men, who called themselves Paul and Milo, drove vehicles with diplomatic plates and claimed they could help make the warrants go away.
Li said Karafa's father paid 250,000 euros that night - and more money later - for their help. She also said her mom paid about 200,000 euros through a lawyer after another man, Sasa (pronounced Sasha), who appeared to be a higher-ranked officer, became involved.
They were first moved to an apartment that belonged to Paul, where their phones were taken from them and fake passports issued.
Li's fake Slovak passport listed her name as Ying Feng Nichta, born in Cebu, Philippines.
Karafa's said his name was Jakub Nichta born in Bratislava.
With their fake passports, Li said they were driven in military-style vehicles across the border into Hungary, where they spent April and half of May in a safe house overseen by a family from Syria in the city of Gyor. Li called it the "pink house" due to its colour.
She said they were told on May 15 that the international warrants were gone, but that Hamilton police were slow to update. They were given their phones back and travelled to Budapest, where Li said they wrongly believed they were free.
Justice closes in
The couple stayed in an Airbnb and Li - who wanted to come home - started looking into getting new Canadian passports. She said they spent a lot of time just walking around the city and visiting a church.
It was outside a church where the pair were arrested June 12.
Police in Hungary released photos of the pair, hands cuffed and heads bowed to avoid cameras.
Li immediately waived extradition and agreed to come back to Canada. Hamilton police travelled to Budapest and escorted her back. She landed in Toronto a month after her arrest on July 13 and was charged.
In Hamilton, she was interviewed for hours by Det. Troy Ashbaugh, an expert interrogator who is now retired. Li, used to flying first class, told him she was "not used to sitting in tight spaces" while being escorted home in economy. Her mom, she said, had offered to fly them all back privately.
Throughout the interview, Li was polite but refused to answer many questions. She repeatedly said there was more to the story, but her lawyer and mother had told her not to talk.
She admitted to being at the scene of the shooting, but claimed to be far away when shots were fired.
Back in Budapest, Karafa had perks in jail, including a contraband cellphone. He fought extradition and instead wanted to go home to Slovakia.
According to court records there, Karafa argued that corruption within Hamilton police and the Hamilton-Wentworth Detention Centre, where Karafa was expected to be held, made it unsafe for him to return to Canada.
His lawyers also argued that they had hired a private investigator to look into Pratt and his "extensive criminal connections."
However, the court there - including the Budapest Court of Appeal - dismissed those concerns. Canadian authorities provided detailed information about Karafa's rights and security plans.
Just over a week after the Appeal Court upheld Karafa's extradition and while Canadian authorities were working out the plan to transport him back to Hamilton, Li was granted bail.
Many people charged with murder in Canada are not granted bail. But granting bail to someone who was an international fugitive is exceptionally rare.
In Li's case, her mother and other sureties promised strict control, including bars on her windows at the family's North York property. Li was released on bail with a $2.7-million bond on Jan. 20, 2022.
Only four months later, Li was again in custody after allegedly breaching her bail conditions by going out to a party and being in contact with someone she was prohibited from communicating with.
By March 30, Karafa - escorted by Hamilton police homicide detectives - arrived back in Canada and was transported to jail. He has been behind bars ever since.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com
CREDIT: Nicole O'Reilly The Hamilton Spectator Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

Subject: Shootings; Surveillance; Restaurants; Vehicles

Document 375 of 500

Do Canadians want more or less government in their lives?: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/do-canadians-want-more-less-government-their/docview/3076368309/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Do+Canadians+want+more+or+less+government+in+their+lives%3F%3A+In+a+recurring+feature%2C+Susan+Delacourt%2C+a+small-l+liberal%2C+and+Matt+Gurney%2C+a+small-c+conservative%2C+bring+their+different+perspectives+-+and+shared+commitment+to+civil+disagreement+-+to+the+political+debates+of+the+moment.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Susan Delacourt Just before the long Canada Day weekend, Matt, you wrote a piece that has stayed with me, even if it wasn't exactly a rallying, hooray-for-Canada tale. You wrote that you're worried about people giving up on this country because many things, big or small, seem to be too hard for governments to do competently. For those who haven't read the piece already (and really, they should) do you want to give us a quick recap of why you felt the need to write it?
Matt Gurney I mean, I mainly write because if I don't write I go crazy and make everyone around me miserable. Writing just makes some of the readers miserable, which is (for me) a huge upgrade. But the column I wrote was a product of a few things. And I admit they're mostly anecdotal. I know the quip that the plural of anecdote is not "data." But when I get whacked over the head repeatedly with something, I start to pay attention. And over the last few years, I've come to suspect that people are leaving Canada, for economic or sometimes (though less often) political reasons, or, interestingly, to access health care they can't reliably get at home. To be clear, I don't think this is a huge number of people, and I 100 per cent understand that for many, Canada is still the promised land. But I think that some of those with means are either leaving outright (this was something my colleague Jen Gerson wrote about recently) or are at least coming up with a Plan B - a backup location where they have a "vacation" property, a bank account, a health plan and maybe residency rights. I stress again that I don't think this is a huge number of people. But I think it might be a lopsided amount of money and talent, both of which Canada badly needs. And for what it's worth, I was reading about something similar just a few days ago right here in the Toronto Star.
SD It is undeniably true that all kinds of government services to citizens are stretched, especially coming out of the pandemic. But it got me thinking about the whole notion of activist government and the weird place we find ourselves in North America. If polls are correct, a lot of Canadians and Americans are flirting with hard-right conservatism. At the same time, maybe as a product of the pandemic, the public seems to want more activist government. They want fixers in power. I wonder how this can be reconciled with politics that used to be all about getting the government out of people's lives?
MG I don't know how hard-right Canadian conservatives will get. Will they be hard-right enough to fund the military, end supply management and open up domestic markets to foreign competition? Or will they mainly just tweet meanly? In any case, I sort of view the problem from the opposite perspective: I think a problem more progressive-leaning governments have, including very much our incumbent federal government, is that they want full value for their proposals. "We are proposing to do X, aren't we good people for doing X?" The problem is, me and about 41 million of my buddies have noticed what you very charitably described as stretched government services. I've put it more bluntly: things are literally and visibly falling apart. And every time a government proposes to do a new thing, I'm not bowled over with gratitude, I'm suspicious they're offering me something new to distract me from the old stuff that's breaking.
SD I too would be grateful, if only out of exasperation with talking points, if all politicians stopped telling me the government has my back. But clichés are built around ideas that are commonplace. There is an assumption now that the public needs to be assured constantly that government is there to meet an ever-expanding list of citizens' needs. It's something that Pierre Poilievre, if he does become prime minister, is going to have to grapple with, as you point out with those examples of military spending and supply management. Poilievre grew up in the old Reform Party, when then leader Preston Manning was repeating the old joke about the scariest words in the English language: "I'm from the government and I'm here to help." I'm not sure that joke would get the same laughs now.
MG I'm not the first person to note this, so give me no credit. But what we need - desperately - is a government that's focused on competency in service delivery and policy execution. I don't think anyone suggests we have that today. So a political party that was based around the notion of "Make Government Competent Again" would, I think, be popular. And that's non-partisan. That could mean more taxes to better fund a big government. It could mean lower taxes to sustain a smaller one. I don't care! Just make me an offer. But we won't get that. We'll get social media wedges and performative grandstanding. And I think we'll keep getting what we started talking about earlier: a slow trickle of at least money and business activity out of Canada, into jurisdictions where you can buy with cash what governments in Canada can no longer provide.
SD Competence, one assumes, should be the low bar. But I guess what I'm saying is that I think citizens have to do some hard thinking too about wants and needs. We're in a housing mess now in part because successive governments of all stripes got out of that business and now everyone wants the government back in. It would be great to have an election debate that got voters assessing just how much they want activist government.
MG You're right that competence should be the low bar. But it isn't. Even talking about it feels like a journey into "if wishes were horses" territory. I'd love a debate about what kind of government we want. I'd love a whole election about that. But everything I've seen in recent years tells me what we'll get is this: a variety of options all basically offering us the same dysfunction, but in a variety of "Don't you just hate those other bastards?" flavours. No one votes for competency. We vote to punish those that annoy or terrify us.
Susan Delacourt is an Ottawa-based columnist for the Star, whereshe has been covering politics on Parliament Hill since the late1980s. A political science graduate of Western University, she'swritten four political books; her latest, Shopping for Votes, was afinalist for the 2014 Hilary Weston Writers' Trust prize innon-fiction. Susan is also a regular commentator on CBC and CTV,and has taught journalism and political communication at CarletonUniversity. Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt@readtheline.ca

'This is our line in the sand and we are making history': LCBO strike enters second day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-our-line-sand-we-are-making-history-lcbo/docview/3076459473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+our+line+in+the+sand+and+we+are+making+history%27%3A+LCBO+strike+enters+second+day&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO workers took to picket lines for the second day of a province-wide strike on Saturday.
"This is our line in the sand and we are making history," said Ontario Public Service Employees Union president JP Hornick at a news conference and rally Saturday afternoon. They were supported by a crowd of approximately 80 people gathered at the picket line at the corner of Bay and Bloor Streets.
At midnight on Friday, more than 9,000 LCBO employees hit picket lines after contract negotiations broke down, demanding, among other things, job security in light of the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.
Last year, Premier Doug Ford announced that the province would allow the sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocers by 2026, starting this September.
This is the first time LCBO staff have gone on strike in the crown corporation's more than 100-year existence.
"We are here today because of the Ford government's plan to try and expand privatization of alcohol sales in such a way to reduce the meaningfulness of the LCBO and the work that we do. That puts every Ontarian at risk," Hornick said to the crowd, who responded with applause.
The first step of the expansion plan is set to start in less than a month, when grocery stores that already sell beer and wine will be able to sell ready-to-drink cocktails. Convenience stores can start selling beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails on Sept. 5. The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario said Friday it has already issued 2,813 convenience store licences.
OPSEU is also advocating for wage increases and more full-time positions, saying 70 per cent of members currently hold part-time and casual roles.
The LCBO said about half of casual workers are guaranteed at least 1,000 working hours a year. It said its most recent proposal responded to a number of the workers' demands but the union did not make a counter-offer.
"We remain hopeful that we can quickly reach an agreement that is fair to our employees, while enabling the LCBO's continued success in a changing marketplace," the LCBO said in an emailed statement Saturday.
According to OPSEU, the LCBO brings $2.5 billion that goes toward funding health care, education and infrastructure. The board also provides good jobs province-wide and urged people to shop for their alcohol only at the public outlet.
"For every drink that you buy, that is outside of an LCBO you are reducing good jobs in your communities. The cost of convenience should not be here shutting down or schools falling apart," said Hornick.
The rally began at 12:45 p.m. and shortly after, there was a brief downpour of rain.
"Ford can send the rain, but we'll still be here, am I right?" NDP Leader Marit Stiles said when she took the mic at a press conference held at the rally. The crowd responded with a loud "Yes!"
"I'm here to stand with the workers, and let them know that I think most Ontarians are standing with them, [and] understand why they're out right now and want Doug Ford to do the right thing, go back to the table and get a deal," Stiles said in an interview with the Star.
LCBO staff are striking for better working conditions and as an effort to maintain the public service that the LCBO offers by putting revenues into health care and education, Stiles said.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy issued a statement on Friday saying the government is "more committed than ever" to its plan.
"We are particularly disappointed that OPSEU is opposed to giving people in Ontario the choice and convenience of buying readymade drinks, like coolers and seltzers, in grocery and convenience stores," he wrote.
Following Stiles and Hornick, three other union leaders delivered messages of support
"We will be at your distribution centres. We're going to have people on the ground, we're going to stop those trucks from going in and we're going to slow the process down every step of the way. Transit workers have your back," said Amalgamated Transit Union president John Di Nino.
"We will be loud and we will be clear and we will not let [Ford] get away with this," said Jason MacLean, Secretary-Treasurer of the National Union of Public and General Employees spoke at the press conference.
"When you come for OPSEU, you come for all of us," said Andria Babbington, President of the Toronto & York Region Labour Council.
Deena Ladd, one of the founders and Executive Director of the Toronto Workers' Action Centre said she and her team have recently held 21 information pickets outside of LCBO stores leading up to the strike and received "so much support" from members of the public, who she said lined up to sign their petitions.
She said even nonstaff have volunteered to support their efforts in solidarity with LCBO workers.
"I think what this government is trying to do is shutdown for two weeks to try to get the public to be mad at the workers, but I think this is going to backfire on them. I think people understand what this fight is all about," Ladd said.
LCBO stores are expected to stay closed for 14 days, followed by a partial reopening. Online ordering, wholesale and free home-delivery services are still available.
"I don't feel it's right, what's happening to my colleagues," said Miguel Correia, who attended the Bay and Bloor Streets rally, and works weekends as an LCBO customer service representative.
"I don't believe in moving the profits that we make from public spending to corporations, which doesn't make a lot of sense to me. I like to keep things public, like money to stay with us. The rich don't need to get richer."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Striking workers picket outside a LCBO location at Yonge and Wellesley streets. On Day 2 of their historic, first-ever strike, LCBO workers will be picketing and hosting a rally in downtown Toronto. in Toronto. July 6, 2024.

Document 377 of 500

Will independent stores, restaurants and bars run out of wine and liquor if LCBO strike drags on? Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-independent-stores-restaurants-bars-run-out/docview/3076281407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+independent+stores%2C+restaurants+and+bars+run+out+of+wine+and+liquor+if+LCBO+strike+drags+on%3F+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's barely been a day since the LCBO strike began, but its impacts are already being felt by Ontarians bracing for a hot summer weekend.
Temperatures are expected to reach 28 C this weekend and consumers have been flocking to the nearest Wine Rack or Beer Store seeking refreshments, while restaurant owners worry that they'll run out of raspberry vodka and vintage wines, especially if the strike continues for the next few weeks.
Why are workers striking?
On midnight Friday, more than 9,000 LCBO employees hit the picket lines, demanding, among other things, job security in light of the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.
Last year, Premier Doug Ford announced that the province would allow the sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocers by 2026, starting this September.
"Doug Ford wants to make life better for his wealthy friends," said Ontario Public Service Employees Union president JP Hornick in a statement Thursday.
"It's why he's wasting upwards of a billion dollars of our money to fast-track privatized alcohol sales and hand more of the public revenues generated by the LCBO over to the CEOs and big box grocery and convenience chains like Loblaws and Circle K."
At Queen's Park on Friday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy reiterated his call for "OPSEU to return to the negotiating table and work towards a deal that prioritizes Ontario consumers and producers."
Bethlenfalvy said the government is "more committed than ever to fulfilling our promise of choice and convenience by expanding access to beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages in convenience, grocery and big-box stores starting later this summer," while retaining the provincial LCBO monopoly.
In a statement, Convenience Industry Council of Canada spokesperson Anne Kothawala said "it is unfortunate that OPSEU leadership are afraid of competition and are fighting for a closed, restrictive market with fewer options for consumers.
"Their claim that expanding the sale of beverage alcohol will somehow hurt jobs and reduce tax revenues defies logic and ignores the benefits that added convenience will provide to consumers."
How long is the strike expected to last?
It's uncertain how long the strike will last, but Hornick said the union is prepared with a "very healthy strike fund" and that "we can weather a strike as long as necessary."
If the strike drags on, the LCBO said it will close its stores for two weeks before opening up 32 select stores, which will operate during restricted hours from Fridays to Sundays, starting on July 19.
Still, store closures won't be as "big a deal as they would have been 30 years ago, when the LCBO was the main point of access," said Brock University professor of health sciences Dan Malleck.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ford's government changed licensing rules so more than 19,000 bars and restaurants are allowed to sell bottles and cans to go, which could alleviate the pressure from consumers in the cities, while more than 400 independent alcohol outlets will remain serving smaller communities.
Where can I buy alcohol?
Online ordering will be available through LCBO.com and the LCBO's mobile app with free home delivery during the strike, with no minimums. There are maximums though: a maximum two units for spirits, 12 units for wine, and 12 units for beer, cider and ready-to-drink.
The LCBO said it will keep processing wholesale orders through a separate online system from LCBO Convenience Outlets, licensed grocery stores, The Beer Store, and winery, brewery, cidery and distillery outlets, as well as from bars and restaurants.
Industry experts, however, say the online-only process might pose an inconvenience to these establishments, as they must order a case with, typically, a minimum of twelve bottles.
"In some cases, you have to buy a case of, say, scotch, and you might need a year to get through it," explained Tony Elenis, president and CEO at Ontario Restaurant Hotel and Motel Association.
Starting Wednesday, the LCBO will also open five locations in Toronto, Mississauga, Woodbridge, London and Ottawa that will exclusively serve licensees, including bars, restaurants, nightclubs and lounges, so they can shop by the bottle rather than the case, but the locations are unknown at this point.
"We have measures in place across our inventory build, our warehouse operations, and our fulfillment approach that reflect the importance of beverage alcohol availability to our wholesale, especially during the busy summer months," said an LCBO spokesperson in a statement.
When could liquor run out?
Most of the largest restaurants and hotel chains bought enough supply to last them around four weeks, while smaller ones may be able to last up to two weeks, said Elenis.
Mom-and-pop shops are especially vulnerable to run into issues as they might not have enough cash reserves to order alcohol in bulk and stock up in advance of possible shortages. (This, the LCBO says, might be alleviated with the opening of five stores exclusive to licensees next week.)
Consumers will generally find the bar rail basics, but could see limited availability of specialty drinks such as high-end scotches, vintage wine or flavoured spirits, said Kris Barnier, vice-president of Restaurants Canada.
Meanwhile, there will be plenty of local wine available, says Wine Rack spokesperson Mark Wasserman.
Wine Rack's owner, Arterra Wines Canada, had been preparing for a possible strike by increasing operating hours, staffing and inventory, he said. Arterra operates eight wineries in Ontario's wine regions, and is the producer behind brands such as Jackson-Triggs, Inniskillin, Sandbanks Estates, Bodacious and others.
"It's a good opportunity, when other places are shut, for people to rediscover Ontario" wines, said Wasserman.
"We have super-high conversion rates with consumers when they walk into a Wine Rack," he said. "I'm hopeful that we can retain some of those customers post-strike."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: The LCBO said it will keep processing wholesale orders from bars and restaurants through a separate online system.

Document 378 of 500

Justin Trudeau is caught in a different kind of Liberal leadership struggle

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-caught-different-kind-liberal/docview/3076281395/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+caught+in+a+different+kind+of+Liberal+leadership+struggle&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Justin Trudeau took over the leadership of his party in 2013, he vowed there would be no more "hyphenated Liberals."
That was a reference to the leadership dramas of the past, particularly the long-running battle between Jean Chrétien and Paul Martin, during which partisans came to define themselves by their loyalty to the prime minister or his likely successor.
That tension coursed through the Liberals for the better part of a decade, until it exploded in 2002. It culminated in Chrétien's announcement late that summer that he would finally step down, but only at the end of 2003 - a long goodbye. Take that, Martin Liberals.
Late last week, with rumours and leaks swirling about Liberal unrest and speculation about a Trudeau goodbye, there was a summer-of-2002 vibe developing. I wrote a book on that long-ago drama, travelling with Martin through the country that summer to get a close look at how the party was shifting its loyalty from one leader to another. It was a heady time for political reporters.
There's a huge temptation to see the current Liberal drama as history repeating itself, but hold on - in so many ways, what's going on now is vastly different.
First of all, if Trudeau is Chrétien in this repetition-of-history scenario, staring down a party that's keen for change, who is the Martin waiting in the wings?
The emerging answer seems to be Mark Carney, the former governor of the Bank of Canada. But apart from his interest in being finance minister, which my colleague Althia Raj wrote about last weekend, Carney does not share much in common with Martin.
He does not, as far as I can tell, have a huge network of organizers, as Martin did, nor any critical mass of MPs ready to proclaim their loyalty to his cause. Nor has a Carney team assumed control of riding associations or young Liberal groups, as the Martin team so massively did in the years before the 2002 explosion.
One intense scuffle in the summer of 2002 revolved around a running list in the Hill Times, identifying which MPs were Martin loyalists and which remained true to Chrétien. It got a bit silly at some points, with MPs being lobbied to get their names on and off one column or the other each time the list was updated.
Now, there was a bit of that feeling in the air last weekend, when all my media colleagues were scrambling to see which MPs had attached their names to emails and open letters about Trudeau's leadership. That kind of fizzled this week.
The old Liberal battles can't really be replayed because it is simply not the same party. Devastated in the 2011 election, which reduced it to third-party status for the first time, the red team was vastly diminished.
Trudeau can claim, quite rightly, that he rebuilt the Liberals and that they owed their phoenix-like rise in 2015 to him. That strength waned as their government went from majority to minority status, but the party built in his image - with "supporters," not members, for instance -- would have to be rebuilt with Trudeau gone.
That's the other big difference between now and 2002. Back then, when it came to power, the Liberals were pretty much the only game in town. The right was divided between the Canadian Alliance and the Progressive Conservatives. Stephen Harper had arrived on the scene to lead the Alliance, but uniting the right was still to come.
In other words, the Liberals could afford a little leadership drama. Trudeau has often said he believes the party took its eyes off the ball. In his debut speech as leader in 2013, he put it this way: "Liberals became more focused on fighting with each other than fighting for Canadians."
I heard echoes of that this week when he spoke with the media for the first time since the disastrous byelection loss in Toronto-St. Paul's.
"I can tell you that, in my conversations with MPs from one end of the country to the other, the emphasis is on how we're going to continue to be there for Canadians," Trudeau said. He slipped in a mention of Pierre Poilievre in his remarks, too - a reminder that unlike in 2002, the Liberals could easily be replaced if Canadians get weary of their leadership drama.
Trudeau was largely successful in excising the hyphens from the Liberal party. Now, as he navigates this summer, he has to avoid letting the party fall into hyphenated camps again: the Trudeau Liberals versus the no-Trudeau Liberals.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks to supporters at a Liberal party fundraiser in Montreal on July 3, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

The double-cross, the shooting and the surviving witness: A Toronto couple looking to escape 'the cocaine life' met a local man with death and prison in his past. Then came a shady business plan and a bloody ambush. In a three-part series, The Hamilton Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of greed and murder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/double-cross-shooting-surviving-witness/docview/3076229505/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+double-cross%2C+the+shooting+and+the+surviving+witness%3A+A+Toronto+couple+looking+to+escape+%27the+cocaine+life%27+met+a+local+man+with+death+and+prison+in+his+past.+Then+came+a+shady+business+plan+and+a+bloody+ambush.+In+a+three-part+series%2C+The+Hamilton+Spectator%27s+Nicole+O%27Reilly+brings+us+a+story+of+greed+and+murder&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was late on a cold Sunday afternoon, the last day of February 2021, when the Audi Q5 pulled into 347 Arvin Ave. and stopped in front of the gate.
Like most businesses in the industrial area of Stoney Creek on the eastern edge of Hamilton, Colonial Pillars was closed for the weekend. The street was quiet, save for the odd passing car. The property was secured, the gate locked.
Oliver Karafa left his wife, Yun Lu (Lucy) Li, in the passenger seat, and approached the gate, returning once when someone walked by. Then it opened - with the padlock now cut - and the Audi disappeared around back.
There were no cameras to capture what happened next.
Just that morning, Karafa and Li had bought the white Audi. They paid Karafa's best friend Jaime Gutierrez about $18,000, using about $4,000 from Li's line of credit.
Karafa had already arranged for someone in Montreal to buy it, along with another vehicle: a Range Rover that didn't belong to them and was also destined for 347 Arvin that day.
From the surveillance cameras of businesses on Arvin, the Audi was unrecognizable, its rims crudely painted black, and untraceable to Karafa or Li.
Colonial Pillars had a for-sale sign out front. No arrangements had been made with a realtor to look at the property, but that is what Tyler Pratt and his pregnant girlfriend, Jordyn Romano, had been led to believe.
The couple thought they were meeting their friends to look at the property for a legal marijuana growing operation. They had already invested nearly a half a million dollars in another of Karafa's businesses and were anxious to see it returned.
"We considered them really good friends," Romano said.
But this Feb. 28 meeting was a ruse.
Karafa did not have the money to pay them back, and he did not want to set up a grow-op on Arvin or do any more business with the couple.
At 5:21 p.m. Karafa messaged Pratt and Romano on the encrypted messaging app Signal to say they would be at the property in 10 minutes, even though they were already there.
Just two minutes after receiving the message, Pratt and Romano arrived in their white Range Rover and drove back to meet their friends.
The shooting
For 31 minutes the two couples remained at the back of the property, unseen by anyone.
What exactly happened may never be known.
What is known is that Karafa shot at Romano three times as she sat inside her Range Rover, one of the bullets lodging in her heart and the other two missing her and hitting the vehicle.
Then he shot Pratt five or six times, with bullets hitting his arm, going through the back of his neck and a graze to his chin that may have been caused by the same bullet that hit his chest. This gunshot to the chest is what killed him, but his death would not have been immediate. He bled out on the cold ground as chaos ensued around him.
Both the Audi and Range Rover were damaged in the melee, either crashing into each other or into one of the warehouses at the back of the large property.
It is "difficult to imagine a more planned murder," said the judge who presided over Karafa's and Li's trials, after a jury found the pair guilty. He described it as an "outright execution."
It's not just that they travelled in an unknown car - Li's identifiable Mercedes was safely parked at their Toronto condo - but there were other steps taken to make it look like they never left their Erskine Avenue residence in the Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue area of Toronto.
Karafa took the stairs where there are no cameras. Li wore a blond wig in the elevator. They switched cellphone SIM cards with a friend to give themselves a "digital alibi" that made it look like their phones never left the city.
The survivor
There was only one problem: Romano didn't die.
As Karafa and Li fled to Europe, Romano underwent life-saving surgery and spent days in a coma before she was able to tell police the identity of her killers.
Her memory of that day is foggy and, in some spots, unreliable because of the trauma she went through. But there are crucial moments she remembers in flashes.
Karafa staring at her with an odd look, his face shaking, he raised a gun and pointed it at her. Romano froze.
"Get out of here, Jo," was all she could remember Karafa saying before she was shot. Panicked, she put the car in reverse and crashed.
She doesn't remember seeing Pratt, who was standing outside, being shot. But she heard him scream, "What the f-k, bro."
Another flash is Karafa dragging her out of her car. Police would later find one of her thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots, the top folded over as if pulled down while she was dragged out, on the ground in the parking lot.
Karafa and Li took both vehicles from the scene, leaving Pratt and Romano for dead.
Video from neighbouring properties shows the Audi, and then Pratt and Romano's Range Rover, coming and going several times during the hour after the shooting. The Audi was left nearby on Green Road. Then the Range Rover returned to Arvin twice.
The prosecution would later argue that was Karafa - and possibly Li - returning to the scene looking for Romano to finish the job. They finally left for good around 6:52 p.m. in the Range Rover.
The getaway
Eleven minutes later, Karafa messaged his car buyer in Montreal, Mohamad Morad.
"Both cars are f-ked," and would need to be towed, Karafa wrote. He offered both the Audi and Pratt and Romano's Range Rover for just $2,000, but only if they were sent out of the country in a shipping container with no trace to Karafa. With Li in the passenger seat, Karafa drove the Range Rover to Etobicoke, and left it parked on Audley Street near the Mimico GO Station.
Back on Arvin Avenue in Hamilton, about 25 minutes after the last time the Range Rover passed, Annette Bright drove by with her boyfriend in the passenger seat.
She saw something on the road in the dark. At first, she thought it was an animal. But something nagged at her, so she turned around. That's when she saw a woman crawling on the road.
Romano was wearing one boot. Her sweater bloody. She said she was cold.
"Help me, I've been shot," she pleaded.
Police arrived by 7:22 p.m., soon followed by paramedics, who took Romano to hospital, where she would live, but lose her unborn baby. She was about 13 weeks pregnant; a fact Li and Karafa knew.
After discovering Romano on the road, police found Pratt's body on the pavement behind the warehouse, his arms outstretched, bullets, shell casings and other debris around him.
The ensuing investigation by Hamilton police followed a complex web of shady business investments and false friendships. The tale spans from Vancouver to Toronto to Hamilton to Europe.
It is a story about money, desperation and betrayal. Perhaps more than anything it is about greed.
The killers
Yun Lu Li was born in Shenzhen, China, a city north of Hong Kong, in 1995. She is a triplet, and the sisters are the oldest children in the family.
Like the rest of her siblings, Li's English name is adapted from her Chinese name Yun Lu, with the Lu becoming Lucy.
When the girls were little, their parents moved to Toronto, and the triplets stayed with family while their parents established themselves in Canada, spending years apart until the girls joined their parents when they were seven. In Canada, the family grew, with a younger sister and brother.
At first, their dad ran a driving school for Chinese immigrants, where their mom also worked. But soon their mom's wealth management business took off. Respon International grew to one of the top financial institutions working with Chinese-Canadians, with Hong Wei (Winnie) Liao becoming a prominent business person, photographed rubbing shoulders with political elites, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
According to Lucy, the bread and butter of her mom's business is selling 20-year life insurance policies that allow clients, after an initial premium paid in Canada, to transfer funds from overseas without being taxed.
This type of insurance policy later plays a role in the murder plot.
Li said she initially struggled at school and was behind her sisters in learning English. Her first language is Mandarin. The family moved to North York, and it was at Earl Haig Secondary School where she met her future husband.
"He was older and cool from the beginning," Li said. "I was pretty nerdy with braces and bangs."
Karafa was born in Slovakia and immigrated to Canada in 2001 with his family, first living in Windsor and then Toronto. Li said she liked Karafa right away.
They went on dates, but Li claimed the short-lived relationship ended when Karafa wanted to have sex and Li wasn't ready. She also claimed he slept with a friend of hers at a party.
In April 2012, when Karafa was 19, he crashed his Range Rover on Mount Pleasant Road in Toronto.
He was only a G2 driver at the time. Karafa walked away, but his passenger - a friend and personal trainer - was killed.
Karafa was sentenced to five years in prison in 2014 and prohibited from driving for 15 years.
When Karafa first applied for parole in January 2016, he was denied because of two incidents in Beaver Creek Institution, where he was involuntarily transferred from minimum to medium security.
"The board suggested your behaviours since being incarcerated is indicative of a young man with an abnormal sense of entitlement; you agreed," the parole decision says. "You present as a person who believes rules made for society are for everyone else to live by and not you."
By December 2016, Karafa was granted day parole, but denied full parole.
Karafa appealed the decision to deny his full parole, and that was rejected. But six months later, he was released on full parole in August 2017. He was 24 and the parole board believed he was motivated to get his life on track. He had a job as a manager at a café and was supported by his sister, her husband and his unnamed girlfriend, the document says.
His parents had already returned to Europe, where they lived in the family home in Levice, Slovakia - a location that would play a role later when Karafa and Li were on the run.
Karafa's first prison sentence for the fatal drunk-driving offence officially expired on May 4, 2019, while the driving ban continued.
Li said her upbringing was strict. Her mother was a "tiger mom" with resolute plans for her children.
All were encouraged to study business. Li's mom offered a reward of a car to the first triplet to acquire accreditation to sell life insurance. Li was the first to do this, focusing on that more than her studies at Western University in London, Ont. Eventually she transferred Ryerson University (now Toronto Metropolitan University) to finish school, while working with insurance companies.
Li, working with her mother, struggled to attract bigger clients herself and realized that she needed more life experience. She, along with her triplet sisters, asked for a break from work at Christmas 2018. The family often had annual meetings around the holidays where they would discuss what they accomplished that year and plans for the future. Li said she was given two years of freedom, with her mom offering a $5,000 a month allowance.
The triplets had an account on Instagram together where they attracted sponsors who paid for the sisters to model their products. Li said they made about $500 to $1,000 per sponsored post, but that the money was split three ways and was more for fun and not enough to live on. One sponsor gave her the blond wig.
It was during this time, when Li was about 23, that Karafa came back into her life. They messaged on Instagram and met for a date.
The relationship moved fast, with Li moving in with Karafa after their second date. Li's mom stopped giving her an allowance because Karafa paid for most things.
Out of prison, Karafa worked as an entrepreneur and seemed to be making good money. According to Li, his businesses included everything from exporting cars, renting out a studio to film social media ads, to selling weighted blankets. But much of his business was in Europe, where he had a partner in Prague.
The couple got a two-storey penthouse condo at 18 Erskine Ave., in Toronto. Because Karafa was a prohibited driver, Li drove him everywhere, including to multiple business meetings.
This included frequent meetings hosted at the Mississauga offices of Karafa's longtime friend Alex (also known as Sasha), where Karafa would later meet Pratt and Romano.
Karafa and Li travelled often, including to Europe to see his family and conduct business. In spring 2020, about two months into lockdowns at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, the couple were going a little "stir crazy," and Karafa wanted to return to Europe.
They bought tickets to Zurich for May 16. But when they got to the airport, they had a problem. Karafa, who had dual citizenship in Canada and Slovakia, was allowed to return to Europe, but Li was not.
At the suggestion of someone working at the airline they decided to get married, Li said. Karafa was going to leave with or without her.
"We were not planning on marrying, but I was very in love with him and due to travel restrictions I made the irresponsible choice and decided to ... marry him on the spot," Li said.
They stepped out of line and married with an officiant presiding over Zoom. They spent months in Europe before returning to Toronto that summer.
The victims
Tyler Pratt was raised in a large and loving family in British Columbia, where he coached kids' hockey and, like Karafa, was also an aspiring entrepreneur.
Pratt grew up in Taylor, B.C., then, as a young man, he lived in Kelowna before relocating to Vancouver, where his two children were born.
Described by family as hardworking, his businesses included a diamond company called Hard as Diamonds and a wellness supplement company, Elite Organic Nutrition.
But he made most of his money selling cocaine. He wasn't a street-level dealer - he arranged shipments by phone. He once made $1 million in a day, Romano said, but that was rare.
By 2020, he had cash and was looking to invest and move away from cocaine.
"He wanted to just become a more legitimate businessman," Romano later said in court. "He wanted to get out of the cocaine life; we wanted to have a family and be normal and escape that negativity."
She grew up in Ontario, with family in Hamilton. In her early 20s, she went back and forth to B.C. for a few years before moving there when she was about 24.
Romano worked as an exotic dancer and met Pratt about six months before they started dating. She stopped working and he paid for their lifestyle, which included luxury goods and travel. Their Toronto condo, photographed by police after the murder, was filled with boxes of designer shoes, bags and other items.
Romano agreed that Pratt was careful to live off the radar because of his cocaine business, but she said he was kind and had an even temper. Pratt's former wife - the mother of his two children - also said she never had issues with Pratt and that he always put his children first.
Romano and Pratt were together for about a year before he was killed, first living in Vancouver and then moving to Toronto not long before the shooting. They wanted to start a new life together.
In 2020, the couple travelled back and forth between Toronto and Vancouver often, both for work and so Pratt could see his kids. On the day he was killed, he had just returned from a trip for his daughter's birthday.
In July 2020, Romano took a solo trip to Toronto and messaged a girlfriend she hadn't seen in a while. This friend was dating a guy named Alex (Sasha) and the group went out for dinner.
The swindle
Later, Romano introduced Sasha, described in court as the owner of a Mississauga construction company, and Pratt. The two men hit it off. Pratt was looking for a business to invest in and Sasha helped arrange meetings.
Among those Sasha introduced was his friend, Oliver Karafa, who he had known for a decade and vouched for.
At that first meeting, Karafa told them: "Yo, I have something that can make us a lot of money."
Soon after, Pratt and Romano invested $170,000 in Karafa's business, selling truckloads of personal protective equipment (PPE) in Europe. Selling everything from hospital gowns to syringes promised to be lucrative, especially during the pandemic. Soon after, they invested more cash and, by late fall, they'd given Karafa roughly $470,000.
"I really liked Oliver," Romano said. "He was just always nice and easygoing. Tyler loved Oliver, they had a great friendship. He adored him."
And they trusted him. The couple never signed a formal contract.
There were a lot of meetings with Karafa and Pratt, often at Sasha's office. It was there they first met Li. By late 2020, Sasha was dating one of Li's sisters, Jane, one of the triplets, and Li would visit with her sister after driving Karafa to the meetings.
Li later testified that she knew Pratt had invested money in the PPE business, but didn't know the particulars. She wasn't involved in the PPE business meetings.
Karafa promised big profits, but claimed the money was stuck in Europe. Romano said he showed them a European account with millions in it. He promised $3 million was their share.
But by January, Karafa had failed to pay Romano and Pratt and was desperately trying to find ways to delay.
His answer: an insurance scheme. This is where Li really enters the story.
Li had let her licence lapse, but she was toying with getting back into insurance and knew the industry well. Karafa sold Pratt on one of the 20-year life insurance policies and set up meetings with the four of them.
The held several meetings, including at the North York home of Li's parents - which the family continued to own despite her parents primarily living in Vancouver - as well as at Pratt and Romano's new Cumberland Street condo in Toronto, where they moved in February.
Romano said Li initially suggested a $2.8-million policy, but she and Pratt couldn't afford that so it was reduced to $1.5 million. Romano believed if they paid the first premium in cash from Canada, their money stuck in Europe could be added later, allowing them to transfer the money without paying tax or risk the attention of authorities, including Canada's national financial intelligence agency, FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada). Romano also said she was told they could borrow against the policy, allowing them to get a loan in Canada to expand business.
The truth is that insurance premiums cannot be paid in cash. But Pratt and Romano didn't understand how it worked.
Li later admitted the insurance scheme was a ruse to buy Karafa more time to come up the money owed. She said she initially was sincere in trying to sell them a policy, but that after learning the premium would need to be in cash, she played along to help Karafa. She never intended to submit the application.
Li, who said she never liked Romano and Pratt, claimed she offered to pretend to pay the first premium. Police would later find a $1.5-million cheque in an envelope of insurance documents. But Romano said she never saw that cheque and instead believed Li was going to loan them $1 million - taken from her mom's account where she "wouldn't notice" it missing.
Pratt needed the loan because he was having cash-flow problems, including losing a cocaine shipment that was seized at the border, court heard.
Romano said the two couples were set to meet at Li's mom's offices on March 1 to finalize the insurance application. Karafa was going to be paying them back the initial $500,000 investment, with the $1-million loan from Li that would make up the $1.5 million required. Then they were going to sign paperwork finalizing their positions with the European PPE business at Karafa's lawyer's office afterward.
Instead, by March 1, Li and Karafa were on a flight to Europe, Pratt was dead and Romano in a coma, all while Hamilton police worked to put together what happened the day before at 347 Arvin Ave.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime
and justice reporter at
The hamilton Spectator.
CREDIT: Nicole O'Reilly the hamilton spectator Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The hamilton Spectator.
