Document 380 of 500

A different kind of leadership struggle

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/different-kind-leadership-struggle/docview/3076228076/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+different+kind+of+leadership+struggle&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Justin Trudeau took over the leadership of his party in 2013, he vowed there would be no more "hyphenated Liberals."
That was a reference to the leadership dramas of the past, particularly the long-running battle between Jean Chrétien and Paul Martin, during which partisans came to define themselves by their loyalty to the prime minister or his likely successor.
That tension coursed through the Liberals for the better part of a decade, until it exploded in 2002. It culminated in Chrétien's announcement late that summer that he would finally step down, but only at the end of 2003 - a long goodbye. Take that, Martin Liberals.
Late last week, with rumours and leaks swirling about Liberal unrest and speculation about a Trudeau goodbye, there was a summer-of-2002 vibe developing. I wrote a book on that long-ago drama, travelling with Martin through the country that summer to get a close look at how the party was shifting its loyalty from one leader to another. It was a heady time for political reporters.
There's a huge temptation to see the current Liberal drama as history repeating itself, but hold on - in so many ways, what's going on now is vastly different.
First of all, if Trudeau is Chrétien in this repetition-of-history scenario, staring down a party that's keen for change, who is the Martin waiting in the wings?
The emerging answer seems to be Mark Carney, the former governor of the Bank of Canada. But apart from his interest in being finance minister, which my colleague Althia Raj wrote about last weekend, Carney does not share much in common with Martin.
He does not, as far as I can tell, have a huge network of organizers, as Martin did, nor any critical mass of MPs ready to proclaim their loyalty to his cause. Nor has a Carney team assumed control of riding associations or young Liberal groups, as the Martin team so massively did in the years before the 2002 explosion.
One intense scuffle in the summer of 2002 revolved around a running list in the Hill Times, identifying which MPs were Martin loyalists and which remained true to Chrétien. It got a bit silly at some points, with MPs being lobbied to get their names on and off one column or the other each time the list was updated.
Now, there was a bit of that feeling in the air last weekend, when all my media colleagues were scrambling to see which MPs had attached their names to emails and open letters about Trudeau's leadership. That kind of fizzled this week.
The old Liberal battles can't really be replayed because it is simply not the same party. Devastated in the 2011 election, which reduced it to third-party status for the first time, the red team was vastly diminished.
Trudeau can claim, quite rightly, that he rebuilt the Liberals and that they owed their phoenix-like rise in 2015 to him. That strength waned as their government went from majority to minority status, but the party built in his image - with "supporters," not members, for instance - would have to be rebuilt with Trudeau gone.
That's the other big difference between now and 2002. Back then, when it came to power, the Liberals were pretty much the only game in town. The right was divided between the Canadian Alliance and the Progressive Conservatives. Stephen Harper had arrived on the scene to lead the Alliance, but uniting the right was still to come.
In other words, the Liberals could afford a little leadership drama. Trudeau has often said he believes the party took its eyes off the ball. In his debut speech as leader in 2013, he put it this way: "Liberals became more focused on fighting with each other than fighting for Canadians."
I heard echoes of that this week when he spoke with the media for the first time since the disastrous byelection loss in Toronto-St. Paul's.
"I can tell you that, in my conversations with MPs from one end of the country to the other, the emphasis is on how we're going to continue to be there for Canadians," Trudeau said. He slipped in a mention of Pierre Poilievre in his remarks, too - a reminder that unlike in 2002, the Liberals could easily be replaced if Canadians get weary of their leadership drama.
Trudeau was largely successful in excising the hyphens from the Liberal party. Now, as he navigates this summer, he has to avoid letting the party fall into hyphenated camps again: the Trudeau Liberals versus the no-Trudeau Liberals.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau can rightly claim that he rebuilt the Liberals and that they owed their phoenix-like rise in 2015 to him. But the party built in his image would have to be rebuilt with Trudeau gone, writes Susan Delacourt.Christinne MuschiThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

LCBO, union ready for long run: Free home delivery offered, as OPSEU claims it has 'healthy strike fund'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-union-ready-long-run/docview/3076227129/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO%2C+union+ready+for+long+run%3A+Free+home+delivery+offered%2C+as+OPSEU+claims+it+has+%27healthy+strike+fund%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's barely been a day since the LCBO strike began, but its impacts are already being felt by Ontarians bracing for a hot summer weekend.
Temperatures are expected to reach 28 C this weekend and consumers have been flocking to the nearest Wine Rack or Beer Store seeking refreshments, while restaurant owners worry that they'll run out of raspberry vodka and vintage wines, especially if the strike continues for the next few weeks.
On midnight Friday, more than 9,000 LCBO employees hit the picket lines, demanding, among other things, job security in light of the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.
Last year, Premier Doug Ford announced that the province would allow the sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocers by 2026, starting this September.
At Queen's Park on Friday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy reiterated his call for "OPSEU to return to the negotiating table and work towards a deal that prioritizes Ontario consumers and producers."
In a statement, Convenience Industry Council of Canada spokesperson Anne Kothawala said "it is unfortunate that OPSEU leadership are afraid of competition and are fighting for a closed, restrictive market with fewer options for consumers."
It's uncertain how long the strike will last, but Hornick said the union is prepared with a "very healthy strike fund" and that "we can weather a strike as long as necessary."
If the strike drags on, the LCBO said it will close its stores for two weeks before opening up 32 select stores, which will operate during restricted hours from Fridays to Sundays, starting on July 19.
Still, store closures won't be as "big a deal as they would have been 30 years ago, when the LCBO was the main point of access," said Brock University professor of health sciences Dan Malleck.
Online ordering will be available through LCBO.com and the LCBO's mobile app with free home delivery during the strike, with no minimums. There are maximums though: two units for spirits, 12 units for wine, and 12 units for beer, cider and ready-to-drink.
The LCBO said it will keep processing wholesale orders through a separate online system from LCBO Convenience Outlets, licensed grocery stores, The Beer Store, and winery, brewery, cidery and distillery outlets, as well as from bars and restaurants.
Industry experts, however, say the online-only process might pose an inconvenience to these establishments, as they must order a case with, typically, a minimum of twelve bottles.
Starting Wednesday, the LCBO will also open five locations in Toronto, Mississauga, Woodbridge, London and Ottawa that will exclusively serve licensees, including bars, restaurants, nightclubs and lounges, so they can shop by the bottle rather than the case, but the locations are unknown at this point.
Most of the largest restaurants and hotel chains bought enough supply to last them around four weeks, while smaller ones may be able to last up to two weeks, said Tony Elenis, president and CEO at Ontario Restaurant Hotel and Motel Association.
Mom-and-pop shops are especially vulnerable to run into issues as they might not have enough cash reserves to order alcohol in bulk and stock up in advance of possible shortages. (This, the LCBO says, might be alleviated with the opening of five stores exclusive to licensees next week.)
Consumers will generally find the bar rail basics, but could see limited availability of specialty drinks such as high-end scotches, vintage wine or flavoured spirits, said Kris Barnier, vice-president of Restaurants Canada.
Meanwhile, there will be plenty of local wine available, said Wine Rack spokesperson Mark Wasserman.
Wine Rack's owner, Arterra Wines Canada, had been preparing for a possible strike by increasing operating hours, staffing and inventory, he said.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: On midnight Friday, more than 9,000 LCBO employees hit the picket lines, demanding, among other things, job security in light of the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.Nick LachanceToronto Star
CREDIT: Ana Pereira Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Four decades of tax cuts, deregulation and privatization equals a serious distribution of wealth problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/four-decades-tax-cuts-deregulation-privatization/docview/3075990399/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+decades+of+tax+cuts%2C+deregulation+and+privatization+equals+a+serious+distribution+of+wealth+problem&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Can you blame people for leaving town? Voters have power to demand more, July 4
What happened to our once civil society? We now live in an Ontario no one recognizes. Everywhere you look there is a crisis - homelessness, affordability, health care, education, building and infrastructure decay.
How did things that were once so good get so bad.? The answer is right in front of us. Most of the public wealth was transferred to the wealthy. We have been fed a steady diet of tax cuts, deregulation, and the need for privatization to get the "innovation and private sector efficiencies" with promises like "all boats will be lifted by the rising economy." As we have clearly seen, false promises. Not only has our civil society been severely damaged but so had trust in democracy .
In the last provincial election, only 17 per cent of the population voted for Premier Doug Ford. After slashing government funding to public services starving them into crisis just to pay for tax cuts to the wealthy and their corporations, they then present privatization as the solution to a problem they created. The only thing deregulation and privatization does is create more profit-making opportunities.
The gap between the haves and have-nots is huge and widening at an ever-increasing rate.
Small tax cuts to the general population have been used as a cover for massive tax cuts to the wealthy and their corporations.
Reversing tax cuts is not raising taxes, it is restoring revenue to rebuild our once civil society. Beware any politician promising tax cuts. We do not have a wealth creation problem. We do have a very serious distribution of wealth problem.
Where is the leadership? We have the power. Don't leave, speak up and vote to stop this insanity.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
We are all gravely concerned about the deterioration of Toronto
Can you blame people for leaving town? Voters have power to demand more, July 4
Toronto is in a rapid state of decline. Urgent action is required by city council in the following key areas:
1. Congestion: Remove underutilized bike lanes on main streets to improve traffic flow and reduce emissions from idling vehicles.
2. Safety: Increase police funding and enforce laws to combat rising crime and antisemitism.
3. Cleanliness: Enforce bylaws to clean up litter, homeless encampments, and maintain public spaces.
4. Housing affordability: Streamline development processes to reduce construction costs and address the housing crisis.
5. Mental health and addiction: Work with other levels of government to improve care and rehabilitation, consider special housing for afflicted individuals, and address issues with drug injection sites.
Current policies are driving away revenue and failing to meet basic standards of cleanliness, safety, affordability, and mobility. We need accountability in spending and decision-making to benefit all residents, not just select groups.
The issues are front and centre with everyone living in Toronto. The deterioration of the city is of grave concern to all business owners, residents and those who come here for leisure or pleasure. City council needs to address these issues for the benefit of all the electorate rather than wasting our funds on changing street names and bike lanes that only benefit the few.
Mary Throop, Toronto
It's about profit not people
Per Bank and Galen Weston continue to bleed the people of Ontario. They sit back and laugh all the way to the bank. I have a perfect example. I was at Shopper's Drug Mart to purchase off brand Imodium. Shoppers price for six tablets for the off brand was almost $10. I then compared it to Walmart's Great Value brand. Their price was also close to $10 but for 12 tablets. Wake up CEO's! You can't fool us. We know how much you love your profits and could care less about consumers.
Debbie Schopp, Thornhill
Liberal MPs are too timid to speak up on behalf of their countryThe lone Liberal MP to call for Justin Trudeau's resignation speaks out about the surprising response, July 2
Kudos to New Brunswick MP Wayne Long for having the courage to say what everyone thinks: at this juncture Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is a liability to the Liberal party. Hopefully there is something happening behind the scenes because if the balance of Liberal MPs, all leaders in their own right, are too timid to speak up on behalf of their own jobs or at least on behalf of their country, we are in some serious trouble. While I do expect that Trudeau is only listening to his "yes-men," all of his MPs surely know that his very name brings the taste of bile to many a mouth in their ridings. Not even the juiciest of Doug Ford-style giveaways can help Trudeau. He just doesn't get it. And, if he alone is responsible for a Pierre Poilievre majority government, despite his long tenure, I am sure that history will not be kind.
Dave Ottenbrite, Cambridge
Quitters don't deserve our votes
We have politicians who ran as Liberals and received votes as a Liberal and are now throwing a tantrum and listening to a few professional s - t disturbers who want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to step down. Trudeau's answer to these politicians who gave up easily, is that he is "committed to doing the work of building a better Canada every single day. There's always going to be lots of reflection after a tough loss (St. Paul's riding) but there's also so much to do."
This is a leader who is dedicated and responsible and loves this country of Canada. The Conservatives would do well to take a page of out Trudeau's book and get on board with one of the most serious problems Canada is facing, a threat to our democracy, not the St Paul's vote. The Conservatives refuse to be investigated for security purposes on the most important issue of foreign interference in our democracy during elections and this will affect us now and in the future. With the parties split on the issue of security, the door will be open for those foreign countries to walk through.
Beverly Northeast, Goodwood
The Ontario Line should be cancelled
Why am I not seeing more challenges to Premier Doug Ford about the Ontario Line? It doesn't go to Ontario Place. Ford wants to move the Ontario Science Centre. Even if the Science Centre stays where it is, it will have rapid transit access for the first time in 50 years through the Eglinton LRT (if Metrolinx ever completes it). Ford should back way off and allow the TTC to build the Downtown Relief Line they had planned. The Ontario Line should be cancelled.
Scott Fairweather, Toronto
Nigel Farage has zero ideas and zero credibilityHow a far-right populist and seven-time loser is changing U.K. politics, June 25
The piece legitimizing Nigel Farage is probably the biggest pile of stinking crap I've ever read in a newspaper. I'm appalled that the Star is giving space to a right-wing zealot with zero ideas, zero credibility and zero interest in the betterment of UK society. Stick to the Leafs.
James Duncan, Toronto
CAPTION: When the traditional rewards of living in the city are falling apart, like public transit, it's little wonder people look elsewhere to live.

Document 383 of 500

Torontonians are leaving the city. Can we blame them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontonians-are-leaving-city-can-we-blame-them/docview/3075990116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Torontonians+are+leaving+the+city.+Can+we+blame+them%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a rousing TikTok video, set to the Proclaimers' anthemic "I'm Gonna Be." You know the one: "But I would walk five hundred miles. And I would walk five hundred more ..."
The crowd pleaser is perfectly deployed in this instance by Irish singer-songwriter Niall Horan, who, choosing not to stew in seemingly magnetically attached bumper-to-bumper Toronto traffic, decided to walk to his destination.
The fact that his destination was the Scotiabank Arena, where Horan was performing, for Pete's sake, causes us both to smile and sigh at the same time.
"Traffic's too bad in Toronto so we're walking to the venue," Horan says, quite jauntily. "Let's do it!" And then more reflectively: "In all of the years playing shows I don't think I've ever walked into a venue."
So that makes Toronto a first, then.
And not in a good way.
The bad ways in which the city is performing have been driven home by the Star's three-part series Anywhere but Toronto. The series cites the hard numbers: In the past two years 220,000 more Canadians have abandoned Ontario's capital than have arrived. Drilling into that number from July, 2022, to July, 2023, we see that fully a third of city escapees were between the ages of 25 and 39.
That's a crisis.
Traffic doesn't flow. Construction blankets the city. East enders have all but given up on visiting west end friends.
We know the knock-on effects that barriers to mobility have in the areas of stress and overall quality of life.
Then there's the money side of life. The cost of housing in Toronto has long been a reliable headline generator, from the stratospheric cost of single-family homes - increasingly an option only to those who are bootstrapped with family money - to a modest one-bedroom apartment now averaging $2,200 a month. As the series reports, Toronto city dwellers are increasingly finding and defining "home" in other areas of the province: Oshawa, Hamilton, the Niagara Region. Or Calgary. Or Halifax.
Descriptions of the capital city they have left behind share a common theme: impersonal. Fraught. A grind. It is not too extreme to suggest we are becoming an unlivable city.
Toronto's tools to meet these immense challenges are limited. Cities are not an official order of government in this country but rather creatures of the province. City Hall has limited ability to tax. It can't accumulate debt. But in the decades since our constitution was written, as more and more people have concentrated in metropolitan centres, especially in Toronto, many of the country's defining policy challenges have been left to relatively impotent - and often inadequately ambitious - city governments to solve, while Ottawa and Queen's Park happily push off their problems to municipalities and, inevitably, to future generations.
But despite these obstacles, even under existing constitutional constraints, progress is possible. Last winter, when the city and the province announced a "New Deal," echoing the phrasing of a Depression-era Franklin Delano Roosevelt, Mayor Olivia Chow championed a collaboration that resulted in significant funding for new subway trains (pending matching federal funds) and uploading the costs of the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway to the province. "When all orders of government work together on shared priorities, we can deliver more for the people of Toronto," Chow said.
Premier Doug Ford acknowledged that when Toronto succeeds, Ontario and Canada succeed.
That's a decades-old truism. That doesn't mean it will continue to hold true. What is needed now is transformational change, a pulling together of all levels of government, alongside the private sector and educational institutions, to chart a course for the city's future prosperity. We must find a way to make the new deal the basis for a lasting new approach to municipal governance that respects our city's outsized role in this country.
If that doesn't happen, well, let's leave it to Coun. Gord Perks to contemplate a city too expensive and dysfunctional for the just-graduated nurse, or your favourite local grocer. "What does Toronto become?" asks Perks. "It's not a pretty picture. It's a terrible, terrible future."
CAPTION: Niall Horan performs on stage during the MTV EMAs on Nov. 3, 2019 in Seville, Spain.

Subject: Traffic

Title: Torontonians are leaving the city. Can we blame them?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jul 5, 2024

Section: opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 385 of 500

Strike to close LCBO stores: Talks break down as union cites fears over job security amid retail overhaul

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strike-close-lcbo-stores/docview/3075886658/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Strike+to+close+LCBO+stores%3A+Talks+break+down+as+union+cites+fears+over+job+security+amid+retail+overhaul&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO workers are gearing up for the first strike in the liquor monopoly's history, after talks between the Crown corporation and its employees broke down Thursday just hours before a midnight strike deadline.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union said more than 9,000 LCBO workers would be out on strike as of 12:01 a.m. Friday, which would lead to the shutdown of LCBO stores for the next two weeks, followed by a partial reopening.
"Tonight, Ford's dry summer begins," OPSEU bargaining committee chair Colleen MacLeod said at a news conference Thursday evening where Ontarians were urged to voice their displeasure to Premier Doug Ford.
The union reiterated that the fundamental issue is job security, expressing concern that thousands of jobs will be lost in the coming years due to the government's plans to expand alcohol sales in the province.
"We haven't even got to the wages and benefits portion of it ... that's not what this is about," MacLeod said. "If we don't have a job on the go-forward, a wage increase really doesn't make much of a difference."
OPSEU president JP Hornick said the union has a "very healthy strike fund" and that "we can weather a strike as long as necessary."
Hornick described the LCBO as facing an existential crisis. "Do we want to have a public provider of alcohol as we've had for the past 100 years, or are we just moving into a Wild West private model?"
The LCBO said in a statement Thursday evening that OPSEU's leadership made clear "numerous times" at the bargaining table that workers would strike "solely over their demand" that the government reverse its decision to expand the sale of ready-to-drink beverages like coolers and seltzers in grocery and convenience stores.
The Crown corporation said it tabled its latest offer just after 4 p.m. on Thursday, which included wage increases for the next three years, and converting about 400 casual employees to permanent full-time positions.
Instead of presenting a counterproposal, the union held a news conference, the LCBO said.
"The LCBO is calling on OPSEU's leadership and bargaining team to counter our latest offer and work with us to achieve a negotiated agreement and avoid a strike."
The LCBO has said a strike would close all of its stores for two weeks, but that 30 would reopen July 19 for limited shopping every Friday, Saturday and Sunday. It also said online ordering would still be available in the event of a strike, but that details wouldn't be available until a strike begins.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office said Thursday evening it was disappointed in the union's decision to walk away from the bargaining table.
"We are particularly disappointed that OPSEU is opposed to giving people in Ontario the choice and convenience of buying ready-made drinks, like coolers and seltzers, in grocery and convenience stores," said a statement from the minister's office.
OPSEU has repeatedly criticized the provincial government's decision to open up Ontario's alcohol retail market, arguing that it puts LCBO jobs and profits at risk, while helping private retailers, including grocery and big-box stores, as well as convenience stores.
The union was proposing to add more LCBO stores and increase operating hours to meet demand and improve convenience.
OPSEU also says that 70 per cent of LCBO workers are "casual," meaning they have no guaranteed hours, benefits or sick pay. The LCBO, meanwhile, said that casual employees do have access to benefits and at least 50 per cent of them are guaranteed at least 1,000 hours per year.
The union says LCBO wages range from $17.65 an hour to roughly $30. The LCBO says the range for customer service representatives goes from $17.85 to $32.58.
Liberal MPP Stephen Blais, the party's critic for labour issues, called on the government to make a deal.
"LCBO workers deserve job security, livable wages and fair treatment. Doug Ford needs to do the right thing and come to a fair deal that respects workers, keeps Ontario working and doesn't disrupt the lives of hard-working Ontarians," Blais said in a written statement.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said in a statement Thursday: "The chaos in the beverage industry is entirely the Ford government's doing, and now Ontarians are the ones paying for it.
"The premier's rush to get beer into corner stores without proper planning or consultation has upended the industry and raised workers' fears about job security in an uncertain future," said Schreiner.
It's a more complicated set of negotiations than typical contract talks, where the main bone of contention is strictly money, noted labour relations professor Rafael Gomez.
"Because of all the changes in alcohol retailing, the union knows that there will be fewer workers at the LCBO by 2030. And likely, fewer LCBO stores," said Gomez, director of the University of Toronto's Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources. "The government is going to push the union to either have high wages in return for less job security, or low wages and higher job security."
CREDIT: Josh Rubin and Jacques Gallant Toronto Star

Document 386 of 500

Talking the road not yet travelled: With election looming, premier touts controversial Highway 413 in new ad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/talking-road-not-yet-travelled/docview/3075885347/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Talking+the+road+not+yet+travelled%3A+With+election+looming%2C+premier+touts+controversial+Highway+413+in+new+ad&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Progressive Conservatives rev up for an early election next year, Premier Doug Ford has filmed a campaign-style video touting progress on Highway 413.
The minute-long promotional spot posted to Ford's social media accounts on Thursday boasts that the Tories are "getting it done" on the controversial 52-kilometre freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan.
While building the highway was the cornerstone promise of the Tories' successful 2022 re-election campaign, there has been little visible progress since - save for some blue and white billboards posted along nearby existing routes.
"Highway 413: part of our $28 billion plan to build roads and highways," blare the signs that are a familiar sight along Highway 400.
With Ford considering an election in early 2025 - more than one year before the scheduled June 2026 vote - the Tories are scrambling to demonstrate that they have kept previous campaign promises.
That's a major reason why the government has earmarked $225 million to liberalize the sale of beer, wine and premixed cocktails by Oct. 31, though critics warn it could cost taxpayers $1 billion to expedite booze in variety stores, more supermarkets and big box outlets.
In the video of him on a tour with Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, shot without any journalists or media cameras present, Ford assures viewers "preliminary work is well underway" on the highway.
"Crews are currently on site flying in drilling equipment by helicopter to test soil and bedrock conditions. It's great to see firsthand ... the crews drilling boreholes and assessing the sample conditions," the premier says.
"This step is critical as we move forward into our next phase of designing the bridges that will cross the Humber Valley," he continues.
"With construction tracking to start early next year, we will be adding 3,500 good-paying jobs each year - along with the highway itself as it will contribute $350 million to Ontario's GDP (gross domestic product)."
A major roadblock to construction was cleared in April when Queen's Park and Ottawa agreed to establish a joint working group for provincial and federal officials to "recommend appropriate measures to minimize environmental impacts in areas of federal environmental jurisdiction."
Even though the freeway was the Tories' signature campaign pledge two years ago - and they won every riding along the proposed route - it remains contentious.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner all oppose the highway and warn it could be a reprise of Ford's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
The opposition parties charge the Tories could use the freeway to reward favoured land owners along the route because adjacent property would become more valuable for potential housing development.
Stiles has criticized Ford for "spending billions of taxpayers' money on a project with no end date in sight ... a project that also threatens Ontario's farmlands and gives preferential treatment to insiders."
Crombie has predicted that "just like the Greenbelt scandal, the ineffective $10-billion Highway 413 is designed to benefit one group of people: Doug Ford's wealthy insiders, Conservative donors and rich land speculators."
Schreiner has dubbed the highway "an expensive sprawl supercharger that will cost Ontarians at least $10 billion and pave over 2,000 acres of prime farmland - all to save commuters a few extra seconds."
Environmental Defence, an activist group, has pointed out the highway would cut through protected lands, pave over thousands of acres of farmland and cross streams and rivers more than 100 times.
But in the video, Ford emphasizes something must be done to tackle gridlock.
"The government promised to build Highway 413 - and we're getting it done," he says.
"We know too many people across the GTA are stuck in traffic and are counting on us to help them get where they need to go faster."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, shown in Toronto last month, has released an election-style video boasting of progress made on the Highway 413 project.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 387 of 500

Can we blame Torontonians for leaving city?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-we-blame-torontonians-leaving-city/docview/3075885258/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+we+blame+Torontonians+for+leaving+city%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a rousing TikTok video, set to the Proclaimers' anthemic "I'm Gonna Be." You know the one: "But I would walk five hundred miles. And I would walk five hundred more ..."
The crowd pleaser is perfectly deployed in this instance by Irish singer-songwriter Niall Horan, who, choosing not to stew in seemingly magnetically attached bumper-to-bumper Toronto traffic, decided to walk to his destination.
The fact that his destination was the Scotiabank Arena, where Horan was performing, for Pete's sake, causes us both to smile and sigh at the same time.
"Traffic's too bad in Toronto so we're walking to the venue," Horan says, quite jauntily. "Let's do it!" And then more reflectively: "In all of the years playing shows I don't think I've ever walked into a venue."
So that makes Toronto a first, then.
And not in a good way.
The bad ways in which the city is performing have been driven home by the Star's three-part series Anywhere but Toronto. The series cites the hard numbers: In the past two years 220,000 more Canadians have abandoned Ontario's capital than have arrived. Drilling into that number from July 2022, to July 2023, we see that fully a third of city escapees were between the ages of 25 and 39.
That's a crisis.
Traffic doesn't flow. Construction blankets the city. East enders have all but given up on visiting west end friends.
We know the knock-on effects that barriers to mobility have in the areas of stress and overall quality of life.
Then there's the money side of life. The cost of housing in Toronto has long been a reliable headline generator, from the stratospheric cost of single-family homes - increasingly an option only to those who are bootstrapped with family money - to a modest one-bedroom apartment now averaging $2,200 a month. As the series reports, Toronto city dwellers are increasingly finding and defining "home" in other areas of the province: Oshawa, Hamilton, the Niagara region. Or Calgary. Or Halifax.
Descriptions of the capital city they have left behind share a common theme: impersonal. Fraught. A grind. It is not too extreme to suggest we are becoming an unlivable city.
Toronto's tools to meet these immense challenges are limited. Cities are not an official order of government in this country but rather creatures of the province. City hall has limited ability to tax. It can't accumulate debt. But in the decades since our constitution was written, as more and more people have concentrated in metropolitan centres, especially in Toronto, many of the country's defining policy challenges have been left to relatively impotent - and often inadequately ambitious - city governments to solve, while Ottawa and Queen's Park happily push off their problems to municipalities and, inevitably, to future generations.
But despite these obstacles, even under existing constitutional constraints, progress is possible. Last winter, when the city and the province announced a "New Deal," echoing the phrasing of a Depression-era Franklin Delano Roosevelt, Mayor Olivia Chow championed a collaboration that resulted in significant funding for new subway trains (pending matching federal funds) and uploading the costs of the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway to the province. "When all orders of government work together on shared priorities, we can deliver more for the people of Toronto," Chow said.
Premier Doug Ford acknowledged that when Toronto succeeds, Ontario and Canada succeed.
That's a decades-old truism. That doesn't mean it will continue to hold true. What is needed now is transformational change, a pulling together of all levels of government, alongside the private sector and educational institutions, to chart a course for the city's future prosperity. We must find a way to make the new deal the basis for a lasting new approach to municipal governance that respects our city's outsized role in this country.
If that doesn't happen, well, let's leave it to Coun. Gord Perks to contemplate a city too expensive and dysfunctional for the just-graduated nurse, or your favourite local grocer. "What does Toronto become?" asks Perks. "It's not a pretty picture. It's a terrible, terrible future."
CAPTION: How bad has traffic become in Toronto? So bad that Irish singer-songwriter Niall Horan decided he was better off walking to his recent performance at Scotiabank Arena.Andreas Rentz Getty Images File Photo
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Songwriters; Traffic

Document 388 of 500

Will the LCBO close for two weeks? Sources say two sides are far apart as strike deadline looms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-lcbo-close-two-weeks-sources-say-sides-are/docview/3076062788/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+the+LCBO+close+for+two+weeks%3F+Sources+say+two+sides+are+far+apart+as+strike+deadline+looms&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They're still at the table - at least for now.
Contract talks between the LCBO and the union representing thousands of its workers are continuing, just hours ahead of a 12:01 a.m. Friday strike deadline, but it's looking more likely that workers are headed for the picket lines.
Sources familiar with negotiations said the two sides are talking but that the LCBO and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union are still far apart, with several major issues - including job security - not even having been discussed in detail yet.
"The province is sticking to its guns," one source said.
OPSEU has repeatedly criticized the provincial government's decision to open up Ontario's alcohol retail market, arguing that it puts LCBO jobs and profits at risk, while helping private retailers, including grocery and big box stores, as well as convenience stores.
"Front-line LCBO workers are proud that the revenues we help to generate give back to their communities, and we won't back down in our fight for a strong future for the LCBO and the public services funded by LCBO revenues," OPSEU bargaining committee chair Colleen MacLeod told the Star earlier this week. "Without a strong commitment to that future, we will have to strike."
The union also says that 70 per cent of LCBO workers are "casual," meaning they have no guaranteed hours, benefits or sick pay. The LCBO, meanwhile, argued that at least 50 per cent of casual employees are guaranteed at least 1,000 hours per year.
The union says LCBO wages range from $17.65 an hour to roughly $30. The LCBO says the range for customer service representatives goes from $17.85 to $32.58.
Colin Blachar, a spokesperson for Ontario's finance minister, said in an email that it's "disappointing that OPSEU could be heading toward an unnecessary strike that threatens people's ability to enjoy their summers. It's never been more clear that Ontario consumers need and deserve the same choice and convenience every other Canadian enjoys.
"We will not be privatizing the LCBO. The LCBO will continue to be a publicly owned retailer, as well as operating as the exclusive wholesaler for all retail, bars and restaurants selling alcohol and spirits."
Liberal MPP Stephen Blais, the party's critic for labour issues, called on the government to make a deal.
"LCBO workers deserve job security, livable wages and fair treatment. Doug Ford needs to do the right thing and come to a fair deal that respects workers, keeps Ontario working and doesn't disrupt the lives of hardworking Ontarians," Blais said in a written statement. "We know OPSEU workers are at the table ready to bargain for a fair deal, the government needs to do the same."
The LCBO has said all its stores will be closed for two weeks if there's a strike, but that 30 would reopen July 19, for limited shopping every Friday, Saturday and Sunday. It also said online ordering would still be available in the event of a strike, but said details wouldn't be available until a strike begins.
It's a more complicated set of negotiations than typical contract talks, where the main bone of contention is strictly money, argued labour relations professor Rafael Gomez.
"Because of all the changes in alcohol retailing, the union knows that there will be fewer workers at the LCBO by 2030. And likely, fewer LCBO stores," said Gomez, director of U of T's Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources.
"The government is going to push the union to either have high wages in return for less job security, or low wages and higher job security," Gomez said. "Either way, through attrition or store closures, the government will have fewer LCBO outlets and fewer LCBO employees by 2030."
CAPTION: The LCBO said online ordering would still be available in the event of a strike, but said details wouldn't be available until a strike begins.

Subject: Alcohol; Employees; Employment security

Business indexing term: Subject: Employees Employment security

With early election looming, Doug Ford is promoting the controversial Highway 413

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-early-election-looming-doug-ford-is/docview/3076062745/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+early+election+looming%2C+Doug+Ford+is+promoting+the+controversial+Highway+413&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 390 of 500

'It is a huge deal': 3 things to know about Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish's first strong-mayor moves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-huge-deal-3-things-know-about-mississauga/docview/3075692249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It+is+a+huge+deal%27%3A+3+things+to+know+about+Mississauga+Mayor+Carolyn+Parrish%27s+first+strong-mayor+moves&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish has made a flurry of staffing moves in the first few days she's been in office that political experts say will impact the culture at the city.
From what strong-mayor moves have happened so far to what experts say about the major staffing changes, here's three things to know about Parrish's early days leading Mississauga.
What's happened so far?
On June 26, Parrish reversed an earlier decision by former mayor Bonnie Crombie to delegate the strong-mayor powers to hire and fire city officials and has since made at least four appointments to the city's senior staff ranks.
Before strong-mayor powers, Mississauga's city manager and chief administrative officer (CAO) was responsible for hiring and dismissing staff.
Crombie essentially reinstated that authority to then CAO Shari Lichterman in July 2023, while keeping the strong-mayor power to remove and appoint the city manager and commissioners, who oversee Mississauga's major service departments like roads, transit and recreation.
Also on June 26, the city announced that Geoff Wright would be Mississauga's new CAO and that Lichterman was no longer in the role.
Lichterman told the Mississauga News that she didn't resign and was "let go" by Parrish.
The city manager/CAO role is essentially the highest-ranking and paid unelected official in a municipality, responsible for overseeing the delivery of all services based on council direction. They also aim to provide council with impartial advice on matters before the city.
According to published strong-mayor decisions, Wright is the city's interim CAO until Dec. 31, 2024.
Mississauga spokesperson Irene McCutcheon confirmed on June 27 that Andra Maxwell, who was city solicitor, had resigned.
"No other staff have been dismissed from the city," McCutcheon said.
The city solicitor advises council, committees and Mississauga executive staff on politically sensitive files and legal matters.
The role is also the head of Mississauga's legal services department, overseeing dozens of staff and handling litigation the city is involved in, including prosecutions and Ontario Land Tribunal hearings.
Maxwell joined the city's legal team in 2015 and was appointed city solicitor in 2019 following the retirement of Mary Ellen Bench, who held the role for 18 years.
Parrish used her strong-mayor powers on June 28 to appoint Domenic Tudino as interim city solicitor.
He has been with Mississauga's legal services team since 2002 and most recently managed the city's real estate law team, which works on commercial property transactions and agreements involving the city.
Parrish also used her strong-mayor powers to appoint Sam Rogers as the interim commissioner of transportation and works, who oversees MiWay and the city's road maintenance efforts, to replace Wright.
According to a June 28 mayoral decision, Parrish also appointed Emma Calvert as the city's interim director of infrastructure planning and engineering services, Rogers' former role.
To date, Parrish has also made strong-mayor-related council appointments.
She placed councillors Brad Butt, Stephen Dasko, Alvin Tedjo, Chris Fonseca, Joe Horneck and John Kovac in chair and vice-chair roles on Mississauga committees.
Parrish put herself as the new chair of the city's planning and development committee, which approves and rejects proposed housing projects in Mississauga.
What experts say
Zachary Spicer, associate professor in the school of public policy and administration at York University, says that when a CAO is removed, it's often because they've done something wrong, or something right, such as giving good advice the mayor doesn't like.
"I think for a lot of folks who are observers of the sector, this one was quite puzzling given Shari Lichterman was an exceptional public servant and, I think, a lot of people thought very, very highly of her," he said.
Spicer added that Wright is a very capable person to take on the role of CAO, but Parrish is risking "demoralizing" the city's staff with abrupt changes after years of stability in the city.
"You risk staff not being as enthusiastic about supporting you or your agenda or council as a whole," he says. "And you have to be very careful about that stuff."
Western University political science professor emeritus Andrew Sancton says the strong-mayor moves, including retaking the authority to hire and fire a large array of city staff, indicates that Parrish "wants to control the whole city staff quite closely."
"To the outside, non-municipal world, this might not seem like a big deal," he says.
But, Sancton added, for senior staff and city councillors, "it is a huge deal when a mayor fires a CAO without really consulting anybody and brings in a new one and a whole new administrative team if you want."
While Parrish has so far appointed only current city staff to fill vacant roles after Lichterman's departure, Sancton says he'll be concerned if the new mayor starts to appoint individuals with less municipal experience into major positions.
"Getting used to the, sort of, fishbowl enterprise that municipal government generally is, it's quite a different environment, than working for a private business."
What Parrish said
Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish did not respond to questions sent by the Mississauga News for this story by publication deadline.
She previously told the Mississauga News that she won't "discuss personnel" issues.
The Mississauga News asked why she felt it was necessary to take back the power to hire and fire city staff and to appoint a new CAO; whether she was concerned those moves would create a chilling effect among city staff and potentially discourage municipal staff; and why she appears to have changed course from a pledge, prior to becoming mayor, to only use strong-mayor powers for housing issues, if necessary.
In 2022, prior to the province giving enhanced powers to Mississauga mayors along with dozens of other municipalities in Ontario, Parrish was critical of the move, saying it would "put partisan politics into local government overnight."
CAPTION: Mississauga CAO Shari Lichterman (far right) along with former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie and city councillors Dipika Damerla, Joe Horneck and Alvin Tedjo last December.New Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish at her June 24 inauguration inside the Living Arts Centre.

Subject: Workforce planning; Mayors; Councils

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning

Pro-Palestine protest should not have stopped Pride parade

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pro-palestine-protest-should-not-have-stopped/docview/3075328508/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pro-Palestine+protest+should+not+have+stopped+Pride+parade&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't stop a protest with a protest
Thousands sing, dance and celebrate at Pride Parade until protesters strand marchers and floats mid-route, June 30
I don't think that any group representing a just cause should be allowed to disrupt a parade or demonstration by another group representing a just cause. In my opinion, the Pride organizers should allow demonstrators for a different cause to interrupt for a limited period, say 15 minutes, after which they should be removed using the police if necessary.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
What gives those who protest for the Coalition Against Pinkwashing - a group of queer and trans activists from Palestine solidarity groups - the right to step on others' right to protest or demonstrate? The Pride organizers had to cancel the parade to ensure the safety of all. The parade is the culmination of many, many protests for other purposes by courageous people as far back as the 1970s. Shame on those who, in fighting for their cause, trashed the well-earned celebration of freedom, and continued demonstration, via the time-honoured parade.
Mike Faye, Toronto
Celebrating Canada
It was a remarkable, exhilarating experience to participate in the Canada Day celebrations at Thomson Memorial Park in Scarborough on July 1. Families with roots in all parts of the world and all cultures were there to celebrate Canada and their contributions to our country. The park was bursting at the seams with families listening to the music, enjoying food, walking about, socializing, and honouring our country and what makes it exceptional. What a privilege to be part of the diverse fabric of Toronto.
Ron Ballantyne, Scarborough
Unknown soldier service 'incredibly moving'
Newfoundland soldier who died in the First World War laid to rest at home, July 1
The service in Newfoundland for the repatriation of an unknown soldier's remains on Canada Day was incredibly moving. No doubt all those who never received closure of a family member lost in battle were able to claim him as their own and the rest of us had the opportunity to express gratitude at an unimaginable sacrifice made for the lives we are living right now. We can look upon this occasion to reflect on the death and destruction war causes but we never learn. The lessons of the past repeat ad nauseam all over the world and threaten our way of life, but we don't seem to have the capacity to believe it until it's too late. Perhaps it's too easy to convince ourselves it won't happen again or too difficult to imagine that it might, but history is a persistent teacher showing us otherwise, and the next time there won't be any remains of an unknown soldier to bring home.
Julia Bowkun, Toronto
Abandoning carbon rebate is a first step for Trudeau
I have a simple solution to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's current unpopularity - abandon the carbon rebate program. It would take a major plank from the opposition's election strategy while addressing a source of consternation with voters of all stripes. Trudeau will be no worse off politically and it gives the liberals a fresh start to addressing climate change. A first step would be initiating consultation with provincial counterparts. We need to get very serious about climate change as a country and Trudeau's leadership is fundamental in developing solutions that have the endorsement of all provinces. Addressing climate change cannot be a political issue. It is a Canadian commitment to building a better world for future generations or rather not destroying the current one.
Ron Simpson, Toronto
Trudeau will lose the next election if he stays, but that's probably a good thing because the party with Trudeau at the helm has moved too far left. The next election, with or without Trudeau, will probably be a train wreck, but it will also be an opportunity to rebuild and bring the Liberal party back to the centre. Move closer to the centre, rebuild and the voters will come (back).
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
Remove Ford from PC caucus
Doug Ford removes MPP Goldie Ghamari from caucus after she met with British far-right figure, June 28
I am a 93 year old Canadian, with three more generations following me. I have no political allegiance or alliance, nor am I an activist. When I vote in an election, whether it be federal, provincial or municipal, I vote for the candidate or party that has the best interests of all Canadians. Premier Doug Ford recently removed Goldie Ghamari from the Progressive Conservative caucus for "repeated instances of serious lapses in judgment." Given that logic, I feel Ford should be removed after his repeated instances of serious lack of judgment, with policies and plans he has pulled back on (Greenbelt, blue licence plates) and others he has not.
Hedley Hooper, Markham
Robbie Robertson Square?
Council votes down attempt to derail Yonge-Dundas renaming, June 27
I have two better suggestions for the square. Each is a Toronto-born musician, tying into Yonge Street's musical history, and would be fitting because the square hosts live music.
Robbie Robertson, an Indigenous Canadian raised in Toronto and Six Nations, has deep ties to Yonge Street. As a young musician, he became a star guitarist performing at many venues on the strip. He made history at the corner of Yonge and Dundas in Friar's Tavern, where his band played often and rehearsed with Bob Dylan. Robertson later formed The Band, one of the most influential acts of the late 1960s and early 1970s, followed by a solo career. He was an advocate for Indigenous culture and is an officer of the Order of Canada.
Archie Alleyne, a Black Canadian drummer raised in the Kensington Market neighbourhood, was also part of the Yonge Street music scene. He performed with legends such as Lester Young, Billie Holiday and Ben Webster. He also opened the Underground Railroad, a Toronto soul food restaurant. He was well known in the Toronto jazz community and is an officer of the Order of Canada.
Alan Burns, Toronto
'Tina' deserves accolades
No great shakes: Here's what's missing from 'Tina - The Tina Turner Musical,' June 21
As lifelong devotees to musician Tina Turner, my husband and I were excited about attending the musical production of "Tina - The Tina Turner Musical" at the Ed Mirvish Theatre on June 26 until we read the negative reviews. It was with some trepidation that we prepared ourselves to experience a mediocre tribute to the late superstar, but thankfully, we instead found ourselves overwhelmed by the excellence of Zurin Villanueva's portrayal of Tina. In just a few hours the audience was given the vital details of Turner's struggles to attain fame while music guided the tragic journey. It would be an added tragedy if the public avoided attending this musical because of negative reviews.
Marlene & Rod Colmer, Scarborough
CAPTION: Pro-Palestinian protesters block Sunday's Pride Parade, near Yonge and Wellesley Streets in Toronto.

Document 392 of 500

Doug Ford did a good thing for Canadian media. Justin Trudeau should follow his lead

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-did-good-thing-canadian-media-justin/docview/3075328433/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+did+a+good+thing+for+Canadian+media.+Justin+Trudeau+should+follow+his+lead&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Across Canada, governments have supported journalism for generations. Since the founding of Canada's first newspaper, the Halifax Gazette, which dates to 1752, government advertising has been an important source of revenue for newspapers. Even prior to Confederation, direct supports, like the former Publications Assistance Program, which subsidized the postal delivery of nondaily newspapers, ensured that Canadians have access to high-quality Canadian news.
Today, community, regional and national news publishers are struggling with declining advertising revenue. In 2012, Canadian newspaper advertising revenue stood at $3.55 billion. Today, it is under $1 billion. Yet, four out of five Canadians still read newspapers, regardless of format, at least once a week - essentially unchanged since 2012.
While things will never go back to the way they were, and nor should they, governments can do a much better job supporting our domestic news businesses by addressing the advertising revenue challenge through their own advertising spending and procurement policies.
We were very encouraged by the recent news coming out of Premier Doug Ford's government in Ontario that it is directing crown corporations like the LCBO, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp. (OCRC), the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation (OLG), and Metrolinx to invest 25 per cent of their advertising budget in Ontario-based news businesses. This is forward-looking smart policy that other provinces, municipalities, and the federal government can follow, and it does not involve any additional taxpayer dollars.
Ford recognizes the importance of journalism to Ontario's economy. According to the Government of Canada's Job Bank, there are 4,350 journalists working in Ontario. The impact of those journalists goes beyond the economy as they keep communities across the province connected, informed, and engaged.
One of the most powerful tools in any government's policy tool kit is procurement. Procurement can help governments advance socio-economic policy objectives, including creating jobs and delivering better outcomes. When the Government of Canada spends more per year on advertising with China-based TikTok ($1,113,056) than it does on all of Canada's print publications combined, something is seriously wrong with government advertising procurement policies. The same holds true for other orders of government.
Ontario isn't the first jurisdiction to adopt an "earmark" approach when it comes to government advertising. New York City's Local Law 83 mandates the creation of an annual spending report on advertising, displaying each agency's spending and compliance toward spending 50 per cent of advertising on ethnic and community media.
Subscription revenue alone may be enough to fund websites devoted to intelligent commentary, but fact-based, fact-checked coverage of schools, cops, courts, politicians, and businesses - holding the powerful to account - costs real money and demands advertising revenue.
We encourage governments at all levels, as well as our leading corporations - the large banks, telcos, and retailers - to take a cue from the Government of Ontario and follow its lead in supporting our domestic news publishing industry by spending 25 per cent of their advertising budget in newspaper and news publications. This will allow us to reinvest in high-quality Canadian journalistic content and in innovative techniques that harness the power of digital tools to engage audiences and sustain viable news businesses that serve the public interest.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford takes part in a news conference at Ottawa city hall on April 29, 2024. Ford is directing crown corporations like the LCBO, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp. (OCRC), the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation (OLG), and Metrolinx to invest 25 per cent of their advertising budget in Ontario-based news businesses.
CREDIT: Paul Deegan Contributor

Subject: Journalism; Advertising revenue; Newspapers; Budgets; Advertising expenditures; Government

Document 393 of 500

Doug Ford promises 25 per cent of government agency, provincial advertising budgets to local media outlets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promises-25-per-cent-government-agency/docview/3075328280/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promises+25+per+cent+of+government+agency%2C+provincial+advertising+budgets+to+local+media+outlets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is throwing the beleaguered journalism industry a financial lifeline.
In memos to government agencies, boards and commissions including the LCBO and Metrolinx, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has mandated that the organizations must spend at least 25 per cent of their advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations.
"Ontario's major government agencies are some of the largest advertisers in the province, together spending well over $100 million on marketing each year," Ford wrote in a memo sent to the LCBO late last week.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," Ford added. Other agencies, boards and commissions received similar memos.
Ford also committed to spending 25 per cent of the government's own advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations. Sources familiar with government finances estimated conservatively that the government itself spends at least $100 million per year in advertising.
Toronto Star owner and publisher Jordan Bitove, who has called for a similar commitment from Canadian businesses, praised the government decision, saying it comes at a crucial time for the industry, and for democracy.
"Premier Ford and his government have shown outstanding leadership in supporting Canadian-owned and operated journalism," said Bitove. "This support will have a profound impact in ensuring Ontarians are better informed."
With the proliferation of misinformation on social media eating away at democracy's foundations, traditional journalism stands as a beacon of reliable information, Bitove added.
"There is no better investment in democracy than supporting quality Canadian journalism. Given what is going on in the world, it has never been more important," Bitove said.
The Ontario government's new mandate will be in place by September, and will apply to Ontario-based publishers defined as "qualified Canadian journalism organizations" by the Canada Revenue Agency.
Paul Deegan, president of industry association News Media Canada, also praised Ford's decision, and called on other levels of government to follow suit.
"This is brilliant. We need municipalities, other provinces and the feds to adopt a similarly smart approach to advertising," Deegan said.
"Governments should be spending taxpayer dollars with trusted news mastheads, rather than with foreign social media behemoths that foment misinformation and disinformation for cheap clicks."
In the 2022-23 fiscal year, the federal government spent just over $86 million on advertising.
Since 2019, the federal government has funded the Local Journalism Initiative, a program that gives journalism organizations funding to hire reporters to cover underserved communities. In March, the federal government announced a three-year, $58.8-million extension of the program.
The Ontario decision comes at a time when journalism organizations across the country are struggling for survival.
In March, SaltWire Network and Halifax Herald Ltd. were forced into receivership by their biggest creditor, which said the companies owed more than $90 million. Last week, the companies received an extension to their protection from creditors until Aug. 9, amid reports that a potential purchaser was closing in on a deal that would see at least some parts of the companies kept alive.
In January, creditors approved an insolvency proposal by Metroland, a sister company of the Star.
Metroland is owned by Torstar Corp., which is in turn controlled by NordStar Capital.
As part of a restructuring plan announced last September, Metroland cut 605 jobs - roughly two-thirds of its workforce - and turned 71 weekly newspapers into online-only publications. Just over 100 of the terminated workers are unionized and represented by Unifor.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford also committed to spending 25 per cent of the government's own advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations.

Subject: Government agencies; Journalism; False information; Advertising; Budgets; Democracy; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising Budgets Social networks

Document 394 of 500

Protesters depart U of T encampment peacefully but defiantly: 'The world is talking about what the students have done here'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/protesters-depart-u-t-encampment-peacefully/docview/3075328246/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protesters+depart+U+of+T+encampment+peacefully+but+defiantly%3A+%27The+world+is+talking+about+what+the+students+have+done+here%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The last of U of T's encampment tents and fencing have been dismantled and protesters vacated the King's College Circle grounds with a defiant march across campus.
"We are leaving on our terms to protect our community from the police violence U of T wants to unleash on us," said spokesperson Mohammad Yassin.
Pro-Palestinian protesters had been given until 6 p.m. Wednesday to vacate King's College Circle, a day after a judge granted the university's request for an injunction against the protest camp that has been in place since the beginning of May.
"I am pleased that the protesters have ended the encampment peacefully so that front campus can be restored and returned to the entire community. Members of our community continue to be free to exercise their right to free speech and lawful protest at the University of Toronto," University of Toronto President Meric Gertler said in a statement on Wednesday evening.
Protesters appeared to be packing up most of the day on Wednesday, and by mid-afternoon, all of the smaller tents had been taken down, with piles of supplies, equipment and garbage being collected by the entrance to be carted away.
The large central tent that had served as a gathering place for protesters and housed a kitchen and other supplies, was the last structure standing, but it too was dismantled.
Large patches of yellowed grass were all that remained of many of the tents that housed protesters for the past two months.
A rally started just after 3:30 p.m. with a few hundred gathered, including some CUPE and OPSEU presence, in the empty field.
Organizer Sara Rasikh, flanked by two large speakers, lead chants drawing attention to the death toll in Gaza. Amid cheers, she pointed out judge Markus Koehnen did not find the encampment was antisemitic.
One speaker specifically thanked the anti-Zionist Jewish community for its support, without which the encampment would not have been possible.
"The world is talking about Palestine, the world is talking about what the students have done here."
Protesters were heard chanting, "U of T you will see, Palestine will be free."
Around 5 p.m. the rally marched through campus away from the remains of the encampment.
While some had speculated that a hardcore group of protesters was still be preparing to confront police, people were quietly and steadily tearing down the encampment throughout the day.
"We are urging co-operation to prevent escalation," Toronto police said earlier in a statement on X.
Some protesters busied themselves working on a papier-mâché sculpture, a last monument to the space they had taken over for more than two months.
In a 96-page decision released Tuesday afternoon, Superior Court Justice Markus Koehnen found that the university had made a strong case for trespassing and that it has been dispossessed of its property.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop - who from the outset had urged the University of Toronto to take action to end the encampment - said Wednesday that "post-secondary institutions have an obligation to protect the safety and well-being of all students, faculty and staff so that students can continue to learn without interruption."
In a statement to the Star, she added that "like any court order, this order should be followed to ensure that happens. We understand the university has been in contact with the local authorities on this matter."
Within days of protesters setting up tents on the grounds at U of T, both Dunlop and Premier Doug Ford said they wanted them removed.
Ford, calling the situation "unacceptable," said he'd heard from parents worried about harassment and bullying on campus.
In May, Dunlop spoke to the president of U of T by phone to express her "concern for what we're seeing on campuses. I want to know what the plan is to end the encampment ... The premier and myself have been very clear that we're not supportive of encampments on campuses."
U of T "had a very strong statement early on that (encampments) would be considered trespassing ... and that action would be taken. I think we're at the point where we need to end the encampments," she said at the time.
Pro-Palestinian demonstrators have also set up camp at a number of schools across the province, including Western, McMaster and University of Ottawa.
York University was able to quickly put an end to an encampment on its campus, calling in the police to dismantle it just hours after it began.
Meanwhile, the University of Waterloo is now suing those involved in the encampment for $1.5 million in damages caused by their protest.
A deal to end the encampment with a resolution eluded both parties
In an effort to avoid going to court, protesters said they and their lawyers submitted a "settlement" offer to U of T in June. This offer dropped one of protesters' core demands - for U of T to sever relations with Israeli universities that have military ties or are operating in occupied Palestinian territory. U of T president Meric Gertler had previously called this ask "antithetical to the university's fundamental mission and values."
Still, U of T rejected the proposal. The university then submitted a counter-offer. Protesters have indicated this offer is unsatisfying. Neither they nor the university would share the terms of the counter-offer with the Star.
"Not only did the university refuse our offer, they told us any offer they agreed to would require us not to fight this injunction, so that the administration could still obtain an order restricting protests on campus," said encampment spokesperson Erin Mackey on Wednesday outside the courthouse. "The University of Toronto would rather call the police on its own students than divest. "
The encampment was started with the goal of pressuring U of T into restructuring its finances and academic relationships in order to sanction Israel amid its war effort in Gaza.
Read the full story by Star reporters Ben Cohen and Marco Chown Oved
Lawyers pushed back on U of T's bid to clear encampmentThe grassy field at the centre of the University of Toronto's downtown campus is not just some piece of private property where the rights of the landlord are paramount, a courtroom heard June 20.
It is a traditional space of public debate, one where society's most divisive issues are hashed out, and it must be protected, argued lawyers for a pro-Palestinian protest camp.
In this case, they said, protecting it means denying the university's request for an order to remove an encampment that has been on campus since the beginning of May.
Read the full story from Star reporters Marco Chown Oved and Ben Cohen
Inside the polarizing U of T encampment
The Star previously visited the encampment for an extended tour, searching for understanding of a movement whose nature and meaning have been bitterly contested.
Inside of the University of Toronto protest encampment - marked by a tall fence reinforced with zip ties and guarded by bouncers - there was an art installation.
Using invasive plant species like the "dog-strangling vine," a local florist was creating a "symbol of ongoing colonialization."
The invasive species were meant to represent Zionists, the florist said.
The dehumanizing display, which sat underneath a Progress Pride flag, was created in a place its architects say was founded on compassion.
Read here for the full story from Star reporter Ben Cohen.
