Document 409 of 500

He depends on his power wheelchair - but is frustrated he can't count on the Ontario government to service it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/he-depends-on-his-power-wheelchair-is-frustrated/docview/3074125847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+depends+on+his+power+wheelchair+-+but+is+frustrated+he+can%27t+count+on+the+Ontario+government+to+service+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three years after a debilitating stroke left him largely immobile, retired lawyer Dan MacDonald is in a constant battle to get his power wheelchair to fit properly.
The struggle - faced by others, disability advocates say - is raising new questions about Ontario's Assistive Devices Program (ADP), which helps people with long-term disabilities afford essential items like wheelchairs, hearing aids, insulin pumps, home oxygen and more.
MacDonald's emailed complaints to Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Premier Doug Ford's office on June 7 about an exclusive power wheelchair supplier arrangement under the ADP, and concerns that clients have nowhere to turn if service is unsatisfactory, got an answer Thursday.
He was not impressed, and shared it with the Star.
"The Ministry of Health is sorry to hear of the challenges you've faced with your wheelchair," said the unsigned email reply from the ministry's correspondence and corporate support unit.
"The ADP strives to provide customer-oriented support ... ADP staff will take your concerns into consideration," the reply added, providing MacDonald with a separate ADP email address to contact with any "further concerns."
MacDonald says he'd already emailed that address, and feels "very frustrated."
"That correspondence begs the question of why ADP has not seen fit to respond to my previous emails concerning the difficulties I have encountered," he said.
Jones's office declined to comment specifically on MacDonald's situation, although the Ministry of Health sent a separate statement saying it stands by the program and noting it has procedures for handling complaints.
But the ministry does not have statistics on complaints readily available and does not track client satisfaction.
MacDonald feels like he's hit a brick wall and filed a complaint with the office of Ontario's ombudsman, hoping the staff there can get action.
"The chair is what I'm in most of the day. It's essential that I'm comfortable," said MacDonald, 66. He suffered a serious brain stem stroke in 2021 that left the retired litigation lawyer with limited use of his hands and arms.
Getting the chair's cushions and other components to fit and not cause pressure sores - the wheelchair equivalent of bedsores - is a "trial-and-error" process that can entail many appointments with a technician, he says.
"The disabled population faces numerous challenges, physically, emotionally and financially," MacDonald wrote in a recent appeal to Jones, which he shared with the Star.
"Obtaining reliable, dependable and timely service for something which is essential to their mobility and independence should not be an additional challenge."
Frustrated and now dealing with a retired power wheelchair seating technician as a last resort, MacDonald is urging the Ford government to open the power wheelchair repair business under the ADP to competition.
"There has to be a better way," said MacDonald, who is frustrated by the continuing challenges following the stroke that "upended" his life with his wife Marie, a former senior IT executive who is now his chief caregiver.
The unexpected stroke hit 18 months after he retired, putting him in hospital for weeks, and causing breathing problems that resulted in two intubations and six months on a feeding tube.
"I took care of myself. I was reasonably fit, wasn't overweight," he said. "I asked one of my doctors, 'Why did this happen?' He said, 'You've lost the lottery.'"
Ministry of Health officials say the wheelchair contract was assumed by a company called Motion LP - which has branches across the province selling and servicing "high technology power wheelchairs" - from Shoppers Home Health Care in 2016.
As such, the ministry funds Motion to run the "central equipment pool" or CEP, first established in 1996, for the power wheelchairs. The pool provides clients with "new and recycled" wheelchairs "at discounted prices and provides a rebate on the client's costs once the equipment is returned."
"Due to the cost and complexity of power dynamic positioning wheelchairs, only a few select vendors have the expertise and technology to undertake management of the CEP," ministry spokesperson Adriana Dutkiewicz said in a statement to the Star.
"The ministry receives and tracks complaints ... the complaints are reviewed, investigated and resolved by the program. Any concerns related to client safety are taken extremely seriously, in accordance with the government's agreement with Motion."
Ontario's auditor general found eight years ago that while the contract with Shoppers was reached through a competitive bidding process, the ministry "assigned" the contract to Motion.
"We noted concerns ... about the quality of services provided (by Motion), including a lack of responsiveness to client inquiries," the auditor general's office said in a statement to the Star this week.
The company was required to submit an annual service plan to the ministry and year-end reports "to ensure the appropriate oversight," including performance metrics, according to a follow-up report in 2020.
But MacDonald's troubles with Motion have included leaving detailed voice-mail messages but not getting return calls, a lack of promised followup from in-person appointments, delays in getting parts and a wheelchair battery, and being called in for a battery replacement that had to wait until the next day for a more experienced technician to install it.
These are problems for disabled people who rely on their power wheelchairs for everything from getting around the house to getting to work or medical and physiotherapy appointments.
MacDonald and others say service provided by Motion is uneven. After his repeated complaints, the company has agreed to service MacDonald, who lives in Mississauga, from its more distant Hamilton office.
Motion says it strives to provide "the best possible client experience in support of our vision to make life accessible for everyone" and notes the customization of power chairs is complicated - something MacDonald acknowledges.
"Each individual has unique needs," chief executive officer Sue Gilpin said in a statement to the Star.
Gilpin said the timeline for repairs and adjustments can depend on recommendations from therapists and approvals from third-party funders, and the ordering and delivery of parts.
"We are not permitted to make changes to a client's equipment without approval and direction from the therapist," she added.
"Given such complexities and the wide range of mobility and accessibility needs of our clients, there have been exceptional circumstances in which we did not achieve the customer service standards that we set out for our business," she acknowledged.
"In such cases we always try to work directly with the client to understand their experience and identify any opportunities for improvement."
In MacDonald's case, that included sending staff for extra training, as Gilpin acknowledged in a letter the retired lawyer shared with the Star.
Jeff Preston, an associate professor of disability studies at King's University College in London, Ont., says the monopoly on service enjoyed by Motion is a "disservice" to the disabled community.
"The result has been to wipe out the service, repair and sales industry," said Preston, who has used a wheelchair for 24 years after being born with a rare form of muscular dystrophy.
"I've heard wildly different reports of the quality of care and quality of support that people receive from one unit to another based on where they're living," he said. "People are trapped as a result."
He applauded MacDonald for going public and drawing attention to the situation.
"Not everybody has the energy, the training, the ability, the passion to be able to speak up."
A leading advocate for the disabled says problems like MacDonald's are a "recurring issue" in a way that other Ontarians might not understand.
"When you take your car in for service, you can rent a car or take an Uber but you can't do that with a wheelchair because a lot of them are customized," says David Lepofsky of the Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act Alliance.
Lepofsky supports MacDonald's call to end the service monopoly.
"You want the providers to compete, to give good service. You don't want to give anyone a monopoly over basic service."
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) - who has been helping Kitchener-area mother Laurie O'Brien get answers six years after her adult son, David, died while waiting for wheelchair improvements - said it's time for a review of ADP.
"We need a robust system to make sure that people are being served properly. A lot of the people I know are worried, if they speak up, are they going to get worse service."
Clancy is planning a town-hall meeting where ADP clients can air their concerns "so that we can start nipping this in the bud."
Previous examinations of the ADP by the auditor general have also focused on weak oversight of sales and claims.
CAPTION: Dan MacDonald is a retired lawyer who relies on an electric wheelchair since he had a stroke.

Subject: Complaints; Stroke; Correspondence; Pressure ulcers; Wheelchairs; Handicapped accessibility; Disability; Ontarians with Disabilities Act-Ontario

Document 410 of 500

The Ontario Science Centre never should have been Doug Ford's to close

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-science-centre-never-should-have-been/docview/3074125646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ontario+Science+Centre+never+should+have+been+Doug+Ford%27s+to+close&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It could be the feel-good story of the year: a beloved and popular attraction is saved from closure and demolition by concerned citizens. Working together, they find wealthy benefactors who pledge the funds to execute needed repairs, and an architect willing to do the work pro-bono. The community effort spares an impoverished government from having to make an unpopular decision, as much as sparing taxpayers the additional financial burden.
Thanks to the altruism of our social betters, and the "outside the box" thinking of a plucky white-collar class, the Ontario Science Centre is saved. A new generation of children are thus encouraged to pursue rewarding careers in STEM.
This might be the spin applied to the saga of the science centre if Ontario Premier Doug Ford can be convinced to accept the numerous generous offers that have come in over the last week to save it.
The problem is that it didn't have to be this way.
That the science centre needed saving in the first place is demonstrative of the features - not the bugs - of our political and governance system.
For your consideration: If the science centre was the city's responsibility, would we be in this predicament? Probably not. This deluge of public support for the science centre has led city council to pass a motion looking into the feasibility of taking over its operations.
Consider as well that the lion's share of public opposition to the science centre's closing has come from the citizens of Toronto. Similarly, it's the people of Toronto who have been most vocal about the privatization and disfigurement of Ontario Place.
This isn't simply a reflection of the fact these public spaces are immediately accessible to most Torontonians, but that it is Torontonians who most need immediate access to public spaces. It shouldn't surprise anyone that the people of Toronto would be most vocal in their opposition to Ford's ideologically-motivated process of public disinvestment, as it has had a debilitating effect on Toronto and its citizens first and foremost.
The consequences of chronic disinvestment in urban areas by upper levels of government has bedevilled North American cities for about 50 years. The problem - particularly in American cities in the area now known as the "rust belt" - was initially one of suburban flight sapping cities' urban tax bases, thus resulting in shrinking budgets, austerity, and eventually, severe population and economic loss. Upper levels of government - either at the county or state level - attempted to rein-in depopulation and deindustrialization, to varying degrees of success and failure.
Toronto experienced aspects of these processes while also being shielded from some of their worst manifestations in part because it evolved into becoming the nation's corporate and business capital around the same time as depopulation and deindustrialization were ravaging other major cities. That Toronto is Ontario's capital certainly helped as well. Most premiers want their capital cities to shine and understand - at least - that the needs of big cities can be very different from other regions of the province, or the province as a whole.
Where Doug Ford is unique amongst premiers is that he seems to take no interest in the fact his often-reckless decisions can negatively impact the lives of so many people he ostensibly represents. He seems to govern in spite of Toronto, all while seeking to remake the city according to his whims. What Torontonians want never seems to enter into consideration.
If Toronto had the financial tools and political sovereignty necessary to provide the services and protect and promote the spaces its citizens desire, we wouldn't be discussing the science centre's closure, but likely its expansion. We likely wouldn't be spending public money to privatize Ontario Place either. Our schools probably wouldn't be falling into disrepair, and our hospitals wouldn't be so overcrowded, because we'd be acting on the recommendations of our public health officials, rather than dismissing them.
That private individuals have come forward to save the science centre - and that this option was not immediately accepted by the government - is indeed suspect, but let's not let this distract us from the underlying problem. Toronto is denied the right to do what's best for itself by politicians who campaign on their contempt for the city. They see public assets as exploitable business opportunities, and they spend public money seemingly without consulting the public.
This is the system we have, and it consistently doesn't work for us. That we are so consistently denied the right to do better for ourselves isn't a bug, it's a feature.
It begs the question: why do we continue to put up with this?
CAPTION: Judging from the outcry of Torontonians at the closure of the Ontario Science Centre, writer Taylor C. Noakes ponders if the public attraction would be in this position if it fell under the city's responsibility. Probably not, he writes.
CREDIT: Taylor C. Noakes Contributor

Subject: Public spaces; Privatization; Science; Deindustrialization; Population decline; Cities

Document 411 of 500

Science Centre needs fixing, not fencing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/science-centre-needs-fixing-not-fencing/docview/3073876464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Science+Centre+needs+fixing%2C+not+fencing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a famous scene in the 1967 film "The Graduate" when a young Dustin Hoffman receives career advice. "One word," says an older man. "Plastics." You see, there was a great future in the plastics industry then.
For any new graduates in Toronto this year, though, that one word might be "fencing." It's seemingly a boom time for the fence industry here. Fences make good neighbours, they say, but let's look at the city through its fencing and you decide if it's good or not.
Last weekend I went to see the new fence at the Ontario Science Centre. A pilgrimage, really, as the fence is so big and extensive I consider it a wonder of modern instant infrastructure. It should have its own interpretive exhibit in, say, a local science centre.
The tall metal fence runs for hundreds of metres along Don Mills Road, along the south side of the site and into the ravine below, well away from the building itself. On Sunday, there were about a dozen security people along the fence and "private property" signs affixed to it. Even the massive sign on Don Mills, alternately flashing "Closed" and "Fermé," had its very own fence surrounding it as if it, too, was a threat.
It's remarkable to think how much fencing is sitting in reserves, ready to be called up at 4 p.m. on a Friday in the summertime when the Doug Ford government announces it's shutting down the Science Centre immediately.
The government says an engineering report warns of imminent structural failure, though Canadian Architect magazine's Elsa Lam did a deep dive into the report and suggests "the building's key exhibition areas could continue to operate safely for years to come - even if the Ontario government chooses not to invest in any structural roof repairs this year." The CBC also reports that about 400 aging public buildings in Ontario have the same roof panels blamed for safety risks at the Science Centre, but they all remain open.
Repairs and routine maintenance are what this and previous governments should have been doing all along. Imagine if fences were put around all the buildings and houses that needed some work in Toronto: most of the city would be fenced off. Repair and maintenance is a normal part of older buildings here and around the world, including ones far older than the Science Centre, a building its architect, Raymond Moriyama, said was built to last 250 years.
Never mind that, the ability of our various governments to fence things off is impressive. World class, even. The new Science Centre fencing now connects to the fence around the nearby - but yet to open - Eglinton Crosstown LRT station, tragically named after the Science Centre. To travel along the length of Eglinton is to see station after station fenced off on this much-delayed transit line. Eerily, they often seem open, with digital screens working and the lights on, as if they're a portal into some alternate Toronto where transit works.
It's too bad the Ontario government can't complete this delayed transit project with the efficiency it can put up a fence. The Crosstown has been delayed so long, I almost fear the stations will get so old they'll need repairs before they can open, necessitating their permanent closure like the Science Centre. That is the Ontario way.
Toronto's recent history with fencing is equally fraught. Fences were put up quickly in several parks after the violent encampment evictions of 2021, preventing the use of large swaths of those parks for months afterwards. This June was also the 14th anniversary of the G20 where a fence contained a large part of the financial district during what became one of the biggest mass violations of human and civil rights in Canadian history.
Our leaders do like fences, but sometimes they don't do what they were intended to do. There's the now-infamous fence at Ontario Place that became a public billboard for protest. At the University of Toronto, a fence erected around King's College Circle to prevent demonstrations has made the pro-Palestinian encampment more formidable, almost defining it - and serving as a billboard for messages.
It is also possible to breach Toronto's fences, literally and figuratively. George Hislop Park east of Yonge between Charles and Isabella streets has been fenced off since 2020 for a renovation, but work is finally well underway. As well, after much painful debate and failed attempts, Torontonians can now enjoy booze in (some) city parks. Unlike patio rules, which keep imbibers safe in fenced corrals, people can wander free range (a little). Like with so many other places around the world, nothing remarkable happened when this was finally allowed.
Putting up a fence may not seem to be the neighbourly thing to do, but anyone looking for unlimited career opportunities in Ontario could do worse than getting in on the barrier business.
CAPTION: The tall metal fence at the Ontario Science Centre runs for hundreds of metres along Don Mills Road, along the south side of the site and into the ravine below. Repairs and routine maintenance are what our governments should have been doing all along, Shawn Micallef writes.Shawn Micallef photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Science; Fences

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: Science Centre needs fixing, not fencing

Document 412 of 500

Rise and fall of a beloved institution: How the province's cherished museum fell into a state of disrepair over the years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rise-fall-beloved-institution/docview/3073873704/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rise+and+fall+of+a+beloved+institution%3A+How+the+province%27s+cherished+museum+fell+into+a+state+of+disrepair+over+the+years&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For a 10-year-old boy, it was a rock from out of this world that left him wanting to reach the stars.
"My biggest 'whoa' moment at the Ontario Science Centre was not just running into this hall of all these theories turned into practice, where I could run around and actually try stuff out, but to see a small piece of something that I had been imagining," said former Canadian astronaut Chris Hadfield. "A small, actual piece of the moon that Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin had picked up and brought all the way back on Apollo and put it into a container and then shipped to Toronto and put right there for me to see it."
Hadfield was featured in a fundraising video for the Science Centre in 2020. His moon rock experience happened shortly after the centre opened and, like generations of children in Ontario, he had fun and memorable moments created at the institution. Things came full orbit for Hadfield, who was the basis of an exhibit there in 2015. It is that kind of a spark that has motivated countless people that have worked at the Science Centre and was the key behind architect Raymond Moriyama's vision of a place where people could interact with and immerse themselves in science.
With the abrupt closing of the Science Centre last month due to an engineering report that said roof panels are in danger of collapsing, there has been an outpouring of emotion and soul searching about what the centre means to us. There have been pledges of financial support, demonstrations by people in the neighbourhood and an outcry from scientists, architects and philanthropists here and around the globe.
But how did we get to this point, where a building designed by one of Canada's most famous architects has fallen into such a state of disrepair? Where a place that should be a beacon for evidence-based discussion has become fraught with differing opinions about what should be done with it now?
In discussions with former and current staff, members of the board of trustees and former CEOs, a number of long-standing issues have been cited. The Science Centre's tight control from the provincial government has left it chronically underfunded, leading to a failure to innovate and evolve and a lack of maintenance on an aging building. Some also point to the issues with an ill-fitting and powerful union that also limits the room to manoeuvre.
To start, we have to look at its origin story. The Ontario Science Centre was a grand experiment for a number of reasons. It was away from downtown, nestled into a ravine at Don Mills and Eglinton. The Exploratorium in San Francisco and our Science Centre were the first museums of their kind, and have now inspired more than 300 similar ones all over the world, with many using the Toronto institution as the blueprint for how they bring science to the masses.
Architect Moriyama and the other collaborators behind the Science Centre wanted to create something different, something interactive where children could touch and feel the science in their hands. In the late '60s, when it was being conceived, it was in vogue for governments to have a strong hand in projects, which meant that it was tightly controlled by the province, as opposed to other institutions, like the Royal Ontario Museum or the Art Gallery of Ontario.
"Bill Davis conceived the Ontario Science Centre as a Centennial project, which actually opened in 1969," said Nancy Lockhart, who was board chair from 1998 to 2006. "And it was the style at the time to have Schedule One government agencies, which tied you very, very closely to government and that became a real impediment as time went on. But when the Science Centre was constituted, it got 92 per cent of its funding, I believe, from the provincial government. The rest was generated through parking and admission."
The year 2006 saw the last major influx of provincial cash for the centre, $15 million as well as a successful $40-million fundraising campaign. The money was used to give the institution a re-energizing facelift and attract blockbuster draws, most notably in 2009 with Body Worlds and the Story of the Heart, an internationally created exhibit focusing on the human body, which helped boost attendance.
But subsequent governments weren't willing to invest, and the institution's ability to generate funds on its own was limited through being beholden to the province.
"We needed government approval for everything, whereas the ROM or the AGO would have had more freedom to operate or to act," said Lockhart.
One example she points out was during her tenure, there was an opportunity identified that would have generated $11 million a year by building exhibitions that would tour the world. But there was no space to do the work, and the ability to staff up or staff down depending on work was limited because of union constraints. She said they needed $2 million as a startup cost, but there was no appetite from government to provide it.
"I'd say benign neglect would be a good way of describing what happened over successive governments of every stripe," said Lockhart. "Each and every year, the Science Centre had to prepare a very detailed business plan using a very lengthy and repetitive government template. That business plan had to be submitted by the end of the year, by the beginning of December, I think. There wasn't a single year I was there where that plan was approved before the following November. So that tells you that nobody was looking at it.
"It was really frustrating. Each and every year we put in requests for repairs, for modifications, but basically most years, we ended up with a flat budget, which meant a negative budget because the wages, which were the biggest part of the budget, went up and we had no control over them."
That was another issue. Because it was run by the government, all the employees are Ontario public servants and part of a much bigger union, which gave Science Centre leadership no negotiating power over their employees.
"The Ontario Science Centre should always have been run like a business," said Lockhart.
The world was also changing, and the centre's information and interactivity had to compete with what people had in the palm of their hands, said Lesley Lewis, who was CEO from 1998 to 2014.
"People had access to vast amounts of information on their computers and ultimately on their phones," Lewis said. "So, it was a different competitive environment."
By the 2010s, bigger screens - and one of the Science Centre's big draws - were becoming much more commonplace.
"Another thing that created some problems in the Science Centre, and similar places around the world, is that many had invested big time in Imax theatres," Lewis said. "And for a while, they brought in a huge amount of people, but suddenly it wasn't drawing unique visitors, because in a city like Toronto, suddenly there were lots of Imax screens, not just the one in Ontario Place and the one at the Science Centre."
Budgets remained flat during the 2010s, and that started to manifest itself in other ways.
"What I saw during my time there was an increasing reliance on what we called partnerships, but were really kind of sponsorships of various spaces," said Kevin Von Appen, who worked at the Science Centre in various capacities over 40 years, before retiring three years ago as the director of science communications.
"So you had the Shoppers Drug Mart Imax Theatre, not the Ontario Science Centre Imax Theatre. You had the Procter and Gamble Great Hall, not simply the Great Hall. Those changes started in the '80s in earnest when I first came to the Science Centre, and they only accelerated over the time that I was there."
He said the problem with that is it leads to hiring more people in roles promoting sponsorships or seeking more entrepreneurial activities.
"It's about where you're putting your resources, and over time, you wind up with maybe fewer scientists, designers and researchers."
A good year for Science Centre attendance was always around one million visitors, with some banner years fuelled by bigger exhibitions.
With a lack of funding, there were a dearth of those big draws.
Maurice Bitran, who was CEO and chief scientist from 2014 to 2019, recently wrote an opinion piece for the Star, saying: "I discovered the many challenges it faced: the roof leaked whenever it rained, there was no working capital to develop new exhibits or to bring new shows, and the auditors had even put a liquidity note in the financial statements. Not surprisingly, attendance was in decline. For decades, and under governments of all political stripes, this cherished Ontario gem had suffered from chronic underfunding and insufficient infrastructure maintenance."
In 2019, a report - and a series of columns by the Star's Martin Regg Cohn - supported the idea of revitalizing Ontario Place by moving the science centre to the waterfront. Over the next few years, the tourism ministry and Infrastructure Ontario released reports on putting the two projects together, calling it a priority project and commissioning business cases and reports on the feasibility of the plan. Then COVID-19 hit, and in March 2020 the centre was shut down for almost two years, shifting to virtual projects.
In 2022, the bridge linking the main hall was closed because of the risk of structural failure, and a report estimated repair costs at $16 million. Since then, shuttle buses have been ferrying people between buildings.
In April 2023, Infrastructure Ontario put a proposal together that said moving the Science Centre to the waterfront, with integrated parking with the Therme Spa, would help meet the province's obligations to that company. On April 18, Premier Doug Ford announced the Science Centre's planned relocation to Ontario Place.
Then, as reported last week, the Rimkus engineering report landed, which found that some of the roof panels were in danger of collapsing due to a snow load or heavy rain. That report said it could cost $22 million to $40 million and take two to five years to repair the three buildings, it also said fixes in "high risk" locations could cost as little as $522,500.
What the report did not say was that the Ontario Science Centre needed to be closed immediately. That was a decision made by the current board of trustees, and supported by the government.
"One must remember that this was a warning, a health and safety warning, and as a representative of government, I have to take that seriously," said Kinga Surma, Ontario's infrastructure minister, last week. "There is nothing more important than the health and safety of people, of workers and of children."
Lewis said the shutdown has been felt throughout the community of science museums around the world, shocking many of her colleagues that hold this Canadian institution in high regard.
On its last day of operation, families from across Ontario were still heading toward the admission desk as the news broke, while workers put up fencing at the entrance to the parking lots. Employees also learned of the closing that afternoon, with a few walking around the entrance near closing time, in a daze.
Some hugged and consoled each other. A building steeped in science had left so many unknowns.
CAPTION: With the abrupt closing of the Science Centre last week due to an engineering report that said roof panels are in danger of collapsing, there has been an outpouring of emotion and soul searching about what the centre has meant to Ontarians. Architect Raymond Moriyama, shown in 2016 next to one of his creations, designed the Science Centre with the purpose of creating a place where people could interact with and immerse themselves in science.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photoSteve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Raju Mudhar Toronto Star

Document 413 of 500

Science Centre and our broken system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/science-centre-our-broken-system/docview/3073871852/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Science+Centre+and+our+broken+system&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It could be the feel-good story of the year: a beloved and popular attraction is saved from closure and demolition by concerned citizens. Working together, they find wealthy benefactors who pledge the funds to execute needed repairs, and an architect willing to do the work pro-bono. The community effort spares an impoverished government from having to make an unpopular decision, as much as sparing taxpayers the additional financial burden.
Thanks to the altruism of our social betters, and the "outside the box" thinking of a plucky white-collar class, the Ontario Science Centre is saved. A new generation of children are thus encouraged to pursue rewarding careers in STEM.
This might be the spin applied to the saga of the Science Centre if Ontario Premier Doug Ford can be convinced to accept the numerous generous offers that have come in over the last week to save it.
The problem is that it didn't have to be this way. That the Science Centre needed saving in the first place is demonstrative of the features - not the bugs - of our political and governance system.
For your consideration: If the Science Centre was the city's responsibility, would we be in this predicament? Probably not.
Consider as well that the lion's share of public opposition to the Science Centre's closing has come from the citizens of Toronto. Similarly, it's the people of Toronto who have been most vocal about the privatization and disfigurement of Ontario Place.
This isn't simply a reflection of the fact these public spaces are immediately accessible to most Torontonians, but that it is Torontonians who most need immediate access to public spaces. It shouldn't surprise anyone that the people of Toronto would be most vocal in their opposition to Ford's ideologically-motivated process of public disinvestment, as it has had a debilitating effect on Toronto and its citizens first and foremost.
The consequences of chronic disinvestment in urban areas by upper levels of government has bedevilled North American cities for about 50 years. The problem - particularly in American cities in the area now known as the "rust belt" - was initially one of suburban flight sapping cities' urban tax bases, thus resulting in shrinking budgets, austerity, and eventually, severe population and economic loss. Upper levels of government - either at the county or state level - attempted to rein-in depopulation and deindustrialization, to varying degrees of success and failure.
Toronto experienced aspects of these processes while also being shielded from some of their worst manifestations in part because it evolved into becoming the nation's corporate and business capital around the same time as depopulation and deindustrialization were ravaging other major cities. That Toronto is Ontario's capital helped as well. Most premiers want their capital cities to shine and understand - at least - that the needs of big cities can be very different from other regions of the province, or the province as a whole.
Where Ford is unique amongst premiers is that he seems to take no interest in the fact his often-reckless decisions can negatively impact the lives of so many people he ostensibly represents. He seems to govern in spite of Toronto, all while seeking to remake the city according to his whims.
If Toronto had the financial tools and political sovereignty necessary to provide the services and protect and promote the spaces its citizens desire, we wouldn't be discussing the Science Centre's closure, but likely its expansion. We likely wouldn't be spending public money to privatize Ontario Place. Our schools probably wouldn't be falling into disrepair, and our hospitals wouldn't be so overcrowded, because we'd be acting on the recommendations of our public health officials, rather than dismissing them.
That private individuals have come forward to save the Science Centre - and that this option was not immediately accepted by the government - is indeed suspect, but let's not let this distract us from the underlying problem.
Toronto is denied the right to do what's best for itself by politicians who campaign on their contempt for the city. They see public assets as exploitable business opportunities, and they spend public money seemingly without consulting the public. This is the system we have, and it consistently doesn't work for us. That we are so consistently denied the right to do better for ourselves isn't a bug, it's a feature.
It begs the question: why do we continue to put up with this?
Taylor C. Noakes is an
independent journalist and public historian from Montreal.
CREDIT: Taylor C. Noakes Contributor Taylor C. Noakes is an independent journalist and public historian from Montreal.;

Document 414 of 500

Unions say feds bleeding firefighters: As wildfire season begins, critics say political leaders need to address crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unions-say-feds-bleeding-firefighters/docview/3073869445/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unions+say+feds+bleeding+firefighters%3A+As+wildfire+season+begins%2C+critics+say+political+leaders+need+to+address+crisis&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At 27, Noah Freedman is among the most experienced wildland firefighters in his unit. In just eight years on the job, he's become a local union executive and a fire crew leader.
It's a feat that would have been unheard of a few decades ago, he says, but after years of low wages, few benefits and a climate crisis that is making weather disasters more extreme, a grave retention crisis has placed a heavy burden on those on the front lines protecting Canadians from forest fires, and forcing those who stick around to step up.
That's why Freedman believes the clock is ticking for political leaders to address this crisis as another busy wildfire season starts. He's not alone: New Democrats, Conservatives and several unions are calling on the Trudeau government to take the lead and make changes to how wildland firefighters are classified by the federal government to improve their working conditions and reflect the growing difficulties they face in their jobs.
"None of us here in this job exist for the money," Freedman told the Star. "But that doesn't mean that we're not struggling to survive and to justify these sacrifices to our families and to our loved ones."
Although wildland firefighters are provincially regulated, a technical decision in Canada's federal job classification system excludes them from jobs deemed to be in "public safety," affecting their standing under the federal Income Tax Act and directly leading to lower pensions and later retirements.
They are considered "silviculture and forestry workers" rather than "firefighters" like their colleagues working in municipalities across the country, angering many in the field who put their lives in danger every year fighting wildfires.
To one national union, it's a matter of equity. It says the Trudeau government - which has in recent years made an effort to step up its role in combating wildfires - must make a change in the classification.
"Here we are with a group who put their lives in harm's way almost every single day during the fire season, and they're not even recognized as firefighters," said Bert Blundon, president of the National Union of Public and General Employees, which represents more than 4,000 wildland firefighters across Canada. "I see it as a bit scandalous."
Blundon says classifying wildland firefighters accurately would increase their potential pension accrual rates from two to 2.33 per cent, and lower their earliest retirement age from 55 to 50 - benefits that can be available to municipal firefighters but not to forest firefighters.
Even more so, critics charge, the classification is ignorant to the realities of their jobs and puts them on lower footing than city firefighters.
"We just think it's outrageous, and it needs to be corrected," Blundon told the Star. "And it may make it easier for our provincial unions to negotiate an overall better package to protect this group of workers and to treat them fairly."
For now, the Trudeau government can only say it's reviewing the matter, according to a spokesperson for Employment and Social Development Canada.
But the change won't solve every problem. For one, working conditions vary across provinces. And because so many firefighters work a lot of overtime, for example, much of their time worked doesn't count toward their pensions, Freedman said.
"If there was a conversation about this at the federal level and it sort of permeated into provincial politics, I think it would do us a big favour," he told the Star.
That push on the federal level has received political backing. The NDP and Conservatives have called on the Trudeau government to make the change in the lead-up to this year's wildfire season, and the idea received cross-partisan support at the House of Commons public safety committee.
It's all the more reason for the federal government to take action now, Blundon said.
Often when wildfires or firefighters come up in the House of Commons, the Liberals tout their initiative in recent years to fund the training of 1,000 wildland firefighters. More than 600 have been trained so far, but fewer than 100 of them had been deployed in their communities by the end of last wildfire season.
Freedman suggested those efforts miss the bigger picture. "It's always cheaper to keep an existing customer," he told the Star.
"You spend all this energy every year, not just in the fight against fire, but in training the new people who are learning, and then it's all for nothing, and you have to do it all over again."
Not only is it a strain on resources and energy, but also on taxpayer dollars, he said.
"To put it in perspective, last year I had two brand new folks and they were amazing, and then neither of them returned."
Inaction has a cost. In the face of the crisis, provinces are increasing their reliance on other provinces, foreign firefighters, and even the Canadian Armed Forces, despite warnings from a strained military that this practice is not sustainable.
Last year, 5,500 international firefighters came to Canada to support strained provincial efforts. While it's the provinces that bear the brunt of the cost, there have been times the federal government has had to chip in.
In 2021, for example, Ottawa paid around $2.75 million for the flights and COVID-19 isolation requirements of 55 personnel from Australia and 109 from South Africa following requests from B.C., Ontario and Manitoba, documents tabled in Parliament show.
James Gault, executive vice-president of the Alberta Union of Provincial Employees, said the situation has left wildland firefighters feeling that governments look at them as "expendable."
It doesn't have to be that way, critics charge, as they call on Ottawa to show its concern by taking the lead.
"At the end of the day, what it boils down to is the respect for what these guys and girls do, right?" Gault told the Star.
But Freedman and others are growing anxious as they await action while wildfires get more intense.
Asked why he sticks around despite these conditions, he pauses to think.
"It's hard to come back watching these brilliant, amazing young people walk away, but at the same time, the job is rewarding, and in that respect, it is not hard to come back," he said. "Each and every year, all of us have a hand in helping people and changing people's lives for the better.
"That enough is reason to come back."
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 415 of 500

'It's essential that I'm comfortable': Man who relies on wheelchair says he's frustrated with service provided by provincial program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/essential-that-im-comfortable/docview/3073869260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+essential+that+I%27m+comfortable%27%3A+Man+who+relies+on+wheelchair+says+he%27s+frustrated+with+service+provided+by+provincial+program&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three years after a debilitating stroke left him largely immobile, retired lawyer Dan MacDonald is in a constant battle to get his power wheelchair to fit properly.
The struggle - faced by others, disability advocates say - is raising new questions about Ontario's Assistive Devices Program (ADP), which helps people with long-term disabilities afford essential items like wheelchairs, hearing aids, insulin pumps, home oxygen and more.
MacDonald's emailed complaints to Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Premier Doug Ford's office on June 7 about an exclusive power wheelchair supplier arrangement under the ADP, and concerns that clients have nowhere to turn if service is unsatisfactory, got an answer Thursday.
He was not impressed, and shared it with the Star.
"The Ministry of Health is sorry to hear of the challenges you've faced with your wheelchair," said the unsigned email reply from the ministry's correspondence and corporate support unit.
"The ADP strives to provide customer-oriented support ... ADP staff will take your concerns into consideration," the reply added, providing MacDonald with a separate ADP email address to contact with any "further concerns."
MacDonald says he'd already emailed that address, and feels "very frustrated."
"That correspondence begs the question of why ADP has not seen fit to respond to my previous emails concerning the difficulties I have encountered," he said.
Jones's office declined to comment specifically on MacDonald's situation, although the Ministry of Health sent a separate statement saying it stands by the program and noting it has procedures for handling complaints.
But the ministry does not have statistics on complaints readily available and does not track client satisfaction.
MacDonald feels like he's hit a brick wall and filed a complaint with the office of Ontario's ombudsman, hoping the staff there can help him get action.
"The chair is what I'm in most of the day. It's essential that I'm comfortable," said MacDonald, 66. He suffered a serious brain stem stroke in 2021 that left the retired litigation lawyer with limited use of his hands and arms.
Getting the chair's cushions and other components to fit and not cause pressure sores - the wheelchair equivalent of bedsores - is a "trial-and-error" process that can entail many appointments with a technician, he says.
"The disabled population faces numerous challenges, physically, emotionally and financially," MacDonald wrote in a recent appeal to Jones, which he shared with the Star.
"Obtaining reliable, dependable and timely service for something which is essential to their mobility and independence should not be an additional challenge."
Frustrated and now dealing with a retired power wheelchair seating technician as a last resort, MacDonald is urging the Ford government to open the power wheelchair repair business under the ADP to competition.
"There has to be a better way," said MacDonald, who is frustrated by the continuing challenges following the stroke that "upended" his life with his wife Marie, a former senior IT executive who is now his chief caregiver.
The unexpected stroke hit 18 months after he retired, putting him in hospital for weeks, and causing breathing problems that resulted in two intubations and six months on a feeding tube.
Ministry of Health officials say the wheelchair contract was purchased by a company called Motion LP - which has branches across the province selling and servicing "high technology power wheelchairs" - from Shoppers Home Health Care in 2016.
As such, the ministry funds Motion to run the "central equipment pool" or CEP, first established in 1996, for the power wheelchairs. The pool provides clients with "new and recycled" wheelchairs "at discounted prices and provides a rebate on the client's costs once the equipment is returned."
"Due to the cost and complexity of power dynamic positioning wheelchairs, only a few select vendors have the expertise and technology to undertake management of the CEP," ministry spokesperson Adriana Dutkiewicz said in a statement to the Star.
"The ministry receives and tracks complaints ... the complaints are reviewed, investigated and resolved by the program. Any concerns related to client safety are taken extremely seriously, in accordance with the government's agreement with Motion."
MacDonald's troubles with Motion have included leaving detailed voice-mail messages, but not getting return calls, a lack of promised followup from in-person appointments, delays in getting parts and a wheelchair battery, and being called in for a battery replacement that had to wait until the next day for a more experienced technician to install it.
MacDonald and others say service provided by Motion is uneven. After his repeated complaints, the company has agreed to service MacDonald, who lives in Mississauga, from its more distant Hamilton office.
Motion says it strives to provide "the best possible client experience in support of our vision to make life accessible for everyone" and notes the customization of power chairs is complicated - something MacDonald acknowledges.
"Each individual has unique needs," chief executive officer Sue Gilpin said in a statement to the Star.
Gilpin said the timeline for repairs and adjustments can depend on recommendations from therapists and approvals from third-party funders, and the ordering and delivery of parts.
"We are not permitted to make changes to a client's equipment without approval and direction from the therapist," she added.
"Given such complexities and the wide range of mobility and accessibility needs of our clients, there have been exceptional circumstances in which we did not achieve the customer service standards that we set out for our business," she acknowledged.
"In such cases we always try to work directly with the client to understand their experience and identify any opportunities for improvement."
In MacDonald's case, that included sending staff for extra training, as Gilpin acknowledged in a letter the retired lawyer shared with the Star.
Jeff Preston, an associate professor of disability studies at King's University College in London, Ont., says the monopoly on service enjoyed by Motion is a "disservice" to the disabled community.
"The result has been to wipe out the service, repair and sales industry," said Preston, who has used a wheelchair for 24 years after being born with a rare form of muscular dystrophy.
"I've heard wildly different reports of the quality of care and quality of support that people receive from one unit to another based on where they're living," he said. "People are trapped as a result."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 416 of 500

84 candidates on a ballot? That's a stunt, and not the way we should be saving democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/84-candidates-on-ballot-thats-stunt-not-way-we/docview/3074182170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=84+candidates+on+a+ballot%3F+That%27s+a+stunt%2C+and+not+the+way+we+should+be+saving+democracy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with the most righteous democracy reformers is how they revel, wrongly, in running down what we already have. And slowing it down unless they get their way.
Case in point: The delayed vote count in this week's hotly-contested Toronto-St. Paul's byelection, which degenerated into an all-night marathon.
Election results are usually revealed within a few hours of the polls closing. Wonder why the vote count dragged on until nearly dawn, far beyond the usual timeline?
Chalk it up to a pointless protest by self-appointed democracy vigilantes ready to bog down the existing system so they can build one of their own design. Thanks to their overzealous recruitment of protest candidates, there were 84 names jammed into a supersized paper ballot on voting day last Monday, most of them utterly unknown - and unknowing.
Quite apart from testing the patience of every voter, the interminable ballots tested the stamina of the Elections Canada staff. What was the point of the so-called "Longest Ballot" protest?
To push proportional representation (PR) upon the people of Canada - like it or not. As one triumphant organizer told the CBC, "If that message hasn't gotten across yet, then next time it won't be 84 candidates, it will be 184 candidates."
You get the idea: We must diminish democracy in order to save it. Which seems an awfully anti-democratic way to get their way.
A better way would be to persuade all Canadians, and their political leaders, to change the existing system - if that is indeed what people want. Trouble is, previous votes on various forms of proportional representation have failed in Ontario, B.C. and P.E.I. in recent years.
That said, the protest got me thinking again about the politics of electoral reform - and the bogus premise that it is a purely non-partisan undertaking. The challenge is that each side is a prisoner of its own ideological, electoral, political and partisan preferences.
Where you stand on this issue depends a great deal on where you sit - and which party you support.
I can see the benefits of a proportional representation outcome that is more aligned with the overall popular vote. But I also worry about distortions from empowering smaller parties (fringe or not) that will always wield a disproportionate balance of power (not just in occasional minority governments).
I've always preferred the ranked ballot, or electronic runoff, by which you rank your candidates in order of preference. That's how most political conventions work - if no single candidate wins a majority of the vote, the second and third choices of the defeated candidates are tallied until someone wins the runoffs. The virtue of ranked ballot is that it inculcates consensus and civility, encouraging rival candidates to court one another - and their supporters - to earn their second-ballot support.
The shortcomings in our current system, known as first past the post, are obvious: a winner-takes-all outcome for the candidate who finishes first with merely a plurality - not a majority - of votes. For example, in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection, the Conservative candidate fell well short of a majority of votes cast, winning 42.1 per cent compared to 40.4 for the Liberal and 11.0 for the New Democrat.
As a thought experiment, if those numbers were mirrored provincially or nationally, you could imagine support for the latter two parties (now allied in Ottawa) adding up to a putatively progressive majority of 51.4 per cent.
One enduring myth is that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau promised to implement PR after winning power in 2015. In fact, he merely promised electoral reform to change the existing system, and his stated preference is the ranked ballot.
Of course, New Democrats and Greens are diehard PR parties because they covet the balance of power. Conservatives oppose PR because they lack natural allies among the other progressive parties, and prefer to wait for the occasional lopsided majority under the existing system.
Overlooked in this equation is the common front among Tories, New Democrats and Greens in opposing the ranked ballot - because an electronic runoff tends to favour the centrist, consensus party - the Liberals. New Democrats and Greens want change because they want to wield power - not by winning a majority, merely the balance of power. Tories don't want to be frozen out of power in perpetuity by PR or the ranked ballot.
That shared self-interest - the impulse to perpetuate and protect one's own political interests - is what bogged down electoral reform in the past. Liberals love the ranked ballot because it gives them the best shot of winning one election after another, but the other parties are in no position to compromise.
As for ordinary voters, proportional representation doesn't achieve the required threshold (or command a decent turnout) in any referendum. That's the political reality the pro-PR side is so reluctant to accept.
Too bad some diehards are now ready to hold the rest of us hostage on voting day until they get their way. That's not democracy, that's sophistry.
CAPTION: The challenge with changing our voting system is that each side is a prisoner of its own ideological, electoral, political and partisan preferences, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Balance of power; Preferences; Electoral reform; Referendums; Democracy; Candidates; Elections; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Balance of power

Document 417 of 500

The Science Centre needs fixing, not fencing off. Doug Ford should know better

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/science-centre-needs-fixing-not-fencing-off-doug/docview/3074182159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Science+Centre+needs+fixing%2C+not+fencing+off.+Doug+Ford+should+know+better&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a famous scene in the 1967 film "The Graduate" when a young Dustin Hoffman receives career advice. "One word," says an older man. "Plastics." You see, there was a great future in the plastics industry then.
For any new graduates in Toronto this year, though, that one word might be "fencing." It's seemingly a boom time for the fence industry here. Fences make good neighbours, they say, but let's look at the city through its fencing and you decide if it's good or not.
Last weekend I went to see the new fence at the Ontario Science Centre. A pilgrimage, really, as the fence is so big and extensive I consider it a wonder of modern instant infrastructure. It should have its own interpretive exhibit in, say, a local science centre.
The tall metal fence runs for hundreds of metres along Don Mills Road, along the south side of the site and into the ravine below, well away from the building itself. On Sunday, there were about a dozen security people along the fence and "private property" signs affixed to it. Even the massive sign on Don Mills, alternately flashing "Closed" and "Fermé," had its very own fence surrounding it as if it, too, was a threat.
It's remarkable to think how much fencing is sitting in reserves, ready to be called up at 4 p.m. on a Friday in the summertime when the Doug Ford government announces it's shutting down the Science Centre immediately.
The government says an engineering report warns of imminent structural failure, though Canadian Architect magazine's Elsa Lam did a deep dive into the report and suggests "the building's key exhibition areas could continue to operate safely for years to come - even if the Ontario government chooses not to invest in any structural roof repairs this year." The CBC also reports that about 400 aging public buildings in Ontario have the same roof panels blamed for safety risks at the Science Centre, but they all remain open.
Repairs and routine maintenance are what this and previous governments should have been doing all along. Imagine if fences were put around all the buildings and houses that needed some work in Toronto: most of the city would be fenced off. Repair and maintenance is a normal part of older buildings here and around the world, including ones far older than the Science Centre, a building its architect, Raymond Moriyama, said was built to last 250 years.
Never mind that, the ability of our various governments to fence things off is impressive. World class, even. The new Science Centre fencing now connects to the fence around the nearby - but yet to open - Eglinton Crosstown LRT station, tragically named after the Science Centre. To travel along the length of Eglinton is to see station after station fenced off on this much-delayed transit line. Eerily, they often seem open, with digital screens working and the lights on, as if they're a portal into some alternate Toronto where transit works.
It's too bad the Ontario government can't complete this delayed transit project with the efficiency it can put up a fence. The Crosstown has been delayed so long, I almost fear the stations will get so old they'll need repairs before they can open, necessitating their permanent closure like the Science Centre. That is the Ontario way.
Toronto's recent history with fencing is equally fraught. Fences were put up quickly in several parks after the violent encampment evictions of 2021, preventing the use of large swaths of those parks for months afterwards. This June was also the 14th anniversary of the G20 where a fence contained a large part of the financial district during what became one of the biggest mass violations of human and civil rights in Canadian history.
Our leaders do like fences, but sometimes they don't do what they were intended to do. There's the now-infamous fence at Ontario Place that became a public billboard for protest. At the University of Toronto, a fence erected around King's College Circle to prevent demonstrations has made the pro-Palestinian encampment more formidable, almost defining it - and serving as a billboard for messages.
It is also possible to breach Toronto's fences, literally and figuratively. George Hislop Park east of Yonge between Charles and Isabella Streets has been fenced off since 2020 for a renovation, but work is finally well underway. As well, after much painful debate and failed attempts, Torontonians can now enjoy booze in (some) city parks. Unlike patio rules which keep imbibers safe in fenced corrals, people can wander free range (a little). Like with so many other places around the world, nothing remarkable happened when this was finally allowed.
Putting up a fence may not seem to be the neighbourly thing to do, but anyone looking for unlimited career opportunities in Ontario could do worse than getting in on the barrier business.
CAPTION: The fence erected around the Ontario Science Centre is an impressive piece of instantly created infrastructure.

Subject: Science; Fences
