Document 418 of 500

Ontario Science Centre insiders recount the rise and fall of a beloved institution

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-science-centre-insiders-recount-rise-fall/docview/3074181858/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Science+Centre+insiders+recount+the+rise+and+fall+of+a+beloved+institution&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For a 10-year-old boy, it was a rock from out of this world that left him wanting to reach the stars.
"My biggest 'whoa' moment at the Ontario Science Centre was not just running into this hall of all these theories turned into practice, where I could run around and actually try stuff out, but to see a small piece of something that I had been imagining," said former Canadian astronaut Chris Hadfield. "A small, actual piece of the moon that Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin had picked up and brought all the way back on Apollo and put it into a container and then shipped to Toronto and put right there for me to see it."
Hadfield was featured in a fundraising video for the Science Centre in 2020. His moon rock experience happened shortly after the centre opened and, like generations of children in Ontario, he had fun and memorable moments created at the institution. Things came full orbit for Hadfield, who was the basis of an exhibit there in 2015. It is that kind of a spark that has motivated countless people that have worked at the Science Centre and was the key behind architect Raymond Moriyama's vision of a place where people could interact with and immerse themselves in science.
With the abrupt closing of the Science Centre last Friday due to an engineering report that said roof panels are in danger of collapsing, there has been an outpouring of emotion and soul searching about what the centre means to us. There have been pledges of financial support, demonstrations by people in the neighbourhood and an outcry from scientists, architects and philanthropists here and around the globe.
But how did we get to this point, where a building designed by one of Canada's most famous architects has fallen into such a state of disrepair? Where a place that should be a beacon for evidence-based discussion has become fraught with differing opinions about what should be done with it now?
In discussions with former and current staff, members of the board of trustees and former CEOs, a number of long-standing issues have been cited. The Science Centre's tight control from the provincial government has left it chronically underfunded, leading to a failure to innovate and evolve and a lack of maintenance on an aging building. Some also point to the issues with an ill-fitting and powerful union that also limits the room to manoeuvre.
To start, we have to look at its origin story. The Ontario Science Centre was a grand experiment for a number of reasons. It was away from downtown, nestled into a ravine at Don Mills and Eglinton. The Exploratorium in San Francisco and our Science Centre were the first museums of their kind, and have now inspired over 300 similar ones all over the world, with many using the Toronto institution as the blueprint for how they bring science to the masses.
Architect Moriyama and the other collaborators behind the Science Centre wanted to create something different, something interactive where children could touch and feel the science in their hands. In the late '60s, when it was being conceived, it was in vogue for governments to have a strong hand in projects, which meant that it was tightly controlled by the province, as opposed to other institutions, like the Royal Ontario Museum or the Art Gallery of Ontario.
"Bill Davis conceived the Ontario Science Centre as a Centennial project, which actually opened in 1969," said Nancy Lockhart, who was board chair from 1998 to 2006. "And it was the style at the time to have Schedule One government agencies, which tied you very, very closely to government and that became a real impediment as time went on. But when the Science Centre was constituted, it got 92 per cent of its funding, I believe, from the provincial government. The rest was generated through parking and admission."
The year 2006 saw the last major influx of provincial cash for the centre, $15 million as well as a successful $40 million fundraising campaign. The money was used to give the institution a re-energizing facelift and attract blockbuster draws, most notably in 2009 with Body Worlds and the Story of the Heart, an internationally created exhibit focusing on the human body, which helped boost attendance.
But subsequent governments weren't willing to invest, and the institution's ability to generate funds on its own was limited through being beholden to the province.
"We needed government approval for everything, whereas the ROM or the AGO would have had more freedom to operate or to act," said Lockhart.
One example she points out was during her tenure, there was an opportunity identified that would have generated $11 million a year by building exhibitions that would tour the world. But there was no space to do the work, and the ability to staff up or staff down depending on work was limited because of union constraints. She said they needed $2 million as a startup cost, but there was no appetite from government to provide it.
"I'd say benign neglect would be a good way of describing what happened over successive governments of every stripe," said Lockhart. "Each and every year, the Science Centre had to prepare a very detailed business plan using a very lengthy and repetitive government template. That business plan had to be submitted by the end of the year, by the beginning of December, I think. There wasn't a single year I was there where that plan was approved before the following November. So that tells you that nobody was looking at it.
"It was really frustrating. Each and every year we put in requests for repairs, for modifications, but basically most years, we ended up with a flat budget, which meant a negative budget because the wages, which were the biggest part of the budget, went up and we had no control over them."
That was another issue. Because it was run by the government, all the employees are Ontario public servants and part of a much bigger union, which gave Science Centre leadership no negotiating power over their employees.
"The Ontario Science Centre should always have been run like a business," said Lockhart.
The world was also changing, and the centre's information and interactivity had to compete with what people had in the palm of their hands, said Lesley Lewis, who was CEO from 1998 to 2014.
"People had access to vast amounts of information on their computers and ultimately on their phones," Lewis said. "So, it was a different competitive environment."
By the 2010s, bigger screens - and one of the Science Centre's big draws - were becoming much more commonplace.
"Another thing that created some problems in the Science Centre, and similar places around the world, is that many had invested big time in Imax theatres," Lewis said. "And for a while, they brought in a huge amount of people, but suddenly it wasn't drawing unique visitors, because in a city like Toronto, suddenly there were lots of Imax screens, not just the one in Ontario Place and the one at the Science Centre."
Budgets remained flat during the 2010s, and that started to manifest itself in other ways.
"What I saw during my time there was an increasing reliance on what we called partnerships, but were really kind of sponsorships of various spaces," said Kevin Von Appen, who worked at the Science Centre in various capacities over 40 years, before retiring three years ago as the director of science communications.
"So you had the Shoppers Drug Mart Imax Theatre, not the Ontario Science Centre Imax Theatre. You had the Procter and Gamble Great Hall, not simply the Great Hall. Those changes started in the '80s in earnest when I first came to the Science Centre, and they only accelerated over the time that I was there."
He said the problem with that is it leads to hiring more people in roles promoting sponsorships or seeking more entrepreneurial activities.
"It's about where you're putting your resources, and over time, you wind up with maybe fewer scientists, designers and researchers."
A good year for Science Centre attendance was always around one million visitors, with some banner years fuelled by bigger exhibitions.
With a lack of funding, there were a dearth of those big draws.
Maurice Bitran, who was CEO and chief scientist from 2014 to 2019, recently wrote an opinion piece for the Star, saying: "I discovered the many challenges it faced: the roof leaked whenever it rained, there was no working capital to develop new exhibits or to bring new shows, and the auditors had even put a liquidity note in the financial statements. Not surprisingly, attendance was in decline. For decades, and under governments of all political stripes, this cherished Ontario gem had suffered from chronic underfunding and insufficient infrastructure maintenance."
In 2019, a report - and a series of columns by the Star's Martin Regg Cohn - supported the idea of revitalizing Ontario Place by moving the science centre to the waterfront. Over the next few years, the tourism ministry and Infrastructure Ontario released reports on putting the two projects together, calling it a priority project and commissioning business cases and reports on the feasibility of the plan. Then COVID-19 hit, and in March 2020 the centre was shut down for almost two years, shifting to virtual projects.
In 2022, the bridge linking the main hall was closed because of the risk of structural failure, and a report estimated repair costs at $16 million. Since then, shuttle buses have been ferrying people between buildings.
In April 2023, Infrastructure Ontario put a proposal together that said moving the Science Centre to the waterfront, with integrated parking with the Therme Spa, would help meet the province's obligations to that company. On April 18, Premier Doug Ford announced the Science Centre's planned relocation to Ontario Place.
Then, as reported last week, the Rimkus engineering report landed, which found that some of the roof panels were in danger of collapsing due to a snow load or heavy rain. That report said it could cost $22 million to $40 million and take two to five years to repair the three buildings, it also said fixes in "high risk" locations could cost as little as $522,500.
What the report did not say was that the Ontario Science Centre needed to be closed immediately. That was a decision made by the current board of trustees, and supported by the government.
"One must remember that this was a warning, a health and safety warning, and as a representative of government, I have to take that seriously," said Kinga Surma, Ontario's infrastructure minister, in a press conference Monday. "There is nothing more important than the health and safety of people, of workers and of children."
Lewis said the shutdown has been felt throughout the community of science museums around the world, shocking many of her colleagues that hold this Canadian institution in high regard.
On its last day of operation, families from across Ontario were still heading toward the admission desk as the news broke, while workers put up fencing at the entrance to the parking lots. Employees also learned of the closing that afternoon, with a few walking around the entrance near closing time, in a daze.
Some hugged and consoled each other. A building steeped in science had left so many unknowns.
CAPTION: In this 2016 Star file photo, architect Raymond Moriyama poses with a model of Science North in Sudbury, one of his creations.A general view of the Ontario Science Centre, in Toronto, Friday, May 5, 2023. Toronto city staff are looking into what legal requirements the province may have to operate or maintain the Ontario Science Centre, after Premier Doug Ford's government abruptly shuttered the facility citing structural concerns. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Chris YoungThe reliance on government and the way the Ontario Science Centre was managed were part of the problem, insiders say.

In photos: Thousands walk in Toronto's annual Trans March to kick off Pride weekend

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/photos-thousands-walk-torontos-annual-trans-march/docview/3073790940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+photos%3A+Thousands+walk+in+Toronto%27s+annual+Trans+March+to+kick+off+Pride+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Light blue-pink-and-white and rainbow flags filled the streets around Toronto's Gay Village downtown Friday evening during the city's annual Trans Rally and March.
The joint events - which Toronto Pride say is the largest in the world - officially kicked off Toronto's Pride weekend, with more events like the Dyke March and Pride Parade planned for the weekend through Sunday.
Friday's trans-focused events were meant as a space for trans, two-spirit, non-binary and gender nonconforming people to "gather and celebrate their identities while also raising awareness and fighting for trans human rights," according to Toronto Pride.
Despite the overcast skies, thousands came out for the rally that started at 7 p.m. at Church and Hayden Streets, and stayed for the march an hour later that wound through downtown from Church Street up to Bloor, then south to Yonge and Gould Street.
The crowd featured people of many identities and ages, all there to celebrate and show support for the Trans community. Politicians such as Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles attended as well.
Along with multicoloured flags, attendees held signs and wore body paint with messages like "We are a Trans positive family" and "Protect Trans kids," all responses to the growing spread of anti-trans legislation and rhetoric aimed at youth in Canada and the U.S.

Title: In photos: Thousands walk in Toronto's annual Trans March to kick off Pride weekend

Document 420 of 500

Doug Ford would upset nearly half of all Ontarians with an early $150M election, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-would-upset-nearly-half-all-ontarians/docview/3073643015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+would+upset+nearly+half+of+all+Ontarians+with+an+early+%24150M+election%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nearly half of all Ontarians would be "unhappy" or "very angry" if Premier Doug Ford called an early $150-million election, a new poll suggests.
With Ford considering a vote a year before the scheduled June 2026 provincial election, Abacus Data used its monthly tracking survey for the Star to ask how Ontarians would feel about heading to the polls early.
"The price tag is a problem," said Abacus president David Coletto.
Indeed, when the cost of the election is factored in, 19 per cent said they would be "very angry" at a snap call while 25 per cent would be "unhappy" - a total of 44 per cent.
Some 39 per cent were "neutral" while nine per cent were "happy" and eight per cent "very happy," suggesting only a total of 17 per cent like the idea.
Findings changed when respondents were asked about an early election without the $150-million cost included - only five per cent were "very angry" while 10 per cent were "unhappy" and 50 per cent "neutral" with 20 per cent "happy" and 16 per cent "very happy."
Coletto said if sticker-shocked voters come to believe an early election is "a very expensive endeavour that's not needed," the premier could pay a price.
"It's risky for Doug Ford to do that in this context because he's built a brand about being frugal," he said, noting the Tories already govern with a majority in the legislature so there is no electoral urgency.
"So he would have to justify the need for an election and the expense."
When former premier David Peterson - now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company - called a snap election in 1990, its $40-million price tag was a key factor in his Liberals' surprise loss to Bob Rae's New Democrats.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from June 20 until Tuesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm also asked about Ford moving "to take advantage of Justin Trudeau's unpopularity to hold the provincial election before the federal election" expected in October 2025.
Ten per cent said they would be "very angry" at that rationale while another 10 per cent said they would be "unhappy" while 50 per cent were "neutral," 20 per cent "happy" and 16 per cent "very happy."
Overall, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 25 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and eight per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
While that's largely unchanged from last month's poll, the PC government's disapproval rating jumped from 40 per cent in May to 45 per cent in June with its approval rating falling from 37 per cent to 34 per cent.
"Something has happened in the last month that may have turned off some people, because that's a change outside the margin of error," said Coletto.
On May 24, Ford announced the government would pay The Beer Store $225 million to expedite the liberalization of beer, wine and premixed cocktail sales in convenience stores, supermarkets and big-box outlets by Halloween.
The premier - whose original plan was for that to be completed by Dec. 31, 2025 - wants to be seen keeping a major 2018 campaign promise in case he opts for an election early next year.
But Crombie's Liberals insist the tab for speeding up the changes could be three or four times more than $225 million, which is why she has dubbed the rushed scheme "a billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
Asked for their impressions of the four party leaders, 35 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 45 per cent had a negative impression with 18 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 10 per cent.
Crombie was 28 per cent positive, 30 per cent negative, 26 per cent neutral and 15 per cent unsure for a minus two per cent net rating.
Stiles had a 28 per cent positive, 24 per cent negative, 30 per cent neutral and 18 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus four per cent.
Schreiner had a 23 per cent positive, 18 per cent negative, 36 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus five per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Provincial elections

Document 421 of 500

For opportunity, community, or because they can't afford to leave: Why these Toronto residents have made the choice to stay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opportunity-community-because-they-cant-afford/docview/3073642013/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+opportunity%2C+community%2C+or+because+they+can%27t+afford+to+leave%3A+Why+these+Toronto+residents+have+made+the+choice+to+stay&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When asked why she stays in Toronto, Lil Moore says it boils down to this: "Better the devil you know than the devil you don't."
That's why she chooses to stay in her "disgusting" rental instead of moving to a more affordable city.
The adult entertainer is 35 now, but she experienced five years of homelessness when she was younger. And with rent control removed for Ontario homes built or first occupied after 2018, she fears losing the stability she has now could land her in a worst-case scenario, again.
Moore is among those choosing to stay in Toronto at a time when people are leaving the city in droves. The number of residents moving to other parts of the province or country from Toronto's census metropolitan area has been trending upwards since about 2010, according to Statistics Canada data, and the figures have more than doubled in recent years. From July 1, 2022 to July 1, 2023 alone, nearly 110,000 more people left Toronto to go somewhere else in Canada than the reverse, and about a third of them were aged 25 to 39.
At just 15, Moore became well-acquainted with the city's crowded shelters, cold streets and scarce affordable housing options. She signed up for the wait-list for affordable housing, but 20 years later, she's still waiting.
"I've been on the public housing list since I was 15 years old. I've never received an offer," she said. (In the first quarter of this year, there were nearly 87,000 people waiting to access social housing in Toronto, according to city data.)
At age 20, a shelter worker helped her get into a program that provided social assistance to help pay for a market-priced apartment.
About a decade later, Moore moved to a different rent-controlled house in Scarborough - one with a backyard so that her dogs could have more space - but she's still "kicking herself" for leaving that cheaper and more stable home for a place that she says has been rife with problems from floods and mould, to disrepair and problematic tenants.
Moore said there's two major reasons she feels she can't leave - one is financial and the other is cultural.
On the first, despite the high cost of living across Ontario, Premier Doug Ford's decision to cut rent control for units built after 2018 means an increasing number of apartments provincewide could be subject to large rent increases. She also describes what she sees as a pervasive trend of bad landlords (in her personal experience and as a former paralegal), and she fears any movement could cost her the stability she has now.
In addition, Moore said she doesn't believe she could find culture that mirrors what's in Scarborough, and that she needs to be somewhere that's safe for her partner, who is transgender.
"I will never leave Scarborough because the connection to Caribbean culture and Asian culture is so strong over here," she said, noting that her father was Indonesian-Chinese and that Caribbean and Indigenous women on the street raised her. "They are community to me. They are culture that I understand. Where am I going to find that?"
Still, Moore dreams of a future where she can own a house. But she knows for that to happen, it can't be in Toronto.
The cost of opportunitiesHilary Henderson, a comedian originally from Hamilton, has higher hopes for Toronto - despite some less-than-ideal experiences she's had in her few years here.
In 2021, Henderson moved to the city with the goal of taking her comedy career all the way. The 27-year-old with a master's degree in public administration works a full-time job in the field, plus comedy gigs, while making money on social media. But after two years of living alone in a one-bedroom apartment, she realized she would need to get a promotion at her day job or downsize if she ever wanted to save enough money to achieve her long-term financial goals.
"I was left with a choice of, do I advance in my career in a way that I can still afford to stay in the place that I was? But advancing in my career means that I would have less time and energy to contribute to my creative work."
Ultimately, she decided to downsize. When plans to move in with a friend fell through, she found someone on Facebook Marketplace to move in with, and rent that cost about $1,000 less than what she was paying. But soon after settling in, she found a mouse infestation and an additional, unexpected roommate. She left after four months.
Now, Henderson is in a studio apartment where she only saves about $200 compared to the one-bedroom she had before. She's looking to the future knowing being in the city will provide more opportunities to entertain, connect with audiences, and network with other comedians - even if for now, the process is costly and exhausting.
'Anything less than $120,000 ... it's just not financially feasible'Leaving Toronto isn't an option for accountant and mother-of-one Alesha Ford, at least for now.
On her $70,000 salary, covering rent and groceries requires precise calculations, the Canada Child Benefit payment and, this month, some help from her child's father - which means the 38-year-old has no money leftover to save for a moving truck, a flight, or first and last month's rent somewhere new.
"I'm going to be getting rid of my Netflix subscription ... but that's only $11," she said. "There's not much I can do with $11 in savings; it's just going to go right back to food."
Ford said she'll stay in Toronto while her teen finishes up French immersion school, but she can see herself landing in Belgium in the future, since she's bilingual and it's more affordable than Toronto; or in New York, where there's a bigger Caribbean community and where she believes she could make a higher income.
For now, she's hoping to change career paths in order to earn enough money to get by.
"I don't know if I'm in a place financially to increase my student loans ... so I have opted to do a self-taught course in product management," Ford said.
"In two to three years, I could be making $120,000, which is exactly what I need to live. Anything less than $120,000, especially if you have children, it's just not financially feasible, in my opinion."

Document 422 of 500

Doug Ford couldn't abandon the Science Centre quick enough. So now who will pay to fix it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-couldnt-abandon-science-centre-quick/docview/3073642012/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+couldn%27t+abandon+the+Science+Centre+quick+enough.+So+now+who+will+pay+to+fix+it%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine that you notice a leak in the roof of your home.
Do you: a) put a bucket under the drip; b) patch or replace the roof; or c) scramble to immediately abandon the house, fence off the entire property, and seek temporary accommodations until you can build a new place to live somewhere else in a few years.
In the case of the Ontario Science Centre, Doug Ford's government has chosen option C.
The analogy was suggested to Star reporters by Elsa Lam, the editor of Canadian Architect magazine, looking into the roof assessment the provincial government said justified the immediate closure of the Ontario Science Centre last week. The opinion Lam and others, including both the architecture firm that built the place and the consultants the province is relying on, are a far cry from the Ford government's claims of an emergency threatening life and limb.
The report did not recommend immediate closure. It said a small percentage of the roof should be replaced immediately, the rest monitored and replaced eventually.
The cost (between $500,000 and $7 million this year, and something like $25 million total over the next decade) is peanuts compared to the possible $1 billion this same provincial government is planning to spend in order to sell booze in corner stores a year ahead of schedule, or the tens of billions they're spending on a subway line originally pitched as delivering riders to the Science Centre, or the maybe $500 million they may spend to build a parking lot at the Science Centre's planned new site.
The roof is in disrepair, but it is not some five-alarm emergency on the verge of imminent collapse.
The building was not condemned - it was abandoned.
That is, the Science Centre was not suddenly forced by physical circumstances to move out. The provincial government chose to move it out because they wanted to. And only the timing is a surprise: the move to a dramatically smaller facility at Ontario Place to open by 2028 was announced long ago.
The frenzied shuttering of the current home only makes sense, I think, if the Ford government started with the conclusion that they wanted to get the heck out. And thus see no value in spending even a single penny to maintain the building. And in the face of a public outcry, they've decided to get out sooner rather than later (cementing the impression there's no going back).
Here's the thing: if the building is going to be put to any future use, these repairs are going to need to be done. There are no savings in moving out now, unless the building is going to be torn down.
Perhaps more so than Ford and his gang anticipated, a lot of people in Ontario love that building. It is massive (more than twice the square footage of the Royal Ontario Museum, for instance), situated in a location of natural beauty, and serves as an attraction that anchors a neighbourhood. Tastes in building styles vary, but it is a significant and globally influential example of mid-century institutional architecture built by one of Canada's foremost architects.
Moreover, many of us have deep attachments to it. A friend I was speaking to had lived across the street after his family moved here from Poland when he was a child, and he'd go often after school during hours when admission was free. "For a time, it was my favourite place in the whole world," he said. I have heard similar sentiments from a lot of people. Mayor Olivia Chow reported this week the city had heard from 35,000 Torontonians upset about the sudden closure.
So can the building so many people love be saved? Maybe put to a new purpose?
Toronto city council took up that question Thursday. The complication is the city doesn't want to be on the hook for paying for repairs resulting from provincial neglect. The mayor and city council are hoping there's a contractual way to force the province to pay for it - and have asked for a report on optionsfor the site. Chow told me in a podcast appearance Friday that the "new deal for Toronto" she struck with Ford last year included negotiations over keeping science programming at the site, and she'll continue to pursue that - expecting that the province would need to fund such a project.
But spending money on it is one thing it seems Ford's government won't consider. I mean, they wouldn't even put a bucket under a leak, so to speak, keeping the building in service long enough to move out properly. What are the chances they're going to spend that money (and more) just to give the building away?
This beloved place has been abandoned by choice. It would be a shame if it is now condemned by budget restraints.
CAPTION: If the Ontario Science Centre building is going to be put to any future use, the repairs are going to need to be done.

Document 423 of 500

By shutting down the Science Centre, Doug Ford strikes another blow to education in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/shutting-down-science-centre-doug-ford-strikes/docview/3073642001/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=By+shutting+down+the+Science+Centre%2C+Doug+Ford+strikes+another+blow+to+education+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Science Centre is the latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect: Editorial, June 22
What is missing in the coverage of the Ontario Science Centre closure is a discussion of the programming we are losing with the building shuttered. That is equally important and more irreplaceable.
The Ontario Science Centre was a site for science education for children in all grades. At a time when education policy is pushing science, technology, engineering and math, it is hugely ironic that this "classroom" is now effectively shut down. The Science Centre averaged 500,000 organized school visitors per year, and given the state of our underfunded schools, most of them will now not be exposed to immersive, real-life science education. That's not even counting the hundreds of thousands of kids who visited with their families, or the thousands that had already registered for summer science camps. With a new subway stop coming online, the Science Centre was only going to become more accessible to Torontonians and visitors alike.
Public education is under attack in Ontario. Science is under attack in Ontario. The environment is under attack in Ontario. The province is focusing on a building that would cost a tiny fraction of the new highway to repair. Our priorities need a rethink.
Ann Peel, Toronto
I've always thought of the Science Centre as being an educational institution. Its location was chosen to provide enrichment to an underserved population. It's been a mainstay of school field trips for generations. As a science teacher (now retired), I used to visit the Science Centre every August to get inspiration for the lessons I was planning for the year. I look at the closure as an extension of the Ford government's lack of support and investment in education, and lack of compassion for those in the neighbourhood who could have continued to be enriched and inspired by it. Education should be considered an investment, not a transaction to be bargained down to the lowest cost.
Grant Davidson, Milton, Ont.
As a teacher in the Don Mills and Eglinton area, the Science Centre offered an accessible opportunity for learning and cultural enrichment in a neighbourhood that had few other options for affordable student excursions. The iconic architectural building itself offered real life lessons in ecological science, inviting curiosity and awe about our natural world, giving students the ability to see life thriving underground as they rode below ground escalators.
I wonder if Premier Doug Ford has even been to the Ontario Science Centre. It needed the Ford government to provide support for maintenance and continued diversification. The last thing we need in Ontario is to destroy this magnificent jewel that we should respect and be proud of. The thought of destroying the Ontario Science Centre building is heartbreaking. Where is our sense of historical excellence if we destroy a building that was designed by the brilliantly innovative architect Raymond Moriyama, becoming a model for science centres around the world? Where in the world do countries set out to deliberately destroy cultural landmarks?
If the idea is to replace the Ontario Science Centre with housing, the whole area of Don Mills and Eglinton is filled with highrise buildings. The people who live in those buildings need opportunities escape their concrete surroundings, learn about science, the living environment, and the world, while enriching their spirit. A walk through the Ontario Science Centre is a mind-expanding adventure that can't be replicated. We must try to save this iconic structure before it is bulldozed to make room for wealthy developers to make money.
Fran Bazos, Newmarket, Ont.
Science Centre building was bleak
The Science Centre was an ugly grey box in Don Mills. We have bigger things to worry about than keeping it alive: Mallick, June 26
Like many in this region, I have fond childhood memories of the Science Centre; the contents of the Science Centre, that is. The building itself was a dark, sprawling labyrinth whose layout I never made sense of, and it was set in a bleak landscape of concrete and arterial roads. Thank you to columnist Heather Mallick for describing it as it was. It never will be considered a standout by people who think architecture is a form of abstract art.
David Arthur, Cambridge, Ont.
Like it or not, Brutalism is a style that has a very present legacy in North American architecture. It is simplistic in the extreme to simply deride the buildings as ugly, and use that as a salve for the pain of knocking them down. As someone in Edmonton once told me, "We never get 100-year-old buildings because we knock them all down when they turn 50." Even ugly architecture is a symbol of times past and should be viewed with appreciation. Far more important however is the cultural legacy of the Ontario Science Centre and the absolutely breathtaking stupidity and cynicism of Ford's plan to dismantle, move, and downgrade it. Ontario Place is a worse location.
Nicholas Uhlig, Toronto
CAPTION: Children attending Ontario Science Centre learn about static electricity.

Subject: Architecture; Education policy; Science education; Buildings

Document 424 of 500

Liberal critics were wrong about Chow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-critics-were-wrong-about-chow/docview/3073550394/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+critics+were+wrong+about+Chow&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last year, I caught some flak from fellow Liberals for supporting Olivia Chow for mayor. Outsiders to politics assume Liberals and New Democrats get along. It would make sense. We are both left-leaning parties that agree on a lot of the key issues and often vote together in parliaments.
The reality is that Liberals and New Democrats have a fierce and bitter rivalry, far more than Liberals and Conservatives.
Most often in municipal elections, without formal party affiliations, Liberal and Conservative operatives work together to back a moderate candidate at the middle of the political spectrum.
But in recent years, the lines have blurred as that centre and Liberals have shifted further leftward. More progressive Liberal operatives (like me) are increasingly backing candidates with more aggressive, downtown-urbanist policies.
Moderate Liberals don't love this trend. Liberals think of themselves as deeply pragmatic and of New Democrats as so ideological that once they're elected, they won't compromise their values and will fail to be popular with voters. As one Liberal said when they learned I was working for Chow: "You help the NDP get into city hall, and they'll tear it apart. And then she'll never win again."
But I argued then that Chow was a deeply pragmatic person, far more interested in getting good policy implemented than she is left wing theories or trying to score points for the NDP. As we look back on the first 12 months of her term, I'm happy to say I would argue the mayor has consistently proven the hypothesis.
Just look at where we were a year ago. The city was in a $1.8-billion fiscal hole. There was no clear affordable housing strategy. Few funds were rolling in from the federal Liberals. And the small stuff also clearly wasn't working. Park washrooms were closed, street garbage overflowed and dead tree stumps were left rotting in sidewalks.
A lot has changed. Toronto has a New Deal with the province to fund transit, infrastructure and fill the financial gap. Unprecedented levels of federal funding are rolling in for housing and for refugees. Garbage bins that work are landing on our streets. And, to my shock, I watched two new trees get planted in the sidewalk at Bay and Richmond streets on Friday.
All this is happening because Chow can and will compromise. She can strike a shocking and historic agreement with Doug Ford and lay down her sword in the Ontario Place battle. She can build a consensus at council. And, maybe most importantly, she knows the fights to have and to avoid. Rarely has the mayor's office waded into a social cause or international issue in which the city has no role.
All this has made her a shockingly popular mayor in the opinion polls.
But risks remain. A recent opinion column in this paper argued her reelection might be at risk with big union raises from the CUPE negotiations at the end of 2024. It's a fair argument, but I think it fails to acknowledge the success of the recent and reasonable TTC union settlement, as well as the massive raises being secured by private and public sector unions across the United States and Canada.
Rather, I would argue the mayor's biggest risks come as tents spring up in more parks across the city. Toronto is a city with no love for disorder. While public compassion and patience were stirred by the horrors of the pandemic and the realities of the housing crisis, both are beginning to stretch as the homelessness problem grows worse and more widespread.
If there is a new wave of public safety events with links to encampments, the conditions will be rife for a right wing candidate in the next election to seize the issue and potentially corner Chow. Her ability to compromise and collaborate with higher levels of government and the Toronto police will be tested and if not managed correctly, so too could her mayoralty.
Dan Moulton is a partner
with Crestview Strategy
and a Liberal strategist.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow can and will compromise, writes Dan Moulton, as seen with her historic agreement with Premier Doug Ford and the funding coming from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal government.Arlyn McAdorey The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Dan Moulton Contributor;

MPP ousted from PC caucus: Removal comes after Ghamari met far-right figure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-ousted-pc-caucus/docview/3073548636/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+ousted+from+PC+caucus%3A+Removal+comes+after+Ghamari+met+far-right+figure&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Goldie Ghamari has been thrown out of the Progressive Conservative caucus after meeting with a controversial, far-right Briton - a meeting that came on the heels of a number of "serious lapses in judgment," Premier Doug Ford's office says.
"The premier has made the decision to remove MPP Goldie Ghamari from the Ontario PC caucus, effective immediately," his office said in a statement Friday morning.
"This decision follows repeated instances of serious lapses in judgment and a failure to collaborate constructively with caucus leadership and as a team member. While this decision did not come easily, it has become clear that MPP Ghamari can no longer continue in her role within our caucus."
Ghamari responded to the move on Friday morning in a post on X.
"When one door closes, another one opens," she wrote. The ouster was lauded by the National Council of Canadian Muslims, which had been urging its supporters to email and call the premier.
"This was the last straw of many, many issues we've had over the years" with Ghamari, said council spokesperson Uthman Quick.
"We're pleased that action has been taken quite swiftly" and that the premier has been clear "that hate has no place in Canada, in Ontario. I think that's the most important factor right now."
Ghamari, who represents the Ottawa-area riding of Carleton, met with Tommy Robinson and had briefly posted on social media about their video conversation before deleting it.
Ford has previously said Ghamari is a "strong voice" speaking out against Islamophobia and antisemitism, but after the post about Robinson he criticized her for giving "a platform to an individual whose behaviour and beliefs are at odds with our government."
Robinson, who founded the anti-Islam English Defence League and has been convicted of a number of offences in the United Kingdom, was recently arrested in Calgary after being accused of misrepresenting himself in coming to Canada.
Ghamari has maintained she was "not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting."
"I chose to meet with him because I am an Iranian-Canadian immigrant who has been speaking out on behalf of human rights violations by the Islamic regime in Iran," she posted on social media, adding that the two discussed the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a branch of the Iranian armed forces that the Canadian government recently listed as a terrorist entity.
On social media, Robinson said he reached out to Ghamari "to ask her out on a date."
The National Council of Canadian Muslims said the interaction was "completely unacceptable" and had been pushing for Ghamari to be removed from caucus.
"We always reach out our hand, even to those who disagree with us, to try to have a dialogue and to try to really bring the feelings of our community across and to really help people understand why seeing certain things, promoting certain things or associating with certain people is clearly creating an unsafe situation for our community - creating division and hate," Quick said.
He said "we've been shocked by some of the things that she's tweeted," adding that "in this case, I think this was quite an extreme step. And that's why the reaction of our community was quite significant."
At Queen's Park, Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said Ghamari meeting with a far-right extremist "was deeply painful to many Canadians, and certainly many of them reached out to our office. Regrettably, when that was pointed out to her, she couldn't muster the courage even to give an apology."
With files from Alex Boyd
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau With files from Alex Boyd

Much more than a building: The Science Centre is the latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect, Editorial, June 22 The Science Centre was an ugly grey box in Don Mills. We have bigger things to worry about than keeping it alive: Mallick,

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/much-more-than-building/docview/3073545966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Much+more+than+a+building%3A+The+Science+Centre+is+the+latest+casualty+of+Doug+Ford%27s+policy+of+neglect%2C+Editorial%2C+June+22+The+Science+Centre+was+an+ugly+grey+box+in+Don+Mills.+We+have+bigger+things+to+worry+about+than+keeping+it+alive%3A+Mallick%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Science Centre is the latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect, Editorial, June 22
What is missing in the coverage of the Ontario Science Centre closure is a discussion of the programming we are losing with the building shuttered. That is equally important and more irreplaceable.
The Science Centre was a site for science education for children in all grades. At a time when education policy is pushing science, technology, engineering and math, it is hugely ironic that this "classroom" is now effectively shut down.
The Science Centre averaged 500,000 organized school visitors per year and, given the state of our underfunded schools, most of them will now not be exposed to immersive, real-life science education.
That's not even counting the hundreds of thousands of kids who visited with their families, or the thousands who had already registered for summer science camps.
With a new subway stop coming online, the Science Centre was only going to become more accessible to Torontonians and visitors alike.
Public education is under attack in Ontario. Science is under attack in Ontario. The environment is under attack in Ontario. The province is focusing on a building that would cost a tiny fraction of the new highway to repair. Our priorities need a rethink.
Ann Peel, Toronto
I've always thought of the Science Centre as being an educational institution.
Its location was chosen to provide enrichment to an underserved population. It's been a mainstay of school field trips for generations.
As a science teacher (now retired), I used to visit the Science Centre every August to get inspiration for the lessons I was planning for the year.
I look at the closure as an extension of the Ford government's lack of support and investment in education, and lack of compassion for those in the neighbourhood who could have continued to be enriched and inspired by it.
Education should be considered an investment, not a transaction to be bargained down to the lowest cost.
Grant Davidson, Milton
As a teacher in the Don Mills and Eglinton area, the Science Centre offered an accessible opportunity for learning and cultural enrichment in a neighbourhood that had few other options for affordable student excursions.
The iconic architectural building itself offered real-life lessons in ecological science, inviting curiosity and awe about our natural world, giving students the ability to see life thriving underground as they rode below-ground escalators.
I wonder if Premier Doug Ford has even been to the Ontario Science Centre. It needed the Ford government to provide support for maintenance and continued diversification. The last thing we need in Ontario is to destroy this magnificent jewel that we should respect and be proud of.
Where is our sense of historical excellence if we destroy a building that was designed by the brilliantly innovative architect Raymond Moriyama, becoming a model for science centres around the world? Where in the world do countries set out to deliberately destroy cultural landmarks?
If the idea is to replace the building with housing, the whole area of Don Mills and Eglinton is filled with highrise buildings. The people who live in those buildings need opportunities escape their concrete surroundings, learn about science, the living environment and the world, while enriching their spirit.
A walk through the Science Centre is a mind-expanding adventure that can't be replicated. We must try to save this iconic structure before it is bulldozed to make room for wealthy developers to make money.
Fran Bazos, Newmarket
The Science Centre was an ugly grey box in Don Mills. We have bigger things to worry about than keeping it alive: Mallick, June 26
Like many in this region, I have fond childhood memories of the Science Centre - the contents that is.
The building itself was a dark, sprawling labyrinth whose layout I never made sense of, and it was set in a bleak landscape of concrete and arterial roads.
Thank you to Heather Mallick for describing it as it was. It never will be considered a standout by people who think architecture is a form of abstract art.
David Arthur, Cambridge, Ont.
Like it or not, Brutalism is a style that has a very present legacy in North American architecture. It is simplistic in the extreme to simply deride the buildings as ugly, and use that as a salve for the pain of knocking them down.
As someone in Edmonton once told me, "We never get 100-year-old buildings because we knock them all down when they turn 50." Even ugly architecture is a symbol of times past and should be viewed with appreciation.
Far more important, however, is the cultural legacy of the Science Centre and the absolutely breathtaking stupidity and cynicism of Ford's plan to dismantle, move and downgrade it. Ontario Place is a worse location.
Nicholas Uhlig, Toronto
CAPTION: Children learn about static electricity at the Science Centre, which one reader calls an immersive, real-life science classroom.Ron Bull Toronto Star File Photo

Document 427 of 500

Repair without closing, firm suggests: Architect says immediate fixes could be done in coming months, rest phased in over next decade

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/repair-without-closing-firm-suggests/docview/3073545964/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Repair+without+closing%2C+firm+suggests%3A+Architect+says+immediate+fixes+could+be+done+in+coming+months%2C+rest+phased+in+over+next+decade&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost all of the exhibition space at the Ontario Science Centre is unaffected by structural concerns raised by engineers and the province did not have to take such drastic action by shutting down the whole building, says an architect from the firm that designed it.
Brian Rudy, a partner and principal architect at Moriyama Teshima, said immediate repairs could be done in the coming months, and the rest phased in over the next decade, all while keeping the building open to the public.
"The vast majority of exhibition spaces are completely safe," he told reporters at Queen's Park on Friday, and of the panels that do need to be replaced, "it's a drop in the bucket, probably less than one per cent. And again, those panels can be very easily repaired, cordoned off where they are an issue and the public can be completely protected."
Closing the building "was not a necessity, but a choice," he added.
The science centre was shuttered last Friday in a move that took many by surprise, after engineers deemed parts of the roof to be at risk of collapsing because of the use of reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete, also known as RAAC.
The Ministry of Infrastructure said parts of the building only had a few months before they would be structurally unsound, and that the centre would be closed to the public immediately, giving staff time to clear out the exhibits.
In the United Kingdom, more than 100 schools had to be shut down last fall after similar issues with RAAC were discovered.
The Rimkus Consulting Group's engineering report on the science centre estimated a cost of $22 million to $40 million and two to five years to repair the existing science centre buildings on Don Mills Road, and said fixes in "high risk" locations could cost as little as $522,500.
That would have allowed the centre to remain at least partially operational before an already scheduled move to the Ontario Place property in 2028 that's part of a controversial renewal that includes a privately owned spa.
The government is now looking to temporarily relocate the science centre.
The 52-page report, now under peer review by another engineering firm, said only one of the RAAC panels is classified as "critical risk," while five per cent were "high risk," two per cent "medium risk" and 92 per cent "low risk."
But the firm emphasized that was a preliminary assessment, warning that "the compromised panels have been functioning with a reduced factor of safety, when compared to the original design intent."
"A significant snow or rain loading occurrence could exceed the reduced load carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse," the report said.
A senior government official told the Star the government had to act because "any risk of collapse is too much risk."
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma has said "we had every intention on keeping the science centre open until the new science centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning ... I have an obligation to protect the public."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Thursday it would cost less to repair the existing site instead of shutting it down, relocating it temporarily and moving it to the Ontario Place redevelopment on the waterfront.
On Friday, Liberal MPP Adil Shamji, whose Don Valley East riding includes the science centre, said "the building is safe ... it should remain open (and) repairs can and should responsibly be done and that the province has the means to do it."
Shamji said the government is "taking details that are technically accurate, but coming forward with the completely flawed interpretation of those technically accurate details."
Rudy said the most pressing repairs "could probably be done in three to six months" and the rest spread out over a years-long schedule.
He said his firm designed the Toronto Reference Library "and it has been fully occupied for the entirety of its life ... and we've done a complete rejig of that building - every square inch of it has been rebuilt and reconstructed. And it's never closed its doors to the public. We do it by co-ordinating all areas, fixing them and opening them up."
"As an architect and as part of the team of professionals that build cities, we don't build cities by tearing down our historic structures," Rudy said. "This is such an amazing building that we all remember from our childhood, and we all remember taking our children through. How can we allow this building to be desecrated in this way and maybe potentially torn down? It's just unthinkable."
CAPTION: The Rimkus Consulting Group's engineering report on the Ontario Science Centre estimated a cost of $22 million to $40 million to repair the existing buildings, and said fixes in "high risk" locations could cost as little as $522,500.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 428 of 500

Home is where the heart is: Some in Toronto are opting to stay at a time when others are fleeing in droves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/home-is-where-heart/docview/3073541288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Home+is+where+the+heart+is%3A+Some+in+Toronto+are+opting+to+stay+at+a+time+when+others+are+fleeing+in+droves&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When asked why she stays in Toronto, Lil Moore says it boils down to this: "Better the devil you know than the devil you don't."
That's why she chooses to stay in her "disgusting" rental instead of moving to a more affordable city.
The adult entertainer is 35 now, but she experienced five years of homelessness when she was younger. And with rent control removed for Ontario homes built or first occupied after 2018, she fears losing the stability she has now could land her in a worst-case scenario, again.
Moore is among those choosing to stay in Toronto at a time when people are leaving the city in droves. The number of residents moving to other parts of the province or country from Toronto's census metropolitan area has been trending upwards since about 2010, according to Statistics Canada data, and the figures have more than doubled in recent years. From July 1, 2022 to July 1, 2023 alone, nearly 110,000 more people left Toronto to go somewhere else in Canada than the reverse, and about a third of them were aged 25 to 39.
At just 15, Moore became well-acquainted with the city's crowded shelters, cold streets and scarce affordable housing options. She signed up for the wait-list for affordable housing, but 20 years later, she's still waiting.
"I've been on the public housing list since I was 15 years old. I've never received an offer," she said. (In the first quarter of this year, there were nearly 87,000 people waiting to access social housing in Toronto, according to city data.)
At age 20, a shelter worker helped her get into a program that provided social assistance to help pay for a market-priced apartment.
About a decade later, Moore moved to a different rent-controlled house in Scarborough - one with a backyard so that her dogs could have more space - but she's still "kicking herself" for leaving that cheaper and more stable home for a place that she says has been rife with problems, from floods and mould to disrepair and problematic tenants.
Moore said there are two major reasons she feels she can't leave - one is financial and the other is cultural.
On the first, despite thehigh cost of living across Ontario, Premier Doug Ford's decision to cut rent control for units built after 2018 means an increasing number of apartments provincewide could be subject to large rent increases. She also describes what she sees as a pervasive trend of bad landlords (in her personal experience and as a former paralegal), and she fears any movement could cost her the stability she has now.
In addition, Moore said she doesn't believe she could find culture that mirrors what's in Scarborough, and that she needs to be somewhere that's safe for her partner, who is transgender.
"I will never leave Scarborough because the connection to Caribbean culture and Asian culture is so strong over here," she said, noting that her father was Indonesian-Chinese and that Caribbean and Indigenous women on the street raised her. "They are community to me. They are culture that I understand. Where am I going to find that?"
Still, Moore dreams of a future where she can own a house. But she knows for that to happen, it can't be in Toronto.
CAPTION: Lil Moore, a 35-year-old adult entertainer who experienced homelessness as a teen in Toronto, says she doesn't want to lose the stability she's found in the city.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Manuela Vega Toronto Star

Document 429 of 500

Inside the revolt against Trudeau: Journalist overheard minister talking to Liberals, taking stock of party's state of mind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inside-revolt-against-trudeau/docview/3073541216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inside+the+revolt+against+Trudeau%3A+Journalist+overheard+minister+talking+to+Liberals%2C+taking+stock+of+party%27s+state+of+mind&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Steven Guilbeault has been taking the temperature of the Liberal party caucus as calls for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's resignation mount.
The environment minister was in Toronto this week to meet with Members of Parliament who were awestruck by Monday's upset defeat in the Toronto-St Paul's byelection. He spent Thursday working the phones with Liberals across the country trying to take stock of how bad things really are.
I know this because Guilbault did some of this work in public, in the Via Rail business lounge, as he sat next to Canada's least-recognizable columnist: Me.
"If we're not trying to address it, it will fester," Guilbeault mused into his wireless headphones, loudly enough for me to hear without moving a muscle. "So this conversation will need to happen whether we want (it) to or not."
The conversation, as he phrased it on a subsequent call in French, is the same one every political pundit in Canada is having this week: Should Justin Trudeau stay or should he go?
Over the course of three phone calls, it became clear Guilbeault was feeling around for who might be most likely to call for Trudeau's departure. "I've been asked by PMO (the Prime Minister's Office) to make some calls and talk to people and report back," he said on one of those calls.
To date, a ragtag crew of mostly retired politicians - ex-MPs Wayne Easter and John Manley, former B.C. Liberal leader Christy Clark, as well as the environment minister's predecessor, Catherine McKenna - have all urged Trudeau to go. On Friday, the Star's Althia Raj reported that New Brunswick MP Wayne Long was in open revolt, passing a letter around caucus calling for Trudeau to resign.
One caucus source told me several other MPs had considered signing the letter, but that none had joined him as of Friday. That may change.
From his meetings, Guilbeault gleaned, "people are in shock. It's not just in the 416, or in Ontario, it's across the country." Some are "tanné," he said - a particularly Quebecois word, a mix of tired and annoyed - whereas others are just "stressed."
It might not exist yet, but Guilbeault repeatedly warned there could soon be a "campaign to show (Trudeau) the door."
I sent Guilbeault a direct message on Twitter Thursday night, which he saw but did not reply to. I also reached out to the prime minister's office, and several supposedly dissident MPs for comment Thursday night and Friday morning. None replied. (I also tried to intercept Guilbeault when we arrived in Montreal in the wee hours Friday, to no avail. We were on the same train but in different cabins and by the time I spotted him he was already in a taxi, driving away.)
In a statement released online after this story was first published, Guilbeault reiterated his support for the prime minister. "One-sided conversations taken out of context do not reflect the open and honest exchanges that I routinely have with my caucus colleagues," he said.
In background conversations with multiple Liberals, including multiple members of Parliament, it became clear that there are several Toronto-area MPs who are growing anxious. After Monday's stunning defeat, they now believe they could lose their reelection bids, and are starting to speak of revolt. I overheard Guilbault mentioning Toronto-Danforth MP Julie Dabrusin and Etobicoke-Lakeshore MP James Maloney by name.
Dabrusin, Guilbeault said, "is not necessarily saying that the boss has to go," using the Liberals' preferred nickname for Trudeau. "But she's saying that we need change."
I heard those same two names from two well-placed Liberal sources, while another source added Don Valley West MP Rob Oliphant, who multiple sources have told me in recent months has been growing increasingly disgruntled, to the mix. All are all worried, unhappy, and "stirring up (the) Toronto caucus." Multiple sources told me that, right now, this is mostly just private griping. Oliphant himself said on Twitter that he has not called for a leadership review.
While calls for Trudeau's departure might be limited right now, it might not stay that way.
As I sat on the train en route to Montreal, I spoke to one Member of Parliament who rattled off the names of a half-dozen Liberal MPs who are becoming increasingly noisy in their calls for the prime minister to quit. They put the "sedition number" at 15 to 20 members of caucus. Some of those rebels are drafting a letter calling for the prime minister to take his walk in the snow. (Given we are heading into July, it might look more like a dip in the lake.)
A bigger group in caucus is open to being swayed either way, the MP said. But they need to see evidence that the leadership is capable of change.
In his calls, Guilbeault was alternately reassuring and speculative.
While some Liberal MPs might be rattling their chains, he said on the phone, Trudeau is still the odds-on favourite to lead the Liberals into the next election. "It's highly unlikely that it happens," he said - it, he made clear, being Trudeau's resignation. But, Guilbeault added, "I wouldn't rule it out."
Unlikely as it may be, Guilbeault began gaming out what a post-Trudeau near future could look like. It wouldn't be easy, he said, and would probably plunge the Liberals into a leadership race next June.
"The three finalists," he speculated, "are Chrystia (Freeland), Anita Anand, Mark Carney." As to whether any of those non-Trudeau Liberal candidates could win the election, slated for fall of 2025, he offered only a "peut-être." It's not a sure thing, he said. (Other sources have told me in recent months that Foreign Minister Melanie Joly, Housing Minister Sean Fraser, and Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne are all, to varying degrees, organizing to run for the top job when Trudeau eventually exits.)
That kind of speculation isn't unique to Guilbeault, sources told me. "Most of cabinet," wants Trudeau gone, the ex-staffer told me Thursday evening, "but won't say anything to him, so they're gossiping with each other." Finger-pointing and blame are running rampant, as the Liberals try to understand how they lost one of the country's most reliably Liberal seats.
Guilbeault seemed at least alive to the threat all that grumbling could pose. "It makes it more difficult for the boss to stay," he said. "The last thing we should do is ignore this."
I don't know whether Guilbeault was making his calls on orders from the PMO, or if he just volunteered for the job. But there is definitely some outreach happening. I heard Guilbeault say in one call and a PMO source later confirmed that Trudeau has personally been making calls to some MPs in recent days, particularly those in vulnerable Toronto ridings.
So far, though, those efforts have been limited. I asked one Member of Parliament whether they'd heard anything from Trudeau, the PMO, or Guilbeault. "No word. Zippo. Nadda. Quiet. Nothing," they texted back.
In one call, Guilbeault ran through some options as to what could happen next to allay nerves: The prime minister could call an emergency caucus meeting, or he could do more direct outreach to the regional caucus chairs.
"We have to do something," he said. "Unless the PM wants to do 150 calls over the summertime," referring to roughly the number of Liberal MPs in the House of Commons. (La Presse reported Thursday that a national caucus retreat had been discussed earlier in June, before the end of the parliamentary session, but ruled out.)
But more talking might not be enough. Another MP told me there are now increasing demands inside caucus for something tangible to happen. "There is a general view that there needs to be changes in cabinet and PMO," the MP said.
They identified half a dozen ministers who they believed should be demoted or dropped from caucus entirely, including some of the most senior members of Trudeau's team.
The tone I'm hearing from caucus is steelier than it has been in the past. At the end-of-session retreat last June, MPs began to make their displeasure heard. The elected Liberals were increasingly frustrated at being marginalized and ignored, particularly as Canadians were growing fed up with nine years of Liberal rule. Trudeau promised he had heard those concerns. That caucus "ended on a high note," Guilbeault said on one call.
A year on, all those complaints remain. Never mind the mounting problems facing the country, the prime minister is having a hard time connecting with the MPs who delivered him three successive election wins.
Things are likely to get worse. There are four more byelections on the horizon, including three Liberal seats - two, in Halifax and B.C. - the Liberals are likely to lose. But the most stinging loss would be in LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, a traditionally safe Liberal seat in Montreal that former justice minister David Lametti won with ease in the last three elections. One influential Montreal Liberal told me the party is almost certain to lose the seat to Craig Sauvé, a popular city councillor running for the NDP.
This is the big, and possibly final, test of Trudeau's leadership. If he can't convince his own caucus that he is capable of change, how can he possibly convince the country?
When he ascended to the helm of the Liberal party in 2013, Trudeau told his team that the days of internal squabbles and out-of-touch politicos were over.
"Canadians want better leadership and a better government," he insisted. "Canadians want to be led, not ruled."
If he can convince his caucus those lofty promises are true today, even if they weren't then, he will likely keep his job. If he can't, he clearly has to go.
Justin Ling is a contributing columnist for the Toronto Star and an independent investigative journalist based in
Montreal. You can reach him by email at justinling@proton.me.
CREDIT: Justin Ling Contributing Columnist

Louis March, a community safety advocate, in wake of a teen being charged in an Etobicoke double homicide
"If the board chairman wants to get rid of the CEO, why would the CEO want to be there? Because it's just very embarrassing ... I would have resigned as well."
Former TTC board vice-chair Alan Heisey, on Rick Leary's abrupt departure
"This was obviously not the result we wanted, but I want to be clear that I hear your concerns and frustrations ... it is clear, I and my entire team have much more hard work to do to deliver tangible, real progress that Canadians can see and feel."
PM Justin Trudeau after his party's loss in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection
"I think what's going to happen now is there's going to be a lot of internal (Liberal) soul-searching ... There is going to be such pressure on (Trudeau) now - not to leave so much as come up with a reason that he should stay."
Former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne
"It's absurd to think that the whole building needs to be immediately shut down ... It's so obviously a political move."
Brian Rudy, a partner with Moriyama Teshima Architects, on the Ontario Science Centre's abrupt closure
"Since the 2014 commodity price crash, which caused a steep drop in business investment, labour productivity in Canada has remained essentially flat while (labour costs) have increased by more than 30 per cent ... This situation is unsustainable."
Consulting firm Deloitte, in new economic forecast

Donald Trump, recounting a suggestion to the UFC's president of having migrants fight as entertainment

Sean Penn, recalling when then-girlfriend Madonna directed the police his way
