Document 431 of 500

Liberal MP calls on Trudeau to resign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-mp-calls-on-trudeau-resign/docview/3073539367/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MP+calls+on+Trudeau+to+resign&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A revolt is brewing.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long has openly called for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to resign, penning a letter to caucus members Friday afternoon, the Star has learned.
"Colleagues, after reflecting on this week's defeat in Toronto-St Paul's and then seeing the response, I want you to know clearly and directly where I stand," he wrote.
"For the future of our party and for the good of our country we need new leadership and a new direction. The voters have spoken loud and clear they want change. I agree."
I'm told more than a handful of MPs were ready to sign Long's letter. It was understood it would leak to the media, and start a national conversation.
But many MPs who feel the same way Long does weren't ready to put their necks on the line in such a public fashion.
"A majority, if not the vast majority, of caucus want to see the prime minister step down," an Ontario Liberal MP told me Friday.
The list includes names that are part of Trudeau's team, who for their own career advancement don't want to be identified as backstabbers, be demoted or be prevented from running under the Liberal banner again. Long, the MP for Saint John-Rothesay, previously announced he wasn't running again.
I did not call 155 MPs. I can't tell you if it's a majority of caucus, but I can tell you many, many MPs feel this way.
Within caucus, it's being referred to as "the most difficult moment" for the Liberal party since the loss of 2011, when then-leader Michael Ignatieff plunged the party to third place with 19 per cent of the vote. Trudeau, according to public opinion polls, is only three points away from that result.
"The wheels are certainly coming off," said another Liberal MP.
Many MPs feel the only solution is for Trudeau to go. That is one group.
In another group are MPs who want to see drastic change. Some in this group feel the prime minister should have resigned last summer, but that it is now too close to the next election to change leaders.
The Liberals have a minority in the House of Commons. Any of the opposition parties could bring forward a non-confidence motion this fall when Parliament returns. The NDP could withhold support for the fall economic statement. The country could go into a Christmas-time election. Even if the NDP supports that confidence vote, another would be scheduled for the spring when the budget is tabled.
Even if Trudeau resigned next week, and a leadership vote was called for mid to late fall, that's a tight timetable to make a new leader election-ready. Another problem is a leadership race would be divisive for the party with the war in Gaza looming in the background.
It's why these MPs are pressing for a big shakeup.
Trudeau - who wants to hang on - seemed to be contemplating such a move.
Sources say he and former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney have spoken at least twice, prior to Monday's byelection. They've discussed Carney joining the team as finance minister. Carney's presence would provide the government with new energy. It could signal a new policy direction, and may help stop some of the bleeding of Liberal votes, enticing Liberals to come back to the fold. And with the prime minister desperate for a lifeline, Carney can easily dictate the terms of his participation.
But does he want to help set the policy direction for a government whose lifespan is unknown? Is it better for him to build support for a future leadership run from inside caucus or would he be better off waiting and seeing whether the push to get rid of Trudeau has legs?
If Carney waits it out, can Trudeau entice any big-name outsiders to join his team?
Bringing Carney on board might be a good start for the many MPs who want wholesale changes in cabinet. At the top of the list of ministers they want dumped is Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland. Other names on an Ontario list passed around include: National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Minister of Citizens' Services Terry Beech, Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, Emergency Preparedness Minister Harjit Sajjan, Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks, and Agriculture Minister Lawrence MacAulay.
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly also comes up, but in the context of being moved to another position.
Some of the people who want a big shuffle feel the prime minister is still surrounded by his 2015 team, and that there are smart, young MPs anxious to step up, and prove they can put a fresh face on a tired government.
But for the first group, who want Trudeau to go, they feel any changes - even big changes - will be seen as superficial.
The problem they see, the problem they hear over and over at the doorstep, is with the leader. No amount of changes to the supporting cast can fix the trouble with the main lead. Canadians have tuned out what the prime minister is saying. Whatever good work the Liberals do, no one wants to listen.
The other person who isn't listening is the prime minister. I could find no MP who could tell me they had spoken to Trudeau directly. His chief of staff, cabinet ministers, and caucus chairs are all sussing out the mood of the Liberal team, but the prime minister - not one to talk to his MPs in regular times - doesn't appear to have picked up the phone to gauge the mood of MPs after Monday's byelection loss. Now, Long has forced a conversation the prime minister hoped to escape.
CAPTION: Liberal MP Wayne Longis forcing a conversation the prime minister had hoped to escape, Althia Raj writes.Liberal Party

Document 432 of 500

Ontario Place doesn't need Science Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-place-doesnt-need-science-centre/docview/3073539182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Place+doesn%27t+need+Science+Centre&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The abrupt closure last week of the Ontario Science Centre by Premier Doug Ford's government has given impetus to some rather drastic "solutions" to what supposedly ails Toronto's urban core.
The province's two most dubious remedies are its plans to relocate a science centre it claims to be in a state of near collapse to Ontario Place.
And Ford also wants to destroy the forests and pebble beaches of Ontario Place's West Island - a rare urban oasis of serenity - to make way for an enormous private luxury spa operated by an Austrian resort developer.
That act of state-sponsored vandalism is already underway.
Power brokers like Ford live to make big statements, which are often vanity projects.
This city's political and urban-planning elite pines for a Toronto equivalent to the Sydney Opera House; the London Eye; or Millennium Park, Chicago's waterfront extravaganza of public amenities.
We should stop here and assess the beneficial impact of recent improvements in Toronto's core amenities before committing to megaprojects we might regret.
The elite blundered with Roy Thomson Hall, an intimidating brutalist structure accessible only to those with often pricey symphony tickets; and with a hideous expansion of the Royal Ontario Museum that juts over Bloor Street near Avenue Road like a menacing tetradactyl.
In recent years, Toronto has quietly embellished the urban core with more free amenities - parks, sculpture gardens, unique natural ecosystems and open spaces.
They include Love Park, Ireland Park at Eireann Quay, Corktown Common and an infrastructure triumph in rerouting the Don River to create a new park where the Don empties into one of the world's greatest freshwater lakes.
And there's Ontario Place, which still bears resemblance to the novel waterfront mecca of arts, entertainment and communion with nature that first opened in 1971.
Eventually, Ontario Place fell victim to the decline of the adjoining Exhibition Place and was allowed by the province to fall into disrepair.
Yet Ontario Place has not been abandoned, as Ford seems to believe.
Ontario Place welcomed more than two million visitors in 2022, the most recent year for which attendance figures are available.
That's despite minimal programming and maintenance at Ontario Place since the park was formally closed in 2012. And it's also before the Ontario Line nearing completion connects Ontario Place, Exhibition Place and the Port Lands with the downtown core.
It is received wisdom that new "iconic" spectaculars add to the economic vitality of their cities.
And they might, but there's scant proof of it.
Cities like London and Chicago were economic powerhouses before their newest landmarks appeared, as Toronto is today. And measuring their incremental economic impact is well-nigh impossible.
What you can measure, using metrics like economic and population growth, is quality of life. Do people want to live here?
For Toronto, the answer is a resounding yes.
In the short space of four years prior to the pandemic, Toronto's GDP grew by almost 15 per cent, to $442 billion - or about one-fifth of Canada's economy.
And even before the recent surge in immigration Toronto's population was among the fastest growing on the continent.
So, what's the way forward?
It's important first to know that major parks like Ontario Place that have a claim on the hearts of the people - public spaces such as Manhattan's Central Park, London's Hyde Park, Montreal's Mount Royal, Vancouver's Stanley Park and the Luxembourg Garden in Paris - are bereft of sprawling commercial enterprises.
Change is due at the 55-year-old Ontario Science Centre, conceived before the internet and AI. In the decade prior to the pandemic, its attendance dropped by about 40 per cent.
The Science Centre's replacement planned by the province, about half the size of the original facility and with updated 21st-century exhibits, makes sense as a showcase for Toronto's growing tech economy.
But it needn't be located in the largely natural setting of Ontario Place's lagoons, walking trails and forests that evoke Venice and the Kawarthas at the city's front door.
Relocated instead to the vast parking lot that Exhibition Place has become, the new science centre and next-door Ontario Place would boost each other's attendance.
Ideal additions to a refurbishment of Ontario Place's natural amenities include small-scale extensions of the Royal Ontario Museum and the Art Gallery of Ontario, following the example of the new Tate Modern museum on the River Thames and an extension of New York's Whitney Museum near the Hudson River.
Ontario Place would also benefit from a comeback of its original Children's Village, a magnet for families, and its Forum revolving amphitheatre, a unique and intimate concert venue.
None of that requires a mooted hundreds of millions of dollars to publicly subsidize a luxury spa that walls off 16 acres from the public for deep-pocketed folks who already have many Toronto spas from which to choose.
With apologies to master architect Daniel Burnham, who asked us to think big or not at all, there are also times to think small. Small-scale is where people live, with free or low-cost amenities that make a positive difference in everyday lives.
CAPTION: Ontario Place has not been abandoned, as Premier Doug Ford seems to believe; it welcomed more than two million visitors in 2022, the most recent yearfor which attendance figures are available, David Olive writes.Chris Young The Canadian Pressfile photo
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Toronto's Pride parades are here! Use our interactive guide to map your weekend

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-pride-parades-are-here-use-our/docview/3073217176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+Pride+parades+are+here%21+Use+our+interactive+guide+to+map+your+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Welcome to Toronto's ultimate guide to Pride 2024!
From upcoming events to local-recommended hot spots to check out, this guide is here to help you navigate all things Pride.
EVENTS
JUNE 5
Screening of "Hearts Beat Loud"
College Park, 444 Yonge St.
Free. All ages.
Bring a lawn chair - and Kleenex - to this sweet family drama starring Nick Offerman (of "Parks and Rec," but mostly that beautiful "Last Of Us" love storyline with Murray Bartlett). Bonus: the pre-show concert and on-site farmer's market.
https://www.pridetoronto.com/events/college-park-after-hours-hearts-beat-loud/
JUNE 6
Shikshook.to
Bangarang, 552 College St.
From $15. 19+
Go all in for an exhilarating evening celebrating the Arab/SWANA LGBTQ+ community: think pulsating Arabic pop DJ sets, live performances and belly dancing by Lebanese drag queen star Samar.
https://www.eventbrite.ca/e/shikshook-queer-arabswana-party-
tickets-898241302917
JUNE 7
Screening of "It's Only Life After All"
Hot Docs Ted Rogers Cinema, 506 Bloor St. W.
$15. All ages.
The Indigo Girls are queer icons; the group gets its flowers in this intimate documentary that melds decades of home movies with contemporary footage. Stay for "Glitter and Doom," a summer romance soundtracked to Indigo Girls hits.
https://hotdocs.ca/whats-on/films/its-only-life-after-all
JUNE 8
Newcomer's Pride Celebration
Central Neighbourhood House, 349 Ontario St.
Free. All ages.
Create a safe space for all, including LGBTQ refugees and asylum seekers at this full-day event. Workshops, art exhibits and networking sessions help create a sense of community.
https://www.pridetoronto.com/events/embrace-diversity-newcomers-pride-celebration/
JUNE 8
Pooch Pride parade
Joel Weeks Park (start), 10 Thompson St.
Free. All ages.
Take your good boy or girl for a serious strut. Festivities kick off at noon, where glitter, body paint and dog treats are on offer, plus doggie goodie bags for the first 40 pooches. Finish up at Black Dog Brewing for pints, ear scratches and a very special performance.
https://www.ticketleap.events/tickets/save-our-scruff/pooch-pride
JUNE 9
Proud Voices: A Pride Cabaret Celebration
12 Alexander St.
From $20. All ages
See stars, both emerging and established, during this evening of stellar performances featuring tenors, sopranos and baritones from cult cabaret indie opera company Opéra Queens. Gird your loins - there's also a community open mic.
https://tickets.buddiesinbadtimes.com/TheatreManager/1/login?event=0
JUNE 14
ROM After Dark: PRIDE
Royal Ontario Museum, 100 Queen's Park
$40. 19+
A night at the museum is also a good idea, particularly when it features performers like gospel choir Inkloosiv Voices, hip hop artist Lex Leosis and Spice Girls drag impersonators, the Spice Queens. Bonus beyond the zig-a-zig ah: admission to the ROM exhibitions.
https://www.rom.on.ca/en/whats-on/rom-after-dark/rom-after-dark-pride
JUNE 14, 15, 16
Aportia Chryptych: A Black Opera for Portia White
Canadian Opera Company theatre, 227 Front St. E.
$75. All ages
This original COC commission - a vibrant sonic mix, including classical opera, spoken word and rap - celebrates Portia White, a trailblazing contralto from Nova Scotia, who sang in European concert halls in the 1940s to great acclaim.
coc.ca/Aportia
JUNE 15, 16
Til Sunset
Cherry Beach, 1 Cherry St.
From $42. 19+
Good vibes are guaranteed at this signature weekend festival. Trinidadian soca sensation Tempa headlines on Saturday, while DJs - including superstar Josh Harrison - drop the Sunday oontz oontz oontz.
https://www.universe.com/events/til-sunset-out-in-the-woods-tickets-4WKYHF?ref=universe-discover

College Park, 444 Yonge St.
Free. All ages.
Bring your own skates, or borrow a pair for free, at this family-friendly roller dance extravaganza, complete with DJ sets, drag numbers and an on-site flea market featuring LGBTQ+ vendors.
https://www.pridetoronto.com/events/skates-sounds-roller-drag-ball/

Ripley's Aquarium of Canada, 288 Bremner Blvd.
$60. 19+
Marvel at the splendour of it all: beautifully crafted cocktails, creative drag performances and the joy of watching one of Toronto's most serene spaces transform into a rainbow wonderland. (And yes, you can still pet the stingrays.)
https://www.ripleys.com/attractions/ripleys-aquarium-of-canada/events/night-at-the-aqueerium

JUNE 24
Screening of "Backspot"
Fort York National Historic Site, 100 Garrison Rd.
Free.
Devery Jacobs ("Reservation Dogs") is a national treasure, and her queer cheerleading drama "Backspot" is so good it deserves to do "Barbie" box office. Bring a lawn chair; pom-poms optional.
https://www.topictureshow.com/fort-york
JUNE 26 to 30
Green Space Festival
Barbara Hall Park, 519 Church St.
Free
The 519, Canada's biggest LGBTQ+ organization, is also one of Toronto's most beloved community hubs. Its four-day outdoor festival of performers, DJs and drag stars is infused with heart, and also acts as a fundraiser. Don't miss it.
https://greenspaceto.org/
JUNE 29, 30
Family Pride
Church Street Public School, 83 Alexander St.
Free. All ages (adults must be accompanying a child 0 to 12)
Bring the fam for a weekend of fun: face painting, storytelling, a magic show, and good old-fashioned community building.
https://www.pridetoronto.com/events/family-pride/
JUNE 30
Pride Parade
Starts at the corner of Church and Bloor, 2 p.m.
Free. All ages.
The big one! Pride Month concludes with the legendary parade - the largest in Canada; one of the largest in the world - that takes over the Village, meanders through the city and ends up at Nathan Phillips Square, where a main stage and beer garden await. The festivities are special, but it's the spirit that matters most: it's a march of solidarity and of love.
QUEER-OWNED BUSINESSES
Toronto is home to dozens of diverse queer-owned businesses that are well worth supporting year-round. We polled the owners of a few of our favourite spots to ask: What are your top tips for enjoying Pride month?
Hot Shot Chocolate, handmade chocolates (use code PRIDE15 for 15 per cent off all June), 568 Bathurst St.
Pride tip: "There's a lot of hype around the traditional parade and being out on Church Street. But it's also okay to stay home or do something casual. Follow your heart." - Kelly Maxifield
Lower East Side Studio, gender-neutral hair studio, 392 College St.; 827 College St.
Pride tip: "There are really cute events happening throughout the city the whole month. Find the smaller ones and support all your businesses and drag kings and queens!" - Daniela Pérez & Nancy Haddad
Bistitchual Yarn Shop, community crafting space, 266 Jane St.
Pride tip: "I'm a deeply anxious introvert, so my favourite way to enjoy Pride is to participate in the Quiet Queer Craft-a-long, mainly on Instagram. You use queer-dyed yarn or make a craft that's by a queer designer. It's a way of promoting all the amazing career fibre artists." - Kelsi Morris
Glad Day Bookshop, multi-use space selling books, coffee, cocktails and home to Canada's longest running drag brunch, 499 Church St.
Pride tip: "Do what you want to do, not what you think you have to do." - Michael Erickson
Lox + Schmear, fish smokehouse and bagel shop, 1030 St. Clair Ave. W.
Pride tip: "Now that I have a child, we like to do more family-friendly events. Do what makes you feel good! Be safe, have fun!" - Lev Levine
Little Ghosts Books, horror bookstore, 930 Dundas St. W.
Pride tip: "Stay hydrated. I always have a dehydrated friend that drinks a million cocktails, and I'm always asking, 'Did you drink water in the last 24 hours?'" - Chris Krawczyk
Craig's Cookies, boutique cookie shop (on offer this month, the Pride mix: six cookies, the colours of the rainbow), 4 locations including 483 Church St.
Pride tip: "Pride offers a great opportunity to do our due diligence as a society, in looking at where Pride started in Toronto specifically. I think the more research and education you do, (the more you see) the problematic amount of work that still has to be done globally, and also in Canada, to preserve the rights of the queer community. Hopefully, that education creates understanding and understanding creates love and acceptance." - Craig Pike
Juju Grooms, dog and cat grooming studio, 1684 Queen St. W.; 801 Dundas St. W.
Pride tip: "Make sure you try to talk to new people, experience as much as you possibly can and just enjoy Pride for what it is. It's an opportunity for us to all get together in a form of protest and love."- Juju Castelo & Britney Bentick
Hurt/Comfort Tattoos, tattoo shop, 1159 Dundas St. E.
Pride tip: "Not liking loud, crowded events doesn't make you less queer. Have fun the way that you want to." - Nicole Williams &Amber Robyn
ICONS' FAVOURITE SPOTS
PRIYANKA
Priyanka is the ultimate multi-hyphenate: performer, musical artist, international entertainer. The first-ever winner of "Canada's Drag Race," she's also the new co-host of the acclaimed, Emmy Award-winning HBO show "We're Here." Priyanka's single "Come Through" is one of Canada's all-time greatest bangers - and her much-anticipated debut album is due this summer.
Crews and Tangos
508 Church St., second floor
"Being a brown girl, I never experienced a dancehall room like the second floor at Crews and Tangos. Dancing my night out every night during Pride 2016 - before I became Priyanka - and having the biggest soca artist Allison Hinds do a pop-up performance of her iconic hit 'Roll It Gyal' was where I realized I have space in the queer community, too!"
Yohomo Fest
Various locations
"Let me tell you something about Yohomo: these events during Pride are the most inclusive, loving, radical, fun events ever. There is so much love at Love on Top, my favourite Pride party. You know when you're nervous to go into a party because no onewill talk to you? At Yohomo events, everyone is smiling and nodding at each other. I love them!"
Geoska Tattoos
20 Eglinton Ave. E, Suite 441
"Pride is so monumental for a lot of people that they want to have the memory last forever. This is most inclusive, loving tattoo shop in Toronto that can literally do any style or size of tattoo you want to commemorate for Pride: it's the perfect place to go!"
Starry Night at Greenspace
Barbara Hall Park, 519 Church St.
"The Super Bowl of drag! I remember being in my first year of drag, and when I was asked to be a part of the show, I felt like, Wow, I made it. All of the money goes back into the 519 and helps our queer and trans community."
The Curated Market Co.
2116-A Queen St. E. and 2473 Yonge St.
"I love, love, love this market. It's the perfect place to get local jewellery to wear to all of your Pride events. Also, if you're like me and love to have people over: candles, candles and more candles. Gotta have the love palace smelling good!"
O'Grady's on Church
518 Church St.
"So I don't know about you but sometimes during Pride season, mama has a few too many. Need a good brunch to gas you up to party again? This is the spot. Plus, it's right on Church Street where all of the action is!"
PHIL VILLENEUVE & ARMAND DIGDOYO
Phil Villeneuve and Armand Digdoyo started community hub Yohomo in 2016 to better connect the diversity and creativity of Toronto's LGBTQ+ communities. Their event listings and blog content spotlight queer parties, events, plays, screenings and marches happening around Toronto. This June, Yohomo is hosting multiple events, including a Gay Gardens block party at DoWestFest; a full-building Drake hotel takeover event called Joy, and Yohomo Fest, a two-day Pride weekend extravaganza at the Bentway.
Three Dollar Bill
1592 Queen St. W.
"A new jewel in the west end, Three Dollar Bill is the perfect Queen dive bar. They offer wildly diverse programming; the staff is so lovely and welcoming - and easy on the eyes - and the drinks are thoughtful and fun. They have a hidden patio, and this would be a great night out if you don't to head to a HUGE Pride party, or into the wilds of Church Street." -Phil
Buddies in Bad Times Theatre
12 Alexander St.
"We highly recommend checking out everything Buddies has programmed for the month of June. The list of performances, shows, parties, and talks is astounding and appeals to a wonderfully wide audience of folks. They've outdone themselves." -Armand
Fuel+
469 Front St. E.
"This is a fave spot of ours during Pride weekend to grab an energy drink, booster, shake and snack, if you're in the east end. (Owners) Gary and Chester run a tight and very healthy ship, and this is a great spot to chill for a second, recharge, and get some much-needed nutrition so you can keep twirling all weekend." -Phil
Spa Excess
105 Carlton St.
"Not only is there a bar inside, but there are four floors of fun to explore - as well as a rooftop patio. This is another spot to rest and recharge... but in a different kind of way.
They're open 24 hours, so pop in at any time for a steam and a good time." -Armand
Gateau Ghost
974 College St.
"This new queer-run bakery and brunch spot just opened on College - the menu is delicious, the pastries are divine and the design is bright and beautiful. In case you need a spot to sit with your pals and plan out the weekend - or just have a brunch moment before heading into the Pride tornado - we highly reco this spot." -Phil
Vertigo
66 Gerrard St. E.
"Finally - the weekend is over. You think you've had enough, but for some reason you have a little fuel left in the tank. We'll see you at Vertigo, one of the city's best after-hours clubs. On Pride (parade) weekend, they stack the lineup with a LGBTQ+ who's-who to keep you bouncing until the sun comes up." -Armand
S. BEAR BERGMAN
S Bear Bergman is the author of nine books, and founder and publisher of children's press Flamingo Rampant. His 10th book, "Special Topics In Being A Parent," comes out July 30.
Toronto Kiki Ballroom Alliance Blended Families Ball
Impact Lounge, 238 Parliament St.
"TKBA's Pride events are always joyful, super-fun, and a great way to see Toronto's ballroom icons and legends make their moments. It's worth pushing bedtime a little later to bask in Toronto's Black queer excellence. Bring earplugs if you're sound-sensitive, and put on something truly fabulous to soak it all in."
Another Story Bookshop
315 Roncesvalles Ave.
"As a family that loves books, we'll take any excuse to go browse the shelves, but Another Story Bookshop is our favorite Pride destination, both for their great Pride displays of kids' books and for their knowledgeable staff, who can advise you no matter what you're looking for."
2-Spirit Pow Wow
Downsview Park
"2-Spirit Pow Wow is a huge, gorgeous event with amazing regalia and beautiful vibes - to say nothing of the fry-bread tacos. With chairs and a blanket, this can be an all-day family event. Never been to a pow wow before? Bay Area American Indian Two-Spirits (BAAITS) has a great etiquette guide for non-Indigenous attendees."
Korean Village Restaurant
628 Bloor St. W.
"Admittedly, this isn't an explicitly queer restaurant, but it remains one of the most welcoming spots in Toronto for families with young kids; our queer family has always been loved and celebrated here. The small rooms along the sides are perfect for a group of six or eight, and when our kids were little, it always felt fractionally less nerve-wracking to take our kiddos out in public for dinner when we had our own little slice of space. Jason, the owner, will tuck your fam into a well-air-conditioned room to enjoy his famous hospitality and decompress from a day of Pride doings."
Family Pride on Church Street
Church St. School, 83 Alexander St.
"This is where we meet up with friends, let the tweens free-range a bit, apply our temporary tattoos and let the kiddos run (safely) amok or watch a children's show- Sharon from Sharon, Lois & Bram headlined the Family Stage a couple years ago."
Trans Pride Stage and Rally
Allan Gardens
"In addition to being a trans fa, with so much love for our larger trans family, we've always found this both a very fun march vibe and a little more manageable than the giant main parade on Sunday when our kids were very small. The stage at the end features amazing musicians and performers."

Document 434 of 500

Doug Ford removes MPP Goldie Ghamari from caucus after she met with British far-right figure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-removes-mpp-goldie-ghamari-caucus-after/docview/3073217162/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+removes+MPP+Goldie+Ghamari+from+caucus+after+she+met+with+British+far-right+figure&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Goldie Ghamari has been thrown out of the Progressive Conservative caucus, after Premier Doug Ford called her meeting with a controversial, far-right Briton "deeply regrettable."
"The premier has made the decision to remove MPP Goldie Ghamari from the Ontario PC caucus, effective immediately," Ford's office said in a statement Friday morning.
"This decision follows repeated instances of serious lapses in judgment and a failure to collaborate constructively with caucus leadership and as a team member. While this decision did not come easily, it has become clear that MPP Ghamari can no longer continue in her role within our caucus."
Ghamari responded to the move on Friday morning in a post on X.
"When one door closes, another one opens," she wrote.
Ghamari, who represents the Ottawa-area riding of Carleton, met with Tommy Robinson and had briefly posted on social media about their video conversation before deleting it.
Ford's office had previously criticized Ghamari for giving "a platform to an individual whose behaviour and beliefs are at odds with our government."
Robinson, who founded the anti-Islam English Defence League and has been convicted of a number of offences in the United Kingdom, was arrested in Calgary this week after being accused of misrepresenting himself in coming to Canada.
Ghamari has maintained she had no idea of Robinson's views.
"I was not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting," she posted on social media.
"I chose to meet with him because I am an Iranian-Canadian immigrant who has been speaking out on behalf of human rights violations by the Islamic regime in Iran." She added that the two discussed the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a branch of the Iranian armed forces that the Canadian government recently listed as a terrorist entity.
On social media, Robinson said he reached out to Ghamari "to ask her out on a date."
The National Council of Canadian Muslims said the interaction was "completely unacceptable" and called for Ghamari's ouster.
Ford has previously said Ghamari is a "strong voice for anti-Islamophobia" and antisemitism.
More to come.
With files from Alex Boyd
CAPTION: In this 2020 photo, Premier Doug Ford is joined by MPP Goldie Ghamari to honour the victims of Ukraine airlines Flight 752, which was shot down near Tehran.

Document 435 of 500

Doug Ford's plan to relocate the Science Centre to Ontario Place will only hasten its destruction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-plan-relocate-science-centre-ontario/docview/3073217103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+relocate+the+Science+Centre+to+Ontario+Place+will+only+hasten+its+destruction&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The abrupt closure last week of the Ontario Science Centre by Premier Doug Ford's government has given impetus to some rather drastic "solutions" to what supposedly ails Toronto's urban core.
The province's two most dubious remedies are its plans to relocate a science centre it claims to be in a state of near collapse to Ontario Place.
And Ford also wants to destroy the forests and pebble beaches of Ontario Place's West Island - a rare urban oasis of serenity - to make way for an enormous private luxury spa operated by an Austrian resort developer.
That act of state-sponsored vandalism is already underway.
Power brokers like Ford live to make big statements, which are often vanity projects.
This city's political and urban-planning elite pines for a Toronto equivalent to the Sydney Opera House; the London Eye; or Millennium Park, Chicago's waterfront extravaganza of public amenities.
We should stop here and assess the beneficial impact of recent improvements in Toronto's core amenities before committing to megaprojects we might regret.
The elite blundered with Roy Thomson Hall, an intimidating Brutalist structure accessible only to those with often pricey symphony tickets; and with a hideous expansion of the Royal Ontario Museum that juts over Bloor Street near Avenue Road like a menacing tetradactyl.
In recent years, Toronto has quietly embellished the urban core with more free amenities - parks, sculpture gardens, unique natural ecosystems and open spaces.
They include Love Park, Ireland Park at Eireann Quay, Corktown Common, and an infrastructure triumph in rerouting the Don River to create a new park where the Don empties into one of the world's greatest freshwater lakes.
And there's Ontario Place, which still bears resemblance to the novel waterfront mecca of arts, entertainment and communion with nature that first opened in 1971.
Eventually, Ontario Place fell victim to the decline of the adjoining Exhibition Place and was allowed by the province to fall into disrepair.
Yet Ontario Place has not been abandoned, as Ford seems to believe.
Ontario Place welcomed more than two million visitors in 2022, the most recent year for which attendance figures are available.
That's despite minimal programming and maintenance at Ontario Place since the park was formally closed in 2012.
And it's also before the Ontario Line nearing completion connects Ontario Place, Exhibition Place and the Port Lands with the downtown core.
It is received wisdom that new "iconic" spectaculars add to the economic vitality of their cities.
And they might, but there's scant proof of it.
Cities like London and Chicago were economic powerhouses before their newest landmarks appeared, as Toronto is today. And measuring their incremental economic impact is well-nigh impossible.
What you can measure, using metrics like economic and population growth, is quality of life. Do people want to live here?
For Toronto, the answer is a resounding yes.
In the short space of four years prior to the pandemic, Toronto's GDP grew by almost 15 per cent, to $442 billion - or about one-fifth of Canada's economy.
And even before the recent surge in immigration Toronto's population was among the fastest growing on the continent.
So, what's the way forward?
It's important first to know that major parks like Ontario Place that have a claim on the hearts of the people - public spaces such as Manhattan's Central Park, London's Hyde Park, Montreal's Mount Royal, Vancouver's Stanley Park and the Luxembourg Garden in Paris - are bereft of sprawling commercial enterprises.
Change is due at the 55-year-old Ontario Science Centre, conceived before the internet and AI. In the decade prior to the pandemic, its attendance dropped by about 40 per cent.
The science centre replacement planned by the province, about half the size of the original facility and with updated 21st-century exhibits, makes sense as a showcase for Toronto's growing tech economy.
But it needn't be located in the largely natural setting of Ontario Place's lagoons, walking trails and forests that evoke Venice and the Kawarthas at the city's front door.
Relocated instead to the vast parking lot that Exhibition Place has become, the new science centre and next-door Ontario Place would boost each other's attendance.
Ideal additions to a refurbishment of Ontario Place's natural amenities include small-scale extensions of the Royal Ontario Museum and the Art Gallery of Ontario, following the example of the new Tate Modern museum on the River Thames and an extension of New York's Whitney Museum near the Hudson River.
Ontario Place would also benefit from a comeback of its original Children's Village, a magnet for families, and its Forum revolving amphitheatre, a unique and intimate concert venue.
None of that requires a mooted hundreds of millions of dollars to publicly subsidize a luxury spa that walls off 16 acres from the public for deep-pocketed folks who already have many Toronto spas from which to choose.
With apologies to master architect Daniel Burnham, who asked us to think big or not at all, there are also times to think small. Small-scale is where people live, with free or low-cost amenities that make a positive difference in everyday lives.
CAPTION: Ontario Place has not been abandoned, as Premier Doug Ford seems to believe; it welcomed more than two million visitors in 2022, the most recent year for which attendance figures are available, David Olive writes.

Document 436 of 500

Justin Trudeau didn't just lose a safe Liberal seat, Pierre Poilievre figured out how to win it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-didnt-just-lose-safe-liberal-seat/docview/3073217101/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+didn%27t+just+lose+a+safe+Liberal+seat%2C+Pierre+Poilievre+figured+out+how+to+win+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For almost a decade, Conservatives have been grappling with whether they can put forward a robust, principled narrative and win government, or whether they would have to shrink down, be inoffensive and abandon their values to eke out a victory. This week's byelection result in Toronto-St. Paul's settles the debate.
Much of the analysis following this week's Toronto federal byelection result centres around the failure of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to hold what was once a safe Liberal seat. No doubt, voters have lost their fondness for Trudeau's brand of politics. But the shocking electoral result says as much about Conservative success as it does about Liberal failure.
Don Stewart's victory for the Pierre Poilievre-led Conservatives, a riding home to among the most Laurentian of elites, proves that putting forward well-articulated and principled conservative solutions can win in ridings across the country, with Canadians from all walks of life - no restraint or demurring required.
It wasn't so long ago that Canada's Conservatives were grappling with an existential crisis. Despite being able to maintain an admirable third of the popular vote and more than a third of seats in the House of Commons following Stephen Harper's 2015 defeat, the party had a voter ceiling problem.
The Conservative voting coalition was solid, but it was just too small to form government, even as the Liberal vote share diminished with each election. It was tempting in these circumstances to argue that Conservatives ought to tone it down a notch - that the safest political strategy was to try not to say too much, avoid the social issues and hope for the best.
Indeed, Andrew Scheer and Erin O'Toole each attempted a version of this strategy, Scheer struggling (at times) to play down his conservative values and O'Toole (at first) struggling to conjure some up. Scheer ran as Harper with a smile, but after winning the leadership, O'Toole went all-in on a centrist voter appeasement strategy, attempting to woo the Liberal-sympathetic media and tempering his economic plans so as not to alienate fans of high spending and big government.
Instead, O'Toole's hard pivot to the middle came off as a pale imitation of the real thing and even though Trudeau's popularity was waning, voters weren't presented with a strongly differentiated alternative.
As many Canadians grew enraged by the government's heavy-handedness through the end of the pandemic and keen fiscal and monetary policy watchers began to see early signs of inflation problems, the Conservative caucus decided they wanted a new leader. Poilievre ran as a freedom Conservative with a powerful message - that Conservatives could be true to themselves and still win.
Not only that, but that making the case for conservative reform was the path to getting up over 35 per cent and winning a majority government. The party membership loved it and Poilievre ran away with the leadership race, but doubters remained. Sure, they said, card-carrying Conservatives love him and his message, but what about the urban and suburban voters Conservatives need to win elections?
Those of us on Poilievre's leadership campaign saw what the pundits didn't. Young, urban voters were showing up for Poilievre during the leadership campaign, seeing him not as an out-of-touch ideologue but as someone who was finally talking about the issues they cared about - from housing affordability to crime and public safety - matching the frustration they felt, with solutions that seemed different and viable.
Despite this early evidence, the doubters persisted. They predicted Poilievre would walk back some of his early policies or risk alienating "soccer moms" and other important voting blocs. But the Conservatives stuck to their guns. And after this week, we know how some Forest Hill soccer moms (along with all kinds of other midtown Torontonians) feel about what Poilievre's offering: it's working for them.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Deputy Leader Melissa Lantsman, right, greets a supporter as she attends an Federal byelection election night event for Toronto-St.Paul's candidate Don Stewart in Toronto on Monday.
CREDIT: Ginny Roth Contributor

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Voters; Elections

Document 437 of 500

Olivia Chow is making deals and winning over critics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chow-is-making-deals-winning-over-critics/docview/3073217084/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow+is+making+deals+and+winning+over+critics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last year, I caught some flak from fellow Liberals for supporting Olivia Chow for mayor. Outsiders to politics assume Liberals and New Democrats get along. It would make sense. We are both left-leaning parties that agree on a lot of the key issues and often vote together in parliaments.
The reality is that Liberals and New Democrats have a fierce and bitter rivalry, far more than Liberals and Conservatives.
Most often in municipal elections, without formal party affiliations, Liberal and Conservative operatives work together to back a moderate candidate at the middle of the political spectrum.
But in recent years, the lines have blurred as that centre and Liberals have shifted further leftward. More progressive Liberal operatives (like me) are increasingly backing candidates with more aggressive, downtown-urbanist policies.
Moderate Liberals don't love this trend. Liberals think of themselves as deeply pragmatic and of New Democrats as so ideological that once they're elected, they won't compromise their values and will fail to be popular with voters. As one Liberal said when they learned I was working for Chow: "You help the NDP get into city hall, and they'll tear it apart. And then she'll never win again."
But I argued then that Chow was a deeply pragmatic person, far more interested in getting good policy implemented than she is left wing theories or trying to score points for the NDP. As we look back on the first 12 months of her term, I'm happy to say I would argue the mayor has consistently proven the hypothesis.
Just look at where we were a year ago. The city was in a $1.8 billion fiscal hole. There was no clear affordable housing strategy. Few funds were rolling in from the federal Liberals. And the small stuff also clearly wasn't working. Park washrooms were closed, street garbage overflowed and dead tree stumps were left rotting in sidewalks.
A lot has changed. Toronto has a New Deal with the province to fund transit, infrastructure and fill the financial gap. Unprecedented levels of federal funding are rolling in for housing and for refugees. Garbage bins that work are landing on our streets. And, to my shock, I watched two new trees get planted in the sidewalk at Bay and Richmond on Friday.
All this is happening because Olivia Chow can and will compromise. She can strike a shocking and historic agreement with Doug Ford and lay down her sword in the Ontario Place battle. She can build a consensus at council. And, maybe most importantly, she knows the fights to have and to avoid. Rarely has the mayor's office waded into a social cause or international issue in which the city has no role.
All this has made her a shockingly popular mayor in the opinion polls, a clear acknowledgment by voters, many who would cast a Liberal vote for their representative at Queen's Park or in Ottawa, that the worst they feared in Chow simply isn't coming true. And if she continues to govern in this manner, is almost guaranteed to succeed in a reelection effort in a few short years.
But risks remain. A recent opinion column in this paper argued her reelection might be at risk with big union raises from the CUPE negotiations at the end of 2024. It's a fair argument, but I think it fails to acknowledge the success of the recent and reasonable TTC union settlement, as well as the massive raises being secured by private and public sector unions across the United States and Canada.
Rather, I would argue the mayor's biggest risks come as tents spring up in more parks across the city. Toronto is a city with no love for disorder. While public compassion and patience were stirred by the horrors of the pandemic and the realities of the housing crisis, both are beginning to stretch as the homelessness problem grows worse and more widespread.
If there is a new wave of public safety events with links to encampments, the conditions will be rife for a right wing candidate in the next election to seize the issue and potentially corner Chow. Her ability to compromise and collaborate with higher levels of government and the Toronto police will be tested and if not managed correctly, so too could her mayoralty.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow are led on a tour of the facilities of vaccine producer Sanofi in Toronto on May 30. "Olivia Chow can and will compromise," writes Dan Moulton. "She can strike a shocking and historic agreement with Doug Ford and lay down her sword in the Ontario Place battle. She can build a consensus at Council. And, maybe most importantly, she knows the fights to have and to avoid."
CREDIT: Dan Moulton Contributor

Document 438 of 500

Some Liberals fear 'woke' image: Shock rippling through party after byelection loss

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/some-liberals-fear-woke-image/docview/3073048462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+Liberals+fear+%27woke%27+image%3A+Shock+rippling+through+party+after+byelection+loss&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shock and disappointment over Monday's byelection defeat in midtown Toronto is still rippling through federal Liberal circles, with some insiders stating their party has veered too far from the political centre and gained a damaging association with the "woke" left.
It's one of the many factors Liberals are pointing at - alongside souring views on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership - to explain how they lost to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives on Monday, in a riding that has elected Liberals reliably in 10 previous campaigns since 1993. On Thursday, former B.C. Liberal premier Christy Clark became the latest prominent voice to call on Trudeau to resign.
For Marcus Powlowski, a Liberal MP from northern Ontario, part of the problem is a public perception - which he thinks is wrong - that the party is connected to a closed-minded political correctness that prioritizes "identity politics" over "universal human values."
Powlowski described this type of politics as including "cancel culture," in which people are shamed for expressing certain opinions, and said the party needs to counter the perception it is too "woke" by better showcasing its centrist values of fiscal discipline, support for free enterprise, and the "traditional" liberalism of free speech, civil liberties and equality.
"In a lot of people's minds, the Liberal party has become synonymous with concepts like 'woke' culture, cancel culture and identity politics," Powlowski said. "I think it has alienated a lot of people."
Sen. Percy Downe, who was chief of staff to prime minister Jean Chrétien, agreed the Liberals have "gone too far left" under Trudeau's leadership, pointing to how Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault declared this year in the House of Commons that he is a "proud socialist."
"He's in the wrong party," Downe said.
He also believes the Liberal government has earned a reputation for lengthy consultations without concrete results, which is now hurting its chances with the electorate. Voters, he said, "think they're a collection of words as opposed to action."
The soul-searching comes as Trudeau continues to distance himself from journalists' questions amid renewed speculation over whether he will stay on as Liberal leader. Trudeau's official schedule for Thursday said the prime minister - who hasn't taken questions since the stunning loss on Monday - was in the GTA for a party fundraiser and was set to deliver prepared remarks.
So far, those who have openly called for a new leader are outside the sitting Liberal caucus. CBC reported Wednesday that former Liberal MP Frank Baylis is weighing a possible leadership bid to replace Trudeau. Baylis did not respond to an interview request from the Star.
At the same time, several cabinet ministers and sitting MPs have publicly stated they believe Trudeau should stay, including Guilbeault, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, Immigration Minister Marc Miller and Health Minister Mark Holland. Trudeau, in his first public statement on the byelection result, suggested he's focused on his job and earning back Canadians' support.
The Prime Minister's Office has not ruled out convening a national caucus meeting to hash out feedback about the disappointing result and to plot a path forward for the party. Because of the summer parliamentary recess, the next scheduled caucus meeting isn't until September.
Downe was one of the first Liberals to publicly call for Trudeau to go, in an op-ed published last fall. But on Thursday, the senator suggested it might now be too close to the next general election for Trudeau to resign without saddling his successor with an unwinnable political situation.
"The window for him leaving may have closed," Downe said.
Many other Liberal insiders who spoke with the Star after the byelection loss stopped short of calling for Trudeau to leave, even if some of them felt the sting of the defeat should spark significant changes in how the party is preparing for the next general election campaign, currently set for the fall of 2025.
For Downe, the party should focus on a handful of core issues, like economic prosperity, immigration and health care. It should also "stop the cheap shots at the Conservatives," he said, referring to a statement Freeland made this week when she said the opposition Tories have a vision that is "cold, cruel and small."
"To mock that support is not helpful," Downe said. "It looks like political separation."
Powlowski, meanwhile, said he still has faith in Trudeau's leadership and believes the party is focusing on the right issues - including a promised school food program, dental care and public coverage of diabetes medication and birth control. He suggested if people are willing to "actually listen" to Trudeau and the policies he's promoting, that will dispel what he sees as a problematic association with the "woke" left.
"I think that's part of the political centrism ... back to the universal human values and getting away from the identity politics," he said. "I don't think it's just white males - I think a lot of different cultures and a lot of people from different countries ... feel the same way about a lot of these issues."
Others say their confidence in the current leadership of the Liberal government has been shaken. One MP, who agreed to speak Thursday on condition they aren't named, said many in the Liberal caucus are now wondering whether their own seats are at risk.
With files from
Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rides a horse during celebrations for the 10th anniversary of the Tsilhqot'in decision, in Nemaiah Valley, B.C., on Wednesday. Trudeau hasn't taken questions about his leadership plans from the media since the Conservatives won this week's byelection in a Toronto riding that had been Liberal since 1993.DARRYL DYCK THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Tonda MacCharles

Document 439 of 500

Opposition slams PCs over closure: NDP leader says repairs cheaper than relocation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opposition-slams-pcs-over-closure/docview/3073047242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+slams+PCs+over+closure%3A+NDP+leader+says+repairs+cheaper+than+relocation&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fix it, don't nix it.
Premier Doug Ford could save money by repairing the existing Ontario Science Centre instead of shutting down and temporarily relocating it before moving it to the waterfront by Ontario Place, says NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Speaking in front of the now-shuttered, sprawling property on Don Mills Road on Thursday morning, Stiles said efforts to save the centre are kicking into high gear, with a petition that has now been signed by more than 55,000 supporters.
"There's a very large part of (the building) not even impacted at all" by deteriorating aerated concrete cited as a concern in a recent engineering report, said Stiles of the facility designed by the late Raymond Moriyama that opened in 1969.
"So they could get in there right now. They could even make some of the repairs right now. And then if we need to close it, just temporarily - for weeks, whatever it takes - to repair it, not to shut it down forever," she added.
The science centre was abruptly closed last Friday after engineers found parts of the roof to be at risk of collapsing because of the type of concrete used in its construction.
The Ministry of Infrastructure said parts of the building only had a few months before they were structurally unsound, and that the immediate shutdown would allow staff to clear out the building.
While the bombshell Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report publicly released last Friday said it could cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two to five years to repair the buildings, it also said fixes in "high risk" locations could cost as little as $522,500.
That would have allowed the science centre to remain at least partially operational before an already scheduled move to a new lakeshore facility at Ontario Place in 2028.
The 52-page report, which is being peer-reviewed by another engineering firm, said only one of the reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete (RAAC) panels is classified as "critical risk" while five per cent were "high risk," two per cent "medium risk" and 92 per cent "low risk."
But the firm emphasized that was a preliminary assessment, warning that "the compromised panels have been functioning with a reduced factor of safety, when compared to the original design intent."
"A significant snow or rain loading occurrence could exceed the reduced load carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse," the report said.
When the Star broke the news last Friday, a senior government official, speaking confidentially to discuss the situation, said the Progressive Conservatives had no choice.
"Any risk of collapse is too much risk," the official said.
All planned summer camps, which start next week, have been relocated to a nearby public school. A weekend wedding planned at the centre was moved to the nearby Aga Khan Museum.
The search for a smaller, temporary location for the science centre is now underway.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said Monday that the government moved as soon as it was made aware of the perils.
"We had received notification about the specific type of concrete and the specific type of panels being used around the world in the '50s, '60s and '70s," said Surma.
"We had every intention on keeping the science centre open until the new science centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning," she said. "I have an obligation to protect the public."
Its closure comes against the backdrop of the government's controversial decision to move the science museum to Ontario Place and become part of the redevelopment there along with a privately owned spa.
Stiles said if the NDP was in power, it would "repair and revitalize" the science centre at its Don Mills location.
"The fight for our science centre is far from over," she said. "It has only just begun. This is my message to my Ontarians - the future of the science centre is not in Doug Ford's hands - it's in our hands; it's in your hands."
The premier "and his politicians think they can close the doors on a beloved world-class cultural science institution with less than a day's notice and nobody would bat an eye? Well, hell no, we won't let him get away with that. We all know Ford's motive here - it is just to justify his mega-spa, his vanity project, at Ontario Place."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who also held a press conference out front of the fences now surrounding the closed facility, said she'd call for a special investigation of the government's "shady backroom deals" including the science centre and Ford's Ontario Place plans.
In its business case to move the science centre to Ontario Place, the province said it would save about $250 million over 50 years, mostly because the new facility would be half the size of the Don Mills site.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Ford condemns MPP's meeting with far-right figure: PC member says she didn't know he led anti-Islam group

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-condemns-mpps-meeting-with-far-right-figure/docview/3073046531/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+condemns+MPP%27s+meeting+with+far-right+figure%3A+PC+member+says+she+didn%27t+know+he+led+anti-Islam+group&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A meeting between Goldie Ghamari and British far-right figure Tommy Robinson is "deeply regrettable," Premier Doug Ford's office said after the Carleton MPP posted - and then deleted - a screenshot of their video conversation.
In an emailed statement, Ford's office condemned the decision to "give a platform to an individual whose behaviour and beliefs are at odds with our government." Robinson was arrested in Calgary this week on an outstanding immigration warrant in connection with allegations he misrepresented himself when entering the country. A founder of the anti-Islam English Defence League, he has been convicted in the U.K. of multiple offences, including assault.
For her part, Ghamari says she did not know who Robinson was. "I was not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting," the MPP posted to X on Wednesday, after removing a post from Instagram that showed the two in conversation, above the caption, "Oh hi there Tommy Robinson." She added that she condemned both Islamophobia and antisemitism.
"I chose to meet with him because I am an Iranian-Canadian immigrant who has been speaking out on behalf of human rights violations by the Islamic regime in Iran." She added that the two discussed the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a branch of the Iranian armed forces that the Canadian government recently listed as a terrorist entity.
Robinson, whose real name is Stephen Christopher Yaxley-Lennon, also took to X with his own explanation: "I contacted @gghamari to ask her out on a date as I'm in Canada, we didn't talk politics," he posted. "You clowns don't control who can & cannot talk to each other."
The interaction drew fire from the National Council of Canadian Muslims, who called it "completely unacceptable conduct" from an elected official.
"This is not only deeply offensive to Ontarians who care about combating hate in our province," the organization said in a petition renewing its calls to remove Ghamari, who has said she grew up in a Muslim family, from the government caucus.
In the wake of protests last fall, Ghamari was also criticized by the NCCM for "recycl(ing) some of the worst Islamophobic fear-mongering" after she took to social media to criticize the Muslim leaders she said had "justif(ied) Hamas's barbarity." She also said hearing the prayer Allahu akbar in the streets - meaning "God is great," a phrase commonly used by Muslims to express joy or faith - is "concerning" and was sometimes appropriated by radicals.
At the time, the premier said he would not remove her from caucus. "She has really been a strong voice for anti-Islamaphobia, making sure we have zero tolerance" he told reporters. "No matter if it's antisemitism or Islamophobia, it has no place here in Ontario."
CREDIT: Alex Boyd Toronto Star

Ontario against gender-based violence, Part 2: Intimate partner violence, a survivor tells her story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-against-gender-based-violence-part-2/docview/3073307078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+against+gender-based+violence%2C+Part+2%3A+Intimate+partner+violence%2C+a+survivor+tells+her+story&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Listen here or subscribe at Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Google Podcasts, or wherever you listen to your favourite podcasts. Stay updated on episodes via our X page. If you would like to support the journalism of the Toronto Star, you can at thestar.com/subscribingmatters.
Guest: Cait Alexander, artist and social activist
In 2022, 184 women were killed violently in Canada. That's one woman killed every 48 hours. More than four million women, 30 per cent of all women aged 15 and older, report that they have experienced sexual assault. It's costing Canada almost $8 billion to deal with the aftermath of spousal violence alone. Is a country that in the past was considered a global champion of human rights, failing to effectively protect women at home? In 2022, the federal government launched a National Action Plan to end gender-based violence. The resultant agreement with several provinces will be seeing $162 million distributed over four years in Ontario alone. But advocates say they're frustrated by the pace of change, if two important pieces of legislation for women currently pending in Ontario is any indication. Over two episodes, we discuss them both.
In today's episode: Bill 173 is the Intimate Partner Violence Epidemic Act, and it calls for intimate partner violence to declared an epidemic in the province. It took a year, but the Ontario government has finally shown its support towards the bill, introduced by the NDP. The bill received a rare standing ovation from all parties in the house after being introduced in April. Last year, similar calls from an inquest into the deaths of three women at the hands of their former partner was rejected by the Ford government.
Our guest today, Cait Alexander, was nearly murdered by her partner. Yet he walks free, and she had to leave Canada. The criminal case for her ex-boyfriend, accused of trying to kill her in 2021, was rescheduled twice before it was ultimately dropped due to the Jordan ceiling under the Criminal Code, that ensures everyone charged with an offence has the right to trial within a limited time-frame.
If you are a victim of intimate partner violence, there is help. The Star has compiled a list of resources here.
This episode was produced by Paulo Marques and Saba Eitizaz

Doug Ford calls Tory MPP's meeting with British far-right figure 'deeply regrettable'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-calls-tory-mpps-meeting-with-british/docview/3073069558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+calls+Tory+MPP%27s+meeting+with+British+far-right+figure+%27deeply+regrettable%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A meeting between Goldie Ghamari and British far-right figure Tommy Robinson is "deeply regrettable," Premier Doug Ford's office said after the Carlton MPP posted - and then deleted - a screenshot of their video conversation.
In an emailed statement, Ford's office condemned the decision to "give a platform to an individual whose behaviour and beliefs are at odds with our government." Robinson was arrested in Calgary earlier this week on an outstanding immigration warrant in connection with allegations he misrepresented himself when entering the country. A founder of the anti-Islam English Defence League, he has been convicted in the U.K. of multiple offences, including assault.
For her part, Ghamari says she did not know who Robinson was. "I was not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting," the MPP posted to X on Wednesday, after removing a post from Instagram that showed the two in conversation, above the caption, "Oh hi there Tommy Robinson." She added that she condemned both Islamophobia and antisemitism.
"I chose to meet with him because I am an Iranian-Canadian immigrant who has been speaking out on behalf of human rights violations by the Islamic regime in Iran." She added that the two discussed the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a branch of the Iranian armed forces that the Canadian government recently listed as a terrorist entity.
Robinson, whose real name is Stephen Christopher Yaxley-Lennon, also took to X with his own explanation: "I contacted @gghamari to ask her out on a date as I'm in Canada, we didn't talk politics," he posted. "You clowns don't control who can & cannot talk to each other."
The interaction drew fire from the National Council of Canadian Muslims, who called it "completely unacceptable conduct" from an elected official.
"This is not only deeply offensive to Ontarians who care about combating hate in our province," the organization said in a petition renewing its calls to remove Ghamari, who has said she grew up in a Muslim family, from the government caucus.
In the wake of protests last fall, Ghamari was also criticized by the NCCM for "recycl(ing) some of the worst Islamophobic fear-mongering" after she took to social media to criticize the Muslim leaders she said had "justif(ied) Hamas's barbarity." She also said hearing the prayer Allahu akbar in the streets - meaning "God is great," a phrase commonly used by Muslims to express joy or faith - is "concerning" and was sometimes appropriated by radicals.
At the time, the premier said he would not remove her from caucus. "She has really been a strong voice for anti-Islamaphobia, making sure we have zero tolerance" he told reporters. "No matter if it's antisemitism or Islamophobia, it has no place here in Ontario."
CAPTION: In this 2020 photo, Premier Doug Ford is joined by MPP Goldie Ghamari to honour the victims of Ukraine airlines Flight 752, which was shot down near Tehran.
