'The Trudeau Liberals are sinking': What the Toronto byelection results say about Canada's political future

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-liberals-are-sinking-what-toronto/docview/3072281330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+Trudeau+Liberals+are+sinking%27%3A+What+the+Toronto+byelection+results+say+about+Canada%27s+political+future&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Byelection results are no surprise
Liberals grapple with stunning Toronto byelection loss and Justin Trudeau's future, June 25
The recent byelection results for Toronto-St. Paul's are telling. Comparing the 2024 byelection to the 2021 general election, one crucial fact stands out: low voter turnout significantly favoured the Conservative candidate, now the incoming federal MP, Don Stewart. Why did 17,316 eligible voters in the Toronto-St. Paul riding abstain from voting? Why did the Conservative party gain 1,968 new supporters, while the NDP lost 4,992 and the Liberal party lost 10,874? The answer lies in strategy. Conservatives had the most organized get-out-the-vote strategy and their supporters were more motivated. The party effectively utilized social media platforms like X, YouTube and Meta platforms (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp). St. Paul's historically has more progressive voters, but many chose not to vote, which is concerning. Progressives must wake up to the political reality. We cannot take our supporters for granted. If this trend continues and voter turnout remains low across Toronto, the biggest winners in the next federal election will be the Conservatives led by Pierre Poilievre, who is taking nothing for granted. Let's learn from this and ensure higher engagement in future elections.
Walied Khogali Ali, Toronto
For most Toronto progressives, news of this loss eclipsed the Oilers' loss on Sunday evening. There is no longer such a thing as a "safe Liberal seat." Conservative candidate Don Stewart's lawn signs were out first (and they wisely didn't include the squib "Team Poilievre," whose name can be polarizing). Former Liberal MP Carolyn Bennett did not use her considerable influence, wealthy families in places like Rosedale, Forest Hill and Cedarvale didn't like the hike on capital gains tax, and one-issue voters on Israel/Gaza got sweet-talked by the Poilievre Conservatives the same way they did by Stephen Harper. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau reneged on his promise to abolish first-past-the-post. A ranked balloting system would have solidified the fact that St. Paul's remains a riding with mostly socially progressive residents. Did some of those residents stay home on Election Day? You bet they did.
Ron Charach, Toronto
As a resident of Toronto-St. Paul's, I am frustrated and concerned. Our newly elected MP does not represent so many in my riding. Past governments have failed to implement simple fair voting changes to our system, resulting in power shifts which endanger policies and programs on which most Canadians agree. Why can't we have simple co-operation among progressive parties? No merger is necessary. Let's co-operate to win in the 50 ridings in Canada where parties could defeat the Conservatives, simply by making space for the progressive candidate most likely to win. Parties can co-operate to their mutual benefit, or we voters can take this into our own hands.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
The first of many Liberals to go
So much for sunny ways and sunny days under the Liberal Party of Canada! After nine years in which this country went from being an affordable and capably run nation into a test lab for Liberal and NDP policies, people are waking up! At least it appears so in the Liberal stronghold in the heart of Toronto, a seat that they kept for the last 31 years and flipped to the Conservatives in the byelection. This is the decisive moment, a moment of hope for Canadians that this miscreant government, run by prime minister who has imposed heavy burdens on Canadians to satisfy a minority of people, has finally reached its expiration date. It's time for them to go.
Steven Eleftheriadis, North York
The results of the federal byelection in Toronto is an indicator that the Trudeau Liberals are sinking. The party must now choose a new leader before they will be on the back benches with their NDP cohorts for many elections to come. The Liberals must either go down with their carbon tax and high interest rates or rise like a phoenix with a new idea of starting the process without hurting Canadians and Canada.
Wayne Robertson, Chatham
After the federal byelection results, one wonders if Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland will take the helm of the Liberal Party, become the next Kim Campbell and leave the Liberals with only two seats like the Progressive Conservatives won in the 1993 election? We are in a similar position with loss of faith in the party leader, but also the party in general.
Mike Walker, Washago, Ont.
Make peace with the name change
Whatever you want to call it, a name change is the least of its problems: Elliott, June 25
Columnist Matt Elliott's article on moving on from the Sankofa name is right on. The name is set, regardless of its popularity or lack thereof. It's what to do now that counts. Many of us with a marketing background might see this as an opportunity. Lemons to lemonade, right? My husband and I were in this business, and in fact ran our own shop for 25 years. We would have told the city that while Sankofa Square is a meaningful name, it's not a marketing name. The square needs a second name to build a personality for the destination, a presence in downtown Toronto, a place people want to go to. It hasn't had the money to do much since COVID. It's grey and barren. A well-thought-out marketing name would create a base on which activities can be built. How can Sankofa be remembered but not pushed in people's faces to cause more disharmony? Make the first big event something that recognizes Sankofa and what it stands for, and then start using a marketing name. Give up fighting over Sankofa and what it means. Use it to shift over to a marketing name that everyone can get excited about.
Nancy Miller, Toronto
I don't agree with columnist Matt Elliott who said Sankofa Square needs more events. When I'm in that part of town, I long for seating, trees and plants and a place to get a good coffee or a gelato to eat in the shade. Couldn't installing these amenities be part of the general refurbishment of Yonge Street, if that's still going to happen?
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
CAPTION: Leslie Church speaks to Liberal party before knowing the results of the byelection vote.Don Stewart, a financial and investment adviser, pulled off a stunning upset win for the Conservatives in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection.

Document 457 of 500

The Science Centre was an ugly grey box in Don Mills. We have bigger things to worry about than keeping it alive

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/science-centre-was-ugly-grey-box-don-mills-we/docview/3072281329/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Science+Centre+was+an+ugly+grey+box+in+Don+Mills.+We+have+bigger+things+to+worry+about+than+keeping+it+alive&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing the Ontario Science Centre has distressed many people for many reasons. Some, like me, are emotional, the big ole place a warm memory from childhood, although the shabby fountainless stained concrete building in an isolated car-dependent part of Toronto makes me wince a bit now. If you love something, keep it in shape.
The bros of modernist architecture stain the concrete further with their tears, like the Globe's embittered architecture columnist mourning "Toronto's great Brutalist temple of childhood wonder, Raymond Moriyama's hillside wonderland."
Brutalism is ugly, like the gloomy concrete hulk of Robarts Library Robarts Library and the unfixable Manulife Centre, architectural manspreading that nobody loves, especially now as life gets grimmer. Sixty years ago, trendy architects wanted huge monoliths to overawe pointless little humans.
Builders now glue glass boxes to these grim buildings to make them look more inviting. It's even worse. Remember, ubiquitous cheap glass is the concrete of our future, no match for global heating.
The late Moriyama doesn't fare well. In 2023 he wrote to the Star claiming he had used "concrete to last far beyond 100 years."But he was designing grey boxes in Don Mills, not the Pyramids, and apparently used the now-notorious lightweight RAAC semi-concrete in parts of the roof.
In the glassy-eyed 1960s, brand-name Siporex might have seemed perfect for those new airy (non-air conditioned) modernist schools. Now the Star reports that 360 of Ontario's 4,500 schools are being monitored for the same aerated concrete that has left many aging British school buildings empty lest roofs collapse on children's heads.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford closed the Science Centre fast and his critics are suspicious and irate. That this was Ford's dream come true doesn't make him wrong. Ontario Place might well be a better spot for a new science centre, beautiful and accessible. Ford has money on his mind, which critics do not, not in the same way.
Toronto has to prepare for hard times. The science centre roof might not cope with a big snowfall but rainfall is an equal danger, the climate emergency having brought us ruinous rain all this month.
Some angry people have misunderstood the import of the Rimkus engineering report that doomed the buildings.
They say it wasn't that bad, not realizing that the weaker parts of the roof are in crucial areas, a steel roof replacement might take five years, and the science centre might now be uninsurable for visitors.
They're worried about the building; the province is also worried about the children inside the building and what might happen even if partial repairs began with construction walled off inside. They say "low risk." Parents want "no risk." That's what makes them parents.
Toronto needs new subway cars on Line 2, the current ones near the end of life just like the roof. Mayor Olivia Chow hopes the feds will offer help though I can't see why it's a federal matter. Prime Minister Trudeau might consider it though.
Journalists think the recent Conservative win in Toronto-St. Paul's matters hugely. But Trudeau isn't worried about ridings, he's worried about the looming U.S. election. If Trump wins, the world is in huge trouble in every sector. I doubt Canadians will think libertarian-minded Conservatives are up to the task of keeping Canada functional in a manic chaotic Trumpy world.
Like the rest of us, Trudeau, Ford and Chow have no idea what will happen next. Will Toronto get subway money? Flood money? Will taxes have to soar? Honestly, a roof in Don Mills is not the priority.
We won't always get what we want but we might get what we need. That's the hope at least.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed June 21, 2024 after an engineering report found problems in the roof panels.

Document 458 of 500

Doug Ford was wrong to close the Science Centre. He made it worse by robbing Ontarians of the chance to say goodbye

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-was-wrong-close-science-centre-he-made/docview/3072281037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+was+wrong+to+close+the+Science+Centre.+He+made+it+worse+by+robbing+Ontarians+of+the+chance+to+say+goodbye&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When a major landmark closes, time is often provided for the public to have a final look. It may take the form of a closing ceremony, or a set period of time to visit before the doors are locked forever.
People require time to process their feelings and memories about places that were a treasured part of their lives, similar to the period of mourning when a beloved person passes.
Two years ago, my hometown high school closed after a century of serving the community. Toward the end of the school year, an open house was held to allow a final look. Displays covering the school's history were assembled and the cafeteria's legendary chocolate chip cookies were sold. The afternoon allowed alumni to revisit that time of their lives and provided a sense of closure.
The sudden shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre by the provincial government on Friday does not provide that sense of closure. Queen's Park has robbed us of the opportunity to celebrate and mourn an institution that, for over half a century, opened the eyes of children to the wonders of science.
When I heard the news, I rushed over to take a final look. Observing those wandering around, the shock of the decision was still settling in for those aware of it, creating a feeling of numbness. I processed a mixture of nostalgia for childhood visits, anger over the site's decay and heartbreak that a pioneer in the field of interactive museums and an architectural landmark was allowed to expire in such a quiet manner.
When I left shortly before the building closed, the emotions were more evident around me, especially during the final firing up of the hair-raising Van de Graaff generator. I regretted not being able to bring my wife, who was working that afternoon, as she grew up going to the Science Centre regularly for the exhibits and other events, such as jazz concerts.
The closing demonstrates how the current provincial government places more value on enriching developers and supporters than maintaining public spaces that benefit Ontarians in ways beyond economic considerations.
There is the example of the Greenbelt lands that the provincial government intended to remove to allow development, which sparked nearly a year of public outcry before the decision was reversed in October.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt," Premier Doug Ford declared. "It was a mistake to establish a process that moved too fast. This process, it left too much room for some people to benefit over others. It caused people to question our motives."
Last week's shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre will cause further questioning of the provincial government's motivations. The rush to close makes little sense given the engineering reports cited as the reason for the closure do not depict immediate danger and would not affect the entire site.
The Science Centre's proposed move to Ontario Place felt driven by political and financial considerations over practical ones, to provide legitimacy to the controversial redevelopment of what could be excellent public waterfront space into a spa complex few people outside the government appear to desire.
As for the current site, some of which was already slated for residential redevelopment, its future transit ties via the Eglinton Crosstown and Ontario Line subway extension would have boosted future attendance.
Government officials associated with both projects, such as Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, are defending the closure based on safety concerns and protecting children, but the satirical website The Beaverton may be on target when it joked that the province "cannot afford to repair the roof while spending more money than it would cost on partisan ads touting his government's alleged successes."
Leaving the site open for even a few more weeks would have disrupted fewer summer plans for families who enrolled their children in camps, or intended to go together.
But, more importantly, it would have shown a genuine sense of respect toward the public's feelings about the Ontario Science Centre and the proper opportunity to say goodbye, instead of simply raising an angry outcry.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was suddenly ordered closed by the Ontario government on Friday.
CREDIT: Jamie Bradburn Contributor

Subject: Science; Redevelopment

Title: Doug Ford was wrong to close the Science Centre. He made it worse by robbing Ontarians of the chance to say goodbye

Document 459 of 500

The inside story of TTC CEO Rick Leary's resignation + slim odds in a lottery for affordable housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/inside-story-ttc-ceo-rick-learys-resignation-slim/docview/3072281035/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+inside+story+of+TTC+CEO+Rick+Leary%27s+resignation+%2B+slim+odds+in+a+lottery+for+affordable+housing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, June 26 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on TTC CEO Rick Leary's resignation, a lottery for affordable rental units and the fallout from the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection.
DON'T MISS"It's just very embarrassing." Inside Rick Leary's resignation as CEO of TTC
Behind the scenes, tensions between TTC chair Jamaal Myers and CEO Rick Leary laid the groundwork for the latter's unexpected resignation last Thursday. In a closed-door meeting last October to address allegations of workplace misconduct, which are under investigation and have not been proven, Myers tried to have Leary put on leave. The relationship further deteriorated from there as Myers took to going around Leary to speak with other TTC staff about agency matters. Here's how the resignation unfolded and what's next for the transit agency responsible for 2.5 million daily trips.
The investigation: The investigation centred on allegations that Leary had violated employees' human rights, tried to land jobs for his friends and engaged in bullying.The resignation: Leary announced his surprise resignation less than a week ago, after seven long years of helming the TTC. As CEO of the city's transit agency he navigated the pandemic and narrowly avoided a strike with the agency's largest union. Leary rejected any assertion that the board's move to put him on leave had anything to do with his exit, instead he said he's looking for new opportunities.The aftermath: Leary is on contract for two more years, with a base salary of $524,560 for 2024, his payout could be more than $1 million. His lawyer approached the board days before his resignation to discuss exit terms, which haven't been released.Thousands entered a city lottery to get a break on rent - their chances of winning are pretty slim
Nearly 12,500 people entered a lottery that only had a one per cent chance of hitting the jackpot - not for money or a new car, but for something much more valuable: an affordable home in Toronto. The odds were ugly, only 135 lottery units were available at the west-end Galleria on the Park, which drew in tens of thousands of mid-income earners looking to secure a spot with reasonable rent in an increasingly unaffordable city. It's one of several new developments with agreements with the city to offer lower-cost rentals via lottery draws. Here's how lucky you need to be.
Wait, the rent is how much? For the lottery units at Galleria, an income ceiling of about $82,000 would mean a one-bedroom that costs $1,589 per month, plus utilities - a steep discount from the $2,493 average rental listing price for a one-bedroom in May.Hunger games: The odds of hitting on an affordable unit at Galleria are actually not bad compared to other developments in the city. With just 37 affordable units available at the Mirvish Village development, the odds were just 0.4 per cent.Context: While renters in the city are keeping their fingers crossed, knocking on wood and praying for some sort of rent relief, a record number of condo units were for sale at the end of May. Experts say the units are too small, often built to cater to developers and investors.Here's how the Conservatives managed to breach Fortress Toronto - and what it might mean for the party
The Conservatives campaigned like they wanted to send a message in Toronto-St. Paul's and across the country. The message was received. The byelection win for Pierre Poilievre and his party means they have the path to a winning strategy: focussing on issues like crime and the capital gains tax. Most importantly, they're already thinking ahead to the next election and if the party will be able to hold onto its newfound gains. Here's how the Conservative strategy led to a political upset Monday evening.
Meanwhile: Justin Trudeau's Liberals are reeling. Though the PM is no closer to stepping down, the loss of what had been steady red territory means some soul searching for the party.Susan Delacourt's take: Poilievre has a new problem on his hands after his party's byelection win - can he change his party's strategy to not only defend their newfound foothold, but to take more?Another angle: Trudeau's political career is over. He can walk away with dignity now or stick around to get creamed by a man he loathes, Andrew MacDougall argues.WHAT ELSE
More than 50 workers will be laid off from the shuttered Ontario Science Centre, their union said.A carjacking task force has made 124 arrests - and nearly half of the suspects were out on bail at the time.Inflation crept up to 2.9 per cent in May, shaving the odds of July rate cut."Nothing is moving." GTA sales of newly built homes plummeted in May.Toronto is gearing up to expand its bike lane network amid growing backlash.She's still busy at 105. What are the secrets and science behind Canada's "super agers?"Melissa Merritt has been granted bail as she awaits a third trial in the death of ex-husband.This 14-year-old wants to know why another 14-year-old would shoot and kill his dad.The number of bird species you live near could dramatically change your mental health. Here's why.Is coffee good or bad for you? It all depends on your DNA, Ontario researchers discover.With all the uncertainty surrounding this year's NBA draft, the Raptors brain trust is still guided by one principle.Don't forget the name of this brilliant play at the Shaw Festival. It deserves to be a modern classic.POV
A Toronto man has been acquitted of his 15-year-old friend's death during a schoolyard attack by an "angry mob of students."

BARRIE: Edwin Ng was a personal support worker at the height of the pandemic, where he contracted COVID-19 amidst a deadly wave at the nursing home where he worked. The virus ravaged his lungs, requiring a double lung transplant to keep him alive. After seven hours of surgery, Ng went home, but now faces a new threat as the lungs he received three years ago are beginning to fail. Still, the 53-year-old, father of three has a relentless optimism that has kept him smiling through physiotherapy.

Document 460 of 500

'Poor living conditions' in Ontario's overcrowded jails top complaints to ombudsman

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poor-living-conditions-ontarios-overcrowded-jails/docview/3072280924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Poor+living+conditions%27+in+Ontario%27s+overcrowded+jails+top+complaints+to+ombudsman&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Concerns about "poor living conditions" in Ontario's overcrowded jails - including rodents - topped complaints to the provincial ombudsman last year.
In his annual report, Paul Dubé said complaints from citizens about government services including child welfare, delays at the Landlord and Tenant Board and supports to developmentally disabled adults and other issues were up 10 per cent to 27,030 in the 12 months ending March 31.
"The systems are struggling," the independent officer of the legislature told a news conference Wednesday. "Urgent attention is needed."
In jails, complaints centred on medical care, overcrowding, solitary confinement and use of force by correctional officers, prompting Dubé and members of his team to visit the Sudbury Jail, Maplehurst Correctional Complex in Milton, the Vanier Centre for Women and the Hamilton-Wentworth Detention Centre.
For example, "poor living conditions and a mouse infestation" were the subject of discussions with management at the Sudbury Jail while overcrowding at the Vanier Centre led to complaints of "no access to services, including showers, due to construction projects," the ombudsman wrote in his 104-page report.
Several shortfalls on medical care in jails were highlighted.
In one case, an inmate transferred to a new jail did not get his regular prescription medication for three weeks until the ombudsman's team got involved.
Dubé would not comment on whether the push for bail reform from Premier Doug Ford and others would worsen overcrowding in jails, but said of inmates, "most of them are awaiting trial and are presumed innocent."
Despite regulations making it unlawful for jails to put inmates known to have "serious mental illness" in segregation, complaints about this happening continue to come in.
"We are making inquiries with facilities and senior ministry officials about these cases, and about how the ministry plans to comply with the regulation."
The problems cited in the report show Ford's Progressive Conservative government has a lot of work to do on improving services and oversight, said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"The premier's disregard for the people of this province shows up everywhere," Schreiner said.
In the 104-page report, Dubé points to a number of disturbing cases where his staff has intervened.
They include a young man who missed four months of payouts because of bureaucratic delays in transferring his file to the Ontario Disability Support Program from the Assistance for Children with Severe Disabilities Program. He was granted $4,500 in retroactive benefits.
When a youth complained about "irregular and insufficient" meals at his group home, government inspectors were sent in to order improvements.
As part of a soon-to-be-completed investigation into adults with developmental disabilities who are inappropriately housed in hospitals due to a lack of support in the community, Dubé's staff is reviewing the case of a 22-year-old man with autism and a history of self-injury.
He had been in hospital for three years, "spending much of that time in restraints." The ombudsman's office flagged his situation to senior bureaucrats and a home was found for him in the community where he has "adjusted well," the report said.
While the Landlord and Tenant Board now has 133 adjudicators, up from 78 a year ago, Dubé noted it is "still grappling" with a backlog of cases as renters and owners in disputes seek justice.
CAPTION: Maplehurst Correctional Complex is a correctional facility located in Milton.

Subject: Prison overcrowding; Prisons

Document 461 of 500

Tories breach Fortress Toronto: Conservative sources not sure if upset can be recreated in a general election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-breach-fortress-toronto/docview/3072196900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+breach+Fortress+Toronto%3A+Conservative+sources+not+sure+if+upset+can+be+recreated+in+a+general+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives began campaigning for the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection with the goal of rattling the foundations of Fortress Toronto: the governing Liberals have held all of the seats in the 416 for years.
They finished having knocked a brick out of the wall: Conservative Don Stewart bested the Liberals' Leslie Church by 590 votes, a result Poilievre described Tuesday as a "shocking upset."
While Stewart's victory raised questions within the Liberal and Conservative camps about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's ability to hang on as his party's leader, for the Conservatives a broader debate is also taking place: did the stars align for one magical night, or was there a galactic implosion birthing a new electoral universe?
The road to a majority government for any party runs through the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, but in recent election campaigns the Conservatives have focused their energies on the "greater" part of that descriptor, rather than the 416.
Winning in Toronto, with its dense and jumbled urban mix of voters, sometimes in a single city block, has made strategic breakthroughs more challenging, especially with the Liberals dominating in every riding in the last three elections and the NDP nipping at their heels. That's not to say the Conservatives weren't trying to win in this campaign.
Stewart was nominated in January, months before the Liberals settled on Church. His campaign manager was Amanda Kimber, a veteran politico who is close to Poilievre adviser Jenni Byrne.
When Kimber showed up Monday night outside Café Landwer, where volunteers were gathered to watch the election results, she was greeted with a spontaneous cheer from dozens of young volunteers spilling onto Spadina Road: the party had not just them, but also candidates from nearby ridings, sitting MPs and others to get out the vote over the last several months.
The party wasn't starting from a position of strength in the riding. It's been held by the Liberals for decades, and there was not much by way of an organized blue machine.
"This a very safe Liberal seat. When the Liberals only won 34 seats (in the 2011 election), this was one of the seats they picked up," Byrne told the Star on Monday night before the results were clear.
But the Tories approached the campaign really wanting to send a message to Canadians that they can win ridings throughout Toronto, Byrne said.
"I don't think the Liberals were ever in a position where they thought they were going to lose this. They are worried about what is the cascading effect in other ridings," she said.
And the Tories are also now contemplating the cascading effect for them. Nomination contests in other 416 and 905 ridings, some already hotly contested, could see an influx of aspiring candidates lured by the fact the 20-point lead for Poilievre in the national polls does appear to be translating into victory.
While the Liberal campaign tried to focus on Poilievre in their campaign literature - just as the Tories used Trudeau's photo on their pamphlets - Byrne said "as people get to know Pierre, they're open and receptive to the messaging and the policies."
The veteran strategist said the Liberals' attacks on Poilievre over matters like abortion, gun control and LGBTQ issues fell flat in the byelection.
"I think it's very tired and it doesn't seem to be working for them," she said.
What worked for Stewart was focusing on some specific issues, several told the Star.
Among them was connecting the Liberals to the spate of auto thefts in the area, through literature and a focus on the "stop the crime" element of Poilievre's core messaging. Economics was also a factor - there are affluent pockets of the riding where voters might be irritated by Liberals' recently announced changes to the capital gains tax system, and receptive to Poilievre's frugality.
A focus on the riding's Jewish voters is understood to have helped as well. Their concerns about rising antisemitism in Toronto, a feeling the Liberals were not dealing with it and what critics characterize as the government's often confusing position on the war between Israel and Hamas were all issues the Tories pulled on to push votes their way.
There, they may have received an assist from Jewish Ally, a third-party campaign effort which targeted Jewish voters in the riding via social media ads.
Longtime conservative strategist Stephen Taylor and former provincial Progressive Conservative candidate Andrew Kirsch developed the campaign in answer to what they saw as a cold response from the Liberal government towards the Jewish community.
"It's democracy, and the way we provide feedback in a democracy is through the vote," Taylor told the Star. "We wanted there to be a political price for the government's inaction and lack of response to the Jewish community's concerns."
Jubilant as they may be, the Conservative jury was very much still out Tuesday on what it all means down the line: the specifics of what came together for them on voting day this time won't replicate themselves in the next election, several Conservatives said.
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Conservatism; Nominations; Jewish life & ethics; Voters; Elections; Political campaigns

Letters: Liberals grapple with stunning Toronto byelection loss and Justin Trudeau's future, June 25 Whatever you want to call it, a name change is the least of its problems: Elliott, June 25

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3072196772/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Liberals+grapple+with+stunning+Toronto+byelection+loss+and+Justin+Trudeau%27s+future%2C+June+25+Whatever+you+want+to+call+it%2C+a+name+change+is+the+least+of+its+problems%3A+Elliott%2C+June+25&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Byelection results are
no surprise
Liberals grapple with stunning Toronto byelection loss and Justin Trudeau's future, June 25
The recent byelection results for Toronto-St. Paul's are telling. Comparing the 2024 byelection to the 2021 general election, one crucial fact stands out: low voter turnout significantly favoured the Conservative candidate, now the incoming federal MP, Don Stewart. Why did 17,316 eligible voters in the Toronto-St. Paul's riding abstain from voting? Why did the Conservative party gain 1,968 new supporters, while the NDP lost 4,992 and the Liberal party lost 10,874? The answer lies in strategy. Conservatives had the most organized get-out-the-vote strategy and their supporters were more motivated. The party effectively utilized social media platforms like X, YouTube and Meta platforms (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp). St. Paul's historically has more progressive voters, but many chose not to vote, which is concerning. Progressives must wake up to the political reality. We cannot take our supporters for granted. If this trend continues and voter turnout remains low across Toronto, the biggest winners in the next federal election will be the Conservatives led by Pierre Poilievre, who is taking nothing for granted. Let's learn from this and ensure higher engagement in future elections.
Walied Khogali Ali, Toronto
Safe seats are a thing of the past
For most Toronto progressives, news of this loss eclipsed the Oilers' loss on Monday evening. There is no longer such a thing as a "safe Liberal seat." Conservative candidate Don Stewart's lawn signs were out first (and they wisely didn't include the squib "Team Poilievre," whose name can be polarizing). Former Liberal MP Carolyn Bennett did not use her considerable influence, wealthy families in places like Rosedale, Forest Hill and Cedarvale didn't like the hike on capital gains tax, and one-issue voters on Israel/Gaza got sweet-talked by the Poilievre Conservatives the same way they did by Stephen Harper. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau reneged on his promise to abolish first-past-the-post. A ranked balloting system would have solidified the fact that St. Paul's remains a riding with mostly socially progressive residents. Did some of those residents stay home on Election Day? You bet they did.
Ron Charach, Toronto
As a resident of Toronto-St. Paul's, I am frustrated and concerned. Our newly elected MP does not represent so many in my riding. Past governments have failed to implement simple fair voting changes to our system, resulting in power shifts which endanger policies and programs on which most Canadians agree. Why can't we have simple co-operation among progressive parties? No merger is necessary. Let's co-operate to win in the 50 ridings in Canada where parties could defeat the Conservatives, simply by making space for the progressive candidate most likely to win. Parties can co-operate to their mutual benefit, or we voters can take this into our own hands.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
The first of many Liberals to go
So much for sunny ways and sunny days under the Liberal Party of Canada! After nine years in which this country went from being an affordable and capably run nation into a test lab for Liberal and NDP policies, people are waking up! At least it appears so in the Liberal stronghold in the heart of Toronto, a seat that they kept for the last 31 years and flipped to the Conservatives in the byelection. This is the decisive moment, a moment of hope for Canadians that this miscreant government, run by prime minister who has imposed heavy burdens on Canadians to satisfy a minority of people, has finally reached its expiration date. It's time for them to go.
Steven Eleftheriadis, North York
Now is the time for a new leader
The results of the federal byelection in Toronto is an indicator that the Trudeau Liberals are sinking. The party must now choose a new leader before they will be on the back benches with their NDP cohorts for many elections to come. The Liberals must either go down with their carbon tax and high interest rates or rise like a phoenix with a new idea of starting the process without hurting Canadians and Canada.
Wayne Robertson, Chatham
After the federal byelection results, one wonders if Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland will take the helm of the Liberal Party, become the next Kim Campbell and leave the Liberals with only two seats like the Progressive Conservatives won in the 1993 election? We are in a similar position with loss of faith in the party leader, but also the party in general.
Mike Walker, Washago, Ont.
Make peace with the name change
Whatever you want to call it, a name change is the least of its problems: Elliott, June 25
Columnist Matt Elliott's article on moving on from the Sankofa name is right on. The name is set, regardless of its popularity or lack thereof. It's what to do now that counts. Many of us with a marketing background might see this as an opportunity. Lemons to lemonade, right? My husband and I were in this business, and in fact ran our own shop for 25 years. We would have told the city that while Sankofa Square is a meaningful name, it's not a marketing name. The square needs a second name to build a personality for the destination, a presence in downtown Toronto, a place people want to go to. It hasn't had the money to do much since COVID. It's grey and barren. A well-thought-out marketing name would create a base on which activities can be built. How can Sankofa be remembered but not pushed in people's faces to cause more disharmony? Make the first big event something that recognizes Sankofa and what it stands for, and then start using a marketing name. Give up fighting over Sankofa and what it means. Use it to shift over to a marketing name that everyone can get excited about.
Nancy Miller, Toronto
I don't agree with columnist Matt Elliott who said Sankofa Square needs more events. When I'm in that part of town, I long for seating, trees and plants and a place to get a good coffee or a gelato to eat in the shade. Couldn't installing these amenities be part of the general refurbishment of Yonge Street, if that's still going to happen?
Julie Beddoes, Toronto

Title: Letters:   Liberals grapple with stunning Toronto byelection loss and Justin Trudeau's future, June 25 Whatever you want to call it, a name change is the least of its problems: Elliott, June 25

Document 463 of 500

Despite outrage, a roof in Don Mills is not the priority

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/despite-outrage-roof-don-mills-is-not-priority/docview/3072196769/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Despite+outrage%2C+a+roof+in+Don+Mills+is+not+the+priority&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing the Ontario Science Centre has distressed many people for many reasons. Some, like me, are emotional, the big ole place a warm memory from childhood, although the shabby fountainless stained concrete building in an isolated car-dependent part of Toronto makes me wince a bit now. If you love something, keep it in shape.
Modernist architecture bros stain the concrete further with their tears, like the Globe's embittered architecture columnist mourning "Toronto's great Brutalist temple of childhood wonder, Raymond Moriyama's hillside wonderland."
Brutalism is ugly, like the gloomy concrete hulk of Robarts Library and the unfixable Manulife Centre, architectural manspreading that nobody loves, especially now as life gets grimmer. Sixty years ago, trendy architects wanted huge monoliths to overawe pointless little humans.
Builders now glue glass boxes to these grim buildings to make them look more inviting. It's even worse. Remember, ubiquitous cheap glass is the concrete of our future, no match for global heating.
The late Moriyama doesn't fare well. In 2023 he wrote to the Star claiming he had used "concrete to last far beyond 100 years." But he was designing grey boxes in Don Mills, not the Pyramids, and apparently used the now-notorious lightweight RAAC semi-concrete in parts of the roof.
In the glassy-eyed 1960s, brand-name Siporex might have seemed perfect for those new airy (non-air conditioned) modernist schools. Now the Star reports that 360 of Ontario's 4,500 schools are being monitored for the same aerated concrete that has left many aging British school buildings empty lest roofs collapse on children's heads.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford closed the Science Centre fast and his critics are suspicious and irate. That this was Ford's dream come true doesn't make him wrong. Ontario Place might well be a better spot for a new science centre, beautiful and accessible. Ford has money on his mind, which critics do not, not in the same way.
Toronto has to prepare for hard times. The science centre roof might not cope with a big snowfall but rainfall is an equal danger, the climate emergency having brought us ruinous rain all this month.
Some angry people have misunderstood the import of the Rimkus engineering report that doomed the buildings. They say it wasn't that bad, not realizing that the weaker parts of the roof are in crucial areas, a steel roof replacement might take five years, and the science centre might now be uninsurable for visitors.
They're worried about the building; the province is also worried about the children inside the building and what might happen even if partial repairs began with construction walled off inside. They say "low risk." Parents want "no risk." That's what makes them parents.
Toronto needs new subway cars on Line 2, the current ones near the end of life just like the roof. Mayor Olivia Chow hopes the feds will offer help though I can't see why it's a federal matter. Prime Minister Trudeau might consider it though.
Journalists think the recent Conservative win in Toronto-St. Paul's matters hugely. But Trudeau isn't worried about ridings, he's worried about the looming U.S. election. If Trump wins, the world is in huge trouble in every sector. I doubt Canadians will think libertarian-minded Conservatives are up to the task of keeping Canada functional in a manic chaotic Trumpy world.
Like the rest of us, Trudeau, Ford and Chow have no idea what will happen next. Will Toronto get subway money? Flood money? Will taxes have to soar? Honestly, a roof in Don Mills is not the priority.
We won't always get what we want but we might get what we need. That's the hope at least.
HEATHER MALLICK IS A COLUMNIST BASED IN TORONTO COVERING CURRENT AFFAIRS. FOLLOW HER
ON X: @HEATHERMALLICK
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed last Friday after engineers found problems in the roof panels.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star

Document 464 of 500

Star wins three Best in Business awards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/star-wins-three-best-business-awards/docview/3072196760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Star+wins+three+Best+in+Business+awards&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Star won three golds - for best scoop, investigative story and podcast - at the Society for Advancing Business Editing and Writing (SABEW) Best in Business Awards on Monday.
Best investigative story went to Sara Mojtehedzadeh and Rachel Mendleson for their "Work Forced" series, which exposed work exploitation schemes and labour trafficking in Ontario. The series previously won a National Newspaper Award for business reporting and the CWA Canada/CAJ Award for Excellence in Labour Reporting.
Richard Warnica won best scoop for his story in November detailing how Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment walked away from the opportunity to bring a WNBA franchise to Toronto.
Ghada Alsharif, Kevin Sexton, Andrea Varsany, Julia De Laurentiis Johnston and Sean Pattendon won for the "Millennial Money" podcast, which explored the financial dilemmas of millennials.
"I'm delighted to see the Star recognized with three golds at this year's Best in Business Awards," said Duncan Hood, the Star's business editor. "It's a strong showing and I'm very proud of the whole team's outsized impact on how business news is covered in Canada."
The Star was also named a finalist for personal finance and investing for David Aston's series on saving for retirement. It included guidance on how to budget for retirement and how much you'll need to retire comfortably.
The Star and the Narwhal won the prestigious Michener Award this month for their coverage of Premier Doug Ford's move to take land away from the Greenbelt, which also earned the publications a National Newspaper Award.
CREDIT: Mark Colley Toronto Star

Document 465 of 500

Rush to close shows lack of respect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rush-close-shows-lack-respect/docview/3072196506/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rush+to+close+shows+lack+of+respect&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When a major landmark closes, time is often provided for the public to have a final look. It may take the form of a closing ceremony, or a set period of time to visit before the doors are locked forever.
People require time to process their feelings and memories about places that were a treasured part of their lives, similar to the period of mourning when a beloved person passes.
Two years ago, my hometown high school closed after a century of serving the community. Toward the end of the school year, an open house was held to allow a final look. Displays covering the school's history were assembled and the cafeteria's legendary chocolate chip cookies were sold. The afternoon allowed alumni to revisit that time of their lives and provided a sense of closure.
The sudden shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre by the provincial government on Friday does not provide that sense of closure. Queen's Park has robbed us of the opportunity to celebrate and mourn an institution that, for over half a century, opened the eyes of children to the wonders of science.
When I heard the news, I rushed over to take a final look. Observing those wandering around, the shock of the decision was still settling in for those aware of it, creating a feeling of numbness. I processed a mixture of nostalgia for childhood visits, anger over the site's decay and heartbreak that a pioneer in the field of interactive museums and an architectural landmark was allowed to expire in such a quiet manner.
When I left shortly before the building closed, the emotions were more evident around me, especially during the final firing up of the hair-raising Van de Graaff generator. I regretted not being able to bring my wife, who was working that afternoon, as she grew up going to the Science Centre regularly for the exhibits and other events, such as jazz concerts.
The closing demonstrates how the current provincial government places more value on enriching developers and supporters than maintaining public spaces that benefit Ontarians in ways beyond economic considerations.
There is the example of the Greenbelt lands that the provincial government intended to remove to allow development, which sparked nearly a year of public outcry before the decision was reversed in October.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt," Premier Doug Ford declared. "It was a mistake to establish a process that moved too fast. This process, it left too much room for some people to benefit over others. It caused people to question our motives."
Last week's shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre will cause further questioning of the provincial government's motivations. The rush to close makes little sense given the engineering reports cited as the reason for the closure do not depict immediate danger and would not affect the entire site.
The Science Centre's proposed move to Ontario Place felt driven by political and financial considerations over practical ones, to provide legitimacy to the controversial redevelopment of what could be excellent public waterfront space into a spa complex few people outside the government appear to desire.
As for the current site, some of which was already slated for residential redevelopment, its future transit ties via the Eglinton Crosstown and Ontario Line subway extension would have boosted future attendance.
Government officials associated with both projects, such as Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, are defending the closure based on safety concerns and protecting children, but the satirical website The Beaverton may be on target when it joked that the province "cannot afford to repair the roof while spending more money than it would cost on partisan ads touting his government's alleged successes."
Leaving the site open for even a few more weeks would have disrupted fewer summer plans for families who enrolled their children in camps, or intended to go together.
But, more importantly, it would have shown a genuine sense of respect toward the public's feelings about the Ontario Science Centre and the proper opportunity to say goodbye, instead of simply raising an angry outcry.
Jamie Bradburn is a Toronto-based writer and historian.
CAPTION: The closing of the Ontario Science Centre shows how the Ford government places more value on enriching developers than maintaining public spaces that benefit Ontarians in ways beyond economic considerations.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star

Document 466 of 500

Trudeau needs to hear that it's time to leave

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-needs-hear-that-time-leave/docview/3072196277/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+needs+to+hear+that+it%27s+time+to+leave&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Justin Trudeau's life were a country music song, now would be the point in the proceedings where he's all sad and lonesome, watching his girl dance with another man.
That's because Pierre Poilievre has just swooped into downtown Toronto, the Big Smoke, Liberal party HQ, and done stole his woman. You can practically hear the pedal steel guitar lick accompanying the prime minister's tears to the floor.
At least, the prime minister should be sad. Because what's happened is bad. Catastrophically bad. If Toronto is now fertile Conservative ground, not much of anything Liberal will be growing come the next general election.
Whether the prime minister is actually sad is another question. Trudeau has never scored highly on humility or self-awareness. It's therefore more likely he's angry. No matter what Trudeau might end up saying in the aftermath of the shocking, razer thin byelection upset to the Conservatives in the safe Liberal riding of Toronto-St. Paul's there is unquestionably a part of him raging about the result.
Of all the people he could lose to, he doesn't want to lose to Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. Not to the man they used to call "Skippy." Not that (expletive) guy.
I imagine a similar thought once flitted through Stephen Harper's mind as Trudeau began pulling away to victory in 2015. But here's the thing: Harper was in the midst of an election. He had no choice but to fight on, even if that meant dragging Rob and Doug Ford up on stage.
Trudeau has another choice. He can leave. He should leave. There is, in theory, a record of accomplishment from the past nine years. Better to tout it now and give someone else the chance of plotting a new course. Because the voters are starting to make themselves heard, even in places where Liberals have seldom heard a discouraging word.
Sure, things could, in theory, turn around. Trump might return to the White House. And Poilievre might get caught French-kissing Alex Jones at a Diagolon rally as a group of Proud Boys livestream it for QAnon. But who's to say these things are going to make people like Justin Trudeau any more?
Trudeau has binned half of his cabinet and ... nothing changed in the polls. He tabled another budget and ... nothing changed. He sent most of his new cabinet to Toronto-St. Paul's to tout the budget and ... the people said "no." What more of a signal does one man need?
This is where a politician's political staff and other trusted advisers need to step in and have a frank conversation. It is the time to say the things that need to be said, not to say the things the boss wants to hear.
Whatever the words, it will need to be a human conversation. The prime minister deserves respect. Life at the top is unforgiving. But it's also a choice. Nobody is forcing him to stay. There is time for another career.
Put differently, just what does the prime minister hope to accomplish by toughing it out until the next election? And how does that stack up to time he could be spending with his children, or pursuing change through a different vehicle other than elected office? The answer appears to be: nothing.
The choice is leave gracefully now, or get smoked later. That's why it's time for those close to Trudeau to pat him warmly on the back and tell him he's done his job. That the Liberal party, despite all appearances, is more than one man, and has more than the past 11 years with him at the top as its history. He rebuilt the party after its decimation in the 2011 election and now it's time for someone to do the same, hopefully ahead of the next election.
There is still time for the Liberals to change their tune, even if time has run out for Justin Trudeau to do the same.
Andrew MacDougall is a director at Trafalgar Strategy, a U.K.-based consultancy. He was a director of communications to prime minister Stephen Harper.
CREDIT: Andrew MacDougall Contributor Andrew MacDougall is a director at Trafalgar Strategy, a U.K.-based consultancy. He was a director of communications to prime minister Stephen Harper.;

Subject: Prime ministers

Location: United Kingdom--UK

People: Harper, Stephen; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 467 of 500

Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives managed to breach Fortress Toronto. Here's how they did it - and what it might mean for the party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-conservatives-managed-breach/docview/3072326530/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+managed+to+breach+Fortress+Toronto.+Here%27s+how+they+did+it+-+and+what+it+might+mean+for+the+party&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives began campaigning for the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection with the goal of rattling the foundations of Fortress Toronto: the governing Liberals have held all of the seats in the 416 for years.
They finished having knocked a brick out of the wall: Conservative Don Stewart bested the Liberals' Leslie Church by 590 votes, a result Poilievre described Tuesday as a "shocking upset."
While Stewart's victory raised questions within the Liberal and Conservative camps about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's ability to hang on as his party's leader, for the Conservatives a broader debate is also taking place: did the stars align for one magical night, or was there a galactic implosion birthing a new electoral universe?
The road to a majority government for any party runs through the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, but in recent election campaigns the Conservatives have focused their energies on the "greater" part of that descriptor, rather than the 416.
Winning in Toronto, with its dense and jumbled urban mix of voters, sometimes in a single city block, has made strategic breakthroughs more challenging, especially with the Liberals dominating in every riding in the last three elections and the NDP nipping at their heels.
That's not to say the Conservatives weren't trying to win in this campaign.
Stewart was nominated in January, months before the Liberals settled on Church. His campaign manager was Amanda Kimber, a veteran politico who also works for Poilievre adviser Jenni Byrne's firm.
When Kimber showed up Monday night outside Cafe Landwer, where volunteers were gathered to watch the election results, she was greeted with a spontaneous cheer from dozens of young volunteers spilling onto Spadina Avenue: the party had not just them, but also candidates from nearby ridings, sitting MPs and others to get out the vote over the last several months.
The party wasn't starting from a position of strength in the riding. It's been held by the Liberals for decades, and there was not much by way of an organized blue machine.
"This a very safe Liberal seat. When the Liberals only won 34 seats (in the 2011 election), this was one of the seats they picked up," Byrne told the Star on Monday night before the results were clear.
But the Tories approached the campaign really wanting to send a message to Canadians that they can win ridings throughout Toronto, Byrne said.
"I don't think the Liberals were ever in a position where they thought they were going to lose this. They are worried about what is the cascading effect in other ridings," she said.
And the Tories are also now contemplating the cascading effect for them.
Nomination contests in other 416 and 905 ridings, some already hotly contested, could see an influx of aspiring candidates lured by the fact the 20-point lead for Poilievre in the national polls does appear to be translating into victory.
While the Liberal campaign tried to focus on Poilievre in their campaign literature - just as the Tories used Trudeau's photo on their pamphlets - Byrne said "as people get to know Pierre, they're open and receptive to the messaging and the policies."
The veteran strategist said the Liberals' attacks on Poilievre over matters like abortion, gun control and LGBTQ issues fell flat in the byelection.
"I think it's very tired and it doesn't seem to be working for them," she said.
What worked for Stewart was focusing on some specific issues, several told the Star.
Among them was connecting the Liberals to the spate of auto thefts in the area, through literature and a focus on the "stop the crime" element of Poilievre's core messaging. Economics was also a factor - there are affluent pockets of the riding where voters might be irritated by Liberals' recently announced changes to the capital gains tax system, and receptive to Poilievre's frugality.
A focus on the riding's Jewish voters is understood to have helped as well. Their concerns about rising antisemitism in Toronto, a feeling the Liberals were not dealing with it, and what its critics characterize as the government's often confusing position on the war between Israel and Hamas were all issues the Tories pulled on to push votes their way.
There, they may have received an assist from Jewish Ally, a third-party campaign effort which targeted Jewish voters in the riding via social media ads.
Longtime conservative strategist Stephen Taylor and former provincial Progressive Conservative candidate Andrew Kirsch developed the campaign in answer to what they saw as a cold response from the Liberal government towards the Jewish community.
"It's democracy, and the way we provide feedback in a democracy is through the vote," Taylor said in an interview with the Star. "We wanted there to be a political price for the government's inaction and lack of response to the Jewish community's concerns."
The Conservatives won more votes in Toronto-St. Paul's than they did in that riding in the 2021 general election, which insiders said could portend well for future voter growth in the city, but which also caught them off guard.
Though they trusted their ability to identify potential voters, their inability to canvass inside apartment buildings left a blind spot that could be one explanation for the votes they weren't expecting.
What also mystified many Conservatives is why the New Democrats didn't do better than the 10.9 per cent of the vote their candidate captured. The NDP holds the riding provincially, and with a high number of renters, Tories expected its share of the vote to be much higher.
Jubilant as they may be, the Conservative jury was very much still out Tuesday on what it all means down the line: the specifics of what came together for them on voting day this time won't replicate themselves in the next election, several Conservatives said, meaning they also may not be able to hold on to Toronto-St. Paul's.
On the other hand, they might.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in the House of Commons on June 12, 2024.

Document 468 of 500

How Scotland started to kick its alcohol problem - and what Ontario could learn from it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-scotland-started-kick-alcohol-problem-what/docview/3072326474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Scotland+started+to+kick+its+alcohol+problem+-+and+what+Ontario+could+learn+from+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: GLASGOW and EDINBURGH, Scotland - One frosty winter morning, Tom Bennett awoke on the cold ground with a bare foot frozen in the snow.
The night before, the 33-year-old man had been too drunk to tie his shoelaces and one of his boots had fallen off. He was homeless and he knew that alcohol was killing him. But he was too sick to care.
"It was just a horrible place to be," said Bennett from his family home near Glasgow.
"I lost multiple jobs, multiple relationships. I lost all my dignity, self-respect and self-esteem. I nearly died on many occasions."
Today, Bennett, 47, is working to help others emerge from the same kind of darkness at a charity that helps those in the throes of addiction to heal. His journey reflects both alcoholism's toll and Scotland's transformative efforts to curb tragedy.
As Ontario moves to make alcohol more affordable and accessible, Scotland's policies are advancing in the opposite direction.
Ontario officials say they are fulfilling a 2018 election promise to increase "choice and convenience for shoppers and support Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry."
But Scotland has cut alcohol-related hospital admissions by 40 per cent and deaths by almost half. While in Ontario, alcohol-related admissions have risen by a third and deaths by almost half, according to the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction.
While Scottish officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
As in many countries, alcohol is central in Canada's social and economic life. Over three quarters of Canadians aged 15 and older report drinking alcohol, according to Statistics Canada. And booze sales contribute $26 billion annually to the national economy.
But new research is showing that drinking alcohol is more dangerous than previously thought. And alcohol's collective harms are vast because the number of people who drink is so high.
While Scottish officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, health experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
Scotland has an age-old drinking problem. For years, the country suffered more alcohol-related deaths - from conditions such as liver disease - than much of the rest of Europe. In 2003, Scots were dying from drink at a rate three times higher than the rest of Britons.
Bennett grew up deep in this tragic alcohol culture. His affluent family viewed alcohol - as many Scots did - as a fundamental part of Scottish life.
In Bennett's community, drinks flowed at the many festivals that hail ancient Celtic traditions. People drank to celebrate, to mourn, to relax and to unwind after work. Bennett enjoyed "wee nips" with his parents from the age of 10 and was buying liquor with friends from the age of 15.
"Alcohol was everywhere," Bennett said with emotion. "And cheap."
Some hard drinks cost less than soft drinks at the local store, he recalled.
"Why buy pop, when you could get 'off your face' for the same money on cheap booze?"
In those days, if you wanted to escape from the despair of drink, you were often on your own.
Bennett remembers a lonely night in jail, shaking violently in severe withdrawal, desperate for a change.
He was ignored. There was no doctor. He was released back to the frigid streets the following day.
"I was not somebody who was having two bottles of wine. ... I was going through seven or eight litres of wine a day ... and two or three bottles of spirits," he recalled as two of his three daughters chatted in the next room.
"I had nowhere to go. I didn't know what to do."
Six months later, Bennett found the remarkable strength to recover from his 15-year dependence with the support of his family and a residential program.
He says he feels lucky to be alive.
"I have known so many people who weren't so lucky .... and have gone on to die," he said.
Today fewer Scots are dying because the influences that led to Bennett's misery are being transformed by Scottish government policy.
Scotland is a global pioneer in alcohol policy. It won the inaugural European Award for Reducing Alcohol Harm in 2016 and has been recognized by the United Nations for its initiatives tackling alcohol's affordability, availability and attractiveness.
It did not happen easily.
When the Scottish government started its drive to reduce alcohol's downfalls, in the early 2000s, it looked to Canada for advice.
Among its consultants was Tim Stockwell, a professor of psychology at the University of Victoria and director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research. Stockwell and his team had studied British Columbia and Saskatchewan alcohol price increases between 2002 and 2009 and found that a 10 per cent rise in average minimum prices reduced alcohol-related hospital admissions by 18 per cent two years later.
Scottish officials asked Stockwell about Canadian alcohol pricing approaches.
"I told them that we do it terribly," he said in a video interview.
Canada's alcohol pricing policy has stemmed less from public health concerns and more from desires to protect local producers and increase government revenue, Stockwell said.
Even so, the evidence Stockwell had studied about the benefits of increased prices was convincing. So Stockwell helped develop the Scottish policy and assisted over eight years to put it into action. It took two years to pass the legislation through the Scottish Parliament and six years to fight legal challenges by the alcohol industry in Scottish, European and United Kingdom courts.
The key part of Scotland's landmark policy was aimed at reducing drinking by introducing minimum unit prices to make drinking more expensive. Raising minimum prices is the most effective way of reducing alcohol's harms, Stockwell and other experts say. High prices cut the consumption of heavy drinkers - who are often partial to dirt-cheap booze - and young people, who can only drink what's low-cost.
At the beginning of 2018, the price of a beer in Scotland was about $1.00, according to public health data - the target Premier Doug Ford promised in his 'buck-a-beer' election slogan that year.
But a month before Doug Ford won a majority in Ontario, Scotland enacted a minimum price of 50 pence (C$0.87) for a unit of pure alcohol (10 mL) that meant a minimum of about $1.75 Canadian for a can of beer, $8.75 for a bottle of wine and $24.50 for a bottle of spirits, according to Dr. Peter Rice, one of the founders of the Scottish Health Action on Alcohol Problems, a medical think tank that has campaigned for change since 2006.
The new policy increased the prices of almost half the liquor on sale and led to a 15 per cent reduction in alcohol-related deaths in just three years. Last April, the Scottish Parliament unanimously voted to renew the policy and approved an increase to 65 pence per unit ($1.14).
"The pandemic hit us hard," Rice said in a phone interview. But with minimum unit pricing "we have still been able to stem the tide."
Alongside its pricing strategy, Scotland reduced the legal limit of alcohol for drivers to 50 mg per 100 mL of blood from 80 mg - the level in Ontario. This change was bolstered by a forceful media campaign that increased public opposition to drinking and driving. "It's not one for the road," a campaign slogan ran. "It's none for the road."
Scotland was also the first country to introduce "alcohol brief interventions." This involves training medical professionals to have short, structured conversations with people who abuse alcohol about their habits and how to access help.
Scotland still tolerates wide alcohol availability in supermarkets and small stores, something that flies in the face of what experts say is second only to pricing on the list of most important ways to reduce alcohol harms: reducing access.
Alex Collop sees first-hand the effects of easy access on low-income families in his work for Circle, a charity that supports families affected by alcohol and drugs.
Alcohol addiction leads to lower incomes, housing insecurity, domestic abuse and violence, Collop said from Circle's cosy meeting room in the heart of Pilton, one of Edinburgh's least-privileged communities.
"It is easier to buy alcohol than healthy food here," Collop said, noting that the stores beside a nearby pub called the Ferry Boat include a gambling house, a pizza parlour and a liquor store.
In his role as an alcohol counselling manager, Bennett also advocates for making alcohol tougher to get. He found it treacherous to go shopping when he came out of a residential rehab and was trying to stay sober.
"I was going to the supermarket for essential domestic items like toilet paper or washing-up liquid. And on the other side of the aisle.... hundreds and hundreds of bottles of whiskey, vodka, gin and everything else under the sun," he said in a video posted online.
Although alcohol is readily available in Scotland, high minimum unit prices prevent retailers from competing for customers with bargains or multi-buy promotions, something that experts worry will transpire in Ontario.
Timothy Naimi, a professor at the University of Victoria and the current director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research, gives Ontario a failing grade on its plan to make alcohol more available.
"Ontario was already doing poorly" in not protecting the public from alcohol harms, Naimi said. But the government's planned tripling of retail outlets will "decimate its performance."
British Columbia research, led by Stockwell, showed that every 20 per cent increase in the number of private retail stores was associated with a three per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This is mainly because increased retail competition drives prices down, Stockwell said. He added that large chains that can buy cheaply in bulk also often use alcohol as a loss leader to bring in customers who will then pay more for food and other higher-margin items.
"It's like a double public health whammy - cheap alcohol and expensive fruit and veg."
A recent study in Finland estimated that allowing alcohol sales in grocery stores, without minimum prices, would lead to a 22 per cent increase in hospital admissions and a nine per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This could play out similarly in Ontario, where current minimum legal price limits are two and half times lower than levels that would curb consumption, according to University of Victoria research.
"It might not sound like a big step," Rice said, "but our experience is that moving alcohol into the grocery market will have many repercussions."
Scottish and Canadian experts questioned Ontario's decision to expand alcohol convenience especially while its hospitals and emergency rooms are unable to meet current demand. Ontario already has 320,000 alcohol-related hospital visits annually - a number bound to rise vastly with more retail access, according to the Canadian Alcohol Policy Evaluation project.
Leslie Buckley, chief of the addictions division at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, told the Star that she hopes Ontario will introduce alcohol brief interventions, like those in Scotland.
Ontario's direction "is also a sign that we need more health promotion around all substances, talking about the harms and making it clearer for people," Buckley said.
And as Ontario pursues its current path, Scotland's success mitigating tragedies is being recognized even in some of its oldest institutions of drink.
On a drizzly Tuesday afternoon, the Ferry Boat pub in Pilton was bustling. Four old-timers clutched pints around a wobbly table as they hooted with laughter. They said they have been meeting at the Ferry Boat almost every afternoon since the 1960s.
"We used to cut school to drink when we were nine years old," said Tommy, a retired fisherman.
"It's the working man's pastime," said David, a retired chef. "We would come in here after a shift and stay all night. Sometimes I slept here."
Even so, the regulars are not surprised to hear that fewer of their countrymen are dying of drink.
"It is too expensive now," said Alistair, 68, a retired painter. "Now I can only afford eight beers a night and I usually make it home."
