Document 469 of 500

Justin Trudeau's political career is over. He can walk away with dignity now or stick around to get creamed by a man he loathes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-political-career-is-over-he-can/docview/3072326104/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+political+career+is+over.+He+can+walk+away+with+dignity+now+or+stick+around+to+get+creamed+by+a+man+he+loathes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Justin Trudeau's life were a country music song, now would be the point in the proceedings where he's all sad and lonesome, watching his girl dance with another man.
That's because Pierre Poilievre - Donald Trump incarnate, according to all right-thinking Liberals - has just swooped into downtown Toronto, the Big Smoke, Liberal party HQ, and done stole his woman. You can practically hear the pedal steel guitar lick accompanying the prime minister's tears to the floor.
At least, the prime minister should be sad. Because what's happened is bad. Catastrophically bad. Toronto Maple Leafs in spring bad. If Toronto is now fertile Conservative ground, not much of anything Liberal will be growing come the next general election.
Whether the prime minister is actually sad is another question. Trudeau has never scored highly on humility or self-awareness. It's therefore more likely he's angry. No matter what Trudeau might end up saying in the aftermath of the shocking, razer thin byelection upset to the Conservatives in the safe Liberal riding of Toronto-St. Paul's there is unquestionably a part of him raging about the result.
Of all the people he could lose to, he doesn't want to lose to Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. Not to the man they used to call "Skippy." Not that (expletive) guy.
I imagine a similar thought once flitted through Stephen Harper's mind as Trudeau began pulling away to victory in 2015. But here's the thing: Harper was in the midst of an election. He had no choice but to fight on, even if that meant dragging Rob and Doug Ford up on stage.
Trudeau has another choice. He can leave. He should leave. There is, in theory, a record of accomplishment from the past nine years. Better to tout it now and give someone else the chance of plotting a new course. Because the voters are starting to make themselves heard, even in places where Liberals have seldom heard a discouraging word. Like downtown Toranna.
Sure, things could, in theory, turn around. Trump might return to the White House. And Poilievre might get caught French-kissing Alex Jones at a Diagolon rally as a group of Proud Boys livestream it for QAnon. But who's to say these things are going to make people like Justin Trudeau any more? What would any of these things do to help alleviate the problems Canadians are facing?
Trudeau has binned half of his cabinet and ... nothing changed in the polls. He tabled another budget and ... nothing changed. He sent most of his new cabinet to Toronto-St. Paul's to tout the budget and ... the people said "no." What more of a signal does one man need?
This is where a politician's political staff and other trusted advisers need to step in and have a frank conversation. It is the time to say the things that need to be said, not to say the things the boss wants to hear. And what should they say?
Whatever the words, it will need to be a human conversation. The prime minister has had a tough few years, whether in government or on the home front. He deserves respect. Life at the top is brutal. It's unforgiving. But it's also a choice. Nobody is forcing him to stay. Trudeau is still a young man. He has three beautiful kids and much to look forward to. There is time for another career.
Put differently, just what does the prime minister hope to accomplish by toughing it out until the next election? And how does that stack up to time he could be spending with his children, or pursuing change through a different vehicle other than elected office? The answer appears to be: nothing. No one is putting themselves through the abuse endemic in modern political life in order to (checks notes) change the capital gains regime.
The choice is leave gracefully now, or get smoked later. That's why it's time for those close to Trudeau to pat him warmly on the back and tell him he's played his part. He's done his job. That the Liberal party, despite all appearances, is more than one man, and has more than the past 11 years with him at the top as its history. He rebuilt the party after its decimation in the 2011 election and now it's time for someone to do the same, hopefully ahead of the next election.
There is still time for the Liberals to change their tune, even if time has run out for Justin Trudeau to do the same.
CAPTION: Liberals react to a stunning byelection loss to the Conservatives in Toronto-St. Paul's. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said in Vancouver that he has much more work to do to deliver progress, while Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland threw cold water on speculation that Trudeau could resign. (June 25, 2024 / The Canadian Press)Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau, right, and Liberal candidate for Toronto-St. Paul's Leslie Church, left, greet supporters at a campaign volunteer event in Toronto on May 30.
CREDIT: Andrew MacDougall Contributor

Toronto Star wins three golds at Best in Business Awards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-star-wins-three-golds-at-best-business/docview/3072326073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Star+wins+three+golds+at+Best+in+Business+Awards&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Star won three golds - for best scoop, investigative story and podcast - at the Society for Advancing Business Editing and Writing (SABEW) Best in Business Awards on Monday.
Best investigative story went to Sara Mojtehedzadeh and Rachel Mendleson for their "Work Forced" series, which exposed work exploitation schemes and labour trafficking in Ontario. The series previously won a National Newspaper Award for business reporting and the CWA Canada/CAJ Award for Excellence in Labour Reporting.
Richard Warnica won best scoop for his story in November detailing how Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment walked away from the opportunity to bring a WNBA franchise to Toronto.
Ghada Alsharif, Kevin Sexton, Andrea Varsany, Julia De Laurentiis Johnston and Sean Pattendon won for the "Millennial Money" podcast, which explored the financial dilemmas of millennials.
"I'm delighted to see the Star recognized with three golds at this year's Best in Business Awards," said Duncan Hood, the Star's business editor. "It's a strong showing and I'm very proud of the whole team's outsized impact on how business news is covered in Canada."
The Star was also named a finalist for personal finance and investing for David Aston's series on saving for retirement. It included guidance on how to budget for retirement and how much you'll need to retire comfortably.
The Star and The Narwhal won the prestigious Michener Award earlier this month for their coverage of Premier Doug Ford's move to take land away from the Greenbelt, coverage that also won the publications a National Newspaper Award (NNA).
The Star also took home an NNA in the business category for Sara Mojtehedzadeh and Rachel Mendleson's reporting on "Work Forced," the same investigative series that garnered them a SABEW. Star photographer Steve Russell won yet another NNA for his photography and photojournalism on a retired couple in their 80s.
CAPTION: From top to bottom, left to right: Rachel Mendleson, Ghada Alsharif, Richard Warnica, Andrea Varsany, David Aston, Kevin Sexton, Sara Mojtehedzadeh, Sean Pattendon, Julia De Laurentiis Johnston

Architecture firm that designed Ontario Science Centre offers free repair services to keep it open

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/architecture-firm-that-designed-ontario-science/docview/3072196784/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Architecture+firm+that+designed+Ontario+Science+Centre+offers+free+repair+services+to+keep+it+open&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The firm of the architect who designed the Ontario Science Centre expressed dismay at the Ontario government's decision to "abruptly, rashly, and permanently" close the Ontario Science Centre (OSC) and is offering free services to repair the building.
In a surprise announcement on Friday, the Ministry of Infrastructure said the iconic 1969 Don Mills building designed by the late Raymond Moriyama will no longer be structurally sound within a few months and announced its closure citing a report by Rimkus Consulting Group which said the building's roof was at risk of collapsing in heavy rain or snow.
The Doug Ford government has blamed bad concrete for the Ontario Science Centre's decay but in a letter to the Star last year, Moriyama said, "As architects we were instructed to use concrete to last far beyond 100 years. We guaranteed that with proper maintenance the life of this project will last far beyond 250 years."
In a released statement, Moriyama Teshima Architects (MTA) called the institution "a critical part of the province's identity - a place of wonder and discovery for Ontarians and visitors alike," and said that the Rimkus report "makes it clear that closing the OSC is not a necessity."
"Repairs are needed, but on a manageable scale and with potentially minimal impact on the public experience of the building," they said, offering the government pro-bono architectural services for the roof repair.
MTA also encouraged the structural and building science community to similarly offer free services to accomplish the recommended repairs immediately.
Members of the public have also offered to donate to help fix the building.
The province did not respond to The Star's questions earlier this week about whether it would accept funding from the public to repair the Science Centre.
But Toronto mayor Olivia Chow was open to the idea.
"It's great to see people raising their hands to provide donations to help provide services," Chow told the Star on Monday. "It's not uncommon for a place like this to have an endowment fund, like, remember The ROM had backing from Michael Lee Chin and the Weston family."
"The offer of our services, combined with funding that has been generously offered by members of the community, should leave zero barriers for the province to reverse this decision and pursue a path that will keep this vital resource safely operating for years to come," MTA said.
With files from Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre stands fenced in and closed on June 24, 2024.

Conservatives pull off an upset in Toronto-St. Paul's + a teenage boy charged in an Etobicoke shooting

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, June 25 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the Conservative win in Toronto-St. Paul's, a teenage boy charged in an Etobicoke shooting and the continued downfall of The One.
DON'T MISSConservatives scored an upset win in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection
Is this the beginning of the end for Justin Trudeau's Liberals? The Conservatives scored a narrow victory in the hotly contested Toronto-St. Paul's byelection Monday, flipping a Liberal stronghold blue. Poll results had shown Liberal candidate Leslie Church steadily in the lead before suddenly flipping for Conservative Don Stewart to take the lead. The byelection was seen as a bellwether for the Liberal party. Win, and it shows the Grits still have loyalty among their voters in one of their safest of seats. Lose, and the whole of the GTA could be in play for the Tories. Here's how a dramatic election night turned sour for the Liberals.
How close? Very. Stewart won with a margin of 1.6 per cent, separated by just 590 votes. Stewart's hide-and-seek strategy, dodging media interview requests and debates, worked in his favour.Bad news for: Trudeau and his Liberals. Even before the polls closed and the election call came, a new Angus Reid survey found Canadian discontent with the PM and his party runs deep. That bore out in the byelection.Good news for: Pierre Poilievre, of course. His party just won a coveted Liberal seat and are eyeing nearby ridings, like Eglinton-Lawrence, home of MP Marco Mendicino. A 14-year-old boy has been charged with murder after two men were fatally shot outside an Etobicoke school
A teenage boy will face charges of first-degree murder for a mass shooting that left two men dead outside North Albion Collegiate Institute in Etobicoke. The 14-year-old, who cannot be named under the Youth Justice Criminal Act, was seen fleeing from the scene in a vehicle that had allegedly been stolen. He is one of at least two suspects behind the June 2 shooting. As yet another young person is charged for a violent crime, one advocate told the Star that he is worried about suspects getting younger and younger, and what that might mean about the state of our kids. Here's what happens next.
What we know: Following a soccer game that Sunday evening, as a group of 15 to 20 men gathered to play dominoes in the parking lot of an Etobicoke school. A dark pickup pulled up and two men shot more than 50 bullets at the group, hitting five of them.Seymour Gibbs, 46: At a vigil held for the victims, Gibbs' mother-in-law collapsed to her knees, overcome with grief. "I cannot bring him back," she said. Gibbs died days after the shooting, succumbing to his injuries in hospital.Delroy "George" Parkes, 61: As he lay dying, George's wife, Heather Parkes, could hear on the phone that her husband was saying he wasn't going to make it. His daughter Jaidyn, 23, said her "whole world was gone" when she found out her father, a pillar of the community known as "Uncle George," had been killed. The value of The One is now about half its asking price. What happens if no one wants to buy it?
Four months after the 85-storey luxury development went into receivership, the asking price for the building has kept buyers away, industry insiders tell the Star's Ana Pereira. The One's current market value sits at $600 million - half the cost of the $1.2 billion bid set by its senior secured lenders, who won't go below that asking price. For the more than 300 buyers who invested in the luxury condos, their contracts remain in limbo. Here's how one of Toronto's biggest (and tallest) condo developments has fallen short.
Context: Nestled in scaffolding at the intersection of Yonge and Bloor, The One was meant to be a future staple of Toronto's skyline. In October, the development went into receivership - overdue and over-budget, with its two co-owners locked in a messy, public fight.The man behind The One: Sam Mizrahi, one of the developers behind the project, has been over his head since the beginning. Court documents and interviews with employees, both former and current, described a man that has overseen contracting problems, budget failures and general mismanagement.WHAT ELSE

The simple science and complex politics of the crumbling Ontario Science Centre
CLOSE-UP
SUNRISE: The curse continues. With a 2-1 loss against the Florida Panthers in Game 7, the Edmonton Oilers failed to break Canada's 31-year Cup-less streak. The Panthers, instead, made history, winning their franchise's first-ever Stanley Cup. "We fought an uphill climb for months and months and months," superstar Connor McDavid said. "Just ... sucks." McDavid's consolation prize? The coveted Conn Smythe trophy, the first awarded to a player on the losing team since 2003.

Title: Conservatives pull off an upset in Toronto-St. Paul's + a teenage boy charged in an Etobicoke shooting

Document 473 of 500

I never thought I would be this angry at the premier: Readers react to the Ontario Science Centre closure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-never-thought-would-be-this-angry-at-premier/docview/3071991778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+never+thought+I+would+be+this+angry+at+the+premier%3A+Readers+react+to+the+Ontario+Science+Centre+closure&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Questions about the Ontario Science Centre
The Science Centre is the latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect: Editorial, June 21
It's ironic that CP24's announcement of the permanent closure of the Ontario Science Centre was followed immediately by Premier Doug Ford's campaign ad proclaiming he and his Conservative party listen to "the people." Exactly which people?
Julia Bowkun, Toronto
I would like to see a second opinion of the state of the roof problems. One that is not chosen by the province, or anyone associated with the premier. That would be fair, don't you think?
Sandra Farrell, Newcastle, Ont.
As a structural engineer, if I inspected a building and found that portions of it were subject to imminent collapse, I would demand the building be evacuated immediately to avoid another Algoma Centre Mall situation. Did I miss something about the Ontario Science Centre roof? When was the engineering report delivered to the government?
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
Who are these engineers? Who hired them? Why did they appear so late in this whole fiasco? What does the province have planned for the property?
Carolynne Canham, St. Catharines
I have heard that the snow this winter was a contributing factor. What snow? Could they, at least, have maintained it well enough until their new one was completed? Could they not have given us time to enjoy this beloved place and give it a proper farewell?
Lynda Hurley, Toronto
Let me get this straight. The new Ontario Line subway will run from a non-existent Ontario Place to a shuttered Ontario Science Centre. Maybe the new line should be called "The No Ontario Subway Line." Just a thought.
Joel Hertz, Toronto
With the Science Centre moving down to Ontario Place, why not build the spa on the property vacated by the Science Centre. Now the Science Centre won't have to be diminished in size, and the spa will have a beautiful landscape.
Glenn Taylor, Innisfil, Ont.
I am devastated at the sudden closing of the beloved Ontario Science Centre. I never thought I would be this dismayed and angry with the premier and his government. They have done nothing but destroy Toronto and Ontario.
Kate Mitchell, Toronto
Let's enjoy the new location
As a young, non-driving mother, and then a grandmother living to the west of Toronto, I found it too hard to travel to Ontario Science Centre and take the children because it was too complicated with public transportation. Since Ontario Place is so central and soon-to-be a hub for transit, it will be available to many more families. So, I am for the relocation. It is the ONTARIO Science Centre for all after all.
Alayne Bailey, Oakville
An island bridge won't serve everyone
The ferries are failing. It's time to build a pedestrian bridge to the Toronto Islands, June 20
The suggestion of a pedestrian-cycling bridge connecting the foot of Cherry Street to Ward's Island ignores the impact on Ward's Island. I can just hear the Islanders screaming "NIMBY!" (not in my backyard). Is it not feasible, and cheaper, to utilize the Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport's pedestrian tunnel connecting Toronto's mainland to the island terminal to provide pedestrian-cycling access?
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
There is no room on Ward's Island for a bridge. It would likely require tearing down homes or ripping out the small forest area on the south end. And if such bridge did happen, how would everyone get to Centre Island and Hanlan's Point safely? There is one small road. There are many improvements that could be made to the current ferry system. Building a bridge is not one of them.
Keith Perrott, Toronto
Thank you for the column on how the public needs better access to Toronto Islands. While the suggested bridge may be one solution, it has problems. There may be a better alternative, such as using the tunnel (an underground bridge) that connects the mainland to the Island Airport at Bathurst Street. This tunnel will be publicly accessible if the Island Airport agreement is not renewed in 2033.
Max Moore, Toronto
Elections cost. Why call them early?
Opposition parties say they're ready if Doug Ford calls a snap election, June 4
Early elections benefit the party in power, not the public. Considering this, candidates (including the premier himself) should be made to raise money to cover the cost of the election in their riding. How many early elections would we have then?
David Williams, Barrie
Consider average EV cost
Automakers concerned about slow demand, June 20
To suggest that the average price of an electric car is a factor in affordability is like saying the average price of a Starbucks determines whether people can afford coffee. There are many high-end electric cars dragging up the average price, but it's the price of the most economical electric cars which should determine whether a person can afford to buy one. There are many EVs that are even cheaper than gasoline-powered cars once one factors in the cost fuel.
Rob Strang, Orangeville, Ont.
Policing morality goes back in history
Homophobic policing is not ancient history, June 18
Don't the police have anything better to do than enforce morality or investigate sexual activities? This current practice goes back far in history. I have long been of the impression that sex in public is illegal, hence entrapment was once a method to discourage this vice. I have memories of happy park cruising in the days of my youth and the emergence of gay life in the early 1970s. The era is well documented. Contributor Marcus McCann's book "Park Cruising" tells of the struggle for the right to privacy and how provincial politicians were once alarmed at the growth of the emerging Toronto gay community and tried to stifle this freedom with raids and other factors. As well, Toronto activist paper "The Body Politic" reported the fight for the freedom we have today, and activist Gerald Hannon's book "Immoral, Indecent, and Scurrilous: The Making of an Unrepentant Sex Radical" has many details about how gay men have been criminalized.
Peter Iveson, Toronto
Time to cut Vladdy from the Blue Jays
Vladimir Guerrero Jr. and the Blue Jays take another haircut against the Red Sox, June 19
It is time to cut bait on Vladimir Guerrero Jr. and trade him for pitching and/or a reliable bat. Unlike his father, Guerrero Jr. cannot and will not master the fine art of swinging at pitches well out of the strike zone. It is tiresome to see him up with runners in scoring position coming out of his shoes seeking the elusive and glorious dinger when a base hit would be fine. The Blue Jays can always find someone else to douse a Jay player of the game with ice water even though this act has gotten tired. Jays can ill afford to resign him.
David Maki, Toronto
CAPTION: Workers erect gates and fences around the Ontario Science Centre on Friday after the province abruptly announced its closure.

Document 474 of 500

Tight race as votes begin to roll in: Counting ballots longer process than usual due to record-breaking 84 candidates vying for post

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tight-race-as-votes-begin-roll/docview/3071774963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tight+race+as+votes+begin+to+roll+in%3A+Counting+ballots+longer+process+than+usual+due+to+record-breaking+84+candidates+vying+for+post&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tight race was on in a key federal byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's, billed as a harbinger of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's political future, as early results began to pour in.
By print deadline, The Liberal candidate Leslie Church had a slightly growing lead over Conservative Don Stewart late Monday night.
Liberals gathered at a St. Clair West bar while Conservatives gathered further to the west of the riding on Spadina, with all eyes first on the Stanley Cup final game, before turning to a frustratingly slow Elections Canada livestream of ballot counts, poll-by-poll.
At publication time, Church had 3,540 votes, while Stewart, her toughest and nearest competitor on a historically long ballot of 84 candidates, garnered 3,046 votes. The NDP's Amrit Parhar had 1,051, while the Greens' Christian Cullis had 258. That was with just 65 of 192 polling stations counted.
The call of who wins, traditionally announced by broadcasters, had not yet been made by 11:32 p.m. with Elections Canada admitting counting was going slowly due to the long ballot.
Senior Liberal and Conservative operatives had predicted a Liberal win during Monday's midday voting lull, with hours left to go before the after-work voting rush and pre-Stanley Cup game crunch.
After polls closed, before official results were reported, Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne set aside any expectations for a Conservative win, saying the party would not win it.
But just as it had during the campaign, the Conservative party viewed a drastic drop in Liberal support as a win of sorts for them - a sign that the Conservatives could make big gains in other swing races in the GTA. As the road to a parliamentary majority runs through big swaths of suburban and ex-urban ridings like those in the 905 region, it would rally Conservative troops and strike fear in the Liberal ranks.
At Cafe Landwer on Spadina Road in Forest Hill Village, excited Conservative supporters gathered in anticipation of their best showing in Toronto in years.
Stewart showed up at 11:20 p.m. to thank volunteers and said when the final results are in, voters will have sent a message that "the country is waiting for change. The country has been waiting for change. We're ready."
"There's a message that our voters would like to send to Mr. Trudeau, and that's one of change."
Byrne, Pierre Poilievre's most senior adviser, said the results send a strong signal for a general election expected next year.
"It's fantastic. We are thrilled with the campaign we have that we've run. Don Stewart is a fantastic candidate. Our local campaign led by Amanda Kimber has been fantastic," said Byrne.
Mindful Toronto-St. Paul's is one of the safest Liberal seats in Canada, she said it proves the Conservatives can compete anywhere in Ontario now.
Byrne said the Tories feel they can win seats like neighbouring Eglinton-Lawrence next year.
"If I were Marco (Mendocino, the Liberal MP there) I'd be worried," she said. "I don't think the Liberals were ever in a position where they thought they were going to lose this."
Deputy Conservative leader Melissa Lantsman, who represents Thornhill, said the reception at the doors has been encouraging.
"This is a strong Liberal riding. They've held it for a long time," said Lantsman.
"But we've had a strong strong local campaign by an engaged team and a great candidate who put the work in and knocked on doors and is really well-liked by the community that he lives in," she said.
Turnout was everything. The parties had to pull out the supporters they had identified to cast ballots on a sunny summer workday, before many would turn their attention to hockey parties and pre-game celebrations.
Elections Canada said all polls opened on time and no problems were reported at polling places early in the day.
CAPTION: Election signs sit in the Toronto-St. Paul's area Monday. Liberal candidate Leslie Church had a slightly growing lead over Conservative Don Stewart, early results indicated. For the latest results, visit thestar.com.Three of the 84 candidates in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection, from left, Liberal Leslie Church, Conservative Don Stewart and New Democrat Amrit Parhar.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto StarJustin SmirliesThe Canadian Press file photo/X
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 475 of 500

Freeland vows to fight China on EVs: Ottawa to launch consultations ahead of response to Beijing's plan to overproduce vehicles

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/freeland-vows-fight-china-on-evs/docview/3071774954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Freeland+vows+to+fight+China+on+EVs%3A+Ottawa+to+launch+consultations+ahead+of+response+to+Beijing%27s+plan+to+overproduce+vehicles&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland alleged Monday that China is threatening Canadian workers by unfairly cranking out cheap electric vehicles, and vowed that the Liberal government will act to defend Canada's nascent - and heavily subsidized - EV sector.
Speaking at a factory in Vaughan on Monday, Freeland said the government will launch 30 days of consultations on July 2 before deciding how to respond to China's "intentional, state-directed policy" of overproducing EVs. She accused Beijing of "taking advantage of the global economic system" by using "very problematic" standards to produce cheap EVs to dump on the world market and undermine Canada's potential to compete in the growing global clean car industry.
The measures under consideration include a special measure to slap a surtax on Chinese-made EVs, changes to which cars are eligible for the $5,000 federal EV purchase rebates, and "broader" restrictions on investment in Canada, Freeland said.
The Chinese embassy did not respond to a request for comment.
"We are going to protect you and defend you from unfair competition, and I want you to know that," Freeland, who is also Canada's finance minister, told a room of workers Monday morning.
While Freeland declined to say whether Canada plans to join U.S. President Joe Biden's new tariff wall against Chinese EVs, she said "nothing is ruled out and all possible tools are on the table" - including higher tariffs.
"We certainly see the need to be very strong in our defence of Canada and the national economic interest," she said.
The United States announced in May it would crank up its own tariffs on Chinese EVs from 25 per cent to 100 per cent. The European Union, citing similar concerns, later announced the bloc would raise its own tariffs on made-in-China EVs to 38 per cent.
All that fuelled expectations that Canada would follow suit, with International Trade Minister Mary Ng repeatedly stating the Liberal government was seriously looking at the question and intended to take action to protect Canada's fledgling EV sector.
A senior government official, speaking on condition they aren't named, told the Star on Monday it is "highly likely" Canada will impose new tariffs on Chinese EVs, and that the "inclination is to match" the American tariffs. But the official said the government will try to calculate during the consultations how much of a cost advantage China gets from practices deemed unfair, and to set Ottawa's response accordingly.
The EV sector in Canada is getting a huge amount of government support, which has helped attract companies like Honda, Stellantis and Northvolt to promise to set up factories for batteries and vehicles in Ontario and Quebec. Last week, the federal Parliamentary Budget Officer calculated the EV sector is slated to receive $52.5 billion in federal and provincial support.
The question of tariffs on China is difficult for the government to navigate, experts have told the Star, with geopolitical tensions and a potential tradeoff between protecting a heavily subsidized domestic industry and pushing hard for people to switch off gas-powered cars to confront the climate crisis.
Premier Doug Ford - whose government has also devoted billions of dollars to luring EV makers to the province - added to the pressure last week. In a statement Monday, Ford welcomed how "the federal government is listening to our calls and taking quick action to protect Ontario and Canadian jobs from unfairly subsidized Chinese imports." Ford called for Ottawa to "work hand-in-hand with our partners in the U.S." to stop the imports from "flooding our markets."
The federal Conservatives, meanwhile, said in a statement Monday that Ottawa "should not allow" China to dump cheap products that threaten Canadian jobs.
Experts such as Flavio Volpe, the head of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, argue China will overrun Canada's fledgling EV sector unless Ottawa matches the American tariffs Biden has promised. Under Beijing's direction, China has pursued an industrial policy that has supported the creation of a wide range of EV companies with access to its large domestic market, lower labour costs and an abundance of raw materials.
Last year, 60 per cent of new electric cars sold globally were registered in China, according to the International Energy Agency. Chinese companies already dominate the global market for plug-in hybrids, and Shenzhen-based BYD has a growing international presence and is threatening Tesla for the biggest share of the global market in battery-operated electric vehicles, according to a February report from TrendForce, a market research firm.
The company also recently launched an EV that costs under $10,000 US.
On Monday, Freeland was clear that Ottawa - despite having a target for all new cars sold in 2035 to be emissions-free - does not want to fuel the green transition with cheap EVs built with what she described as lower labour and environmental standards in China.
"That is not the way to meet climate goals," she said.
As it stands, Canada has a six-per-cent tariff on EVs made in China. The only EVs made there that are currently available in Canada are Teslas manufactured in Shanghai. The federal government also doles out $5,000 to people who buy zero-emission vehicles, and the made-in-China Teslas are currently eligible for this cash.
In a statement Monday, the federal New Democratic Party urged the government to ensure these rebates are only available for EVs from countries with labour and environmental standards like Canada's.
Ng, the federal trade minister, said the moves Ottawa is considering are necessary to confront China's alleged unfair practices in the EV market that undermine Canadian workers.
"It isn't just targeting companies. It is targeting the livelihood of our workers and the stability of our communities," Ng said.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said the federal government is opposed to fuelling the green transition with cheap electrical vehicles built with lower labour and environmental standards.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Tight race as votes begin to roll in: Counting ballots longer process than usual due to record-breaking 84 candidates vying for post

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tight-race-as-votes-begin-roll/docview/3071774904/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tight+race+as+votes+begin+to+roll+in%3A+Counting+ballots+longer+process+than+usual+due+to+record-breaking+84+candidates+vying+for+post&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

At publication time, Church had 1,159 votes, while Stewart, her toughest and nearest competitor on a historically long ballot of 84 candidates, garnered 904 votes. The NDP's Amrit Parhar had 286, while the Greens' Christian Cullis had 96. That was with just 20 of 192 polling stations counted.
The call of who wins, traditionally announced by broadcasters, had not yet been made by 10:25 p.m.
Senior Liberal and Conservative operatives had predicted a Liberal win during Monday's midday voting lull, with hours left to go before the after-work voting rush and pre-Stanley Cup game crunch.
After polls closed, before official results were reported, Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne set aside any expectations for a Conservative win, saying the party would not win it.

Byrne said the Tories feel they can win seats like neighbouring Eglinton-Lawrence next year.
"If I were Marco (Mendocino, the Liberal MP there) I'd be worried," she said.
"I don't think the Liberals were ever in a position where they thought they were going to lose this."

"This is a strong Liberal riding. They've held it for a long time," said Lantsman. "But we've had a strong local campaign by an engaged team and a great candidate who put the work in and knocked on doors and is really well-liked by the community that he lives in," she said.
Turnout was everything. The parties had to pull out the supporters they had identified to cast ballots on a sunny summer workday, before many would turn their attention to hockey parties and pre-game celebrations.
Elections Canada said all polls opened on time and no problems were reported at polling places early in the day.
The ballot's length was causing delays because it was more than a metre long and must be unfolded like a map in order for each one to be counted.
A protest group calling themselves Longest Ballot Committee stacked the ballot with independent candidates to draw attention to the drawbacks of the first-past-the-post system.
With files from Mark Ramzy,
Robert Benzie and
the Canadian Press

Document 477 of 500

Simple science and complex politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/simple-science-complex-politics/docview/3071774845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Simple+science+and+complex+politics&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Follow the science at the Ontario Science Centre.
But beware the dubious political science over the Ontario Place controversy.
By suddenly closing off the aging science centre to the public last week, citing a safety scare at the aging Don Mills facility, Doug Ford reignited every possible conspiracy theory.
Behold his corrupt motives, the critics cry: a scam and a sham!
So is this really a ruse to cover up the premier's scheme to relocate the science centre to Ontario Place while enriching his friends? That reaction, so soon after the deception and bungling over last year's Greenbelt boondoggle, is understandable and predictable.
But disregarding an engineering report that warns of an imminent roof collapse would be incomprehensible. To do so would be to assume that an entire government - not just the politicians but the civil servants - is putting political calculations ahead of civil engineering.
Surely it is possible for a politician who is sometimes dishonest to be honest some of the time. Just as it's entirely possible for the critics to be intellectually dishonest at times.
Much of the incredulity over the sudden closing of the science centre comes from people who refuse to believe it should ever be closed - whether it's the risk of a collapsing roof or the reality of declining attendance amid structural decline. Much of the opposition to relocating the science centre to the old Ontario Place site comes from waterfront minimalists who want more of the same green space for the personal enjoyment of privileged downtown residents.
I come at it from a different direction.
I've long argued - long before the Ford government came onside - that the best way to revitalize the waterfront was with an ambitious redesign that relocated a neglected Don Mills science centre to a reinvigorated Ontario Place. I watched the last Liberal government shutter Ontario Place (barely noticed), all the while neglecting the Ontario Science Centre (barely a peep).
To its credit, the Ford government dusted off previous reports - long ignored by the Liberals - that recommended a strong anchor tenant for a waterfront project built not on nostalgia but a future vision, noting that Singapore had similarly relied on a cutting edge science exhibition. Our waterfront deserves something more compelling - not to mention modern - than the decades-old Harbourfront complex.
To be sure, Ford's proposal for a foreign-owned spa with an over-the-top plan for underground parking made it a harder sell with a distinctive smell. The proposed spa rubs people the wrong way.
I'm not a spa person, but if you think of it as a water park by another name, alongside an ambitious science centre attraction, against the backdrop of a beautifully landscaped Ontario Place setting, it will be a crowd-pleaser. Notwithstanding the naysayers.
Against that backdrop, I see the engineering report (and the government's response) not as a contrived scenario but a predictable outcome.
But back to the politics. And the science.
People assume that Ford is pulling a fast one to shutter the science centre before anyone can stop him. That's a misreading of his deal-making with an unlikely ally, Mayor Olivia Chow, to plunge full speed ahead with the relocation.
When the two politicians shook hands last year on an entente to shore up Toronto's financing, Chow agreed to get out of the way - politically and legally. It's already a done deal, so the last thing Ford needed was to trigger a predictable public outcry by moving up the closing last week.
As for the science, it has become urban lore that the Don Mills centre is on par with the pyramids, destined to endure forever. Designed by famed Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama, time has not been kind to the structure that dates to 1969 - nor to its aging exhibits.
In fact, the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report, released last week, found the air-filled concrete used by Moriyama in construction - similar to that in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life. The report, which will be peer-reviewed for a second opinion by another engineering firm, said the risk of rain or snow buildup means the reinforced steel underneath could further corrode and fail.
Engineers quantify risk. So do politicians.
If a collapsed roof killed members of the public, our judgment of the collective failing would be scathing. It was a decade ago, after a rooftop parking lot crumbled at an Elliot Lake mall, and the judicial inquiry blamed "its designers and builders, its owners, some architects and engineers, as well as the municipal and provincial officials charged with the duty of protecting the public."
It's easy to criticize the "provincial officials" - not just the premier - who have publicly defended the closure of the Ontario Science Centre. But when lives are on the line, they have a duty to do the right thing - even if it's not necessarily a politically easy thing.
Follow the science. Mind the politics.
CAPTION: Much of the incredulity over the sudden closing of the Ontario Science Centre comes from people who refuse to believe it should ever be closed, despite safety concerns or declining attendance, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 478 of 500

Roof panels key to Science Centre shutdown: Technical report outlines years of deferred maintenance while critics want closure reversed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/roof-panels-key-science-centre-shutdown/docview/3071773951/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Roof+panels+key+to+Science+Centre+shutdown%3A+Technical+report+outlines+years+of+deferred+maintenance+while+critics+want+closure+reversed&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The path to the shockingly sudden shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre after 55 years in operation began quietly last fall.
While controversy swirled over Premier Doug Ford's plan to open a new science centre - and a spa - at Ontario Place in 2028, an engineering firm hired by the provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario sent experts to the iconic building as winter approached.
What they found prompted last week's technical report detailing problems with poorly maintained and crumbling aerated concrete roof panels at risk of failing. Several pages of photos were included.
"We had received notification about the specific type of concrete and the specific type of panels being used around the world in the '50s, '60s and '70s," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma confirmed Monday.
"We had every intention on keeping the Science Centre open until the new science centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning," Surma added, pushing back at growing suspicions about the closure. "I have an obligation to protect the public."
Despite the report, which points to water infiltration of the concrete panels causing it to deteriorate and reinforcing the bar inside it to rust, critics smell a rat over the closure and want it reversed.
They maintain it conveniently pads Ford's narrative about the overall poor condition of the Science Centre following years of neglect by successive governments.
"We know what this is about - his vanity Ontario Place project," New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles wrote on social media.
"Everything about this feels wrong," said Liberal MPP Adil Shamji, whose riding of Don Valley East includes the science centre.
The centre's days were already numbered: the government's "business case" for a new one found it would cost $600 million more to fix up the existing building designed by the late architect Raymond Moriyama.
According to Ontario's auditor general last December, "deferred maintenance projects that were at risk of critical failure have been repeatedly denied funding" at the Science Centre. They include 42 such projects since 2017, the year before Ford's Progressive Conservatives took power.
Last week's 52-page engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group concluded concrete panels used in the Science Centre are reaching the end of their lifespan, similar to those in more than 100 now-closed British schools of the same era.
As the Associated Press reported from London last September on the schools, this type of concrete panel can fail "with very little warning," said Phil Purnell, a professor of materials and structures at the University of Leeds.
A deadline of Oct. 31 has been set to vacate the Science Centre, before another seasonal onslaught of rain and snow risks a collapse on staff and visitors, many of them schoolchildren.
Surma said the immediate closure to the public gives staff time to pack up and move to a temporary science centre - for which a request for proposals went out Monday - or to pop-up exhibitions being arranged.
"We have exhibits, we have animals, we have staff in the building. We have to essentially turn the building off," she added. "It's a job and we're doing all of that planning now."
Aware that the report would be questioned in today's political climate, Infrastructure Ontario chief executive Michael Lindsay pledged it will be peer reviewed by another engineering firm, providing a second opinion of sorts.
While the report stated just one Science Centre roofing panel is in the "critical" category, six per cent of the panels are considered "high risk," nine per cent are "medium risk" and the remaining 84 per cent are "low risk."
But the authors warned "a significant snow or rain ... could exceed the reduced load-carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse."
The recommendation? That the roof panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two to five years.
Officials said the lengthy time frame is because many of the roof panels are hard to reach because of the Science Centre's unique location straddling a ravine.
The material in question - known as RAAC for reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete - was a popular choice in institutional and industrial buildings because of its light weight and thermal insulation capacities that also made it a good sound and fire barrier, the Rimkus report said.
It is made from an aerated blend of sand, Portland cement and aluminum powder and has increasingly come on the radar for problems as water seeps in.
"As a building material, RAAC has an overall reduced robustness compared to steel decks or traditional concrete, making it more susceptible to damage from impact and raising the risk of sudden failure," the Rimkus report continues.
About 80 other public buildings across Ontario with the same type of concrete are being examined to determine if further action is necessary.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre must be vacated by Oct. 31, before rain and snow create the risk of a roof collapse. In the meantime, staff will pack up and move to a temporary science centre or pop-up exhibitions.Nick LachanceToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Full text: Questions about the Ontario Science Centre
The Science Centre is the
latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect: Editorial, June 21
It's ironic that CP24's announcement of the permanent closure of the Ontario Science Centre was followed immediately by Premier Doug Ford's campaign ad proclaiming he and his Progressive Conservative party listen to "the people." Exactly which people?
Julia Bowkun, Toronto
I would like to see a second opinion of the state of the roof problems. One that is not chosen by the province, nor anyone associated with the premier. It would be fair, don't you think?
Sandra Farrell, Newcastle, Ont.
As a structural engineer, if I inspected a building and found that portions of it were subject to imminent collapse, I would demand the building be evaluated immediately to avoid another Algoma Centre Mall situation. Did I miss something about the Ontario Science Centre roof? When was the engineering report delivered to the government?
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
Who are these engineers? Who hired them? Why did they appear so late in this whole fiasco? What does the province have planned for the property?
Carolynne Canham, St. Catharines, Ont.
I have heard that the snow this winter was a contributing factor. What snow? Could they, at least, have maintained it well enough until their new one was completed? Could they not have given us time to enjoy this beloved place and give it a proper farewell?
Lynda Hurley, Toronto
Let me get this straight. The new Ontario Line subway will run from a non-existent Ontario Place to a shuttered Ontario Science Centre. Maybe the new line should be called "The No Ontario Subway Line."
Joel Hertz, Toronto
With the Science Centre moving down to Ontario Place, why not build the spa on the property vacated by the Science Centre. Now the Science Centre won't have to be diminished in size, and the spa will have a beautiful landscape.
Glenn Taylor, Innisfil, Ont.
I am devastated at the sudden closing of the beloved Ontario Science Centre. I never thought I would be this dismayed and angry with the premier and his government. They have done nothing but destroy Toronto and Ontario.
Kate Mitchell, Toronto
Let's enjoy the new location
As a young, non-driving mother, and then a grandmother living to the west of Toronto, I found it too hard to travel to Ontario Science Centre and take the children because it was too complicated with public transportation. Since Ontario Place is so central and soon-to-be a hub for transit, it will be available to many more families. So, I am for the relocation. It is the ONTARIO Science Centre for all after all.
Alayne Bailey, Oakville
Elections cost money, so why call them early?
Opposition parties say they're ready if Doug Ford calls a snap election, June 4
Early elections benefit the party in power, not the public. Considering this, candidates (including the premier himself) should be made to raise money to cover the cost of the election in their riding. How many early elections would we have then?
David Williams, Barrie
An island bridge won't serve everyone
The ferries are failing. It's time to build a pedestrian bridge to the Toronto Islands, June 20
The suggestion of a pedestrian-cycling bridge connecting the foot of Cherry Street to Ward's Island ignores the impact on Ward's Island. I can just hear the islanders screaming "NIMBY!" (not in my backyard). Is it not feasible, and cheaper, to utilize the Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport's pedestrian tunnel connecting Toronto's mainland to the island terminal to provide pedestrian-cycling access?
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
There is no room on Ward's Island for a bridge. It would likely require tearing down homes or ripping out the small forest area on the south end. And if such bridge did happen, how would everyone get to Centre Island and Hanlans Point safely? There is one small road. There are many improvements that could be made to the current ferry system. Building a bridge is not one of them.
Keith Perrott, Toronto
Thank you for the column on how the public needs better access to Toronto Islands. While the suggested bridge may be one solution, it has problems. There may be a better alternative, such as using the tunnel (an underground bridge) that connects the mainland to the island airport at Bathurst Street. This tunnel will be publicly accessible if the island airport agreement is not renewed in 2033.
Max Moore, Toronto
Consider average cost of EVs
Automakers concerned about slow demand, June 20
To suggest that the average price of an electric car is a factor in affordability is like saying the average price of a Starbucks determines whether people can afford coffee. There are many high-end electric cars dragging up the average price, but it's the price of the most economical electric cars that should determine whether a person can afford to buy one. There are many EVs that are even cheaper than gasoline-powered cars once one factors in the cost of fuel.
Rob Strang, Orangeville, Ont.
The policing of morality goes back in history
Homophobic policing is not ancient history, June 18
Don't the police have anything better to do than enforce morality or investigate sexual activities? This current practice goes back far in history. I have long been of the impression that sex in public is illegal, hence entrapment was once a method to discourage this vice. I have memories of happy park cruising in the days of my youth and the emergence of gay life in the early 1970s. The era is well documented. Contributor Marcus McCann's book "Park Cruising" tells of the struggle for the right to privacy and how provincial politicians were once alarmed at the growth of the emerging Toronto gay community and tried to stifle this freedom with raids and other factors. As well, Toronto activist paper "The Body Politic" reported the fight for the freedom we have today, and activist Gerald Hannons' book "Immoral, Indecent, and Scurrilous: The Making of an Unrepentant Sex Radical" has many details about how gay men have been criminalized.
Peter Iveson, Toronto

Toronto-St. Paul's byelection tight race as results delayed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-st-pauls-byelection-tight-race-as-results/docview/3071773917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto-St.+Paul%27s+byelection+tight+race+as+results+delayed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tight race was on in a key federal byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's, billed as a harbinger of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's political future, as early results began to pour in.
The Liberal candidate Leslie Church had a slightly growing lead over Conservative Don Stewart late Monday night.
Liberals gathered at a St. Clair West bar while Conservatives gathered further to the west of the riding on Spadina, with all eyes first on the Stanley Cup final game, before turning to a frustratingly slow Elections Canada livestream of ballot counts, poll-by-poll.
At publication time, Church had 3,540 votes, while Stewart, her toughest and nearest competitor on a historically long ballot of 84 candidates, garnered 3,046 votes. The NDP's Amrit Parhar had 1,051, while the Greens' Christian Cullis had 258. That was with just 65 of 192 polling stations counted.
The call of who wins, traditionally announced by broadcasters, had not yet been made by 11:32 p.m. with Elections Canada admitting counting was going slowly due to the long ballot.
Senior Liberal and Conservative operatives had predicted a Liberal win during Monday's midday voting lull, with hours left to go before the after-work voting rush and pre-Stanley Cup game crunch.
After polls closed, before official results were reported, Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne set aside any expectations for a Conservative win, saying the party would not win it.
But just as it had during the campaign, the Conservative party viewed a drastic drop in Liberal support as a win of sorts for them - a sign that the Conservatives could make big gains in other swing races in the GTA. As the road to a parliamentary majority runs through big swaths of suburban and ex-urban ridings like those in the 905 region, it would rally Conservative troops and strike fear in the Liberal ranks.
At Cafe Landwer on Spadina Road in Forest Hill Village, excited Conservative supporters gathered in anticipation of their best showing in Toronto in years.
Stewart showed up at 11:20 p.m. to thank volunteers and said when the final results are in, voters will have sent a message that "the country is waiting for change. The country has been waiting for change. We're ready."
"There's a message that our voters would like to send to Mr. Trudeau, and that's one of change."
Byrne, Pierre Poilievre's most senior adviser, said the results send a strong signal for a general election expected next year.
"It's fantastic. We are thrilled with the campaign we have that we've run. Don Stewart is a fantastic candidate. Our local campaign led by Amanda Kimber has been fantastic," said Byrne.
Mindful Toronto-St. Paul's is one of the safest Liberal seats in Canada, she said it proves the Conservatives can compete anywhere in Ontario now.
Byrne said the Tories feel they can win seats like neighbouring Eglinton-Lawrence next year.
"If I were Marco (Mendocino, the Liberal MP there) I'd be worried," she said.
"I don't think the Liberals were ever in a position where they thought they were going to lose this."
Deputy Conservative leader Melissa Lantsman, who represents Thornhill, said the reception at the doors has been encouraging.
"This is a strong Liberal riding. They've held it for a long time," said Lantsman.
"But we've had a strong strong local campaign by an engaged team and a great candidate who put the work in and knocked on doors and is really well-liked by the community that he lives in," she said.
Turnout was everything. The parties had to pull out the supporters they had identified to cast ballots on a sunny summer workday, before many would turn their attention to hockey parties and pre-game celebrations.
Elections Canada said all polls opened on time and no problems were reported at polling places early in the day.
CAPTION: Election signs in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection happening this Monday in Toronto on Sunday.

Document 481 of 500

Chrystia Freeland calls China's EVs a threat to Canada and vows to fight back

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-calls-chinas-evs-threat-canada/docview/3072043614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+calls+China%27s+EVs+a+threat+to+Canada+and+vows+to+fight+back&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland alleged Monday that China is threatening Canadian workers by unfairly cranking out cheap electric vehicles, and vowed that the Liberal government will act to defend Canada's nascent - and heavily subsidized - EV sector.
Speaking at a factory in Vaughan on Monday, Freeland said the government will launch 30 days of consultations on July 2 before deciding how to respond to China's "intentional, state-directed policy" of overproducing EVs. She accused Beijing of "taking advantage of the global economic system" by using "very problematic" standards to produce cheap EVs to dump on the world market and undermine Canada's potential to compete in the growing global clean car industry.
The measures under consideration include a special measure to slap a surtax on Chinese-made EVs, changes to which cars are eligible for the $5,000 federal EV purchase rebates, and "broader" restrictions on investment in Canada, Freeland said.
"We are going to protect you and defend you from unfair competition, and I want you to know that," Freeland, who is also Canada's finance minister, told a room of workers Monday morning.
While Freeland declined to say whether Canada plans to join U.S. President Joe Biden's new tariff wall against Chinese EVs, she said "nothing is ruled out and all possible tools are on the table" - including higher tariffs.
"We certainly see the need to be very strong in our defence of Canada and the national economic interest," she said.
The United States announced in May that it would crank up its own tariffs on Chinese EVs from 25 per cent to 100 per cent. The European Union, citing similar concerns, later announced the bloc would raise its own tariffs on made-in-China EVs to 38 per cent.
All that fuelled expectations that Canada would follow suit, with International Trade Minister Mary Ng repeatedly stating the Liberal government was seriously looking at the question and intended to take action to protect Canada's fledgling EV sector.
A senior government official, speaking on condition they aren't named, told the Star on Monday that it is "highly likely" Canada will impose new tariffs on Chinese EVs, and that the "inclination is to match" the American tariffs. But the official said the government will try to calculate during the consultations how much of a cost advantage China gets from practices deemed unfair, and to set Ottawa's response accordingly.
The EV sector in Canada is getting a huge amount of government support, which has helped attract companies like Honda, Stellantis and Northvolt to promise to set up factories for batteries and vehicles in Ontario and Quebec. Last week, the federal Parliamentary Budget Officer calculated that the EV sector is slated to receive $52.5 billion in federal and provincial support.
The question of tariffs on China is difficult for the government to navigate, experts have told the Star, with geopolitical tensions and a potential tradeoff between protecting a heavily subsidized domestic industry and pushing hard for people to switch off gas-powered cars to confront the climate crisis.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford - whose government has also devoted billions of dollars to luring EV makers to the province - added to the pressure last week. He alleged China's "low labour standards and dirty energy" is letting it flood the market with cheap EVs, and called on Ottawa to match the American tariffs to protect Canadian jobs.
Experts such as Flavio Volpe, the head of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, argue that China will overrun Canada's fledgling EV sector unless Ottawa matches the American tariffs thatBiden has promised. Under Beijing's direction, China has pursued an industrial policy that has supported the creation of a wide range of EV companies with access to its large domestic market, lower labour costs and an abundance of raw materials.
Last year, 60 per cent of new electric cars sold globally were registered in China, according to the International Energy Agency. Chinese companies already dominate the global market for plug-in hybrids, and Shenzhen-based BYD has a growing international presence and is threatening Tesla for the biggest share of the global market in battery-operated electric vehicles, according to a February report from TrendForce, a market research firm.
The company also recently launched an EV that costs under $10,000 (U.S.).
On Monday in Vaughan, Freeland was clear that Ottawa - despite having a target for all new cars sold in 2035 to be emissions-free - does not want to fuel the green transition with cheap EVs built with what she described as lower labour and environmental standards in China.
"That is not the way to meet climate goals," she said.
As it stands, Canada has a six-per-cent tariff on EVs made in China. The only EVs made there that are currently available in Canada are Teslas manufactured in Shanghai. The federal government also doles out $5,000 to people who buy zero-emission vehicles, and the made-in-China Teslas are currently eligible for this cash.
Ng, the federal trade minister, said the moves Ottawa is considering - including new tariffs and changing which cars are eligible for the federal cash incentive - are necessary to confront China's alleged unfair practices in the EV market that undermine Canadian workers.
"It isn't just targeting companies. It is targeting the livelihood of our workers and the stability of our communities," Ng said. "We will stand up to practices that distort the market."
Asked what Canada will do if China retaliates against any new trade measures, Freeland declined to be specific but said the government is "not naive" and will be prepared "for all eventualities."
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland speaks during a news conference in Ottawa on June 18, 2024.

Subject: Automobiles; Electric vehicles; Factories; Tariffs

Document 482 of 500

The simple science and complex politics of the crumbling Ontario Science Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/simple-science-complex-politics-crumbling-ontario/docview/3072043516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+simple+science+and+complex+politics+of+the+crumbling+Ontario+Science+Centre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Follow the science at the Ontario Science Centre.
But beware the dubious political science over the Ontario Place controversy.
By suddenly closing off the aging science centre to the public last week, citing a safety scare at the aging Don Mills facility, Doug Ford reignited every possible conspiracy theory.
Behold his corrupt motives, the critics cry: a scam and a sham!
So is this really a ruse to cover up the premier's scheme to relocate the science centre to Ontario Place while enriching his friends? That reaction, so soon after the deception and bungling over last year's Greenbelt boondoggle, is understandable and predictable.
But disregarding an engineering report that warns of an imminent roof collapse would be incomprehensible. To do so would be to assume that an entire government - not just the politicians but the civil servants - is putting political calculations ahead of civil engineering.
Surely it is possible for a politician who is sometimes dishonest to be honest some of the time. Just as it's entirely possible for the critics to be intellectually dishonest at times.
Much of the incredulity over the sudden closing of the science centre comes from people who refuse to believe it should ever be closed - whether it's the risk of a collapsing roof or the reality of declining attendance amid structural decline. Much of the opposition to relocating the science centre to the old Ontario Place site comes from waterfront minimalists who want more of the same green space for the personal enjoyment of privileged downtown residents.
I come at it from a different direction.
I've long argued - long before the Ford government came onside - that the best way to revitalize the waterfront was with an ambitious redesign that relocated a neglected Don Mills science centre to a reinvigorated Ontario Place. I watched the last Liberal government shutter Ontario Place (barely noticed), all the while neglecting the Ontario Science Centre (barely a peep).
To its credit, the Ford government dusted off previous reports - long ignored by the Liberals - that recommended a strong anchor tenant for a waterfront project built not on nostalgia but a future vision, noting that Singapore had similarly relied on a cutting edge science exhibition. Our waterfront deserves something more compelling - not to mention modern - than the decades-old Harbourfront complex.
To be sure, Ford's proposal for a foreign-owned spa with an over-the-top plan for underground parking made it a harder sell with a distinctive smell. The proposed spa rubs people the wrong way.
I'm not a spa person, but if you think of it as a water park by another name, alongside an ambitious science centre attraction, against the backdrop of a beautifully landscaped Ontario Place setting, it will be a crowd-pleaser. Notwithstanding the naysayers.
Against that backdrop, I see the engineering report (and the government's response) not as a contrived scenario but a predictable outcome.
But back to the politics. And the science.
People assume that Ford is pulling a fast one to shutter the science centre before anyone can stop him. That's a misreading of his deal-making with an unlikely ally, Mayor Olivia Chow, to plunge full speed ahead with the relocation.
When the two politicians shook hands last year on an entente to shore up Toronto's financing, Chow agreed to get out of the way - politically and legally. It's already a done deal, so the last thing Ford needed was to trigger a predictable public outcry by moving up the closing last week.
As for the science, it has become urban lore that the Don Mills centre is on par with the pyramids, destined to endure forever. Designed by famed Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama, time has not been kind to the structure that dates to 1969 - nor to its aging exhibits.
In fact, the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report, released last week, found the air-filled concrete used by Moriyama in construction - similar to that in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life. The report, which will be peer-reviewed for a second opinion by another engineering firm, said the risk of rain or snow buildup means the reinforced steel underneath could further corrode and fail.
Engineers quantify risk. So do politicians.
If a collapsed roof killed members of the public, our judgment of the collective failing would be scathing. It was a decade ago, after a rooftop parking lot crumbled at an Elliot Lake mall, and the judicial inquiry blamed "its designers and builders, its owners, some architects and engineers, as well as the municipal and provincial officials charged with the duty of protecting the public."
It's easy to criticize the "provincial officials" - not just the premier - who have publicly defended the closure of the Ontario Science Centre. But when lives are on the line, they have a duty to do the right thing - even if it's not necessarily a politically easy thing.
Follow the science. Mind the politics.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre stands fenced in and closed on June 24, 2024.

Subject: Science; Engineering firms; Permits; Aging; Politics; Politicians; Civil engineering

Business indexing term: Subject: Engineering firms Civil engineering
