Document 483 of 500

How the Ontario Science Centre was suddenly shut down - and why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-ontario-science-centre-was-suddenly-shut-down/docview/3072043509/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+the+Ontario+Science+Centre+was+suddenly+shut+down+-+and+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The path to the shockingly sudden shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre after 55 years in operation began quietly last fall.
While controversy swirled over Premier Doug Ford's plan to open a new science centre at Ontario Place in 2028 - and a spa - an engineering firm hired by the provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario sent experts to the iconic building as winter approached.
What they found prompted last week's technical report detailing problems with poorly maintained and crumbling aerated concrete roof panels at risk of failing. Several pages of photos were included.
"We had received notification about the specific type of concrete and the specific type of panels being used around the world in the '50s, '60s and '70s," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma confirmed Monday.
"We had every intention on keeping the science centre open until the new science centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning," Surma added, pushing back at growing suspicions about the closure.
"I have an obligation to protect the public."
Despite the report, which points to water infiltration of the concrete panels causing it to deteriorate and reinforcing bar inside it to rust, critics smell a rat over the closure and want it reversed.
They maintain it conveniently pads Ford's narrative about the overall poor condition of the science centre following years of neglect by successive governments.
"We know what this is about - his vanity Ontario Place project," New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles wrote on social media.
"Everything about this feels wrong," said Liberal MPP Adil Shamji, whose riding of Don Valley East includes the science centre.
The centre's days were already numbered: the government's "business case" for a new one found it would cost $600 million more to fix up the existing building designed by the late architect Raymond Moriyama.
According to Ontario's auditor general last December, "deferred maintenance projects that were at risk of critical failure have been repeatedly denied funding" at the science centre. They include 42 such projects since 2017, the year before Ford's Progressive Conservatives took power.
What did the report find?
Last week's 52-page engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group concluded concrete panels used in the science centre are reaching the end of their lifespan, similar to those in more than 100 now-closed British schools of the same era.
As the Associated Press reported from London last September on the schools, this type of concrete panel can fail "with very little warning," said Phil Purnell, a professor of materials and structures at the University of Leeds.
A deadline of Oct. 31 has been set to vacate the science centre, before another seasonal onslaught of rain and snow risks a collapse on staff and visitors, many of them schoolchildren.
Surma said the immediate closure to the public gives staff time to pack up and move to a temporary science centre - for which a request for proposals went out Monday - or to pop-up exhibitions being arranged.
"We have exhibits, we have animals, we have staff in the building. We have to essentially turn the building off," she added. "It's a job and we're doing all of that planning now."
Aware that the report would be questioned in today's political climate, Infrastructure Ontario chief executive Michael Lindsay pledged it will be peer reviewed by another engineering firm, providing a second opinion of sorts.
One concrete panel is 'critical'
While the report stated just one science centre roofing panel is in the "critical" category, six per cent of the panels are considered "high risk," nine per cent are "medium risk" and the remaining 84 per cent are "low risk."
But the authors warned "a significant snow or rain ... could exceed the reduced load-carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse."
The recommendation? That the roof panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two to five years.
Officials said the lengthy time frame is because many of the roof panels are hard to reach because of the science centre's unique location straddling a ravine.
The material in question - known as RAAC for reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete - was a popular choice in institutional and industrial buildings because of its light weight and thermal insulation capacities that also made it a good sound and fire barrier, the Rimkus report said.
It is made from an aerated blend of sand, Portland cement and aluminum powder and has increasingly come on the radar for problems as water seeps in.
"As a building material, RAAC has an overall reduced robustness compared to steel decks or traditional concrete, making it more susceptible to damage from impact and raising the risk of sudden failure," the Rimkus report continues.
"Owner and facility managers are often not aware of the problems ... until there is physical evidence of a failure or deterioration."
"The consequences of RAAC failure include potential building damage and risk to public safety," the report cautions.
About 80 other public buildings across Ontario with the same type of concrete are being examined to determine if further action is necessary.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed June 21, 2024 after an engineering report found problems in the roof panels.

Subject: Shutdowns; Science; Infrastructure; Engineering firms; Aerated concrete

Document 484 of 500

They risk their lives fighting deadly forest fires - but say 'scandalous' treatment by Ottawa is bleeding an essential service

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/they-risk-their-lives-fighting-deadly-forest/docview/3071490809/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=They+risk+their+lives+fighting+deadly+forest+fires+-+but+say+%27scandalous%27+treatment+by+Ottawa+is+bleeding+an+essential+service&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-At 27, Noah Freedman is among the most experienced wildland firefighters in his unit. In just eight years on the job, he's become a local union executive and a fire crew leader.
It's a feat that would have been unheard of a few decades ago, he says, but after years of low wages, few benefits and a climate crisis that is making weather disasters more extreme, a grave retention crisis has placed a heavy burden on those on the front lines protecting Canadians from forest fires, and forcing those who stick around to step up.
That's why Freedman believes the clock is ticking for political leaders to address this crisis as another busy wildfire season starts. He's not alone: New Democrats, Conservatives and several unions are calling on the Trudeau government to take the lead and make changes to how wildland firefighters are classified by the federal government to improve their working conditions and reflect the growing difficulties they face in their jobs.
"None of us here in this job exist for the money," Freedman told the Star. "But that doesn't mean that we're not struggling to survive and to justify these sacrifices to our families and to our loved ones."
Although wildland firefighters are provincially regulated, a technical decision in Canada's federal job classification system excludes them from jobs deemed to be in "public safety," affecting their standing under the federal Income Tax Act and directly leading to lower pensions and later retirements.
They are considered "silviculture and forestry workers" rather than "firefighters" like their colleagues working in municipalities across the country, angering many in the field who put their lives in danger every year fighting wildfires.
To one national union, it's a matter of equity. It says the Trudeau government - which has in recent years made an effort to step up its role in combating wildfires - must make a change in the classification.
"Here we are with a group who put their lives in harm's way almost every single day during the fire season, and they're not even recognized as firefighters," said Bert Blundon, president of the National Union of Public and General Employees, which represents more than 4,000 wildland firefighters across Canada. "I see it as a bit scandalous."
Blundon says classifying wildland firefighters accurately would increase their potential pension accrual rates from two to 2.33 per cent, and lower their earliest retirement age from 55 to 50 - benefits that can be available to municipal firefighters but not to forest firefighters.
Even more so, critics charge, the classification is ignorant to the realities of their jobs and puts them on lower footing than city firefighters.
"We just think it's outrageous, and it needs to be corrected," Blundon told the Star. "And it may make it easier for our provincial unions to negotiate an overall better package to protect this group of workers and to treat them fairly."
For now, the Trudeau government can only say it's reviewing the matter, according to a spokesperson for Employment and Social Development Canada.
But the change won't solve every problem. For one, working conditions vary across provinces. And because so many firefighters work a lot of overtime, for example, much of their time worked doesn't count toward their pensions, Freedman said.
"If there was a conversation about this at the federal level and it sort of permeated into provincial politics, I think it would do us a big favour," he told the Star.
That push on the federal level has received political backing. The NDP and Conservatives have called on the Trudeau government to make the change in the lead-up to this year's wildfire season, and the idea received cross-partisan support at the House of Commons public safety committee.
It's all the more reason for the federal government to take action now, Blundon said.
Often when wildfires or firefighters come up in the House of Commons, the Liberals tout their initiative in recent years to fund the training of 1,000 wildland firefighters. More than 600 have been trained so far, but fewer than 100 of them had been deployed in their communities by the end of last wildfire season.
Freedman suggested those efforts miss the bigger picture. "It's always cheaper to keep an existing customer," he told the Star.
"You spend all this energy every year, not just in the fight against fire, but in training the new people who are learning, and then it's all for nothing, and you have to do it all over again."
Not only is it a strain on resources and energy, but also on taxpayer dollars, he said.
"To put it in perspective, last year I had two brand new folks and they were amazing, and then neither of them returned."
Inaction has a cost. In the face of the crisis, provinces are increasing their reliance on other provinces, foreign firefighters, and even the Canadian Armed Forces, despite warnings from a strained military that this practice is not sustainable.
Last year, 5,500 international firefighters came to Canada to support strained provincial efforts. While it's the provinces that bear the brunt of the cost, there have been times the federal government has had to chip in.
In 2021, for example, Ottawa paid around $2.75 million for the flights and COVID-19 isolation requirements of 55 personnel from Australia and 109 from South Africa following requests from B.C., Ontario and Manitoba, documents tabled in Parliament show.
James Gault, executive vice-president of the Alberta Union of Provincial Employees, said the situation has left wildland firefighters feeling that governments look at them as "expendable."
It doesn't have to be that way, critics charge, as they call on Ottawa to show its concern by taking the lead.
"At the end of the day, what it boils down to is the respect for what these guys and girls do, right?" Gault told the Star.
But Freedman and others are growing anxious as they await action while wildfires get more intense.
Asked why he sticks around despite these conditions, he pauses to think.
"It's hard to come back watching these brilliant, amazing young people walk away, but at the same time, the job is rewarding, and in that respect, it is not hard to come back," he said. "Each and every year, all of us have a hand in helping people and changing people's lives for the better.
"That enough is reason to come back."
CAPTION: Noah Freedman, a wildland firefighter in Northwestern Ontario, wants governments across Canada to recognize the dangerous work they do.

Subject: Firefighters; Income Tax Act-Canada; Pensions; Forest & brush fires; Public safety; Job classification; Decades; Employment; Working conditions

Business indexing term: Subject: Income Tax Act-Canada Pensions Job classification Employment Working conditions

Location: Canada

Document 485 of 500

Toronto-St. Paul voters go to the polls today + politicians rally to save the Ontario Science Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-st-paul-voters-go-polls-today-politicians/docview/3071490754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto-St.+Paul+voters+go+to+the+polls+today+%2B+politicians+rally+to+save+the+Ontario+Science+Centre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, June 24 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on today's Toronto-St. Paul's byelection, the rally to save the Science Centre and how the OPP refused a misconduct hearing after a speeding officer killed a man.
DON'T MISSToronto-St. Paul's voters head to the polls today in a byelection seen as a test for Trudeau's Liberals
Voters are heading to the polls today in Toronto-St. Paul's - a historically Liberal stronghold. While Liberal candidate Leslie Church is confident she can replace former cabinet minister Carolyn Bennett to continue the riding's red reign, some political watchers think Conservative Don Stewart has a chance to flip the seat. "I think that people always try to read the tea leaves in byelections," Church said, downplaying the idea that the byelection could be a bellwether for a national election. Here's what to look out for as the ballots get counted.
On the ground: Voters in the midtown riding know the stakes. "I would like to not vote for Trudeau. And I don't see a very good option, so that's the thing," one resident told the Star's Tonda MacCharles.Back and forth: Columnists Susan Delacourt and Matt Gurney share their takes on the byelection - from their view, even a win for the Liberals could be a bad sign.Big picture: The outlook for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his Liberals isn't good. As of last week, his party trailed the Conservatives across the country, in all age groups, with men and women and in all provinces, except for Québec.Politicians are rallying to save the Ontario Science Centre
Provincial and municipal politicians rallied outside of the Science Centre on Sunday, protesting the museum's sudden closure. "Something smells bad here," NDP leader Marit Stiles told the Star's Brendan Kennedy, drawing parallels to the Greenbelt scandal that has plagued the provincial government. One father at the rally said he took his 2-year-old son to the Science Centre on a monthly basis - he hopes Doug Ford's government changes its mind about shuttering the fixture for families in the area. Here's how politicians and Torontonians alike are pushing back against the Science Centre's closure.
Context: The Ford government abruptly closed the Science Centre late Friday, blaming the building's aging roof. Engineering reports found that the facility was safe until at least the end of October, but heavy rain or snow could increase the risk of sudden collapse of some areas.What we know: The iconic Toronto museum was previously slated to relocate in 2025. In April 2023, the Ford government announced the Science Centre's relocation to Ontario Place, which is also undergoing redevelopment to include a private, water-themed spa.History lesson: The Science Centre was built in 1969, designed by legendary Japanese-Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama. It opened in 1970 and has been a staple of school field trips. Moriyama told the Star in 2023 that he had designed the building to stand for 250 years. He died months later in September.An Ontario cop on a coffee run fatally hit a man - the OPP refused to hold a hearing
Tyler Dorzyk was killed when a speeding, on-duty OPP officer on a coffee run struck him just after midnight on Sept. 29, 2020. The officer's misconduct was "not of a serious nature," the OPP found. Now, Dorzyk's partner, Courtney D'Arthenay, is appealing that decision. While the civilian police watchdog had determined reasonable grounds for officer misconduct, the OPP did not agree, sending a letter with generic and vague reasoning. The case has broader implications for police transparency, as civil liberties lawyers urge more meaningful reasons behind police responses to complaints against officers. Here's what's at stake.
About Tyler: "He was my calm, my rock. A deep hug from him, and I would instantly feel at peace," D'Arthenay said. Dorzyk was a boat builder - a gentle man with a streak of craftiness; he had customized a car for road trips for him and D'Arthenay. He was 35 years old when he died.What we know: Dorzyk and a colleague were in cottage country to pick up a boat for a client. Clad in garbage bags to protect from the rain, he crossed against a red light and was hit by an OPP officer going 70 in a 60 km/h zone.The aftermath: On Tuesday, D'Arthenay will ask an Oshawa judge to overturn the OPP's findings and order a public disciplinary hearings over the officers' conduct.WHAT ELSE
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says he's winding down Gaza operations - but he warns a Lebanon war could be next.Dental groups are rejecting a Liberal claim that Conservatives are fuelling opposition to a new plan - instead claiming it's Ottawa that's spreading misinformation.Gunmen have killed 15 police officers and several civilians in Russia's southern Dagestan region.Thousands of homes lost power across Toronto on Sunday due to strong winds.They risk their lives fighting deadly forest fires - but say "scandalous" treatment by Ottawa is bleeding an essential service.How a 300-square-foot rental with noisy neighbours and paper-thin walls turned me into a Toronto homeowner.Bombardier workers at a Toronto facility are on strike after failed contract talks.The plants in this new Toronto garden share one thing in common: a problematic past.Canadians are throwing parties to cheer on the Edmonton Oilers in the Stanley Cup final.Marc Savard has joined the Toronto Maple Leafs coaching staff as an assistant.Top Blue Jays prospect Orelvis Martinez has been suspended for 80 games, but says fertility medication is behind his failed drug test."The Secret Garden" at the Shaw Festival adds music and magic - but loses its crucial message.POV
Wait until next year? The Blue Jays might be saying that next week
CLOSE-UP
NBA DRAFT: Seven-foot-four Toronto native Zach Edey is expected to go high in the upcoming NBA draft. The towering 300-pound Purdue centre is a two-time NCAA player of the year who wears size 20 shoes and is taller than Shaquille O'Neal. So how do you quantify a man expected to be a monster on the court? The Star's Andres Plana gave his best shot at illustrating it.
Thank you for reading First Up. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca
Sign up here to get this newsletter in your inbox.

There are more unsafe cyclists with school out. Road safety and etiquette should be taught

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-are-more-unsafe-cyclists-with-school-out/docview/3071490728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+are+more+unsafe+cyclists+with+school+out.+Road+safety+and+etiquette+should+be+taught&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School is out. Be safe!
School is just about out for the summer and the bells are going quiet, but not necessarily so for roads and sidewalks as pedestrians try to walk in between skateboards, scooters, electric bikes and bicycles. For whom do those bells toll? How many times have you been walking on the sidewalk and silently a bike zooms by? With school out there will be even more of them, which means "Yikes, that was close" season is upon us. Perhaps some education in schools could help the problem, if you teach safety and etiquette early. Bring back Elmer (the safety elephant), invite police officers to class to talk about danger, give out bike bells and even make it fun with a bell ringing song. Finally, have a sit down session with students in grades seven and eight. They are the age of the cyclists who haphazardly lay their bikes in front of coffee shops and in parks. To think someday these kids will be driving cars!
Bill Wilson, Stouffville, Ont.
Innocent civilians are not the priority
Hezbollah has threatened war against the enemy. No, not just Israel: Cohn, June 19
Columnist Martin Regg Cohn may be correct that the odds of war between Israel and Lebanon are increasing but what is absolutely certain is that in the war with Hamas and a potential war with Hezbollah more innocent people will die. The reason for this is simple. Political organizations and countries are playing politics and the welfare of innocent civilians is not their priority.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
What is Poilievre hiding?
Trudeau and Poilievre need to smarten up and stop politicizing foreign interference, Bloc leader says, June 18
The question of whether Justin Trudeau will be leading his party into the next election has brought much speculation. However, such speculation has not extended to the present leader of the federal Conservatives. Present federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre refuses to acquire the high security clearance necessary to view the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NISCOP) report. That, in itself, is an alarming decision for a man who aspires to become prime minister. Even more disturbing is the fact that the report has found outside interference in Conservative leadership races. There is a dark cloud forming over this issue that makes one wonder what Poilievre is trying to hide. Every political leader knows full well that unanticipated events can derail the best of plans. Is it time to start speculating on whether Poilievre will lead the Conservative party into the 2025 federal election? It is possible he won't. Much stranger things have happened in Canadian politics.
Roger Hilderley, Stratford, Ont.
What's wrong with going digital?
Insiders say politics and privacy concerns halted Doug Ford's promise to put drivers' licences and health cards on digital wallets, June 13
As the Star reports, the Ontario government has delayed the introduction of digital driver's licences and health cards. It's a very wise move by the same government that screwed up new licence plates for vehicles.
Bill Hozy, Toronto
Though security is certainly a concern, it is disappointing that the provincial government is backpedalling on their plans to allow citizens to add their Ontario drivers licences and health cards to their digital wallets. This was, one assumes, something each citizen could decide for themselves and the government's role was to provide a secure way to do it. This sounds like the program to move everyone to photo OHIP cards. These were introduced in 1994 and the red and white cards were supposed to all be replaced within three years. The most recent "last day" for the red and white ones (which really are a security risk) was to be June 30, 2020, but they were later accepted over the pandemic.
David Crawford, Toronto
I am still going to The Beer Store
One of the reasons I plan to continue shopping at The Beer Stores is because of the delays when shopping at a convenience store. The longest delays come from lottery tickets, game cards and cigarettes. These transactions are time consuming and lead to wait times of five to 10 minutes to get to the front of the line. The Beer Stores offer excellent service by focusing on one product. I hope they keep it that way.
Mike Smith, Oakville
So you cannot only buy beer at The Beer Store, soon you also purchase lottery tickets. I cannot wait until they sell chips and chocolate bars. I wonder how long before gas pumps are installed?
Barb Gabovic, Peterborough
Donald Sutherland: 1935-2024
Remembering Donald Sutherland, towering legend of cinema, June 20
We lost a great one. Legendary actor Donald Sutherland passed away at the age of 88. From classics like "Invasion of the Body Snatchers" to "The Hunger Games" films, he's always been a phenomenal onscreen presence. May he rest in peace. We'll miss him.
Paul Bacon, Hallandale Beach, Fla.
Voyager 1 keeps on trucking!
NASA's Voyager 1, the most distant spacecraft from Earth, is doing science again after problem, June 14
Voyager 1 just keeps going, the little probe that launched in 1977! Built by the Jet Propulsion Laboratory in California, Voyager 1 is indeed a great success story in our exploration of the final frontier! It continues to send back information from 24.4 billion kilometres from Earth as it drifts through interstellar space. On board Voyager is a gold-plated record. Should extraterrestrial life locate this probe and access the gold-plated record, they will hear and see a diversity of life from our little rock called Earth! Godspeed to Voyager 1!
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek, Ont.
Not a fan of the Rogers Centre reno
It's a swing and a miss for Rogers Centre's lower bowl transformation, June 20
I completely agree with contributor Timothy Dewhirst about the renovations to the Rogers Center (though I still call it the SkyDome). There aren't clear views of the entire field. A huge problem is the addition of the protective screen all around the lower level. It is very hard to follow a pitched or batted ball with that screen in the way. You have to watch how the fielders react if you want to see what is happening.
Danny Matenko, Toronto

Document 487 of 500

How Scotland started to kick its alcohol problem - and what Ontario could learn from it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-scotland-started-kick-alcohol-problem-what/docview/3071490711/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Scotland+started+to+kick+its+alcohol+problem+-+and+what+Ontario+could+learn+from+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: GLASGOW and EDINBURGH, Scotland - One frosty winter morning, Tom Bennett awoke on the cold ground with a bare foot frozen in the snow.
The night before, the 33-year-old man had been too drunk to tie his shoelaces and one of his boots had fallen off. He was homeless and he knew that alcohol was killing him. But he was too sick to care.
"It was just a horrible place to be," said Bennett from his family home near Glasgow.
"I lost multiple jobs, multiple relationships. I lost all my dignity, self-respect and self-esteem. I nearly died on many occasions."
Today, Bennett, 47, is working to help others emerge from the same kind of darkness at a charity that helps those in the throes of addiction to heal. His journey reflects both alcoholism's toll and Scotland's transformative efforts to curb tragedy.
As Ontario moves to make alcohol more affordable and accessible, Scotland's policies are advancing in the opposite direction.
Ontario officials say they are fulfilling a 2018 election promise to increase "choice and convenience for shoppers and support Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry."
But Scotland has cut alcohol-related hospital admissions by 40 per cent and deaths by almost half. While in Ontario, alcohol-related admissions have risen by a third and deaths by almost half, according to the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction.
While Scots officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
As in many countries, alcohol is central in Canada's social and economic life. Over three quarters of Canadians aged 15 and older report drinking alcohol, according to Statistics Canada. And booze sales contribute $26 billion annually to the national economy.
But new research is showing that drinking alcohol is more dangerous than previously thought. And alcohol's collective harms are vast because the number of people who drink is so high.
While Scottish officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, health experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
Scotland has an age-old drinking problem. For years, the country suffered more alcohol-related deaths - from conditions such as liver disease - than much of the rest of Europe. In 2003, Scots were dying from drink at a rate three times higher than the rest of Britons.
Bennett grew up deep in this tragic alcohol culture. His affluent family viewed alcohol - as many Scots did - as a fundamental part of Scottish life.
In Bennett's community, drinks flowed at the many festivals that hail ancient Celtic traditions. People drank to celebrate, to mourn, to relax and to unwind after work. Bennett enjoyed "wee nips" with his parents from the age of 10 and was buying liquor with friends from the age of 15.
"Alcohol was everywhere," Bennett said with emotion. "And cheap."
Some hard drinks cost less than soft drinks at the local store, he recalled.
"Why buy pop, when you could get 'off your face' for the same money on cheap booze?"
In those days, if you wanted to escape from the despair of drink, you were often on your own.
Bennett remembers a lonely night in jail, shaking violently in severe withdrawal, desperate for a change.
He was ignored. There was no doctor. He was released back to the frigid streets the following day.
"I was not somebody who was having two bottles of wine. ... I was going through seven or eight litres of wine a day ... and two or three bottles of spirits," he recalled as two of his three daughters chatted in the next room.
"I had nowhere to go. I didn't know what to do."
Six months later, Bennett found the remarkable strength to recover from his 15-year dependence with the support of his family and a residential program.
He says he feels lucky to be alive.
"I have known so many people who weren't so lucky .... and have gone on to die," he said.
Today fewer Scots are dying because the influences that led to Bennett's misery are being transformed by Scottish government policy.
Scotland has cut alcohol-related hospital admissions by 40 per cent and deaths by almost half. It won the inaugural European Award for Reducing Alcohol Harm in 2016 and has been recognized by the United Nations for its initiatives tackling alcohol's affordability, availability and attractiveness.
It did not happen easily.
When the Scottish government started its drive to reduce alcohol's downfalls, in the early 2000s, it looked to Canada for advice.
Among its consultants was Tim Stockwell, a professor of psychology at the University of Victoria and director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research. Stockwell and his team had studied British Columbia and Saskatchewan alcohol price increases between 2002 and 2009 and found that a 10 per cent rise in average minimum prices reduced alcohol-related hospital admissions by 18 per cent two years later.
Scottish officials asked Stockwell about Canadian alcohol pricing approaches.
"I told them that we do it terribly," he said in a video interview.
Canada's alcohol pricing policy has stemmed less from public health concerns and more from desires to protect local producers and increase government revenue, Stockwell said.
Even so, the evidence Stockwell had studied about the benefits of increased prices was convincing. So Stockwell helped develop the Scottish policy and assisted over eight years to put it into action. It took two years to pass the legislation through the Scottish Parliament and six years to fight legal challenges by the alcohol industry in Scottish, European and United Kingdom courts.
The key part of Scotland's landmark policy was aimed at reducing drinking by introducing minimum unit prices to make drinking more expensive. Raising minimum prices is the most effective way of reducing alcohol's harms, Stockwell and other experts say. High prices cut the consumption of heavy drinkers - who are often partial to dirt-cheap booze - and young people, who can only drink what's low-cost.
At the beginning of 2018, the price of a beer in Scotland was about $1.00, according to public health data - the target Premier Doug Ford promised in his 'buck-a-beer' election slogan that year.
But a month before Doug Ford won a majority in Ontario, Scotland enacted a minimum price of 50 pence (C$0.87) for a unit of pure alcohol (10 mL) that meant a minimum of about $1.75 Canadian for a can of beer, $8.75 for a bottle of wine and $24.50 for a bottle of spirits, according to Dr. Peter Rice, one of the founders of the Scottish Health Action on Alcohol Problems, a medical think tank that has campaigned for change since 2006.
The new policy increased the prices of almost half the liquor on sale and led to a 15 per cent reduction in alcohol-related deaths in just three years. Last April, the Scottish Parliament unanimously voted to renew the policy and approved an increase to 65 pence per unit ($1.14).
"The pandemic hit us hard," Rice said in a phone interview. But with minimum unit pricing "we have still been able to stem the tide."
Alongside its pricing strategy, Scotland reduced the legal limit of alcohol for drivers to 50 mg per 100 mL of blood from 80 mg - the level in Ontario. This change was bolstered by a forceful media campaign that increased public opposition to drinking and driving. "It's not one for the road," a campaign slogan ran. "It's none for the road."
Scotland was also the first country to introduce "alcohol brief interventions." This involves training medical professionals to have short, structured conversations with people who abuse alcohol about their habits and how to access help.
Scotland still tolerates wide alcohol availability in supermarkets and small stores, something that flies in the face of what experts say is second only to pricing on the list of most important ways to reduce alcohol harms: reducing access.
Alex Collop sees first-hand the effects of easy access on low-income families in his work for Circle, a charity that supports families affected by alcohol and drugs.
Alcohol addiction leads to lower incomes, housing insecurity, domestic abuse and violence, Collop said from Circle's cosy meeting room in the heart of Pilton, one of Edinburgh's least-privileged communities.
"It is easier to buy alcohol than healthy food here," Collop said, noting that the stores beside a nearby pub called the Ferry Boat include a gambling house, a pizza parlour and a liquor store.
In his role as an alcohol counselling manager, Bennett also advocates for making alcohol tougher to get. He found it treacherous to go shopping when he came out of a residential rehab and was trying to stay sober.
"I was going to the supermarket for essential domestic items like toilet paper or washing-up liquid. And on the other side of the aisle.... hundreds and hundreds of bottles of whiskey, vodka, gin and everything else under the sun," he said in a video posted online.
Although alcohol is readily available in Scotland, high minimum unit prices prevent retailers from competing for customers with bargains or multi-buy promotions, something that experts worry will transpire in Ontario.
Timothy Naimi, a professor at the University of Victoria and the current director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research, gives Ontario a failing grade on its plan to make alcohol more available.
"Ontario was already doing poorly" in not protecting the public from alcohol harms, Naimi said. But the government's planned tripling of retail outlets will "decimate its performance."
British Columbia research, led by Stockwell, showed that every 20 per cent increase in the number of private retail stores was associated with a three per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This is mainly because increased retail competition drives prices down, Stockwell said. He added that large chains that can buy cheaply in bulk also often use alcohol as a loss leader to bring in customers who will then pay more for food and other higher-margin items.
"It's like a double public health whammy - cheap alcohol and expensive fruit and veg."
A recent study in Finland estimated that allowing alcohol sales in grocery stores, without minimum prices, would lead to a 22 per cent increase in hospital admissions and a nine per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This could play out similarly in Ontario, where current minimum legal price limits are two and half times lower than levels that would curb consumption, according to University of Victoria research.
"It might not sound like a big step," Rice said, "but our experience is that moving alcohol into the grocery market will have many repercussions."
Scottish and Canadian experts questioned Ontario's decision to expand alcohol convenience especially while its hospitals and emergency rooms are unable to meet current demand. Ontario already has 320,000 alcohol-related hospital visits annually - a number bound to rise vastly with more retail access, according to the Canadian Alcohol Policy Evaluation project.
Leslie Buckley, chief of the addictions division at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, told the Star that she hopes Ontario will introduce alcohol brief interventions, like those in Scotland.
Ontario's direction "is also a sign that we need more health promotion around all substances, talking about the harms and making it clearer for people," Buckley said.
And as Ontario pursues its current path, Scotland's success mitigating tragedies is being recognized even in some of its oldest institutions of drink.
On a drizzly Tuesday afternoon, the Ferry Boat pub in Pilton was bustling. Four old-timers clutched pints around a wobbly table as they hooted with laughter. They said they have been meeting at the Ferry Boat almost every afternoon since the 1960s.
"We used to cut school to drink when we were nine years old," said Tommy, a retired fisherman.
"It's the working man's pastime," said David, a retired chef. "We would come in here after a shift and stay all night. Sometimes I slept here."
Even so, the regulars are not surprised to hear that fewer of their countrymen are dying of drink.
"It is too expensive now," said Alistair, 68, a retired painter. "Now I can only afford eight beers a night and I usually make it home."
CAPTION: Shelves of alcoholic drinks are displayed for sale in an Edinburgh off-licence as Scotland.In 2018 Scotland became the first country in the world to introduce minimum unit pricing for drinks.Ontario Premier Doug Ford pretends to drink from a beer can and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy holds one at an announcement saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales in May.Ontario Premier Doug Ford pretends to drink from a beer can and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy holds one at an announcement saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales, in May.
CREDIT: Katharine Lake Berz Special to the Star

Subject: Hospitals; Public health; Prices; Beer; Addictions; Alcoholism; Liquor industry; Patient admissions; Pricing policies; Drug use

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Liquor industry Pricing policies

Location: Canada; Scotland; United Kingdom--UK

Title: How Scotland started to kick its alcohol problem - and what Ontario could learn from it

Document 488 of 500

The latest casualty of Ford's policy of neglect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/latest-casualty-fords-policy-neglect/docview/3071391454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+latest+casualty+of+Ford%27s+policy+of+neglect&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A failing common to successive Ontario governments in recent decades is the unconscionable neglect that has seen provincial jewels fall into decay.
Both Ontario Place and the Ontario Science Centre - the latter closed for good on Friday after a report that said its roof was in danger of collapsing - were, in their heydays, popular venues that celebrated energy and adventure, knowledge and possibility.
That they would have to be renovated and updated over time was inevitable.
That they should have been, in the case of Ontario Place, shuttered and left to rot, and in the case of the Science Centre, poorly maintained and maligned, shames those who have led the province.
It's galling to know that governments in Ontario failed to make the necessary investments to maintain and renew prized provincial assets.
Premier Doug Ford, in particular, did nothing to reverse this decay. As the Star reported, Ontario's auditor general said in December that maintenance projects on the Science Centre were deferred and repeatedly denied funding, including 42 such projects since 2017, the year before the Ford government took power.
Presumably, Ford shed few tears at the closure, using it to justify a planned transfer of the Science Centre to Ontario Place as part of a major redevelopment on the waterfront that will include a massive, privately owned Therme spa.
Ford's proposed move has been controversial, with allegations of arbitrary decision-making that denied public consultation.
Local residents have also said the closure of the Science Centre - designed by the late Raymond Moriyama and opened in 1969 at Don Mills and Eglinton avenues - marks the loss of one of the few attractions in the area.
The latest engineering report said the concrete used in construction of the Science Centre was nearing the end of its lifespan. To upgrade the roof would cost from $22 million to $40 million and take about two years or more, it said.
So Ford let the Science Centre decay and then, when it became dangerous, immediately shut down a facility he already wanted closed.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma promptly touted the "eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility" on the waterfront.
An earlier report by Ernst & Young said the Science Centre was "in operational crisis due to a failing structure with mounting critical maintenance costs."
That report said it would cost $600 million more to repair and upgrade the existing Science Centre than to build a new one. It should never have gotten to that point.
After six years of government by Ford and his team, with continual impulsive decisions, pet projects and a cadre of too-close-by-half insiders, it's hard not to see the case of the Science Centre as emblematic of Ford's approach to infrastructure: allowing decay of prized public places and taking the opportunity his neglect created to justify new for-profit developments he wanted all along.
But much is lost along the way, as today's sad news again shows.
"For more than five decades, the Ontario Science Centre has been a beloved landmark and an integral part of our community and our province," said the centre's CEO Paul Kortenaar.
"Our building itself has been part of the experience and a cherished space for generations of visitors, sparking wonder and curiosity about science and the world around us."
But for Ford, the Science Centre appears merely to be someplace that we used to love.
If ever there was an example of knowing the price of everything and the value of nothing, Ontario's mishandling of these assets may be it.
CAPTION: Workers put up gates and fencing around the Ontario Science Centre on Friday after the province abruptly announced its closure due to safety concerns with its roof.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Science; Research centers; Renovation & restoration

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Document 489 of 500

Ford's Ontario is less yours to discover than yours to fix

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-ontario-is-less-yours-discover-than-fix/docview/3071391368/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Ontario+is+less+yours+to+discover+than+yours+to+fix&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I'd love to live in an Ontario where seniors mattered as much as booze, clean air mattered as much as private sector profit, science mattered as much as a casino and community mattered as much as political power. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where the public waterfront mattered as much as private spas, accessible streets mattered as much as licence plate stickers, autism families mattered as much as Ford fests and helping vulnerable people mattered as much as LCBO bags. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where farmland mattered as much as highways, education mattered as much as gaming, people experiencing homelessness mattered as much as good times and affordable housing mattered as much as advertising campaigns. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where small businesses mattered as much as foreign corporations, cyclists mattered as much as cars, addiction mattered as much as convenience and justice mattered as much as judgment. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where organizations that help our most vulnerable neighbours are supported, people of all backgrounds and incomes are valued equally, the rule of law is respected and the premier is trusted. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where my neighbours have equal access to health care, affordable housing, long-term care homes, emergency rooms, climate action, education and a fiscally responsible government for all. But instead, we have Ford.
Premier Doug Ford has the province speculating that an early election is in the works. He is flooding the airwaves and the online world with the expensive "It's Happening Here" advertising campaign, which we are paying for to promote an Ontario that seems unrecognizable to anyone needing health care, trying to get an affordable place to rent, or trying to own a home.
He has run those ads during the Grammys and the Super Bowl to project an image of our province that would lure investment and attract talent. But it feels a lot like gaslighting when we see the homeless encampments in our communities, the massive food lines queueing around downtown city blocks and the stress on the faces of students trying to afford post-secondary education or find a good job.
Ford has not met his housing targets and has focused on costly, unnecessary projects like a spa at Ontario Place and a Highway 413 that is unnecessary when the 407 is empty enough most days to land a plane on.
But the Greenbelt scandal, the closing of Service Ontario small business locations to put our tax dollars into an American company and paying up to three times more to private nursing agencies while our public health-care system collapses grates on our morale. His government making secret deals and using private email and phones erodes our trust.
After two majorities for his government, this is an opportunity to reflect on the kind of Ontario we would love to live in and examine the values that made us the envy and economic engine of Canada.
Does it feel like the future of our parents, ourselves and our children is headed in the right direction or veering off course into scandals and spending we can't afford?
Does it feel like we are on the right track or watching a gravy train wreck?
Does it feel like Ontario is still ours to discover, or does it feel more like Ontario is ours to fix?
Is Ontario a Place to Grow or a place to grow older worried about poverty, renoviction, private health care costs and access to long-term care?
It's up to the other three provincial parties to promote a compelling vision for Ontario.
But it's up to us to examine our values and the value of our tax dollars and decide if the next election is the one to get out the vote for change.
Laura Babcock is president of POWERGROUP Communications and host of The OShow.
CREDIT: Laura Babcock Contributor Laura Babcock is president of POWERGROUP Communications and host of The OShow.;

Subject: Small business; Long term health care; Political power; Affordable housing; Homeless people

Document 490 of 500

Ford urged to save Science Centre: Speakers at rally question province's rationale for closing popular attraction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-urged-save-science-centre/docview/3071391351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+urged+to+save+Science+Centre%3A+Speakers+at+rally+question+province%27s+rationale+for+closing+popular+attraction&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Provincial and municipal politicians of nearly all stripes vowed Sunday to fight Doug Ford's decision to abruptly shutter the Ontario Science Centre and eventually relocate it to Ontario Place.
Speaking at a rally, both Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, alongside several MPPs and city councillors, criticized the Ford government's decision and said the fight isn't over.
"The government needs to reverse this," Stiles told the Star in a short interview during the rally, which was attended by roughly 250 people at Wells Hill Park, near Bathurst Street and St. Clair Avenue West.
"I think (Ford) is underestimating what this means to people."
In a surprise decision Friday afternoon, the province announced the Science Centre was closing immediately and would not reopen at its current Don Mills location, following an engineering report that found structural issues with the building's roof.
The report said the facility was safe at least until the end of October, but a heavy snow or rain could increase the risk of a sudden collapse of a small percentage of roof panels.
"Any risk of collapse is too much," a senior government official told the Star's Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson on Friday.
Stiles accused the Progressive Conservative government on Sunday of "concocting an excuse" to shut down the popular tourist attraction in order to hasten its controversial relocation.
"Something smells bad here," she said.
"I believe this is just the latest in a long list of scandals this government has been involved in and, just like with the Greenbelt, it has mobilized people who are concerned," Stiles added.
The Ford government did not respond to a question about Stiles's comments.
A government spokesperson said the decision to close the facility - recommended by the Ministry of Infrastructure and the Science Centre's chief executive - was made to protect the health and safety of staff and visitors, while giving staff enough time to safely preserve and store exhibits.
In a statement announcing Friday's decision, the province said it would be releasing a request for proposals to identify a temporary location for the Science Centre while "work continues" to build a permanent home at Ontario Place. They estimate the new location could reopen "as early as 2028."
The Ford government announced plans to move the Science Centre to Ontario Place last year, drawing sharp criticism from city councillors and opposition MPPs.
They later released a report that found the iconic building, opened in 1969 and designed by the late Raymond Moriyama, was nearing the end of its useful life and relocating it would save more than $250 million over the next 50 years.
Several speakers at Sunday's rally questioned the rationale for Friday's decision, and blamed the Ford government for the Science Centre falling into disrepair.
"The reason he's doing this is to justify the $600-million parking lot (and) the Therme spa that we'll be building on public land," Crombie said during the rally, referring to the province's Ontario Place redevelopment plans that feature a private, water-themed spa.
Among the crowd at Sunday's rally was Saren Singham, 38, with his two-year-old son, Amaren.
Singham said he and his partner have been taking Amaren to the Science Centre on a monthly basis.
"He loves it," Singham said, gesturing to his son.
"It seems like such a missed opportunity not to fix it."
Singham said the Science Centre's current location at Don Mills and Eglinton is "so important" for families like his that live in the area and can't easily travel downtown. It's also one of the few attractions in the city suitable for children of all ages, he said.
"Especially at this age, they're so curious. To get them interested in the sciences is so special."
He also worries about how long it will take to reopen and the years his son won't be able to visit.
"I just hope there's enough community support to put pressure on the government to change their mind."
CREDIT: Brendan Kennedy Toronto Star

Document 491 of 500

Two things stopping waterfront from being as good as it could be

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-things-stopping-waterfront-being-as-good/docview/3071391323/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+things+stopping+waterfront+from+being+as+good+as+it+could+be&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's waterfront suffers from two particular afflictions. One is nostalgia, the other is a striving, sometimes pathetic, desire to be world class.
Nostalgia manifests in a variety of ways. Memories and heritage are good things, but nostalgia can be a toxic lens, one that alters reality and disconnects from history itself.
The Toronto Island ferries are an example.
The ride to the island on a perfect day is magic. I've been twice on recent weekdays. Lines were relatively short, and getting there and back was painless - the romantic Toronto Islands visit ideal.
All it takes is one perfect trip to create a permanent memory and nostalgic image. The problem is this experience has been increasingly rare for years now, especially on weekend summer days.
Nostalgia obscures the fact that for many people who can only go to the island on weekends the lines, the shuffling, waiting for multiple ferries before getting on while packed in pen-like conditions, as well as other indignities, destroys the romance.
These island-bound people might not have a cottage (that's most people, despite how powerful the Ontario cottage mythology is), and might also be people who are apartment dwellers without a backyard of their own (like hundreds of thousands of people in Toronto).
The island is a critical part of summer, but their romantic memories are ruined because of someone else's nostalgia.
Nostalgia for a pleasant and reliable ferry ride that hasn't existed in a long time means thinking seriously about updating the ferries hasn't been taken seriously.
Star columnist Matt Elliot explained last week how the city told people not to visit their island last weekend, and how dismal and delayed the saga of procuring new ferries has gone.
It's simply not been a priority for generations of civic leadership, people who often have had their own backyards or even cottages, where a visit to the island is occasional at best and not part of regular summer life.
Two years ago, when there was much back-patting for procuring an all-electric ferry rather than a dirtier electric-diesel ferry, I argued the design is still a problem.
The new ferry plans are nostalgic and old-timey, with the same pinch points that make getting crowds on and off excruciatingly slow.
I beg civic officials to visit Amsterdam, where fast passenger ferries load, unload and scoot across the water quickly, or even see how fast ferries in Halifax or Vancouver work.
It's also been nearly a decade since the winning design was selected to rebuild the veal pen ferry docks, but nothing has happened since.
Nostalgia creates an image that everything is fine, so it isn't a priority.
Nostalgia means that advocacy for a pedestrian and cycling bridge to the island is mocked and laughed at, or worse, villainized as something that might destroy the island.
The only thing a bridge will destroy is the island's exclusivity, but finally small steps toward a pedestrian and cycling connection are being made.
Next week, city council will debate a motion directing city staff to report on the cost and feasibility of a fixed link to the island.
I often hear people say they don't want a bridge or even better ferry service because it will bring more people to the island and ruin it.
Misanthropy aside, as we learned during the pandemic, public recreational space in Ontario has not grown with the population.
All of what we have is precious, and needs to be far more accessible and expanded.
This summer, Toronto's other affliction, world class striving, has further reduced the amount of public space in Toronto.
Preparation for the Therme Spa, a project that will privatize a large chunk of Ontario Place, has closed the popular beach there.
The process of choosing Therme has been so opaque, despite being on public land and using considerable public money, that it's hard to tell why, exactly, Doug Ford's government is working so hard to bring it to Toronto's waterfront.
However, one underlying motivation is surely to have a "world class" attraction on Toronto's waterfront.
It's a familiar, almost desperate feeling that makes Toronto seem like Springfield in "The Simpsons" episode where a carpetbagger tries to sell the town a monorail.
As with the Sidewalk Labs saga, a portion of Toronto's establishment plays willing boosters, though often they're involved financially, as with urbanist Richard Florida's recent report supporting the spa project, a report funded by Therme itself though the publisher behind it states it maintained editorial independence.
Florida's report mentions the Tate museum in London as an example of what Toronto needs, but Toronto's Tate would be a museum created in the defunct Hearn generating plant in the Port Lands, not a lousy private spa.
Even Doug Ford's old dream of a Ferris wheel would be a far better addition to Ontario Place than the spa.
The quality public waterfront Toronto created at Love Park and Sugar Beach, both by late Montreal designer Claude Cormier, has resulted in instant, popular, landmarks.
Neither nostalgia or world classism puts Torontonians' quality of life first, but that should be the guiding principle on our waterfront.
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Nostalgia; Design; Ferries

Location: Florida; United States--US

Document 492 of 500

Key test for Liberals in byelection: Toronto-St. Paul's vote today could reveal cracks in Trudeau's stronghold

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/key-test-liberals-byelection/docview/3071391165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Key+test+for+Liberals+in+byelection%3A+Toronto-St.+Paul%27s+vote+today+could+reveal+cracks+in+Trudeau%27s+stronghold&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Decked out in door-knocking shorts and sneakers, and buzzing with the energy of someone who can see the finish line, Leslie Church sounded like a professional athlete spouting clichés before a big game.
"Not taking anything for granted," she said Saturday at a farmers market following a lightly attended campaign event featuring candidates for the upcoming Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection. "We're working really hard, right up until the polls close."
Church is the Liberal candidate in the midtown riding, and the perceived front-runner in the high-stakes contest to replace former cabinet minister Carolyn Bennett.
Voters will be heading to the polls Monday in a race that has drawn significant national attention. It was once considered the safest of Liberal seats, but some political watchers believe Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, along with local candidate Don Stewart, have a chance to flip it.
If they do, it would be the first crack in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Toronto fortress and a potential harbinger of his party's fortunes in the next general election.
Speaking to the Star on Saturday, Church said she isn't thinking about any broader implications of Monday's vote.
"I think that people always try to read the tea leaves in byelections, but the reality is we're filling a vacancy with someone who you want to represent you in Parliament," she said.
The riding, which stretches roughly from Dufferin Street to Mount Pleasant Road, between Eglinton Avenue and Dupont Street, has been in Liberal hands for more than 30 years. Bennett, who was elected nine consecutive times, won it by more than 20 percentage points in 2021.
But, given Trudeau's plummeting popularity and Poilievre's commanding lead in national polls, some Liberals fear Toronto-St. Paul's could slip from their grasp.
The latest polling figures compiled by Abacus Data for the Star show Conservative support at 36 per cent nationally, compared to the Liberals' 19 per cent - the party's lowest share of committed voters since 2015.
Church, a longtime Liberal staffer, downplayed the suggestion that the election could be a bellwether for the national race. "This is just about, who do you trust to be the strongest voice to deliver on your priorities?"
At Saturday's event, Church mostly spoke about her priorities - housing affordability, investing in health care and the Liberals' school food program - but she also took an opportunity to issue a warning about what a Poilievre government might look like.
In response to a question about the province's decision to abruptly close the Ontario Science Centre, Church said that, if you disagree with Doug Ford's government, you should be "equally if not more concerned" about Poilievre.
"He said he's going to shut down the CBC, he's going to defund our arts and culture," she told the small crowd.
"So the Science Centre will be the beginning," she added.
Stewart, the Conservative candidate in the race, did not attend Saturday's event and has kept a low media profile throughout the campaign. He declined the Star's interview request at his campaign office on Saturday, as he has previous interview requests.
Joining Church and Stewart on the ballot are 82 other candidates - the most ever, according to Elections Canada. They include NDP candidate Amrit Parhar, the Green Party's Christian Cullis and Dennis Wilson of the People's Party of Canada - who all attended Saturday's campaign event in June Rowlands Park - as well as dozens of people who signed up as part of the Longest Ballot Committee, which is trying to draw attention to Trudeau's broken promise on electoral reform.
Last week, Elections Canada estimated nearly 10,800 people cast a ballot in the advance polls.
Sara Spencer, an undecided voter who attended Saturday's event, said that, while she understood the national implications of Monday's vote, she still saw it as a local race, not a contest between party leaders.
"I have to vote for the person, the person that sees what I see," she said.
A self-described political junkie, Spencer said she was a longtime supporter of Bennett, but she has grown frustrated with Trudeau's leadership. After hearing some of the candidates speak on Saturday, she said she felt a little clearer about who she would vote for. She wouldn't divulge which way she was leaning, but she said she would be shocked if the longtime Liberal riding went Conservative.
"Then again, you never know."
CREDIT: Brendan Kennedy Toronto Star

Subject: Electoral reform; Candidates; Voters; Elections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/something-smells-bad-here-politicians-leaders/docview/3071593705/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 494 of 500

Toronto-St. Paul's voters head to the polls Monday in a byelection being seen as a test for Trudeau's Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-st-pauls-voters-head-polls-monday/docview/3071593621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto-St.+Paul%27s+voters+head+to+the+polls+Monday+in+a+byelection+being+seen+as+a+test+for+Trudeau%27s+Liberals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Decked out in door-knocking shorts and sneakers, and buzzing with the energy of someone who can see the finish line, Leslie Church sounded like a professional athlete spouting clichés before a big game.
"Not taking anything for granted," she said Saturday at a farmers' market following a lightly attended campaign event featuring candidates for the upcoming Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection. "We're working really hard, right up until the polls close."
Church is the Liberal candidate in the midtown riding, and the perceived front-runner in the high-stakes contest to replace former cabinet minister Carolyn Bennett.
Voters will be heading to the polls Monday in a race that has drawn significant national attention. Once considered the safest of Liberal seats, some political watchers believe Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, along with local candidate Don Stewart, have a chance to flip it.
If they do, it would be the first cracks in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's fortress in Toronto and a potential harbinger of what may be on the horizon for his party in the next general election.
Speaking to the Star on Saturday, Church said she isn't thinking about any broader implications of Monday's vote.
"I think that people always try to read the tea leaves in byelections, but the reality is we're filling a vacancy with someone who you want to represent you in Parliament," she said.
The riding, which stretches roughly from Dufferin Street to Mount Pleasant Road, between Eglinton Avenue and Dupont Street, has been in Liberal hands for more than 30 years. Bennett, who was elected nine consecutive times, won it by more than 20 percentage points in 2021.
But given Trudeau's plummeting popularity and Poilievre's commanding lead in national polls some Liberals fear Toronto-St. Paul's could slip from their grasp.
The latest polling data compiled by Abacus Data for the Star show Conservative support at 36 per cent nationally, compared to the Liberals' 19 per cent - the party's lowest share of committed voters since 2015.
Church, a longtime Liberal staffer, downplayed the suggestion that the election could be a bellwether for the national race.
"This is just about, who do you trust to be the strongest voice to deliver on your priorities?"
At Saturday's event, Church mostly spoke about her priorities - housing affordability, investing in health care and the Liberals' school food program - but she also took an opportunity to issue a warning about what a Poilievre government might look like.
In response to a question about the province's decision to abruptly close the Ontario Science Centre, Church said that if you disagree with Doug Ford's government, you should be "equally if not more concerned" about Poilievre.
"He said he's going to shut down the CBC, he's going to defund our arts and culture," she told the small crowd. "So the Science Centre will be the beginning."
Stewart, the Conservative candidate in the race, did not attend Saturday's event and has kept a low media profile throughout the campaign. He declined the Star's interview request at his campaign office on Saturday, as he has previous interview requests.
Joining Church and Stewart on the ballot are 82 other candidates - the most ever, according to Elections Canada. They include NDP candidate Amrit Parhar, the Green Party's Christian Cullis and People's Party of Canada's Dennis Wilson - who all attended Saturday's campaign event in June Rowlands Park - as well as dozens of people who signed up as part of the Longest Ballot Committee, which is trying to draw attention to Trudeau's broken promise on electoral reform.
Last week, Elections Canada estimated nearly 10,800 people cast a ballot in the advance polls.
Sara Spencer, an undecided voter who attended Saturday's event, said that while she understood the national implications of Monday's vote, she still saw it as a local race, not a contest between party leaders.
"I have to vote for the person, the person that sees what I see," she said.
A self-described political junkie, Spencer said she was a longtime supporter of Bennett, but she has grown frustrated with Trudeau's leadership. After hearing some of the candidates speak on Saturday she said she felt a little clearer about who she would vote for. She wouldn't divulge which way she was leaning, but she said she would be shocked if the longtime Liberal riding went Conservative.
"Then again, you never know."
CAPTION: The Appletree Group held an all-candidates "meet and greet" on Saturday for the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection. Voters head to the polls on Monday.Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau, centre, speaks to supporters at a campaign volunteer event for Liberal candidate for Toronto-St. Paul's, Leslie Church, in Toronto on Thursday, May 30, 2024.Three of the 84 candidates in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection, from left, Liberal Leslie Church, Conservative Don Stewart and New Democrat Amrit Parhar.Toronto-St. Paul's Liberal candidate Leslie Church speaks at June Rowlands Park in Toronto on Saturday.
