Dental groups reject Liberal claim that Conservatives are fuelling opposition to new plan - instead claiming it's Ottawa spreading misinformation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dental-groups-reject-liberal-claim-that/docview/3071280970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dental+groups+reject+Liberal+claim+that+Conservatives+are+fuelling+opposition+to+new+plan+-+instead+claiming+it%27s+Ottawa+spreading+misinformation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The heads of several dental groups are rejecting a Liberal minister's framing that the federal Conservatives are to blame for oral health providers' opposition to aspects of Ottawa's dental care plan.The dismissal of Health Minister Mark Holland's comments, which were made earlier this week, are the latest source of friction between Canada's dental associations and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government over the rollout of what's been deemed a "historic" dental coverage program.Several dental association presidents told the Star that the Conservative party has nothing to do with their outstanding questions over implementing the plan, while Liberal and NDP messaging around the program do. "There has been zero pressure, really, from the Conservatives on us in any kind of fashion that would result in the low uptake of dentists registering for the program," said Dr. Robert Wolanski, president of the British Columbia Dental Association."The misinformation that has been provided has been provided almost solely by ... the Liberal government and the NDP," Wolanski said, of a program that was born out of the minority Liberals' parliamentary alliance with the New Democrats. During a news conference on Tuesday, Holland suggested that the country's dental associations were "actively seeking" to oppose the plan because the Conservative party was against its success and was "calling these dental associations and screaming at them and getting angry at them." "If you're a conservative party, (and) you want to run against taking dental care away, fine. But calling around and bullying and creating all of this negativity, you know, I think that's what the underlying force is," Holland said.Both the Canadian Dental Association (CDA) and the Conservative party have denied the minister's claims, which Holland says are rooted in fears that Pierre Poilievre would be at a political disadvantage should the program be viewed as a success. Wolanski said provincial and territorial dental associations have had "very little" contact with the federal Conservatives over the past two years, and that their health critic, Stephen Ellis, only reached out to them in April to have conversations Wolanski characterized as "inquisitive, open and respectful."The meetings mostly saw Ellis ask questions about how the program works and dentists' concerns, Wolanski said.While dental groups support the goals of the program - which is open to seniors and will expand to include eligible adults with disabilities and children under the age of 18 on June 27 - they say they still have questions about language in the plan's agreements, patient privacy and the claims process, among other concerns.They've also taken issue with messaging that suggests the program is entirely free and offered to any Canadian for any service at no cost. Holland has said he's never misguided Canadians about how the program actually works, and told the Star earlier this month that after many talks with dental groups and attempts to rectify their concerns, he no longer understands what their issues are.The minister said Tuesday that more than 200,000 seniors have accessed care under the plan and that "a little over 40 per cent" of oral health providers have signed up to deliver the program.But it is the politicization of the issue, Wolanski said, that's a new cause for concern."The politics, to be honest, have been frustrating for us because that's not how ... our minds work. We're about patients, not politics," he said."There were claims of bullying and things like that. I've spoken to every association president across the country, and none of us have experienced that."Dr. Jenny Doerksen, head of the Alberta Dental Association, said Holland is simply engaging in "campaign tactics." "We are not here to be part of this political game that they're playing," she said. The new president of the Ontario Dental Association, Dr. Maneesh Jain, told the Star that dental groups meet with parties of all political stripes. He said Holland's comments took him "by surprise" given the positive conversations he's had with the minister's office. "We've had good discussions. I think the minister has been listening," Jain said.In a statement to the Star, Holland's office said the minister has "heard some comments that aren't fact-based opposition to the program, but more so reflect partisan objections.""We need oral health care providers to get on board, this is an important program that will help nine million Canadians. Conservative MPs continue to peddle misinformation about our plan and vote against it at every opportunity," the statement read.NDP health critic Peter Julian would not directly address Holland's comments when asked by the Star if he had heard similar reports of partisan tactics, but said it's "unfortunate" Conservatives continue to sow "disinformation" about the program in the House of Commons and to their constituents.But it's largely messaging that isn't coming from Conservative quarters about the program's coverage that's prompted the CDA to launch a national campaign educating Canadians about the realities of the program: in most cases, co-payments are required, coverage doesn't start right away, additional charges may apply, and not all services are covered. Wolanski pointed to an April news conference in Victoria, in which Trudeau touted the number of seniors who had registered for the program to receive "free dental care." "We have produced papers for all of our dental offices to help them navigate this process. And one of them includes a new letter that says that the ... abuse of our dental team will not be tolerated," Wolanski said, of the reaction staff receive when patients learn the caveats of the program."We've never had to do that in my 36-year career. Now we do."
CAPTION: Federal Health Minister Mark Holland responds to a question during a news conference in Ottawa on June 18, 2024.

Document 496 of 500

There are two things stopping Toronto's waterfront from being as good as it could be

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-are-two-things-stopping-torontos-waterfront/docview/3071280932/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+are+two+things+stopping+Toronto%27s+waterfront+from+being+as+good+as+it+could+be&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's waterfront suffers from two particular afflictions. One is nostalgia, the other is a striving, sometimes pathetic, desire to be world class.
Nostalgia manifests in a variety of ways. Memories and heritage are good things, but nostalgia can be a toxic lens, one that alters reality and disconnects from history itself.
The Toronto Island ferries are an example. The ride to the island on a perfect day is magic. I've been twice on recent weekdays. Lines were relatively short, and getting there and back was painless - the romantic Toronto Islands visit ideal.
All it takes is one perfect trip to create a permanent memory and nostalgic image. The problem is this experience has been increasingly rare for years now, especially on weekend summer days.
Nostalgia obscures the fact that for many people who can only go to the island on weekends the lines, the shuffling, waiting for multiple ferries before getting on while packed in pen-like conditions, as well as other indignities, destroys the romance.
These island-bound people might not have a cottage (that's most people, despite how powerful the Ontario cottage mythology is), and might also be people who are apartment dwellers without a backyard of their own (like hundreds of thousands of people in Toronto).
The island is a critical part of summer, but their romantic memories are ruined because of someone else's nostalgia.
Nostalgia for a pleasant and reliable ferry ride that hasn't existed in a long time means thinking seriously about updating the ferries hasn't been taken seriously. Earlier this week Star columnist Matt Elliot explained how the city told people not to visit their island last weekend, and how dismal and delayed the saga of procuring new ferries has gone. It's simply not been a priority for generations of civic leadership, people who often have had their own backyards or even cottages, where a visit to the island is occasional at best and not part of regular summer life.
Two years ago, when there was much back-patting for procuring an all-electric ferry rather than a dirtier electric-diesel ferry, I argued the design is still a problem. The new ferry plans are nostalgic and old-timey, with the same pinch points that make getting crowds on and off excruciatingly slow. I beg civic officials to visit Amsterdam, where fast passenger ferries load, unload and scoot across the water quickly, or even see how fast ferries in Halifax or Vancouver work.
It's also been nearly a decade since the winning design was selected to rebuild the veal pen ferry docks, but nothing has happened since. Nostalgia creates an image that everything is fine, so it isn't a priority.
Nostalgia means that advocacy for a pedestrian and cycling bridge to the island is mocked and laughed at, or worse, villainized as something that might destroy the island. The only thing a bridge will destroy is the island's exclusivity, but finally small steps toward a pedestrian and cycling connection are being made. Next week city council will debate a motion directing city staff to report on the cost and feasibility of a fixed link to the island.
I often hear people say they don't want a bridge or even better ferry service because it will bring more people to the island and ruin it. Misanthropy aside, as we learned during the pandemic, public recreational space in Ontario has not grown with the population. All of what we have is precious, and needs to be far more accessible and expanded.
This summer, Toronto's other affliction, world class striving, has further reduced the amount of public space in Toronto. Preparation for the Therme Spa, a project that will privatize a large chunk of Ontario Place, has closed the popular beach there.
The process of choosing Therme has been so opaque, despite being on public land and using considerable public money, that it's hard to tell why, exactly, Doug Ford's government is working so hard to bring it to Toronto's waterfront.
However, one underlying motivation is surely to have a "world class" attraction on Toronto's waterfront.
It's a familiar, almost desperate-feeling that makes Toronto seem like Springfield in "The Simpsons" episode where a carpetbagger tries to sell the town a monorail. As with the Sidewalk Labs saga, a portion of Toronto's establishment plays willing boosters, though often they're involved financially, as with urbanist Richard Florida's recent report supporting the spa project, a report funded by Therme itself though the publisher behind it states it maintained editorial independence.
Florida's report mentions the Tate museum in London as an example of what Toronto needs, but Toronto's Tate would be a museum created in the defunct Hearn generating plant in the Port Lands, not a lousy private spa. Even Doug Ford's old dream of a Ferris wheel would be a far better addition to Ontario Place than the spa.
The quality public waterfront Toronto created at Love Park and Sugar Beach, both by late Montreal designer Claude Cormier, has resulted in instant, popular, landmarks.
Neither nostalgia or world classism puts Torontonians' quality of life first, but that should be the guiding principle on our waterfront.
CAPTION: The ride to the island on a perfect day is magic,but thathas been increasingly rare for years now, especially on weekend summer days.

Tories silent on safe injection sites: Poilievre supports treatment and recovery programs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-silent-on-safe-injection-sites/docview/3071193810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+silent+on+safe+injection+sites%3A+Poilievre+supports+treatment+and+recovery+programs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As debate around the Liberals' drug policy dominated parts of the spring House of Commons sitting, the Conservatives are offering little insight into what approach they would take when it comes to supervised consumption sites.
When asked earlier this month if a future Tory government would look to change the application process for opening such centres, the party's addictions critic said she couldn't speculate.
"But I do think that this has been politicized into a space that is pitting one perspective versus another and that's not helpful," Laila Goodridge said.
At supervised consumption sites, users can inject or inhale drugs under the watch of staff who can intervene in the case of an overdose. The centres also often offer drug testing, clean supplies to prevent the spread of disease and referrals to detox or treatment facilities.
Poilievre has been clear about his opposition to other harm-reduction strategies that seek to mitigate the opioid overdose crisis.
Those include decriminalization efforts to help keep users out of jail and "safer supply" programs that provide pharmaceutical alternatives to toxic street drugs.
Instead, he wants to offer people "a path to a drug-free life" by emphasizing treatment and recovery. In a 2022 opinion piece, Poilievre also promised to create a national distribution program for nasal naloxone to help reverse overdoses.
But what about supervised consumption sites? Asked for the party's position, Goodridge pointed out that the Supreme Court of Canada has already ruled on the matter. "I would recommend that you read that," she said.
In a landmark 2011 decision, the court unanimously ruled that closing the doors of Canada's first supervised drug injection site would deprive users of their Charter rights.
The decision kept Insite in operation despite opposition from the then-Conservative government, and the health minister was required to grant an exemption to federal drug laws.
Poilievre's office declined to say whether he supports supervised consumption sites or would include them as part of his approach to tackling the toxic drug crisis.
It also wouldn't respond to a question about a reported statement by Poilievre at a town hall in northern Ontario in January. The Sault Star reported that he told the crowd he wouldn't be prepared to fund supervised consumption sites.
And Poilievre's office also didn't answer whether he would review any current operations or change the requirements for operators.
"It sounds like they're trying to have it both ways, which you can't do," said University of British Columbia law professor Benjamin Perrin, once an adviser to Harper.
"You either support them or you don't."
Four years after the Supreme Court decision that prevented it from shutting down Insite, the Harper government passed the Respect for Communities Act. It required prospective supervised consumption sites to meet a suite of criteria in order to operate, such as tracking crime rates and consulting with local residents and police.
After they were elected, the Liberals passed their own law allowing facilities to open with more ease. There are now 39 supervised consumption sites, according to Health Canada, and another 10 open applications.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Stephanie Taylor The Canadian Press

Provinces bolster relationships with their U.S. trading partners: Officials reach out to both Democrats and Republicans ahead of close race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-bolster-relationships-with-their-u-s/docview/3071193762/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provinces+bolster+relationships+with+their+U.S.+trading+partners%3A+Officials+reach+out+to+both+Democrats+and+Republicans+ahead+of+close+race&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Provinces are reaching out to Democrats and Republicans alike to make their case for co-operation, bolstering efforts by the federal government to ensure Canada is prepared for any outcome in the upcoming United States election.
"I see provinces and Team Canada both ramping up their efforts," said Laura Dawson, an expert on Canada-U.S. relations and executive director of the Future Borders Coalition.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, whose Liberals came to power in October 2015, was criticized for being unprepared for the first Donald Trump presidency after the 2016 U.S. election.
Their relationship was notably rocky throughout the Republican leader's four-year tenure.
The Liberal government is taking a more proactive approach this time around to bolster connections with both the Trump and Joe Biden camps as the tight presidential race continues.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne is making regular visits south of the border as part of the Team Canada engagement strategy. Canada's ambassador to the U.S., Kirsten Hillman, has been pounding the pavement from state to state along with business leaders and stakeholder groups.
Dawson said federal-level representatives are going to their American counterparts with topline statements about how "we build things together."
Provinces, on the other hand, drill down on specifics in areas like hydroelectric power, agriculture issues, integrated supply chains in a particular sector and joint investments at the local and regional level.
"I think Canada really relies on the provincial representatives to get specific, to identify the areas where Americans and Canadians are working together," she said.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew, accompanied by former premier and former Canadian ambassador to the United States Gary Doer, recently travelled to Washington, D.C., and New York City.
"The purpose of our trip was really to build relationships on both sides of the aisle," Kinew said.
Top of mind for Canadians shaking hands with American counterparts is the looming review of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement in 2026.
Both presidential candidates are selling protectionist policies that could cause uncertainty for Canadian trade.
During his presidency, Trump forced the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement and his administration brought in billions of dollars worth of tariffs, particularly on Chinese imports.
Biden largely kept those tariffs in place, despite promises to reverse them. There's also been tension over the Biden administration's Buy American procurement rules.
Kinew said his team wants to ensure it's understood that trade with Manitoba is good for the U.S. and its citizens.
"If we make that self-interest argument to an American audience, that's a very good foundation for us to have continued prosperity on both sides of the border," he said.
It's essential to maintain a constructive working relationship, said James Rajotte, Alberta's senior representative to the U.S.
"The quality of life of people who live in Alberta is obviously very directly linked to policy decisions here and the trading relationship we have," Rajotte said.
When navigating conversations with his American counterparts, Rajotte said, "you have to read your audience."
In some talks, he might lead the conversation with Alberta's record on reducing emissions. In others, Rajotte may focus on the role Canada and Alberta can play in energy reliability and security.
"On a lot of trade issues, the Trump administration and the Biden administration are not really that far apart," he said.
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey also travelled to Washington recently. His office said it was important to show that the relationship "is one characterized by complementary and not competitive interests."
A key selling point for many Canadians is the country's critical mineral supply.
"This is one area where even in the midst of all the election season heating up, there's some bipartisan appeal," Kinew said.
The Democrats want critical minerals to advance electrification and the climate agenda, Kinew added, and Republicans want it for defence applications and general economic development.
"We are now seeing great interest in our Ontario critical minerals and nuclear power advantages among our partners in the United States," David Paterson, the province's representative in Washington, said in an emailed statement.
Dawson said Canadians need to focus on a key word that resonates with Americans: security. That can mean energy security, financial security or even Arctic security, she said.
"Any (phrase) that has 'security' in it has much more attention than just 'we build things together.'"
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: U.S. President Joe Biden and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in Ottawa in 2023. The Liberal governmentis taking a proactive approach to strengthen connections with both the Biden and Donald Trump camps as the tight presidential race continues.Mandel NganAFP viaTgetty images
CREDIT: Kelly Geraldine Malone The Canadian Press

Subject: Provinces; Strategic materials; Canadians; Presidential elections; Prime ministers; Tariffs; Canada-US relations

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: Manitoba Canada; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Biden, Joseph R Jr; Trudeau, Justin

Nenshi ready to 'rebuild' Alberta: Former Calgary mayor named new provincial NDP leader in his first foray into partisan politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nenshi-ready-rebuild-alberta/docview/3071193706/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nenshi+ready+to+%27rebuild%27+Alberta%3A+Former+Calgary+mayor+named+new+provincial+NDP+leader+in+his+first+foray+into+partisan+politics&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Naheed Nenshi has officially been named the new leader of the Alberta NDP party, concluding a closely-watched competition to replace Rachel Notley and putting the outspoken former mayor of Calgary on a collision course with Danielle Smith.
"I was increasingly troubled by this indulgent provincial government, but also being told by so many people, 'the stakes are too high,'" he told a crowd of orange-clad party faithful in a downtown Calgary ballroom on Saturday. The race for leader was Nenshi's first foray into partisan politics, during which he was often seen as a high-profile outsider.
"I realized I do have a political home, and my political home is here in the Alberta NDP."
But Nenshi, who has been largely consulting since finishing up as mayor in 2021, won by a landslide in a seemingly clear endorsement of what he has described as a "pragmatic" vision for progressive politics in the Prairies. His high-profile entry into the race helped swell the province's NDP party membership fivefold, past 85,000, most of whom turned out to vote.
Nenshi got 86 per cent of the vote, which is more than Smith got in her own leadership race in 2022 and more than 10 times that of the second place finisher, former justice minister Kathleen Ganley.
The other contenders were former health minister Sarah Hoffman, and sitting MLA Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse.
This was the provincial party's first leadership competition since it's metamorphosis from a handful of MLAs to a political force elected to government in 2015, ending the Progressive Conservatives' four-decade run.
The hotly contested race spurred debate about what it means to be NDP, whether or not to cut ties with the federal party, as well as to how best to take on Smith.
The spectre of Smith loomed large in Nenshi's victory speech. "We're going against a machine that, unlike us, is not based on values and ethics and morals, and what is best for the province," he said in his speech, painting a picture of the road to the election, not expected until 2027.
"The UCP wants to be small," he said. "They see Alberta as a fortress to be defended. But what Alberta has always been is a wide-open door with a welcome mat, inviting the best people and the best ideas from every corner."
Nenshi has made no secret about his concerns with Smith, his former college classmate, calling her an "existential threat" to Alberta. He's been sharply critical of the way in which her party has veered more sharply to the right, and policies that include restricting health care for transgender youth, a moratorium on new renewable energy projects and allowing provincial politicians to weigh in on municipal matters.
Speaking to media after his win, Nenshi invoked the example of former premier Jason Kenney who, after defeating the NDP in 2019, embarked on what he called a "summer of appeal," in an attempt to undo as much left-leaning policy as possible.
At the time, he thought Kenney's moves were "sort of tacky and not very kind and not very gracious," Nenshi said.
But now, he said if he's elected premier: "Here's the thing. I'm having trouble thinking about what we won't repeal."
"Because we've got to rebuild the Alberta that is systematically being destroyed."
But Nenshi has also sparked questions about his commitment to the NDP brand, as a fledgling member of the party who will now be expected to lead its 38 sitting MLAs. Towards the end of his speech, he invited each of the women who ran against him to join him on stage, greeting each with a hug.
"We may have taken different paths in this leadership race, but we are a completely unified team."
Nenshi's victory means the party will say goodbye to Notley as leader, who received a standing ovation from the crowd and briefly took the stage as the final results were announced.
The daughter of Grant Notley, a previous long-time provincial NDP leader, she led the party to its historic win nine years ago and while in office raised the province's minimum wage, cut child poverty, boosted labour standards and introduced more accessible child-care options.
But after two consecutive losses to the United Conservatives, she announced in January that she would step down.
In her last speech as leader on Friday, Notley encouraged cheering delegates to be ready to rally around whoever replaces her and to fight to win again in 2027.
"So many of us made immense sacrifices to do this work, and today we live in an Alberta that is better for it," Notley said in her final address as leader at Calgary's BMO Centre.
"We can say together: it was worth it."
With files from
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Outgoing AlbertaNDP leader Rachel Notley congratulates Naheed Nenshi after he was named the provincial party's new leader in Calgary on Saturday. Nenshi won86 per cent of the vote.Jeff McIntoshTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Alex Boyd Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

No matter how Toronto byelection shakes out, every scenario is some kind of bad for Trudeau's Liberals: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-matter-how-toronto-byelection-shakes-out-every/docview/3071193697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+matter+how+Toronto+byelection+shakes+out%2C+every+scenario+is+some+kind+of+bad+for+Trudeau%27s+Liberals%3A+In+a+recurring+feature%2C+Susan+Delacourt%2C+a+small-l+liberal%2C+and+Matt+Gurney%2C+a+small-c+conservative%2C+bring+their+different+perspectives+-+and+shared+commitment+to+civil+disagreement+-+to+the+political+debates+of+the+moment.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Matt Gurney: There's a byelection in the Toronto-St. Paul's riding on Monday - typically a pretty safe red seat. I'll start with a couple of confessions. First, I normally hate talking about byelections. I think they're usually stories we just use to fill airtime and column inches and don't really mean much. You can draw conclusions from 338 simultaneous elections - more, now, I guess - but not much from one at a time. But I think this one matters because there's no good-news scenario for the Liberals here. They either lose a seat they shouldn't ever lose, and that's bad, or they barely hold a seat they shouldn't ever lose, and that's bad. Or they win it, and everyone is shocked and relieved, and that's a kind of bad, too. So I'd welcome your thoughts on that. But also, confession two. I'm taking time off next week, and I'm not going to be around to say much about whatever happens. I'll just read your reaction.
Susan Delacourt: I spent some time in Toronto-St. Paul's earlier this month and my sense was that it was looking pretty bad for the Liberals. Lawn signs may not be an accurate predictor, but on the streets where I was roaming, the blue ones far outnumbered the red ones. There is no sugar-coating that. I talked to someone who had been knocking on doors, who reported many people saying that Justin Trudeau had to go. My colleague Tonda MacCharles did her own reconnaissance and aptly painted that bleak picture in a story last weekend. One thing that surprised me in that story was the Conservative strategist who said they still expected to lose Toronto-St. Paul's. That sounded like spin to me, laying the groundwork for Conservatives to claim in victory that Pierre Poilievre had somehow defied the odds. Last time I checked, the odds were massively in favour of a Conservative win in Toronto-St. Paul's. They're 20 per cent ahead in the latest Abacus poll, for heaven's sake. I'm not buying the suggestion that they're the underdogs in this byelection.
MG: That's interesting. I honestly don't have a prediction. I read the polls so, yeah, I get it. But I always assume that in Canadian politics, the least interesting thing is usually the most likely thing. And the least interesting thing, I think, would be the Liberals hanging on to Toronto-St. Paul's by any margin. "Red seat stays red, film at 11." But I have friends in that riding and they agree entirely with your view and Tonda's. As for Conservatives I've talked to, I think caution has been morphing gradually into an expectation that yeah, they'll win. And that in turns leads to a realization they're going to have to defend the damn riding in a year or so.
SD: I am not making any predictions either. It's perhaps a reflection of how long I've been in this crazy business, but I don't see Toronto-St. Paul's as a Liberal stronghold. It was a Carolyn Bennett stronghold; she owned that constituency from the moment she decided to give up her job as a popular family physician and jump into politics in 1997. But I also remember it as Barbara McDougall's riding in the 1980s, a solid blue Progressive Conservative bastion in Brian Mulroney's era. I'm intrigued by the caution you heard from Conservatives. It tells me Poilievre's team is far more attached to the idea of playing offence than defence in the next year.
MG: There's a whole crop of homeowning (if they're wealthy!), job-holding, kid-having voters who have only seen a Liberal hold that riding, and it's in a pretty red part of the city. I understand it's a riding that can go blue, but it only really goes blue if the blue team is basically coming as close to running the board as it can reasonably ever hope. And like I said above, every scenario is bad for the Liberals. I don't know how the summer is going to go at party headquarters, but it might be awkward at times.
SD: When I interviewed Trudeau earlier this year, I asked him who he consults in tough times. He said, "Define 'tough times,' because that sounds like 'Monday' to me." That will be this Monday, for sure. Actually, though, I've been thinking about another quote, from Bill Clinton, who famously said that Democrats need to fall in love, while Republicans need to fall in line. When I spoke to some people in Toronto-St. Paul's, they said there's no Poilievre-mania out there - the kind of enthusiasm one might expect for a leader with those soaring poll numbers. But maybe Conservatives don't need voters to fall in love, they just need them to fall in line. And that does mean Toronto-St. Paul's is a test for them, too.
MG: Two summers ago, I observed a palpable shift in attitudes toward the Liberals in my own riding, which is just a few minutes from Toronto-St. Paul's. I know the polling didn't begin to move until about a year after that, but I felt something happening sooner. I'd be watching my daughter play soccer and some other parent would randomly confide in me, "I usually vote Liberal but I'm done with Trudeau." And I'd just sort of smile and nod and try to return my attention to the game, but that kept happening. I know that anecdotes aren't data, but after like the fifth time this happened, I began to realize that something was afoot. And maybe a perfect way of putting it is that people the Liberals count on, especially here in the Centre of the Universe, fell out of love. And then about a year later, they started falling in line. And the polls spotted that.
SD: That's the on-the-ground intelligence I don't have. Interesting. I know you're off next week, but I may try to lure you into a private chat so we can dissect what it all means when the dust settles on Monday night.
Susan Delacourt is an Ottawa-based columnist for the Star, whereshe has been covering politics on Parliament Hill since the late1980s. A political science graduate of Western University, she'swritten four political books; her latest, Shopping for Votes, was afinalist for the 2014 Hilary Weston Writers' Trust prize innon-fiction. Susan is also a regular commentator on CBC and CTV,and has taught journalism and political communication at CarletonUniversity. Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt@readtheline.ca
CAPTION: Arlyn McAdoreyTHE CANADIAN PRESS
